Digitized by Google Tres
translation: But Venus and Mercury, from which either of them separates from the degrees of the Sun in the middle of its retrogradation, they appear until they are combust by the Sun in their direct motion, they are called oriental: and from which they separate from the Sun in the middle of their direct motion until they are again combust in their retrogradation, they are called occidental masculine when they are in their rising, that is, when they are oriental they are called dextri, and they are said to be in their setting, that is, when they are occidental, they are called sinistri, they are said to be feminine, then they are stronger.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Aspects and Latitudes
text: ò austrałem de siderat. Exempli gratia, si ☉ fit in principio Capricorni, cum australi latitudine descendente p.7. Venus verò sit in principio Cancri, habens latitudinë borealem ascendentem similiter p.7. Mars Veneri perfectissimè & Gualenes opponetur. Stellæ verò lógitudinis partibus 180.distantes, si latitudines contrarias non habuerint, id est, si vna septentrionem lustrante, altera in parte meridionali non sit, in partili diametro longitudinis versantur, & non latitudinis aut Gualenes, quantūuis latitudines equales & diuersas, hoc est, vnam ascendentem, altera verò descenden tem habuerint. Porro reliquarum corporearum copulationu, quas Ptolemæus contemplít, autor vnico saltem verbo infinua re videtur, Soli iuniores obseruant: Triquetræ, quamuis contra rias, non tamen æquales latitudines desiderant. Hexagonæ nec æquales nec contrarias, sed saltem ad vnam plagam inæqualiter tendentes, exposcunt. Quadrangulares denique corporum co pulæ nihil horum admittunt, sed omninò id flagitant, vt vtraq; stella, vel saltem earum altera sit in Ecliptica, atque latitudinis omnis expers. Quæ omnia facilimè intelliguntur ex specula tione de aspectibus Ioannis Blanchini, qui in circulo quodam magno super Eclipticam inclinato, & per corpus stellæ quan tamcunq; latitudinéhabentis transeunte, radiationum seu aspe ctuum interualla accipit. Huius circuli vterque polus exiſtit in circulo latitudinis per polos Eclipticæ & centrum stellæ descri pto,& Digitized by Google DIFFERENTIA to, & tanta intercapedine distat à polis Ecliptica, quanta est ipsius stellæ latitudo, seu ab ipsa Ecliptica remotio. Cum autem omnes circuli magni in sphæra sese mutuò bifariam interſe cent, perspicuum est, stellarum latitudinem quantamcunque habentium, vtrumque quadrangulum perinde ac si latitudine carerent, in Ecliptica perpetuò manere, vtroque trigono & he xagono vnà cum diametro extra Eclipticam non vno modo, sed variabiliter cadentibus. Nam si latitudo stellæ fuerit septentrio nalis, vterque eius hexagonus in latitudine septentrionali ma nebit, diametro cum ambobus triangulis ad meridionalem pla gam procurrentibus. Econtrariò verò si latitudo stellæ meri dionalis fuerit, vterque quoque eius aspectus sextilis in meri dionali parte mundi commorabitur, oppositione cum suis col lateralibus triangulis ad septentriones transmisis: cuius rei ti bi compendium grammatico typo subiecimus. Apposuimus au tem & numeros, qui indicant latitudines triquetrarum & sex tilium radiationum, quæ à stellis plus octonis partibus ab Ecli ptica minimè distantibus deriuantur. Vt in exemplo pauldante ànobis posito, Mars principium Capricorni tenens cum latitu dine australi p. 7. vtrunque hexagonum in meridie relinquit, distantem ab Ecliptica p.3.29 1/2,37 2/3, reliquis radijs omnibus, exceptis quadraturis, qui in Eclipticam ad vnguem procidunt, ad septentriones eiaculatis. Ac oppositæ radiationis tanta est la titudo, quanta ipsius Martis, nempe p.7.septent. triquetrarum verò par. 3.29 1/2,37 2/3, etiam septent. id quod ostendunt ad scripti numeri, & prætereà id quoque monstrant, trigonos & hexagonos tanto penè interuallo distare ab Ecliptica, quanta est. medietas stellarum latitudinis. i Digitized by Google TABEL }
translation: For example, if the Sun is at the beginning of Capricorn, with a southern descending latitude of 7 parts, and Venus is at the beginning of Cancer, having a northern ascending latitude of likewise 7 parts, Mars will be perfectly and equally opposed to Venus. However, stars distant by 180 parts of longitude, if they do not have contrary latitudes, that is, if one is not in the northern part while the other is in the southern part, are in a partile diameter of longitude and not of latitude or equality, even if they have equal and diverse latitudes, that is, one ascending and the other descending. Furthermore, the author seems to hint at the other corporeal conjunctions that Ptolemy contemplates with a single word, which younger astrologers observe: Trines, although they desire contrary, do not desire equal latitudes. Sextiles require neither equal nor contrary latitudes, but at least tending unequally to one side. Finally, quadrangular corporeal conjunctions admit none of these, but absolutely demand that both stars, or at least one of them, be in the Ecliptic, and entirely devoid of latitude. All these things are most easily understood from the speculation on aspects by Johannes Blanchinus, who, in a large circle inclined above the Ecliptic and passing through the body of a star, however much latitude it has, takes the intervals of radiations or aspects. Both poles of this circle exist in the circle of latitude described through the poles of the Ecliptic and the center of the star, and are distant from the poles of the Ecliptic by as much as the latitude of the star itself, or its distance from the Ecliptic. Since all great circles in the sphere intersect each other mutually in two parts, it is clear that for stars having any latitude, both quadrangles remain perpetually in the Ecliptic, as if they lacked latitude, while both trines and sextiles, along with the diameter, fall outside the Ecliptic not in one way, but variably. For if the latitude of the star is northern, both of its sextiles will remain in northern latitude, with the diameter and both triangles extending to the southern side. Conversely, if the latitude of the star is southern, both of its sextile aspects will also remain in the southern part of the world, with the opposition and its collateral triangles transmitted to the north: a summary of which we have provided for you in a grammatical form. We have also added numbers, which indicate the latitudes of trine and sextile radiations, which are derived from stars not less than eight parts distant from the Ecliptic. As in the example we just gave, Mars holding the beginning of Capricorn with a southern latitude of 7 parts leaves both sextiles in the south, distant from the Ecliptic by 3.29 1/2, 37 2/3 parts, with all other rays, except the squares, which fall exactly on the Ecliptic, cast to the north. And the latitude of the opposite radiation is as much as that of Mars itself, namely 7 parts north. The trines, however, are 3.29 1/2, 37 2/3 parts, also north, which the inscribed numbers show, and furthermore, they also show that trines and sextiles are almost as distant from the Ecliptic as half the latitude of the stars.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Mutual Deflux and Application
text: DIFFERENTIA in parte 24. procedens. in parte 25 retrocedens. Hic & & & fibi mutuò occurrunt, & Ggnificant duplicationem effectuum,qui per huiufmodi applicationem fieri folent. Defluxus autem mutuus fit, cùm planeta qui eſt in pluribus partibus, eſt directus, & qui in paucioribus retrocedens, & lignificat fruftrationem ſignificatorum, veluti ſi ſit 4 in parte 9.४ procedens. ♀ in parte 8.४ retrocedens. Hi funt modi generales applicationum & defluxun,& haben tur per diuifionem.Aut enim vterque planetarum reperitur di retus, aut vterque retrogradus:aut denique alter eorum dire Autus,alter verò retrogradus, nec poſſunt inueniri plures cafus, in quibus omnibus quotiefcunque iunguntur intra metamluminis,applicationem, quoties verò iunguntur antequam leuio res grauiorum metam luminis exeant, ſeparationem ſeu deflu. xum facient, ficut diximus. In his copulationibus & ſequentibus omnibus facilimè & breuifsimè dijudicandis non parum iuuaberis compendio generaliter omnium planetarum omnes aſpectus tam ad ſe ipſos, quam ad reliquas figure partes effufos complectente, quod in fingulorú genituris aliarūne rerum principijs πινακικοōs tibi deſcribere poteris in hunc modú.Duc in lõgitudiné liness circiter tredecim,atq; in ſpacijs ſummitatú ab Ariete incipiédo duodecim figniferi ſigna deſcribe.Poftea lineas prioribus tranſuerfales ſeptem ſpacia pro numero ſepté planetarum complectentes ducito. Denique in primo ſupremoque ſpacio depinge characterem illius planetz, qui paucio. res ſuiſigni gradus perfecit, idque ſub ſigno illo in quo plane. taille paucifsimarum partium reperitur. Proximum ſpacium occupare debet is planeta, qui poſt priorem pauciores partes ſui ſigni perambulauit. Pari ratione ſequentia ſpacia reliquis planetis tribuenda funt, ita vt vltimum ſpaciŭ fortiaturis,qui cæteris omnibus plures ſui ſigni partes peragrauit. His ita ſe habentibus,ſi ſingulorum planetarum epochis ad marginem annotatis, ex vtroq; latere planetarum, aſpectus vniuerfos.conuenientibus ſignorum ſpacijs inſcripſeris, tabellam radiorum Altronomicam perfectam obtinebis. : TABELTERTIA. 335 TABELLA ASPECTVVM PLANETARVM non tantùm ad applicationes & ſeparationes dijudicandas, fed & ad Apheticorum locorum deductiones conferens, ad exemplum à nobis aſſumptum accommodata. ४/४७27m+ X P. Nam planetæ in Ecliptica per par.60.diftantes, hexagono, qui par.90.ex quadrangulo, qui p. 120.ex triangulo, qui par.180.ex diametro ſeſe vident. Quòd ſi non-ad vnguem inueneris hunc partium numerum, afpice pri mùm ad Saturnum, & perpende quis planetarum reliquorum aut an alicuius planetz aſpectus eum præcedat,intra metas radiorum, ſuprà de ſententia Ptolemæi annotatas, aut fi aliorum opiniones te dele&ant, intra medietatem, vt vocant, orbis, nam is idem ipſiapplicare dicitur. Similiter ſi planetam vel aſpectú intra dictos limites Saturnum ſequi deprehenderis, eundem planetam à Saturno ſeparari ſeu defluere pronunciabis.In propoſito exemplo Venus Gniſtro ſexangulo Saturno applicat.Et Iuppiter à dextro hexagono Saturni defluens, triangulo Lunz & corpori Mercurij applicat, &c. Et cùm fe paratur &c. 4 Translatio luminis ſeu naturę eſt, quæ fit cú tres fuerint plaplaneta, netæ, & leuiſsimus fuerit medius inter reliquos & intra metas: Tunc leuiſsimus dicitur transferre lume anterioris ad ſequentem: Eſt igitur ſententia verborum autoris, quando leuis ſeparatur à ponderoſo & ante completam ſeparationem alteri corpore vel aſpectu coniungitur: tuncleuis ille transfertlumen & naturam primi ad ſecundum:verbi gratia, fit 4 in parte 4. Quod ideo adijcio, ne quis translationem luminis & prohibitionem ab aspectu, de qua poſteà dicetur, nihil differre putaret.Sequitur alius modus trá- lationis luminis priori valdè affinis. Hæc eft generalis via translationum, quam recentiores quidam ad paucos cafus conftringunt. Vocant enim translationem luminis & virtutis tum demum fieri, quando planeta leuis ſeparatur à ponderofiore ſe, & applicat alteri qui eiufdem ſit qualitatis: veluti ſi ſit • in parte 1.. > in parte 1.II. in parte 8. translation: The DIFFERENCE is that in the 24th part it proceeds, in the 25th part it retrogrades. Here, they meet each other, and signify a duplication of effects, which usually occur through such an application. Mutual deflux, however, occurs when the planet that is in more parts is direct, and the one in fewer parts is retrograding, and it signifies the frustration of significations, as if Mars were in the 9th part of Taurus proceeding, and Venus in the 8th part of Taurus retrograding. These are the general modes of applications and defluxes, and they are obtained by division. For either both planets are found direct, or both retrograde; or finally, one of them is direct, and the other retrograde, and no more cases can be found, in all of which, as often as they are conjoined within the orb of light, they will make an application; but as often as they are conjoined before the lighter planets leave the orb of light of the heavier ones, they will make a separation or deflux, as we have said. In these conjunctions and all subsequent ones, you will be greatly helped in judging them most easily and briefly by a compendium that generally embraces all aspects of all planets, both to themselves and to the other parts of the figure, which you can describe for yourself in the nativities of individuals or the beginnings of other things in this way: Draw about thirteen lines in length, and in the spaces at the top, starting from Aries, describe the twelve signs of the zodiac. Then draw seven transversal lines to the previous ones, encompassing spaces for the number of the seven planets. Finally, in the first and uppermost space, depict the character of that planet which has completed fewer degrees of its sign, and this under the sign in which the planet of the fewest parts is found. The next space should be occupied by that planet which, after the first, has traversed fewer parts of its sign. By the same reasoning, the subsequent spaces are to be assigned to the remaining planets, so that the last space is allotted to the one who has traversed more parts of its sign than all the others. With these things thus arranged, if you have noted the epochs of each planet in the margin, and inscribed all the aspects on both sides of the planets in the appropriate sign spaces, you will obtain a perfect Astronomical Table of Rays. TABLE THREE. 335 TABLE OF PLANETARY ASPECTS, useful not only for judging applications and separations, but also for the deductions of Aphetic places, adapted to the example we have assumed. This table clearly shows all planetary aspects, applications, and defluxes. For planets distant by 60 parts in the Ecliptic see each other by a sextile, by 90 parts by a square, by 120 parts by a trine, by 180 parts by an opposition. If you do not find this exact number of parts, first look at Saturn, and consider which of the other planets, or if the aspect of any planet, precedes it, within the orbs of rays, noted above according to Ptolemy's opinion, or if the opinions of others delight you, within the half-orb, as they call it, for it is said to apply to the same. Similarly, if you find a planet or aspect following Saturn within the said limits, you will pronounce the same planet to be separating or defluxing from Saturn. In the proposed example, Venus applies to Saturn by a sinister sextile. And Jupiter, separating from Saturn by a dexter sextile, applies to the Moon by a trine and to the body of Mercury, etc. After Saturn is completed, the remaining heavier planets are to be similarly compared to the lighter ones. What we should attribute to the latitudes of the stars in judging aspects, we have discussed at length above. 336 DIFFERENCE. And when it is 2. Kenodromia, or the empty course of a planet, is when it is not mixed with other stars, etc., that is, when it is separated from the conjunction of any other, and is not joined to another by body or aspect, and as long as the planet is in the same sign, it is said to be empty in course, which is to be understood according to the orbs of rays, which we have just defined. This accident is observed especially in the Moon (an example is in Ptolemy, book 3, chapter on parents), and is almost neglected in others. For example, let the Moon be in conjunction with Jupiter, and after its separation from Jupiter, let there be no planet in Sagittarius to which it can be joined within the prescribed limits, nor any ray of anyone it can aspect; the Moon will be said to be empty in course until, having passed Sagittarius, it is joined to someone by body or aspect. And when it is, 3. A feral planet is named when it is alone in a sign, separated from the conjunction of others and from their aspecting rays, thus named from the likeness of a man alienated from society, or a wild beast living in solitude. In this way, the strength of planets becomes languid and is significantly weakened, especially if they are also peregrine at the same time. A planet is called peregrine when it is not in any of its essential dignities, as Saturn in the 6th part, because it has no dignity, it is considered peregrine. The difference between kenodromia and feralitas is that in the former, the star in the sign it traverses has a companion ray from which it separates; in the latter, however, it is deprived of all light from others. And when it separates, etc. 4. Translation of light or nature is what occurs when there are three planets, and the lightest is in the middle between the others and within the orbs: Then the lightest is said to transfer the light of the anterior to the subsequent: Therefore, the meaning of the author's words is, when the light planet separates from the heavy one and, before the complete separation, joins another by body or aspect: then that light planet transfers the light and nature of the first to the second: for example, let Jupiter be in the 4th part of Pisces, the Moon in the 15th part of Pisces, Mars in the 21st part of Leo. Here the Moon separates from Jupiter corporally, and before the complete separation, applying itself from a square to Mars, it transfers the light of Jupiter to him. For the light of Jupiter is 12 parts, and that of Mars is 7 parts, as we have placed above. Another example, let Venus be in the 8th part of Gemini, the Moon in the 10th part of Gemini, Jupiter in the 10th part of Pisces. Here Venus separates from the sextile aspect of Mercury, and is partially joined to Jupiter by a square, and transfers the light of Mercury to Jupiter himself. However, it does not prohibit Mercury from conjunction with Jupiter. For Gemini and Pisces are disjunct signs. I add this so that no one would think that the translation of light and the prohibition from aspect, which will be discussed later, do not differ. Another mode of translation of light, very similar to the former, follows. When, he says, a light planet joins a heavier one by any conjunction, and that heavier one in turn joins another still heavier one: then the middle one is said to transfer the nature of the lightest to the heaviest. Example, let Jupiter be in the 15th part of Leo, the Moon in the 14th part of Taurus, Saturn in the 16th part of Taurus. Here Jupiter, mixed with the Moon by a sextile aspect, makes Saturn partake of the nature of the Moon, although the Moon itself is plainly unconjoined with Saturn. Otherwise and better. When three planets are in one sign, if the heavier is in more parts, and the lighter in fewer, and not within the orbs, but the middle one is within the orb of application to the slower, and within the orb by which the lighter applies to it: then the middle one is said to transfer the power of the lighter to the slower: as if the Sun is in the 29th part of Leo, Jupiter in the 28th part of Leo, Saturn in the 18th part of Leo. Here Jupiter transfers power to Saturn. This is the general way of translations, which some moderns restrict to a few cases. For they call the translation of light and virtue to occur only when a light planet separates from a heavier one, and applies to another of the same quality: as if the Sun is in the 1st part of Aries, the Moon in the 1st part of Gemini, Mars in the 8th part of Gemini. In this case, the Moon, separating from the Sun, and applying to the body of Mars, transfers heat to Mars, who is also hot. For the Sun bestows its power, which the Moon received from it by aspect, to Mars. 6. Reddi.

title: Calculating Conception Time and House Cusps
text: 3 } QUARTA. 375 eſt, ſempper æſtimatam præcedat.Cæterùm hora &reliqua ſcru pula conceptionis fic inquirantur. Si me 376 DIFFERENTIA Si medium cæli cognitum fuerit, aſcenſio eiufdem recta & reliquarum cuſpidum aſcenſiones obliquæ per ea quæ ſupra in differentia prima de domiciliorum cæleſtium fabrica diximus, non latebunt. Quòd ſi horoſcopum examinatum habueris, tuncab aſcenſione eiufdem in climate ſubtrahe partes 90. accommodata integrareuolutione ſi opus fuerit, & relinquetur aſcenſio medij cæli, qua cognita,per continuam partium 30. additionem reliquarum quinque ſequentium cuſpidumaſcenſiones inquiras, atque ſingulis aſcenſionibus partes Eclipticę correſpondentes ex tabulis ſingulis cuſpidibus conuenientibus , vt ſuprà eſt expoſitum,elicias. Porrò quomodo dato_aſcendente vel medio cæli reliquæ cuſpides ex tabula æquationis domorum deſcribendæ fint, ſatis ex ipſa tabula apparet, quæ à lateribus cuiuſuis cardinis ſitum hemispherij continet. Hæc omnia ex ſe clariora ſunt, quàm vt exemplis elucidari debeant. Ad extremum tempus veræ genituræ inueſtigabis hoc modo. Quere aſcenſionem obliquam afcendentis gradus tum eſtimati, tum emendati, atque minorem à maiore ſubtrahe:Differentiam verò conuerte in tempus,idq; tempori eſtimato adijcias, ſi aſcenſio obliqua emendatihorofcopi fuerit maior quam eſtimati, hoceſt, ſi natiuitatem veram eſtimata preceſsſit, aut ab eadem ſubtrahe, ſi contrarium acciderit. Quòd ſi tabulis æquandarum domorum vtimauis, confer tempus ſcriptum in tabula equationum iuxta gradum mediy cæli eſtima ti ad tempus ſcriptum in eadem tabula iuxta gra dum medijcœliemendati, atque minus aufer à maiori:differen tiam verò adde ad tempus eſtimatum, ſi gradus eſtimato tempore horoſcopans preceſsit horoſcopum verum, vel ſubtrahe idem ab eodem, ſi contrarium acciderit, & proueniet tempus geniturę vere, ad quod ſi etiam motus ſtellarum cum nodis Lu ne & forte fortunarum deſcripſerimus, figuram cæliomnibus numerisabſolutam obtinebimus. I Et ex hoc Hylech, id eſt, locus vitæ in natiuitati. bus, cuius ſcientia eſt, vt primò aſpicias horam natiuitatis, que ſi fuerit in die, incipies à Sole: quiſi fuerit ante gradum 1 1. QUARTA 877 gradum ſigni aſcendentis per V. gradus aut infrà: aut fuerit in X. vel Xl.fiue masculinum frerit ipſimo ſigenum ſen fæmininu, aptus erit vt fit bylech. Si autem fuerit in his tribus locis,ſcilicet in ſeptima, velociana, vel nona in ſigno masculino, ſimiliter aptus ert ut fit byslech. Si verò fuerit in his tribus locisin ſigno feminino, non erit aptus vt fit hylesh. Hinc aſpicies Lunem: que ſi fuerit in aſcendente vel in ſecunda vel tertia,aut in feptima vel oclana, erit apta hylech, fiue masculinum fiue femininum fuerit fignum. Et ſi fuerit ante gradum ſigs ni aſcendentis per V. gradus vel infrà: aut aut ſi fo fuerin fuerint in viderle X.vel XI. aut in quarta vel quinta vel in ſeptimu* in gédum: in figno feminino, aptetur bytech. Si autem Luna non fuerit in eis, aſpicies poſt hoc ad Solem, qui ſi fuerit ante gradum ſeptimæ per V.gradus vel infrà, aut ſi fuerit in quarta vel in quinta, erit aptus De fit bylech, ſiue ſignum fuerit masoutenum ſiue femininum. Si verò fuerit ante aſcendantem K. gradibas vel" Videt reinfrà:aut in aſcendente, vel in ſecundo ſigno masculo, Nam talis aptabituretiam bylech. Si autem fuerit in aliquo iſtozŭ politus docoram infigno feminino, wan wansritapeus erit aptushytech. Qadd tem, ſed fo luminarium ali quod non fuerit aptum bylech: Afpice diem effipoſt hoc natiuitatem, vtrum fit coniundionalis aut pre mentionalis, ideft, tram fit poſt coniunctionem velpre uentio. ==Start of page 1== 378 DIFFERENTIA 'mentionem. Quòd fi fuerit natiuitas coniunctionalis, & fuerit gradus coniunctionis in aliquo angulorum: vel in ſuccedenti angulo:erit in loco apto hylech. Si verò prædi &us gradus fuerit cadens ab his octo locis .i. ab angulis & à ſuccedentibus angulis, non erit hylech. Afpicies tunc gradum partis fortunæ: qui fi fuerit in aliquo angulorũ, vel in fuccedentibus angulis: erit in loco apto hylech. Si verò cadès fuerit ab his VIII.locis: afpicies poſt hoc gra dum aſcendentis & conſtitues eam hylech. Si verò natiuitas prænentionis fuerit, incipies à gradu prænentionis, afpiciesq́ eum ficut priùs fecifti in gradu coniunctio nis:& poſt ipſum gradu partis fortunæ:poſt ipſum gra dum aſcendentis eo ordine quo prædiximus in gradu co iunctionis. De ſexu autem ſignorum non curabis.i. non aſpicies vtrum ſint ſigna illa maſcul.vel fœmin.niſi tan -tummodo in luminaribus, hoc eſt, in gradu cõjunctionis, vel prænentionis & aſcendentis & partis fortunæ non -conſideres verum fint in maſcul.ſignis vel fœmin.tam in angulis, quàm in ſuccedentibus angulorum:ſed hæc qua tuor tantum erunt tunc apta bylech, cùm fuerint in his o&o locis, id est, in angulis vel ſuccedentibus eorum : fi verò fuerint in locis recedentibus, id est, cadentibus ab angulis, non erunt apta bylech. 2. Apparet itaq quòd Sol fit bylech aptus in die, & no &e ſuper terram, & ſub terra in XI.locis, ſuper terram in VI.locis & ſub terra in V. Luna quoq apta erit hylech in die, & nocte ſuper terram, & ſub terra ſimiliter in XI.locis, ſuper terram in X.& ſub terra in VI. 3 Et cum Digitized by Google ==End of page 1==
translation: However, the hour and remaining minutes of conception are inquired as follows: From the ascension of the climate, beginning from the start of Aries, of the place which the Moon held at the estimated time of birth, subtract the right ascension, beginning from Capricorn, of the Sun's place for the completed day of conception. For what remains, if converted into time, will show the hours and minutes of conception. Or, alternatively, enter with the Sun's place for the estimated time of conception into the table of houses for your climate under the title of the tenth house, and note the corresponding time in the margin. Then, similarly, by the motion of the estimated day of birth, extract the time from the same table under the title of the first house. Having done this, subtract the time from the time (assuming 24 hours if subtraction cannot be performed), and the hours and minutes of the true conception will remain, which are to be added to the previously found completed days. To the time thus formed, the true place is to be sought, which is judged to be the true degree of the rising cardinal point in the genesis according to this method. In our example, the lunar epoch's oblique ascension calculated from Aries is 341 degrees, 318 minutes. The Sun's true place at midday of the given day is found to be 28 degrees, 12 minutes of Leo, and the ascension of the same place, beginning from Capricorn, is 240 degrees, 231 minutes. Subtracting this from the oblique ascension of the Moon's place leaves 100 degrees, 4013 minutes, which is 6 hours, 43 minutes, 12 seconds. The same time is more easily collected from the table of houses constructed for latitude 41 degrees, thus: Hours, minutes, seconds. Time of Moon's birth under the title of the first house: 16. Time of Sun's conception under the title of the tenth house: 10. Post-meridian hours of conception: 6 hours, 45 minutes, 2 seconds, 43 minutes. Therefore, according to this calculation, conception occurred in the year of Christ 1526, on August 12, at 6 hours, 43 minutes, 1 second. At this time, the Moon's true place is 24 degrees, 101 minutes of Libra, and this is the same part of the sky horoscoping at the time of birth, explored by the balance of Hermes. How, once the horoscope or midheaven is known, the other cusps become known. If the midheaven is known, its right ascension and the oblique ascensions of the other cusps will not be hidden by what we said above in the first difference concerning the construction of the celestial domiciles. But if you have examined the horoscope, then from its ascension in the climate, subtract 90 degrees, accommodating a full revolution if necessary, and the ascension of the midheaven will remain. Knowing this, by continuous addition of 30 degrees, you inquire into the ascensions of the remaining five subsequent cusps, and for each ascension, you extract the corresponding degrees of the Ecliptic from the tables suitable for each cusp, as explained above. Furthermore, how, given the ascendant or midheaven, the other cusps are to be described from the table of the equation of houses, is sufficiently apparent from the table itself, which contains the position of the hemisphere on the sides of each cardinal point. All these things are clearer in themselves than to need elucidation by examples. Finally, you will investigate the time of true birth in this way: Find the oblique ascension of the ascendant degree, both estimated and corrected, and subtract the smaller from the larger. Convert the difference into time, and add it to the estimated time if the oblique ascension of the corrected horoscope was greater than that of the estimated one, that is, if the true birth preceded the estimated one, or subtract it if the opposite occurred. But if you use tables for equating houses, compare the time written in the table of equations next to the degree of the estimated midheaven with the time written in the same table next to the degree of the corrected midheaven, and subtract the smaller from the larger. Add the difference to the estimated time if the horoscoping degree at the estimated time preceded the true horoscope, or subtract it from the same if the opposite occurred, and the time of true birth will result. To this, if we also describe the motions of the stars with the nodes of the Moon and perhaps the parts of fortune, we will obtain a celestial figure complete in all respects. And from this, the Hylech, that is, the place of life in nativities, whose knowledge is such that you first look at the hour of birth. If it is in the day, you begin with the Sun: if it is within 5 degrees or less before the degree of the ascendant sign, or if it is in the 10th or 11th house, whether the sign is masculine or feminine, it will be suitable to be the Hylech. But if it is in these three places, namely in the 7th, 8th, or 9th house in a masculine sign, it will similarly be suitable to be the Hylech. If, however, it is in these three places in a feminine sign, it will not be suitable to be the Hylech. Next, you look at the Moon: if it is in the ascendant or in the 2nd or 3rd house, or in the 7th or 8th house, it will be suitable to be the Hylech, whether the sign is masculine or feminine. And if it is within 5 degrees or less before the degree of the ascendant sign: or if it is in the 10th or 11th house, or in the 4th or 5th house, or in the 7th house in a feminine sign, it will be suitable to be the Hylech. But if it is in any of these in a masculine sign, it will not be suitable to be the Hylech, if the birth was in the day. If, however, the birth was at night, we begin with the Moon: if it is in any of the aforementioned places, according to what we have said, it will be suitable to be the Hylech. But if the Moon is not in them, you then look at the Sun, which if it is within 5 degrees or less before the degree of the 7th house, or if it is in the 4th or 5th house, it will be suitable to be the Hylech, whether the sign is masculine or feminine. But if it is within 5 degrees or less before the ascendant: or in the ascendant, or in the second masculine sign, then it will also be suitable to be the Hylech. But if it is in any of these places in a feminine sign, it will not be suitable to be the Hylech. If neither of the luminaries is suitable to be the Hylech: Then look at the day of birth, whether it is conjunct or pre-conjunct, that is, whether it is after the conjunction or before the conjunction. If the birth was conjunct, and the degree of conjunction was in any of the angles: or in a succeeding angle: it will be in a suitable place for the Hylech. But if the aforementioned degree was falling from these eight places, i.e., from the angles and from the succeeding angles, it will not be the Hylech. Then you look at the degree of the Part of Fortune: if it is in any of the angles, or in the succeeding angles: it will be in a suitable place for the Hylech. But if it was falling from these eight places: you then look at the degree of the ascendant and establish it as the Hylech. If, however, the birth was pre-conjunct, you begin with the degree of the pre-conjunction, and you look at it as you did before with the degree of conjunction: and after it, the degree of the Part of Fortune: after it, the degree of the ascendant in the order we mentioned for the degree of conjunction. You will not care about the gender of the signs, i.e., you will not look whether those signs are masculine or feminine, except only in the luminaries, that is, in the degree of conjunction, or pre-conjunction and ascendant and Part of Fortune, you do not consider whether they are in masculine or feminine signs, both in the angles and in the succeeding angles: but these four will only be suitable to be the Hylech when they are in these eight places, that is, in the angles or their succedents: but if they are in receding places, that is, falling from the angles, they will not be suitable to be the Hylech. It appears therefore that the Sun is suitable to be the Hylech by day, and by night above the earth, and below the earth in 11 places, above the earth in 6 places and below the earth in 5. The Moon will also be suitable to be the Hylech by day, and by night above the earth, and below the earth similarly in 11 places, above the earth in 10 and below the earth in 6. And with

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Malefic Squares and Anareta
text: quadratos in principium Geminorum: hi quadrati radij maleficę, poterunt opprimere Aphetam Solem, quamuis Saturnus ab initio non refpiciat. Sed eadem conditio etiam requiritur in obedientibus, quibus malefici veluti tyranniimperant. Quando itaque locus Aphetz obedit loco Anzretz, tunc Anareta ad Aphetam deductus perimit. Verùm enimuerò cùm corpora ſenio confecta leui ex cauſa diſsipentur, poteruntantiſcia malorum in ſenibus, vitæ dominum per ſeſſou abſque prædicta conditione opprimere.. Hinc trigoni maleficaru è ſignis breuiú afcéſionú afflicti interficiunt:Et econtrariò è ſignis longarū aſcenſionú deprauati hexagoni,eadé ex cauſa occidut. Quas radiationes vt facilè diſcer nas, aſpice locum refpicientem & locum in quem cadunt radij, & fi domus horum locorum ſe reſpexerint ſextili, erit natura radij illius ſextilis: et fi domus ſe reſpexerint ex quadrangulo, erit natura radij quadrati:& fi trigono, trigoni, vnde imum medium cæli ſemper refpicit-horoſcopum quadrato æquino&ia liss veluti ſi aſcendat, in imo medio cæli intelligitur verfari Taurus fignum equinoctialis, quamuis ratione zodiaci ſignum Geminorum ibidem reperiatur. Interimunt autem hu iufmodi quoque quadrati, quia ſigna ex quadrato ſe reſpicientia vniuerfaliter funt naturæ contrariæ. Ad extremum.cum vitæ moderationem inijt, Solis quoque Locus interimit, quando videlicet malefici alicuius congreſſu oppreſſus à beneficorum nemine juuaturaut liberatur. Quapropter cùm malefica ſit in cauſa, vt focus incurrens Lunam exiſtenté aphetam perimat, fequitur quòd fi benefica loco malefice ponatur cum Sole, locum Solis nequaquam interfe&urum. Arabes Anæretis, quos numerauimus, cufpidem ſextam, ſeptimam, & octauam, atq; earundem cuſpidum dominos adijciunt. Similiter & cufpidem quartz, quando aquea & infortunata eſſe deprehenditur. Item loca tenebroſa, loca nebuloſa, lo ca Eclipfis, loca Cometarum tempore natiuitatis apparentium, cor Scorpij,cor Leonis, Item & Lune, partem Martis, 1 partem azemena; Denique mercurij radios & & impedire dicunt,
translation: squares into the beginning of Gemini: these square rays of the malefic can oppress the Apheta Sun, even if Saturn does not aspect it from the beginning. But the same condition is also required in the obedient, whom malefics rule like tyrants. Therefore, when the place of the Apheta obeys the place of the Anareta, then the Anareta, led to the Apheta, destroys it. But indeed, since bodies worn out by old age are easily dissipated by a slight cause, the antiscia of evils in the elderly can oppress the lord of life through itself without the aforementioned condition. Hence, the trines of malefics from short ascension signs, when afflicted, kill; and conversely, the corrupted sextiles from long ascension signs kill for the same reason. To easily discern these radiations, look at the aspecting place and the place into which the rays fall, and if the houses of these places aspect each other by a sextile, the nature of that ray will be sextile; and if the houses aspect each other by a square, the nature of the ray will be square; and if by a trine, a trine. Hence, the Imum Coeli always aspects the Ascendant by a square in equinoctial signs, as if Taurus, an equinoctial sign, is understood to be in the Imum Coeli when Gemini ascends, even though by zodiacal reckoning, the sign of Gemini is found there. Such squares also kill because signs aspecting each other by a square are universally of contrary nature. Finally, when one enters a moderate life, the place of the Sun also kills, namely, when oppressed by the conjunction of some malefic, it is helped or freed by no benefic. Therefore, since a malefic is the cause, so that a focus falling upon the Moon, being the Apheta, kills, it follows that if a benefic is placed in the place of the malefic with the Sun, it will by no means kill the place of the Sun. The Arabs add the sixth, seventh, and eighth cusps to the Anaretas we have enumerated, and the lords of those cusps. Similarly, also the fourth cusp, when it is found to be watery and unfortunate. Also dark places, nebulous places, places of Eclipses, places of Comets appearing at the time of birth, the Heart of Scorpio, the Heart of Leo, also the Moon, the part of Mars, the part of Azemena; finally, they say that the rays of Mercury also impede.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Profections of the World's Years
text: QUARTA. 405 4 Quod attinet ad profectiones annorum mundi, quarume- Profetie pochæ magnis coniunctionibus fumuntur, earum latiorem auté, &c. expofitionem de induſtria omifimus, tum quod tota hec res no bis eſſet in vniuerfum ſuſpecta,atque propter caufas anteà di- &as plena abſurdorum errorum videatur: tum verò quòd per ſe ſit facilis,atque ex ijs quæ de profectionibus geniturarum diximus, fatis perſpicua, niſi quòd ſpeciem aliquam difficultatis ipſi adfert Alcabicius, dum annorum mūdi profectiones nõ ad annos Solares, ſed ad annos Perfarum accommodat. De directione ſignificatoris. A I Et ex hoc ſequitur atazir, ideſt,directio:hoc est vt dirigas ſignificatorem aliquem, ad aliquem locum ſignorum, & ſcias quid ſit inter eos ex gradibus directionis, accipiesá vnicuique gradui annum vnum. Cùm ergo volueris ſcire hoc, & fuerit ille ſignificator in afcendente, quem vis dirigere ad aliquam partem circuli, minue aſa cenſiones gradus in quo fuerit ſignificator quem volue ris dirigere, per aſcenſiones regionis de aſcëſionibus gra dus illius, ad quem volueris dirigere eū:& quod reman- ſerit, erit gradus directionis. Quòd ſi fuerit ſignificator in gradu ſeptimæ:minues aſcenſiones nadir, id est, oppo- ſiti gradus in quo eſt ſignificator, de aſcenſionibus nadir illius gradus ad quem volueris ipſum dirigere in illa regiõë. Si verò fuerit ſignificator in medio cæli vel in angu lo terræ: minues aſcenſiones gradus ſignificatoris de aſs ceſionibus illius gradus, ad que volueris eu dirigere per aſcenſiones circuli directi:& quod remanſerit, erit gra dus directionis. 2 Si verò fuerit ille ſignificator quem volueris dirigere,in aliquo loco extra quatuor angulos: aſpicies longitudinem eius ab aliquo horum angulorũ, 3 qui 406 DIFFERENTIA qui funt angulus medij cæli, atq angulus terræ: hoc est, vt aſpicies ſignificatorem, qui ſi fuerit inter aſcendens & medium cæli, minues aſcenſiones gradus medý cæli per circulum directum de aſcenſionibus gradus ſignificatoris. Et ſi fuerit inter VII. & medium cæli: minues aſcenſiones gradus ſignificatoris de aſcenſionibus gra. dus medij cæli per circulú directum:& quod remanſes rit,ex qualicunque horum locorum fuerit, illud diuides per partes horarum illius diei gradus in quo fuerit ſigni ficator:& quod exierit, erunt horæ longitudinis ab angulo. Et ſi fuerit inter aſcendens & angulum terræ: mis nues aſcenſiones gradus ſignificatoris per circulum dire Etum de aſcenſionibus gradus anguli terræ. Rurfus ſi fuerit ſignificator inter angulum terræ & VII. minues aſcenſiones gradus anguli terræ per circutum direclum de aſcenſionibus gradus ſignificatoris: & quod reman. ſerit ex qualicunque horum locorum, diuides per tempo id est, per ra horarum diei nadir:id est, oppoſiti gradus ſignificato tempora ris:& quod exierit, ipſæ erunt longitudinis horæ ab an horaria aſtaurna. gulo, gulo. Cung ſciueris longitudinis horas ab angulo, & volueris dirigerefignificatorem ad aliquem locum circuli ſignorum: & fuerit ſignificator in medio circuli orientalis, qui est à medio cæli vſque ad angulum terræ, de eis quæ ſuccedunt aſcendens : minue aſcenſios *nes gradus illius, in quo fuerit ſignificator de aſcenſioni bus gradus illius, ad quem volueris dirigere per circulū directum: & quod remanſerit, erit ſignificator circuli directi:ſerua cum. Post hoc minues aſcenſiones illius gra dus, QUARTA 407 dus, in quo fuerit ſignificator per aſcenſiones regiones de aſcenſionibus illius gradus, ad quem volueris eum dirigere:& quod remanſerit, erit ſignificator regionis: post hoc aſpice reſiduum, quod fuerit inter ſignificatorem circuli directi, & ſignificatorem regionis, accipiesá ſes xtam parte eius, & multiplicabis in horas longitudinis ab angulo, & quod fuerit, erit æquatio: & ſi fuerit ſignificator circali directi minor ſignificatore regionis:ad des æquationem ſuper ſignificatorem circuli directi. Et ſi fuerit ſignificator circuli direcliplus, minues æquatio nem ex eo: & quod remanſerit, erit gradus directionis. 3 Item ſi fuerit ſignificator, quem volueris dirigere, in medietate circuli occidentalis: quæ est ab angulo terræ, vſque ad medium cæli de eo quod ſuccedit VII. minues aſcenſiones nadir gradus ſignificatoris, quem volueris dirigere de aſcenſionibus nadir gradus illius,ad quem volueris dirigere eum, per circulum directum : & quod remanſerit, erit ſignificator circuli directi. Post hoc minues aſcenſiones nadir gradus ſignificatoris per aſcenſio nes regionis de aſcenſionibus nadir gradus illius, ad que volueris eum dirigere:& quod remanſerit, erit ſignifica tor regionis:deinde accipies reſiduum, quod fuerit inter ſignificatorem circuli directi, & ſignificatore regionis, accipiesá ſextam partem eius, & multiplicabis in horas longitudinis ab angulo: & quod fuerit, erit æquatio. Post hoc aſpicies ſignificatorem circuli directi, ſi fuerit minor ſignificatore regionis,addes æquationem ſuper ſo gnificatore circuli directi: & ſi fuerit ſignificator circus li dire-
translation: Regarding the profections of the world's years, whose epochs are taken from great conjunctions, we have deliberately omitted a broader and more detailed explanation. This is partly because the entire matter seems suspicious to us in general, and full of absurd errors for the reasons previously stated. It is also because it is inherently simple and sufficiently clear from what we have said about the profections of nativities, except that Alcabitius introduces some difficulty by adapting the profections of the world's years not to Solar years, but to Persian years.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: On the Direction of the Significator
text: QUARTA. 405 4 Quod attinet ad profectiones annorum mundi, quarume- Profetie pochæ magnis coniunctionibus fumuntur, earum latiorem auté, &c. expofitionem de induſtria omifimus, tum quod tota hec res no bis eſſet in vniuerfum ſuſpecta,atque propter caufas anteà di- &as plena abſurdorum errorum videatur: tum verò quòd per ſe ſit facilis,atque ex ijs quæ de profectionibus geniturarum diximus, fatis perſpicua, niſi quòd ſpeciem aliquam difficultatis ipſi adfert Alcabicius, dum annorum mūdi profectiones nõ ad annos Solares, ſed ad annos Perfarum accommodat. A I Et ex hoc ſequitur atazir, ideſt,directio:hoc est vt dirigas ſignificatorem aliquem, ad aliquem locum ſignorum, & ſcias quid ſit inter eos ex gradibus directionis, accipiesá vnicuique gradui annum vnum. Cùm ergo volueris ſcire hoc, & fuerit ille ſignificator in afcendente, quem vis dirigere ad aliquam partem circuli, minue aſa cenſiones gradus in quo fuerit ſignificator quem volue ris dirigere, per aſcenſiones regionis de aſcëſionibus gra dus illius, ad quem volueris dirigere eū:& quod reman- ſerit, erit gradus directionis. Quòd ſi fuerit ſignificator in gradu ſeptimæ:minues aſcenſiones nadir, id est, oppo- ſiti gradus in quo eſt ſignificator, de aſcenſionibus nadir illius gradus ad quem volueris ipſum dirigere in illa regiõë. Si verò fuerit ſignificator in medio cæli vel in angu lo terræ: minues aſcenſiones gradus ſignificatoris de aſs ceſionibus illius gradus, ad que volueris eu dirigere per aſcenſiones circuli directi:& quod remanſerit, erit gra dus directionis. 2 Si verò fuerit ille ſignificator quem volueris dirigere,in aliquo loco extra quatuor angulos: aſpicies longitudinem eius ab aliquo horum angulorũ, 3 qui 406 DIFFERENTIA qui funt angulus medij cæli, atq angulus terræ: hoc est, vt aſpicies ſignificatorem, qui ſi fuerit inter aſcendens & medium cæli, minues aſcenſiones gradus medý cæli per circulum directum de aſcenſionibus gradus ſignificatoris. Et ſi fuerit inter VII. & medium cæli: minues aſcenſiones gradus ſignificatoris de aſcenſionibus gra. dus medij cæli per circulú directum:& quod remanſes rit,ex qualicunque horum locorum fuerit, illud diuides per partes horarum illius diei gradus in quo fuerit ſigni ficator:& quod exierit, erunt horæ longitudinis ab angulo. Et ſi fuerit inter aſcendens & angulum terræ: mis nues aſcenſiones gradus ſignificatoris per circulum dire Etum de aſcenſionibus gradus anguli terræ. Rurfus ſi fuerit ſignificator inter angulum terræ & VII. minues aſcenſiones gradus anguli terræ per circutum direclum de aſcenſionibus gradus ſignificatoris: & quod reman. ſerit ex qualicunque horum locorum, diuides per tempo id est, per ra horarum diei nadir:id est, oppoſiti gradus ſignificato tempora ris:& quod exierit, ipſæ erunt longitudinis horæ ab an horaria aſtaurna. gulo, gulo. Cung ſciueris longitudinis horas ab angulo, & volueris dirigerefignificatorem ad aliquem locum circuli ſignorum: & fuerit ſignificator in medio circuli orientalis, qui est à medio cæli vſque ad angulum terræ, de eis quæ ſuccedunt aſcendens : minue aſcenſios *nes gradus illius, in quo fuerit ſignificator de aſcenſioni bus gradus illius, ad quem volueris dirigere per circulū directum: & quod remanſerit, erit ſignificator circuli directi:ſerua cum. Post hoc minues aſcenſiones illius gra dus, QUARTA 407 dus, in quo fuerit ſignificator per aſcenſiones regiones de aſcenſionibus illius gradus, ad quem volueris eum dirigere:& quod remanſerit, erit ſignificator regionis: post hoc aſpice reſiduum, quod fuerit inter ſignificatorem circuli directi, & ſignificatorem regionis, accipiesá ſes xtam parte eius, & multiplicabis in horas longitudinis ab angulo, & quod fuerit, erit æquatio: & ſi fuerit ſignificator circali directi minor ſignificatore regionis:ad des æquationem ſuper ſignificatorem circuli directi. Et ſi fuerit ſignificator circuli direcliplus, minues æquatio nem ex eo: & quod remanſerit, erit gradus directionis. 3 Item ſi fuerit ſignificator, quem volueris dirigere, in medietate circuli occidentalis: quæ est ab angulo terræ, vſque ad medium cæli de eo quod ſuccedit VII. minues aſcenſiones nadir gradus ſignificatoris, quem volueris dirigere de aſcenſionibus nadir gradus illius,ad quem volueris dirigere eum, per circulum directum : & quod remanſerit, erit ſignificator circuli directi. Post hoc minues aſcenſiones nadir gradus ſignificatoris per aſcenſio nes regionis de aſcenſionibus nadir gradus illius, ad que volueris eum dirigere:& quod remanſerit, erit ſignifica tor regionis:deinde accipies reſiduum, quod fuerit inter ſignificatorem circuli directi, & ſignificatore regionis, accipiesá ſextam partem eius, & multiplicabis in horas longitudinis ab angulo: & quod fuerit, erit æquatio. Post hoc aſpicies ſignificatorem circuli directi, ſi fuerit minor ſignificatore regionis,addes æquationem ſuper ſo gnificatore circuli directi: & ſi fuerit ſignificator circus li dire-
translation: From this follows the atazir, that is, the direction: this means that you direct a certain significator to a certain place of the signs, and you know what is between them in degrees of direction, and you will take one year for each degree. Therefore, when you wish to know this, and that significator is in the ascendant, which you wish to direct to some part of the circle, subtract the ascensions of the degree in which the significator you wish to direct is, by the ascensions of the region from the ascensions of that degree to which you wish to direct it: and what remains will be the degree of direction. But if the significator is in the degree of the seventh house: you will subtract the ascensions of the nadir, that is, the opposite of the degree in which the significator is, from the ascensions of the nadir of that degree to which you wish to direct it in that region. But if the significator is in the Midheaven or in the Imum Coeli: you will subtract the ascensions of the significator's degree from the ascensions of that degree to which you wish to direct it by the ascensions of the direct circle: and what remains will be the degree of direction.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Significator Outside Angles
text: QUARTA. 405 4 Quod attinet ad profectiones annorum mundi, quarume- Profetie pochæ magnis coniunctionibus fumuntur, earum latiorem auté, &c. expofitionem de induſtria omifimus, tum quod tota hec res no bis eſſet in vniuerfum ſuſpecta,atque propter caufas anteà di- &as plena abſurdorum errorum videatur: tum verò quòd per ſe ſit facilis,atque ex ijs quæ de profectionibus geniturarum diximus, fatis perſpicua, niſi quòd ſpeciem aliquam difficultatis ipſi adfert Alcabicius, dum annorum mūdi profectiones nõ ad annos Solares, ſed ad annos Perfarum accommodat.
