A I Et ex hoc ſequitur atazir, ideſt,directio:hoc est vt dirigas ſignificatorem aliquem, ad aliquem locum ſignorum, & ſcias quid ſit inter eos ex gradibus directionis, accipiesá vnicuique gradui annum vnum. Cùm ergo volueris ſcire hoc, & fuerit ille ſignificator in afcendente, quem vis dirigere ad aliquam partem circuli, minue aſa cenſiones gradus in quo fuerit ſignificator quem volue ris dirigere, per aſcenſiones regionis de aſcëſionibus gra dus illius, ad quem volueris dirigere eū:& quod reman- ſerit, erit gradus directionis. Quòd ſi fuerit ſignificator in gradu ſeptimæ:minues aſcenſiones nadir, id est, oppo- ſiti gradus in quo eſt ſignificator, de aſcenſionibus nadir illius gradus ad quem volueris ipſum dirigere in illa regiõë. Si verò fuerit ſignificator in medio cæli vel in angu lo terræ: minues aſcenſiones gradus ſignificatoris de aſs ceſionibus illius gradus, ad que volueris eu dirigere per aſcenſiones circuli directi:& quod remanſerit, erit gra dus directionis. 2 Si verò fuerit ille ſignificator quem volueris dirigere,in aliquo loco extra quatuor angulos: aſpicies longitudinem eius ab aliquo horum angulorũ, 3 qui 406 DIFFERENTIA qui funt angulus medij cæli, atq angulus terræ: hoc est, vt aſpicies ſignificatorem, qui ſi fuerit inter aſcendens & medium cæli, minues aſcenſiones gradus medý cæli per circulum directum de aſcenſionibus gradus ſignificatoris. Et ſi fuerit inter VII. & medium cæli: minues aſcenſiones gradus ſignificatoris de aſcenſionibus gra. dus medij cæli per circulú directum:& quod remanſes rit,ex qualicunque horum locorum fuerit, illud diuides per partes horarum illius diei gradus in quo fuerit ſigni ficator:& quod exierit, erunt horæ longitudinis ab angulo. Et ſi fuerit inter aſcendens & angulum terræ: mis nues aſcenſiones gradus ſignificatoris per circulum dire Etum de aſcenſionibus gradus anguli terræ. Rurfus ſi fuerit ſignificator inter angulum terræ & VII. minues aſcenſiones gradus anguli terræ per circutum direclum de aſcenſionibus gradus ſignificatoris: & quod reman. ſerit ex qualicunque horum locorum, diuides per tempo id est, per ra horarum diei nadir:id est, oppoſiti gradus ſignificato tempora ris:& quod exierit, ipſæ erunt longitudinis horæ ab an horaria aſtaurna. gulo, gulo. Cung ſciueris longitudinis horas ab angulo, & volueris dirigerefignificatorem ad aliquem locum circuli ſignorum: & fuerit ſignificator in medio circuli orientalis, qui est à medio cæli vſque ad angulum terræ, de eis quæ ſuccedunt aſcendens : minue aſcenſios *nes gradus illius, in quo fuerit ſignificator de aſcenſioni bus gradus illius, ad quem volueris dirigere per circulū directum: & quod remanſerit, erit ſignificator circuli directi:ſerua cum. Post hoc minues aſcenſiones illius gra dus, QUARTA 407 dus, in quo fuerit ſignificator per aſcenſiones regiones de aſcenſionibus illius gradus, ad quem volueris eum dirigere:& quod remanſerit, erit ſignificator regionis: post hoc aſpice reſiduum, quod fuerit inter ſignificatorem circuli directi, & ſignificatorem regionis, accipiesá ſes xtam parte eius, & multiplicabis in horas longitudinis ab angulo, & quod fuerit, erit æquatio: & ſi fuerit ſignificator circali directi minor ſignificatore regionis:ad des æquationem ſuper ſignificatorem circuli directi. Et ſi fuerit ſignificator circuli direcliplus, minues æquatio nem ex eo: & quod remanſerit, erit gradus directionis. 3 Item ſi fuerit ſignificator, quem volueris dirigere, in medietate circuli occidentalis: quæ est ab angulo terræ, vſque ad medium cæli de eo quod ſuccedit VII. minues aſcenſiones nadir gradus ſignificatoris, quem volueris dirigere de aſcenſionibus nadir gradus illius,ad quem volueris dirigere eum, per circulum directum : & quod remanſerit, erit ſignificator circuli directi. Post hoc minues aſcenſiones nadir gradus ſignificatoris per aſcenſio nes regionis de aſcenſionibus nadir gradus illius, ad que volueris eum dirigere:& quod remanſerit, erit ſignifica tor regionis:deinde accipies reſiduum, quod fuerit inter ſignificatorem circuli directi, & ſignificatore regionis, accipiesá ſextam partem eius, & multiplicabis in horas longitudinis ab angulo: & quod fuerit, erit æquatio. Post hoc aſpicies ſignificatorem circuli directi, ſi fuerit minor ſignificatore regionis,addes æquationem ſuper ſo gnificatore circuli directi: & ſi fuerit ſignificator circus li dire-
translation: If, however, the significator you wish to direct is in some place outside the four angles: you will look at its longitude from one of these angles, which are the angle of the Midheaven and the angle of the Imum Coeli: that is, you will look at the significator, and if it is between the ascendant and the Midheaven, you will subtract the ascensions of the degree of the Midheaven by the direct circle from the ascensions of the significator's degree. And if it is between the seventh house and the Midheaven: you will subtract the ascensions of the significator's degree from the ascensions of the Midheaven's degree by the direct circle: and what remains, from whichever of these places it may be, you will divide by the parts of the hours of that day of the degree in which the significator is: and what comes out will be the hours of longitude from the angle. And if it is between the ascendant and the Imum Coeli: you will subtract the ascensions of the significator's degree by the direct circle from the ascensions of the degree of the Imum Coeli. Again, if the significator is between the Imum Coeli and the seventh house: you will subtract the ascensions of the degree of the Imum Coeli by the direct circle from the ascensions of the significator's degree: and what remains from whichever of these places, you will divide by the time, that is, by the hours of the day of the nadir, that is, the opposite of the significator's degree: and what comes out will be the hours of longitude from the angle. When you have known the hours of longitude from the angle, and you wish to direct the significator to some place of the circle of signs: and the significator is in the middle of the eastern circle, which is from the Midheaven to the Imum Coeli, of those that succeed the ascendant: subtract the ascensions of that degree in which the significator is from the ascensions of that degree to which you wish to direct it by the direct circle: and what remains will be the significator of the direct circle: keep it. After this, you will subtract the ascensions of that degree in which the significator is by the ascensions of the region from the ascensions of that degree to which you wish to direct it: and what remains will be the significator of the region: after this, look at the remainder, which is between the significator of the direct circle and the significator of the region, and you will take its sixth part, and multiply it by the hours of longitude from the angle, and what results will be the equation: and if the significator of the direct circle is less than the significator of the region: add the equation to the significator of the direct circle. And if the significator of the direct circle is greater, subtract the equation from it: and what remains will be the degree of direction.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Significator in Western Circle
text: QUARTA. 405 4 Quod attinet ad profectiones annorum mundi, quarume- Profetie pochæ magnis coniunctionibus fumuntur, earum latiorem auté, &c. expofitionem de induſtria omifimus, tum quod tota hec res no bis eſſet in vniuerfum ſuſpecta,atque propter caufas anteà di- &as plena abſurdorum errorum videatur: tum verò quòd per ſe ſit facilis,atque ex ijs quæ de profectionibus geniturarum diximus, fatis perſpicua, niſi quòd ſpeciem aliquam difficultatis ipſi adfert Alcabicius, dum annorum mūdi profectiones nõ ad annos Solares, ſed ad annos Perfarum accommodat. De directione ſignificatoris. A I Et ex hoc ſequitur atazir, ideſt,directio:hoc est vt dirigas ſignificatorem aliquem, ad aliquem locum ſignorum, & ſcias quid ſit inter eos ex gradibus directionis, accipiesá vnicuique gradui annum vnum. Cùm ergo volueris ſcire hoc, & fuerit ille ſignificator in afcendente, quem vis dirigere ad aliquam partem circuli, minue aſa cenſiones gradus in quo fuerit ſignificator quem volue ris dirigere, per aſcenſiones regionis de aſcëſionibus gra dus illius, ad quem volueris dirigere eū:& quod reman- ſerit, erit gradus directionis. Quòd ſi fuerit ſignificator in gradu ſeptimæ:minues aſcenſiones nadir, id est, oppo- ſiti gradus in quo eſt ſignificator, de aſcenſionibus nadir illius gradus ad quem volueris ipſum dirigere in illa regiõë. Si verò fuerit ſignificator in medio cæli vel in angu lo terræ: minues aſcenſiones gradus ſignificatoris de aſs ceſionibus illius gradus, ad que volueris eu dirigere per aſcenſiones circuli directi:& quod remanſerit, erit gra dus directionis. 2 Si verò fuerit ille ſignificator quem volueris dirigere,in aliquo loco extra quatuor angulos: aſpicies longitudinem eius ab aliquo horum angulorũ, 3 qui 406 DIFFERENTIA qui funt angulus medij cæli, atq angulus terræ: hoc est, vt aſpicies ſignificatorem, qui ſi fuerit inter aſcendens & medium cæli, minues aſcenſiones gradus medý cæli per circulum directum de aſcenſionibus gradus ſignificatoris. Et ſi fuerit inter VII. & medium cæli: minues aſcenſiones gradus ſignificatoris de aſcenſionibus gra. dus medij cæli per circulú directum:& quod remanſes rit,ex qualicunque horum locorum fuerit, illud diuides per partes horarum illius diei gradus in quo fuerit ſigni ficator:& quod exierit, erunt horæ longitudinis ab angulo. Et ſi fuerit inter aſcendens & angulum terræ: mis nues aſcenſiones gradus ſignificatoris per circulum dire Etum de aſcenſionibus gradus anguli terræ. Rurfus ſi fuerit ſignificator inter angulum terræ & VII. minues aſcenſiones gradus anguli terræ per circutum direclum de aſcenſionibus gradus ſignificatoris: & quod reman. ſerit ex qualicunque horum locorum, diuides per tempo id est, per ra horarum diei nadir:id est, oppoſiti gradus ſignificato tempora ris:& quod exierit, ipſæ erunt longitudinis horæ ab an horaria aſtaurna. gulo, gulo. Cung ſciueris longitudinis horas ab angulo, & volueris dirigerefignificatorem ad aliquem locum circuli ſignorum: & fuerit ſignificator in medio circuli orientalis, qui est à medio cæli vſque ad angulum terræ, de eis quæ ſuccedunt aſcendens : minue aſcenſios *nes gradus illius, in quo fuerit ſignificator de aſcenſioni bus gradus illius, ad quem volueris dirigere per circulū directum: & quod remanſerit, erit ſignificator circuli directi:ſerua cum. Post hoc minues aſcenſiones illius gra dus, QUARTA 407 dus, in quo fuerit ſignificator per aſcenſiones regiones de aſcenſionibus illius gradus, ad quem volueris eum dirigere:& quod remanſerit, erit ſignificator regionis: post hoc aſpice reſiduum, quod fuerit inter ſignificatorem circuli directi, & ſignificatorem regionis, accipiesá ſes xtam parte eius, & multiplicabis in horas longitudinis ab angulo, & quod fuerit, erit æquatio: & ſi fuerit ſignificator circali directi minor ſignificatore regionis:ad des æquationem ſuper ſignificatorem circuli directi. Et ſi fuerit ſignificator circuli direcliplus, minues æquatio nem ex eo: & quod remanſerit, erit gradus directionis. 3 Item ſi fuerit ſignificator, quem volueris dirigere, in medietate circuli occidentalis: quæ est ab angulo terræ, vſque ad medium cæli de eo quod ſuccedit VII. minues aſcenſiones nadir gradus ſignificatoris, quem volueris dirigere de aſcenſionibus nadir gradus illius,ad quem volueris dirigere eum, per circulum directum : & quod remanſerit, erit ſignificator circuli directi. Post hoc minues aſcenſiones nadir gradus ſignificatoris per aſcenſio nes regionis de aſcenſionibus nadir gradus illius, ad que volueris eum dirigere:& quod remanſerit, erit ſignifica tor regionis:deinde accipies reſiduum, quod fuerit inter ſignificatorem circuli directi, & ſignificatore regionis, accipiesá ſextam partem eius, & multiplicabis in horas longitudinis ab angulo: & quod fuerit, erit æquatio. Post hoc aſpicies ſignificatorem circuli directi, ſi fuerit minor ſignificatore regionis,addes æquationem ſuper ſo gnificatore circuli directi: & ſi fuerit ſignificator circus li dire-
translation: Likewise, if the significator you wish to direct is in the middle of the western circle: which is from the Imum Coeli to the Midheaven of what succeeds the seventh house: you will subtract the ascensions of the nadir of the significator's degree, which you wish to direct, from the ascensions of the nadir of that degree to which you wish to direct it, by the direct circle: and what remains will be the significator of the direct circle. After this, you will subtract the ascensions of the nadir of the significator's degree by the ascensions of the region from the ascensions of the nadir of that degree to which you wish to direct it: and what remains will be the significator of the region: then you will take the remainder, which is between the significator of the direct circle and the significator of the region, and you will take its sixth part, and multiply it by the hours of longitude from the angle: and what results will be the equation. After this, you will look at the significator of the direct circle, if it is less than the significator of the region, you will add the equation to the significator of the direct circle: and if the significator of the direct circle...

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Calculating Solar Arc to Setting
text: QUARTA. 415 ria tempora eiuſdem diurna par. 18. fcrupu.27.14.Occafus verò diſtat à medio cœli horis temporalibus ſex, quæ dutz in horaria tempora Solis diurna, proferunt horaria tempora part. 110. ſcrup.42 14, quibus à culmine diſtat locus Solis quando occidit. Aberat verò iuxta primum fitum verſus eandem partem temporum partibus 56.fcrupu. 57 14, quibus à diſtantia ſecunda, nempe à par. 110.fcrup.421 sublatis, relinquuntur tempora 53.fcr.45 18, quibus Sol fupra terram repertus in occafum fertur. Atque planè ijſdem temporibus ferè deſcendit arcus inter locum Solis & occaſum interceptus, in latitudine loci part.41. His ita fupputatis, conſidero radiationes inter locum Solis & occidentem cadentes, & reperio eas eſſe Luna, & Martis,* Solis,&* Veneris. Horum radiorú nullus ſuo occurfu Aphetam perimit, ſed quidam eorum Aphetæ robur adijciunt, quidam verò debilitant eundem. Sane quantum addit ſextilis Veneris, tantum adimere videtur Lunæ quadratus. Rurfus quantum adimit Martis oppoſitus, tantum adijcere videtur Solis ſextilis.Sed tamen vt huic ſupputationi in noſtro exemplo locus fit, ſupponamus relinqui ſolam diametralem radiationem Mar tis infra terram in par. 28. fcrup.21 1* Scorpijcommorantis, que Aphetæ ad partem occiduam directioni aliquos annos pofsit adimere. Quapropter vt quantum adimat ſcias, cape partes horarias loci Martis in parte 28. fcrup.21 14 Scorpij commorantis nocturnas, quæ fub eleuatione poli par.41.funtpar.16.fcrupu. 50 14, non hercle ob id,quia genitura nocturna fit, nocturna horaria tempora capimus: fed quia planeta, cuius funt radii, infra terram verſatur, qui ſi ſupra terram commoraretur, horaria tempora diurna nobis capienda fuiffent, quamuis genitura noturna fuiſſet. His ita perſpectis, quære deinceps, quot horis nocturnis diſtet Mars ab occidente, & inuenies horas decem ad vnguem ferè. Iam procede iuxta regulam proportionú hoc modo.Hor212.adimunt par.16.fcrup.50 14, quantum auferent horz 10?Duc numerum ſecundum in tertiu, hoc eſt, horas 10. multiplica in par. 16. fcrup.50 14,& produces fexage.2 14.p.48. fcrupu.2014,hoceſt, par.168.fcrup.20 14, quæ ſecta per nume rum primum, hoc eft, per 12.produnt par.14.fc. 2 14 ferè, à par tibus 53.fcrupu.45 14, quibus Solin occaſum fertur auferenda, 3 quo fa- } • 416 DIFFERENTIA quo fato, relinquuntur huius directonis part.39.fcrup.43 1. Hanc eſſe veram Ptolemzi de hac forma directionis ſententiam, intelligi poteſt ex ipfius verbis, quæ extant Apotelefmatico tertio, vbi de ſpacio vitæ dimetiendo agens, ſic ſcripfit: αναιρετικαή γίνονται μοίραι κατὰ μὲν τὼ εἰς τὰ προηγόμενα τ ζωδίωμ ἄφεσιμ ἢ το δυτικό ὁρίζοντο μόνα, 21ὰ τὸ ἀφανίζλυτόμ αύριομτζωῆς: αἱ δὲ τὴν ὅτως ἀπαντώντωμή μαρτυρόντωμ Thửa είρωμἀφαιρέσιμ μόνομἢ προςιθέασιμἔτη βῆς ὑπό το ἀφέτα συνα γομοίοις μέχρι τ' καταδύσεως τε ἀφέτα, καὶ ἐκ αὐαιρέσι, λλάτο μὲ αὐτὸς ἐπιφέρεοθαι τῷ ἀφελικό τόπῳ, ἀλλ' ἐκεῖνομοῖς αὐτήν. καὶ προς ιθέασιμ οι ἀγαθοποιοί, ἀφαιρῶσι δε οι κακοποιοί. ὁ δι ·§ πάλιμ ὁποτέροις αὐτῇ συληματιθῇ προςιθέμλνΘ. ὁ διαρίθα μός η προθέσεως ἢ ἀφαιρέσεως θεωρείται δια τ' καθέκαςομ μοιρος θεσίας, ὅοι γαρ ἄμὦσιμ ὡριαῖοι χρόνοι τ' εκάςο μοίρας, ἡμέρας μὲν ἔσως οι τ' ἡμέρας, νυκτός δι, οι τνυκτός, Τοσυμπλῆθε ἐτῶν ἔςαιό τέλλομ, ὅπως ἐκ τ' αναβλῆς αὐτὴν ὄντωμόλομλογις έομ, καὶ κατὰ το ανάλογομ ἀποχωρήσεως ὑφαιρετέομ,ἕως ἄμπρὸς τὰς δύσφς εἰς τὸ μηδέμ καταντήσῃ, hoc eſt: Vitæ moderationis, quæ per præcedentia figna perficitur, partes interemptoriæ nullæ fiunt, przter ſolam finientis occidentalis lineam, eò quòd dominum vitæ abſcondat.Porro partes ſiderum corpore aut per aſpectum ad hunc modum occurſantium, adimunt tantùm vel adijciunt annos ijs, qui ab apheta vſque ad occaſum labente colligebantur, & non interimunt,propterea quòd hæ non in locum Aphe ticum impingant, ſed potius ille in loca harum feratur.Ac adijciunt quidem benefici, malefici verò adimunt. Mercurius rur- ſus vt folet, adijcit robur ijs cum quibus cófiguratur. Numerus autem temporis adijciendus aut auferendus ex partiŭ vniuſcuiuſque ſitu colligitur. Etenim quot fuerint horaria tempora vniufcuiuſque partis, interdiu diurna, nocturna notu, tot fignificantur anni, ſedita, vtipfis in ortu commorantibus integer hic numerus ſeruetur, ac poftea ſecundum diſceſſus ab ortu proportionem taliter attenuetur, donec ad occaſum in nihilúabeat. Cùmia etymologia, definitioné & ſpecies directionú recenfuerimus, & in ſuper exéplo τώ τεἰς τὰ προκγύμβια ζώδια ἀφέσεως πραγματείαμ plana fecerimus, reliquum est vt modos, quibus loca Aphetica per ſigna cófequétia ducútur, aperiamus. Primus QUARTA 437 Primus & facilimus eſt per Spheram ſolidam, quem vide'in traGatibus de vſu Sphæræ folidæ. Secundus eſt per Aſtrolabum aut Planiſphærium magnæ amplitudinis, vbi exterior limbus in partes 3600.diſtribuitur. Tertius, & exactifsimus eſt, quem expoſuit in tabulis directionum, profe&ionumq; do&ifsimus vir Ioannes Regiomontanus. Quartus eſt autoris ex ſententia Ptolemai deriuatus. Quintus & antiquior modus eſt proprius Ptolemæo, qui alijs facilior & breuior eſt:nam Solis tabulis af cenſionum rectarum,&per tabulam quantitatis dierum omniad poteſt abſolui. Etſi verò viam dirigendi ſecundum do&rinam & tabulas Regiomontani plerique noſtri ſeculi Aftrologi probent: nobis tamen propter rationes ſuprà, cùm de copulationibus corporeis ageremus, dictas modus Ptolemei ſatisfa cit,atque fimpliciter iplius de arte præceptis, & ob id veritati magis confentire videtur. Nullum enim verbum in quadripartito deprehendere potuimus, quo diligentiſsimus atq; ſapientissimus mortalium Ptolemæusinfinuaſſe ſaltem nobis videri poſſet, in Apheticorum locorum dedu&ionibus, quibus ſola tempora euentuum definiuntur,ab ecliptica deuiationum ob-' ſeruatione eſſe vtilem. Cardanus quoq; maximus artifex, hanc antiquam rationem dirigendi à Ptolemæo traditam magis pro bare, illam verò exactamrationem Regiomontani, quam in pri ma ætate exercuerat, tandem in ſenio propter ſummam difficultatem & laborem operandi quaſiaſpernans negligit. Verba eius de hac re in commentarijs in lib.3.Ptol.hec funt:ExactifGmus modus dirigendi eſt per tabulas Ioannis de Regiomonte, quiætate noſtra laborem hunc illuſtriſsimum & difficillimum fufcepit: ſed ſi conficiendi difficultas parem laboribus vtilitatem afferret, certè omnino illis vti præſtaret. Et paulò infra vſum aptorum inſtrumentorum tabulis iſtis præfert. Stellæ, inquit, coaſcendere dicuntur, quotiefcunque fuerint in eodem ſemicirculo poſitionis: hoc autem ſi velis inuenire, per Planiſphæriam licebit, melius etiam per Sphæram ſolidam. Non veto quin per tabulas Ioannis Monteregij id peragas, & ſunt exactiores inſtrumentis, niſi inſtrumenta sint magna valde: Tamen vſus tabularum eſt valde tædioſus, laborioſus & peciculofus de errore, & maximè, quia Monteregius malè fecit in 8 hoc,
translation: The fourth part. 415. The diurnal temporal hours of the same are 18 parts, 27 scruples, 14 seconds. The setting, however, is distant from the midheaven by six temporal hours, which, when multiplied by the diurnal temporal hours of the Sun, produce 110 parts, 42 scruples, 14 seconds of temporal hours, by which the Sun's place is distant from the culmination when it sets. According to the first position, it was distant towards the same part of the times by 56 parts, 57 scruples, 14 seconds, which, when subtracted from the second distance, namely from 110 parts, 42 scruples, 14 seconds, leave 53 parts, 45 scruples, 18 seconds of time, during which the Sun, found above the earth, moves towards its setting. And in plainly the same times, the arc intercepted between the Sun's place and the setting descends almost, in the latitude of the place, 41 parts. Having thus calculated these, I consider the radiations falling between the Sun's place and the west, and I find them to be of the Moon, and Mars, the Sun, and Venus. None of these rays destroys the Apheta by its opposition, but some of them add strength to the Apheta, while others weaken it. Indeed, as much as the sextile of Venus adds, the square of the Moon seems to subtract. Again, as much as the opposition of Mars subtracts, the sextile of the Sun seems to add. But nevertheless, for this calculation to have a place in our example, let us suppose that only the diametral radiation of Mars remains below the earth, residing in 28 parts, 21 scruples, 1 second of Scorpio, which can subtract some years from the direction of the Apheta towards the western part. Therefore, to know how much it subtracts, take the nocturnal temporal parts of Mars's place residing in 28 parts, 21 scruples, 1 second of Scorpio, which under an elevation of the pole of 41 parts are 16 parts, 50 scruples, 14 seconds. Not, indeed, because the nativity is nocturnal do we take nocturnal temporal hours, but because the planet whose rays these are is below the earth. If it were above the earth, we would have to take diurnal temporal hours, even if the nativity were nocturnal. Having thus considered these, next inquire how many nocturnal hours Mars is distant from the west, and you will find almost exactly ten hours. Now proceed according to the rule of proportions in this manner: 12 hours subtract 16 parts, 50 scruples, 14 seconds; how much will 10 hours subtract? Multiply the second number by the third, that is, multiply 10 hours by 16 parts, 50 scruples, 14 seconds, and you will produce 62 parts, 48 scruples, 20 seconds, that is, 168 parts, 20 scruples, 14 seconds, which, divided by the first number, that is, by 12, yield approximately 14 parts, 2 scruples, 14 seconds, to be subtracted from 53 parts, 45 scruples, 14 seconds, by which the Sun moves towards its setting. 416. By which fact, there remain 39 parts, 43 scruples, 1 second of this direction. That this is Ptolemy's true opinion concerning this form of direction can be understood from his own words, which are found in the third Apotelesmatic, where, speaking of measuring the span of life, he wrote thus: "The destructive degrees in the moderation of life, which is perfected through the preceding signs, are none, except only the western limiting line, because it hides the lord of life. Moreover, the parts of the stars occurring in this manner, either corporeally or by aspect, only subtract or add years to those collected from the apheta up to its setting, and do not destroy, because these do not impinge upon the aphetic place, but rather that place moves into theirs. And the benefics add, but the malefics subtract. Mercury, again, as usual, adds strength to those with whom it is configured. The number of time to be added or subtracted is observed from the position of each part. For as many temporal hours as there are for each part, diurnal during the day, nocturnal during the night, so many years are signified, but in such a way that for those remaining at the rising, this entire number is preserved, and afterwards, according to the proportion of their departure from the rising, it is attenuated until it vanishes at the setting." Since we have reviewed the etymology, definition, and species of directions, and moreover, in an example, have made clear the treatment of the aphesis in the preceding signs, it remains for us to explain the methods by which the aphetic places are led through the consequent signs. The first and easiest is by the solid sphere, which you can see in treatises on the use of the solid sphere. The second is by an astrolabe or planisphere of great amplitude, where the outer limb is divided into 3600 parts. The third, and most exact, is that which the most learned man Johannes Regiomontanus expounded in his tables of directions and progressions. The fourth is the author's, derived from Ptolemy's opinion. The fifth and older method is proper to Ptolemy, which is easier and shorter than others: for everything can be accomplished by the Sun's tables of right ascensions and by the table of the quantity of days. And although most astrologers of our age approve the method of directing according to the doctrine and tables of Regiomontanus, nevertheless, for the reasons stated above when we were dealing with bodily conjunctions, Ptolemy's method satisfies us, and seems to agree more simply with his precepts of the art, and therefore with truth. For we could not find a single word in the Quadripartitum by which the most diligent and wisest of mortals, Ptolemy, could seem to us to have even insinuated that in the deductions of aphetic places, by which only the times of events are defined, the observation of deviations from the ecliptic is useful. Cardan also, a great master, preferred this ancient method of directing handed down by Ptolemy, and in his old age, almost disdaining and neglecting that exact method of Regiomontanus, which he had practiced in his youth, because of its extreme difficulty and labor. His words on this matter in his commentaries on Ptolemy's Book 3 are these: "The most exact method of directing is by the tables of Johannes de Regiomonte, who in our age undertook this most illustrious and difficult labor; but if the difficulty of execution brought equal utility to the labors, it would certainly be altogether preferable to use them." And a little further on, he prefers the use of suitable instruments to these tables. "Stars," he says, "are said to co-ascend whenever they are in the same semicircle of position; and if you wish to find this, it can be done by a planisphere, or even better by a solid sphere. I do not forbid you to do it by the tables of Johannes Monteregius, and they are more exact than instruments, unless the instruments are very large. However, the use of tables is very tedious, laborious, and prone to error, and especially because Monteregius did badly in this, 8.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Position and Horary Times
text: ==Start of OCR for page 4== QUARTA. 457 metraliter oppoſitum à M.C. remouetur, & hora nocturna loca infra terram æqualis eſt horæ diurnæ punai diametraliter oppoſiti ſupra terram. Porro quomodo horaria tempora Solis aliorumque locorum tam diurna quàm nocturna, facilè queant Fol.265 inueniri, ſuprà in fine differentiæ ſecundæ in capite de horis,abundè demonſtratum eft. Quòd fi quando de ſtella,an fupra vel infra terram commoretur, in occidentali ne,an verò orientali medietate verſetur, dubitaueris, de his accidentibus ex pun Ais, cum quibus ſtella cooritur vel condeſcendit, cœluma; mediat, facilimè fies certior. Si enim punctum cum quo ſtella oritur, fuerit in parte orientali, nondum tamen ortum fit, ſtellam quoque nondum ortam eſſe ſcias. Rurfus punctum cum quo ftella codeſcendit, ſi fuerit in parte occidentali, nondum tamé occiderit, ſtellam quoque nondum occubuiſſe certum eft.Pari. fermiter ex puncto cum quo ſtella cœlum mediat, ſtellam effe in medietate orientali vel occidentali ratiocinabimur:fi enim punctum cum quo ſtella meridianum tranfit, fupra terram fue rit, nondum tamen meridianum tranfierit, ipſam quoqueſtelt lam in parte orientali commorari fcias. Quòd ſi idem punctum infra terram fuerit, imum tamen cœli nondum tranfierit, ſtella quoque in medietate occidentali commorari colligemus. Hoc pacto ex habitudine punti, cum quo ſtella oritur, occidit,cœ lumque mediat, an ipſa ſtella ſupra vel infra terram fit,aut medietatem orientalem occidentalemúe teneat, an denique in ip fum finientem vel meridianum incidat, planè colligitur.Adex tremum dicta puncta, quum aſcenſionibus deſcenſionibuſque ftellarum correfpondeant, latere non poterunt.Sed modus in ueniendi pun&um, cum quo ſtella occidit, adhuc eſt alius & facilior.Si enim aſcenſioni oblique ipfius ſtelle arcum diurnum eius adijcias, prodibit aſcenfio obliqua punti oriétis dum ſtel la occidit: quo cognito punctus quoque ei diametraliter oppofitus, cum quo propoſitam ſtellam occidere neceffe eft, ne quaquam latebit. His ita preparatis, Aphetam feu fignificatorem perinde ac fi in cufpidibus exifteret, bis ad promiſſorem dedu cito. Ac primò quidem per afcenſiones aut deſcéfiones ſphæræ, colliges directionis arcum, quem autor hic ſignificatorem cir culi directinominat, Secúdò per aſcenfiones vel deſcenſiones clima- 83 Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 4== ==Start of OCR for page 5== 412 DIFFEREΝΤΙΑ climatis ſupputa directionis arcum ſecundum, quem autorfignificatorem regionis vocat: quo facto ſubtrahe minorem arcum dire&ionis à maiori, & id quod reſtat per 6. (nam vnus qua dras, vt ex ſupradictis liquet, 6. horas temporales continet)par Liaris, atque per eandem ſextam partem quæ hinc exit, multipli ca per numerum horarum, quibusAphetam ſen ſignificatorem A meridiano diſtare priùs ſupputatum eft: id quod ex huiufmodi multiplicatione gignitur, vocat autor equationem, que quidem addenda eſt directioni ſphæra, ſi eadem directione climazis minor fuerit, aut fubtrahenda ab eadem, ti contrarium acciderit, nam numerus reſtans vel compoſitus quæfita tempora dire&ionis oftendet. Eft autem per aſcenſiones operandum, quando Apheta in medietate aſcendente fuerit: per defcenfiones verò, aut per punctorum oppofitorum afcenfiones, quando idem ſignificator medietatem deſcendentem tenuerit. Summa itaque huius loci eſt: ſi Apheta fit ſupra terram,capiendam eſſe differentiam aſcenſionis recte à C. M. eandemq́s per horaria tempora diurna partiendam: ſi autem infra terram fuerit, accipiendam eſſe differentiam aſcenſionis rectæ ab imo cæli, eandemq; per horaria tempora nocturna apheta partiendam, vt horæ temporales, quibus Apheta à culmine cœli diſtat, exeant.Si igitur apheta fuerit inter M.C.& afcendens,exibunt horæ temporales diſtantiæ ſequentis: Si autem inter C. M. & Cardinem occidentalem, diſtantiæ antecedentis, pariformiter fiapheta fuerit inter horoscopu & imum cæli, prodibunt hora diſtantiæ antecedentis, quæ per omnia æqualis eſt diſtantiz diurnæ antecedentis puncti oppoſiti in hæmiſphærio ſuperiore. Denique ſi inter imum cœli & finitorem occidentalem fuexit, exurgent horæ diſtantiæ ſequentis, pariformiterque per omnia æqualis diſtantiz ſequenti in ſuperiore hemifphzrio & in puncto diametraliter oppoſito, quia quartæ cœli oppoſitz omninò fibicorreſpondent.Cognita tali diſtantia, mox differentia directionum duarum eo modo,quem expoſuimus, duplici operatione, ſphære videlicet & climatis, colligenda, cademque fic collecta, per ſex ſecanda eft, vt numerus προπαφαι ρίσεως, id eft, qui dire&ioni ſphere iungendus aut adimendus fit, exeat. Exemplum de ſignificatore in medietate afcendente, &in. Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 5==
translation: The diametrically opposite point is removed from the Midheaven, and the nocturnal hour below the earth is equal to the diurnal hour of the diametrically opposite point above the earth. Furthermore, how the horary times of the Sun and other places, both diurnal and nocturnal, can easily be found, has been abundantly demonstrated above at the end of the second difference in the chapter on hours. If you ever doubt whether a star is above or below the earth, or whether it is in the western or eastern half, you will most easily be certain of these occurrences from the points with which the star rises or descends, and with which it culminates. For if the point with which the star rises is in the eastern part, but has not yet risen, know that the star has not yet risen. Again, if the point with which the star descends is in the western part, but has not yet set, it is certain that the star has not yet set. Similarly, from the point with which the star culminates, we will deduce whether the star is in the eastern or western half: for if the point with which the star crosses the meridian is above the earth, but has not yet crossed the meridian, know that the star itself is in the eastern part. But if the same point is below the earth, but has not yet crossed the Imum Coeli, we will conclude that the star is also in the western half. In this way, from the position of the point with which the star rises, sets, and culminates, it is clearly deduced whether the star itself is above or below the earth, or occupies the eastern or western half, or finally falls on the ascendant/descendant or meridian. Finally, the said points, since they correspond to the ascensions and descensions of the stars, cannot be hidden. But there is still another and easier way to find the point with which a star sets. For if you add its diurnal arc to the oblique ascension of the star itself, the oblique ascension of the rising point will emerge while the star sets: once this is known, the point diametrically opposite to it, with which the proposed star must set, will by no means be hidden. With these preparations, bring the Apheta or significator, as if it were on the cusps, twice to the promittor. And first, by the ascensions or descensions of the sphere, you will collect the arc of direction, which the author here calls the significator of the direct circle. Secondly, by the ascensions or descensions of the climate, calculate the second arc of direction, which the author calls the significator of the region: having done this, subtract the smaller arc of direction from the larger, and divide the remainder by 6 (for one quadrant, as is clear from the above, contains 6 temporal hours), and multiply the resulting sixth part by the number of hours by which the Apheta or significator was previously calculated to be distant from the meridian: what is generated from this multiplication the author calls the equation, which is to be added to the direction of the sphere if the direction of the climate was smaller, or subtracted from it if the opposite occurred, for the remaining or composite number will show the sought-after times of direction. And one must work by ascensions when the Apheta is in the ascending half: but by descensions, or by the ascensions of opposite points, when the same significator holds the descending half. The sum of this passage is therefore: if the Apheta is above the earth, the difference of the right ascension from the Midheaven must be taken, and divided by the diurnal horary times: but if it is below the earth, the difference of the right ascension from the Imum Coeli must be taken, and divided by the nocturnal horary times of the Apheta, so that the temporal hours by which the Apheta is distant from the culmination of the heavens emerge. Therefore, if the Apheta is between the Midheaven and the Ascendant, the temporal hours of the subsequent distance will emerge: but if between the Midheaven and the western Cardine, of the preceding distance, similarly if the Apheta is between the horoscope and the Imum Coeli, the hours of the preceding distance will emerge, which is in all respects equal to the diurnal preceding distance of the opposite point in the upper hemisphere. Finally, if it is between the Imum Coeli and the western ascendant/descendant, the hours of the subsequent distance will emerge, and similarly in all respects equal to the subsequent distance in the upper hemisphere and in the diametrically opposite point, because opposite quadrants of the heavens correspond entirely to each other. Once such a distance is known, immediately the difference of the two directions, collected in the manner we have explained, by a double operation, namely of the sphere and of the climate, and thus collected, must be divided by six, so that the number of the προπαφαιρίσεως, that is, which is to be added to or subtracted from the direction of the sphere, emerges. An example of the significator in the ascending half, and in.

Pars annorum accipitur in die à domino dos mus Solis in Saturnum:& in nocte econuerfo: & proiicitur ab afcendente.Et si fuerit in domo ſua Sol, vel aliqua domorum Saturni :accipe in die à Sole in Saturnu: in nocte econuer ſo, & proiice ab afcendente, & non confideres, vtrum Saturnus sit apparens vel sub radiis. Quòd si Sol fuerit in gradu exaltationis fua in die, et Luna in nočle in gradu exaltationis fue, erit pars in gradu afcendentis, et erit signisicatio gradus afcendentis. Pars finis rerum accipitur in f die et nocte à Saturno vſque ad dominum domus coniunctionis, aut peruentionis, et additur deſuper gradus afcendentis, et proiicitur ab aſcendente. translation: FIFTH DIFFERENCE in the commemoration of all parts. And since we have completed the explanations of those things that follow from the names of the astrologers, let us now execute the commemoration of all parts. And when we have noted in the place of the parts: take from that place to that place, project this from the ascendant or from another place. We say that you add to that which was between both places the degrees of the ascendant, and project from the ascendant or from the beginning of the sign from which you project. An example of this is the operation of the Part of Fortune: because you take whatever is between the Sun and the Moon in degrees: you add to these the degrees of the ascendant sign: and what is collected, you project it from 30 to 30 degrees, and you begin to project from the beginning of the ascendant sign. Where the number ends, there is the Part of Fortune: and at night, conversely, from the Moon to the Sun: and you add the degrees above the ascendant sign, and project this from 30 to 30 degrees. Begin to project from the beginning of the ascendant sign. Let us begin here, therefore, after the Part of Fortune, the parts of the 12 houses, and let us begin from the ascendant. The First House is the Part of Life, and it is taken by day from Jupiter to Saturn: and at night conversely, and it is projected from the ascendant. The Part of Hyleg is taken from the degree of conjunction or prevention, which was before the nativity up to the degree of the Moon: and it is projected from the ascendant. The Part of Future Events, that is, the Part of the Sun, is taken by day from the Moon to the Sun: and at night conversely, and it is projected from the ascendant. The Part of Love and Concord, that is, the Part of Venus: is taken by day from the Part of Fortune to the Part of Future Events: and at night conversely, and it is projected from the ascendant. The Part of Stability and Trust of the duration of the native's ascendant, is like the Part of Concord and Love. The Part of Courage and Audacity, that is, the Part of Mars, is taken by day from Mars to the degree of the Part of Fortune and at night conversely, and it is projected from the ascendant. The Second House is the Part of Substance: and it is taken by day and night from the lord of the house of substance, to the degree and minute of the house of substance, and it is projected from the ascendant. The Part of Poverty and Smallness of Ingenuity, that is, the Part of Mercury, is taken by day from the Part of Future Events to the Part of Fortune: and at night conversely, and it is projected from the ascendant. The Part of Beatitude and Triumph and Victory, that is, the Part of Jupiter, is taken by day from the Part of Future Events to Jupiter, at night conversely, and is projected from the ascendant. The Third House is the Part of Brothers: and it is taken by day from Saturn to Jupiter: and at night conversely, and it is projected from the ascendant. The Part of the Benevolence of Brothers is taken by day from the Sun to Saturn and at night conversely, and is projected from the ascendant: but if Saturn is under the Sun's rays: it is taken by day from the Sun to Jupiter, and at night conversely, and is projected from the ascendant. The Fourth House is the Part of Fathers, it is taken by day from the Sun to Saturn: and at night conversely: and it is projected from the ascendant. The Part of the Father's Death is taken by day from Saturn to Jupiter. And at night conversely: and it is projected from the ascendant. The Part of Years is taken by day from the lord of the house of the Sun to Saturn: and at night conversely: and it is projected from the ascendant. And if the Sun is in its own house, or in any of Saturn's houses: take by day from the Sun to Saturn: at night conversely, and project from the ascendant, and do not consider whether Saturn is visible or under the Sun's rays. The Part of Inheritances and Possessions is taken by day and night from Saturn to the Moon: and is projected from the ascendant. The Part of the Nobility of the Native, and of him about whom there is doubt, whether he is the son of that father to whom he is attributed, or of another: is taken by day from the degree of the Sun to the degree of its exaltation: and is projected from the ascendant. But if the Sun is in the degree of its exaltation by day, and the Moon at night in the degree of its exaltation, the part will be in the degree of the ascendant, and it will be the signification of the degree of the ascendant. The Part of the End of Things is taken by day and night from Saturn up to the lord of the house of conjunction, or prevention, and the degrees of the ascendant are added above it, and it is projected from the ascendant. The Fifth House is the Part of Children and is taken by day from Jupiter to Saturn, at night conversely, and is projected from the ascendant. The Part which signifies the time, in which a child should be born, and their number, whether male or female, and what the woman will bear, is taken by day and night from Mars to Jupiter and is projected from the ascendant. The Part of the Knowledge of a Male and Female Child is taken by day and night from the Moon to Jupiter, and is projected from the ascendant. The Part by which the native, about whom the question is asked, is known, whether male or female, is taken by day from the lord of the house of the Moon to the Moon: and at night conversely, and is projected from the ascendant. The Part signifying the child when Jupiter reaches it, that is, to the degree of the part signifying the child, is taken by day and night from Mars to Jupiter, and is projected from the ascendant. The Part of Love and Joy is taken by day and night from Venus to Saturn, and is projected from the ascendant. The Part of the Knowledge of the Existence of Children is taken by day and night from the Moon to Venus, and is projected from the ascendant. The Sixth House is the Part of Infirmity and Azemena, that is, inseparable weakness of some member: it is taken by day from Saturn to Mars, at night conversely, and is projected from the ascendant. The Part of Servants is taken by day from Mercury to the Moon, and at night conversely, and is projected from the ascendant. And Alendez said, it is taken by day from Mercury to the Part of Fortune: and at night conversely. And he said: it is necessary that we use both parts together. The Seventh House is the Part of the Betrothal of Men or Marriage: and it is taken by day and night from the Sun to Venus, and is projected from the ascendant. And this part is similar to the father of rumored commands. And the part of the betrothal of women or marriage according to Hermes is taken by day and night from Venus to Saturn, and is projected from the ascendant. And this part corresponds to the father of the cultivation of the earth. Likewise, the part of the betrothal of women according to Velius, is taken by day and night from the Sun to Mars, and is projected from the ascendant. The Part of Love and Pleasure is taken by day and night from Venus to the degree of the seventh, and is projected from the ascendant. The Part of Marriages is taken by day and night from Saturn to Venus, and is projected from the ascendant.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Moon's Application to Planets
translation: The Moon applies to a planet when it is in the same sign as that planet, or in an adjacent sign, and is moving towards a conjunction, opposition, or square aspect with it. This application is considered stronger when the Moon is in a cardinal sign, and weaker in a fixed sign. The application is also stronger when the Moon is in a sign of its own nature, such as a watery sign for the Moon, or an airy sign for Jupiter. The application is further strengthened when the Moon is in a sign where it has dignity, such as exaltation or triplicity. Conversely, the application is weakened when the Moon is in a sign of its detriment or fall. The application is also considered stronger when the Moon is swift in motion, and weaker when it is slow. Finally, the application is stronger when the Moon is free from combustion, and weaker when it is combust.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Dedication to the City Council of Cologne
text: ENARRATIO ELEMENTORVM ASTROLOGIAE, IN QVA PRAETER ALCABICII, QVI ARA bum doctrinam compendio prodidit, expofitionem,atq; cum Ptolemæi principijs collationem, reiectis fortilegijs & abfurdis vulgoq; receptis opinionibus, de vera artis præceptorum origine & vſu ſatis differitur in celeberrima Colonienfi Academia ſtudiofis philo- ſophiæ propofita A Valentino Nabod, ampliſsimi Senatus Colonienfis Mathematicarum ordinario. τέχνη τύχωμ ἔςερξε καὶ τέχνωμ τύχη.
