D. 1 OMINES inter tot animantia folos diuinitus diuinis.animatos mētibus in mediu vniuerfitatis globum, queterra dicitur, ideo effe collocatos rectèamagnis hominibus proditum eße opinor, amplißimi conſultißimiq; viri, vt inde ad omnia extremis mūdi mænijs concluſa oculos conuertentes, variarum naturarum, quas fingulæ partes mundi continent, diligenti perfcrutatione ſapientiam atq; bonitate Dei agnofcerent, atq; res ad rectè beateq; viuendum vtiles eligerent. Etenim nihil in rerum natura adeò tenue est aut abiectum, quod innatis viribus infitisq; à natura officijs careat, que altiùs introspecta, non folùm diuinam rerum humanarum curam in bonorum animis confirmant, verùm etiam ſua præſidia vitæ adferunt. Quid terræ plantis & ſtirpibus vulgò habetur viliſ- us?adeo enim communes atque vbique ad manus funt, vt vel à nolentibus pedibus conculcentur. At harum virtus atque cum noſtris corporibus συμπάθſa conciliatioque 4 42 1 *Digitized by Google : EPISTOLA • & ἀντιπραξία, postquam à naturæ ſtudioſis & peritis hominibus mediocriter cognita & exculta eft, pulcherrimam atque generi humano vtilißimam artem medendi, qua potißimùm fragili mortalium vitæ atque faluti conſulitur, peperit: Cuius præſidio in hominum corporibus temperatura humorum æquabilis vitæq; conueniens conferuatur, morbi verò atroces & ſauißimè graßantes ita manifeſtè profligantur, vt herbarum alias ad certa membra corporis fouenda conferuandaq;, alias verò ad certos morbos depellendos, diuinitus ordinatas atq; hominibus diuinitus monſtratas effe pudeat non credere. Tales & ſimiles virtutes alijs quoque naturæ rebus omnibus inſunt, quas ſi homines propius aſpicere & intelligere poßent, haud dubiè ad beatam vitam proximè accederent. Etfi verò in fingulis partibus mundi longè plures contineantur naturæ, quam ut alicuius intellectu in vniuerſum comprehenſæ, definitis generibus & formis concludi poßint, cùm ne vnius quidem rerum generis integram vim homines perfectè capiant : tamen ea ipſa que labore & diligentia cognofci poſſunt, quæque à multis claris ingenys comprehenſa, atque artificioſa methodo proditafunt, magnum aditum ad virtutem & agnitionem diuinæ bonitatis atque ſapientiæ patefaciant,& manifeſtum vſum in tota vita habeant, Deum contumelia afficiunt, & ſocietati humanæ nocent mon- ſtroſi homines, qui artes à maioribus admirabili & planè diuina ſolertia deprehenſas atque poſteritati traditas contemnunt, ſtudiumque vlterius conſiderandæ naturæ: Digitized by Google
translation: ENARRATIO ELEMENTORVM ASTROLOGIAE, IN WHICH, BESIDES ALCABITIUS, WHO PRODUCED THE ARABIC DOCTRINE IN A COMPENDIUM, AN EXPOSITION, AND A COLLATION WITH PTOLEMY'S PRINCIPLES, REJECTING FORTUNE-TELLING AND ABSURD AND COMMONLY ACCEPTED OPINIONS, THE TRUE ORIGIN AND USE OF THE ART'S PRECEPTS ARE SUFFICIENTLY DISCUSSED IN THE MOST CELEBRATED COLOGNE ACADEMY FOR STUDENTS OF PHILOSOPHY, PROPOSED BY VALENTINE NABOD, ORDINARY PROFESSOR OF MATHEMATICS TO THE MOST AMPLE SENATE OF COLOGNE. ART LOVES FORTUNE AND FORTUNE LOVES ART. ARNOLD BIRCKMAN COLOGNE, At the heirs of Arnold Birckmann. | M. D. LX. With the Grace and Privilege of His Imperial Majesty. Digitized by Google Digitized by Google: TO THE MOST SPLENDID AND ORNAMENTED MEN, SIR ARNOLD A SIEGEN, KNIGHT, SIR HERMANN SUDERMAN, SIR JOHN PIEL, SIR CONSTANTINE A LYSKIRCHEN, SIR GODFREY HITTORPFF, SIR PHILIP GEIL, OFTEN AND AGAIN CONSULS AND MOST VIGILANT RESPECTIVE PROVOSTS OF THE RENOWNED UNIVERSITY OF COLOGNE, AND TO THE MOST AMPLE SENATE OF THE SAME FLOURISHING REPUBLIC, HIS REVERED LORDS, VALENTINE NABOD OF SILESIA SENDS GREETINGS. I believe, most ample and most prudent men, that it has been rightly handed down by great men that among all living beings, only humans, divinely animated by divine minds, are placed in the middle of the universal globe, which is called earth, so that from there, turning their eyes to all things enclosed by the outermost boundaries of the world, they might recognize the wisdom and goodness of God through diligent scrutiny of the various natures that each part of the world contains, and might choose things useful for living rightly and blessedly. For nothing in the nature of things is so subtle or abject that it lacks innate powers and duties implanted by nature, which, when more deeply examined, not only confirm divine care for human affairs in the minds of the good, but also bring their own aids to life. What is commonly held as more vile than plants and roots of the earth? For they are so common and everywhere at hand that they are trampled underfoot even by those unwilling. But their virtue and the sympathy and reconciliation with our bodies, after being moderately known and cultivated by diligent and skilled men of nature, gave birth to the most beautiful and most useful art of healing for the human race, by which the fragile life and health of mortals are especially preserved: By its aid, in human bodies, the equitable temperament of humors suitable for life is preserved, and indeed atrocious and most severely raging diseases are so manifestly overcome that one should not be ashamed to believe that some herbs were divinely ordained and divinely shown to humans for nourishing and preserving certain body parts, and others for expelling certain diseases. Such and similar virtues are also inherent in all other things of nature, which if men could observe and understand more closely, they would undoubtedly approach a blessed life. And although far more natures are contained in individual parts of the world than can be comprehended by anyone's intellect as a whole, and confined to definite genera and forms, since men do not even perfectly grasp the entire power of one kind of thing: nevertheless, those very things that can be known by labor and diligence, and which have been comprehended by many brilliant minds and handed down by an artful method, open a great path to virtue and the recognition of divine goodness and wisdom, and have a manifest use in all of life. Monstrous men, who despise the arts discovered by our ancestors with admirable and truly divine ingenuity and handed down to posterity, and the study of further contemplating nature, insult God and harm human society.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Astrology's Medical and Financial Utility
text: 1 EPISTOLA fariam præcepiffet, ſtatim hac verba fubijcit: Soxior τις ταῦτα μετεωρολογια εἶναι, εί με τις αίμ το γνώμης, μάθοι ἄμότε ἐκ ἐλάχιςομ μέρα συμβάλλεται αςρονομία εἰς ἰατρικέμ, αλλά πανύπλείςομ, id eft, quòd fi quis fublimiora hac effe opi natur, atque ab hac fententia diffentiat, is ſciat, astrono miam non parum, fed omnino plurimum conferre ad ar tem medicam. Hac etſi Medici de generalibus circumfuſicali conftitutionibus principaliter dici putent, vt quibus locis, Italiæ ne potius,an verò Germania vel Gal lia,& cuius conditionis hominibus, viris ne magis an mulieribus, biliofis ne grauius an pituitofis pericula mor borum metuenda fint preuideatur:ad caufas verò fingu lares imbecillioresque, quamuis ad afficienda corpora, quibus propriè inherent, fatis efficaces rarò referre fo leant:cùm tamen inter generalia & fingularia, quod ad hac vim attinet, nihil interfit,eadé ratione in particula ribus vniufcuiusque conſtitutionibus rectè & vtiliter obferuantur.Sicut enim communes ness Solis ardores minùs fentiunt, qui in fubterraneis locis latent, vel alias res frigefacientes opponunt, eodem modo immoderatos calo res, qui peculiariter in fingulorum corporibus ex vniuf cuiusque propria causa exiftunt, contrarijs remedijs mi tigari haud eft dubium. Poftremò bec malos cali natu rales effectus amoliēdi diligentia, vt publicas comunesq; afflictiones, quales à defectibus luminum deriuatur, iam planè filentio tranfeam, in inopinatis quoque humanis. cafibus euitandis leniendiſque non parum momenti ha bere videtur.Nam fi cafus ab alto, aut periculu ab aquis, aut vulnera DEDICATORIA wut vulnera prouiſa ſint,loca alta, cõtetiones, nauigia Aumina aquarū vitantes incolumes euadere poterimus. Quod verò ad opes, gloria, amicost&talia hui generis bo na attinet, eori, vt rectè iudicat Ptolemeus,etiaſi per ſe nõ fit largitrix aftrologia, ficut nec vlla alia philofophiæ pars, dū tamen futurarū rerum copia aut inopiam pra. uidere docet, in rebus aconomicis & politicis ad queſtu faciēdum, reliquis omnibus meritò vtilior cenferi poteft liberalior: fiquidem hac in re animi virtutis pluri. mum, corporis verò vfum minimum deſiderat. Ferunt, fcribitPlinius, Democritum, qui primus intellexit often ditque cum terris cali focietatem, fpernentibus hanc cu ram eius opulentißimis ciuibus, previſa olei caritate ex futuro certa ratione Vergiliarum exortu, magna vili. tate tune propter fpem oliua, coëmiße in toto tractu oma ne oleum, mirantibus qui paupertatem is quietem doEtrinarum eifciebant in primis cordi cordieße. eße. Atque vt ap paruit, cauſa ingens diuitiarum curſus, restituiße mercedem anxie & auide dominorum pænitentia,con tentus ita probaße, opes fibi (cùm vellet) in facili fore. Eiufde rationis pecuniæ faciende Thale Milesiu, profpe Ela olearū copia infigne olim ſpecime edidiße refert Ari ſtoteles, cuius verba hic adfcribere non piguit, vt ipfius autoritas huic comemoratióni aliquid põderis adýcéret. ἔτι δικους τὰ λεγόμενα αποράδωμ, δ᾽ ὦμ ἐπιτπτυχήκασιμ ἔνιοι χρή μαλιζόμλνοι, δεῖ συλλέγομ. παίτα γαρ ὠφέλιμα ταῦτα ἐπὶ βῖς Για μῶσι τὸ χρημαλιςικώμ. διομ καὶ τὸ θάλεω το μιλησία. το γαρ όξι κατανόημα τι χρηματικόμι ἀλλ᾽ ἐκείνῳ μὲν λλὰ τὸ ἀφίαμ προσά β 2 • EPISTOLA πήγσι, τυγχάνη δι καθόλα Τι ὄμ. ὀναδιζόντωμ γαρ αὐτῷ τὰ τιώ πες νίαμὡς αἰωφελός τ' φιλοφίας ἔσης, κατανομουντά φασιμαὐτόμ ἐλαιῶμ φοράμ ἐσμάν ἐκ τ' ἀφρολογίας, ἔτι χειμῶν ὄντο, θυπερήφαντα χρημάτωμ ὀλίγωμ αρραβῶνας δια δῦναι τὴν ἐλαιορα γίωμ τῶν τε οὐ μιλάτῳκὴ χίῳ παίτωμ, ὀλίγο μιθωσάμθνομ, ἀτ' ἐθενὸς ἐπιβάλλοντα. ἐπιδὴ δ' ὁ καιρὸς ἦκε πολλῶμ (πτομάωμ ἅμα καὶ ἐξαίφνης, ἐκμιθέντα όμ τρόπομ ἐβόλει πολλά χρήματα συλλέγαντο ἐπιδεῖξαι ότι ράδιομ βὶ πλοτῶν δῖς φιλοσόφοις ἄμβόλωνται, ἀλλ᾿ ὁ τετ' ἐὶ τοὶ ὁ αποδάζωσι, boc eft: Dea inceps paßim recitatas rationes, quibus nonnulli fibi rem fecerunt, colligere debemus: qua quidem in re ijs po tißimùm proderimus, apud quos in honore & in pre tio eft pecunia faciende modus. Ac buius generis eft illud Thalis Mileſij, quod inſtar cuiufdam parandarum opum inuentionis haberi poteft. Et quanquàm id foli illi ob fummam fapiëtiam attribuitur, generale tamen faci enda pecunia praceptum existit. Cum enim ob pauper. tatem ſtudium philoſophie ei tanquàm inutile obijcere tur, &ipfe ex Aftrologia futuram olearum vbertatem animaduertißet, tempore hyberno, antequam florere ca. pißent, paruo pretio, quòd tunc nemo pluris liceretur, atque faltem datis arris, cùm magna pecuniam in prom. ptu non haberet, omnem oliua in Mileſio & Chio agro ſpe coëmiße ferunt:vt verò tempus apparuit, fubitò multis emptoribus fimul concurretibus, tanti, quanti voluit, vendidiße:atque ita magna vi pecuniarum collecta ofte. diße facilè fi velint, pecuniam poße facere Philoſophos, ſed in ea re ſtudia corum non verfari. Tot DEDICATORIA. Tot tantisq; & multis alys rationibus, quas perfequi nimis longum eſſet, cùm hæc ſcientia hominibus prodef ſe, atque à nulla planè re, quod plurimi tentarut, iure re prehendi queat, grato pectore amplectenda, & non vul garis doni Dei inftar tuenda eft. Sed dicat aliquis: Si aftrorum motui eterno atque lege inuiolabili, vt aßeris, procedente, rerum euentus aßignandi funt, duorum ab. furdorum alterum, & vnum tertium cum vtrouis crede. re oportebit. Aut enim de fententia Stoicorum Deo vin cula inijcienda erunt, que eum calo alijsq; caufis inferio ribus ita connectant, vt non liberènec aliter quam ficut ab illis ducitur, agere poßit:aut certè ſic ſtatuendu, De. um, postquam ſemel omnium naturalium caufarum ferie conftituit atq; ordinauit, jfdema omnes futuros vnqua rerum exitus vim a perfectam ſummamque perficiendi po testatem tribuit, ad perpetuum feceßiße otium, perinde vt architectus, abſoluta naui, vacuus curis fecedit, eandema in pofterum naucleris curandam gubernandamá relinquit,id quod Epicureorum fententice affine est.De. nique ſi vtrumuis horum alterum tanquam verű recipia mus, fiunulalia maior admittenda erit abſurditas, qua fi ftellas quouis modo malas inclinationes vitiaque in ho minibus facere credamus, Deŭ, qui ſtellas condidit,cau Jam talium malorum abſurdè dicere compellimur. Hac vulgo Gincautioribus plauſibiliter dici videri poßunt, fed mediocriter exercitatis omnium membrorum vitiu est perfpicuum. Ac primùm per aßiduam conuerfionem atque neceßariam fiderum actione, nec libertatem agen di Deo β 3 1. 1 EPISTOLA : di Deo adimi, nec prouidentiam Dei, qua nature condi. te aßidud adest, tolli, fed vtrumq; potiùs aßeuerari, inq; mentibus ſanorum hominum confirmari, fatis conftare - potest ex iis, que Phyſici οπίταἰαγκής, & Ptolemeus τποεί η θείας καὶ φυσικῆς εἰμαρμαίης prodiderunt. Sanè que de eßentia aterna, deq; virtutibus Dei pronunciari pof funt,omninò atq; abſolutè, vt loquuntur, neceßaria funt, neq; vllo tempore contradictionis implicationem admit tunt. Nam cùm Deus ex ſeſe & à nulla præcedenti cauSa procedat, idcirco fimpliciter atque abſq; vlla contradictionis imaginatione omnipotens, intelligens, benefi cus, iuftus, liberè agens, &c.per omne eternitatem dici meretur. Rerum verò ſequentium, que à prima caufa ef fuſe ſunt, atq; aliquando eſſe cœperunt, alia eft conditio, longè minor conftantia, & deniq; connexio langui. dior:fiquidem vis agendi cunctis mundi corporibus in fita, nec fine Deo nature conditore conferuatore per. petua eſſe potest, nec ex ſeſe ſemel fufceptam action vnquam interrumpere. Celestia corpora, verbi gratia, ficut nec equalibus statisque temporibus periodos con ficere, nec ſempiternas leges motuum obferuare poffent, nifi ipforum vis vis diuina dinina potentia potentia & & bonitate bonitate ſemper incolumis & integra conferuaretur: ita rurfus fatali neceßitate conftanter circumducta, ingenitum fuum ſeu potiùs diuinitus infitum curſum non ex ſeſe, neque ad ho minum vlliusúe alterius creaturæ voluntatem, fed folùm diuino mādato inhibere aut immutare poffunt. Sic edax ignis perpetua vim adurědi nõ ex fefe,fed Dei beneficio. retinet,
translation: If anyone thinks these things are too sublime, and disagrees with this opinion, let him know that astronomy contributes not a little, but indeed very much, to the medical art. Although physicians may primarily think these things refer to general circumstances and constitutions, such as in which places, Italy rather than Germany or Gaul, and for what condition of people, men more than women, or bilious more than phlegmatic, dangers of diseases are to be foreseen: they rarely apply them to singular and weaker causes, even though these are sufficiently effective in affecting bodies to which they properly adhere. However, since there is no difference between general and singular in terms of this power, they are rightly and usefully observed in the particular constitutions of each individual. For just as those who hide in subterranean places or apply other cooling things feel less the common heat of the Sun, so too it is doubtless that immoderate heats, which arise particularly in the bodies of individuals from each one's own cause, can be mitigated by contrary remedies. Finally, this diligence in removing bad natural effects of the heavens, to pass over in silence public and common afflictions, such as those derived from defects of the luminaries, also seems to have no small importance in avoiding and alleviating unexpected human misfortunes. For if a fall from a height, or danger from waters, or wounds are foreseen, by avoiding high places, contentions, ships, and rivers, we will be able to escape unharmed. As for wealth, glory, friends, and goods of this kind, although astrology itself is not a bestower, as Ptolemy rightly judges, nor is any other part of philosophy, yet since it teaches to foresee abundance or scarcity of future things, it can rightly be considered more useful and liberal than all others in economic and political matters for making profit: for in this matter, it requires much virtue of mind, but very little use of the body. Pliny writes that Democritus, who first understood and showed the connection of the heavens with the earth, when his wealthy citizens scorned this care, foresaw a scarcity of oil from the certain rising of the Pleiades in the future, and bought all the oil in the whole region at a low price, because of the hope of olives, to the astonishment of those who thought poverty and quiet were most dear to his studies. And when it appeared, a great cause of wealth flowed, and the anxious and eager repentance of the owners restored the price, content that he had thus proven that wealth would be easy for him (when he wished). Aristotle relates that Thales of Miletus gave a notable example of making money by the same reasoning, having foreseen an abundance of olives, whose words I have not been reluctant to transcribe here, so that his authority might add some weight to this commemoration. We must collect the reasons, recited here and there, by which some have made money: in this matter, we will be most useful to those who value and honor the method of making money. And of this kind is that of Thales of Miletus, which can be considered as a kind of invention for acquiring wealth. And although it is attributed to him alone for his supreme wisdom, it is nevertheless a general precept for making money. For when his poverty and the study of philosophy were objected to him as useless, and he himself had perceived from astrology the future abundance of olives, in winter, before they began to bloom, he is said to have bought all the olives in the Milesian and Chian territory at a low price, because no one then offered more, and at least by giving earnest money, since he did not have much money at hand: but when the time appeared, with many buyers suddenly converging, he sold them for as much as he wanted: and thus, having collected a large sum of money, he showed that philosophers can easily make money if they wish, but that their studies are not directed to that end. For so many and so great, and many other reasons, which it would be too long to pursue, since this science benefits men, and cannot be rightly reprehended by any means, as many have attempted, it is to be embraced with a grateful heart, and guarded as a gift of God not common. But someone may say: If the events of things are to be assigned to the eternal motion of the stars and an inviolable law, as you assert, then one of two absurdities, and a third with either, must be believed. For either chains must be placed on God, according to the opinion of the Stoics, which so connect him to the heavens and other inferior causes, that he cannot act freely or otherwise than as he is led by them: or certainly it must be established that God, after he once constituted and ordered the series of all natural causes, and attributed to them the perfect and supreme power of accomplishing all future outcomes, retired to perpetual leisure, just as an architect, having completed a ship, retires free from cares, and leaves it to be cared for and governed by sailors in the future, which is akin to the opinion of the Epicureans. Finally, if we accept either of these as true, a greater absurdity will be admitted, than if we believe that the stars in any way cause bad inclinations and vices in men, for we are absurdly compelled to say that God, who created the stars, is the cause of such evils. These things may seem plausible to the common and less cautious, but to those moderately trained, the defect of all parts is clear. And first, by the assiduous and necessary action of the stars, neither the freedom of God to act, nor the providence of God, which assiduously attends to created nature, is taken away, but rather both are affirmed, and confirmed in the minds of sound men, as can be sufficiently established from what the Physicists have handed down concerning necessity, and Ptolemy concerning divine and natural fate. Indeed, what can be pronounced concerning the eternal essence and virtues of God are altogether and absolutely, as they say, necessary, nor do they ever admit the implication of contradiction. For since God proceeds from himself and from no preceding cause, therefore he deserves to be called simply and without any imagination of contradiction omnipotent, intelligent, beneficent, just, freely acting, etc., through all eternity. But the condition of subsequent things, which have flowed from the first cause, and have at some time begun to exist, is different, far less constant, and finally the connection is weaker: for the power of acting inherent in all bodies of the world cannot be perpetual without God the creator and preserver of nature, nor can it ever interrupt the action once undertaken from itself. Celestial bodies, for example, just as they could not complete their periods at equal and constant times, nor observe eternal laws of motion, unless their power were always preserved intact and whole by divine power and goodness: so again, constantly circumducted by fatal necessity, they cannot inhibit or change their innate, or rather divinely implanted, course from themselves, nor according to the will of men or any other creature, but only by divine command. Thus, devouring fire retains its perpetual power of burning not from itself, but by the benefit of God.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Dedication: The Divine Art of Celestial Observation
text: DEDICATORIA. tura vituperant, vel ſtolidis ſermonibus irrident:19 contrariò honorem debitum Deo tribuunt atque Rem pub.fuo loco adiuuant, qui difciplinas vtiles vel ipſi din. fcunt, vel per alios ſua liberalitate propagari curant. Sed nulla pars eruditionis diuinior eſt, nec eruditior, nec homine magis digna, quàm de celi conuerſionibus Siderumq; effectibus fobria difputatio. Nam cùm excel- ſa illa & ſplendidißima, clara, illuſtris & plena ſtella. rum moles caleſtis, quam tanta varietate regularium, certorum & ordinatorum motuum inſtructam cerni. mus, ſit diuinum amplißimumque opus, tanquam a liquis magnus codex, in quo Deus optimus maximus, tum magnitudinem ſapientie, & bonitatis, aliarumque ſuarum innumerabilium virtutum veſtigia expreßit, tă verò omnium ſubiectarum rerum, hoc eſt, regionum, gen tium & vrbium,imò verò ſingulorum hominum futuros exitus fatorumq; naturalem viam, ſempiterno illo cor. porum celeſtium motu, inuariabili ordine &lege proce dente, tanquam proprio digito deſcripſit, nulla profe Eto difciplina nobilior, nec admirabilior, nec ad animi corporisq; bona cumulanda conducibilior cogitaripo.. test, quàm eſt ea que nos ad legendum, & intelligendum iſtum librum Dei inſtruit.Cum enim bomines, vt Ariſto teli placuit, naturaliter cognitionemrerum deſiderent, quid poteſt eſſe optabilius? quid ad'animi tranquillitatem comparandam aptius?quid denique ad naturale de ſiderium explendum conuenientius ? quàm buius diuini codicis omnium naturalium caſuum cauſas complectens : 3. tis co EPISTOLA tis cognitio, qua non ſolùm præſentium aut vicinarum, ſed etiam remotißimarum & futurarum, totiusque na turæ præſtantißimarum, hoc eſt, cæleſtium diuinarumque rerum contemplatores efficimur. Prætered nume. rorum Geometrie & vniuerſa Coſmographiæ noticia, artis Medica, Philoſophiæ naturalis, & Dialectice ve bemens ſtudium, amor denique veritatis laborumq; af- ſiduitas, & talia plura que hæc ſcientia continet, & absque ipſis tanquam imperfecta nunquam cum vera laude &honore exercetur, ad mentis illuſtrationem, va riarumque rerum cognitione inſtructionem, momenti plurimum habent. Quid? quòd ſtudia virtutum omnium. que artium meliùs augeri & rectius regi poſſunt, pra cognita naturali ingenj inclinatione, atque à ſtellis in ſita ad res gerendas aptitudine ineptitudineque feu ἀδιναμία. Sicut enim ineptos Muſicos, Poëtas ingratos, & milites infelices eſſe videmus, qui repugnante natu rali inclinatiõe aut canere aut verſificari aut militare, & talia, que artifices quaſi naturali inſtinctu magna cum venuſtate ſuauiter peneq; citra laborem peragunt, laborioſe imitari conantur: ita qui Philoſophia, qui ar. ti medendi, qui Iuriſprudentia,& talibus facultatibus Student, ſi nature beneficio adiuuentur, omnia ſue artis capita facilius & rectius citiusq; conſequentur, maio. req; cũ dexteritate exercebunt, quàm ý, qui repugnante quodammodo natura, quauis magna diligentia diuq; ad illa contedunt. Hoc igitur eſt, quod Horatius præcipit: Tu nihil inuita dices faciesúe Minerua. atque DEDICATORIA. atque quod Autor Centiloquý præcipit inquiens: ἐπ τηδεία ψυχὴ πρὸς γνῶσιμ απλέομ ἐπιτυγχάνε το ἀληθῆς, ἢ ὁ ἄκρως ἀσκήσας τιὼ ἐπιςήμηp, id eſſ, animus ad cognitionem aptus veri plus aßequitur, quàm qui ſupra modum ſe in Scientia exercuit. Hec tamen ita accipienda ſunt, vt ar tibus&diſciplinæ ſuus locus 15 ſua laus relinquatur. Nam natura quamuis in plerisq; arteſit potentior, ipSatamen nifiexercitatione & ſtudio excitetur confir meturq;,nunquam ſola alicuius rei perfectum pariet ha bitum. Ac plurimum ad perfectionem conferre doſtris nam, manifeſtè oſtendit diſcrimen, quod cerni poteſt in ter ipſos ingenioſos:quorum alij qui arte reguntur, aptè, perſpicue & conuenienti ordine negotia tractant: aliy verò qui limitibus præceptorum non cohercetur, ſue ſo lius nature affectus ſequetes, perſepè impertinentia in medium aſſerre, cognata diſtungere, minimè cobe rentia contexere conſueuerut. Etenim ficut duorum na tura æquè fertilium agrorum, diligenter cultus, copiam, neglectus verò prater rerum bonarú inopiam ſilueſtria producit:ita bona ingenia doctrina excellere, negligen tia degenerare ſolent: & hoc est, quod autor Centiloquij alia ſententia docet, Gon Jυχή, inquit, συνεργεῖ τῇ ὀρανία ενεργεία απερ ὁ ἄρις Ο γεωργός συνεργεῖ τῇ φύαφτγῆς διὰ τ αροτριάσεως καὶ ανακαθάρσεως, id eſt,ſapies animus adiuuat vimceleſtem, ficut bonus agricola cultura terram adiuuat. Prodeſt igitur aliquo modo ex poſitu calipræ widere felices aut infelices inclinationes, vt quid vnus. quiſque in qualibet profeßione, in aßequedis honoribus, Gin EPISTOLA & in ſimilibus actionibus naturæ hominum familiari. bus, vtiliter & cum laude aßequi poßit vel nequeat, in telligatur. Sed & ad pericula, que plerumque immode. ratas animi perturbationes ſequuntur, declinanda bac in re plurimu iuuamur. Si quis enim animi ad furioſam iram, ad inconſtantiam,ad vagas libidines, Utalia legi. bus vetita vitia rapidos impetus ex praua luminis cæle. ſtis permiſtione exortos proſpexerit, ſobrietate, circum ſpectione, ad reprimendam iram, ad domandas cupidita tes cordis aßuefactione,legum pænas & multa ſeua ma la vitare poterit, que aliquando incurrere neceßum eſt eos, quos laxis frenis cæcus ducit impetus, vt dicitur, ὃς ἐπιθυμία ήνιοχεῖ μαργοις χαλινοῖς. Laude digna igitur eſt Sententia eiufdem autoris Centiloquy: δυναται ο ίπιςήμωμ πολλὰς ἀποτρέψαθαι ενεργείας την αςέρωμ, ὅτε ὀξιμ εἰδήμων ης φύσεως αὐτῆν, καὶ προπαρασκευάζει ἑαυτόμ πρὸ το συμπτώσεως τη ἀεργφών, id eſt: Poteſt qui ſciens eſt, multos ſtellarum effectus auertere, quando naturam earum nouerit, ac ſe. ipſum ante illorum euentum preparare. Atque hæc qui. dem omnia que de iuuandis bonis, & reprimendis malis inclinationibus dicta ſunt, non ſolùm ad animi dotes cu mulandas pertinet, ſed & ad corporis bona conſeruada: oſi quodamodo collapſa ſint, quomodo recuperari debe ant, plurimu conducūt. Nulla enim præter hanc eſt ſcien tia, qua melius & rectius intelligi poßit, qualis vnius. cuiufq; corporis fit conſtitutio, & qualis qualitatum tem peratura, & quibus rebus ad ſupprimendam humorum δυσκρασίας potißimum vti conueniat. Veluti ſi ſtellarum poſitu DEDICATORIA, poſitu coſiderato, deprehendatur fore, vt ex vitalis cali partis per infauſtam radiatione Martis tranſitu, febris acutior exoriatur,tunc cibis potionibusq; frigidioribus diutius vtentes, Martis occidentem calorem, ſi in vni uerſum non abolere, ſaltem magna ex parte ſedare pote rimus: ſiquidem natura ita comparatum eſt, vt humidi. tas & adurens vis cõtraria minimè conueniant, neq; in vna materia morentur: Sic ſi Saturnus exuſcitatis ſue nature, hoc eſt, frigidis &exuccis humoribus agria tudinem corpori illaturus intelligatur, corpus illud bu mectantibus & calefacientibus rebus temperare conabi mur. Hinc ἰατρομαθηματικόμ στωτάξιωμ μέθοδο nata eſt as pud Aegyptios, qui vt Ptolemæus refert, cum maximè huius artis vimauxiffent, præuiſionibus aſtronomicis vbique arte medendi adiungere ſolebant, vt illinc cau- ſas præſentium & futurarum ægritudinum venarentur: binc verò remedia, quæ ſingulis humoribus cõgrunt aut aduerſantur, deſumentes,præſentes morbos remedijs in fallibilibus quantum fieri poteſt tollerent, futuros verò ταῖς προφυλακαῖσιμ feu remedys, vt vocant, præſeruatibus auerterent. Nam niſi circumdantis nos cali & tempera turarum proprietas tota præuideatur, ſæpè etiam reme diafallere rectè arbitrabantur, cùm non omnibus cor. poribus & morbis eadem conueniant. Sed nec parens medicina Hippocrates de his diſciplinis aliter ſentie. bat. Is enim in libello, quem πολὶ ἀἐρωμύδάτων τόπων inſcri pſit, cùm mutationum, quæ à πρόδοις exortibus &occaſio bus ſtellarum proueniunt, obſeruationem medico neces β fariam
translation: DEDICATION. Those who condemn useful disciplines or mock them with foolish words, conversely, give due honor to God and assist the commonwealth in its proper place, who either learn useful disciplines themselves or ensure their propagation through others' generosity. But no part of erudition is more divine, nor more learned, nor more worthy of man, than a sober discussion of the heavens' revolutions and the effects of the stars. For since that exalted and most splendid, clear, illustrious, and star-filled celestial mass, which we observe to be equipped with such a variety of regular, certain, and ordered motions, is a divine and most ample work, as it were, a great book, in which God, the best and greatest, has expressed the traces of the greatness of His wisdom, and goodness, and His other innumerable virtues, and indeed, by that eternal motion of the celestial bodies, proceeding in an unchangeable order and law, as if with His own finger, He has described the future outcomes of all subject things, that is, regions, peoples, and cities, and indeed, of individual men, and the natural path of their fates, no discipline can truly be conceived as nobler, nor more admirable, nor more conducive to accumulating the goods of mind and body, than that which instructs us to read and understand this book of God. For since men, as Aristotle pleased, naturally desire the knowledge of things, what can be more desirable? What more suitable for acquiring tranquility of mind? What, finally, more convenient for fulfilling natural desire? Than the knowledge of this divine book, which embraces the causes of all natural events, by which we are made contemplators not only of present or near things, but also of the most remote and future, and of the most excellent things of all nature, that is, celestial and divine things. Moreover, the knowledge of numbers, geometry, and universal cosmography, the vehement study of the medical art, natural philosophy, and dialectic, and finally, the love of truth and the assiduity of labors, and many such things that this science contains, and without which it is never exercised with true praise and honor as if imperfect, have great importance for the illumination of the mind and the instruction in the knowledge of various things. What? That the studies of all virtues and arts can be better augmented and more rightly governed, with a foreknowledge of the natural inclination of genius, and the aptitude or inaptitude (or ἀδυναμία) implanted by the stars for managing affairs. For just as we see musicians to be inept, poets ungrateful, and soldiers unfortunate, who, with a natural inclination resisting, try to sing or versify or fight, and such things, which artists perform with great grace, sweetly and almost without labor, as if by natural instinct, laboriously imitate: so those who study philosophy, who study the art of healing, who study jurisprudence, and such faculties, if they are aided by the benefit of nature, will achieve all the main points of their art more easily and rightly and quickly, and will exercise them with greater dexterity, than those who, in a way, with nature resisting, strive for them with great diligence and for a long time. This, therefore, is what Horace prescribes: "You will say or do nothing against Minerva's will." And what the author of the Centiloquy prescribes, saying: "ἐπ τηδεία ψυχὴ πρὸς γνῶσιμ απλέομ ἐπιτυγχάνε το ἀληθῆς, ἢ ὁ ἄκρως ἀσκήσας τιὼ ἐπιςήμηp," that is, "a soul apt for knowledge attains more truth than one who has exercised himself excessively in science." These things, however, are to be understood in such a way that their place and praise are left to the arts and disciplines. For although nature is more powerful in most things, yet if it is not stimulated and strengthened by exercise and study, it will never alone produce a perfect habit of anything. And that doctrine contributes greatly to perfection, is clearly shown by the distinction that can be observed among ingenious people themselves: some of whom, who are governed by art, handle affairs aptly, clearly, and in a convenient order: while others, who are not restrained by the limits of precepts, following the affections of their own nature alone, are accustomed to often bring up irrelevant things, to distinguish related things, and to weave together things that are minimally coherent. For just as a diligently cultivated field, equally fertile as another, produces abundance, but neglect, besides a lack of good things, produces wild growth: so good talents are accustomed to excel through doctrine, and degenerate through negligence: and this is what the author of the Centiloquy teaches in another sentence, saying: "Gon Jυχή, inquit, συνεργεῖ τῇ ὀρανία ενεργεία απερ ὁ ἄρις Ο γεωργός συνεργεῖ τῇ φύαφτγῆς διὰ τ αροτριάσεως καὶ ανακαθάρσεως," that is, "a wise mind assists the celestial power, just as a good farmer assists the nature of the earth through plowing and clearing." It is therefore useful in some way to foresee happy or unhappy inclinations from the position of the heavens, so that it may be understood what each person can usefully and commendably achieve or not achieve in any profession, in acquiring honors, and in similar actions familiar to human nature. But also, we are greatly helped in this matter to avoid dangers, which often follow immoderate disturbances of the mind. For if someone has foreseen that impulses to furious anger, to inconstancy, to wandering lusts, and other vices forbidden by laws, arising from a bad mixture of celestial light, he will be able to avoid the penalties of laws and many severe evils, which sometimes those whom blind impulse leads with loose reins, as it is said, "ὃς ἐπιθυμία ήνιοχεῖ μαργοις χαλινοῖς," must incur, by sobriety, circumspection, by accustoming the heart to repress anger and to subdue desires. Therefore, the saying of the same author of the Centiloquy is worthy of praise: "δυναται ο ίπιςήμωμ πολλὰς ἀποτρέψαθαι ενεργείας την αςέρωμ, ὅτε ὀξιμ εἰδήμων ης φύσεως αὐτῆν, καὶ προπαρασκευάζει ἑαυτόμ πρὸ το συμπτώσεως τη ἀεργφών," that is: "He who is knowing can avert many effects of the stars, when he knows their nature, and prepares himself before their occurrence." And all these things that have been said about helping good inclinations and repressing bad ones, not only pertain to accumulating mental endowments, but also to preserving the goods of the body: and if they have somehow collapsed, how they ought to be recovered, they are very conducive. For there is no science other than this by which it can be better and more rightly understood what the constitution of each body is, and what the temperament of its qualities, and what things are most suitable to use to suppress the dyscrasia of the humors. As if, considering the position of the stars, it is found that an acute fever will arise from the vital part of the sky through the inauspicious radiation of Mars' transit, then by using colder foods and drinks for a longer time, we will be able to calm the western heat of Mars, if not abolish it entirely, at least for the most part: since nature is so constituted that humidity and burning force are minimally compatible, and do not remain in the same matter: So if Saturn is understood to bring illness to the body by exciting his nature, that is, cold and dry humors, we will try to temper that body with moistening and warming things. Hence the "ἰατρομαθηματικόμ στωτάξιωμ μέθοδο" was born among the Egyptians, who, as Ptolemy relates, when they had most increased the power of this art, were accustomed to everywhere combine astronomical previsions with the art of healing, so that from there they might hunt for the causes of present and future illnesses: and from here, taking remedies that are congruent or adverse to individual humors, they would remove present diseases with infallible remedies as much as possible, and avert future ones with "ταῖς προφυλακαῖσιμ" or, as they call them, preservative remedies. For unless the property of the surrounding sky and temperaments is entirely foreseen, they rightly believed that remedies often fail, since the same things are not suitable for all bodies and diseases. Nor did the father of medicine, Hippocrates, think differently about these disciplines. For in the book he inscribed "πολὶ ἀἐρωμύδάτων τόπων," when he considered the observation of changes, which arise from the "πρόδοις" (risings) and occurrences of the stars, necessary for a physician.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Occasion for Writing
text: PRAEFA TIO. ortus fimul exoriatur, quibus in locis luminaria reliquaque fi dera teneatur, & quomodo inter ſe colligata aut diſiuncta fint, & talia fimilia inuenire, Aſtronomi eſt officium. Quod verò apud Ptolemeum dicitur, eos qui naſcuntur Luna & Mercurio inconiun&is, Saturno perdiu, aut Marte noctu ad horoscopum ſcandente, prædi&amq; figurationem intuente, plerunquefieri epilepticos, ad Aſtrologie coniectationem pertinet. Vtriuf que artis inter ſcriptores princeps eſt Claudius Ptolemæus,qui cæteris elegantius & verius has artes prodidit: is tamen propter rerum fubtilitatem altitudinemque, & præcipue in hac par te breuitatem nimiam, non ita multum profuerit rudi lectori, qui prima elementa prius non cognouit ex libellis iſagogicis, quorum præcipui funt: in Aſtronomia Procli & Georgij Purbachij: In Aftrologia Alcabicij, cuius breuia & obfcurius quedam dicta in gratiam huius artis Tyronum latius exponere, & exemplis, quando res ita poſtulauerit, explanare conabimur. Sed alterum & precipuum noſtrum in haccommentatione erit ſtudium, vt hæc Alcabicii, quæ ad traditiones Arabum intelligendas precipuè deſeruiunt, ad ſententiam Ptolemei, quam om nibus veris artificibus probari video, diligenter conferamus. Speramus autem hunc noſtrum laborem fore nec ingratum nec infrugiferum ſtudioſis adoleſcentibus. Nam ex his noftris inftitutionibus, non tantùm omnia & neceſſaria huius philoſophię pręcepta cognofcere, fed & vera à falfis fuperftitiofis, τοῖς αἰαιτιολογήτοις diiudicare,& in quibus Arabes Ptolemaum fecuti fint, in quibus ipſum reliquerint, & quæ denique abfurda commenti ſint, facilime intelligire poterunt. &c. • Ab inuocatione incipit more veterum, qui etiam in ſoluta oratione ſicut poetæ ab inuocatione exordiri folent.Demoſthe. translation: The diction and context of the speech are barbarous, but the meaning and the matter itself, which alone we will always look for in this author, are better. Although, he says, there are not wanting those who have written introductions to Astrology, most of them have either not comprehended necessary matters, or have so obscured them by the prolixity of their discourse, that while they profit the unlearned reader nothing, they seem to have wasted all their labor; or finally, having no regard for order and method of teaching, they have confusedly heaped up precepts. For this reason, he says he was compelled to write a Methodical Introduction to Astrology, which should contain all necessary precepts, and in which nothing useless or superfluous or wrongly placed should be found. And so on.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Divine Will and Astrological Necessity
text: DEDICATORIA. retinet, & materij's penetrationi obnoxijs admotus,nea ceßariam ipfarum inflammationem dißipationemque non nifi diuinitus fufpendere poteft. Similiter reliquas res omnes in mundo comprehenfas, fiue ee in athere, five in elementis eße intelligantur, aut he illis comparetur, nõ niſi huiufmodi phyfice, vt vocant, neceßitatis vincu lo cohærere oftendi poteft, vt facile ſit intelligere, per in ferioris materia cum celi motibus fatale, hoc eft, quo ad naturam, neceßarium confenfum, diuinæ volutatis liber tatem, atq; eiufdem in omnibus rebus præfentiam ſtabili ri confirmariq;, adeò errant, quicunque aftrorű decretis Deo vel Stoicam neceßitatem imponi, vel eternum otiŭ dari dari fomniant. fomniant. Nam commotio celorum neceßaria, prædictiones futurorum cafuum actionumq;, qua ex mo tu poſitu mutuaq; ſiderum glutinatione, tanquàm à viribus diuinitus nature tributis deriuantur, non ſim. pliciter, fed quatenus Deus natura dominus naturalibus actionibus non aduerfatur, perinde atq; artis medendi pradictiões vere eße aftruuntur.Sicut enim medici,ex empli gratia, intuentes homine continuis defluxibus ob. noxium,in cuius corpore pulmo putridis humoribus gra uatus atque apoſtemate leſus, inſtar pertufi follis non fac tis copiofum efficacemque fpiritum cordiminiftrat, ſi di cant talem hominem breui tépore aucto calore humi ditate fuppreßa, morti futurű obnoxin, Deo natura Domino fuam potentiam atq; miraculofas actiones, quibus extra fupra comunem naturæ curfum etia mortuis, cũ vult, vitam reftituere, nedű agritudine lafos fanare conferuare EPISTOLA: conferuarė potest, non adimunt, Jed caufarum viribus à Deo infitis naturaliter agentium proximè fecuturos effectus exitusque demonftrant, idque fatis certò, in bac præfertim genere morbi, qui adeò fauit, vt à Poeta fic notandus videretur: Cernis vt è molli ſanguis pulmone remiſſus Ad ſtygias certo limite ducat aquas. Eodem modo prudens Mathematicus, in primo ali. cuius exortu Saturnwn per Libram, qua maximè gaudet, euntem, celorumg ſtationem ſeptimam aut fextam, que valetudini cõſentiunt, occupantem, atque hoftiliter ex quadrangulo corpus Luna Cancru perluftrantis pul Santem afpiciens, firatione Luna & Cancri, que core. bro & pectori mirè conciliatur, ad hunc modú afflictis, in cerebro nati frigus immoderatum, pectoris angustia, noxia pituita copiam,& quide ob debilem calorem ficcitatemque ſtelle Saturni ad pulmones defcendentem, pulmonum exulcerationem, ſpirandi difficultatem, cor.. dis tremorem,& denique totius corporis deficcationem talificam m& & lethalem predicat, eum feriem caufarum naturaliter agentium exponere iudicare debemus, mini meque infringere potentiam diuinam, qua Deus condita nature Dominus, actiones eius inhibere, celi curfum fie ſtere, corpora vitijs morbiſg, aliâs immedicabilibus ob. noxia fanare, ac multa alia fimiliter facere poteft: per que haud dubiè declaratur, pleraque natura, quæ noftri spirtuum respectu neceßaria funt, fiad Dei potentia referantur, reuera eße contingentia, ipfi effectus ab aftris de. DEDICATORIA. aftris decreti minimè, vt multi putauerunt, ita necessar ria lege fanciri, vt illis planè nulla alia vis obſiſtere valeat, quin ad intentionem fuam primam euentumq; pér ficiendum fimpliciter progrediantur. Nam Lamp praterquam quòd Deus ſequentibus caufis omnibus imperare, atque earundem actionem intendere, remittere, aut deniq; pla nè immutare vel tollere pro arbitrio poteft, ipfa quoque hominŭ prouidētia & diligentia, vt dixi, multa magnæ &graues calamitates à naturalibus actiõibus fufcitata euitari, aut certè mitigari poßunt. Hinc exercitati Me dici morbos lethales à curabilibus prudenter difcernen. tes, borum quidem cauſas procreantes, naturalibus & contrariarum virium remedys impediunt & auertunt: illos verò,cùm à multis, magnis & infuperabilibus caufis exortos videant, aut Deo curandos relinquut, aut ad corporis cui inhærent, dißipatione tranfire permittunt: ad quam non rarò peruadunt etiam aly leuiores & cura biles morbi, cùm aut natura eorum à Medicis non agnofcitur, aut vis contraria remediorum oſtenſa ſtultè negli gitur: tumenim leuiora mala abſque vllo impedimento ſuam natura fequentia aßumpto robore perimunt. Eandem planè ephodum eundemq; finem in aftrologia,cuius beneficio non tantùm præſentium morborum proxime cauße oftenduntur, quod medicorú proprium est, verùm etiam fuperiorum potentiorumque caufarum, nonrarò Juas actiones ad longum tempus fufpendentium, ſequentese tes effectus definiuntur, ſpectare debemus. Nam fi cafus à magnis, validis multisque caufis concurrentibus exori nis, validis multisque caulis concurren ४ turos EPISTOLA suros, atque ob id ineuitabiles planeq; nullis contraria rum viriumrebus fuccumbetes pracognouerimus, tunc, vtpote à rerum natura deferti, ad inuocationem Dei con fugientes precabimur, vt ipfe, cùm fupra totam rerum naturam & fupra ſtellas ſedens, nullo neceßitatis vincu To teneatur, feque inuocantibus, etiamiam à reliqua nao tura abdicatis, opem ferre ſoleat, noſtras quoque calami. tates benignè tollere aut faltem mitigare dignetur.Rea liquas verò cali minusq; efficaces caufas, cum ea quoq; ignorate aut neglecta ſue nature intentiones neceßa rid efficiant, fi prauifas oppoſitis remedys vim contra riam habentibus auertere ſtuduerimus, multas calami. tates vitabimus, multaque mala, que fue natura relicta futura erant intolerabilia, vt ferantur fuperenturque efficiemus:quam diligentiam tam corpori quam animo vtilem & penè neceßariam eße, cum fuprafatis often. derimus, reliquum est, ut Deum conditorem & aßidua um nature ftellarumque, quas prater virtutum femina, grauiorum quoque vitiorum flammas hominibus inge. nerare diximus, rectorem, ab omni contagio & pollu tione abſoluamus. Querunt multi boni viri, quomodo fie ri poßit, vt fi à ſideribus vitioſe inclinationes aut fcele ra oriuntur, Deus humanorum flagitiorum caußa non fit? Cum enim Deus fit fons bonitatis, & ſtellas valde bo nas fecerit, atque ad vnam perpetuam constantemque bonarum rerum effectionem ordinauerit, quomodo fieri potest, vt de celo malarum inclinationum caußanatu. Kalis defcendat? Ab istis ego vicißim quærendum puto, cùm DEDICATORIA. cùm natura ad vnum eundemque & inuariabilem mo dum agendi feratur, quomodo fieri poßit, vt vinum fub. ſtantia & qualitate vnum idemque, vno eodemque tem pore & aquali, fed tamen quodammodo immoderatiore menfura à compluribus hauſtum, in alijs letitiam hila ritatemque, in alijs mœrorem & triftitiam, in alijs fue rorem, Grixandi pugnandique cupiditatem, in alys der nique pigritiam profundißimos fomnos pariat? Hic rpsos actutum refponfuros arbitror, caufam diuerfitatis talium actionum, non in virtute vini agente, fed in fub iectis mero perfuſis effe quærendam. Quum enim homi. nes temperatura rarò conueniant, Gipsa crafis in ini. tio pro ratione circumfufi cali fuas qualitates femel in duta,nunquam in alium ſtatum permutetur, omneq; id, quod fuccedentibus temporibus nutritionis Gaugmen tationis gratia corpori adiungitur, fibi ita conciliet at temperetque, vt in primum illum ſtatum Juum peculia. rem priusquam calo ſancitam conformationem tran. feat:idcirco quid mirum?temperaturarum copia genero fifacilè alterabilis vini fibi aßimilata mirè adaucľa rum, fanguineam quidem bilaritatis, biliofam verò rixa ram, melancholicam lachrimari, denique phlegma ticam ſomnolentia, & talium fuæ vniufcuiusque natura congruentium effectuum egregia declaratione peperiffe vulgatum verfum : oικα τρύποις την χραιμοίωμ κεράννυται Benè habet, iam fcilicet ad noftram sententiam defici unt. Quid enim interest obfecro, quod ad has naturales actiones difcernendas attinet, duo ne fint subiecta, an verò 72
translation: DEDICATION. It retains, and when applied to materials susceptible to penetration, it cannot but divinely suspend their necessary inflammation and dissipation. Similarly, all other things comprehended in the world, whether they are understood to be in the ether or in the elements, or whether one is compared to the other, can only be shown to cohere by this kind of physical, as they call it, bond of necessity. Thus, it is easy to understand that through the fatal, that is, as far as nature is concerned, necessary agreement of inferior matter with the motions of the heavens, the freedom of the divine will and its presence in all things are established and confirmed. Therefore, those who dream that either Stoic necessity is imposed on God by the decrees of the stars, or that eternal idleness is given to Him, greatly err. For the necessary commotion of the heavens, and the predictions of future events and actions, which are derived from the motion, position, and mutual connection of the stars, as if from powers divinely attributed to nature, are not simply asserted to be true, but insofar as God, the Lord of nature, does not oppose natural actions, just as the predictions of the art of healing are asserted to be true. For just as physicians, for example, observing a person subject to continuous defluxions, in whose body the lung, burdened with putrid humors and injured by an aposteme, like a perforated bellows, does not adequately supply the heart with copious and effective spirit, if they say that such a person will soon be subject to death due to increased heat and suppressed humidity, they do not deny God, the Lord of nature, His power and miraculous actions, by which He can, beyond and above the common course of nature, even restore life to the dead when He wills, let alone heal and preserve those afflicted by illness. EPISTLE: They do not deny, but rather demonstrate the effects and outcomes that will proximately follow from the naturally acting forces inherent in causes by God, and this with sufficient certainty, especially in this kind of disease, which has raged so much that it seemed worthy of being noted by the Poet thus: 'You see how the blood, released from the soft lung, leads with a certain limit to the Stygian waters.' In the same way, a prudent Mathematician, at the first rising of someone, observing Saturn passing through Libra, in which it especially rejoices, and occupying the seventh or sixth house of the heavens, which are conducive to health, and hostilely striking from a square aspect the body of the Moon traversing Cancer, if the Moon and Cancer, which are wonderfully reconciled to the brain and chest, are afflicted in this way, predicts an immoderate cold born in the brain, tightness of the chest, an abundance of noxious phlegm, and indeed, due to the weak heat and dryness of the star of Saturn descending to the lungs, ulceration of the lungs, difficulty in breathing, trembling of the heart, and finally, a fatal and lethal desiccation of the entire body. We must judge him to be expounding a series of naturally acting causes, and by no means infringing upon divine power, by which God, the Lord of created nature, can inhibit its actions, stop the course of the heavens, heal bodies subject to otherwise incurable vices and diseases, and do many other similar things. Through these, it is undoubtedly declared that most things in nature, which are necessary with respect to our spirits, if referred to the power of God, are truly contingent, and the effects decreed by the stars are by no means, as many have thought, sanctioned by such a necessary law that no other force can oppose them, but that they simply proceed to accomplish their primary intention and outcome. For besides the fact that God can command all subsequent causes, and can intensify, relax, or finally completely change or abolish their action at will, human foresight and diligence themselves, as I have said, can avoid or at least mitigate many great and serious calamities aroused by natural actions. Hence, experienced physicians, prudently distinguishing lethal diseases from curable ones, indeed impede and avert the procreating causes of the latter with natural and contrary remedies; but the former, when they see them arising from many, great, and insurmountable causes, they either leave to God to cure, or permit them to pass into the dissolution of the body to which they adhere. To this dissolution, not infrequently, even other lighter and curable diseases lead, when either their nature is not recognized by physicians, or the contrary force of remedies, when shown, is foolishly neglected; for then lighter evils, following their nature without any impediment, acquire strength and destroy. We ought to look for the same method and the same end in astrology, by the benefit of which not only the proximate causes of present diseases are shown, which is proper to physicians, but also the subsequent effects of superior and more powerful causes, which not infrequently suspend their actions for a long time, are defined. For if we have foreknown events that will arise from great, powerful, and many concurrent causes, and are therefore inevitable and completely unsubmissive to any things of contrary forces, then, as if deserted by the nature of things, we will flee to the invocation of God and pray that He, since He sits above the whole nature of things and above the stars, is bound by no bond of necessity, and is accustomed to bring help to those who invoke Him, even those already rejected by the rest of nature, may deign to benignly remove or at least mitigate our calamities. But as for other less effective causes, since they also, if unknown or neglected, bring about the necessary intentions of their nature, if we strive to avert them, when foreseen, with opposing remedies having a contrary force, we will avoid many calamities, and we will make many evils, which if left to their nature would be intolerable, to be borne and overcome. Since we have sufficiently shown that this diligence is useful and almost necessary for both body and mind, it remains for us to absolve God, the creator and constant ruler of nature and the stars, which, besides the seeds of virtues, we have said also engender the flames of more serious vices in humans, from all contagion and pollution. Many good men ask how it can be that if vicious inclinations or crimes arise from the stars, God is not the cause of human transgressions? For since God is the fount of goodness, and made the stars very good, and ordained them for one perpetual and constant effectuation of good things, how can it be that the cause of evil inclinations naturally descends from heaven? I think that in turn, it should be asked of them, since nature tends to one and the same and unchangeable mode of action, how it can be that wine, one and the same in substance and quality, drunk by many at one and the same time and in equal, but nevertheless somewhat immoderate measure, produces in some joy and hilarity, in others sadness and melancholy, in others fury, a desire to quarrel and fight, and finally in others laziness and profound sleep? Here I think they will immediately answer that the cause of the diversity of such actions is not to be sought in the active virtue of the wine, but in the subjects permeated by the pure wine. For since men rarely agree in temperament, and the very crasis, once it has assumed its qualities at the beginning according to the surrounding heaven, never changes into another state, and whatever is added to the body in subsequent times for the sake of nourishment and augmentation, it so reconciles and tempers to itself that it passes into that first peculiar state of its own, sanctioned by heaven before; therefore, what is surprising? The abundance of temperaments, wonderfully increased by the easily alterable wine assimilated to them, has indeed produced the well-known verse: 'The blood-filled temperament is mixed with wine,' declaring with excellent clarity the effects congruent with each nature: the sanguine producing hilarity, the bilious quarrels, the melancholic tears, and finally the phlegmatic sleepiness and such effects. Well, now they certainly fall in line with our opinion. For what difference does it make, I ask, as far as distinguishing these natural actions is concerned, whether there are two subjects, or indeed 72?

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Author's Intention
text: PRAEFA TIO. ortus fimul exoriatur, quibus in locis luminaria reliquaque fi dera teneatur, & quomodo inter ſe colligata aut diſiuncta fint, & talia fimilia inuenire, Aſtronomi eſt officium. Quod verò apud Ptolemeum dicitur, eos qui naſcuntur Luna & Mercurio inconiun&is, Saturno perdiu, aut Marte noctu ad horoscopum ſcandente, prædi&amq; figurationem intuente, plerunquefieri epilepticos, ad Aſtrologie coniectationem pertinet. Vtriuf que artis inter ſcriptores princeps eſt Claudius Ptolemæus,qui cæteris elegantius & verius has artes prodidit: is tamen propter rerum fubtilitatem altitudinemque, & præcipue in hac par te breuitatem nimiam, non ita multum profuerit rudi lectori, qui prima elementa prius non cognouit ex libellis iſagogicis, quorum præcipui funt: in Aſtronomia Procli & Georgij Purbachij: In Aftrologia Alcabicij, cuius breuia & obfcurius quedam dicta in gratiam huius artis Tyronum latius exponere, & exemplis, quando res ita poſtulauerit, explanare conabimur. Sed alterum & precipuum noſtrum in haccommentatione erit ſtudium, vt hæc Alcabicii, quæ ad traditiones Arabum intelligendas precipuè deſeruiunt, ad ſententiam Ptolemei, quam om nibus veris artificibus probari video, diligenter conferamus. Speramus autem hunc noſtrum laborem fore nec ingratum nec infrugiferum ſtudioſis adoleſcentibus. translation: In all arts there are two kinds of teaching. One they call 'Toli', by which we show a thing most simply and in plain words, without any mention of causes. The other, 'Si ou', which by accurate disputation investigates the causes of the precepts. Just as when in the art of healing, abstinence from wine is prescribed for those with fever, it is a precept of the first kind: but when the reason is added, that the drinking of wine increases the inflammation of bilious matter, it pertains to the latter kind. In the same way, when in the Spherical Elements the zodiac is defined as a great circle, cutting the equinoctial circle at oblique angles, so that its middle part, which they call the ecliptic, recedes from the same equinoctial by a maximum distance of about 32 parts, under which all the planets move by their proper motions from west to east: such a simple and bare description of the zodiac refers to the 'Toli' genus of teaching. But when practitioners, by more accurate disputation, give the causes of its obliquity, namely that it is necessary that such an obliquity of the zodiac be believed, because experience and many consenting observations have found and explored that the sun never declines from the equinoctial circle by more than 32 parts: and that this was so ordained by God, the author of nature, so that the sun with the other wanderers, for the life of living beings and other nascent things, might wander to the sides of the world, and not, if they were perpetually carried around under only one parallel by the conversion of the first motion, leave the middle earth scorched by the excessive heat of direct rays, and the other parts also barren and perpetually rigid with ice: they differ in the genus of teaching: for they endeavor not only to simply describe the zodiac, but also to show the cause of its obliquity. Thus in introductory books of Astrology, when, for example, the simple distribution of the zodiac into 12 signs is taught, with the causes concealed: it is 'Toli' doctrine. But through the 'Tudi ok' doctrine, the reasons and causes of such a distribution are more diligently sought, namely that the zodiac was first divided by the ancients into twelve parts, neither more nor fewer, because in the annual space, the moon, having completed its period, overtakes the sun twelve times, and is conjoined with it once in each of the twelve parts of the sky: or because out of 48 celestial images to be explained hereafter, only twelve fall into the path of the sun. From these it can be understood that the scope of this work and the entire intention of the author is, by the 'Tõ õlu' genus of teaching, omitting accurate disputation about the causes of the precepts, and neglecting the refutation of inept discourses by which some think this whole philosophy can be undermined, to simply expound the bare precepts of Astrology, as is customary and ought to be done in introductory books. For the 'rõ diõli' of astrological precepts, and the fortification and defense of the entire art against rivals, he says, have been sufficiently expounded in Ptolemy's Quadripartite Construction, and in certain works written by himself, of which I have not yet had the opportunity to see a copy.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Human Nature and Celestial Influence
text: ==Start of OCR for page 1== EPISTOLA verò vnum, quod prima praftantißimaque forma pend abolita, longè deteriorem conditionem induit? Faten dum eft autem,talem conditionem ineffe nature homie nis:Is enim cùm corpus immortale animumque luce dia wina plenum à conditore accepiffet, per inobedientiam non solùm priftina perfectione fpoliatus, verùm etiam miserè fauciatus & veneno perfuſus mortique, qua nihil terribilius, obnoxius factus eft. Hac cùm ita se habeat, si calum aliquando hominibus malas inclinationes inge neret,id malum non ad ſtellas, que in incolumi minime. que vitiata natura hominis, nihil prater id quod bonum est, fufcitare potuißent,sed ad hominum naturam ob ma gnum fcelus deprauatam referre debemus. Id autem non in ſtellis tantum sed to in alijs innumeris rebus accide re facilè oftendi poteft. Vinum, quo ad nutrienda valida corpora ad innatum calorem augendum conferuan. dumque nihil eft melius, vehementius febricitantibus exitiofum eft,sed id non ob fuam naturam que bono eſt, atque vt dixi, bona valetudinis amica, sed quia sordide materiæ ob putredinem calore præternaturali inflam mate adiectum, suo quamuis vtili calore incendium ad. augeat. Et cicuta, que in vitiata parumque calente nas tura sanguinem intenſo frigore coagulans perimit, natu ra integre nulloque vitio contaminata potuißet eße ci bus non minùs conueniens, quam iam noftris corporibus. lactuce conueniunt. Hac prolixiùs & craßiore Minerua egi, vt planè ab omnibus facilè intelligeretur, hanc philofophiæ parte, quam diuinatrice vocant, non eße, vt vulgò putatur, ina nium vel illicitarum rerum fuperfluam curiofam in dagationem, sed phyficam quandam feu naturalem tra Etationem, in qua potiores effectiones in elementis, crafes feu temperature mixtorum corporum indicantur, atque ad caußas caleſtes referuntur, perinde vt medici eafdem res confiderare confueuerunt, nifi quòd hi in par ticularibus corporum conftitutionibus, caufis propiorin bus, hoc eft, in ipsa materia berentibus contenti, celestes potetiores caußas plerumq; negligunt. Nam qui vel immotis artium principys, vel manifeſta experientia ſophisticè vel petulanter contradicunt, merito cenfentur indigni, cum quibus boni & veritatis amantes virinegotium ha bere debeant. Non enim est confentaneum, cùm parua fragilia huius fublunaris mundi corpora in vniver. fum certis viribus & actionibus Dei beneficio inſtruEta sint, vaftisima illa & cæleftis lucis plena corpora vi ribus carere aut eße otiofa. Quis enim eft, qui non animaduertat Solem prater quatuor anni temporum vices, quotidiana quoque conuerfione, siccitatis, caloris, humiditatis & frigiditatis manifeſtä differentiam adducere: & iuxta habitudinem Lune ad Solem augeri diminuig humores in rebus crefcentibus. Rudis eft agricolarum, piſcatorum &paftorum vita sine literis, magis tame ingenioſa in illis obſeruatio acti onu nature apparet, quim in nonnullis, qui in literis con senuerunt, & falfa perfuafione cognitionis philofophia Digitized by Google tument. ==End of OCR for page 3== ==Start of OCR for page 4== DEDICATORIA. tumět. Na illi Luna crescite, cochilia vtpate plena ſucci venāda, decreſcete verò materia exuccarob id vermin corroſioni minimè obnoxia cedenda eße norut, parig in duſtria aptiora tëpora ſationu, plantationŭ, admißionis pecudu ad coitu, incubatiõis auin domesticaru, & simia lium operarū ruſticarŭ vsu, & quotidiana experientia edocti obſeruat, atque ad motu Lune accommodant.At bos præclaros natura interpretes, nullú ſe luminarium aliorumúe ſiderum infigne effectum sentire, & nullam eße ſtellarum actionem, prodere non pudet. Illos quoq;, qui in terra adiacentem materiam ſiderum actionem atque influxum concedentes, negant eße artem seu ſcie. tiam, qua futurorum effectuum tépora definiri poßint etſi saniores videri poterant, non minùs tamen nonnihil erudiendos putamus in eadem ſchola agricolarum vel po tiùs nautarum, qui ex Lune adfaftigium velimum celi Scanfione, marinaru aquaru acceßum, & per eiufde Lua na à meridionali circulo ad cardines sinñitaris lapſum, aquarum illarum reciprocationem atque ad oceanum re fluxum expedite precognoscunt, atque alijs ad vnguem pradicere norūt, quamuis supputationum ſint rudes, & aftus illi marini ob ocyorem disiunctionem luminarium flata tempora minimè obſeruent. Quòd si ruralia hec cui piam adhuc subti¬liora & ἀκατάληπα videbuntur, is qua re brutorum ſen ſchola seu exemplis erudiendus non sit, non video. translation: But does it not happen that what was once a primary and most excellent form, having been abolished, assumes a far worse condition? It must be admitted, however, that such a condition is inherent in human nature: for man, having received from the Creator an immortal body and a soul full of divine light, through disobedience was not only stripped of his pristine perfection, but also miserably wounded and infused with poison, and made subject to death, than which nothing is more terrible. Since these things are so, if the heavens sometimes generate evil inclinations in men, we ought to attribute this evil not to the stars, which in the sound and uncorrupted nature of man could have produced nothing but what is good, but to the depraved nature of men due to great wickedness. This can easily be shown to happen not only in the stars but also in innumerable other things. Wine, than which nothing is better for nourishing strong bodies and for increasing and preserving innate heat, is destructive to those suffering from violent fevers; but this is not due to its nature, which is good, and, as I said, a friend to good health, but because, when added to foul matter inflamed by preternatural heat due to putrefaction, its own useful heat increases the conflagration. And hemlock, which in a vitiated and insufficiently warm nature kills by coagulating the blood with intense cold, could have been, in an integral nature uncontaminated by any defect, no less suitable as food than lettuces are now suitable for our bodies. Therefore, just as we see gold and silver purified and strengthened by a stronger fire, while other metals are consumed and dissipated, for no other reason than that the former are perfect and the latter imperfect, impure, as it were, diseased bodies: so it is fitting to think and believe that the natural action of the stars, in the untainted and pure matter of our bodies and souls, would undoubtedly produce all good effects, and that they inflict evil impulses, sometimes perverse inclinations, diseases, brevity of life, and such plagues, for no other reason than the impurities and defilement of our nature. I have discussed these matters at greater length and with a coarser Minerva, so that it might be easily understood by all that this part of philosophy, which they call divinatory, is not, as is commonly supposed, a superfluous and curious investigation of vain or illicit things, but a certain physical or natural treatise, in which the more potent effects in the elements, the crases or temperaments of mixed bodies are indicated, and referred to celestial causes, just as physicians are accustomed to consider the same things, except that they, content with particular bodily constitutions and more proximate causes, that is, those inherent in the matter itself, often neglect the more potent celestial causes. But indeed, it has been abundantly said above that both proximate and remote true causes are everywhere usefully observed. There are great books of childish and ridiculous accusations collected by Pico and others in reprehension of this science, to which some, either from slowness or malice of nature, so applaud that they think that from this, besides the utility of predictions, the whole action of the heavens and the comprehension of the whole art can be shaken as impossible. Here they achieve so little and are so inept, that it is no fault to pass over in silence and safely despise all those things which they ignorantly conceive and object to the subversion of the energy of the heavens and the comprehension of the whole science, since they can scarcely be admitted without fault to a discussion of serious matters. For those who sophistically or petulantly contradict either the immovable principles of the arts or manifest experience, are rightly judged unworthy with whom good and truth-loving men should have dealings. For it is not consistent that, while the small and fragile bodies of this sublunar world are universally endowed with certain powers and actions by the benefit of God, those vast bodies full of celestial light should lack powers or be idle. And besides, Ptolemy and Aristotle have sufficiently demonstrated that celestial bodies are the universal causes of the changes and motions that occur in the mass of the elements; the coarser and more evident effects of celestial light in the elements and in the bodies of animals and plants also fall within the common senses. For who is there who does not observe that the Sun, besides the four seasons of the year, also brings about a manifest difference of dryness, heat, humidity, and coldness by its daily revolution: and that according to the Moon's relation to the Sun, humors in growing things increase and decrease. The life of farmers, fishermen, and shepherds is rude and without letters, yet a more ingenious observation of the actions of nature appears in them than in some who have grown old in letters and are puffed up with a false persuasion of philosophical knowledge. For they know that when the Moon is waxing, shells should be sought full of juice, but when it is waning, matter should be yielded that is dry and therefore least susceptible to worm corrosion; and with similar diligence, taught by use and daily experience, they observe and adapt to the Moon's motion the more suitable times for sowing, planting, admitting cattle to mating, incubating domestic birds, and similar rural operations. But these excellent interpreters of nature are not ashamed to declare that they perceive no significant effect of the luminaries or other stars, and that there is no action of the stars. Those also, who, conceding the action and influx of the stars on the adjacent matter of the earth, deny that there is an art or science by which the times of future effects can be defined, although they might seem saner, we nevertheless think they need some instruction in the same school of farmers, or rather of sailors, who from the Moon's ascent to the zenith or the highest point of the sky, readily foreknow the access of marine waters, and through the same Moon's descent from the meridional circle to the cardinal points, the reciprocation of those waters and their reflux to the ocean, and know how to predict them to others with precision, although they are rude in calculations, and those marine tides, due to the more rapid disjunction of the luminaries, do not observe fixed times. But if these rural matters still seem too subtle and incomprehensible to anyone, I do not see why he should not be instructed by the school or examples of brute animals. For some animals are known to habitually observe and presage the more significant effects of the heavens. Hence the wisest poet Hesiod, the ant, because it is a small creature

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Division of the Work and Order of Teaching
text: PRAEFA TIO. ortus fimul exoriatur, quibus in locis luminaria reliquaque fi dera teneatur, & quomodo inter ſe colligata aut diſiuncta fint, & talia fimilia inuenire, Aſtronomi eſt officium. Quod verò apud Ptolemeum dicitur, eos qui naſcuntur Luna & Mercurio inconiun&is, Saturno perdiu, aut Marte noctu ad horoscopum ſcandente, prædi&amq; figurationem intuente, plerunquefieri epilepticos, ad Aſtrologie coniectationem pertinet. Vtriuf que artis inter ſcriptores princeps eſt Claudius Ptolemæus,qui cæteris elegantius & verius has artes prodidit: is tamen propter rerum fubtilitatem altitudinemque, & præcipue in hac par te breuitatem nimiam, non ita multum profuerit rudi lectori, qui prima elementa prius non cognouit ex libellis iſagogicis, quorum præcipui funt: in Aſtronomia Procli & Georgij Purbachij: In Aftrologia Alcabicij, cuius breuia & obfcurius quedam dicta in gratiam huius artis Tyronum latius exponere, & exemplis, quando res ita poſtulauerit, explanare conabimur. translation: And the division in all things, and especially in handing down arts, is most necessary: for it brings light to things, and wonderfully aids memory, so that without it hardly any understanding or memory can exist. Order, moreover, is the arrangement of many conclusions, which constitute a certain discipline, in suitable and appropriate places. For since that from which a discipline is composed is manifold, order is necessary, otherwise confusion results, so that young people, because of the difficulty, become disgusted with the art, or at least are led to its knowledge later. Therefore, the author rightly, before the exposition of the precepts, indicates the order he will follow in expounding them. Furthermore, it is easy to judge how much the author's order differs from or agrees with Ptolemy's order.

Arabs eſt hic ſcriptor, & interpres eius Hiſpalen ſis, cuius oratio , etiamſi non ſit tam nitida, vt delectare eruditos poſſit: cùm tamen reliquos, qui Arabū ſcripta olim in latinú cõuerterut, facilè ſuperet, ferendū boniqs conſulendū ſtudiũ ipſius putaui. Adhæc cùm hac inſtru Ețione non tantú ad Ptolemæi, verùm etiã & præcipuè ad omnium Arabum doctrinã intelligendã lectore inſti tuere valuerit, fortes inanes, decurias, nouenarias, duodenarias, ſerdarias, & talia à phyſica doctrina diſſentientia, & ne ab ipſo quidẽ probata, ſimul cum veris introducere neceſſum babuit. Nosverò, quibus non tam multorũ huius generis ſcriptorũ, quam vera & natu ralis artis diligens indagatio curæ fuit, his ſimilibus, quæ metas veræ artis enagatur, obiter aſpectis, & mox reiectis, nihil præter ſinceræartis præceptoru perſpicu am explicatione & vbiq; ad Ptolemæi ſententia collatione quæſiuimus. Reliquas verò otioſas & inanes opini ones, etiamſi tanquãm ab arte alienas reſecādãs eſſe paſi ſim oſtenderimus; eas tamen, cùm aliorű ſcripta integra emittendæ eſſe probè intelligeremus, nõ eiecimus, atq; ipſas quoque vel æconomine vtiliter extareatque ab incautioribus aſpici poſſe vel debere putauimus, vt inde ve ra à falſis, phyſica à vanis faciliùs diſcernantur, & quid Ptolemæus αριθμός, κλήρος καὶ τὰ ἀπαιτιολόγητα nominet, re ctius intelligatur.
