Etſi ve. rò ea, quoq; a à me iuuaūdi ſtudia iuuentutis gratia hic adie cta ſunt,ego minimè ita expolita eſſe agnoſcere, vt cen ſura iudicioque multitudinis absq; formidine aliqua cõ mitti poſſent:tamẽ cùm librariorũ præſtantißimus vir, Arnoldus Birckman, veſtræ reip.ciuis honeſtißimus, cu optimo ſene Domino Conrado Tegemanno diue Vrfule Canonico, quem præter inſignem ſacrarum literarú peri tiã non tantú Mathematum, totiusq; huius philoſophiæ minimè rudem, ſed & multarũ aliaru admirabiliu natu, ra actioni conſciu comperi, cum alijs naturæ ſtudioſiffi mis minimèq; vulgaribus hominibus, vt typis excuſa pu blicarentur, me adhortari non deſiſterent, nõ duxi ipfis repugnadum in ea re, quam vel ſoli Antonio Rinco viro & nobilitatis generisq; nomine eximio, & præter pulcherrimarum virtutum, optimarum artium impensèſtu dioſo, apud quem tam diu diuerfor, nullo pacto negare po tuiſſem. Ab ipso ego ſepè, & tu maximè, dum ſicubi nos aliorum negotiorũ fatietas capit, de cælo, de ſtëllis, alijs que naturæ rebus admirabilibus colloquimur, fibi mole ſtissimü eſſe intellexi, quòd aſtrologia artis omninõ præ. ſtantißimæ nullam ſinceram, ſed vel obſcurã vel nanus oppletã principiorum tractatione haberet: qua re quan tum bono iudicio valeret & quàm ſolida virtutis cupidus eſſet, ſignificare mihi viſus eſt. Res magnæ eſt, inges . 2 genus Digitized by Google I EPISTOLA genus à proauis, ſed maior laus nataliū ſplendore codecor rare eximia doctrina, cuius impetus etiãſi de cælo, vt di xi,ſit, cùm tamẽ & corporú & ingeniorú aliqua ſimili. tudine à parentibus in ſobole propagari ſcia,idcirco pa tri ipſius Ioanni Rinco viro clarißimo(qui & ab anti qua & adprimè nobili familia ducit originem, & ob mi ram ingenij dexteritatem, qua liberalia ſtudia ab ineun te ætate tractauit, Doctoris vtriuſq; iuris inſignia nulo lius lucri cauſa, ſed ſaltem vt vrbis & Academiæ, in qua mirabilem in modum prælucet, ſit decori,iamdiu geſtat, cùm eum iam ſenem & ingraueſcente ætate, in qua ta men valetudine firma eſt, & robur militare tuetur,ma gnoperè amare & colere omnes bonas artes & res in natura præſtantißimas vnïcè admirari viderem) non ſi ne gratia aliquando ſum gratulatus de hærede, qui non tantum patris nobilem familiam, ſed etiam patrem vir tute referre queat. Quare peritos & candidos lectores ſpe ro boni conſulturos eſſe, quòd tractatiõẽ rei nobilissimæ, morem geſsi nobilißimis hominibus & amicis, qui mihi autores fuerunt, vt pulcherrimæ de motibus corporum cœleſtium doctrinæ, quæ ex certis & perpetuã ratis nu marorum & Geometriæ demonſtrationibus extruitur, proximè ſequentem partem phyſicæ, quæ ſtellarú cœli vires,actiones & effectus, admirabili & haud dubiè di uinitus monſtrata ratione explicantur,adiungerem,atq; hac de primis artis principijs iam à me ad vtilitatem in uentutis collecta ederem. Veſtræ autẽ, amplißimi ſplen didißimiq; viri prudentia, quæ in delectuxerũ perſo narumas Digitized by Google
translation: This little creature adapts its operations to the change of lunar splendor by a wonderful natural instinct, the same, that is, to name the knowing, and to mark it with this bare epithet. And it is commonly known that many kinds of birds sense changes in the weather, and because of this, before winter, they fly in flocks to warmer places, and when spring approaches, they return for nesting. Now, if brute animals and uneducated men, ignorant of the powers of the stars and the places in the heavens where they are, can foresee and observe more significant effects proceeding from more efficacious causes, it is an injustice to deny the same sagacity to artists, and madness not to attribute greater skill and dexterity in judgment. Therefore, it is manifest from what we have briefly said that studious and skilled men, who, instructed in the philosophy of natural things, have learned to deduce consequent effects from antecedent causes, and moreover have a thorough understanding of the nature of celestial places and stars, and finally are so instructed in calculation that they can infallibly place before their eyes the position of the entire heaven at any given time and the certain epochs of the stars, can judge and predict, by physical consideration, without entering into any futility of opinions, the proper state of the air surrounding us, and the proper temperatures of mixed bodies, and what follows from these in natural order. But it has been shown that the doctrine of previsions is felicitous, admirable, a demonstration of divinity, and useful both in political and economic matters, and indeed for acquiring, preserving, and increasing private goods of all kinds. Therefore, it seemed to me not undeservedly most worthy that, freed as much as possible from the sorceries and other innumerable trivialities with which it is commonly overwhelmed, insofar as it agrees with natural actions, to use Ptolemy's word, "according to fitting philosophy," I should propose it to liberal minds for observation and study. And since it is the most excellent of all, and lies buried under the calumnies of so many sycophants, I commend it to men outstanding in wisdom and authority, and to the guardians of the liberal sciences, especially since this argument is by no means alien to the Mathematics, which you wished me to publicly illustrate in your most celebrated Republic's Academy, with the proposal of an honorable annual stipend from the public treasury. And no one will easily deny that the artful method of previsions, by which more important and serious unforeseen events are commonly foreseen, is most fitting for the providers of great things. But since in the doctrine of numbers, figures, and celestial motions, and in all other disciplines, we are accustomed, by what I believe to be a good and useful custom of the schools, to propose the very beginnings of precepts to youth, so that from there there may be easier access to more subtle discussions of things, therefore, desiring to promote and adorn this part of Physics as well, we have endeavored, explaining Alcabitius's Isagoge (because we knew that nothing in this genre had been produced more correctly by others), to discuss all the precepts that seemed necessary for acquiring and practicing the true art, not so artfully and perspicuously, but, as it is said, with a coarser Minerva. This writer is an Arab, and his interpreter a Spaniard, whose language, even if it is not so polished as to delight the learned, yet since it easily surpasses the others who formerly translated Arabic writings into Latin, I thought his zeal should be tolerated and taken in good part. Moreover, since this instruction could not only instruct the reader to understand Ptolemy, but also and especially the doctrine of all Arabs, it was necessary to introduce empty lots, decanates, novenaries, duodenaries, serdaries, and such things dissenting from physical doctrine, and not even approved by him, along with the true ones. But we, who have been concerned not so much with many writers of this kind as with a diligent investigation of the true and natural art, having cursorily viewed and then rejected these similar things that deviate from the goals of true art, have sought nothing but a clear explanation of the precepts of sincere art and a comparison everywhere with Ptolemy's opinion. But as for the other idle and empty opinions, even if we have often shown that they should be cut off as alien to the art, nevertheless, since we well understood that the writings of others should be emitted intact, we did not cast them out, and we thought that they too could or should exist usefully for economy or be seen by the incautious, so that from them the true may be more easily distinguished from the false, the physical from the vain, and what Ptolemy calls "number, lot, and the inexplicable" may be better understood. For just as in all other disciplines, for the sake of sharpening and strengthening judgment, it is useful to compare one or another of the more important writers, so we believe that this comparison of the Arabs with Ptolemy's doctrine can also bring some acuteness to young people. It seemed easier to write a new work of this kind, but it is more useful to adapt the first institution to the common treatment of Greek materials. And although these things, which I have added here for the sake of helping the studies of youth, I acknowledge are by no means so polished that they could be committed to the censure and judgment of the multitude without any fear: nevertheless, since the most excellent bookseller, Arnold Birckman, a most honorable citizen of your republic, with the excellent old man Master Conrad Tegemann, Canon of St. Ursula, whom, besides his remarkable knowledge of sacred literature, I found to be not only not ignorant of Mathematics and all this philosophy, but also conscious of many other admirable actions of nature, with other most studious and by no means common men of nature, did not cease to exhort me that they should be published in print, I did not think I should resist them in a matter which I could not have denied in any way even to Antonio Rinco alone, a man eminent by name of nobility and lineage, and besides being intensely studious of the most beautiful virtues and best arts, with whom I so long sojourned. From him I have often, and you especially, when the satiety of other business seizes us somewhere, conversing about the heavens, about the stars, and other admirable things of nature, understood that it was most troublesome to him that the most excellent art of astrology had no sincere, but either obscure or vainly filled, treatment of principles: by which he seemed to me to signify how much good judgment he possessed and how desirous he was of solid virtue. It is a great thing, a lineage from ancestors, but a greater praise to adorn the splendor of one's birth with excellent doctrine, whose impetus, even if it is from heaven, as I said, since it is known that both bodies and minds are propagated from parents in offspring by some similarity, therefore I have sometimes congratulated his father, John Rinco, a most distinguished man (who both derives his origin from an ancient and most noble family, and because of the wonderful dexterity of his genius, with which he cultivated liberal studies from an early age, has long borne the insignia of Doctor of both laws, not for the sake of any gain, but at least that he might be an ornament to the city and the Academy, in which he shines in a wonderful way, since I saw him now old and in advanced age, in which however he is in firm health and maintains military vigor, greatly loving and cultivating all good arts and uniquely admiring the most excellent things in nature) not without gratitude for an heir who can resemble not only his father's noble family but also his father in virtue. Therefore, I hope that skilled and candid readers will take it in good part that I, in treating a most noble subject, have complied with most noble men and friends who were my authors, that to the most beautiful doctrine of the motions of celestial bodies, which is constructed from certain and perpetually established demonstrations of numbers and geometry, I should add the immediately following part of physics, in which the powers, actions, and effects of the stars of heaven are explained by an admirable and undoubtedly divinely shown reason, and that I should publish these things concerning the first principles of the art, now collected by me for the benefit of youth. But your prudence, most ample and splendid men, which in the selection of persons

text: DIFFERENTIA illis habeant, quæ & antecedunt, & quæ ſequuntur. Vnde li quet, ſigna vniuſcuiuſque quartz, tali ratione & modo diſtingui,quemadmodum generalis ſingulorum anni temporum có- titutio variatur. Si tamen omnia curioſius ad ordinem,quem fuprà ex Ptolemeo propofuimus, referenda funt, dicendum eſt, quòd in hoc capite recenſeantur quedam ſignorum proprietates fimplices, fingulis dodecatemorijs per ſe peculiariserq; inſitz, feu vt Ptolemeus loquitur, αἱκατὰ τὸ ἀμιγές αὐτλί μόνωμ δωδεκατημορίωμ καθ᾽ αὐτὰ δυνάμεις. Sunt qui exiſtimét, hanc partitionem ſumptam eſſe à mobilitate, ſtabilitate & mediocri duratione rerum, quæ ipfis autaſcendentibus inchoantur, aut planetis ibidem collocatis fignificantur. Vnde qui de electionibus præceperunt, putant omnia generaliter, quæ σιμα fragiliaque eſſe velimus, exordienda eſſe Luna diſcurrente per ſignum aliquod mobile:In rebus verò firmioribus & diutiùs perennaturis, attendendum eſſe ad ſigna fixa:In rebus-denique pauciores annos duraturis, indifferentia communiaque Igna eſſe obferuanda. Sed nos talia non curamus. DE ASPECTIBVS PLANETARVM. ■ Dicuntur etiam figna ſe afpicere, boc est: Omne fig num afpicit tertium ante ſe, tertium post ſe, quod est vndecimum. Et hic afpectus dicitur ſextilis, & est afpe Etus dilectionis & medie amicitia. Et dicitur etiam fextilis, eò quòd teneat fextam partem circuli, id eft, LX. gradus. Verbi gratia: Planeta qui fuerit in initio Arietis, afpicit eum qui fuerit in Geminis ante fe, eu qui fuerit in Aquario poſt ſe, qui eſt aſpectus amicitia. Et eft afpectus diſcordiæ atque medie inimicitia. 3 Et afpicit quoque quintum fignum ante ſe, & quin tum poft ſe, quod est nonum. translation: And if two planets are in one sign, they are said to be conjoined.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Dedication to the Republic
text: DEDICATORIA. narumq; ad pulcherrimam imaginem beatæ Reipub.ex primendam pertinentium, plurimum valetis, hanc meam lucubrationem dedicandam duxi, cùm ob alias cauſas, tum verò ob liberalitatem inſignem, quam & olim à vo bis munificè ſenſi, & ia continenter experior, poſtquam quicquid eſt hoc oneris ſeu muneris, quod pro mea tenui tate in celeberrima veſtra Academia tueor ac ſuſtineo. Neque verò veritus ſum, vt ne hac mea gratificandi vo luntas, vobis ſatis apta aut grata videretur,cùm maxi mis difficilimisque rebus Curiæ occupatos, rem li terariam ſeriò inſpicere, atque conſtitutis totius philo ſophiæ, linguarum verò quotquot publicè exerceri pro deſt,& denique aliarum facultatū omnium idoneis per ſonis & doctoribus, celebritate Academiæ augere cer namus omnes: & ob id in eam opinionem fiduciamque adducamur, vt exiſtimemus minimè fieri poſſe, vt veſtra autoritate iudicetis indignam earum artium acceptati onem, quarum aſsiduum in vrbe tractatione ad Reipub. admirationem plurimum conducere putatis. Hinc verò veſtrorum animorum celſitudinem conſiliorumq altitu dinem æſtimare nos decet. Nam ad hunc modum Reip. prouidentes, ad gubernatorú munus vtrumq; pertinere, & vt corpora ciuit defendatur, & vt animi eorú imbua tur erudianturq; diſciplinis optimis, nõ tantu vos intelli gere oſtenditis, verum etiã re ipſa haud obſcurè declara tis. Nanicularij naues voragini illatas aut fluctibus ocea ni diutiùs vexatas, vix ſeruare poßut: At vos in ſpatio ſo bello per Europam inſtar flammiferæ furiæ graßano 3 te, quo Digitized by Google ==Start of OCR for page 1== EPISTOLA te, quo plurimorum ſedes aut vastatas, aut ambuſtas vidi mus, ciuitate veftram & ciues incolumes conferuare potuistis. Anno verò ab hinc circiter tertio, in caritate an nonæ maxima, cum loga fames fic vicinis locis orbus eßet, frumentu granarys veftris exportatu, ciuibus ve ftris fuffeciffe,& vicinorū pluri plurimos fame perituros feruaße ipfi vidimus. Tales & fimiles res in Repub. indies infigniter geste, etiamfi vobis eximiam & maximam laudem pariant, omnes tamen hominum celebratione cosinentur. Verum beneficia, quæ per conferuatione Aca demie ad ciues, ad vicinos, & denique ad vndique cir. cumiacentes regiones tranfmittitis, mente omnia conci. pere difficile eft, oratione aliqua certè fatis laudari ne. queunt. De his tantis virtutibus, ad quas omnes, qui ad gubernacula fedent,ferid inuitandi & diligenter exci. tandi funt, vos qui eas iam partes poßidetis, vt deinceps integras retinere ſtudeatis, folummodo commonefacio. Taceo etia iam liberalia premia, quibus vt Vniuerfitas Vniuerfarum artiu cuftos iurè dici ſemper mereatur, col lapfas profesionum ſtationes, vos feriò instaurare, vel meo exemplo omnibus fatis conftare potest. Nam ego hac tranfiens, cùm afpecta forma fcholæ omnes partes ip ruditionis prater Mathematum difciplinas, quas in ple rifque fcholis, vel neglientia, vel inaniu rerum fuperflua cura de lana capriciana philofophātiŭ extinxit,pulchrè Sonare audire, cœpi bic harum artiu, quas fcholam defi derare videba, initia in gratiam ſtudioſa iuuentutis ex. ponere, & fimul meam operam, ſi à vobis fchole ptilis fu tura ==End of OCR for page 1== ==Start of OCR for page 2== DEDICATORIA. tura iudicaretur, ipfi non defutură fignificaui. Id fimul at que reclè intellexißetis, non tantum ſtatio in fchola, adincto annuo folario mihi à vobis addicta eft, verùme. tiam pro onere, quod fpontè in ſchola ad tempus fubieră, bonorificentißimu munus tributú eft:pro quo vobis, qua licunq; iam oportunitate oblata gratias ago, fimula ro go, vt me meag ſtudia vobis deinceps commedata eße ve litis. Sed iam epistolam finiam tali claufula:Si mæniorű, vrbis ciuiung, nuda defenfione, quidam gubernatores, multorum gratiam virtutuma eximiarum laude fibi cõ parare potuerunt, non facilè dictu eft, quantum fauori: gratitudinis lauduma debeant ciues veftri vobis, qui inter refpublicas quietas, vrbem pacatißima retine. tis, & Germanie Academiaru folaic Germania Academiaru ſolam ciuitatis alumnam fouetis. Valete feliciter. Datum in republica veftra, An no falutiferi partus M.D.LX.in equinoctio verno Iulij & Augufti CAESS.hoc eft, die XXV. Marty, quo CHRISTVS DEVS RISTYS DEVS in vtero beata Maria Virginis, nata ex regia ftirpe Das uidis, humanam na turam aßum plit. Errata. Cr.verſu quarto, lege ardentem pro occidentem. Item lege priufque pro priufquam. Item lege τρόποι pro Jinpis ==End of OCR for page 2== ==Start of OCR for page 3== ::. ==End of OCR for page 3== ==Start of OCR for page 4== PRAEFATIO AVTORIS. Oftulata à Domino prolixitate vita,glas dj regni durabilitatefui honoris ſtodia quoque operum eius & bonorum, atque extenfione fui imperij:exordiamur id quod volumus narrare. 2 Cùm vidiſſem conuentum quorundam antiquorum ex autoribus, magiſterij de iudicijs aftrorum edidiffe li bros, quos vocauerunt huius magiſterij, id eft,iudiciorum de aftris introductorios:fed quofdam ex eis non fuiſſe Scrutatos fatis diligenter vniuersa de his, quæ introdus Etorio conueniunt, inq; eodem magisterio funt neceſſa. ria:quofdam verò ea que neceſſaria funt, protuliffe pro lixè,adeò vt quod neceſſarium est, in eo peruſſe iure vi. deri poßit: quofdam denique in ordinatione eorum que protulerunt, non inceßifſſe itinere difciplinæ confpexif. Sem, cæpi edere hunc librum. 3 Et nominaui eum introductorium, & non introdu xi ratiocinationes difputationi fiue defenfioni eorum que protulimus neceffarias, cùm fint in libro Ptolemai, qui appellatur quatuor tractatuŭ:& in libro meo, quem edidi pro confirmatione magiſterij iudiciorum aftro. rum:& in deſtructione epiſtole Haiſſen ben Hali, quæ confutata iacet prolatis rationibus, quæ ad hoc poſſunt suficere. 4 Et diuiſi eum in quinque differentias:Prima diffe. rentia eft de eſſe circuli fignorum effentiali acciden. tali. Secunda differentia de naturis planetarum ſeptem, A quid ==End of OCR for page 4== ==Start of OCR for page 5== 2 PRAEFATIO. quid illis proprium, & quid fignificent. Tertia diffe. rentia eft de his, que accidunt planetis ſeptem in femet ipfis,& quid accidat eis ad inuicem. Quarta differentia verfatur in expofitione nominum Aftrologicorum. Quin ta differentia in vniuerfitate partium expofitione ef. Se earum in gradibus. Duæ funt difciplinæ teſte Ptolemzo præcipuz & maximz, neceſſariz ad przuidendos & præcognoſcendos futuros euentus, qui caufas & proprietates rerum naturali & diuinitus inſtituto ordine conſequuntur. Vna eſt do&rim de motibus cœlorum corporumque cœlestium:alia diuinatrix feu μαντική. Veteres vtramque nomine seu Aſtronomie, seu Aſtrologiæ complectuntur, sed recentiores vocibus, quæ olim idem fignificabant, distinctis, do&rinam de motibus Aſtronomiam,diuinatri cem verò Aſtrologiam nominant. Est autem Aſtronomia scientia omnium certifsima. Nam per Geometricas & Arithmeticas demonſtrationes siderum conſtantes & certos motus, poſitus, magnitudines, diſtantias cùm à terra, tum à ſe inuicem perferutatur. Aftrologia verdeſtars, qua sagaciter & modo philoſophię naturali conſentienti, præuidemus & præcognofcimus effetus & fignificationes, quæ hunc vel illum siderum poſitum tanquam caufam efficientem neceſſariò comitantur, omnino neceſſaria ad præuidendas τας ευκρασίας καί δυσκρασίας aliquado in corporibus elementorum exorituras, & præuidenda talia accidentia, quæcunque vel corporis noftri, vel animi temperaturis iuxta communem naturę cursum inhærent, & obid vtiliſsima ad augendam conferuandamque valetudinem hominum,adtuendam rem familiarem, atque deniq; ad præcogno- ſcendas conſtitutiones aéris, quibus corpora tam inanimatoruin quam animatorum cuidenter affici compertum eft,& argumentis rerum doceri poteſt.Hinc perſpicuŭ eſt, inter Aſtrologiam & Aftronomiam id intereſse, quòd hæc fcrutetur ſtellarum situs, ortus, occaſus, progreſſus, ſtationes & configuratio nes: illa verò ex his omnibus ſtellarum affectionibus futura præuidcat. Vt quæ pars coeli vel ſtella in principio alicuius ex ortus ==End of OCR for page 5==
translation: DEDICATION. ...to the most beautiful image of the blessed Republic, which you are very capable of expressing, I have decided to dedicate this work of mine, both for other reasons and especially for the remarkable generosity which I have experienced from you in the past and continue to experience, since whatever burden or duty I bear and sustain in your most celebrated Academy, according to my humble ability. Nor indeed was I afraid that this willingness of mine to please would not seem suitable or pleasing enough to you, since we all see that you, occupied with the greatest and most difficult matters of the Curia, seriously inspect literary matters, and augment the celebrity of the Academy with suitable persons and scholars for all philosophy, and for as many languages as are publicly exercised, and finally for all other faculties: and for this reason we are led to the opinion and confidence that it is impossible for you to judge the acceptance of those arts unworthy by your authority, whose assiduous practice in the city you believe contributes greatly to the admiration of the Republic. Hence it is fitting for us to estimate the loftiness of your minds and the depth of your counsels. For by thus providing for the Republic, you show not only that you understand that it pertains to the duty of governors both to defend the bodies of citizens and to imbue and instruct their minds with the best disciplines, but you also declare it clearly by your actions. For sailors can scarcely save ships caught in a whirlpool or long vexed by ocean waves: But you, in the space of a war raging like a fiery fury throughout Europe, in which we have seen the homes of many either devastated or burned, have been able to preserve your city and citizens unharmed. And about three years ago, in a time of great scarcity, when a long famine afflicted neighboring places, we ourselves saw that the grain exported from your granaries sufficed for your citizens, and saved many of your neighbors who would have perished from hunger. Such and similar deeds, remarkably performed daily in the Republic, even if they bring you exceptional and greatest praise, are nevertheless contained by the celebration of all men. But the benefits which you transmit through the preservation of the Academy to citizens, to neighbors, and finally to regions everywhere surrounding, are difficult to conceive entirely in the mind, and certainly cannot be sufficiently praised by any speech. Concerning these great virtues, to which all who sit at the helm should be seriously invited and diligently exhorted, I only admonish you who already possess these parts, that you strive to retain them intact hereafter. I also pass over the generous rewards by which, so that the University may always deservedly be called the guardian of all arts, you seriously restore collapsed professional positions, as can be sufficiently confirmed by my own example. For as I was passing through here, when I saw the form of the school, and heard all parts of erudition, except for the mathematical disciplines, which in most schools either negligence or the superfluous care of philosophers disputing about trifles had extinguished, beautifully sounding, I began here to expound the beginnings of these arts, which the school seemed to lack, for the sake of studious youth, and at the same time I signified that my labor, if it were judged useful to the school by you, would not be wanting. As soon as you rightly understood this, not only was a position in the school, with an annual salary, assigned to me by you, but also a most honorable gift was bestowed for the burden which I had voluntarily undertaken in the school for a time: for which I now thank you, whenever an opportunity arises, and at the same time I ask that you be pleased to commend me and my studies to you hereafter. But now I will end the letter with such a clause: If some governors, by the mere defense of the walls, the city, and the citizens, have been able to acquire the favor of many and the praise of extraordinary virtues, it is not easy to say how much favor, gratitude, and praise your citizens owe to you, who maintain a most peaceful city among quiet republics, and foster the only alumna of German academies among German academies. Farewell happily. Given in your republic, in the year of the saving birth 1560, at the vernal equinox of July and August CAESARS, that is, on the 25th day of March, on which CHRIST GOD in the womb of the blessed Virgin Mary, born of the royal lineage of David, assumed human nature. Errata. In the fourth verse, read ardentem for occidentem. Also read priusque for priusquam. Also read τρόποι for Jinpis.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Rays
text: DIFFERENTIA illis habeant, quæ & antecedunt, & quæ ſequuntur. Vnde li quet, ſigna vniuſcuiuſque quartz, tali ratione & modo diſtingui,quemadmodum generalis ſingulorum anni temporum có- titutio variatur. Si tamen omnia curioſius ad ordinem,quem fuprà ex Ptolemeo propofuimus, referenda funt, dicendum eſt, quòd in hoc capite recenſeantur quedam ſignorum proprietates fimplices, fingulis dodecatemorijs per ſe peculiariserq; inſitz, feu vt Ptolemeus loquitur, αἱκατὰ τὸ ἀμιγές αὐτλί μόνωμ δωδεκατημορίωμ καθ᾽ αὐτὰ δυνάμεις. Sunt qui exiſtimét, hanc partitionem ſumptam eſſe à mobilitate, ſtabilitate & mediocri duratione rerum, quæ ipfis autaſcendentibus inchoantur, aut planetis ibidem collocatis fignificantur. Vnde qui de electionibus præceperunt, putant omnia generaliter, quæ σιμα fragiliaque eſſe velimus, exordienda eſſe Luna diſcurrente per ſignum aliquod mobile:In rebus verò firmioribus & diutiùs perennaturis, attendendum eſſe ad ſigna fixa:In rebus-denique pauciores annos duraturis, indifferentia communiaque Igna eſſe obferuanda. Sed nos talia non curamus. DE ASPECTIBVS PLANETARVM. ■ Dicuntur etiam figna ſe afpicere, boc est: Omne fig num afpicit tertium ante ſe, tertium post ſe, quod est vndecimum. Et hic afpectus dicitur ſextilis, & est afpe Etus dilectionis & medie amicitia. Et dicitur etiam fextilis, eò quòd teneat fextam partem circuli, id eft, LX. gradus. Verbi gratia: Planeta qui fuerit in initio Arietis, afpicit eum qui fuerit in Geminis ante fe, eu qui fuerit in Aquario poſt ſe, qui eſt aſpectus amicitia. translation: But when a planet is in some sign, its rays will be in those signs that aspect that sign in the same degree and minute. For example, if Mars is in the first degree of Aries, and in the first minute of the same degree, its rays will be in the first degree of the sign Libra, and in the first minute of the same degree: and thus understand the other aspects, as is most clearly evident in this figure.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Preface to the Reader
text: PRAEFA TIO. ortus fimul exoriatur, quibus in locis luminaria reliquaque fi dera teneatur, & quomodo inter ſe colligata aut diſiuncta fint, & talia fimilia inuenire, Aſtronomi eſt officium. Quod verò apud Ptolemeum dicitur, eos qui naſcuntur Luna & Mercurio inconiun&is, Saturno perdiu, aut Marte noctu ad horoscopum ſcandente, prædi&amq; figurationem intuente, plerunquefieri epilepticos, ad Aſtrologie coniectationem pertinet. Vtriuf que artis inter ſcriptores princeps eſt Claudius Ptolemæus,qui cæteris elegantius & verius has artes prodidit: is tamen propter rerum fubtilitatem altitudinemque, & præcipue in hac par te breuitatem nimiam, non ita multum profuerit rudi lectori, qui prima elementa prius non cognouit ex libellis iſagogicis, quorum præcipui funt: in Aſtronomia Procli & Georgij Purbachij: In Aftrologia Alcabicij, cuius breuia & obfcurius quedam dicta in gratiam huius artis Tyronum latius exponere, & exemplis, quando res ita poſtulauerit, explanare conabimur. Sed alterum & precipuum noſtrum in haccommentatione erit ſtudium, vt hæc Alcabicii, quæ ad traditiones Arabum intelligendas precipuè deſeruiunt, ad ſententiam Ptolemei, quam om nibus veris artificibus probari video, diligenter conferamus. Speramus autem hunc noſtrum laborem fore nec ingratum nec infrugiferum ſtudioſis adoleſcentibus. Nam ex his noftris inftitutionibus, non tantùm omnia & neceſſaria huius philoſophię pręcepta cognofcere, fed & vera à falfis fuperftitiofis, τοῖς αἰαιτιολογήτοις diiudicare,& in quibus Arabes Ptolemaum fecuti fint, in quibus ipſum reliquerint, & quæ denique abfurda commenti ſint, facilime intelligire poterunt. &c. • Ab inuocatione incipit more veterum, qui etiam in ſoluta oratione ſicut poetæ ab inuocatione exordiri folent.Demoſthe. Poftulata nesaduerfus Aefchinem pro Chteſiphonte : πρῶτομμύὦαΐà Dorina δρες Αθηναῖοι τοῖς θεοῖς ἔυχομαι πᾶσι καὶ πάφεις ὅσίω ἔννοιαμ ἔχωμἐγὼ διατελῶ τῇτε πόλει καὶ πᾶσιμὑμῖμ, τασάντῶ ὑπάρε ξαι μοι παρ' ὑμῶμείς τγτονὶ ὁμἀγῶνα, hoceſt: Primum quidem viri Athenienfes Deos atque Deas omnes precor, vt quantum beneuolentię ipſe ſemper habui & erga hanc vrbem & erga vos, tantum mihi à vobis preſtetur in preſenticertamine. Et Lycurgus in Leocratem : ἔυχομαι τῇ Αθηνᾷ καὶ τοῖς ἄλλοις A 2 θεοῖς Digitized by Google PRAEFATIO. 4 θεοῖς κίτοῖς ἥρωσι τοῖς κατὰ τὼ πολίμ κὴ χώραμ ίδρυμένοις, δες Cum vidif 2 Occaſio ſcribendi. Dictio contextuſque orationis barbara ſem, &c. funt, ſed ſententia & res ipſa, que ſola in hocautore vbiq; fpe- @abimus,meliora.Etfi, inquit, non defint, qui in Aftrologiam Iſagogas ſcripſerunt, plerique tamen aut res neceſſarias non comprehenderunt, aut prolixitate orationis odioſa eafdem ita obſcurarunt, vt cùm rudiori lectori nihil profint, totam fuam. operam luſiſſe videantur:aut denique nulla habita ratione ordinis & viæ docendi, precepta con fuſe cogeſſerút. Ob hanc cau ſam compulſum ſe eſſe ait, vt Aftrologiæ ſcribat Iſagogen Methodicam, quæ omnia neceſſaria precepta contineat, nihilý; in ea inutile aut fuperuacaneum perperamúe fit poſitum. Etnomi. 3 Intentio autoris. In omnibus artibus duo funt genera donaui cum, cendi. Vnum vocant Tök, quo rem ſimplicissime nudiſque &c. verbis nulla facta mentione caufarum oftendimus. Alterum Si ou quod accurata difputatione cauſas præceptorum rimatur. Veluti cùm in arte medendi febricitantibus abſtinétia vini pręcipitur, primi generis preceptum eft:fed cum additur ratio, quòd potio vini augeat bilioſæ materiæ incendium, ad pofterius genus pertinet. Adeundem modum quando in elementis. Sphericis definitur ſignifer, quòdfit circulus magnus, fecans. translation: It is the duty of the Astronomer to discover the rising of the stars, in what places the luminaries and other stars are held, and how they are connected or disjoined among themselves, and similar things. But what Ptolemy says, that those who are born with the Moon and Mercury disjoined, with Saturn by day, or Mars by night ascending to the horoscope, and beholding the aforesaid configuration, often become epileptic, pertains to the conjecture of Astrology. The chief writer in both arts is Claudius Ptolemy, who has handed down these arts more elegantly and truly than others: yet he, because of the subtlety and profundity of the matters, and especially in this part, his excessive brevity, will not have benefited much the unlearned reader who has not first learned the first elements from introductory books, of which the principal ones are: in Astronomy, those of Proclus and George Purbach; in Astrology, those of Alcabitius, whose brief and somewhat obscure sayings, for the sake of the beginners in this art, we will endeavor to explain more broadly, and illustrate with examples, when the matter so demands. But our other and principal aim in this commentary will be to diligently compare these teachings of Alcabitius, which serve especially for understanding the traditions of the Arabs, with the opinion of Ptolemy, which I see approved by all true practitioners. We hope, moreover, that this labor of ours will be neither ungrateful nor unfruitful to diligent young students. For from these our instructions, they will not only learn all the necessary precepts of this philosophy, but also be able to distinguish the true from the false and superstitious, the 'unreasoned', and easily understand in what respects the Arabs followed Ptolemy, in what they departed from him, and what absurdities they finally invented. And so on. He begins with an invocation in the manner of the ancients, who even in prose, like poets, are accustomed to begin with an invocation. Demosthenes, in his speech against Aeschines for Ctesiphon: 'First, indeed, O men of Athens, I pray to all the gods and goddesses that as much goodwill as I have always had both towards this city and towards all of you, so much may be granted to me by you in this present contest.' And Lycurgus in Leocrates: 'I pray to Athena and the other gods and heroes established throughout the city and country.'

title: Ptolemy's Astrological Principles
text: PRAEFATIO. iudicare. Vniuerfaliter in tra&atione Aftrologica, aut partes, hoc eſt, longitudo, latitudo, & ſubſtantia ſituſque cœli perpenduntur, horumque omnium vires examinantur: aut ſtellarum poteſtates propriæque effectiones explorantur : aut denique hæc duo inter ſe conferuntur. Ptolemæus ergo in quadripartita Coſtru&ione primd qua lis vniufcuiufq-ſtellæ inſita propriaq; natura per ſe exiſtat exponit, ac deinde veluti ſecundo loco eorundem naturam aduenticiam comparatione ad ſolem ediſſerit. Tertiò ponit ſignorum generalem conſtitutioné, quam à temperatura quatuor anni temporum capiunt. Hinc quædam verna, quædam æſtiva, quædam autumnalia, & deniq; quzdam hyemalia ſigna nominantur. Subdit ibidem naturam quatuor cæli angulorú, quibus pariter tam ſtellæ quam ſigna comparari debet. Quarto recitat ſignorum conſtitutiones ab vniufcuiuſque peculiari proprietate atq; inſita natura deſumptas, quo pacto alia tropica, alia equinoctialia, alia bicorpora, alia rurfus maſculina,alia fœminina, & denique alia quadrupeda, alia humana, alia terre- ſtria, aut aliter denominata vocantur. Quinto enumerat proprietates ſignorum, quæ ex collatione ipforum erga ſe inuicé oriuntur. Qua de cauſa quædam imperantia, quædã obedientia, quędam diametro quadrangulo, trigone, ſexagone,alijfque modis ſeſe contuentia, & deniq; quædã inconiuncta ſigna appellantur. Expofitis ita qualitatibus vniufcuiufq; feorfum, ſexto tádem atq; vltimo loco ſtellarum adſigna comparationem inſtituit,atq; partes coeli alias Soli, alias Lunæ, alias erronibus alijs domo, altitudine, trigone & finibus conſentire docet. Ad extremű habitudines ſingularŭ ſtellarŭ quatuor modis examinandas eſſe tradit. Primò ex propria natura ſtellę vniufcuiufq;, ſecundò à natura ſigni in quo conſtiterit: tertiò à configuratione ad Solem: quartò à configuratione ad cardines. Verba eius, quibus primum librú principia artis continentem claudit, hec funt:cn δι'τότωμ απάντωμ ἐυστεύσπλου, ὅτι τὸ μὲν ποιόμ εκας γτ ας έρωμἐπισκεπτέομ ἔκτε τ' ἰδίας αὐτὴν φυσικής ίδιο τροπίας, καὶ ἔτι τῆς την ποριεχόντωμ δωδεκατημορίωμ, καὶ τ την πρός τε όμἥλιομ καὶ τὶς γωνίας χηματισμώμ. Tali ordine præcepta Aftrologica expoſuit Ptolemæus, que hic Digitized by Google PRAEFATΙΟ. 7 νε hic autor reliquit, atque nonnihil immutauit, ſed ita tamen, ve mutatio hæc non alicuius momenti incommodum lectoriadferat. Et ſanè, fi quod res habet dicendum eſt, hæc quoque via docendi, quæ hic proponitur facilitatis, & perſpicuitatis ratio nem habet. Primo enim loco variam oftendit diſtributionem ſigniferi, ſignorumque conſtitutiones gonerales, quas ab anni quatuor temporibus & ſemeſtribus capiunt, breuiter ſtrictimque exponit.Proximum erat & Ptolemai methodo magis con ſentiens, fingularum ſtellarum proprias naturas exponere.Sed autor his in ſequentem locum, nempe ad ſecundam differentiam dilatis, mox ipſa ſigna ſtellis cœlorumq; ſtationibus comparat, atque quemadmodum generales illæ fignorum conftitutiones præſentia ſtellarum & ratione diuerfi in cælo poſitus promoueantur diminuanturúe edocet. Veluti ſigni Leonis vis calida intenditur præſentia Martis aut Solis. Econtrario ve rò diluitur, ſi corpori aut radio Saturni commiſceatur. Similiter maior eſt vis ſignorum cardinantium quam ſuccedentium. Etrurfum horum maior virtus quàm cadentium comperta eſt. Hinc perſpicuum eſt, vnius eiufdemque Leonis vel etiam ſigni alterius naturam atque conſtitutionem diuerfarű ſtellarum commiſtione, & ratione variarum poſitionum ad Meridionale & finientem non manere ſemper eandem, ſed mul tipliciter variari. Et hoc eft quod dicit autor, ſe ſcripturum in prima differétia de eſſe ſignorum eſſentiali & accidentali. Nam generalem ſignorum conftitutionem atque eorundem infitam & innatam proprietatem, ſimiliterque incrementa vel decrementa virium, quas à Sole & ſtellis capiunt, nominat ſignorum vim eſſentialem. Ptolemeus verò vocat cam ἐνέργφαμκατὰ το κοσμικόμ, hoc eft, ſecundum mundum diſpoſitionem: intenGones verò vel remiſsiones qualitatum, quæ ſignis ratione diuerfarum ſtationum cæli contingunt, nominat autor accidentia,Ptolemæus verò τζώκατατιὼ γεύεσιμ ἢ ἄλλίω αρχιω ἐνέργειαμ, hoc eſt, diſpoſitionem quæ in genitura aut in alio rerum exordio ſignis euenit, nuncupat. Id verò non tantùın de ſignis, ſed etiam, & multo magis de ſtellis ſimiliter accipiendum eft. Quemadmodum igitur in prima differentia fignorű, itacodem modo in ſequétibus dù Thν πλακα τον και τα τε αυτό Digitized by Google ==Start of OCR for page 1== DIFFERENTIA κοσμικέμ, καὶ κατά γεύεσιμ διάθεσιμ, hoc eft, planetarum infitas temperaturas atque proprietates, & harum virium incrementa decrementaq;, quę tam ſecundum mundi quàm genituræ ſtatú capiunt, enarrat. Sanè in ſecunda differentia ſtellarum innatas temperaturas & peculiares, propriaſq; fingulis ſignificationes ordinat. In tertia differentia eafdem cùm ad æquinoctialem & eclipticam, tum verò ad ſuas adidas & paruos circulos, deinde verò tres ſuperiores & reliquos inferiores primo ad luminaria, & inde inter ſe, & denique ad centra & reliquas cælorum ſtationes comparat. Quarta differentia continet expoſitioné vo cabulorum artis & modos operandi, quibus in exponendis a- ſtrorum effectibus frequenter vtuntur iudices. Quinta differentia confumitur enumeratione vniuerfarum partium, ſeu forcium, quibus Arabes præter ſententiam Ptolemæi & extra artem,vt opinor, effectus & fignificationes quafdam affinxerunt. Hæc ſumma præceptorum eſt, quorum fingula eodem ordine Deo auxiliante examinabimus. DIFFERENTIA PRIMA DE DIVI- ſione Circuli ſignorum. 1 Nitach, id est, Circulus fignorü diuiditur in 12. par. tes equales, ſecundum diuifionem circuli ſignorum, & hæ 12.partes dicuntur ſigna. 2 Et referuntur ad imagines, quæ funt fub eodem cir culo Zodiaco, quæ funt: Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, Leo, Virgo, Libra, Scorpius, Sagittarius, Capricornus, Aquarius, Piſces. Et vnūquodq; iftorum ſignorú diuüdi tur in 30.partes equales, quæ gradus vocantur. Et gradus diuiditur in 60.minuta,& minutum in 60. ſecunda, & ſecundum in 60.tertia. Similiter quæ ſequuntur quarta ſcilicet & quinta aſcendendo vſque ad infinita. 3 Et ex his ſignis ſeptem ſunt ſeptentrionalia, id est, ab ==End of OCR for page 1== ==Start of OCR for page 2== PRIMA. ab initio Arietis vfq; in finem Virginis, quæ funt ſepten trionalia à linea æquinoctiali, & ſex meridiana, id est, ab initio Libræ vſque in finem Piſcium. 4 Et ſex ex illis dicuntur directè aſcendentia, id est, ab initio Cancri ad finem Sagittarij: Et ſex tortuoſe afcendentia dicuntur, id est, ab initio Capricorni vfque in finem Geminorum. 5 Et tortuoſe aſcendentia obediunt directè aſcenden tibus, hoc est, duo figna que fuerint vnius longitudinis à capite Cancri, obediunt fibi, vt Gemini Cancro, Taurus Leoni, Aries Virgini: Et Piſces Libræ, Aquarius Scorpioni, & Capricornus Sagittario. Et duo figna que fuerint erint vnius vnius longitudinis longitudinis à à capite capite Arietis, dicuntur cõ- cordantia in itinere, vt Aries & Piſces, Taurus & Aquarius, Gemini & Capricornus, Cancer & Sagittarius, Leo & Scorpio, Virgo & Libra. 6 Et medietas circuli, quæ est ab initio Leonis vſq; in finem Capricorni, vocatur medietas maxima, & est medietas Solis, quia Sol in omni hac medietate habet *principium,ficuti habent Planeta in ſuis terminis Et *id est, alia medietas quæ est ab initio Aquarij vſque in finem tum feu Cancri, vocatur minima, & est medietas Lunæ, quia Lu Poteftate, na fimiliter habet in omni hac medietate principium, fi cut Solin maxima. 7 Et vocatur illa medietas, quæ est ab initio Arietis, vſque in finem Virginis,medietas calida. Et alia que est ab initio Libræ, vſque in finem Piſcium, vocatur me dietas frigida. Et vocatur illa quarta pars circuli, quæ B est ab principali ==End of OCR for page 2==
translation: PREFACE. to judge. Universally in astrological treatment, either the parts, that is, the longitude, latitude, and substance and position of the heavens are considered, and the forces of all these are examined; or the proper powers and effects of the stars are explored; or finally, these two are compared with each other. Ptolemy, therefore, in his Quadripartite Construction, first explains what the inherent and proper nature of each star is in itself, and then, as if in the second place, he sets forth their adventitious nature by comparison with the Sun. Thirdly, he establishes the general constitution of the signs, which they derive from the temperament of the four seasons of the year. Hence some are called vernal, some aestival, some autumnal, and finally some hibernal signs. He then adds the nature of the four angles of the heavens, to which both stars and signs should be equally compared. Fourthly, he recites the constitutions of the signs derived from the peculiar property and inherent nature of each, by which means some are called tropical, some equinoctial, some bicorporeal, others again masculine, others feminine, and finally others quadrupedal, others human, others terrestrial, or otherwise denominated. Fifthly, he enumerates the properties of the signs which arise from their mutual collation. For this reason, some are called commanding, some obedient, some in diameter, square, trine, sextile, and other ways beholding each other, and finally some are called unconnected signs. Having thus explained the qualities of each separately, in the sixth and last place, he institutes a comparison of the stars to the signs, and teaches that some parts of the heavens agree with the Sun, others with the Moon, others with the other planets by house, exaltation, trigon, and bounds. Finally, he states that the dispositions of individual stars are to be examined in four ways. First, from the proper nature of each star; secondly, from the nature of the sign in which it is constituted; thirdly, from its configuration to the Sun; fourthly, from its configuration to the angles. His words, with which he concludes the first book containing the principles of the art, are these: "From all these things it is evident that the quality of each star is to be examined from its own natural peculiarity, and from the dodecatemoria surrounding it, and from its configurations to the Sun and the angles." Ptolemy expounded astrological precepts in such an order, which this author here (Digitized by Google) left, and somewhat changed, but in such a way that this change does not bring any significant inconvenience to the reader. And indeed, if the truth must be told, this method of teaching, which is proposed here, also has the advantage of facility and clarity. For in the first place, he shows the varied distribution of the zodiac, and briefly and concisely explains the general constitutions of the signs, which they derive from the four seasons and half-years. The next step, and more consistent with Ptolemy's method, would have been to explain the proper natures of individual stars. But the author, having deferred these to a later place, namely to the second difference, immediately compares the signs themselves with the stars and the stations of the heavens, and teaches how those general constitutions of the signs are promoted or diminished by the presence of the stars and by reason of their diverse positions in the heavens. For example, the power of the sign Leo, which is hot, is intensified by the presence of Mars or the Sun. Conversely, it is diluted if it is mixed with the body or ray of Saturn. Similarly, the power of cardinal signs is greater than that of succedent signs. And again, the virtue of these is found to be greater than that of cadent signs. Hence it is clear that the nature and constitution of one and the same Leo, or even of another sign, does not always remain the same due to the mixture of different stars and by reason of various positions to the Meridian and the setting point, but varies in many ways. And this is what the author says, that he will write in the first difference about the essential and accidental being of the signs. For he calls the general constitution of the signs and their inherent and innate property, as well as the increases or decreases of powers which they receive from the Sun and stars, the essential power of the signs. Ptolemy, however, calls it "energeia kata to kosmikon," that is, disposition according to the world; but the author calls the intensifications or remissions of qualities which happen to the signs by reason of different stations of the heavens, accidents, while Ptolemy calls it "energeia kata ten genesin e allon archen," that is, the disposition which occurs to the signs in a nativity or in another beginning of things. And this is to be understood not only of the signs, but also, and much more, of the stars in a similar way. Therefore, just as in the first difference of the signs, so in the following ones (Digitized by Google)

Nam eſt in domo Martis, & in domicilio. Nunc fes 11 Redditus ſeu potius reſtitutio virtutis fit, cùm inferior quitur afpicit ſuperiorem combuftum aut retrogradum, qui effectus reddi. &c. debilis remittit inferiori virtutem quam ſibi miſerat: vt fit in 8,& afpiciat combuſtuum aut retrogradum in Capricorno. In hoc caſu committit naturam fuæ altitudinis Mercurio:ſed quia Mercurius eſt combuſtus, non poteſt retinere naturam libi commiſſam, idea reddit ei lumen, quod ab eo acceperat. Quando verò vtiliter & quando cum detrimento fratre-. ſtitutio, patet euidenter in textu.. Hic Venus vaditado, verum ſi partem 8.vel 9.attingens: ante copulam cum Saturno incipiat retrocedere, refrenabitur huiusmodi caſu & à copulatione Saturni, mutabiturque hæc, que primo aſpectu videbatur ſimplex applicatio, in ſeparationé mutuam, ſi videlicet Venere retrocedente, Saturnus progre diatur. Eft enim defluxus mutuus, cùm planeta qui eſt in pluribus partibus, eſt directus, vt fuprà dictum eft: qui verò paucio, res tenet partes, eſt retrogradus.Idem verò eſt iudicium de co figurationibus alijs, ſi nimirum vadat planeta ad aſpectum,fed. antequam eum affequatur, incipiat retrocedere.. translation: 8. DIFFERENCE. But if there is a collection of light, when two planets are not plainly conjoined, but both are conjoined to some third planet: then that third planet collects the light of both, and receives their nature, and projects it to the parts of the zodiac which it aspects by radiation, in which places any planet existing receives the light of the aforesaid two. For example, let the Sun be in Aries and Venus in Virgo, not seeing each other by any aspect, but Mars, traversing Cancer, sees the Sun from Aries and Venus from Virgo. In this case, Mars collects the lights of the Sun and Venus, and projects them to the places of their rays, or even to the planets existing there. That is, Mars, as a medium, conjoins the Sun and Venus, who are not conjoined by themselves, and renders or attributes their light both to places and to stars which it aspects. Again, if 7. In another way, the return of light, or the conjunction of two unconjoined stars, is said to occur if some third planet mixes with two planets that do not touch each other with rays, so that separating from one, it carries its light to the other to which it is conjoined. But this case does not differ from the translation of light, of which we spoke a little earlier. It follows that 8. He teaches that the conjunctions of planets are impeded in two ways. First, when three planets traverse one sign, if the slower planet is between the two lighter ones: then that middle planet prohibits their light from being communicated. For example, if Jupiter is in the 1st part, the Moon in the 11th part, and Saturn in the 5th part. In this case, Jupiter seeks the Moon, but Saturn prohibits this conjunction, who, being in the middle and closer, repels Jupiter and himself receives the Moon. If such a conjunction of the lighter planets would be bad, then the prohibition is useful and helps; but if good, it harms. Secondly, when two planets are in one sign, if the lighter of them is conjoined to the heavier, and a third planet strikes the same heavier planet with an equally near ray, that is, if the ray of the aspecting planet and the body of the planet existing in the same sign are equally distant from the heavier planet, then the one who is conjoined by body prohibits the aspecting planet from the aspect. But if the rays of the aspecting planet approach closer to the heavier planet, the society of the planet existing in the same sign being repelled, the conjunction of the aspecting planet with the heavier planet will be perfected. As if Saturn is in the 10th part, Mars in the 6th part of the same sign, Jupiter in the 6th or 11th part, casting his rays to the 6th part, then Mars is conjoined to Saturn by body, being repelled from Jupiter's aspect. On the contrary, if Jupiter is placed in the 7th part, irradiating the 7th part with a trine, he prohibits Mars from Saturn, and himself conjoins with Saturn, which is especially true if the one who prohibits is faster in motion than the one who is prohibited. But if the one who is prohibited is faster than the prohibitor, and will reach the body of the heavier planet sooner, the opposite seems to be true, since in this matter, everything that is potentially such, is understood to be actually such. 9. Pulsation first occurs when a planet is in some dignity, that is, in the house, exaltation, triplicity, or term of another planet, and the aspecting planet is said to pulsate, that is, to send the nature of that dignity to the lord himself, whose dignity it is: for the lord of the place is always understood to receive his virtue from the aspecting planet: as if the Sun is in Aries, aspecting Saturn traversing Virgo with a trine, then the Sun will send the nature of the house of Saturn to Saturn himself, the lord of the place. Similarly, if a light planet existing in one of its said dignities aspects another heavier planet having no dignity in the place where the light planet is: then the light planet transmits its own nature to the heavier and peregrine planet: as if the Moon is in the 7th part of Taurus, and Saturn in the 7th part of Virgo. Here the Moon sends its strength or virtue of exaltation to Saturn, although Saturn has no dignity in the Moon's place. 10. Reception occurs when a planet existing in its dignity aspects another who obtains dignity in the same place: as if Mars is in Aries, and constituted in his house and exaltation, sees Venus in Pisces, then Mars communicates both natures, namely his own and that which he obtains by reason of the house, to Venus. But if the inferior planet exists in the dignity of the superior, and vice versa the superior in the dignity of the inferior, there is a mutual and perfect reception, as in Aries and Libra, they receive each other by mutual reception. For Mars is in the house of Venus, and Venus in the domicile of Mars. Now follows 11. The return or rather restitution of virtue occurs when an inferior planet aspects a superior planet that is combust or retrograde, who, being weak, remits to the inferior planet the virtue which it had sent to him: as if the Moon is in Taurus, and aspects Mercury, combust or retrograde, in Capricorn. In this case, the Moon commits the nature of its exaltation to Mercury: but because Mercury is combust, he cannot retain the nature committed to him, therefore he returns to the Moon the light which he had received from her. When, however, the restitution is useful and when it is detrimental, is clearly evident in the text. Then follows 12. Refranation from a conjunction or aspect occurs when an inferior planet seeking the conjunction of a superior planet, is already retrograde, or because before it is conjoined partilely with the heavier planet, it will become retrograde. As if the Moon is in the 12th part of Virgo, and Saturn in the 5th part of Virgo. Here Venus is direct, but if reaching the 8th or 9th part, before the conjunction with Saturn, she begins to retrograde, she will be refranated in such a case from the conjunction of Saturn, and this, which at first sight seemed a simple application, will be changed into a mutual separation, if, namely, Venus retrograding, Saturn proceeds direct. For there is a mutual deflux, when the planet which is in more parts is direct, as was said above: but the one which holds fewer parts is retrograde. The same judgment applies to other configurations, if, namely, a planet goes to an aspect, but before it reaches it, begins to retrograde. This follows 13. When three planets are in the same sign, of which one is heavy, and the other two are lighter, so that one of the lighter planets has passed the heavy one, and the other seeks the conjunction of the same, but before it is conjoined to it, the one who passed the heavy one becomes retrograde, and by retrogradation is conjoined to the first heavy one: then passing the same, he also conjoins with the lighter one, and in this way destroys the conjunction of the former with the heavy one: as if Jupiter is in the beginning of the sign, Mars in the 8th part, and Saturn in the 10th part, retrograding. Here Jupiter seeks Saturn, but before he reaches him, Mars, having become retrograde in the 10th part, is first conjoined, and then meets Jupiter, and thus destroys Jupiter and Saturn. The author calls this contrariety; Cardan calls it perturbation. Perturbation, he says, occurs when two planets apply to each other, as to their natural motion: but there is a good third planet: a planet in more parts than both, who, before the first two are conjoined, either because he is already retrograde, or because he will become retrograde a little later, conjoins himself to the slower, and interposes himself between the first two, and thus perturbs the conjunction of the first planets. 14. Frustration from a conjunction or aspect occurs when a light planet seeks the conjunction of a heavy planet, but before it is conjoined to it, the heavy planet enters another sign, at the beginning of which are the rays of another planet, which aspects that sign. These rays, first meeting the heavy planet, then the light one, can frustrate the conjunction of the light planet with the heavy one in two ways: for example, let Mars be in the 29th part of Leo, and Jupiter in the 19th part of Leo, seeking the conjunction of Jupiter, but before he comes to him, Jupiter enters Virgo, at the beginning of which we imagine the rays of Saturn, who, first receiving Jupiter, then Mars, frustrate the conjunction with Jupiter. 15. The meaning is: When a light planet seeks the conjunction of a heavy planet, but before it reaches it, another planet is conjoined to that heavy planet by retrogradation, and thus cuts off the light of the first from the heavy planet: as if the Moon is in the 29th part of Taurus, Jupiter in the 24th part of Virgo, and Mars in the 1st part of Virgo, retrograding, looking at Saturn very closely from Virgo. In this case, Jupiter seeks the conjunction of Saturn, but before he completes it, Mars, by retrocession from Virgo, is conjoined partilely to Saturn, and thus cuts off the light of Saturn from Jupiter. Here the third planet, namely the retrograde one, or its rays, are placed at the beginning.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Zodiacal Quarters and Planetary Order
text: ==Start of OCR for page 3== DIFFERENTIA Nitach. est ab initio Arietis vſque in finem Geminoram, quarta calida, humida, vernalis, puerilis, fanguinea. Et illa que est ab initio Cancri vfq; in finem Virginis,dicitur quar ta calida, ficca, æstivalis,iuuenilis cholerica.Et illa quæ eſt ab initio Libræ vſque in finem Sagittary, dicitur quarta frigida, ficca, autumnalis, melancholica: & figni ficat initium diminutionis mediocris ætatis. Et illa quæ eſt ab initio Capricorni vſque in finem Piſcium, dicitur quarta frigida, humida, defectiua, ſenilis, hyemalis, flegmatica. 8 Omnibus autem planetis qui feruntur in his ſignis, (9 non quòd fint in eis, fed quia feruntur ſubeis, hoc eſt, in directo eorum) altior & propior circulo ſignorum & curſu tardior eſt Saturnus, deinde Iuppiter, deinde Mars, deinde Sol, deinde Venus, deinde Mercurius, deinde Luna, quæ est terræ propior & curſuomnibus velocior. 10 Significantur autem etiam per caput Draconis & caudam cum planetis quædam ſignificationes, ficut exponemus in ſequentibus. • Principio partitur ſigniferi longitudinem in 12.partes, quas latini ſigna, Græci à numero δωδεκατημόρια vocant,vt numerus eorum numero menſium anni ſynodicorum reſpondeat. Nam Luna ſpatio 29 dierum, ſolem ſua velocitate ſemper aſſequitur, eidemque ſingulis annis ſolaribus duodecies, hoe eſt, in ſingulis dodecatemorijs ſemel coniungitur. Quòd verò quidam anni nobis 13, lunationes oftendunt, id reuera non tempus annuum, fed 11. dierum, quo ſpatio temporis circuitus ſolis duodecim luminum congreſſus ſuperat, intercalationes. efficiunt. Hac ratione Arabes moti, annuo temporis ſpatio 354.dies ==End of OCR for page 3== ==Start of OCR for page 4== PRIMA 354.dies attribuerunt. Nam dies 29.quibus luminaria congre diuntur, duodecies collecti, dies 354. efficiunt.Sed Aegyptij & C.Iulius Cæfar neglecto menſtruo Lunæ curſu, periodico mo eu Solis anni metas concluſerunt. Verùm nec hi per omnia inter ſe conſentiunt. Nam Aegyptii & Perſæ diebus 365.tantum, Iulius verò diebus 365. & diei quadrante, id est, horis ſex,annum ſolarem finiuerunt. Sed nos hac varietate annorum quæ alibi explicatur, relicta, ad ſequentes partitiones figniferi tranſeamus. Is fanè in minores arcus, quàm funt ſigna, cùm eſſet ſecādus, vtiliſsimum videbatur, ſi eum in tantas partes diſtribuerent, quantas Sol diurnis curriculis peragrat. Verùm enimuerò cùm numerus dierum anni, qui eſt 365.ſupputationibus minimè conueniret, partiti ſunt Aſtronomi, non folum ſigniferum, verùm etiam in vniuerſum circulos cæle- ſtes omnes in partes 360. qui numerus numero dierum anni tropici ſeu vertentis proximus eſt, & pluribus partibus componentibus ſeu multiplicibus, veluti ſemiſſe, triente, quadrante, parte quinta, ſextante, beſſe, dodrante, & aliis ſequentibus conftat, atque ob id omnis generis ſupputationes facile admittit. Iam verò ſi partes 360. per 12. partiamur, deprehendemus vnumquodque δωδεκατημόριομ ſeu fignum figniferi tricenis conſtare gradibus. Etenim fingulorum ſignorum tricenas partes æquis diuifionibus partitas, iuniores Aſtronomi gradus, veteres verò latini ſcriptores partes, Gręci denique μοίρας appellant. Et herclè hæc quoque vnius ſigni in trecenas partes partitio ad motum Solis & Lunæ congruit. Nam cùm ſpacium temporis, quo Luna à Sole digreſſa, eundem rurſus aſſequitur, comprehendat fere 30. dies, idcirco pars cœli duodecima, quam Sol interea ſuo motu emenſus eſt, à natura ipſa intelligitur ſecari in partes totidem, quas recentiores à Solis greſſu diurno gradus, vt dixi, non abſurde vocant. Ex his perſpicuum eſt, quòd in potioribus partitionibus ſigniferi, nempe in duodena ſigna, ſignorumque tricenas partes, ſapiens antiquitas ipfam naturam ducem ſecuta ſit. Cæterùm quod fubiungit autor, fingulas partes veluti integra rurſus dirimi in ſexaginta minuta ſcrupula, & vnum ſcrupulum primum in ſexaginta ſcrupula ſecundaria, vnum ſeB 2 cun- ==End of OCR for page 4== ==Start of OCR for page 5== 10 DIFFERENTIA cundum in 60. tertia, fimiliterque deinceps ad quarta, quinta, ſexta, & quæ ordine alia ſequuntur ſcrupula, Mathema ticam partitionem calculo id ita exigente progredi, id non à natura, ſed ab arte conſtitutum eſt. Has cæli minutas partes vo cant Græci λιστα μεξήκοςα πρῶτα, δέυτερα, τριτά, τετάρτα, πιμπτά, έκτα, επτα, ſicque deinceps. Sed in aſsignandis motibus vltra ſcrupula prima progredi, curiofitatis eſt non neceſſariæ, cùm minutæ cœli partes omnino ſufficiant. Nec ignoro ad eruendos ex tabulis ſtellarum motus veros, ſequentia minima quoque ſcrupula vtiliter obſeruari. In hoc quoque ἵυxοντά- δωμ, id eſt, ſexagenarum numero veteres eandem calculandi commoditatem ſpectarunt, quam in numero 360. qui & ipfe ſextantibus circuli ſeu ſexagenis graduum ſex,vt dictum eſt, componitur. Quapropter ſexagenarium reliquis numeris om nibus pretulerunt. Nam inter minores numeros integrum cir culum componentes, nullus habet tot partes multiplices ſicut ſexagenarius. Primò enim conſtatur ex ſemiſſe, id est, ex 30.deinde ex triente, id eſt, 20. tertiò ex quadrante, id eſt, 15. quartò ex parte quinta, id eſt, 12. quintò ex ſextante, id eſt, 10. ſextò ex parte decima,id eſt, 5. octauò ex parte decimaquinta, id eſt,4.nond ex parte vicefima, id eſt, 3. denique ex parte triceſima, id eſt, 2. Proinde artifices nó ſolùm gradus circuli tanquam integra in minutissimas partes ſexagefimas perpetua partitione ſexagenaria, ficut dictum eſt, attenuant, verumetiam ipſa integra eodem ſexagenario continenter amplificantes, εξηκονταδες, id eſt, ſexagenas integrarum partium coagmen tant: veluti ſextans circuli, qui duo dodecatemoria, ſeu 60. partes continet, vnam ſexagenam primam conſtituit.Et ſexaginta ſexagenæ primæ, id eſt, decem integri circuli vnam ſexa genam ſecundam efficiunt, & ſic vlterius aſcendendo, prout vfus calculi poſtulauerit.In tali numeratione in medio con- ſtit gradus vel alia rerú forma, cui vim integri aſsignat ſupputantis cogitatio. Vtrinque collocantur. ſexagenæ, ac verſus dextram quidem antecedunt ſexagenæ collectæ ex integris: verſus ſiniſtram verò ſequuntur ſexagefimæ ſcrupulorum mi nutorum.Sed huius rei in logiſtica ſcrupulorum aſtronomicorum maior vſus & latior tractatio habetur.. 2 Ad ==End of OCR for page 5==
translation: The first quarter, from the beginning of Aries to the end of Gemini, is hot, humid, vernal, youthful, and sanguine. The quarter from the beginning of Cancer to the end of Virgo is called hot, dry, aestival, juvenile, and choleric. The quarter from the beginning of Libra to the end of Sagittarius is called cold, dry, autumnal, melancholic, and signifies the beginning of the diminution of middle age. The quarter from the beginning of Capricorn to the end of Pisces is called cold, humid, defective, senile, hiemal, and phlegmatic. Among all the planets that are carried in these signs (not that they are in them, but because they are carried beneath them, that is, directly below them), Saturn is higher and closer to the circle of the signs and slower in its course, then Jupiter, then Mars, then the Sun, then Venus, then Mercury, then the Moon, which is closer to the Earth and swifter than all in its course. Certain significations are also indicated by the Head and Tail of the Dragon with the planets, as we will explain in the following sections. First, the length of the zodiac is divided into 12 parts, which the Latins call signs, and the Greeks call dodecatemoria, so that their number corresponds to the number of synodic months in a year. For the Moon, in a space of 29 days, always overtakes the Sun with its speed, and is conjoined with it twelve times each solar year, that is, once in each dodecatemorion. The fact that some years show us 13 lunations is not truly due to the annual time, but to the 11 days by which the Sun's circuit exceeds the twelve conjunctions of the luminaries, which cause intercalations. Moved by this reasoning, the Arabs attributed 354 days to the annual period. For 29 days, in which the luminaries are conjoined, collected twelve times, make 354 days. But the Egyptians and C. Julius Caesar, neglecting the monthly course of the Moon, concluded the limits of the year by the periodic motion of the Sun. However, even these do not agree in all respects. For the Egyptians and Persians ended the solar year in only 365 days, while Julius ended it in 365 days and a quarter of a day, that is, six hours. But leaving aside this variety of years, which is explained elsewhere, let us proceed to the following divisions of the zodiac. It seemed most useful to divide it into smaller arcs than the signs, if they were to distribute it into as many parts as the Sun traverses in its daily circuits. But indeed, since the number of days in a year, which is 365, was not at all suitable for calculations, astronomers divided not only the zodiac, but also all celestial circles in general into 360 parts, which number is close to the number of days in the tropical or turning year, and consists of many component or multiple parts, such as a half, a third, a quarter, a fifth part, a sixth, two-thirds, three-quarters, and others that follow, and for this reason easily admits all kinds of calculations. Now, if we divide 360 parts by 12, we will find that each dodecatemorion or sign of the zodiac consists of thirty degrees. For the younger astronomers call the thirty equally divided parts of each sign degrees, while the ancient Latin writers call them parts, and the Greeks call them moirai. And indeed, this division of one sign into thirty parts also corresponds to the motion of the Sun and Moon. For since the period of time in which the Moon, having departed from the Sun, again overtakes it, comprises almost 30 days, therefore the twelfth part of the sky, which the Sun has meanwhile traversed by its motion, is understood by nature itself to be cut into as many parts, which the more recent (astronomers) not absurdly call degrees from the Sun's daily progress, as I said. From these it is clear that in the more important divisions of the zodiac, namely into twelve signs, and the thirty parts of the signs, wise antiquity followed nature itself as its guide. Moreover, what the author adds, that each part is again divided as if whole into sixty minutes, and one first minute into sixty secondary minutes, one second into 60 third minutes, and similarly thereafter to fourth, fifth, sixth, and other minutes that follow in order, that mathematical division proceeds as calculation demands, this is established not by nature, but by art. The Greeks call these minute parts of the sky first, second, third, fourth, fifth, sixth, seventh, and so on. But to proceed beyond the first minutes in assigning motions is a matter of curiosity, not necessity, since the minute parts of the sky are entirely sufficient. Nor am I unaware that to extract the true motions of the stars from tables, even the following smallest minutes are usefully observed. In this sexagesimal system, that is, the number of sixty, the ancients also looked for the same computational convenience as in the number 360, which itself is composed of six sextants of a circle or sixty degrees, as was said. Therefore, they preferred the sexagesimal system to all other numbers. For among the smaller numbers composing a whole circle, none has so many multiple parts as sixty. For first, it consists of a half, that is, 30; then of a third, that is, 20; thirdly, of a quarter, that is, 15; fourthly, of a fifth part, that is, 12; fifthly, of a sixth, that is, 10; sixthly, of a tenth part, that is, 5; eighthly, of a fifteenth part, that is, 4; ninthly, of a twentieth part, that is, 3; and finally, of a thirtieth part, that is, 2. Therefore, practitioners not only attenuate the degrees of a circle as if whole into the smallest sexagesimal parts by perpetual sexagesimal division, as was said, but also continuously amplify the integers themselves with the same sexagesimal system, forming sexagesimals, that is, sexagesimal groups of integer parts: just as a sextant of a circle, which contains two dodecatemoria, or 60 parts, constitutes one first sexagesimal. And sixty first sexagesimals, that is, ten whole circles, make one second sexagesimal, and so on, ascending further, as the use of calculation may require. In such a numbering system, the degree or other form of things is placed in the middle, to which the calculator's thought assigns the force of an integer. Sexagesimals are placed on both sides, and to the right precede the sexagesimals collected from integers; to the left, however, follow the sexagesimal parts of the minute scruples. But the greater use and broader treatment of this matter is found in the logistics of astronomical scruples.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Saturn and Journeys
text: . 4 J 9 Quod dicit Saturnum ſignificare peregrinationes longin. Primus ſtatus ab exortu quo radios Solis exuunt, capit principium, & ad primam quadraturam extenditur. translation: What is said about Saturn signifying long journeys, prisons, bonds, slowness, and affliction, and similar impediments to travel, should be understood thus: Saturn threatens these evils if he hostilely aspects the places of journeys (such as those of the Moon, Sun, Mars, and the Part of Fortune, according to Ptolemy) when they are in the seventh, ninth, or third house, and succeeds them. Ptolemy's words in Book 4, Chapter on peregrinations, are these: "If benefics aspect or succeed these aforementioned places, they decree honorable, glorious, and profitable journeys, and swift and unimpeded returns. But malefics decree laborious, harmful, dangerous, and impeded returns." These mentioned places, that is, those held by the Moon, or the seventh, ninth, or third house, if aspected by benefics and succeeded by them, decree honorable, clear, and profitable journeys, and swift and expeditious returns. But malefics decree laborious, harmful, dangerous, and impeded returns.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Constellations and Astrological Characters
text: ==Start of OCR for page 4== i 1 16 DIFFERENTIA Camerarius complexus eſt verſibus, quos propter elegantiam hîc adſcripfi. Nomina diſce puer veterum ſtudioſe laborum Syderea, & cæli cognofce micantia ſigna: Signa vocant, quibus illa vides infignia templa, Sed numerum demum quatuor atque o to eſſe dederunt. Vrſa ſumma tenent Aquilonis frigora, per quas Ingens in morem fluuij perlabitur Anguis. Hunc ſubit Arctophylax, ſed & hunc mox gemma Coronæ, Inde Genu Nixus, poſt quem Lyra, deinde Volucris. Tum Cepheus & Caſtrope, tum Vector & ille Falcatum extollens cæſa enſem gorgone Perſeus. Hinc Deltoton, & Andromede, hinc ſtas Pegaſe, ſed te Delphin excipit, hunc Telum, hoc Aquila, Anguitenenſq;: Signifer hæc ſequitur decliui circulus orbe, In quo Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, Leo, Virgo, Chelæ, Scorpius, Arcitenens, Caper, Amphora, Piſces. At Notus vndifero tenet hæc fimulach ra ſub axe, Armatum Oriona, Canem in quo Syrius ardet. Quis Procyong; Lepuſq; & Iaſonis additur Argo, Hydruſq; & Centaurus & Ara incognita nobis, Tum notius Piſcis, Cetuſq; ac Eridani vnda. Priores verſus ſolum 19. ſimulachra exprimere videtur, ſed duo ſigna duplicant numerú. Pegasus enim hic ſignificat equi ſectionem, ſeu equum primum, & Pegaſum ſeu equum ſecundum. Similiterq; vox Anguitenens in ophiuchū & ſerpentem ophiuchi tribuenda eſt. Poſteriores quoque verſus vndecim fatem videntur nominare ſidera. Sed Centaurus duplicat numerum. Num lupus ſeu beſtia quam Centaurus geſtat, à Centauro diſtinctum eſt ſidus: Hydrus verò triplicat numeru, Nam dorſo crateram & coruum ſitientem ſuſtinet. His adijcienda eſt Corona auftrua, quam alij γρανίσκομ, id eſt, paruum cœlum, alii retam ixion is vocant. In vniuerſa hac tractatione appellationes ſignorum, graduum ſcrupulorumque, planetarum, nec non aſpectuú ſeu radiationum, & interlectionum, quas planum luna cum plano ſolis eſticit, ſepissime erunt viurpande. Quapropter ſubiicimus hic ſignifica- ==Start of OCR for page 5== PRIMA. ſignificationes characterum, quibus omnia iſta compendio citraque lectoris tædium exprimere deinceps poterimus. Saturnus, 4 Iupiter, Mars, Sol, & Venus, Mercurius, Luna, Φαίνωμ ἢ κρόνο. Φάεθωμ ἢ ζεὺς. πυρόεις καρης. ἵλιθ. ἀφροδίτκι φωσφόρο. ἕρμας ἢ τίλβωμ. σελήνη. CHARACTERES SPATIO. rum cæli. Sig. Signum, δωδεκατημόριομτοιχειομ ἢ ζώδιομ. P. 1. 2. 3. 4. Pars ſeu gradus μοίρα. Minutum ſen ſcrupulum primum, λεπτόμ πρώτομ, ſc.1. Secundum ſcrupulum, δεύτερου λεπόμ, ſc. Tertium ſcrupulum, τριόμλεπόμ, ſc.3. Quartum ſcrupulum, τέταρτομ λεπόμ, ſc.4. M.C.Medium cœli,μεσγράνημα. translation: Camerarius embraced these verses, which I have transcribed here for their elegance. Learn, studious boy, the names of the ancient labors, the starry ones, and recognize the shining signs of the heavens. They call those signs by which you see those temples distinguished, but they finally gave the number four and eight. The Great Bear holds the cold of the North, through which a huge Serpent glides like a river. Arctophylax follows this, but also the gem of the Crown, then Genu Nixus, after which Lyra, then Volucris. Then Cepheus and Cassiopeia, then Vector and Perseus, raising his scythe-like sword against the Gorgon. From here Deltoton, and Andromeda, from here you stand Pegasus, but the Dolphin receives you, this one the Arrow, this one the Eagle, and Ophiuchus. The Ecliptic follows these with its sloping orbit, in which are Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, Leo, Virgo, Libra, Scorpio, Sagittarius, Capricorn, Aquarius, Pisces. But the South holds these images under its axis: armed Orion, the Dog in which Sirius burns. Who is Procyon? And Lepus? And Jason's Argo is added, and Hydrus, and Centaurus, and the Altar unknown to us, then the more familiar Pisces, Cetus, and the waters of Eridanus. The former verses seem to express only 19 images, but two signs double the number. For Pegasus here signifies the section of the horse, or the first horse, and Pegasus or the second horse. Similarly, the word Ophiuchus is to be attributed to Ophiuchus and the serpent of Ophiuchus. The latter eleven verses also seem to name the stars. But Centaurus doubles the number. For the wolf or beast that Centaurus carries is a distinct constellation from Centaurus; Hydrus, however, triples the number. For on its back it carries the Crater and the thirsty Corvus. To these must be added the Southern Crown, which some call γρανίσκομ, that is, a small heaven, others call it retam ixionis. In this entire treatise, the appellations of the signs, degrees, and minutes, of the planets, as well as the aspects or radiations, and the intersections which the plane of the moon forms with the plane of the sun, will very often be used. Therefore, we here subjoin the significations of the characters, by which we can henceforth express all these things concisely and without tiring the reader. CHARACTERS OF THE TWELVE SIGNS OF THE ZODIAC. Northern. Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, Leo, Virgo. κριός. ταύρο. δίδυμοι. κάρκιν. λέωμ. παρθένα. Southern. Libra, Scorpio, Sagittarius, Capricorn, Aquarius, Pisces. ζυγός ἤχαλαί. σκόρπιο. τοξότης. αιγόκερω. υδροχόο. ἰχθῦς. CHARACTERS OF THE SEVEN WANDERING STARS. Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Sun, Venus, Mercury, Moon. Φαίνωμ ἢ κρόνο. Φάεθωμ ἢ ζεὺς. πυρόεις καρης. ἵλιθ. ἀφροδίτκι φωσφόρο. ἕρμας ἢ τίλβωμ. σελήνη. CHARACTERS OF THE SPACES OF THE HEAVENS. Sig. Sign, δωδεκατημόριομτοιχειομ ἢ ζώδιομ. P. 1. 2. 3. 4. Part or degree μοίρα. Minute or first scruple, λεπτόμ πρώτομ, sc.1. Second scruple, δεύτερου λεπόμ, sc. Third scruple, τριόμλεπόμ, sc.3. Fourth scruple, τέταρτομ λεπόμ, sc.4. M.C. Midheaven, μεσγράνημα. M.I.C. Imum Coeli, ὑπόγειο ὑπὸ τὴ μεσκράνημα. CHARACTERS OF RADIATIONS, INTERSECTIONS & FORTUNE.

translation: Regarding sects, Cardan noted these few things in his Supplement to the Ephemerides. He says the Jewish law is from Saturn, the Christian from Jupiter and Venus, and the Mohammedan from Mars and the Sun, according to how they rule. Hence, it guards justice, but with great impiety and cruelty. Idols are from Mercury and the Moon. And each law is overcome by its opposite: Saturn is overcome by Jupiter by authority and reason; Jupiter and Mercury are overcome by not listening to reasons and acting against authority; Mars and the Sun are overcome by Saturn through cunning, and Venus through lust; finally, the Moon and Mars are overcome by Jupiter and Venus through authority, dignity, and truth. These are general principles and apply to all peoples. Furthermore, Albubather and other Arabs teach that the future quality of each religion is to be inferred from the ninth house and the natural constitution of Jupiter as the significator of religion.
