translation: DIFFERENCE & Conjunction, synod. Sextile Aspect, hexagon. Square Aspect, tetragon. Trine Aspect, trigon. Opposite Aspect, diameter. Dragon's Head, anabibazon. Dragon's Tail, katabibazon. Part of Fortune, that is, Lot or Part of Fortune. And from these, the equinoctial circle not only divides the zodiac into its principal signs, etc., but also the entire globe of the world into two equal parts, one of which is called boreal and the other austral, from the winds erupting from these regions. Therefore, six signs of the zodiac, namely, Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, Leo, Virgo, and also other stars, whatever incline into the boreal part from the equinoctial, are called boreal. The remaining six dodecatemoria of the zodiac, namely, Libra, Scorpio, Sagittarius, Capricorn, Aquarius, Pisces, and whatever other stars have receded to the south from the equinoctial, are called austral. Now, in order that the reason may be understood why some signs are called direct and others oblique or tortuous in their ascensions, it must be assumed from spherical principles that ascension is called the arc of the equinoctial co-rising with a given arc of the Ecliptic. There are two types of ascensions of signs, namely, direct and oblique. And in tables of directions or ascensions, direct ascension is called the arc of the equinoctial which co-ascends with a given arc of the zodiac in a right sphere. In Greek, anaphora epi tes orthos sphairas. Oblique or climatic ascension is called the arc of the equinoctial which rises with a given arc of the Ecliptic in the position of an oblique sphere. In Greek, anaphora epi tes enklimenes sphairas. The author here does not mean such ascensions of signs, which take their differences by reason of the right and oblique sphere, but speaks in general of the direct and oblique rising of signs, which is common to both the right and oblique sphere. According to this reasoning, we call ascension direct when a greater arc of the equator ascends than of the Ecliptic, and the parts of the Ecliptic consume more time in rising than the parts of the equator. Conversely, we call it oblique when fewer degrees of the equator than of the Ecliptic are carried above the Horizon, and the parts of the Ecliptic require less time in rising than the parts of the equinoctial. In the same way, the signs themselves are made some direct, which, while ascending, occupy a little more time; others oblique, which are carried upwards in a shorter time. And although in both the right and oblique sphere, some signs are found to have direct ascensions and others oblique ascensions, there is nevertheless some dissimilarity in their number. For in the oblique sphere, there are six signs of direct ascensions and as many of oblique ascensions. The direct ones are those which extend in the descending half of the zodiac from the beginning of Cancer to the end of Sagittarius, namely, Cancer, Leo, Virgo, Libra, Scorpio, Sagittarius. The oblique ones are the remaining ones, which extend in the ascending half of the zodiac from the end of Sagittarius to the beginning of Cancer, namely, Capricorn, Aquarius, Pisces, Aries, Taurus, Gemini. But in the right sphere, only four signs, of which two on each side touch the solstitial points, namely, Gemini, Cancer, Sagittarius, and Capricorn, ascend directly; the remaining eight ascend obliquely. For this, we have made you a compendium in the following description. Direct Signs in the Oblique Sphere: Descending Half. Tortuous Signs in the Oblique Sphere: Ascending Half. Signs in the Right Sphere: Direct: Gemini, Cancer, Sagittarius, Capricorn. Tortuous: Aries, Taurus, Leo, Virgo, Libra, Scorpio, Aquarius, Pisces. The tables of ascensions also clearly demonstrate that any given sign located in the descending half of the oblique sphere draws more parts of the equinoctial with it in rising than any sign placed in the other ascending half. And similarly, in the right sphere, the four signs contiguous to the solstitial points consume more time in ascension than the other eight signs. We have appended examples of this, first for the position of the right sphere, in which both poles lie on the horizon; then for the position of the oblique sphere and the altitude of the pole calculated for 51, 52, and 53 degrees, from which you will easily judge, in both situations of the sphere, not only which signs are direct and oblique, but also which have equal anaphoras. The anaphoras of the signs calculated for the right position of the world. Signs: Parts, Scruples. Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, Leo, Virgo, Libra, Scorpio, Sagittarius, Capricorn, Aquarius, Pisces. The anaphoras of the signs calculated for three oblique positions of the world. Pole: 51, 52, 53. Signs: Parts, Scruples. Aries, Taurus, Gemini, Cancer, Leo, Virgo, Libra, Scorpio, Sagittarius, Capricorn, Aquarius, Pisces. And tortuous. However, what the author wrote here, that six signs ascending in a direct path rule over the other six ascending obliquely and tortuously, namely, Cancer rules over Gemini, Leo over Taurus, Virgo over Aries, and others similarly rule over others which are equally distant from the tropical points, cannot be confirmed. For Ptolemy teaches us that these are not ruling or obedient, but beholding and of the same power. Similarly, those which the author calls concordant in their course because of equal anaphoras (which, whether they are in both situations of the sphere, the preceding descriptions of anaphoras sufficiently declare) Ptolemy transmits as ruling and obedient signs. But we here and everywhere will always prefer Ptolemy's opinion as superior to all others, and below, in its proper place, in his chapter on competent signs, we will clearly explain the entire doctrine of ruling and obedient signs. For we see no reason by whose aid we might dare to excuse the author in this, that either having abandoned Ptolemy, he subscribed to the opinion of others here, or he himself, by his own authority, ought to have changed these most elegant and most proper names of the parts of the zodiac transmitted by Ptolemy. Moreover, not contemptible physical reasons, and the very practice of the art, which we see approved by artisans, admonishes and exhorts us to keep the direct and oblique signs separate and distinct from the ruling and obedient ones. For it is known that illustrious writers judge some events from ruling and obedient signs, and others from direct and tortuous signs. From this it follows that events arising from the former do not at all correspond to the latter, and similarly, those effected by the latter cannot be accommodated to the power of the former without error. For example: One of the causes that declare the bodily quantity of those born is the rectitude or obliquity of the ascending signs, not the dominion or obedience of the signs. And conversely, one of the causes that generate benevolence and love towards others in the native is the ruling and obedient signs, and not, as the author seems to think, the rectitude or tortuosity of the signs. But in order that less learned readers may understand how the rectitude or tortuosity of the signs helps to form the stature of the body, and may be admonished by an easy example of the use of these distinctions, by which experts distribute the zodiac into various and distinctly named parts, I will here subjoin the 52nd statement of the Centiloquium, which, although it is by Hermes, we always see printed and circulated under Ptolemy's name. "The lords of the nativity of tall people are in the heights, and their horoscopes are in the beginnings of the signs. But those of short stature are found in the low places. To these, inquire whether the signs are direct or oblique." Pontanus thus interpreted these words: "The lords of the nativity of tall people are in the heights, and their horoscopes are in the beginnings of the signs. But those of short stature are found in the low places. To these, inquire whether the signs are direct or oblique." According to this opinion, there are three most powerful causes producing the tallness or shortness of the human body. The first is the lord of the nativity, or rather the lord of the ascending sign, placed in its orbs. For if it surveys the highest part, it confers tallness; but if it traverses the lowest part, it confers shortness of stature. The second is the number of degrees of the horoscoping sign; if its first parts pass the eastern horizon, then because of their strength, they produce tallness; if the extreme parts, because of exhausted strength, shortness; if finally the middle parts, by their moderate power, they produce a moderate stature. The third cause is the rectitude or obliquity of the horoscoping sign: the former, by prolonging the time of rising, also makes the body somewhat long; the latter, by shortening the time of ascending, shortens the body of the native. title: Significations of Planets
text: . 4 J 9 Quod dicit Saturnum ſignificare peregrinationes longin. Et ſignifiquas, carceres, vincula, tarditatem & afflictionem, ſimiliaqueiti catperegri nerum impedimenta, id ita accipiatur, Saturnum hæc mala mi- nationes nari, ſi loca itinerum, qualia ſunt apud PtolemæumLune, Solis, &c. Martis & fortis fortunarum, cùm in ſeptima vel nona vel tertia domo fuerint, hoſtiliter intueatur, eiſue ſuccedat. Per annos diemq; maiores, medios & minores, confueuerunt Arabes elicere ſpacia vitæ in genituris hominű, ficut infra diffe rentia quarta, capite de Alcocodes videbimus. Porrò huius generis annorú quotquot hoc loco cótinentur, Ptolemeus nufquam meminit, ſilentio inutile eſſe minorú obſeruationé haud dubiè indicans.Maiorum tamen annorum apoteleſmatico primo cap. de finibus, mentionem facere videtur: fed ita vt eos quoque contemnendos relinquat.. 14 Illud quoque quòd dicit, fortitudinem Saturni intendi Fortitudo in plaga ſeptentrionali, no confentit cum dictis Ptolemæi.Su- eius in pla: prà enim demonftratum eſt, ſeptentrionem congruere Ioui,fi- gis &c. cut Venerem meridiei, Saturnumq; téperari in oriente,&Martem in occcidente.. 15 Dicta autem Meſſahal & Dorothei que ſequuntur, ad Et dixit figuram corporis alicuius nati definiendam, de qua fuprà mo- Meffahala nuimus, referenda funt... &c. 16. Nam Luna à primo exortu vſque ad primæ quadraturaincrementum, cùm quaſi dimidiata refulget, efficiens eſt hu miditatis maximè:Inde verò vſq; ad plenilunium, caloris; Deinde à plenilunio ad dimidiationem vſque diminuta, ficcitatis: Denique à ſecunda quadratura ad occultationé vſque, frigiditatis.Stella quoq; errantes matutinæ vfque ad primam ſtationem, humoris habent plurimum:à prima verò ſtatione vſq; dú oriuntur de nocte, magis calefaciunt:Hinc à nocturna fulfione vſque ad ſtationem ſecundam, magis deficcant: à ſecunda deniq; ſtatione ad occaſum vſque, magis frigefaciunt. Ex his verbis eſt perſpiciuum, quòd Luna & tres ſuperiores (Nam Venerem & Mercurium ab hac comparatione, quòd nuquàm ad oppoſitionem Solis veniat, Ptolemeus eximit) reſpectu Solis qua tuor ſtatus habeant. Primus ſtatus ab exortu quo radios Solis exuunt, capit principium, & ad primam quadraturam extenditur. translation: Concerning garments, colors, tastes, parts of the human body, and innumerable such forms of things which agree with certain stars due to a similar temperament, much has been said above. We will also explain at the end of this difference to which stars days, nights, and hours are attributed.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Body Stature and Ascendant
text: àm eriginta partes de equinoctiali peroriri indicant exempla anaphorarum. Afcendat autem etiam libre principium, ita vt gradus eius primus ſecundusúe, aut etiam tertius, quartus,quintus, vel ſextus finientem orientalem contingat. Denique Venus genitura Domina in ſumma fuerum orbium alide feratur. His tribus caufis Titio naſcente ſic, vt dixi,concurrentibus, Titium procero corpore futurum enunciabimus, etiamfi is à breuibus ſatus offet parentibus. Nam parentum bonam ſtaturam, item regionem, in qua communiter productiores ſunt homines, ad proceritatem ſtaturę multum quoque conferre haud eſt dubium. Econtrarid, ſi naſcente Titio, orien talem horizonta partes ſigni poſtrema occupent, & ipſum fi gnum exoriens præterea ſit obliquum, veluti Arietis aut Pifcium, & denique o vel 4 dominium geniturę adeptus, ad imas ſuorum orbium adidas depreſſus reperiatur, Titium quoque breuiore corpore, & contra&a ſtatura futurum non dubitabimus. Hi ſunt ſtaturę corporee ſtatus extremi, Mediocritas verò, quę vbi hec non ex omni ſimul parte conuenerint efficitur, relinquitur ſagacitati prudentis Phyſici ita diiudican da, perinde vt illarum caufarum plures pauciorefúe conuenerint.δικοδιασότης feu dominus geniturę qui fit, & quibus ratio nibus eligendus, infra magis in oportuno loco explicabitur. It medie, Ratione longarum breuiumq; afcenfionum, medietas figsas circuli niferi vna, que ab initio Leonis ad finem Capricorni protenditur, nominatur medietas maxima, quia dum afcendit, plurimum temporis occupat. Hęc eadem etiam nominatur medietas Solis, quòd Leo Solaris nature fit,atque in ea medietate vni uerſa ==Start of OCR for page 1== בי } ; = PRIMA. 23 anerſä eam poteftatem exerceat Sol, quam reliqui Planeta in fuis finibus. Altera, quę ab initio Aquarij ad finem Cancri extenditur, cùm peroriens tempus plurimum corripiat, medietas minima nuncupatur. Dicitur & Lune medietas, quòd ficut Sol in maxima, ita Lunain hac minima principatum obtineat. Eft enim Cancer familiaris Lunę. In reliquis autem huius medietatis partibus omnibus Luna ita regnat, quemadmodum Plane tę in ſuis finibus:de quibus infrà dicetur. קו Fines 2 Fines D X ४ ++ Ⅱ 6 7 Signorum Zodiaci, vnde ortus & interitus præcipuæ caufæ Et voca defluunt, qualitates alias eſſe generales, alias à familiaritate So- tur illa, lis atque Lunæ, & reliquarum ſtellarum deſumptas, alias ex col &c. Latione ipforum erga ſeſe ortas, alias denique ſimpliciter atq; abſque vila commiftione infitas, atque fingulis peculiariter ingenitas, fuprà de ſententia Ptolemzi ditumeſt. Eum verò in hunc modum hæc diſtinxiſſe, ex his ipfius verbis apparet: αἱ μέγαὶ ὁλολοβέτεραι καθ' έκαςομ αὐτῇ κράσος αναλόγως ἔχουσι τοῖς κατὰ αὐτὰ γινομοίαις ώραις. (κοίςανται δε λινού αὐ ἰδιότητος ἀπὸ τε τὸ πρὸς τὸμ Ο κλ τώ (κλιού ας δρας οἰκλώσεως, Ο αἰκατὰ τὸ ἀμιγές αὐτ μόνωμ την δωδεκατημορίων καθ' αΰτα, το καὶ πρὸς ἄλληλα θεωρομοίαι διωάμας. hoc eft: Generales quidem conſtitutiones rationem eandem habent cum tempo ribus. Exiſtunt verò in ſingulis aliæ proprietates, quarum quz dam fumuntur à familiaritate Solis, Lune, aliarumq; ſtellarum: quædam verò ſimplices funt, quæ dodecatemorijs fingulis per ſe infunt, quzá; ex collatione ipforum erga ſe inuicem oriuntur. Ad eundem planè modum Alcabicius poſtquam partitionem ſigniferi abſoluit, partium eius qualitates & proprietates explicat. Et primò qualitates & naturas ſignorű generales proportiõe quadam quatuor temporibus anni, quatuor ętatibus, & denique quatuor animantium humoribus correſponden tes infinuat. In omnibus enim iſtis prima pars humida eft & ca. lida, prædominante tamen humiditate. Similiter pars ſecunda. calidior quam ficca: tertia ficcior quam frigida:quarta frigidi er quam humida exiſtit. Verùm enimuerò cùm ſignifer, quate nuss Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 1== ==Start of OCR for page 2== DIFFEREΝΤΙΑ nùs eft circulus, neque principium neque finem habeat, vbi illa prima pars eius inchoari ſtatuiq; debeat, queri poteſt. Reſpon demus eam à conſenſu qualitatum in rebus effectis herentium cum qualitatibus, quæ potioribus efficiendi caufis infunt, eſſe æſtimandam capiendamq;. Obliquus circulus feu ſignifer re rum naſcentium cauſa effectrix eſt. Tempora verò anni & animantium humores funt quæ ab eo efficiuntur. Etenim Sol figniferum luſtrans qualitates temporum anni ſuſcitat, quarum ingula certarum ætatum humoribus conſentiunt. Veluti ver conſentit ætati puerili, in qua ſanguis redundat: æſtas iuuenili & cholerica: autumnus decreſcenti & melancholice: hyems denique ſenili & pituoſe. Quod cùm ita fit, perfpicuum eſt, vnamquamque ztatem animalium, & vnumquemque humo. rem non tantùm certis annitemporibus, fed & ijs portionibus ſigniferi, que talia tempora faciunt, ordine & poteftate qualita tum principaliter congruere. Nam ex qua cauſa vnumquodq; tale exiſtit, illud huiufmodi qualitate abundare oportet. Proinde prima etas, qua animalia humido abundant,& mollia teneraq; funt, non tantùm tempori, quod ver nominamus, fed & cum ea parte ſigniferi, quæ verni temporis effetrix eſt, ordine & natura congruit. Talis autem eſt ea quæ à principio Arietis ad finem Geminorum extenditur, fiquidem à Sole perluſtrata aërem aëriq; corpora incluſa excellenter humectat. Ob hanc tantam fimilitudinem qualitatum in di&a portione cœli:verno tempore:& prima ætate animalium æquè dominá- tium, confentanee hæc quarta ſigniferi pars prima, vernalis, puerilis & fanguinea dicitur. Omnia enim hæc ficut principia rerum faciunt, ita quoque caliditate & humiditate conſentiunt: fed tamen longè magis humore quam calore abundant. Porro ætas hominis(de hoc enim principaliter hic agitur)prima,quam puerilem ſeu potius ætatem incrementi nominant, à partu ad annú 21.extéditur. Ad eundé modú ſequétia anni tepora, & ætates ſequentes reliquis ſequentib' ſigniferi quadran tibus ordine accomodari debét. Secúda ætas roboris nominata, ab anno 21.in annű 35. in robuſtis verò in annum 40.protenditur, & propter caliditatem & ficcitatem æſtati coſentit. Hinc portio ſigniferi eſtatem faciés vocatur, quarta ſecunda, calida& ficca, Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 2==
translation: Examples of anaphora indicate that thirty parts of the equinoctial rise. Let the beginning of Libra also ascend, so that its first or second, or even third, fourth, fifth, or sixth degree touches the eastern horizon. Finally, let Venus, the Lady of the geniture, be carried in the highest of her orbs. With these three causes concurring, as I have said, at Titius's birth, we will declare Titius to be of tall stature, even if he were born of short parents. For it is not doubtful that the good stature of parents, as well as the region in which people are commonly taller, also contributes much to tallness of stature. Conversely, if at Titius's birth, the last parts of the sign occupy the eastern horizon, and the rising sign itself is moreover oblique, like Aries or Pisces, and finally, if the Sun or Saturn, having obtained dominion of the geniture, is found depressed to the lowest parts of its orbs, we will not doubt that Titius will also be of shorter body and contracted stature. These are the extreme states of bodily stature. Mediocrity, however, which is produced when these do not all concur at once, is left to the sagacity of the prudent Physician to judge, according to whether more or fewer of those causes have concurred. The dikodiasotes or lord of the geniture, who it is, and by what reasons it is to be chosen, will be explained below in a more opportune place. In the meantime, by reason of long and short ascensions, one half of the zodiacal circle, which extends from the beginning of Leo to the end of Capricorn, is called the maximum half, because while it ascends, it occupies the most time. This same half is also called the half of the Sun, because Leo is of a solar nature, and in this half the Sun exercises that power which the other planets have in their own bounds. The other half, which extends from the beginning of Aquarius to the end of Cancer, since it shortens the time of rising the most, is called the minimum half. It is also called the half of the Moon, because just as the Sun holds principality in the maximum, so the Moon holds it in this minimum. For Cancer is familiar to the Moon. In all the other parts of this half, the Moon reigns just as the planets do in their own bounds, about which more will be said below. The bounds of the signs of the Zodiac, from which the principal causes of rising and setting flow, are of different qualities: some are general, others are derived from the familiarity of the Sun and Moon and other stars, others arise from their mutual collation, and finally, others are simply and without any mixture inherent and peculiar to each, as Ptolemy's opinion has been stated above. That he distinguished these in this way is apparent from his own words: "The general constitutions have the same ratio with the times. But in each there are other properties, some of which are taken from the familiarity of the Sun, Moon, and other stars; others are simple, which are inherent in each dodecatemory by itself, and which arise from their mutual collation." In exactly the same way, Alcabitius, after completing the division of the zodiac, explains the qualities and properties of its parts. And first, he insinuates the general qualities and natures of the signs by a certain proportion corresponding to the four seasons of the year, the four ages, and finally, the four humors of living beings. For in all these, the first part is humid and hot, with humidity predominating. Similarly, the second part is hotter than dry; the third is drier than cold; the fourth is colder than humid. But since the zodiac, inasmuch as it is a circle, has neither beginning nor end, it can be asked where its first part should be established and begin. We respond that it must be estimated and taken from the agreement of the qualities inherent in the effects with the qualities inherent in the more potent efficient causes. The oblique circle or zodiac is the efficient cause of nascent things. The seasons of the year and the humors of living beings are what are produced by it. For the Sun, traversing the zodiac, excites the qualities of the seasons of the year, each of which agrees with the humors of certain ages. Just as spring agrees with childhood, in which blood abounds; summer with youth and the choleric humor; autumn with decline and the melancholic humor; and finally, winter with old age and the phlegmatic humor. Since this is so, it is clear that each age of animals, and each humor, agrees principally in the order and power of qualities not only with certain times of the year, but also with those portions of the zodiac that produce such times. For from whatever cause each such thing exists, it must abound in that kind of quality. Therefore, the first age, in which animals abound in humidity and are soft and tender, agrees not only with the season we call spring, but also with that part of the zodiac which is the efficient cause of springtime, in order and nature. Such is that which extends from the beginning of Aries to the end of Gemini, since, when traversed by the Sun, it excellently moistens the air and the bodies enclosed in the air. Because of this great similarity of qualities equally dominating in the said portion of the sky, in springtime, and in the first age of animals, this first, vernal, childish, and sanguine quadrant of the zodiac is congruously named. For all these, just as they constitute the beginnings of things, so also they agree in heat and humidity; but they abound much more in humor than in heat. Furthermore, the first age of man (for this is principally discussed here), which they call childhood or rather the age of growth, extends from birth to the 21st year. In the same way, the subsequent seasons of the year and subsequent ages must be accommodated in order to the remaining subsequent quadrants of the zodiac. The second age, called the age of strength, extends from the 21st year to the 35th year, but in robust individuals to the 40th year, and because of its heat and dryness, it agrees with summer. Hence the portion of the zodiac that produces summer is called the second quadrant, hot and dry.

translation: The orb of a star is the space of the sky that the star affects circularly and from all sides, not so much by its ray or visible light, but by a hidden power and efficacy. The half or semi-diameter of this luminous orb, according to the tradition of the Arabs, is 15 parts for the Sun, 12 parts for the Moon, 9 parts for Saturn and Jupiter, 8 parts for Mars, and 7 parts for Venus and Mercury. However, Ptolemy shows these limits of rays somewhat differently, namely, the semi-diameter of the Sun extends 17 parts in all directions, the Moon 12 parts, Jupiter 12 parts, Saturn 10 parts, Venus 8 parts, and Mars 7 parts. This consideration is useful. For aspects are said to last as long as the orbs last, and according to them, both applications to conjunctions and separations from them are taken, as will be more clearly shown below in the doctrine of applications and defluxions. Many also call a planet combust when it is less than half its orb distant from the Sun, or precisely that much before and after.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Ages, Seasons, and Temperaments
text: ==Start of OCR for page 3== PRIMA. 25 ficca, æſtiualis, iuuenilis & cholerica. In omnibus tamen iſtis dominatur calor. Tertia ætas fapiétiæ, incipit ab anno 35. & por rigitur ad annum so. in robuſtis verò ab anno 40. ad annum 60.progreditur. Hæc humori melancholico & tempori autumnali propter caloris decrementum, ficcitatiſq; dominiú comparatur. Quo efficitur, vt portio ſigniferi autumnum faciens dicatur quarta pars frigida & ficca, autumnalis, melancholica & deficiens. Quarta ætas debilitatis, vniuerfaliter inter annum 50. & 60. capit principium, & durat vſque ad mortem. Comparatur autem pituitæ, hyemi & quartz ſigniferi vltimz,quę ob id frigida & humida redundante tamen frigore, phlegmatica, hyemalis & fenilis dicitur. Hecautemin ętatibus annique temporibus temperaturarum fimilitudo medicorum ſententiæ confentit. Nam quòd Ptolemeus vernum tempus facit humidum & calidum, Galenus verò temperatum, reipſa non diſsident, fiquidem ver ſubſtantia quidem humidum, qualitate verò temperatum eſt. Similiter autumnus ſub- ſtantia ficcus eſt, quauis qualitate humidus appareat,&c. Quod verò ad temperaturas ætatum attinet, Hippocrates pafsim enú ciauit, quòd corpora, quæ creſcunt, plurimum habent innati caloris, qui vt Galenus docet, humidus eſt & calidus ſecundum ſubſtantiam, vtpotè, qui in ſubſtantia aérea & aquoſa hereat. Se quentem ætatem vocant conſiſtentem, in qua etſi calor ſecundum qualitatem intendatur acuaturq;, tamen remittiturἔμε φυομ θόρμόμ ratione ſubiecte ſubſtantię, in qua propter impuritatem adiecti nutrimenti diminuto humido innato,aute ſunt partes terreſtres. Similibus de caufis ficcior eſt etas tertia, & ſenium ad modum hyemis frigidum exiſtit. Que omnia quũ cum ſigniferi quadrantibus, quod ad temperaturas attinet,cofentiant, euidenter patet natura ſeu generalis conſtitutio dodecatemoriorum omnium. Nam ſemper tria vniufcuiufq; quadrantis ſigna, que ſeſe mutuo ab aliquo punctorum cardinalium ordine conſequuntur,temperarura conueniunt. Veluti figna ४ & II in ſectione vernali reperta, calida funt & humida:quę in ęſtiuali ſectione ſequuntur, nempe & my calida & licca. Hincin autumnali ſectione poſita, nempem &++, ficca & frigida:Denique & X, que hyemem faciunt, D humiDigitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 3== ==Start of OCR for page 4== 26 DIFFERENTIA humida & frigida. Sed media horum, hoc eft, fixa maximè talia exiſtunt. Quocirca Taurus calidiſsimus eſt & humidiſsimus,& ob id omnium fignorum in cælo temperatissimus. Sic & calidifsimus eft & ficciſsimus, biliofifsimus & animoſiſsimus.Simi liter Scorpius frigidifsimus eft & ficcifsimus, fed tamen ficcior quàm frigidus, perniciofiſsimus & humanæ naturæ valdèinimicus, ob quam caufam ei venenum meritò attributum eft. Signum denique Aquarij frigidiſsimum eſt & humidiſsimu, & ob id ignauifsimum timidiſsimumque. Adhæc cardinalia, hoceſt, vnicuiuſque quadrantis ſigna prima, vna qualitateremiſſa, altera maximè excellunt. Hinc in dodecatemorio Arietis caliditatem longè ſuperat humiditas. In Cancro ficcitatem caliditas, in frigiditatem ficcitas, in humiditatem gelu facilè excedit. Ad eundem modum in ſignis quadrantum vltimis, quæ infrà bicorpora & communia nominabuntur, altera viget qualitas, ſed ordine reuerſo, & vbi vna qualitas alteram non ita excellenter & nimium ſupprimit. In dodecatemorio igitur Ge minorum calor humidum: in my liccitas calorem: in frigus ficcitatem:in X deniq; humidum excedit frigidum. Hæc elt ge neralis conſtitutio duodecim dodecatemoriorú, ex qua liquet, medietatem figniferi ſeptentrionalem totam eſſe calidam,meridionalem verò frigidam, quemadmodum autor voluit. Omnibus 8 (Sequitur admonitio de ordine & fitu planetarum, in quo netis, &c. Ptolemæum ſecutus, eos ſuperiores reliquis ſtatuit, qui longio re tempore vnam periodum conficiunt. Hinc Saturni ſtellam, quum annis 29. diebus 162. horis 7 ferè, motu ſuo æquali vnam reuolutionem abfoluat, atque propter nimiam à terra diſtantiam minima appareat, ſummam eſſe atque maximo circulo ambire confentaneum eft. Lineæ enim mediorum motuum & & & ſemper coniun&a funt, ob qua caufam ἰσόδρομοι, ὁμόδρομοι καὶξανδρομοι τῷ ἡλία dicunautem pla I Σ' Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 4== ==Start of OCR for page 5== PRIMA. 27 dicuntur.Nouifsimum fidus terrisque familiariſsimum, & in tenebrarum remedium ab natura repertum eſt Lunæ,quæ vná periodum diebus 27. horis 8. ferè complet. Hæc eſt Ptolemei de ordine planetarum fententia, à qua nonnulli diſſentiunt. Sanè de ordine trium ſuperiorum conſentiunt omnes. Luna quoque omnium ſententia ima eſt, quòd & Solis aſpectum nobis adimat, & πραλλαξιμ,quam diuerfitatem aſpectus vulgò no minant,cognitu manifeſtiorem habeat. Relinquitur ergo de ſituatque ordine, & & & controuerfia. Plato & Ariftoteles ſupra proximè collocant, deinde ſupra proximè ponunt &,fupra hanc ✗, tandem vt prius ordine aſcendendo 4 & 1. Huic ſententiæ qui patrocinantur, hanc rationem adferunt: Si, inquiunt, & & & eſſent infra ſiti, no minùs quàm (ſi non totum, ſaltem partem eius aliquam obfcurarent, quoties Soli coniun&i,inter & noſtrum viſum poneretur: quod cùm nulla ętate obferuatum fit, idcirco credendum eſſe putant ſtellas & & & fupra eſſe poſitas. Verùm hæcobiectio, qua per interuentum & aut & Solis obſcurationem imaginantur, facilè confutari poteſt. Ac primò Solis interuentu Lunam occultari euidens eſt ratio: Nam corpus Lunæ, quod non nifi radijs accenfum refulget, cùm fua natura opacum fit & obfcu rum, adeoq; denſum, vt radiis incidentibus tranfitum minimè concedat, omnemq; lucem exceptam fortiter refundat, non mi rum eſt ſi ſuo interpofitu confpectum nobis auferat. At Veneris & & cùm non fint opaca, ſed lucidiſsima corpora, Solem tegere nequaquam poffunt.Secundò & & & vt plurimum extra eclipticam vagantur, & rarò ita cum Sole coniungi poffunt, vt in lineam ex viſu noſtro per centrum Solis actam incur rant, & fic inter aſpectum noftrum Solemque ponantur. Ad extremum etiamfi hoc quoque accidat, atque ficut fieri poteſt, fiat alterutrius horum aſpectabilis coniunctio : tamen cùm harum ſtellarum tanta appareat paruitas, vt aliquam partem So lis,que ſenſu percipi poſsit, nobis adimere nequeant, non videntur audiendi, qui Solem his ſtellis non ob aliam cauſam fubijciunt, nifi quòd in congreſsibus cum Sole eius confpeAum nobis non auferant. translation: The first age is dry, summery, youthful, and choleric. In all these, however, heat dominates. The third age of wisdom begins at age 35 and extends to age 50; in robust individuals, it progresses from age 40 to age 60. This age is compared to melancholic humor and the autumn season due to the decrease in heat and the dominance of dryness. This makes the portion of the zodiac that creates autumn called the fourth part: cold and dry, autumnal, melancholic, and deficient. The fourth age of debility universally begins between ages 50 and 60 and lasts until death. It is compared to phlegm, winter, and the last quarter of the zodiac, which is therefore called cold and humid, with redundant coldness, phlegmatic, wintry, and senile. This similarity of temperaments in ages and seasons of the year agrees with the opinion of physicians. For what Ptolemy makes the spring season humid and hot, and Galen temperate, they do not in fact disagree, since spring is humid in substance, but temperate in quality. Similarly, autumn is dry in substance, although it appears humid in quality, etc. As for the temperaments of the ages, Hippocrates widely stated that bodies that grow have a great deal of innate heat, which, as Galen teaches, is humid and hot according to its substance, as it inheres in an airy and watery substance. They call the subsequent age consistent, in which, although heat is intensified and sharpened according to quality, the innate heat is nevertheless diminished due to the underlying substance, in which, because of the impurity of added nourishment, the innate moisture is reduced, and the earthy parts are prominent. For similar reasons, the third age is drier, and old age is cold like winter. Since all these agree with the quadrants of the zodiac regarding temperaments, the nature or general constitution of all the dodecatemoria is clearly evident. For the three signs of each quadrant, which follow each other in order from any of the cardinal points, always agree in temperament. Just as the signs Taurus and Gemini found in the vernal section are hot and humid; those that follow in the aestival section, namely Leo and Virgo, are hot and dry. Hence, those placed in the autumnal section, namely Libra and Scorpio, are dry and cold. Finally, Sagittarius and Capricorn, which make winter, are humid and cold. But the middle ones of these, that is, the fixed signs, are especially such. Therefore, Taurus is very hot and very humid, and for this reason, the most temperate of all signs in the sky. Similarly, Leo is very hot and very dry, very bilious and very spirited. Likewise, Scorpio is very cold and very dry, but drier than cold, very pernicious and very inimical to human nature, for which reason poison is rightly attributed to it. Finally, the sign of Aquarius is very cold and very humid, and for this reason, very sluggish and very timid. Furthermore, the cardinal signs, that is, the first signs of each quadrant, excel greatly in one quality, while the other is diminished. Hence, in the dodecatemorion of Aries, humidity far surpasses heat. In Cancer, heat surpasses dryness; in Libra, dryness surpasses coldness; in Capricorn, coldness easily surpasses humidity. In the same way, in the last signs of the quadrants, which will be called bicorporeal and common below, one quality is strong, but in reverse order, and where one quality does not so excellently and excessively suppress the other. Therefore, in the dodecatemorion of Gemini, heat exceeds humidity; in Virgo, dryness exceeds heat; in Sagittarius, cold exceeds dryness; and finally, in Pisces, humidity exceeds cold. This is the general constitution of the twelve dodecatemoria, from which it is clear that the entire northern half of the zodiac is hot, and the southern half is cold, as the author intended. (There follows an admonition about the order and position of the planets, in which, following Ptolemy, he places those superior to the others that complete one period in a longer time. Hence, the star of Saturn, since it completes one revolution in its equal motion in 29 years, 162 days, and almost 7 hours, and appears smallest due to its great distance from Earth, it is consistent that it is the highest and encompasses the largest circle. The star of Jupiter traverses the entire Zodiac in 11 years, 315 days, and almost 174 hours, and therefore occupies a lower orbit than Saturn. Mars follows this, completing one revolution in one year, 320 days, and 222 hours. The Sun, leader and moderator of the others, placed in the middle, traverses the Zodiac in 365 and a quarter days. First Mercury, then Venus, are placed below the Sun, each of which completes the entire Zodiac in the same amount of time as the Sun. For the lines of the mean motions of Mercury, Venus, and the Sun are always conjoined, for which reason they are called isodromoi, homodromoi, and xandromoi to the Sun. The newest star and most familiar to Earth, and found by nature as a remedy for darkness, is the Moon, which completes one period in 27 days and almost 8 hours. This is Ptolemy's opinion on the order of the planets, from which some dissent. Indeed, all agree on the order of the three superior planets. The Moon is also, by everyone's opinion, the lowest, because it deprives us of the Sun's aspect and has a more manifest parallax, which they commonly call the diversity of aspect. Therefore, the controversy remains regarding the position and order of Mercury, Venus, and Mars. Plato and Aristotle place Mercury immediately above the Moon, then Venus immediately above Mercury, then Mars above Venus, and finally, as before, ascending in order, Jupiter and Saturn. Those who advocate this opinion offer this reason: If, they say, Mercury, Venus, and Mars were situated below the Sun, they would obscure it no less than the Moon (if not entirely, at least some part of it) whenever they were conjoined with the Sun and placed between the Sun and our sight; since this has never been observed in any age, they therefore believe that the stars Mercury, Venus, and Mars are placed above the Sun. However, this objection, by which they imagine an obscuration of the Sun by the intervention of Mercury or Venus, can be easily refuted. And first, it is evident that the Moon is occulted by the intervention of the Sun: For the body of the Moon, which shines only when illuminated by rays, since it is opaque and dark by its nature, and so dense that it hardly allows the passage of incident rays, and strongly reflects all received light, it is not surprising if it obstructs our view by its interposition. But since Venus and Mercury are not opaque, but very luminous bodies, they cannot obscure the Sun at all. Secondly, Mercury, Venus, and Mars mostly wander outside the ecliptic, and can rarely be so conjoined with the Sun that they fall into a line drawn from our sight through the center of the Sun, and thus be placed between our aspect and the Sun. Finally, even if this also happens, and as it can happen, an observable conjunction of one of these occurs: nevertheless, since the apparent smallness of these stars is such that they cannot deprive us of any part of the Sun that can be perceived by the senses, those who subject the Sun to these stars for no other reason than that they do not deprive us of its sight in conjunctions with the Sun, do not seem to be heard. Furthermore, although the Moon can be shown to be 6644 times smaller than the Sun: nevertheless, due to its low position, it appears of such magnitude.

translation: We have described all the planetary years mentioned by this author in the following table. The years of Fridaria: Sun 10, Moon 9, Saturn 11, Jupiter 12, Mars 7, Venus 8, Mercury 13. Maximum years: Sun 1461, Moon 520, Saturn 465, Jupiter 427, Mars 284, Venus 551, Mercury 480. Major years: Sun 120, Moon 108, Saturn 57, Jupiter 79, Mars 66, Venus 82, Mercury 76. Medium years: Sun 69, Moon 66, Saturn 43, Jupiter 45, Mars 40, Venus 45, Mercury 48. Minor years: Sun 19, Moon 12, Saturn 5, Jupiter 13, Mars 12, Venus 15, Mercury 18. What the Arabs mean by Fridaria years will be explained in the third to last chapter of the fourth difference. But the Arabs call maximum years the duration of time during which the effects of planets last in universal and great world changes. Through major, medium, and minor years, the Arabs are accustomed to deriving the spans of life in human nativities, as we will see below in the fourth difference, in the chapter on Alcocodes. Furthermore, Ptolemy nowhere mentions any of this kind of years contained in this place, undoubtedly indicating by his silence that the observation of minor years is useless. However, he seems to make mention of major years in the first apotelesmatic chapter on boundaries, but in such a way that he also leaves them to be despised.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Houses and Dignities
text: ==Start of Page 1== 28 DIFFERENTIA tudine apparet, vt nonnunquam totum Solem nobis facilè auferat. Eſt autem ) remotissime diameter apparens ſcrupulorum 29. humilimæ verò ſc. 36. ferè. Solis à terra remotiſsimi diameter apparens implet partes minutas cæli 315. humilimi 34. Iam ex collatione diametri apparentis ) humilimæ ad diametrum apparentem Solis vbicunque conſtituti perfpicuum eſt, corpus Solis interuentu corporis), etſi non cum mora,totum tamen poſſe occultari. Ediuerſo diameter & apparens iuxta obferuationes artificum,vix decimam partem diametri equat. Et cùm plana duorum orbium habeant ſe ad inuicem in duplicata ratione ſuorum diametrorum, efficitur ſolaris plani apparentis ad planum & apparens, eſſe rationem eam, que estad 100. Quare ſi tegeret Venus Solem, tegeret centefimam eius partem apparentem, quæ ſenſu oculorum notari no poſſet. Regiomontanus in princ. lib. 9. epitomes Ptolemæum ita defendit: Non eſt conſentaneum, ſpatium quod eſt inter &), ſtellis effe vacuum, cum fit maius interuallo à centro terrę ad orbes) vſque protenſo. Nam minima Solis à centro mundi diſtantia continet ſemidiametros terrę 1070. & Luna altif- ſima diftat à centro vniuerfi ijfdem ſemidiametris terræ ferè 64.quibus ſubtractis à diſtantia Solis, relinquuntur ſemidiametri terræ 1006. hoc eſt, milliaria Germanica 865160. quod tantum interuallum duorum luminarium omnino ſtellis carere non eſt veriſimile.. Non quòd 9 De exiſtentia ſtellarum in ſignis, quando celo affixa or- ſunt,&c. bes luminarium aliorumque planetarum continere intelliguntur. Cum ergo dicimus, Solem exempli gratia verſari iny, non ſic accipiendum eſt, quaſi Solis ſupremum cælum accedens inter fixas ſtellas feratur, fed quòd ſub illa ſigniferi parte, que dicitur Aries, ambulet. Autor Spherę docet planetas in fignis quadrifariam exiſtere. Sed locum Solis vel planetarum carentium latitudine in ecliptica verum, exactiſsimè indicat linea à centro terræ per centrum ſideris ad extimum cælum protenſa. Quæ verò fidera latitudinem habent, atque extra eclipticam verfantur, eorum vera loca certiſsimè monſtrat pars peripherię, quę per polum Zodiaci & centrum ſtelle ad eclipticam porrigitur:nam punctum illud contactus in Zodiaco verus eſt locus. ==Start of Page 2== PRIMA. 29 focus illius fideris ſecundum longitudinem. Hoc modo ſtella polaris, vt vocant, magnitudinis tertiæ, quamuis ab ecliptica P.66.abſit: tamen iam ad P.20. ſc.52.Geminorü refertur,iuxta calculum prutenicum. 10 Draco figura eft, quam Solis ac Lunæ meatus efficiunt. Significan Nam planum alidis Lunæ eclipticam Solis ſuper diametro tur autem mundi diſpeſcit, ita vtvna eius parsin aquilonem,altera in au- &. ſtrum ab ecliptica ſpacio 5. P. diſcedat. Hanc in contrarias partesalidis) inclinationem, vocant meatum flexuosum Draconum, à ſimilitudine quadam ingentium ſerpentum, quorum venter capite & cauda laxior eft & amplior. Vnaautem harum interſectionum,vnde) ad Arctoam plagam ſcandit, Draconis caput appellatur, Græci vocant σύδεσμομ αναβιβάζοντα, id eſt, nodum attollentem euehentemque, cuius vfitatus charater eft. Altera ſectio, vnde ad auſtrum) deſcendit, cauda Draconis vulgo appellatur, Græci vocant στιύδεσμομ καταβιβά Corta, id eft, nodú deprimentem ) deuchentemq;, & vfitatus eius chara&er eſt Boreus limes βόρειομ πόρας, eſt medium punctum ſemicirculi Lunæ borealis. Auſtralis limes priori è regione oppoſitus eſt, ſimiliter punctum medium ſemicirculi Lunæauſtralis: Vulgus ventres Draconum appellat. His ita ſe habentibus manifeſtum eft,inter vtrunque limitem & ipfos no dos quadrantes circuli comprehendi, & Lunam in nodispotitam planè priuari latitudine, atque eandem in limitibus maximam confequi.Ad'extremum ſciendum eſt, hæc punta nodorum limitumque Lunæ non eſſe fixa, ſed quotidie ab exortu in occaſum ſc. 3. tranfire, vt ratio reddi queat, cur Lunæ defeAus ſemper contingat citra quàm proximè fuerit. DE DOMIBVS PLANETARVM.. I Habent quoque Planetæ in his ſignis poteſtates, quafdam per naturam, quafdam per accidens. Que funt per naturam, funt he: Domus, Exaltatio, Terminus, D3 Triplis ==Start of Page 3== i 1 31 DIFFERENTIA Triplicitas, Facies. De illis autem, que per accides funt, loco conuenienti tractabimus. Domus funt he: Aries & Scorpius, domus Martis: Taurus Libra, domus Veneris:Gemini Virgo, do. mus Mercuriý: Cancer, domus Lunæ: Leo, domus Solis: Sagittarius &Pisces, domus Iouis: Capricornus & A quarius, domus Saturni. 2 Septimum autem ſignum à domo vniufcuiufq; Pla netæ dicitur eiufdem fore detrimentum. 3 Et fi duo ſigna fuerint domus vnius planeta, dicun. tur concordantia in Almantica, hoc eft, in circulo, qui la tus est in medio,& in ligaturaſtrictus, & habet ſignis ficare circulum Zodiacum. 4 Signa autem, in que planetæ dum intrant, dicun tur gratulari in eis, & domini eorum,fecundum Dorotheum funt hac: Saturnus dum intrat Aquarium, gau dere dicitur:Iuppiter in Sagittario, Mars in Scorpio. ne, Venus in Tauro, Mercurius in Virgine. Aabent I Signiferi partitione ſignorumq; generali conſtitutione ex quoque pofita,iamvt ordo ſuprà expoſitus exigit, vult oftendere, alias &c. ſignorum proprietates à luminarium & reliquorum erronum familiaritate deſumptas. Sed reprehédet me fortaſſe hic aliqs, hanceſſe nouam aut alienam à ſentétia autoris expoſitionem. Nam cùm Autor præmittat hic, ſe diturum de poteſtatibus planetarú tam inſitis quàm fortuitis, non videtur dicere velle quibus modis intendatur remittaturue ſignorum generalis conſtitutio. Verùm noſtra expoſitio ratione non caret. Nam qui diſputat, quemadmodum virtus planetarum cognatis qua litatibus ac familiaritate conuenientium ſignorum intendatur, aut contrarijs diluatur: is ſimul tacitè de intenfione & remiſsione qualitatum ſignis inſitarum tractat. Quæ enim tem peratura ==Start of Page 4== : PRIMA. 31 peratura ſimili ſociata ſunt, qualitatem vtriufque concurrentis faciunt efficaciorem, vt:quoniam o & crafi conueniunt:Igi tur cum & per graditur, calore ficcitatemq; figni adauget,& vis ſideris viciſsim iuuat cognata qualitate ſigni Leonis. Hinc perfpicuum eſt, hanc tractationem de ſocietate & conuenientia planetarum cum ſignis,tam ad vim ſignorum quam poteſta tem planetarum augendam minuendamúe ſpectare. Cæterùm quòd autor familiaritatem planetarum cum ſignis aliam naturalem, aliam accidentalem facit, quomodo conſtare poſsit non video.Cùm enim ſtellas domo, altitudine, trigono & terminis naturaliter ſignis conciliari fateatur, quænam reſtare poteſt ratio, qua ſtellæ ſignis per accidens conſocientur?nulla. Quod cùm ita fit, ideo capite vltimo, cui titulum fecit, de poteftatibus planetarum accidentalibus, non ſtellas ſignis, ſed tempera turæ diurni nocturniúe temporis iuſtè comparare potuit. Signorum quoque ſexus non eſt fortuitus, ſed naturalis. Ipſę verd duodecim ſtationes & quadrantes mundi, quos vel motus cœ- li quotidianus, vel loca luminarium, vt infra patebit, faciunt,ni hil planè commune cum ſignis habere poffunt. Teneamus igitur methodum ſuprà à nobis expoſitam, & hic denuò ob magnam vtilitatem repetendam, nempe quòd & ſignorŭ & ſtellarú δίαθεσιμκατά τε τὸ αὐτό κοσμικόμ καὶ κατά γεύεσιμ obſeruare debeamus. Ac vt de ſignis priùs dicat, reperiunt in eis quęda quali tates generales, quales primo capite ia funt expofitæ. Hæ ipſæ qualitates augent poſtea, vt ia dicetur, ſocietate planetarú quan do temperatura conueniunt, aut diluuntur debilitanturque, cùm qualitates contrariæ fuerint. His ſi adiungas ſignorŭ proprietates peculiariter vnicuique inſitas, & ex collatione mutua genitas, vniuerfas ſignorum κατα'το κοσμικό conſtitutiones ha bebis. Reliqua ſignorum poteſtas vel αδυναμία ratione cœli poſitus accedens, vocatur κατά τὼ γεύεσιμ ή ὡς ἔτυχε διάθεσις, hoc eſt, ſecundum genituram vel fortuita diſpoſitio. Motus enim cæli diurnus, inenarrabili ſua celeritate non ſolum ſingulis horis, fed & ſingulis temporum ſcrupulis ſitum cæli variat, atque ſigna cæleſtia in alia atque alia loca deducit. Quo efficitur, vt vnius eiufdemq; ſigni, eodéq; modo ſua naturaaffe&i, diuerfi fint effectus & fignificati
translation: 28. The apparent diameter of the Moon, when it is most distant, is 29 minutes, but when it is closest, it is almost 36 minutes. The apparent diameter of the Sun, when it is most distant from the Earth, fills 31.5 minutes of the sky, and when it is closest, 34 minutes. Now, from a comparison of the Moon's closest apparent diameter to the Sun's apparent diameter, wherever it may be, it is clear that the body of the Sun can be completely obscured by the intervention of the Moon's body, even if not for a long duration. Conversely, the apparent diameter of Venus, according to the observations of skilled astronomers, barely equals a tenth part of the Sun's diameter. And since the planes of two orbs relate to each other in the duplicate ratio of their diameters, the ratio of the apparent solar plane to the apparent plane of Venus is as 100 to 1. Therefore, if Venus were to cover the Sun, it would cover only one hundredth of its apparent part, which could not be noticed by the senses of the eyes. Regiomontanus, in the beginning of the 9th book of Ptolemy's Epitome, defends him thus: It is not consistent that the space between the Sun and Moon should be empty of stars, since it is greater than the interval extended from the center of the Earth to the orbs of the Moon. For the minimum distance of the Sun from the center of the world contains 1070 Earth semidiameters, and the Moon at its highest is distant from the center of the universe by almost 64 of the same Earth semidiameters. Subtracting these from the Sun's distance leaves 1006 Earth semidiameters, which is 865,160 German miles. It is not likely that such a vast interval between the two luminaries should be entirely devoid of stars. 9. Concerning the existence of stars in the signs, when the orbs of the luminaries and other planets are understood to be affixed to the sky, etc. Therefore, when we say, for example, that the Sun is in Aries, it is not to be understood as if the Sun's highest heaven, approaching, is carried among the fixed stars, but rather that it moves under that part of the zodiac which is called Aries. The author of the Sphere teaches that planets exist in the signs in four ways. But the true and most exact place of the Sun or planets lacking latitude in the ecliptic is indicated by a line extended from the center of the Earth through the center of the star to the outermost heaven. As for those stars that have latitude and move outside the ecliptic, their true places are most certainly shown by that part of the periphery which extends through the pole of the Zodiac and the center of the star to the ecliptic; for that point of contact in the Zodiac is the true place. 29. the focus of that star according to longitude. In this way, the polar star, as they call it, of the third magnitude, although it is 66 degrees away from the ecliptic, is now referred to 20 degrees 52 minutes of Gemini, according to the Prutenic calculation. 10. The Dragon is a figure that the paths of the Sun and Moon create. For the plane of the Moon's orbit intersects the Sun's ecliptic along the world's diameter, so that one part of it deviates 5 degrees to the north from the ecliptic, and the other to the south. This inclination of the Moon's orbit in opposite directions is called the winding path of the Dragon, from a certain similarity to huge serpents whose belly is looser and wider than their head and tail. One of these intersections, where the Moon ascends to the northern region, is called the Dragon's Head; the Greeks call it anabibazon, that is, the ascending or rising node, whose usual character is ☊. The other intersection, where the Moon descends to the south, is commonly called the Dragon's Tail; the Greeks call it katabibazon, that is, the descending or lowering node, and its usual character is ☋. The northern limit (boreum poras) is the midpoint of the northern lunar semicircle. The southern limit is directly opposite the former, similarly the midpoint of the southern lunar semicircle: the common people call these the bellies of the Dragons. With these things being so, it is manifest that between both limits and the nodes themselves, quadrants of a circle are encompassed, and the Moon, when positioned in the nodes, is entirely deprived of latitude, and attains its maximum latitude at the limits. Finally, it must be known that these points of the Moon's nodes and limits are not fixed, but daily move from east to west by 3 minutes, so that a reason can be given why lunar eclipses always occur when the Moon is not at its closest. CONCERNING THE HOUSES OF THE PLANETS. 1. The planets also have certain powers in these signs, some by nature, some by accident. Those that are by nature are these: House, Exaltation, Term, Triplicity, Face. We will treat those that are by accident in a convenient place. The Houses are these: Aries and Scorpio, houses of Mars; Taurus and Libra, houses of Venus; Gemini and Virgo, houses of Mercury; Cancer, house of the Moon; Leo, house of the Sun; Sagittarius and Pisces, houses of Jupiter; Capricorn and Aquarius, houses of Saturn. 2. The seventh sign from the house of each planet is said to be its detriment. 3. And if two signs are the house of one planet, they are said to be concordant in the Almantica, that is, in the circle which is broad in the middle and tightly bound, and is meant to signify the Zodiacal circle. 4. The signs in which planets are said to rejoice when they enter them, and their lords, according to Dorotheus, are these: Saturn is said to rejoice when it enters Aquarius; Jupiter in Sagittarius; Mars in Scorpio; Venus in Taurus; Mercury in Virgo. 1. Having set forth the division of the Zodiac and the general constitution of the signs, as the order explained above requires, it now wishes to show other properties of the signs derived from the familiarity of the luminaries and other wandering stars. But perhaps someone here will criticize me, saying that this is a new exposition or one alien to the author's meaning. For since the Author here prefaces that he will speak of the powers of the planets, both inherent and accidental, he does not seem to want to say in what ways the general constitution of the signs is intensified or remitted. But our exposition is not without reason. For he who discusses how the virtue of the planets is intensified by kindred qualities and the familiarity of suitable signs, or diluted by contrary ones, at the same time tacitly treats the intensification and remission of the qualities inherent in the signs. For those temperaments that are associated with similar ones make the quality of both concurring more efficacious, as: because the Sun and Leo agree; therefore, when the Sun passes through Leo, it increases the heat and dryness of the sign, and the power of the star in turn aids the kindred quality of the sign of Leo. From this, it is clear that this treatment of the association and agreement of the planets with the signs pertains to increasing or decreasing both the power of the signs and the power of the planets. However, I do not see how the author can make the familiarity of the planets with the signs partly natural and partly accidental. For since he admits that the stars are naturally reconciled to the signs by house, exaltation, trigon, and terms, what reason can remain by which the stars are accidentally associated with the signs? None. Since this is so, therefore, in the last chapter, which he titled 'Concerning the Accidental Powers of the Planets,' he could justly compare not the stars to the signs, but the temperament of diurnal and nocturnal time. The sex of the signs is also not accidental, but natural. But the twelve stations themselves and the quadrants of the world, which either the daily motion of the heavens or the places of the luminaries, as will appear below, create, can have nothing in common with the signs. Let us therefore hold to the method explained by us above, and to be repeated here again for its great utility, namely, that we must observe the disposition of both the signs and the stars, both cosmically and natally. And so that he may first speak of the signs, certain general qualities are found in them, such as those already explained in the first chapter. These same qualities are then increased, as will now be said, by the association of the planets when their temperaments agree, or they are diluted and weakened when the qualities are contrary. If you add to these the properties of the signs peculiarly inherent in each, and generated from mutual comparison, you will have all the cosmic constitutions of the signs. The remaining power or weakness of the signs, which accrues by reason of the position of the heavens, is called 'according to birth' or 'fortuitous disposition.' For the daily motion of the heavens, with its ineffable swiftness, varies the position of the heavens not only every hour, but every moment of time, and leads the celestial signs to other and other places. From this it follows that the effects and significations of one and the same sign, affected in the same way by its nature, are diverse.

Nam Luna à primo exortu vſque ad primæ quadraturaincrementum, cùm quaſi dimidiata refulget, efficiens eſt hu miditatis maximè:Inde verò vſq; ad plenilunium, caloris; Deinde à plenilunio ad dimidiationem vſque diminuta, ficcitatis: Denique à ſecunda quadratura ad occultationé vſque, frigiditatis.Stella quoq; errantes matutinæ vfque ad primam ſtationem, humoris habent plurimum:à prima verò ſtatione vſq; dú oriuntur de nocte, magis calefaciunt:Hinc à nocturna fulfione vſque ad ſtationem ſecundam, magis deficcant: à ſecunda deniq; ſtatione ad occaſum vſque, magis frigefaciunt. Ex his verbis eſt perſpiciuum, quòd Luna & tres ſuperiores (Nam Venerem & Mercurium ab hac comparatione, quòd nuquàm ad oppoſitionem Solis veniat, Ptolemeus eximit) reſpectu Solis qua tuor ſtatus habeant. Primus ſtatus ab exortu quo radios Solis exuunt, capit principium, & ad primam quadraturam extenditur. Hinc fecundus ſtatus, ad oppoſitionem vſque durat, à qua certius Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 2==
translation: That which says the strength of Saturn is intensified in the northern region does not agree with Ptolemy's statements. For it has been demonstrated above that the north agrees with Jupiter, just as Venus agrees with the south, Saturn is tempered in the east, and Mars in the west.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Domiciles and Qualities
text: ==Start of OCR for page 1== •: : PRIMA. 33 prudenter deputatum eſt, vt pro qualitate generis ſui vterque ſigno ſibi ſexu congruenti præeffet. Cæteri autem planetæ, nempe 14 & & & finguli binis in ſignis hoſpitantur, quorum vnum maſculinum, alterum fœmininum eſt: vnum item in medietate deſcendente, alterum in medietate aſcendente collocatur. Sed hoſpitijs luminarium vtrinque proxima ſunt Mercurij domicilia, quæ rurfus contingunt habitationes Veneris, has circundant Martis ſedes, has Iouis, has denique Saturni, cuius rei compendium tibi in ſequenti figura de- ſcripfimus. カ 4 : : ن X ४ ४ C 우 Ecce quam aptè & prudenter ſapiens antiquitas fingulis planetis ſua domicilia conſtituerit. Nam & 5, cùm proximè ad vertices noſtros accedant, acreliquis ſignis rectius noſtras regiones feriant, duobus luminaribus, quæ magnitudine, claritate atque effectibus reliquos planetas facilè ſuperant, hoſpitium præbent, idque non indifferenter, ſed ſignum masculinum, diurnum & calidum, Solem ijſdem qualitatibus abundantem excipit:Lunam verò femininam, noturnam, frigidam & humidam, Cancer eiufdem naturæ fouet. Singula quoque domicilia luminaribus conuenienter attributa eſſe videntur, neluminarium vis & poteftas, qua ſanè omnium corporum cœle- ſtium eſt præcipua, pluribus imperijs diſtracta diſsiparetur & redderetur languidior. Neque enim fine Cam ſa Capricornú & Aquarium Saturno familiares eſſe voluerunt. Nam cùm Saturnus ſit maior infortuna, temperatura frigidiſsimus & tenebricoſus, atque ſummum remotifsimumq; orbem teneat, maximè veritati conſentaneum eſt, eum imperium ac potentiam fuam imprimis oftendere in ſignis à domicilijs luminarium remotifsimis, maleficis & frigidiſsimis, atque domibus luminarium oppoſitis, qui intuitus inſigniter malus eſt. - Opponitur autem Capricornus domicilo Lunæ, hoc eſt, Cancro:& Aquarius domicilio Solis, hoc eſt, Leoni. Proximè in ordine Saturnum ſequitur Iuppiter, qui cùm fit fortuna maior, itaque conuenienter eum hoſpitantur Sagittarius & Pifces, quorum vtrunque & naturæ Iouiæ eſt, quum Piſcium ſignum fœcundum fit, Sagittarius verò ventos moueat. Intuentur præ- terea optimo, hoc eſt, trino aſpectu domus luminarium. Sed videt, Pisces vero 5.Iouem ſequitur in ordine infortuna minor. Proinde familiaris eſt & m ſignis luminarium domus tetragono, id eſt, aſpectu mediocriter malo reſpicientibus. Verùm Scorpius Leonem, Aries Cancrum percutit. Eſt aurem Tauri ſignum fœcundum inter animalia, ſignum Libra fecundum in ſeminibus. Ergo poſtquam breuiter eſt indicatum, qui planetæ quibus in ſignis domeſticas vires colligant, ſubiiciuntur iam ſigna, in quibus tanquàm in exilium pulſi, exulant, fuarumque virium non exigua iacturam faciunt. Econtrà verò in conſtitú- tus, etſi perinde vt in a domo exulet, tamen cognata ſigni na tura vim nocendi ob immoderatam frigiditatem aſſumit. Nam Setſi ſignum eſtium fit, tamen propter familiaritatem Lunæ frigidum eſt & humidum. At cum figna, &c ſigna bina, quibus vnum planetam præeſſe dictúm eſt, inæquaJes aſcenſiones habeant, ſatis apparet, autorem hic aliud intelEz ligere Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 3== ==Start of OCR for page 4== 36 DIFFERENTIA ligere per concordantiam ſignorum in ſignifero, quam ſupri per concordantiam eorundem in itinere. Fortaſſe bina ſigna vnum planetam hoſpitantia, ideo nominat concordantia in Zo diaco, quòd equaliter à domicilijs luminariumabfcedant: vt ४ &,domus Veneris funt, & à & 5, domibus Solis & Lu næ diſtant vtrinque diſtantia equali. Pari ratione reliquorum planetarum domicilia à domibus luminarium æqualiter remota in figura paulò antè deſcripta vides. Quòd verò dicit, huiuf modi figna vocari concordantia in Almantica, hoc eft,circulo, qui latus eſt in medio & in ligatura ſtriaus, notatio ligniferi eſt, quam ex ſequenti deſcriptione Zodiaci facilè intelliges. A communi ſe&ione coluri ſolſticiorum & eclipticæ vtrinq; numerentur 6.vel 8. partes (nam Venus penè partes 8. à meta So lis ſolet diſcedere) atque ad fines horum arcuum intelligantur ex centro mundi duazlineæ, qua vnà cum coluro ſolſticiali ſuper polis ecliptica reuoluta, deſcribent Zodiaci formam, quæ à centro terræ parte anguſtiſsima exiens, in extremo orbe fatis latum amplumque ſpacium occupat. Hanc latitudinem ipfius ecliptica,id eft, planum in quo Sol perpetuò ambulat,bifariam & in æquales partes dirimit. Per ligaturam igitur intelligit commiſſuram æquino&ialis cum ecliptica, quæ ſicut Hori zon, meridianus, aut alius circulus, extremitas plana abſque om ni latitudine eſſe intelligitur. Quádo dicit: Latus eſt in medio, intelligit craſsiciem ſigniferi, que cùm non tấtùm in extremo, fed & in medio cœlo, per quodSol decurrit, ſatis lata ſit, nullam certam ligaturam ſeu interſe&ionem cum æquinoctiali efficere poteſt. Signs au- 4 Vltima clauſulæ ſententia eſt, quòd præter & vnuftem, &c. quiſque planeta duas habeat domos, & harum altera magis ipſi familiaris, dicatur eius gaudium. Talem tantamque dignitatem precipuam & primam eſſe, haud eſt dubium. At Ptolemzo vice verſagaudium eſt, planetz dignitas ceteris omnibus poftrema. Nam etſi ſtella fuerit in locis nu
translation: It was wisely appointed that each of the two luminaries, according to the quality of its genus, should preside over a sign congruent to its sex. The other planets, namely Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, and Mercury, each reside in two signs, one masculine and one feminine: one is placed in the descending half, the other in the ascending half. But the domiciles of Mercury are closest to the luminaries on both sides, which in turn touch the habitations of Venus, these are surrounded by the seats of Mars, these by Jupiter, and finally by Saturn, a summary of which we have described for you in the following figure. Behold how aptly and wisely ancient wisdom established their domiciles for each planet. For the Sun and Moon, as they approach our zenith most closely, and strike our regions more directly than the other signs, provide hospitality to the two luminaries, which easily surpass the other planets in magnitude, clarity, and effects, and this not indifferently, but a masculine, diurnal, and hot sign receives the Sun, which abounds in the same qualities: while Cancer, being feminine, nocturnal, cold, and humid, fosters the Moon of the same nature. Each domicile also seems to have been conveniently attributed to the luminaries, lest the power and might of the luminaries, which is indeed the chief of all celestial bodies, should be distracted by too many commands and rendered weaker. That the kingdoms of the other wandering planets were assigned with equal prudence can easily be shown.

Nam Luna à primo exortu vſque ad primæ quadraturaincrementum, cùm quaſi dimidiata refulget, efficiens eſt hu miditatis maximè:Inde verò vſq; ad plenilunium, caloris; Deinde à plenilunio ad dimidiationem vſque diminuta, ficcitatis: Denique à ſecunda quadratura ad occultationé vſque, frigiditatis.Stella quoq; errantes matutinæ vfque ad primam ſtationem, humoris habent plurimum:à prima verò ſtatione vſq; dú oriuntur de nocte, magis calefaciunt:Hinc à nocturna fulfione vſque ad ſtationem ſecundam, magis deficcant: à ſecunda deniq; ſtatione ad occaſum vſque, magis frigefaciunt. Ex his verbis eſt perſpiciuum, quòd Luna & tres ſuperiores (Nam Venerem & Mercurium ab hac comparatione, quòd nuquàm ad oppoſitionem Solis veniat, Ptolemeus eximit) reſpectu Solis qua tuor ſtatus habeant. Primus ſtatus ab exortu quo radios Solis exuunt, capit principium, & ad primam quadraturam extenditur. translation: As for regions, they first ascribe the first climate to Saturn with his houses and signs. The second to Jupiter with his signs. The third to Mars with his signs. The fourth to the Sun with his sign. The fifth to Venus with her signs, which inclines Italians to luxury and effeminacy. The sixth to Mercury with his signs. The seventh to the Moon with her sign.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Detriment and Exaltation
text: ==Start of OCR for page 1== •: : PRIMA. 33 prudenter deputatum eſt, vt pro qualitate generis ſui vterque ſigno ſibi ſexu congruenti præeffet. Cæteri autem planetæ, nempe 14 & & & finguli binis in ſignis hoſpitantur, quorum vnum maſculinum, alterum fœmininum eſt: vnum item in medietate deſcendente, alterum in medietate aſcendente collocatur. Sed hoſpitijs luminarium vtrinque proxima ſunt Mercurij domicilia, quæ rurfus contingunt habitationes Veneris, has circundant Martis ſedes, has Iouis, has denique Saturni, cuius rei compendium tibi in ſequenti figura de- ſcripfimus. カ 4 : : ن X ४ ४ C 우 Ecce quam aptè & prudenter ſapiens antiquitas fingulis planetis ſua domicilia conſtituerit. Nam & 5, cùm proximè ad vertices noſtros accedant, acreliquis ſignis rectius noſtras regiones feriant, duobus luminaribus, quæ magnitudine, claritate atque effectibus reliquos planetas facilè ſuperant, hoſpitium præbent, idque non indifferenter, ſed ſignum masculinum, diurnum & calidum, Solem ijſdem qualitatibus abundantem excipit:Lunam verò femininam, noturnam, frigidam & humidam, Cancer eiufdem naturæ fouet. Singula quoque domicilia luminaribus conuenienter attributa eſſe videntur, neluminarium vis & poteftas, qua ſanè omnium corporum cœle- ſtium eſt præcipua, pluribus imperijs diſtracta diſsiparetur & redderetur languidior. Neque enim fine Cam ſa Capricornú & Aquarium Saturno familiares eſſe voluerunt. Nam cùm Saturnus ſit maior infortuna, temperatura frigidiſsimus & tenebricoſus, atque ſummum remotifsimumq; orbem teneat, maximè veritati conſentaneum eſt, eum imperium ac potentiam fuam imprimis oftendere in ſignis à domicilijs luminarium remotifsimis, maleficis & frigidiſsimis, atque domibus luminarium oppoſitis, qui intuitus inſigniter malus eſt. - Opponitur autem Capricornus domicilo Lunæ, hoc eſt, Cancro:& Aquarius domicilio Solis, hoc eſt, Leoni. Proximè in ordine Saturnum ſequitur Iuppiter, qui cùm fit fortuna maior, itaque conuenienter eum hoſpitantur Sagittarius & Pifces, quorum vtrunque & naturæ Iouiæ eſt, quum Piſcium ſignum fœcundum fit, Sagittarius verò ventos moueat. Intuentur præ- terea optimo, hoc eſt, trino aſpectu domus luminarium. Sed videt, Pisces vero 5.Iouem ſequitur in ordine infortuna minor. Proinde familiaris eſt & m ſignis luminarium domus tetragono, id eſt, aſpectu mediocriter malo reſpicientibus. Verùm Scorpius Leonem, Aries Cancrum percutit. Ijfdem de caufis Venus fortuna:minor ४ & fortita eſt, qua ſigna luminarium domos radio hexagono mediocriterque bo nopulſant.Ad hæc, cùm Veneris ſtella reliquis omnibus fit fecundior, meritò ſignorum fæcundorum cenfetur ſocia. Eſt aurem Tauri ſignum fœcundum inter animalia, ſignum Libra fecundum in ſeminibus. Reliqua duo ſigna, nempe II & np, Min.lib.z. quæ domus luminarium non vident, ſed vtrinque contingunt, cap.17. reli&a ſunt, quòd is à Sole nunquam plus 27. partibus ab- ſcedat. Quo fit, vt Solem nunquam aliquo aſpectu afpiciat. Veneris autem à Sole διάςασις maxima eſt par.48. ferè, itaque Soli aſpectu hexagono tantùm,& quidem platico coniungi poteſt. Beptimum 2 Neque verò tantum ſtellarum locorumque conciliationes, autem fio fed & loca aduerſantia his quibus familiariter copulantur, obgoum, &c. ſeruarę oportet. Nam quemadmodum virtus ſtellarum fi cum cognata qualitate locorum vniatur, longè fit efficacior:ita econ trario, vt Ptolemæus ait, ſi in locis alienis, & ſue conditionicon trariis, hoc eft, quæ opponuntur iis ſignis in quibus hoſpitancur extollunturus, autalio modo vires adaugent, reperiantur, vis Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 2== ==Start of OCR for page 3== 1 :: : FRIMA 35 vis corundem contraria & diſsimili ſignorum conſtitutione diluta & diſsipata vehementer langueſcit. Verba autem Ptolemei de hac re, hæc funt: ὅταμα'οῖς ἀλλωβιωμένος καλη και ίας αἱρέσεως τόπους καταλαμβάνονται, πολύ πραλύεται το π οικείας αὐτὴν διωάμεως, ἄλλημ τινά φύσιμματιὼ ἀποτελέσης τ κατά το ανόμοιομ την ποριεχόντωμζωδίωμκράσεως. Ergo poſtquam breuiter eſt indicatum, qui planetæ quibus in ſignis domeſticas vires colligant, ſubiiciuntur iam ſigna, in quibus tanquàm in exilium pulſi, exulant, fuarumque virium non exigua iacturam faciunt. Septimum, inquit autor, ſignum à domo vniAuſcuiufque planetæ, hoc eſt, ſignum oppofitum, planeta detrimentum dicitur: Alii vocant exilium. Ptolempus generaliter τόμ ἀλλωτριωμένομ καὶ τεναντίας αιρέσεως τόπομ nominat. Ex- "empli gratia, ſeptimum fignum à domo Solis, id eſt, à Leone cùm fit fignum Aquarij, idcirco Aquarij ſignum detrimentum Solis exiſtit.Sic Luna in Capricorni ſigno Cancrino dodecatemorio oppoſito, detrimentum patitur.Similiter in &४, in && domeſticas fuas vires diſsipant. Id detrimentum non parum adaugebitur, ſi præterea planetæ viribus ſigninatu rafuerit contraria. Veluti cùm eſt in, tum non tantùm exilio domus, fed & contraria ſigni intemperie debilitatus vires ad benefaciendum contrahit. Econtrà verò in conſtitú- tus, etſi perinde vt in a domo exulet, tamen cognata ſigni na tura vim nocendi ob immoderatam frigiditatem aſſumit. Nam Setſi ſignum eſtium fit, tamen propter familiaritatem Lunæ frigidum eſt & humidum. Ad eundem modum in altitudinibus vires fuas exerunt, atque eafdem in locis oppoſitis contrahunt, veluti in m, ficut dicemus, exaltatur, & in Cancri ſigno oppoſito proſternitur. Et fic de cæterarŭ dignitatum ſignis oppoſitis iudicandum eſt. Verùm in trigonis & finibus cùm id detrimétum non fit infigne, ſed breuiuſculum, ideo oppofita horum locorum Aſtrologi neglexerunt, & oppoſita domorum al titudinumque tantùm conſiderant. 3 Ex ſupradictis patet, ſigna concordantia in itinere vocari, Et fi duo quz in quolibet climate habent æquales anaphoras. At cum figna, &c ſigna bina, quibus vnum planetam præeſſe dictúm eſt, inæquaJes aſcenſiones habeant, ſatis apparet, autorem hic aliud intelEz ligere Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 3== ==Start of OCR for page 4== 36 DIFFERENTIA ligere per concordantiam ſignorum in ſignifero, quam ſupri per concordantiam eorundem in itinere. Fortaſſe bina ſigna vnum planetam hoſpitantia, ideo nominat concordantia in Zo diaco, quòd equaliter à domicilijs luminariumabfcedant: vt ४ &,domus Veneris funt, & à & 5, domibus Solis & Lu næ diſtant vtrinque diſtantia equali. Pari ratione reliquorum planetarum domicilia à domibus luminarium æqualiter remota in figura paulò antè deſcripta vides. Quòd verò dicit, huiuf modi figna vocari concordantia in Almantica, hoc eft,circulo, qui latus eſt in medio & in ligatura ſtriaus, notatio ligniferi eſt, quam ex ſequenti deſcriptione Zodiaci facilè intelliges. A communi ſe&ione coluri ſolſticiorum & eclipticæ vtrinq; numerentur 6.vel 8. partes (nam Venus penè partes 8. à meta So lis ſolet diſcedere) atque ad fines horum arcuum intelligantur ex centro mundi duazlineæ, qua vnà cum coluro ſolſticiali ſuper polis ecliptica reuoluta, deſcribent Zodiaci formam, quæ à centro terræ parte anguſtiſsima exiens, in extremo orbe fatis latum amplumque ſpacium occupat. Hanc latitudinem ipfius ecliptica,id eft, planum in quo Sol perpetuò ambulat,bifariam & in æquales partes dirimit. Per ligaturam igitur intelligit commiſſuram æquino&ialis cum ecliptica, quæ ſicut Hori zon, meridianus, aut alius circulus, extremitas plana abſque om ni latitudine eſſe intelligitur. Quádo dicit: Latus eſt in medio, intelligit craſsiciem ſigniferi, que cùm non tấtùm in extremo, fed & in medio cœlo, per quodSol decurrit, ſatis lata ſit, nullam certam ligaturam ſeu interſe&ionem cum æquinoctiali efficere poteſt. Signs au- 4 Vltima clauſulæ ſententia eſt, quòd præter & vnuftem, &c. quiſque planeta duas habeat domos, & harum altera magis ipſi familiaris, dicatur eius gaudium. Talem tantamque dignitatem precipuam & primam eſſe, haud eſt dubium. At Ptolemzo vice verſagaudium eſt, planetz dignitas ceteris omnibus poftrema. Nam etſi ſtella fuerit in locis nu
translation: Nor did they wish Capricorn and Aquarius to be familiar to Saturn without cause. For since Saturn is the greater infortune, coldest and darkest in temperament, and holds the highest and most remote orb, it is most consistent with truth that he should primarily show his dominion and power in signs most remote from the domiciles of the luminaries, malefic and coldest, and opposite to the houses of the luminaries, which aspect is notably evil. Capricorn is opposed to the domicile of the Moon, that is, Cancer: and Aquarius to the domicile of the Sun, that is, Leo. Next in order, Jupiter follows Saturn, who is the greater benefic, and thus Sagittarius and Pisces conveniently host him, both of which are Jovian in nature, since the sign of Pisces is fertile, and Sagittarius moves the winds. Moreover, they behold the houses of the luminaries with the best, that is, a trine aspect. But Jupiter sees Pisces, and Mars follows Jupiter in order as the lesser infortune. Therefore, he is familiar with Aries and Scorpio, which strike the houses of the luminaries with a square, that is, a moderately evil aspect. But Scorpio strikes Leo, and Aries strikes Cancer. For the same reasons, Venus, the lesser benefic, has Taurus and Libra, which strike the houses of the luminaries with a sextile aspect, which is moderately good. Moreover, since the star of Venus is more fertile than all others, it is rightly considered an associate of fertile signs. Taurus is a fertile sign among animals, the sign of Libra is fertile in seeds. The remaining two signs, namely Gemini and Virgo, which do not see the houses of the luminaries, but touch them on both sides, are left, because Mercury never departs more than 27 degrees from the Sun. Hence it is that he never aspects the Sun with any aspect. The maximum elongation of Venus from the Sun is about 48 degrees, and thus she can only be conjoined to the Sun by a sextile aspect, and that a platic one. But it is necessary to observe not only the conciliations of stars and places, but also the places adverse to those with which they are familiarly conjoined. For just as the virtue of stars, if united with a kindred quality of places, becomes far more efficacious: so on the contrary, as Ptolemy says, if they are found in alien places, and contrary to their condition, that is, which are opposed to those signs in which they are hosted or exalted, or in some other way increase their strength, their contrary and dissimilar virtue is greatly weakened and dissipated by the constitution of the signs. Ptolemy's words on this matter are these: "When they are found in places alien and of a contrary sect, much of their proper power is weakened, producing another nature according to the dissimilar mixture of the surrounding signs." Therefore, after it has been briefly indicated which planets gather their domestic strengths in which signs, there are now subjoined the signs in which, as if driven into exile, they are exiled, and suffer no small loss of their strengths. The seventh sign, says the author, from the house of each planet, that is, the opposite sign, is called the detriment of the planet: others call it exile. Ptolemy generally calls it "the alien place and of a contrary sect." For example, the seventh sign from the house of the Sun, that is, from Leo, since it is the sign of Aquarius, therefore the sign of Aquarius is the detriment of the Sun. Thus the Moon in the sign of Capricorn, opposite to the Cancerian dodecatemorion, suffers detriment. Similarly, Mars in Libra and Venus in Aries dissipate their domestic strengths. This detriment will be considerably increased if, moreover, the nature of the sign is contrary to the strengths of the planet. As when Mercury is in Sagittarius, then not only is he exiled from his house, but also weakened by the contrary intemperance of the sign, he contracts his powers for doing good. On the contrary, when Mercury is in Pisces, although he is exiled from his house just as in Sagittarius, yet the kindred nature of the sign assumes the power to harm due to immoderate coldness. For although Pisces is a watery sign, yet because of its familiarity with the Moon, it is cold and humid. In the same way, planets exert their strengths in their exaltations, and contract them in opposite places, as Mars, as we shall say, is exalted in Capricorn, and cast down in the opposite sign of Cancer. And so it must be judged concerning the opposite signs of the other dignities. But in triplicities and terms, since this detriment is not significant, but rather brief, astrologers have therefore neglected the opposites of these places, and only consider the opposites of houses and exaltations.

translation: What is to be thought of the parts of strength and stability, and all other strong parts, will be explained below in the fifth Difference. From what we have extensively noted here about Saturn, the diligent reader will easily grasp the descriptions of the other subsequent planets. For the method of explanation is everywhere the same and equal, and once understood in the consideration of one planet, it will not be difficult to adapt it as an example to the descriptions of the others.

Sed hoſpitijs luminarium vtrinque proxima ſunt Mercurij domicilia, quæ rurfus contingunt habitationes Veneris, has circundant Martis ſedes, has Iouis, has denique Saturni, cuius rei compendium tibi in ſequenti figura de- ſcripfimus. カ 4 : : ن X ४ ४ C 우 Ecce quam aptè & prudenter ſapiens antiquitas fingulis planetis ſua domicilia conſtituerit.
