Pari ratione reliquorum planetarum domicilia à domibus luminarium æqualiter remota in figura paulò antè deſcripta vides. Quòd verò dicit, huiuf modi figna vocari concordantia in Almantica, hoc eft,circulo, qui latus eſt in medio & in ligatura ſtriaus, notatio ligniferi eſt, quam ex ſequenti deſcriptione Zodiaci facilè intelliges. A communi ſe&ione coluri ſolſticiorum & eclipticæ vtrinq; numerentur 6.vel 8. partes (nam Venus penè partes 8. à meta So lis ſolet diſcedere) atque ad fines horum arcuum intelligantur ex centro mundi duazlineæ, qua vnà cum coluro ſolſticiali ſuper polis ecliptica reuoluta, deſcribent Zodiaci formam, quæ à centro terræ parte anguſtiſsima exiens, in extremo orbe fatis latum amplumque ſpacium occupat. Hanc latitudinem ipfius ecliptica,id eft, planum in quo Sol perpetuò ambulat,bifariam & in æquales partes dirimit. Per ligaturam igitur intelligit commiſſuram æquino&ialis cum ecliptica, quæ ſicut Hori zon, meridianus, aut alius circulus, extremitas plana abſque om ni latitudine eſſe intelligitur. Signs au- 4 Vltima clauſulæ ſententia eſt, quòd præter & vnuftem, &c. quiſque planeta duas habeat domos, & harum altera magis ipſi familiaris, dicatur eius gaudium. Talem tantamque dignitatem precipuam & primam eſſe, haud eſt dubium. At Ptolemzo vice verſagaudium eſt, planetz dignitas ceteris omnibus poftrema. Nam etſi ſtella fuerit in locis nu
translation: From the foregoing, it is clear that signs are called concordant in transit when they have equal anaphoras in any climate. But since the two signs, which are said to be ruled by one planet, have unequal ascensions, it is quite clear that the author here understands something different by the concordance of signs in the zodiac than by their concordance in transit. Perhaps he calls the two signs hosting one planet concordant in the Zodiac because they recede equally from the domiciles of the luminaries: for Taurus and Libra are the houses of Venus, and they are equally distant from Leo and Cancer, the houses of the Sun and Moon, on both sides. By the same reasoning, you see the domiciles of the other planets equally removed from the houses of the luminaries in the figure described a little earlier. But when he says that such signs are called concordant in the Almantica, that is, in a circle which is wide in the middle and narrow at the ligature, it is a notation of the zodiac, which you will easily understand from the following description of the Zodiac. From the common section of the solstitial colure and the ecliptic, count 6 or 8 parts on each side (for Venus usually departs almost 8 parts from the Sun's limit), and at the ends of these arcs, imagine two lines from the center of the world, which, together with the solstitial colure revolved around the poles of the ecliptic, will describe the form of the Zodiac, which, emerging from the center of the earth at its narrowest part, occupies a sufficiently wide and ample space in the outermost orb. This latitude of the ecliptic itself, that is, the plane in which the Sun perpetually moves, divides it into two equal parts. By ligature, therefore, he understands the junction of the equator with the ecliptic, which, like the Horizon, meridian, or other circle, is understood to be a flat extremity without any latitude. When he says: "It is wide in the middle," he understands the thickness of the zodiac, which, since it is sufficiently wide not only at the extreme but also in the middle of the sky through which the Sun runs, cannot form any certain ligature or intersection with the equator.

Nam Luna à primo exortu vſque ad primæ quadraturaincrementum, cùm quaſi dimidiata refulget, efficiens eſt hu miditatis maximè:Inde verò vſq; ad plenilunium, caloris; Deinde à plenilunio ad dimidiationem vſque diminuta, ficcitatis: Denique à ſecunda quadratura ad occultationé vſque, frigiditatis.Stella quoq; errantes matutinæ vfque ad primam ſtationem, humoris habent plurimum:à prima verò ſtatione vſq; dú oriuntur de nocte, magis calefaciunt:Hinc à nocturna fulfione vſque ad ſtationem ſecundam, magis deficcant: à ſecunda deniq; ſtatione ad occaſum vſque, magis frigefaciunt. Ex his verbis eſt perſpiciuum, quòd Luna & tres ſuperiores (Nam Venerem & Mercurium ab hac comparatione, quòd nuquàm ad oppoſitionem Solis veniat, Ptolemeus eximit) reſpectu Solis qua tuor ſtatus habeant. Primus ſtatus ab exortu quo radios Solis exuunt, capit principium, & ad primam quadraturam extenditur. Hinc fecundus ſtatus, ad oppoſitionem vſque durat, à qua certius Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 2==
translation: The last part of the narration about the Moon, which begins there (And some said that it signifies childhood from the beginning of the month, up to 7 days, etc.), is worthy of annotation, and especially what follows there (And some said that the Moon, indeed, at the conjunction, up to half of its first light, will be of a humid nature, etc.). For the former of these parts expresses the opinion of others; the latter, however, expounds Ptolemy's view on the different configurations of the Moon and other planets to the Sun. Ptolemy's words on this matter are these: "The Moon and the three wandering planets, namely Saturn, Jupiter, and Mars, receive more or less of their inherent powers according to their configurations to the Sun. For the Moon, from its first rising until the increment of the first quadrature, when it shines as if halved, is most effective of humidity; from there until the full moon, of heat; then from the full moon until the diminished half, of dryness; finally, from the second quadrature until its occultation, of coldness. The wandering stars, when morning stars, until the first station, have most humidity; from the first station until they rise at night, they heat more; from the nocturnal shining until the second station, they dry more; finally, from the second station until their setting, they cool more." From these words, it is clear that the Moon and the three superior planets (for Ptolemy excludes Venus and Mercury from this comparison, because they never come to opposition with the Sun) have four states with respect to the Sun. The first state begins from their rising, when they cast off the Sun's rays, and extends to the first quadrature. From here, the second state lasts until the opposition, from which more certainly...

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary States and Qualities
text: ==Start of OCR for page 3== : : } 1 : : 1 3 3 S ; ; ب SECVNDA 255 tertius ad quadraturam fecundam deriuatur. Vltimus deniq; ſtatus occultatione finitur. Hinc manifeſtum eſt, ſidera cum Sole meantia eiuſq; radijs oppreſſa, quaſi extra omnem ftatum pofita, nullas obtinere vires. Quòd ſi itaque illud quoque ſpacium, quo fidera ob occultatione ad primam fulfionem vſque radijs Solaribus inuoluta tenentur, huic comparationi coniun gere voluerimus, quinque omninò ſtatus ipforum reſpectu So lis exiftere deprehendemus, quorum vnus, poſtremus videlicet, eſt in quo nihil penitùs poſſunt, & idcirco à Ptolemzo meritò omittitur.Differentia quoque quatuor ſtatuum confidera tione digniſsıma eſt. In primo hercle ſtatu ſtellæ pofitæ illu- ſtres funt.Nam recens radios Solis egreſſæ omnibus fiunt confpicuz, & puram finceramque ſubſtantiam laminis obtinent, perinde, vt corpora recenter nata, humidiore & puriore con- ſtant ſubſtantia quam adultorů. Proindè tum infantiam & pueritiam repræſentantes, naturæ ipforum conſentanee, ſubſtantias & impreſsiones magis humidas quàm calidas faciét, & prefertim Luna. Ea enim cum, vt Ptolemæus docet, propter vicini tatem humores augeat, maximè profectò humectabit, cùm in quadraturis terræ proximè admota fuerit. Habebit etiam mediocré calore, quòd in tali ſtatu à Solemediocre lumé hauriat: itaque ob redundantiam humoris calorem facilè ſupprimentis maximè putrefaciet. Hac de cauſa primam quadram à nonnullis puerilem nominatam puto. Nam & in horum corporibus ſubſtantia puri humidi calorem facilè ſuperare videmus.Stella fecundum ſtatum adeptæ, calidiores funt,idq; cùm propter ab- ſumptioné humidi, tumverò propter incrementu caloris.Nam cùm fidera lumine à Sole hauſto principaliter calefaciant, maxi mè tú calefaciét, cùm impleta creſcenti lumine fulſerint. Actas quoq; que pueritia ſequit, vehemétius calet, & abundantia pue riliú humorú magis magiſq; abolet. In tertio ſtatu dominatur ficcitas.Sed dicat aliqs, quomodo tres ſuperiores in oppoſitione Solis, pleno amplissimoq; lumine micantes, & prætereà peri geum parui circuli luſtrantts, terræ propinquiores facti, deficcabunt,cùm dictum fit, ſtellas tantò magis vapores trahere atque humectare, quantò propinquiores terræ fuerint. Luna quoque ab oppoſitione Solis, ad fecundam quadraturam tendens Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 3== ==Start of OCR for page 4== 256 DIFFERENTIA dens, fimul quoque ab apogeo eccentri ad weirdo eiufdem properat? Refpondemus, ſtellas in oppoſito Solis collocatas fieri ficciores ſecundum qualitatem, non ſecundum ſubſtantiam. Cui dubium eſt, corpora à vigore declinantia,&preſertim obeſa maiorem habere copiam ſeu quantitatem humiditatis, quam antea in puerili ętate habuerunt, & tamen dicuntur ficca, non quidem ſecundum quantitatem, quæ maxima eſt, ſed ſe cundum qualitatem. Nam in ętate priori partes humide & con coctæ dominantur ficcitati & cruditati:in poſteriore verò partes ficcæ & crude humiditatem innatam facilè ſupprimunt.Sic ſubſtantia ſeu quantitas luminis trium fuperiorum, in Solis oppoſito collotatorum, amplissima eſt & illuſtriſsima, ſed ficca ſecundum qualitatem.Similiter de Luna iudicandum eſt. Sed fi ficca eft, quare tunc & præfertim in quadratura impletio nem ſequente humectat plurimú, & humectando maximè putrefacit,quæ omnia ea experientia conſtant, quòd nullo tempo re magis quàm in vltima quadra peſtis ſæuiat, nulloq; tempore oceani æſtus magis exundare foleant? Refpondemus, Lunæ cor pus per ſe reliquis omnibus eſſe humidius, & ob ocyſsimos cir cuitus, non ita multum conſeneſcere, fuamq; naturam humidi tatis perpetuò maxima ex parte tutari. Ergo in quadratura fe cunda terram proximè accedens, humectabit plurimum,&propter decrementum luminis & caloris maximè putrefaciet,& oceanum, quòd tunc vtraque qualitate, humiditate nimirum & frigiditate elemento aquarum potifsimum confentiat, ingenti impetu rapiet. In quarto denique ſtatu fidera frigere haud eft dubium:tum enim à terra tolluntur, & Solem propius acceden tia minus luminis nobis obuertunt. Et dixerut 19 Circa finem huius narrationis adijcit quorundam opinioquida &c. nes de planetarum cum animi qualitatibus συνοικφώσφ. Ptole mæus duobus planetis, nempe Mercurio & Lunæ qualitates aLib.3.cap. nimi principaliter conſentire docet, atque Mercurio quidem πουλί ποιός το νοερομική λογικόμη ψυχῆς μέρθ,hoceft, partem animz intelTATO ligentem atque ratiocinantem : Lune verò τὸ ἀθικὸμ κὶ ἄλογομ Xus. μέρ, id eft, partem animæ hebetiorem & mores regentem attribuit.Sanè intellectus habet imaginationé, ratiocinationem, memoriam,&reliquas animi qualitates, que ad ingenium exco len dum Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 4== ==Start of OCR for page 5== : : : SECVNDA. 259 lendum mentemque illuſtrandam pertinent, quibus omnibus cùm Mercurius principaliter conſentiat compatiaturque,per- ſpicuum eſt, cur afflicto Mercurio, ingenium quoque & mens ipſa obtufa caligine perturbetur. At pars animæ hebetior nihil aliud eft, quam inferiorum virium & voluntatis erga iudicium intelle&us obediétia. Habet ergo iuftitiam, fortitudinem, tem perantiam,& reliquas virtutes morales, quæ sub his generibus continentur:quibus omnibus cùm Luna conſentiat, liquet,cur Luna laborante exoriantur homines à communiconfuetudine & vfu vitę declinantes. Sed cur Lunam viribus inferioribus, Mercurium verò parti animæ ſuperiori pręeſſe voluerut? Quia, vt supra di&um eſt, Luna cerebro, vnde nerui ſenſus organa deriuantur, præeft, atque ob id non immeritò regit eas virtutes, quæ à ſenſibus pendent, eifdemque funt propinquiores. Mercurius verò cùm habeat cerebri fpiritus, qui ſunt organa mentis a&ionum, idcirco magis omnibus ijs dominatur, quæ à mente proficifcuntur. Ad hæc quiuis alius quoque planeta peculıhrium animæ qualitatum fons exiftit. Saturnus itaque fons eſt eius potentię quæ retentiua dicitur, vnde memoriam firmă, perfeuerantiam, laborumque tolerantiam gignit. Iuppiter habet probitatem fimplicitatemque. Mars fortitudinem, promptitudinem, audaciam, iram & crudelitatem. Soli familiaris eſt φιλοτιμία & faſtus. Venus amat luxum & elegantiam.Sicut autem finguli planetæ certis membris principaliter conſentiunt: ita quoque hæ animi qualitates ex diuerfis partibus corporis fumunt originem, nec vnquam educuntur ad actum, nifi priùs à neruis & ſpiritibus cerebri pulſatæ exuſcitentur. Quapropter ſapienter Ptolemæus non tantùm loca) & \ perpendit, ſed & horum locorum dominatores, fiquidem illi ipſi ſuas in anima qualitates ſuſcitant. 20 Vltima claufula, quæ incipit,ibi (Et quia diximus in hoc ca pitulo,&c.) quid fignificare voluerit, vix conijcio. Ac nefcio, in. Et quia di terpreti ne, an verò fcriptori potius hæc obfcuritas adfcribi ximus, &c. debeat. Puto tamen ſententiam eſſe, ex commiſtione mutua ſtel larum, plurimas alias temperaturas exoriri, vt ſit verborum expoſitio:Sicut diuerſi planetarum ad Solem poſitus vires eorum variant, vt paulò antea dictum eſt:ita eodem modo mutua inter fe comKk : i Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 5==
translation: The third state is derived to the second quadrature. Finally, the last state ends in occultation. Hence it is clear that stars moving with the Sun and oppressed by its rays, as if placed outside all states, obtain no powers. If, therefore, we wish to join to this comparison that space also, in which the stars are held enveloped by the Sun's rays from occultation until the first shining, we will find that there are five states of them with respect to the Sun, one of which, namely the last, is that in which they can do absolutely nothing, and therefore it is rightly omitted by Ptolemy. The difference of the four states is also most worthy of consideration. In the first state, indeed, the stars are illustrious. For having recently emerged from the Sun's rays, they become conspicuous to all, and obtain a pure and sincere substance in their plates, just as recently born bodies consist of a more humid and purer substance than those of adults. Therefore, then representing infancy and childhood, consistent with their nature, they will produce substances and impressions more humid than hot, and especially the Moon. For when, as Ptolemy teaches, it increases humors due to its proximity, it will certainly moisten most, when it has been moved closest to the earth in the quadratures. It will also have moderate heat, because in such a state it draws moderate light from the Sun: and so, due to the superabundance of humor easily suppressing heat, it will putrefy most. For this reason, I think the first quarter is called childish by some. For even in the bodies of these, we see that the substance of pure humidity easily overcomes heat. Stars that have attained the second state are hotter, both because of the absorption of humidity and because of the increase in heat. For since the stars primarily heat by the light drawn from the Sun, they will heat most when they shine with increasing light. The age that follows childhood also heats more vehemently, and the abundance of childish humors diminishes more and more. In the third state, dryness dominates. But someone may say, how will the three superior planets, shining with full and ample light in opposition to the Sun, and moreover traversing the perigee of the small circle, becoming closer to the earth, dry out, since it has been said that stars draw vapors and moisten more, the closer they are to the earth? The Moon also, moving from the opposition of the Sun to the second quadrature, and at the same time hastening from the apogee of the eccentric to its weirdo? We answer that stars placed in opposition to the Sun become drier according to quality, not according to substance. Who doubts that bodies declining in vigor, and especially obese ones, have a greater abundance or quantity of humidity than they had before in childhood, and yet they are said to be dry, not indeed according to quantity, which is greatest, but according to quality. For in the former age, humid and concocted parts dominate dryness and crudity: in the latter, however, dry and crude parts easily suppress innate humidity. Thus the substance or quantity of light of the three superior planets, placed in opposition to the Sun, is most ample and illustrious, but dry according to quality. Similarly, the Moon is to be judged. But if it is dry, why then, and especially in the quadrature following the fullness, does it moisten most, and by moistening, does it putrefy most, all of which are confirmed by experience, that at no time does the plague rage more than in the last quarter, and at no time do the tides of the ocean usually overflow more? We answer that the body of the Moon is by itself more humid than all the others, and because of its very swift circuits, it does not age so much, and for the most part perpetually preserves its nature of humidity. Therefore, in the second quadrature, approaching the earth most closely, it will moisten most, and because of the decrease of light and heat, it will putrefy most, and it will carry away the ocean with great impetus, because then it agrees most with the element of waters in both qualities, namely humidity and coldness. Finally, in the fourth state, there is no doubt that the stars are cold: for then they are removed from the earth, and approaching the Sun more closely, they turn less light towards us. And they said 19. Towards the end of this narration, he adds the opinions of some about the sympathy of the planets with the qualities of the soul. Ptolemy teaches that two planets, namely Mercury and the Moon, principally agree with the qualities of the soul, and indeed to Mercury he attributes the intellectual and rational part of the soul: but to the Moon he attributes the duller and moral-governing part of the soul. Indeed, the intellect has imagination, ratiocination, memory, and the other qualities of the soul that pertain to cultivating genius and illuminating the mind, with all of which Mercury principally agrees and sympathizes, it is clear why, when Mercury is afflicted, genius itself and the mind itself are disturbed by dull darkness. But the duller part of the soul is nothing other than the obedience of the lower powers and will to the judgment of the intellect. It therefore has justice, fortitude, temperance, and the other moral virtues that are contained under these categories: with all of which the Moon agrees, it is clear why, when the Moon is afflicted, men arise who deviate from common custom and the practice of life. But why did they want the Moon to preside over the lower powers, and Mercury over the higher part of the soul? Because, as was said above, the Moon presides over the brain, from which the nerves and organs of sensation are derived, and therefore it not undeservedly governs those virtues that depend on the senses and are closer to them. Mercury, however, since it has the spirits of the brain, which are the organs of the mind's actions, therefore dominates more than all those things that proceed from the mind. In addition to these, every other planet is also the source of peculiar qualities of the soul. Saturn, therefore, is the source of that power which is called retentive, from which it generates a firm memory, perseverance, and tolerance of labors. Jupiter has probity and simplicity. Mars has fortitude, promptness, audacity, anger, and cruelty. Ambition and pride are familiar to the Sun. Venus loves luxury and elegance. Just as individual planets principally agree with certain members: so also these qualities of the soul originate from different parts of the body, and are never brought into action unless they are first stimulated and aroused by the nerves and spirits of the brain. Therefore, Ptolemy wisely considered not only the places, but also the rulers of these places, since they themselves arouse their qualities in the soul. 20. The last clause, which begins, there (And because we said in this chapter, etc.) I can hardly guess what it meant to signify. And I do not know whether this obscurity should be attributed to the interpreter or rather to the writer. I think, however, that the meaning is that from the mutual mixture of the stars, many other temperaments arise, so that the exposition of the words is: Just as the different positions of the planets to the Sun vary their powers, as was said a little earlier: so in the same way, mutual inter se comKk

He funt • ὕψωμος, Exaltatio ſeu altitudo planetæ apud Aſtrologos, nó exaltatio intelligitur de ſitu locali ſeu diſtantia à terra, vel ab alio punnes, &c. &o,quali imaginatione deceptus eſt Plinius, ſed de parte ſigniLib.2.c.16. feri, quam cum ſtelle adeptæ fuerint, maximam mutationem in aére atque reliquis rebus inferioribus oftendunt. Sol extollitur in Ariete, quòd per Arietem in ſemicirculum Aquilonarem ſcandens, & dierum & caloris in corporibus euidentia incrementa faciat. Idem ex contraria cauſa in deie ctus iacet, ſiquidem, vt antea dicebamus, quantum virtutis & energiæ fignorum cognata qualitas ſtellis adijcit, tantum proprij roboris contrariorum ſeu oppoſitorum locorum diſsimilis conſtitutio eiſdem adimit. Quando luminaria coniunguntur in, tum Luna primùm apparet, luceque aucta refulget in Tauro, trianguli ſui ſigno principe,idcirco Taurus altitudo eius perhibetur, & Scorpius deiectio. Ad hæc cùm Iouis & Martis vires agendi fint planè diſsimiles: alter enim exempli gratia iuſtitia atque humanitatis, alter verò iniuſtitia atque feritatis autor eft, ob eam caufam altitudinum aduerfantia loca iure ſortiti ſunt. Sed Venus humiditatis & voluptatum domina, maximam vim oftendit in X,& in my deiicitur. Porro ſtella, vtaridiuſcula eſt, ingenii, eruditionis & reliquorú me tis bonorum largitrix, & omnino contrarię efficientiæ, ita con trariam Veneri altitudinem deiectionemque ſortita eft:altitudinem quidem in my, in qua Autumnalis ficcitas impendet: Deiectionem verò in Piſcibus. Dicit Haly, Mercurium in my exaltari, quòd cùm Soleſtin Leone, Mercurius veſperi in my apparere incipiat, id quod à fimilitudine Lunæ ſumptum eſt.. Hæc eftri ύψωματωμ καὶ ταπεινωματωμ ratio. Sed tamen hæc & pleraque alia in aſtrologia magis experientia quàm demon- ſtrationibus conſtant. 2 Sequitur dubitatio, an in vniuerfum per integra ſigna pla. Ptolemæ netæ extollantur, an verò in certis partibus fint exaltationes autem po obſervandæ. Hermes ſigna quæ hic enumerantur, altitudinis nit,&c. bus planetarum relinquit, ſed vnumquemque planetarum in parte 15. fui ſigni maximè exaltari ſtatuit, id quod cauſa carere minimè videtur. Eſt enim rationi conſentaneum, maiorem eſſe. vim planetæ, fi in medio ſigni ſuæ naturæ congruentis exiſtat, quam fi in eiuſdem principio aut fine verſetur. Iuniores Aſtrologos Ptolemæum, qui in totis ſignis planetas exaltari docet, ſequi video, concedentes tamen, maxime exaltari eos in partibus, quæ hic ab autore deſcribuntur poſitos. DE TRIPLICITATIBVS. i Triplicitates verò fic distinguimus. Omnia tria fig na, quæ in vna natura videntur concordare,ſaciunt tri plicitatem, & eodem nomine vocantur, hoc eft, Triplici tas. Connexionem trium ſignorum, quæ ex trigono ſe vident atq; naturam habent ſimilem, triplicitaté vulgò vocant, Græci Triplicita τρίγωνομ, Pontanus triangularitatem.Sed dicat aliquis,ſiquidé fic &c. id verum, quod paulò antè aſſerebamus, nempe ſignorum gene rales conſtitutiones temperaturæ & qualitatibus quatuoranni temporum conſentire, atque ſemper tria vniufcuiuſque quadrantis ſigna ſeſe mutuò ab aliquo punctorum cardinalium có ſequentia, quod ad craſin attinet, conuenire, nullo modo fieri poteſt, vt ſigna in diuerfis quadrantibus poſita, qualia in trigonis proponuntur, eandem naturam vimq; agendi ſimilem habeant. Refpondeo, ſigna vnius trigoni re vera per ſe non ſunt eiuſdem naturæ, quantum attinet ad temperaturam, quæ fuprà fignis tributa eſt, ſed ſunt ferè, & vt autor bene dicit, fiue id ca- ſu, fiue ſciens dixerit, videntur eſſe eiuſdé naturæ, cùm quia ob ſexú & fimilitudiné figurę, & radiú trigonű, familiaritatis quadá ſocietate cóueniunt, tú verò & maximè, quia eifdé dominis, quos F Bit quo, que hac triplicitas &c.. 42 DIFFERENTIA quos βιγωνοκράτορας, id eſt, Triagulatores nominat, paret atq; ab ijfdem gubernantur, regunturq;. Quemadmodum enim aliquis dominus tres famulos, vnum Germanum, exempli gratia, alterum Gallum, tertium Hiſpanum, quorum non ſolum natio, ſed & temperaturæ & mores per ſe diuerſa fint, gubernatione & imperio ad conſen ſum amicitiamq;, aut ſaltem ad vniformem & vnius modi omnium negotiorum tractationem affuefacere ſeu cogere poteſt:Ita quoque tria figna trigonú vnú contexentia, ob obediétiam, quam ex æquo ſimul eiſdem eiuſ- demq; ſectæ ſtellis preſtant, ad vnam naturam, eandemque vim agendi compelluntur, etiamſi per ſe ipſis inſita natura & moribus diſsideant.Sunt autem trigoni quatuor, quorű vnum Arabes vocant igneum, alterum terreum, tertium aëreum, quartu aqueum. Igneum trigonum faciunt &». Hec enim figna præter quàm quòd triquetra radiatione ſeſe aſpiciuut,dupliciter cõueniunt. Primò ratione ſimilitudinis formæ,omnia enim funt agreſtia, quadrupeda & fera: deinde natura, volunt enim omnia in genere eſſe naturæ igneæ, hoc eft, calida & ficca, masculina, diurna, choleram ſeu rubram bilem augentia, ſapore amara. Hinc, fi tempore & aut & luminarium aliquod ex his luſtret horoſcopum, hyemis frigus mitigare, æſtatis verò ardo rem immoderatum efficere tradunt, præſertim ſi domini vel triangulatores ſigni horoſcopantis præſentes fuerint, aut alibi trigonum occupauerint, aut denique ex aliquo figuræ angulo horoſcopum fortiter pulſauerint. Sed in genituris talem poſitum natum ad ignem choleramque inclina
translation: Just as the Sun is exalted in the 19th degree of Aries, so it falls in the 19th degree of Libra, and so on for the others. Ptolemy, however, places the entire sign of Aries as the exaltation of the Sun, and the entire sign of Taurus as the exaltation of the Moon, and similarly for the rest. These are the 'hypsomata', the exaltation or altitude of a planet among astrologers, not understood as a local position or distance from the earth, or from another point, etc., by which imagination Pliny was deceived (Book 2, Chapter 16), but rather as that part of the zodiacal sign which, when the stars have reached it, they show the greatest change in the air and in other inferior things. For example, when the Sun first enters the 19th part of Aries, it immediately shows us manifest spring. The reason for these altitudes is this: The Sun is exalted in Aries because, by ascending through Aries into the northern semicircle, it causes evident increases in both days and heat in bodies. For the opposite reason, it lies in its debility, since, as we said before, as much virtue and energy as the kindred quality of the signs adds to the stars, so much of their own strength is taken away by the dissimilar constitution of contrary or opposite places. Since each of the planets is exalted in that place where another of a different nature and efficiency suffers the contrary, and the Sun is the author of clarity and heat, while Saturn is the author of darkness and cold: therefore, just as with houses, so the places of Saturn's altitude are deservedly opposed to the Sun. Therefore, Saturn's altitude is in Libra, and its debility is in Aries. For where heat is intensified, there cold must be remitted; and conversely, where heat diminishes, cold increases. When the luminaries conjoin in Aries, then the Moon first appears, and with increased light, it shines forth in Taurus, the principal sign of its triplicity; therefore, Taurus is said to be its altitude, and Scorpio its debility. But Jupiter is exalted in Cancer, because it is the mover of the northern and more fertile winds. Therefore, being carried closest to the north, it shows its greatest strength in Cancer and Pisces, and is debilitated in Capricorn. And Mars, by its nature fiery, is exalted in Capricorn, opposite the sign of Jupiter's altitude, and by reason of Cancer being most remote from our zenith, and thereby tempering Mars' ardor. For the power of Mars, the more moderate it is, the greater it becomes. For the opposite reason, it is debilitated in Cancer. Moreover, since the powers of Jupiter and Mars in action are plainly dissimilar: for one, for example, is the author of justice and humanity, while the other is the author of injustice and ferocity, for this reason they have rightly obtained adverse places of altitude. But Venus, the mistress of humidity and pleasures, shows her greatest strength in Pisces, and is debilitated in Virgo. Furthermore, Mercury, which is rather dry, the giver of intellect, learning, and other mental goods, and altogether of contrary efficiency, has thus obtained an altitude and debility contrary to Venus: an altitude indeed in Virgo, where autumnal dryness impends; and a debility in Pisces. Haly says that Mercury is exalted in Virgo because when the Sun is in Leo, Mercury begins to appear in Virgo in the evening, which is taken from the similarity of the Moon. This is the reason for exaltations and debilities. But nevertheless, these and many other things in astrology are based more on experience than on demonstrations. A doubt follows, whether planets are exalted universally throughout entire signs, or whether exaltations are to be observed in certain parts. Hermes leaves the signs enumerated here to the altitudes of the planets, but he establishes that each planet is most exalted in the 15th part of its sign, which certainly does not seem to lack cause. For it is consistent with reason that the power of a planet is greater if it exists in the middle of a sign congruent with its nature, than if it is at its beginning or end. I see younger astrologers following Ptolemy, who teaches that planets are exalted in whole signs, conceding, however, that they are most exalted in the parts described here by the author. Regarding Triplicities: We distinguish triplicities as follows: All three signs that appear to agree in one nature form a triplicity, and are called by the same name, that is, Triplicity. Therefore, Aries, Leo, and Sagittarius form the first triplicity, because each of these signs is fiery, masculine, diurnal, namely hot and dry, choleric, and bitter in taste. This triplicity is also oriental. Its lords are the Sun by day, and Jupiter by night, and Saturn is a participant by day and night. The second triplicity consists of Taurus, Virgo, and Capricorn, because these are feminine, nocturnal, earthy signs, namely cold and dry, melancholic, and acrid in taste. This triplicity is meridional. The lords of this triplicity are Venus by day, and the Moon by night, of which Mars is a participant by day and night. The third triplicity consists of Gemini, Libra, and Aquarius, because these signs are masculine, diurnal, sanguine, namely hot, humid, airy, and sweet in taste. This triplicity is occidental. The lords of this triplicity are Saturn by day, and Mercury by night, of which Jupiter is a participant by day and night. The fourth triplicity consists of Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces, because these are feminine, nocturnal, northern, watery, phlegmatic, cold, humid, and salty in taste. The lords of this triplicity are Venus by day, and Mars by night, of which the Moon is a participant by day and night. Table of Houses, Exaltations, Joys, Triplicities, Detriments, and Falls of the Planets. Signs, Houses of the Planets, Exaltations, Joys, Triplicities, Detriments, Falls. Aries 19, Taurus 3, Gemini 21, Cancer 3, Leo 15, Virgo 27, Libra 19, Scorpio 3, Sagittarius 21, Capricorn 28, Aquarius 15, Pisces 27. The connection of three signs, which behold each other by trine aspect and have a similar nature, is commonly called a triplicity; the Greeks call it 'trigonon', Pontanus 'triangularity'. But someone might say, if this is true, what we asserted a little earlier, namely that the general constitutions of the signs agree with the temperament and qualities of the four seasons of the year, and that always three signs of each quadrant, following each other from one of the cardinal points, as far as their crassness is concerned, agree, it is in no way possible for signs placed in different quadrants, such as are proposed in trines, to have the same nature and similar power of action. I respond that the signs of one trine are not truly of the same nature in themselves, as far as the temperament attributed to the signs above is concerned, but they are nearly so, and as the author rightly says, whether he said it by chance or knowingly, they appear to be of the same nature, both because of their gender and similarity of figure, and the trine ray, they come together in a certain familiarity and society, and truly and most importantly, because they obey the same lords, whom he calls 'trigonokrators', that is, Triangulators, and are governed and ruled by them. For just as a master can accustom or compel three servants, one German, for example, another French, and a third Spanish, whose not only nationality, but also temperaments and customs are diverse in themselves, to agreement and friendship, or at least to a uniform and consistent handling of all affairs, so also three signs forming one trine, because of the obedience they equally render simultaneously to the same stars of the same sect, are compelled to one nature and the same power of action, even if they differ in their inherent nature and customs. There are four trines, one of which the Arabs call fiery, another earthy, a third airy, and a fourth watery. The fiery trine is formed by Aries, Leo, and Sagittarius. For these signs, besides beholding each other by trine radiation, agree in two ways. First, by reason of similarity of form, for all are wild, quadrupedal, and fierce; then by nature, for all wish to be of the fiery nature, that is, hot and dry, masculine, diurnal, increasing choler or red bile, and bitter in taste. Hence, if at the time of birth or at the time of a question, one of these luminaries illuminates the ascendant, they say it mitigates the cold of winter, but causes immoderate heat in summer, especially if the lords or triangulators of the ascendant sign are present, or have occupied the trine elsewhere, or finally have strongly struck the ascendant from some angle of the figure. But in nativities, such a position inclines the native to fire and choler.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Eastern Triplicity and Ptolemy's View
text: re coſentaneum eft. 2 Hanc primam triangularitatem Orientalem eſſe tradunt Arabes. Sed magis eſt credendum Ptolemzo, cuius præcepta argumentis ex ipſa natura depromptis & per experientiam có probata funt. Docet autem Ptolemæus, hunc trigonum eſſe ſeptentrionalem propter Sagittarium domum Iouis, participantem aliquomodo cuin occidente propter Arietem domú Martis. Cuius diſtributionis vt cauſa pateat, ſciédum eſt, quòd Ptolemeus ſingulis planetis ſingulas conuenienteſq; mudi plagas attribuat:vt Saturno orientem, quæ pars cùm diurna ſit atque calida, Saturni frigus quodammodo reducit ad temperiem. Septentrio Ioui, quòd ex hac parte fæcun di venti erumpere ſoleant, deputatur. Veneris ſtella propter humiditatem meridionali plagæ familiaris eſt. Ab hac diſtributione excipiuntur primò luminaria, quòd generales caufæ ventorum exiſtant:deinde Mercurius, quòd luminaribus perpetud adfit atque famuletur. Et cùm de ſententia Ptolemei vni cuique triangulo duo, vt mox patebit, preſint planetæ, decebat omnino non vnam, vt faciunt Arabes, ſed totidem omnino mundi plagas vnicuique triangulo attribuere, ex quibus prediti dominatores ſuos ventos excitare intelligerentur.Veluti in primo triangulo dominantur & 4. Idcirco primo triangulo eę partes attribuendę veniunt, ex quibus & 4 ventos eliciunt. Sed quoniam Sol generalis, vt diximus, omniú vento rű ex quo motor eſt, nec vni alicui vento peculiariter attribui queat, ideo Mars natura Soli perſimilis, atque domum in hoc trigono habens, in locum Solis fubrogatur. Hinc ventus ex bo reali & occidentali commiſtus colligitur, quem Ptolemeus βορρολιβυκόμ nominat. Nam Iuppiter ventos ſeptentrionales adducit, Mars verò occidentales, quòd plaga hęc fœminina fit, & ipſe Mars ex ſecta Lune, que occidenti dominatur, ficut Sol Orienti. Verba Ptolemei hec ſunt. γίνετης προκγομοίως μαν τ8ο το ζίγωνομ βόρφομ, δὲ τώ το 4 (ανοικοδεσποτείαμ. ἐπιδήπερ ούτος γόνιμα τε ότι καὶ πνδυματώδης, οἰκείως δῖς ἀλὸ ἢ αρκτωμ ἀνέμοις. λλά δε τόμ το σ' οἶκομ λαμβάνο μίξιμ το λιβός: καί (νίςας ται βορρολιβυκομ, ἐπιδήπερ ὁ το οι βιότωμόζὶ ανθυμάτωμ ποικε τικός, λλά το τότ) αἵρεσιμ, καὶ τὸ τὴν δυσμῶμ τεθηλυμένωμ. translation: The Arabs say that this first triplicity is Eastern. But Ptolemy is more to be believed, whose precepts are proven by arguments drawn from nature itself and by experience. Ptolemy teaches that this trigon is northern because of Sagittarius, the house of Jupiter, participating in some way with the west because of Aries, the house of Mars. So that the reason for this distribution may be clear, it should be known that Ptolemy attributes to each planet its own suitable region of the world: for example, to Saturn the east, which part, being diurnal and hot, reduces Saturn's cold to a certain temperateness. For a similar reason, he assigns Mars, the driest planet, to the humid and nocturnal western region. The north is assigned to Jupiter, because fertile winds usually break forth from this part. The star of Venus is familiar to the southern region because of its humidity. From this distribution, the luminaries are first excepted, because they are the general causes of winds; then Mercury, because he is perpetually present with and serves the luminaries. And since, according to Ptolemy's opinion, two planets preside over each trigon, as will soon be clear, it was entirely fitting to attribute not one, as the Arabs do, but exactly as many regions of the world to each trigon, from which the assigned rulers would be understood to excite their winds. As in the first trigon, Jupiter and Mars rule. Therefore, those parts from which Jupiter and Mars draw winds must be attributed to the first trigon. But since the Sun is, as we have said, the general mover of all winds, and cannot be attributed particularly to any one wind, Mars, being very similar to the Sun in nature and having a house in this trigon, is substituted in the Sun's place. Hence, a wind mixed from the boreal and occidental is collected, which Ptolemy calls Borrholibycus. For Jupiter brings northern winds, and Mars western winds, because this region is feminine, and Mars himself is of the sect of the Moon, which rules the west, just as the Sun rules the East. Ptolemy's words are these: "This trigon is primarily northern, because Jupiter exercises dominion there. For he is the author of blasts, and fertile, like the Aquilonian winds. But because of the house of Mars, Aphricus is mixed with it, and this trigon becomes Borrholibycus, that is, mixed from the north and Aphricus, because Mars excites Aphricus winds due to his familiarity, and because the western part is feminine." Ptolemy and the Arabs also disagree in the number and order of the triplicities. Ptolemy, since the seven planets were to be distributed conveniently among the four trigons, therefore assigned two planets to the first three trigons, and at least one to the fourth, as will be clear. The Arabs, however, make two principal lords for each trigon, one diurnal and the other nocturnal; and besides these, they add a third, participating in such dominion at both times. As in the first trigon, the Sun and Jupiter principally rule, to whom Saturn is added as a third participant in the dominion. But in nativities and the beginnings of other things, the diurnal lord is to be considered first in diurnal nativities, and the nocturnal lord in nocturnal nativities; then the remaining and participating lord. Therefore, if it is a diurnal nativity, and Leo is placed in the ascendant, for the nourishment of the native, according to the opinion of the Arabs, the lords of the Leonine triplicity should be considered, and in the first place the Sun, because it is a diurnal nativity, and afterwards Jupiter with Saturn as participant. Ptolemy, however, does not disagree with the Arabs in the ordering of the lords of the first triplicity, except that he postponed Saturn as a participant in both, and not without reason. For since Saturn is in detriment in Aries, it is not fitting for him to rule in the same trigon. Therefore, if three lords are absolutely required for each triplicity, the opinion of those who, rejecting Saturn, assume Mars as a participant seems more correct, which Ptolemy, however, rightly removes from the dominion of this diurnal triplicity because he is a nocturnal planet. For the dominion of a triplicity is nothing other than a certain power arising from the dignity of the house, exaltation, and sect, which the barbarians call Hayz. That is, in choosing the lords of the trigons, three things must be observed. First, that in diurnal trigons there are diurnal planets, and nocturnal planets in nocturnal trigons. Second, that masculine planets are referred to masculine trigons, and feminine planets to feminine trigons. Third, that since in every trigon there is one movable, one fixed, and finally one common sign, the ruler of the fixed sign is preferred to the others. Whatever planet has the greater testimony of suitability in these three is admitted to the office of triplicity ruler. Thus, because of the first two reasons, Mars is expelled from the first trigon, but because of the last reason, the Sun, the ruler of Leo, is preferred to Jupiter, etc. The earthy trigon is formed by Taurus, Virgo, and Capricorn, which signs, besides their gender and triplicity coherence, similarly agree in form, which is that of gentle and domesticated animals, except that to Virgo, because of Mercury's great power in that sign, human form is attributed, and then nature: for they are generally said to be cold and dry, melancholic, feminine, nocturnal, and acrid in taste. They also say that in the conjunction or opposition of the luminaries, if any of these, and especially Capricorn, illuminates the horoscope, the rigor of winter is greatly increased, especially if Saturn is found in that trigon.

translation: This trigon is also southern according to Ptolemy's opinion, because of Venus's dominion in it, whose humidity and heat agree with the temperament of the southern region, participating in the east because of Capricorn, the house of Saturn, who, being of the Sun's sect, is judged to be the mover of eastern winds. The Moon, however, since she is a general producer of winds like the Sun, and cannot be attributed particularly to any one wind, is excluded from this particular administration, and Saturn is substituted for her because of his house, which he possesses in this trigon. We have already warned above that Mercury is also excluded everywhere with the luminaries for a certain reason. Therefore, these signs, because of Venus and Saturn, agreeing with the south and east, move winds mixed from the southern and eastern regions, which Ptolemy therefore calls Notapelioticus. Ptolemy's words are these: "This trigon is primarily southern, because of the dominion of Venus, whose star excites the winds of that part, because of the efficiency of heat and humidity. But when it also admits the subsolanus because of Saturn's dwelling in Capricorn, this trigon is constituted Notapelioticus, that is, mixed from the south and subsolanus, so that it is opposite to the first. For Saturn excites such winds, that is, eastern, since he is familiar with the east because of his solar condition." Ptolemy likewise assigns diurnal dominion here to Venus, nocturnal to the Moon, but neglects Mars as a participant.

IⅡ Occidentale νοτολιβυκόμ occiden.partici.cum Meri.5m X Septétriona. DE SIGNIS MOBILIBVS, fixis & communibus. I Quatuor quoque ex his fignis dicuntur effe mobi. lia,fcilicet Aries, Cancer, Libra Capricornus. Et qua tuor fixa,fcilicet Taurus, Leo, Scorpius & Aquarius. Reliqua verò quatuor, ſcilicet Gemini, Virgo, Sagitta rius &Pifces, funt communia. Dicuntur autem mobilia, fixa vel communia, quia quando Sol ingreditur aliquod istorum, mouetur, id est, mutatur tempus:vel figitur, id eft, in eodem ſtatu perfeuerat:aut fit commune, id eft,me= dietas illius vnius temporis erit vt medietas alterius. Verbi gratia: Quando Sol ingreditur ſignum Arietis, tempus mutatur, id est, vertitur hyems in ver. Et quan. do intrat Taurum, figitur idem tempus vernale. Quando verò Sol ingreditur Geminos, fit tempus commune, id eſt, dimidium veris & dimidium aſtatis, & fic de cæ teris. I A generali ſignorum conftitutione, quam fuprà capitepriQuatuor moexpofuimus, deducta eſt preſens dodecatemoriorum partiquoq; &c₁ tio & differentia. Quemadmodum enim ipſi quatuor temporum ſtatus fibi perpetuò fimiles non funt, ſed ſinguli incremen to, conſiſtentia & declinatione temperaturarum diſcernuntur: ita etiam fingularum quartarum figna huiufmodi varietatem temporis facientia, à mobilitate, ſtabilitate & mediocritate nomen Digitized by Google PRIMA. men acceperunt. Verbi gratia:Sicut vniuerfum tempus veris in nouum,adultum & præceps ver diſtinguitur: ita etiam integra quarta vernalis in tria diuerfarum appellationum ſigna diſcreta eft, quorum primum eſt mobite, ſecundum fixum, tertium commune. Similiter de reliquis tribus quartis iudicandum eſt. Etenim vniuerfaliter prima quatuor figna quatuor anni temporum, mobilia vocantur: fic dicta, quòd ab illis anni tempora commutentur. Vt Sole Arietem intrante, hyems in ver nouum commutatur: Cancrum, ver in æſtatem: Libram, æſtas in autumnum: Capricornum, autumnus in hyemem conuertitur Tropica ſigna de ſententia Ptolemæi ita ſubdiuiduntur, vt alia fint σημερινά, id eft,quinoctialia,nempe &, quæ nomen à reipfa acceperunt, ſiquidem in ipforum initio Sol conſtitutus zquinoctium in vniuerfa terra efficit:alia τροπικά, nimirum &, quibus itidem nomen à reipſa eſt inditum.Sol enim in ipſis conſtitutus, in quidem à nobis, in o verò ad nos conuertitur, & præter hęc vicissitudinem dierum & noctium efficit. Etenim Sole 5 ingrediente,nox quæ ante minuebatur, iam crefcere incipit, econtrà Sole o attingente, luces quę antea perpetuò decreſcebant, iam paulatim incrementa ſumunt. Hinc ex reliquis octo ſignorum locis quatuor seped', id est, firma ſeu fixa, quatuor δίσωμα, id est, bicorpora vocat Ptolemæ. us. Fixa funt, quæ tropica & æquino&ialia ſequuntur, ſcilicet ४ &, fic dicta, quòd in his ſtatus temporis efficiantur firmi & vehementiores, non quidem quòd in 8 fit tempus ſua natura temperatius, aut in & ardentius, fed quòd corpora hominum iamdudum eam qualitatem in mobili ſigno experta, ob ipfam moram in ſigno fixo eandem quaſi immutatam ſimplicioremque ſentiant. Bicorpora ſunt, que poſt fixa numerantur, nempe II my & X, fic dicta, quòd duobus corporibus content. Nam II duos habet infantes, Piſces totidem animalia, Sagittarius ex equi & hominis corpore componitur. Virginis verò ſignum, quòd Libre adhæreat, δίσωμου dicitur. Sed huius etymologiæ ratio ab imaginum cæleftium ad dodecatemoria accommodatione nata eft. Hæceadem vocantur communia, quoniam inter firma & mobilia poſita, communes naturas cum G illis Digitized by Google !
translation: The third difference: The airy trigon consists of Gemini, Libra, and Aquarius. Their form is human and vocal. Their nature, in general, is hot and humid, sanguine, masculine, diurnal, and sweet in taste. If at that time, or if any of the luminaries ascend the horoscope from these signs, it is said to announce winds in that lunation. This trigon, according to Ptolemy, is oriental due to Saturn's maximum dominion in it, participating with the north because of Jupiter, who, as far as sect is concerned, agrees with Saturn. For it is diurnal and masculine. Therefore, the winds that arise from this triangularity will be between the north and the east, which they call borrascliolicus. Ptolemy's words on this matter are: "This trigon primarily agrees with the subsolanus because of Saturn. But by reason of mixture, it becomes borrapelioticus, that is, mixed from the north and subsolanus, because by name of condition it is reconciled to Saturn." The order of the trigon lords is the same in Ptolemy, except that he, as in all other trigons, neglects the third participant.

translation: The watery trigon connects the signs Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces, whose form is that of aquatic and mute animals. For although Scorpio does not live in water, it lives in humid places. As for their nature, all are generally said to be cold and humid, phlegmatic, feminine, nocturnal, and salty in taste. Therefore, if any of these signs occupies the horoscope at the synod or diameter of the luminaries, they assert that rain is portended in that lunation, and indeed on that day when the Moon touches the horoscopant part, before the opposition if it was a conjunction figure, but before the conjunction whenever you judge from the opposition figure. Abraham Avenezra considers this to be most true, and if the horoscopant sign was one of the cardinal points of that earth figure. He calls the earth figure the theme of the world's revolution, which varies differently according to places. The same Abraham wrote: "If at the hour when the luminaries immediately precede the ingress into any of the aquatic signs, the horoscope is cast, and the luminaries themselves are in the angles, there will be excessive rain and heavy showers in that year."

IⅡ Occidentale νοτολιβυκόμ occiden.partici.cum Meri.5m X Septétriona. DE SIGNIS MOBILIBVS, fixis & communibus. I Quatuor quoque ex his fignis dicuntur effe mobi. lia,fcilicet Aries, Cancer, Libra Capricornus. Et qua tuor fixa,fcilicet Taurus, Leo, Scorpius & Aquarius. Reliqua verò quatuor, ſcilicet Gemini, Virgo, Sagitta rius &Pifces, funt communia. Dicuntur autem mobilia, fixa vel communia, quia quando Sol ingreditur aliquod istorum, mouetur, id est, mutatur tempus:vel figitur, id eft, in eodem ſtatu perfeuerat:aut fit commune, id eft,me= dietas illius vnius temporis erit vt medietas alterius. Verbi gratia: Quando Sol ingreditur ſignum Arietis, tempus mutatur, id est, vertitur hyems in ver. Et quan. do intrat Taurum, figitur idem tempus vernale. Quando verò Sol ingreditur Geminos, fit tempus commune, id eſt, dimidium veris & dimidium aſtatis, & fic de cæ teris. I A generali ſignorum conftitutione, quam fuprà capitepriQuatuor moexpofuimus, deducta eſt preſens dodecatemoriorum partiquoq; &c₁ tio & differentia. Quemadmodum enim ipſi quatuor temporum ſtatus fibi perpetuò fimiles non funt, ſed ſinguli incremen to, conſiſtentia & declinatione temperaturarum diſcernuntur: ita etiam fingularum quartarum figna huiufmodi varietatem temporis facientia, à mobilitate, ſtabilitate & mediocritate nomen Digitized by Google PRIMA. men acceperunt. Verbi gratia:Sicut vniuerfum tempus veris in nouum,adultum & præceps ver diſtinguitur: ita etiam integra quarta vernalis in tria diuerfarum appellationum ſigna diſcreta eft, quorum primum eſt mobite, ſecundum fixum, tertium commune. Similiter de reliquis tribus quartis iudicandum eſt. Etenim vniuerfaliter prima quatuor figna quatuor anni temporum, mobilia vocantur: fic dicta, quòd ab illis anni tempora commutentur. Vt Sole Arietem intrante, hyems in ver nouum commutatur: Cancrum, ver in æſtatem: Libram, æſtas in autumnum: Capricornum, autumnus in hyemem conuertitur Tropica ſigna de ſententia Ptolemæi ita ſubdiuiduntur, vt alia fint σημερινά, id eft,quinoctialia,nempe &, quæ nomen à reipfa acceperunt, ſiquidem in ipforum initio Sol conſtitutus zquinoctium in vniuerfa terra efficit:alia τροπικά, nimirum &, quibus itidem nomen à reipſa eſt inditum.Sol enim in ipſis conſtitutus, in quidem à nobis, in o verò ad nos conuertitur, & præter hęc vicissitudinem dierum & noctium efficit. Etenim Sole 5 ingrediente,nox quæ ante minuebatur, iam crefcere incipit, econtrà Sole o attingente, luces quę antea perpetuò decreſcebant, iam paulatim incrementa ſumunt. Hinc ex reliquis octo ſignorum locis quatuor seped', id est, firma ſeu fixa, quatuor δίσωμα, id est, bicorpora vocat Ptolemæ. us. Fixa funt, quæ tropica & æquino&ialia ſequuntur, ſcilicet ४ &, fic dicta, quòd in his ſtatus temporis efficiantur firmi & vehementiores, non quidem quòd in 8 fit tempus ſua natura temperatius, aut in & ardentius, fed quòd corpora hominum iamdudum eam qualitatem in mobili ſigno experta, ob ipfam moram in ſigno fixo eandem quaſi immutatam ſimplicioremque ſentiant. Bicorpora ſunt, que poſt fixa numerantur, nempe II my & X, fic dicta, quòd duobus corporibus content. Nam II duos habet infantes, Piſces totidem animalia, Sagittarius ex equi & hominis corpore componitur. Virginis verò ſignum, quòd Libre adhæreat, δίσωμου dicitur. Sed huius etymologiæ ratio ab imaginum cæleftium ad dodecatemoria accommodatione nata eft. Hæceadem vocantur communia, quoniam inter firma & mobilia poſita, communes naturas cum G illis Digitized by Google !
translation: Furthermore, this trigon, according to Ptolemy, is western due to the dominion of Mars and the Moon, participating with the south because of Venus. Therefore, it also stirs up such winds, namely those collected from the western and southern regions, which they call notolybicus, that is, Austriaphricus. And Ptolemy says: "This triangle is primarily Aphricus because of the dominion of Mars and the Moon. But because of Venus, it becomes Notolybicum by mixture, that is, Austriaphricus." The Arabs want Mars and Venus to be the principal lords of this triangle, assigning Mars to the day and Venus to the night, with the Moon participating with both. In this, they differ from Ptolemy not in the number or diversity of stars, but only in their arrangement. Ptolemy established Mars alone as the principal triangulator for both day and night, and Venus and the Moon as participants, Venus by day and the Moon by night. His words are: "The fourth triangle, Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces, is left to Mars, who alone remains and claims his right because of his domicile. However, Venus and the Moon are joined to him as co-administrators, both because of their condition and because of the feminine signs, the Moon by night and Venus by day, etc."

translation: Ptolemy's Triangulators: Day, Night, Participant. Aries, Leo, Sagittarius: Sun, Jupiter, Saturn. Taurus, Virgo, Capricorn: Venus, Moon, Mars. Gemini, Libra, Aquarius: Saturn, Jupiter, Sun. Cancer, Scorpio, Pisces: Mars, Venus, Moon. Arab Triangulators: Day, Night, Participant. Aries, Leo, Sagittarius: Sun, Jupiter, Saturn. Taurus, Virgo, Capricorn: Venus, Moon, Mars. Gemini, Libra, Aquarius: Saturn, Jupiter, Sun. Cancer, Scorpio, Pisces: Mars, Venus, Moon. Ptolemy's Trigons: North-West, South-East, North-East, South-West. Arab Trigons: North-West, South-East, North-East, South-West.

Vt Sole Arietem intrante, hyems in ver nouum commutatur: Cancrum, ver in æſtatem: Libram, æſtas in autumnum: Capricornum, autumnus in hyemem conuertitur Tropica ſigna de ſententia Ptolemæi ita ſubdiuiduntur, vt alia fint σημερινά, id eft,quinoctialia,nempe &, quæ nomen à reipfa acceperunt, ſiquidem in ipforum initio Sol conſtitutus zquinoctium in vniuerfa terra efficit:alia τροπικά, nimirum &, quibus itidem nomen à reipſa eſt inditum.Sol enim in ipſis conſtitutus, in quidem à nobis, in o verò ad nos conuertitur, & præter hęc vicissitudinem dierum & noctium efficit. Etenim Sole 5 ingrediente,nox quæ ante minuebatur, iam crefcere incipit, econtrà Sole o attingente, luces quę antea perpetuò decreſcebant, iam paulatim incrementa ſumunt. Hinc ex reliquis octo ſignorum locis quatuor seped', id est, firma ſeu fixa, quatuor δίσωμα, id est, bicorpora vocat Ptolemæ. us. Fixa funt, quæ tropica & æquino&ialia ſequuntur, ſcilicet ४ &, fic dicta, quòd in his ſtatus temporis efficiantur firmi & vehementiores, non quidem quòd in 8 fit tempus ſua natura temperatius, aut in & ardentius, fed quòd corpora hominum iamdudum eam qualitatem in mobili ſigno experta, ob ipfam moram in ſigno fixo eandem quaſi immutatam ſimplicioremque ſentiant. Bicorpora ſunt, que poſt fixa numerantur, nempe II my & X, fic dicta, quòd duobus corporibus content. Nam II duos habet infantes, Piſces totidem animalia, Sagittarius ex equi & hominis corpore componitur. Virginis verò ſignum, quòd Libre adhæreat, δίσωμου dicitur. Sed huius etymologiæ ratio ab imaginum cæleftium ad dodecatemoria accommodatione nata eft. Hæceadem vocantur communia, quoniam inter firma & mobilia poſita, communes naturas cum G illis Digitized by Google !
translation: Four of these signs are also called movable, namely Aries, Cancer, Libra, and Capricorn. And four are fixed, namely Taurus, Leo, Scorpio, and Aquarius. The remaining four, namely Gemini, Virgo, Sagittarius, and Pisces, are common. They are called movable, fixed, or common because when the Sun enters one of them, time moves, that is, it changes; or it is fixed, that is, it perseveres in the same state; or it becomes common, that is, half of that one time will be like half of the other. For example: When the Sun enters the sign of Aries, time changes, that is, winter turns into spring. And when it enters Taurus, the same spring season is fixed. But when the Sun enters Gemini, the time becomes common, that is, half spring and half summer, and so on for the others. The present division and difference of the dodecatemoria is derived from the general constitution of the signs, which we explained in the first chapter above. For just as the four states of the seasons are not perpetually similar to each other, but each is distinguished by the increase, consistency, and decline of temperatures, so also the signs of each quarter, creating such a variety of time, have received their names from mobility, stability, and mediocrity. For example: Just as the entire spring season is distinguished into new, adult, and declining spring, so also the entire vernal quarter is divided into three signs of different appellations, the first of which is movable, the second fixed, and the third common. Similarly, one must judge concerning the remaining three quarters. For universally, the first four signs of the four seasons of the year are called movable: so called because the seasons of the year are changed by them. As when the Sun enters Aries, winter changes into new spring; Cancer, spring into summer; Libra, summer into autumn; Capricorn, autumn into winter. Tropical signs, according to Ptolemy, are subdivided so that some are equinoctial, namely Aries and Libra, which received their name from the thing itself, since at their beginning the Sun, being constituted, causes the equinox throughout the whole earth; others are tropical, namely Cancer and Capricorn, to which likewise the name is given from the thing itself. For the Sun, being constituted in them, turns away from us in Cancer, but towards us in Capricorn, and besides these, causes the vicissitude of days and nights. For when the Sun enters Cancer, the night, which was previously diminishing, now begins to increase; conversely, when the Sun reaches Capricorn, the days, which were previously continuously decreasing, now gradually begin to increase. Hence, from the remaining eight places of the signs, Ptolemy calls four sterea, that is, firm or fixed, and four disoma, that is, bicorporeal. Fixed are those that follow the tropical and equinoctial signs, namely Taurus, Leo, Scorpio, and Aquarius, so called because in these the state of time is made firm and more vehement, not indeed because in Taurus the time is by its nature more temperate, or in Leo more ardent, but because human bodies, having already experienced that quality in a movable sign, feel the same as if unchanged and simpler due to the very delay in the fixed sign. Bicorporeal are those that are numbered after the fixed signs, namely Gemini, Virgo, Sagittarius, and Pisces, so called because they contain two bodies. For Gemini has two infants, Pisces as many animals, Sagittarius is composed of the body of a horse and a man. But the sign of Virgo, because it adheres to Libra, is called disomous. But the reason for this etymology is born from the accommodation of celestial images to the dodecatemoria. These same are called common because, being placed between the firm and movable signs, they share common natures with them.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Differences in Signs
text: DIFFERENTIA illis habeant, quæ & antecedunt, & quæ ſequuntur. Vnde li quet, ſigna vniuſcuiuſque quartz, tali ratione & modo diſtingui,quemadmodum generalis ſingulorum anni temporum có- titutio variatur. Si tamen omnia curioſius ad ordinem,quem fuprà ex Ptolemeo propofuimus, referenda funt, dicendum eſt, quòd in hoc capite recenſeantur quedam ſignorum proprietates fimplices, fingulis dodecatemorijs per ſe peculiariserq; inſitz, feu vt Ptolemeus loquitur, αἱκατὰ τὸ ἀμιγές αὐτλί μόνωμ δωδεκατημορίωμ καθ᾽ αὐτὰ δυνάμεις. Sunt qui exiſtimét, hanc partitionem ſumptam eſſe à mobilitate, ſtabilitate & mediocri duratione rerum, quæ ipfis autaſcendentibus inchoantur, aut planetis ibidem collocatis fignificantur. Vnde qui de electionibus præceperunt, putant omnia generaliter, quæ σιμα fragiliaque eſſe velimus, exordienda eſſe Luna diſcurrente per ſignum aliquod mobile:In rebus verò firmioribus & diutiùs perennaturis, attendendum eſſe ad ſigna fixa:In rebus-denique pauciores annos duraturis, indifferentia communiaque Igna eſſe obferuanda. Sed nos talia non curamus. DE ASPECTIBVS PLANETARVM. ■ Dicuntur etiam figna ſe afpicere, boc est: Omne fig num afpicit tertium ante ſe, tertium post ſe, quod est vndecimum. Et hic afpectus dicitur ſextilis, & est afpe Etus dilectionis & medie amicitia. Et dicitur etiam fextilis, eò quòd teneat fextam partem circuli, id eft, LX. gradus. Verbi gratia: Planeta qui fuerit in initio Arietis, afpicit eum qui fuerit in Geminis ante fe, eu qui fuerit in Aquario poſt ſe, qui eſt aſpectus amicitia. Et afpicit quartum ante ſe & quartum poſt ſe, id aft, decimum. Et hic afpectus vocatur tetragonus, ed 2 quòd Digitized by Google コ = ب 2 PRIMA quòd tenet quartam partem cæli, id eft, LXXXX.gra dus. Et eft afpectus diſcordiæ atque medie inimicitia. 3 Et afpicit quoque quintum fignum ante ſe, & quin tum poft ſe, quod est nonum. Et hic afpectus dicitur tri gonus vel trigona radiatio, eò quòd tertiam partem cœ- li, id est, CXX.gradus tereat, & est afpectus concordia dilectionis perfecte. 4 Afpicit autem feptimum per oppoſitionem, & eft. afpectus inimicitiæ perfecta. Et fi in his fignis ita ſe aſpi cientibus fuerint planete, dicuntur ſe afpicere. Hic eft afpectus. 5 Et fi duo planete fuerint in vno ſigno, dicuntur coniuncti. 6 Cùm verò fuerit planeta in aliquo figno, erunt radij illius in fignis illis, que afpiciunt ipſum ſignum in fimi. ligradu atque minuto. Verbi gratia, fi fuerit Mars in primo gradu Arietis, & in primo minuto eiufdem gradus, erunt rady eius in primo gradu ſigni Libra,& in primo minuto eiufdem gradus:& fic intellige de cate ris afpectibus, vt in hac fia gura patet euiden tißimè. GSCHE Digitized by Google E DIFFERENTIA SCHEMA ASPECTVVM SEV configurationum. A *오오 日 B an ६ Hoc caput totum confumitur tractatione de proprietatiDicuntur bus dodecatemoriorum, quæ ex collatione ipforum erga ſe inetiam, &cuicem oriútur, hoc eft, de fignorů mutuis afpe&ibus. Sunt aute aſpectus interualla in Zodiaco computata, ex quibus abi ſtella maximam vim communicant. Et quamuis quælibet ſtella innu merabiles radios orbiculariter effundat:tamen experientiado cuit, quinque ex his, quæ per integra ſigna & partes totius circuli integras conftituuntur, reliquam multitudiné efficacia ſu perare. Primò ſextilis ſeu εξάγων aſpectus eſt, qui ſextam partem Zodiaci, hoc eſt, duo figna ſeu P.60.continet:vt fi initium Cancri fit A,à quo capiantur P.60. inter A & C contents. ducatur que reda AC, ipſa ſic dusta, erit ſextilis radius, qui notatur ! translation: They have differences, both those that precede and those that follow. Hence it is clear that the signs of each quarter are distinguished by such a reason and manner, just as the general constitution of each season of the year varies. If, however, all things are to be referred more curiously to the order which we proposed above from Ptolemy, it must be said that in this chapter certain simple properties of the signs are enumerated, peculiar to each dodecatemory by itself, or as Ptolemy says, the powers of the dodecatemories themselves, pure and unmixed. There are those who think that this division is taken from the mobility, stability, and moderate duration of things, which are begun by the ascendants themselves, or are signified by the planets placed therein. Hence those who have given precepts concerning elections, think that all things generally, which we wish to be fragile and weak, should be begun when the Moon is passing through some movable sign: but in more stable things and those that will endure longer, attention should be paid to the fixed signs: finally, in things that will last fewer years, indifferent and common signs are to be observed. But we do not care about such things. CONCERNING THE ASPECTS OF THE PLANETS. Signs are also said to aspect each other, that is: Every sign aspects the third before it, and the third after it, which is the eleventh. And this aspect is called sextile, and it is an aspect of affection and moderate friendship. And it is also called sextile because it holds the sixth part of the circle, that is, 60 degrees. For example: A planet that is at the beginning of Aries aspects the one that is in Gemini before it, and the one that is in Aquarius after it, which is an aspect of friendship. And it aspects the fourth before it and the fourth after it, that is, the tenth. And this aspect is called square, because it holds the fourth part of the sky, that is, 90 degrees. And it is an aspect of discord and moderate enmity.

translation: DIFFERENCE SCHEMA OF ASPECTS OR CONFIGURATIONS. A *오오 日 B an ६ This entire chapter is consumed with the treatment of the properties of the dodecatemories, which arise from their collation with each other, that is, from the mutual aspects of the signs. Aspects are intervals computed in the Zodiac, from which the stars communicate the greatest power. And although any star diffuses innumerable rays orbicularly: nevertheless, experience has shown that five of these, which are constituted by whole signs and whole parts of the entire circle, surpass the remaining multitude in efficacy. First, the sextile or hexagonal aspect is that which contains the sixth part of the Zodiac, that is, two signs or 60 degrees: as if the beginning of Cancer is A, from which 60 degrees are taken, contained between A and C. And if the line AC is drawn, it will be a sextile ray, which is noted!

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Aspects to Angles and Stars
text: angulos domorum cæli, aut ad alias errantes ſtellas comparantur. Si fiat comparatio ad angulos cæli, tunc dextri, qui contra ſignorum ordinem feruntur, validiores ſunt ſiniſtris. Nam fidera ad centra cæli non proprio, ſed motu firmamenti feruntur. Contrà ſi fiat collatio eorum ad alias ſtellas, tunc ſiniſtri dextris proponendi funt, quòd ſtellæ ſtellis non alieno, ſed proprio motu appropinquent. Infinuauit nobis hanc diſtinctionem radiorum Ptolemæus in tractatu de vita, & lib.4.cap.dedignitate & honoribus. 6 Alia denique partitione afpe&uum, vnus dicitur πλατικός Cum verò Platicus, qui eſt à ſignis tantùm, & ſigna quidem ipſa ſeſe aſpifuerit &c. ciunt. Alter Patilis, cùm à ſtella ad ſtellam tot numerantur cœli partes, quot configurationem ipſam conſtituunt: hoceft, cùm radij ſtellæ proijciuntur in partes ſigni aſpicientis ſimiles. Sunt quoque Planetarum in fignis termini vel fines: Quia in vnoquoque ſigno habent quinque Planeteter. minos, per diuerfos gradus diſpoſitos. Nam ab initio A rietis vfque ad fextum gradum eiufdem Arietis,eft ter minus louis: & à ſexto vfque ad XIL terminus Vene. ris: &à XII. vfque ad XX. terminus Mercury: & à XX.adXXV.terminus Martis:& deinceps ad finem figni,id est,à XXV. in XXX.terminus Saturni. Aquarij Piſcium ☿ 7 7 ♃ 7 14 ♀ 8 22 ♂ 4 26 ♄ 4 30 ♀ 7 7 ♃ 6 13 ♄ 7 20 ♂ 5 25 ☿ 5 30 ♄ 12 12 ♃ 4 16 ♄ 3 19 ♂ 9 28 ☿ 2 30 Termini Digitized by Google PRIMA 59 Termini seu fines, nihil enim refert quo nomine vtaris, sunt peculiares cœli partes, quæ in vnoquoque signo ob certas causas distribuuntur quinque stellis errantibus, nempe ♄ ♃ ♂ ♀ & ☿. Nam duo luminaria carent finibus, locoque ipsorum duas medietates signiferi vfurpant, ficut fupra dictum est. Græ- ci τὰ ὅρια vocant, & dominum finium ὁριοκράτορα seu τήν ὁρίων ἀρχήν. Cùm verò finium gradus numero inæquales sint, & parum stabli ordine planetis distribuuntur, ita vt memoria eos comprehendere sit difficile, & retinere difficillimum: idcirco vtilius & commodius est visum autori, fines planetarum in tabella cópendio depictos, quàm longa oratione expositos proponere. Ratio tamen eorum talis esse videtur. Principium vniuſcuiuſque signi vniuerſaliter arripit is planeta, qui in eodem reliquos numero dignitatum superauerit. Id autem si non accidat, & plures singulas in signo potestates habeant: tunc triangulator semper præfertur domino altitudinis, & hîc domino domus. Cæterùm hæc dignitatum præstantia nihil promouet maleficas. Hæ enim non nisi in Solis carpentis preferuntur, quo si destituantur, non tantùm à principijs, sed & à medijs locis signorum explosi ad extrema propelluntur. Hoc adeo verum est, vt Mercurius in Aquario ob solum trigonum primas partes teneat, Saturno ibidem præter trigonum etiam domicilium habente, non tantùm à primatu repulso, veruetiam ad extremas partes detruso. Et Mars initium Cancri nullam aliam ob causam occupauit, nisi quòd is eius trigoni solus sit dominus: Scorpij verò propter trigonum & domum. In Libra autem propter nimiam potestatem prælatus est Mercurio Saturnus, qui ibi præter trigonum altitudinem adeptus est. Reliquorum signorum omnium non tantùm principia, sed & loca intermedia & principijs succedentia, à maleficarum imperio sunt libera, & reliquis planetis ratione prærogatiuarum iam dicta subijciuntur. Exempli gratia, in signo Piſcium, quoniam Mars ob solum trigonum principatum tenere non potuit, Venus propter exaltationem primis partibus præsidet, hanc Iuppiter Piſcium dominus proximè sequitur: Hunc Mercurius plane peregrinus. Extremas partes tenent malefici, & Mars cùm sit dominus trianguli, Saturnum præcedit. In signo Arietis Iuppiter ob trianH 2 gulum Digitized by Google 60 DIFFERENTIA gulum primum locum occupat. Hîc verò etsi tam Mercurius quàm Venus nulla ratione regnent, tamen ob potentiam & beneficentiam Venus præfertur Mercurio. Penultimum locum habet Mars Arietis dominus, Saturno rursum ad extrema detruſo. In Tauro cùm trigonum habeat Venus,idcirco meritò primis eius partibus præsidet. Rursus ♃ & ☿, etsi nullo modo hic imperitent: tamen Mercurius prefertur Ioui, quòd ille in signo Virginis Tauro triangulari familiaritate deuincto altitudinem domiciliumque habeat, hic verò plane alienus ab hoc trigono existat. Extremas partes adminiſtrant malefici, Saturno Martem propter Capricornum precedente. Et sic de cæteris iudicandum est. Sed quare Veneri præponitur Mercurius in Ca pricorno, cùm illa trianguli ius ibi exerceat, hic vero quasi peregrinus esse videatur? Quia vt iam diximus, Mercurius propter Virginem maiorem penè potestatem in illo trigono habet quam ipsa Venus, cui saltem Taurus præter trigonum præbet hospitiú: Aut quia veteres in principio Capricorni aliquam insignem vim Mercurij frequentius experti sunt. Possibile quoque est, Venerem pro Mercurio, & Mercurium pro Venere ob figurarum similitudinem à librarijs esse suppositum. Rursus quæri potest, Quare ius trianguli in distribuendis finibus reliquas potestates omnes antecedat? Ad hanc hæsitationem respondere debemus, finium distributionem propemodum esse huiusmodi, qualis est in politia iudiciorum aliorú publicorú officiorum diuisio. Quapropter sicut in aliqua bene conſtituta ciuitate concordi consensu senatus, conuenientes personæ rerum gubernaculis præficiuntur: ita hic quoque fines seu terminos imperiorum potiores concorditer partiuntur inter sese planetæ, potestatem in signa triangularitatis, quæ consensum significat, exercentes. Etsi verò triangulatores in hoc negotio reliquis dominis præferantur: tamen nihilominus cedunt ijs, qui in signis solium, hoc est, plures quam vnam iurisdictionem habent, & obid more tyrannorum violentia quadam optimam partem semper occupant. Hæc est ratio ordinis. Cæterùm dispu: tationem,cur inæquali numero partium finium dominia contineantur, hic omittimus, quòd ea perplexior sit, multisque inuoluta ambagibus:& quòd existimem, veteres Aegyptios con- ſtituisse Digitized by Google
translation: The angles of the houses of the sky, or to other wandering stars, are compared. If the comparison is made to the angles of the sky, then the dexter (right) aspects, which are carried against the order of the signs, are stronger than the sinister (left) aspects. For the stars are carried to the centers of the sky not by their own motion, but by the motion of the firmament. On the contrary, if the comparison is made to other stars, then the sinister aspects are to be preferred to the dexter, because the stars approach other stars not by an alien motion, but by their own motion. Ptolemy insinuated this distinction of rays to us in the treatise on life, and in book 4, chapter on dignity and honors. 6 Finally, by another division of aspects, one is called 'Platicus' when it is only from the signs, and the signs themselves aspect each other, etc. The other is 'Patilis', when from star to star as many parts of the sky are counted as constitute the configuration itself: that is, when the rays of the star are projected into similar parts of the aspecting sign. For example, if the Sun is in Aries 15°00', its square sinister ray will be in Cancer 15°, and dexter in Capricorn 15°. And its trine dexter in Sagittarius 15°, sinister in Leo 15°. Finally, the opposition in Libra 15°. This is to be understood of the minute parts of the sky: so that if the Sun were in Aries 9°47', all the said rays would fall into the 9°47' of the said signs. We have appended a compendium of all radiations, both dexter and sinister, and derived from each sign. RADIATIONS. * 0 Δ 8 dexter. * ++ 0 sinister 2 dexter. X 8 sinister mp mm sinister dexter. 213 sinister in dexter. mp 74 * Π Δ 8 II * 3 X - dexter. X dexter. 0 : sinister mp I sinister X 2 dexter. 44 X dexter. m sinister m sinister dexter. II 44 dexter. → m sinister. m ++ nil 4 0 II α dexter. Π dexter. ++ m sinister m ++ XX sinister 11 I. CONCERNING THE TERMS OF THE PLANETS. There are also terms or bounds of the planets in the signs: Because in each sign five planets have terms, arranged through different degrees. For from the beginning of Aries up to the sixth degree of the same Aries, is the term of Jupiter: and from the sixth up to the twelfth, the term of Venus: and from the twelfth up to the twentieth, the term of Mercury: and from the twentieth to the twenty-fifth, the term of Mars: and then to the end of the sign, that is, from the twenty-fifth to the thirtieth, the term of Saturn. However, because of the diversity of these degrees, which is not easy to remember, we have described them in a table, so that the work might be easier. H TER : Digitized by Google

Nam duo luminaria carent finibus, locoque ipsorum duas medietates signiferi vfurpant, ficut fupra dictum est. Græ- ci τὰ ὅρια vocant, & dominum finium ὁριοκράτορα seu τήν ὁρίων ἀρχήν. Cùm verò finium gradus numero inæquales sint, & parum stabli ordine planetis distribuuntur, ita vt memoria eos comprehendere sit difficile, & retinere difficillimum: idcirco vtilius & commodius est visum autori, fines planetarum in tabella cópendio depictos, quàm longa oratione expositos proponere. Ratio tamen eorum talis esse videtur. Principium vniuſcuiuſque signi vniuerſaliter arripit is planeta, qui in eodem reliquos numero dignitatum superauerit. Id autem si non accidat, & plures singulas in signo potestates habeant: tunc triangulator semper præfertur domino altitudinis, & hîc domino domus. Cæterùm hæc dignitatum præstantia nihil promouet maleficas. Hæ enim non nisi in Solis carpentis preferuntur, quo si destituantur, non tantùm à principijs, sed & à medijs locis signorum explosi ad extrema propelluntur. Hoc adeo verum est, vt Mercurius in Aquario ob solum trigonum primas partes teneat, Saturno ibidem præter trigonum etiam domicilium habente, non tantùm à primatu repulso, veruetiam ad extremas partes detruso. Et Mars initium Cancri nullam aliam ob causam occupauit, nisi quòd is eius trigoni solus sit dominus: Scorpij verò propter trigonum & domum. In Libra autem propter nimiam potestatem prælatus est Mercurio Saturnus, qui ibi præter trigonum altitudinem adeptus est. Reliquorum signorum omnium non tantùm principia, sed & loca intermedia & principijs succedentia, à maleficarum imperio sunt libera, & reliquis planetis ratione prærogatiuarum iam dicta subijciuntur. Exempli gratia, in signo Piſcium, quoniam Mars ob solum trigonum principatum tenere non potuit, Venus propter exaltationem primis partibus præsidet, hanc Iuppiter Piſcium dominus proximè sequitur: Hunc Mercurius plane peregrinus. Hîc verò etsi tam Mercurius quàm Venus nulla ratione regnent, tamen ob potentiam & beneficentiam Venus præfertur Mercurio. Penultimum locum habet Mars Arietis dominus, Saturno rursum ad extrema detruſo. In Tauro cùm trigonum habeat Venus,idcirco meritò primis eius partibus præsidet. Rursus ♃ & ☿, etsi nullo modo hic imperitent: tamen Mercurius prefertur Ioui, quòd ille in signo Virginis Tauro triangulari familiaritate deuincto altitudinem domiciliumque habeat, hic verò plane alienus ab hoc trigono existat. Extremas partes adminiſtrant malefici, Saturno Martem propter Capricornum precedente. Et sic de cæteris iudicandum est. Sed quare Veneri præponitur Mercurius in Ca pricorno, cùm illa trianguli ius ibi exerceat, hic vero quasi peregrinus esse videatur? Quia vt iam diximus, Mercurius propter Virginem maiorem penè potestatem in illo trigono habet quam ipsa Venus, cui saltem Taurus præter trigonum præbet hospitiú: Aut quia veteres in principio Capricorni aliquam insignem vim Mercurij frequentius experti sunt. Possibile quoque est, Venerem pro Mercurio, & Mercurium pro Venere ob figurarum similitudinem à librarijs esse suppositum. Rursus quæri potest, Quare ius trianguli in distribuendis finibus reliquas potestates omnes antecedat? Gemini: Saturn 6, Mercury 6, Venus 5, Mars 7, Jupiter 6. Cancer: Mars 7, Mercury 6, Venus 6, Jupiter 7, Saturn 4. Leo: Saturn 4, Mercury 5, Venus 7, Jupiter 6, Saturn 6. Virgo: Saturn 7, Mercury 10, Venus 4, Jupiter 7, Saturn 2. Libra: Saturn 6, Mercury 8, Jupiter 7, Venus 7, Mars 2. Scorpio: Mars 7, Mercury 4, Jupiter 8, Venus 5, Mars 6. Sagittarius: Saturn 12, Mercury 5, Jupiter 4, Venus 5, Mars 4. Capricorn: Mercury 7, Jupiter 7, Venus 8, Mars 4, Saturn 4. Aquarius: Venus 7, Jupiter 6, Saturn 7, Mars 5, Mercury 5. Pisces: Saturn 12, Jupiter 4, Saturn 3, Mars 9, Mercury 2. Terms Digitized by Google
