translation: Terms or bounds, for it matters not by what name you call them, are peculiar parts of the sky, which in each sign are distributed for certain reasons among the five wandering stars, namely Saturn, Jupiter, Mars, Venus, and Mercury. For the two luminaries lack bounds, and in their place, they occupy the two halves of the zodiac, as has been said above. The Greeks call them τὰ ὅρια, and the lord of the bounds ὁριοκράτορα or τήν ὁρίων ἀρχήν. Since the degrees of the bounds are unequal in number and distributed to the planets in a somewhat unstable order, so that it is difficult to comprehend them by memory and very difficult to retain them: therefore, it seemed more useful and convenient to the author to present the planetary bounds depicted concisely in a table, rather than explained in a long discourse. However, their rationale seems to be as follows. The beginning of each sign is universally seized by that planet which has surpassed the others in the number of dignities in the same sign. But if this does not happen, and several planets have individual powers in the sign: then the triplicity ruler is always preferred to the lord of exaltation, and here to the lord of the house. However, this preeminence of dignities does not advance the malefics. For these are preferred only in the chariots of the Sun, and if they are deprived of this, they are not only expelled from the beginnings but also from the middle places of the signs and driven to the extremes. This is so true that Mercury in Aquarius holds the first parts solely by reason of the trine, while Saturn, having both the trine and domicile there, is not only repelled from primacy but also pushed to the extreme parts. And Mars occupied the beginning of Cancer for no other reason than that he is the sole lord of its trine; but of Scorpio, because of the trine and domicile. In Libra, however, Saturn was preferred to Mercury due to excessive power, having obtained exaltation there in addition to the trine. The beginnings of all other signs, as well as the intermediate places and those succeeding the beginnings, are free from the dominion of the malefics and are subject to the other planets by reason of the prerogatives already mentioned. For example, in the sign of Pisces, since Mars could not hold primacy solely by reason of the trine, Venus presides over the first parts due to exaltation; Jupiter, the lord of Pisces, follows her closely; then Mercury, who is completely peregrine. The malefics hold the extreme parts, and Mars, being the lord of the triplicity, precedes Saturn. In the sign of Aries, Jupiter occupies the first place due to the trine. Here, although neither Mercury nor Venus rule for any reason, yet due to power and beneficence, Venus is preferred to Mercury. Mars, the lord of Aries, has the penultimate place, with Saturn again pushed to the extremes. In Taurus, since Venus has the trine, she rightly presides over its first parts. Again, Jupiter and Mercury, although they do not rule here in any way: yet Mercury is preferred to Jupiter, because he has exaltation and domicile in the sign of Virgo, which is bound to Taurus by triplicity affinity, while Jupiter is completely alien to this trine. The malefics administer the extreme parts, with Saturn preceding Mars due to Capricorn. And so it must be judged for the rest. But why is Mercury preferred to Venus in Capricorn, when she exercises the right of the triplicity there, while he seems almost peregrine? Because, as we have already said, Mercury, due to Virgo, has almost greater power in that trine than Venus herself, to whom at least Taurus offers hospitality in addition to the trine; or because the ancients frequently experienced some significant power of Mercury at the beginning of Capricorn. It is also possible that Venus was substituted for Mercury, and Mercury for Venus, by scribes due to the similarity of the figures. Again, it can be asked, why does the right of the triplicity precede all other powers in the distribution of terms? To this hesitation, we must respond that the distribution of terms is almost like the division of judicial and other public offices in a polity. Therefore, just as in a well-constituted city, by the harmonious consent of the senate, suitable persons are placed in charge of the reins of government: so here too, the planets, exercising power in the signs of triplicity, which signifies consent, harmoniously divide among themselves the more important terms or bounds of dominion. Although the triplicity rulers are preferred to the other lords in this matter: nevertheless, they yield to those who have a throne in the signs, that is, more than one jurisdiction, and on that account, like tyrants, always occupy the best part by a certain violence. This is the reason for the order. However, we omit here the discussion of why the dominions of the terms are contained in an unequal number of parts, because it is more perplexing and involved in many ambiguities; and because I believe that the ancient Egyptians established

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Triplicities vs. Terms
text: = PRIMA. ſtituiſſe planetis fines in alijs ſignis ampliores, in alijs contractiores, potius ob experietiam longo vsu comprobatam, quam vllam aliam rationem. Generaliter tamen beneficis latiores termini tributi sunt quam maleficis. Etenim vniuerſi Saturni fines, quos in signis accepit, continentur partibus LVII. Iouis partibus LXXIX. Martis partibus LXVI. Veneris partibus LXXXII. Mercurij partibus LXXVI. Ex quibus numeris partium æstimare licet, beneficos longe latiùs imperare maleficis. Id verò ideo fieri putant Arabes, quòd benefiçi longioris vitæ homines producant quam malefici. Tradunt enim vnumquenque planetam, cùm dominium vitæ adipiscitur, meritò tot annos vitæ nato conferre, quot partibus continentur eiusdem planetæ fines, quos in integro signifero sortitus est: atque ex hoc fonte suam persuasionem de Alcocodeo communiri posse arbitrantur. Nos verò sicut Ptolemæum ex alijs causis longe potentioribus spacia vitæ metiri scimus: ita quoque huius inequalitatis terminorum alias rationes quærendas esse putamus. Natura hunc finem agendi habet propositum, vt formarum perpe: tuitatem conſtanter custodiat. Hac intentione eam fæpius frustrari necesſum erat, nisi singularum formarú indiuidua, vt vocant, quæ sine fine assidua destructione corruere videmus, continenti & longè frequentius repetita generatione propagarentur. Conſtat autem, & infra latius explicabitur, stellas beneficorum ob suam temperaturam esse causas fœcunditatis generationisque: maleficorum verò interitus, exitij & destructionis.. Quapropter vt formarum indiuidua per interitum sublata, afsidud inſtaurentur, & ob frequentissimam generationem, planè nullum periculum de vllius formæ interitu sit reliquum, ipsa natura stellis beneficis ampliores potioresque imperii fines quam maleficis, benigne largita est. Adiuuat nos hic ipsa finium tam beneficorum quam maleficorum diuerſa quantitas. Cur enim Venus latiùs regnaret Ioue, qui Venere longè est temperatior, nisi vt omniaad amorem generationis magis crebriusq;; accederentur. Ad eundem modum Mars, quòd ob caliditatem suam generationem promoueat, latiùs imperat Saturno ad Venerem planè frigido.. Declarant id ipſarum stellarum effectus.. Videmus enim Martios veluti milites, amare, & robusta geneH 3 rare Digitized by Google 61 DIFFERENTIA rare corpora: cùm Saturnini, quales sunt decrepiti, rarò generent, & si quando generent, infirmos debilesque generant. Mercurius verò etsi ambiguæ naturæ sit, tamen à causa generationis magis accedit ad beneficos quàm maleficos, cùm ob amplitudinem regni, qua maleficos facilè superat, tum verò propter familiaritatem Solis, & assiduos cú Venere coitus. Atque hactenus de finibus. Ptolemæus de ipsis tres opiniones recitat, nimirum hanc Aegyptiorum, de qua diximus: alteram Chaldeorum, qua reiicit: & aliam tertiam, quam ex antiquo quodam & corroſo volumine se descripsisse ait, atque eandem reliquis duabus præ- fert, etsi Aegyptiorum distributioni exempla geniturarum congruere fateatur. Tabella autem finium, quos Ptolemæus probat, hæc est, cuius latiorem expositionem, hoc est, causam ordinis & numeri, si qui desiderant, ipsius Ptolemæi caput de finibus consulere debent. Quidam autem preponunt triplicitates terminis, eò quòd Domini triplicitatum funt fortiores in nutritio. ne : quia fignificant nutritionem,& quia nulla difcor. dia eft in triplicitate, ficut eft in terminis, cum prapo. nuntur.Et quidam preponūt terminos triplicitatibus, eò quòd domini terminorü funt fortiores in directione. Hæſitatio eft, vtrum trigonorum vel finium maior sit poteſtas? Reſpondemus per diftin&ionem, Si generalis harum dignitatum fiat collatio, vniuerfaliter trigoni præcedunt terminos. Sed fi eafdem ad certa negotia comparemus, deprehendemus cafus alios in quibus trigoni terminis præferendi, & alios in quibus poftponendi fint. Veluti in nutritionis negotio trianguli præponuntur terminis, & hi vicissim in ordinatione generalium chronocratorum præferuntur triangulis Ipſe verò idem dicit, ſed confuſiore vt apparet ordine. Quidam, inquit, ius triangularitatis dupliciter præferunt terminis. Primò & in ſpecie reſpectu certorum negotiorum, veluti in quzſtione nutritionis. Nam cùm quæſtio, an illi qui natus eft, fit à cælo conceſſum vt creſcat vel non crefcat, explicanda proponitur, tunc maior vis triangulis quàm finibus tribuenda eft, Aquidé domini triangulorú, vt placet Arabibus, nutritioné fig. nificant.Secundo, & generaliter, tană de euidétiore, & ob id magis certa cauſa maior fit conſenſus Aftrologorum de ordine triangulatorum, quam de ordine Thì dκιοκρατόρωμ, quandoquidem 64 DIFFERENTIA quidem, vt in precedenti capite viſum eft, magna eft contentio, quis ex planetis finium poteſtate in ſignis præcedere debeat. Rurfus aliquos in ſpecie dicit fines præferre triangulis propte reà, quòd cùm per progreſsiones & tranfitus locorum Apheticorum temporum arbitros ordinamus, maior fit comperta efficacia την τὰ ὅρια ἐπεχόντωμ αςέρωμ, quam 7 τριγωνοκρα- τόρωμ. Quid autem fint directiones & aliæ progreſsiones, & quomodo perficiantur, infrà in loco magis oportuno explicabimus. DE FACIEBVS SIGNORVM,ET CVI planetarum attribuuntur. I Facies autem fignorum funt he. Vnumquodque fig. num diuiditur in tres partes æquales. Quælibet pars con flatur ex X.gradibus, & vocatur facies, quarum initi um eft à primo gradu Arietis. Prima ergo facies eft à pri mo gradu Arietis vsque in X. datur Marti. Secunda vsque ad XX.gradum, & datur Soli, um, & datur Soli, qui fuccedit ei in ordine circulorum. Tertia vſque ad finem pradicti ſig. ni,& eft Veneris. Similiter prima facies Tauri est Mer cury, qui Veneri fuccedit, & ita vſque in finem fignorű. 2 Cum igitur habueris gradum in aliquo figno, & vo lueris fcire, ex cuius Planetarum facie fit:fume ab initio Arietis ſigna integra, vfque ad fignum de quo volueris fcire, triplica ea, numeroque ex tali triplicatione ge nito, adýcias id, quod præterijt ex faciebus illius figni, de quo faciem fcire volueris, cum eadem facie, cuius do. minum ſcire volueris, & proijce à planeta, qui est domi. nus facieiſigni Arietis, qui est Mars, fecundum fucceßio nemcirculorum ſeptem ſeptem. Et quod remanferit infra PRIMA. 65 infra ſeptem, numera à Marte per fucceßionem planeta rum:& vbi finitus fuerit numerus, erit prima facies illius ſigni, cuius gradum aſſumpſiſti, ſi videlicet idem ille gradus intra primos decem gradus eiufdem ſigni con tineatur, & quidem illius planete, apud quem numerus erit finitus. A decimo verò gradu vſque in XX. alterius planete, qui illi fuccedit, & à XX.vſque in finem ſigni, alterius qui ei fuccedit. Et gradus figniſemper erit fa. cies illius planeta, cuius denario comprehenfus fuerit. Vocantur etiam domini facierum iftarum à quibusdam Decani, cuius rei fequitur tabula. translation: Some, however, prefer triplicities to terms, because the lords of triplicities are stronger in nourishment: because they signify nourishment, and because there is no discord in triplicity, as there is in terms when they are preferred. And some prefer terms to triplicities, because the lords of terms are stronger in direction. There is hesitation as to whether the power of trigons or bounds is greater. We respond by distinction: If a general comparison of these dignities is made, universally trigons precede terms. But if we compare them to certain matters, we will find other cases in which trigons are to be preferred to terms, and others in which they are to be postponed. As in the matter of nourishment, triangles are preferred to terms, and these in turn are preferred to triangles in the ordering of general chronocrators. He himself says the same thing, but in a more confused order, as it appears. Some, he says, prefer the right of triangularity to terms in two ways. First, and specifically with respect to certain matters, such as in the question of nourishment. For when the question, whether it is granted from heaven for the native to grow or not to grow, is proposed for explanation, then greater force must be attributed to triangles than to bounds. Indeed, the lords of triangles, as the Arabs please, signify nourishment. Second, and generally, as concerning a more evident, and therefore more certain cause, there is greater consensus among astrologers regarding the order of triangulators than regarding the order of chronocrators, since, as was seen in the preceding chapter, there is great contention as to which of the planets should precede in the power of the bounds in the signs. Again, he says that some specifically prefer bounds to triangles, because when we order the arbiters of times through progressions and transits of aphetic places, the efficacy of the stars holding the bounds is found to be greater than that of the triangulators. What directions and other progressions are, and how they are accomplished, we will explain below in a more opportune place.

Húc in ordine ſequit 4,9 ob eam cauſam primò in ſigno II ad deca natu admittit, pariterq; fequétes ſequétib decurijs preficiunt. nis &c. 2 Modus verò, per quem dato cuiuſlibet ſigni gradu per arté Cum igie tur habue calculatoriam abſque præſidio tabulæ exploratur, cui decano datus gradus obtemperet, eſt talis: Supputetur numerus fignorum à principio Arietis vſque ad principium ſigni propoſ ti. DE VIRTUTIBVS Planetarum. * Et quia iam annuente Deo tractauimus de potefta tibus Planetarum in ſignis, quæ funt domus,exaltatio, triplicitas, terminus & facies: Nunc igitur tractemus de virtutibus feu fortitudinibus eorum in ipfis.Nam do minus domus habet quinque fortitudines,& dominus exaltationis quatuor, dominus triplicitatistres, dos minus termini duas, & dominus faciei vnam. Intellige ergo ex hoc numero fortitudines planetarum. Qui enim magis abundant in numero,magis abūdant in fortitudi ne. Et quida preponut terminu triplicitati,ideft, volut, quòd dominus termini fit fortior domino triplicitatis. Sed iam expofuimus ſuperiùs, in quo valet vnusquiſque. 2 Quidam etiam de hac re talem dederunt comparationem, dicentes, quòd planeta cùm fuerit in domo fua, eſt ſimilis viro in domo atque in dominatione fua. Et cùm fuerit in exaltatione fua, eſt ſimilis viro in regno fuo & gloria. Et cùm fuerit in termino fuo, eſt ſicut vir inter parentes & cognatos suos atque gentes. Et cùm fuerit in triplicitate fua, erit ficut vir in honore ſuo,& inter auxiliatores atque ministros suos. Et cùm fuerit in facie fua, erit ficut vir in magiſterio suo. He ſunt vni verſe poteftates planetarum effentiales in ſignis, & fe quuntur bas figuræ fignorum. 1 In vniuerfum familiaritates ſtellarum locorumq;, quas po- Et quia teſtates ſeu dignitates planetarum in ſignis eſſentiales vocant, iam, &c. conftituit autor numero quinque, nempe domum, altitudiné, 12 trigo- ==Start of OCR for page 1== 68, DIFFERENTIA trigonum, finem & faciem. Harum vnaqueque quid fit, cum haAenus expofuerimus, proximum eſt, vt dicatur, quanta vniufcuiuſque ſit vis & potentia, hoc eſt, quantum roboris viriumá vnaquzq; poteſtas planetis conferre queat vel nequeat. Et hoc eſt, quod autor dicit, ſe nunc tractaturum de virtutibus ſeu for titudinibus planetarum in ipfis dignitatibus collocatorum. Cùm autem omnium dignitatum nobilissima ſit domus, idcirco quinque calculos fortitudinis ei adſcribit. Huic proxima eſt altitudo, & ob id quatuor rationibus virium prędita. Deinde ſequitur trigonus, trium poteſtatis numero diſcretus. Poſt finium dignitas, numero duum infignita.vltima eſt facies, & ob id monade ſignificata. Eft autem hicordo dignitatum ideo in numeros digeſtus, vt magnitudinem roboris vel imbecillitatis ſtellarum, quam in quolibet loco cali τῇ συνοικειώσει confcquuntur, faciliùs eſtimare & dijudicare, atque fic victorem & prędominantem planetam rectius eligere poſsimus. Etenim ſtellam, quam in cæli aliquo loco maiorem numerum fortitudinum ſeu poteftatum.colligere deprehenderimus, eam in tali loco dominatu reliquas ſtellas vincere pronunciare poterimus.Exempli gratia, in primis ſex partibus Libræ, cùm Saturnus ob domum poteſtates s.obaltitudinem 4. ob trigonum 3.. ob fines duas conſequatur, atque in ſumma numero poteftatum 14.0mnes reliquos errones facilè ſuperet:idcirco Saturnú in hujufmodi loco dominatorem victoremque eſſe haud eſt du bium.Hic ordo dignitatum generalis eft, & ob id non eſt opinandum,cum planè vbique conſtanter obferuari. Nam dignitates iſtæ reſpectu negotiorum explicandorum robur & fortitudines ſuas multipliciter commutare.confueuerunt. Sanè in his quæ de vita quæruntur, ceteris plus poteſt domus:in honoribus & magiſtratibus altitudo:in fortuna & amicis & populari gloria, trigonus:in rebus ſingularibus & proprijs finis.Atque hac admonitione, eius quoque hęſitationis, quam autor hic de triangulorum finiumque prærogatiua mouet, perfpicuam ſolutionem fecimus. Porro quod autor in fine capitis dicit has eſſe vniuerfas planetarum poteftates eſſentiales in ſignis, Ptolemzi doaring & verbis, fi faciem quam contempfit tollas, con fentit. translation: The faces of the signs are called Decans. There are three Decans for each sign, each of which exercises authority over ten parts of the 30 parts of any sign. The order of the Decans begins with Aries and Mars, and continues in order to the end of the zodiac according to the natural order of both signs and planets. For example, the first Decan is [Mars], the second [Sun], the third [Venus]. When this order is followed, the first Decan of Taurus is [Venus], the second [Mercury], the third [Moon]. Following this order, [Saturn] is admitted as the first Decan in the sign of Gemini, and similarly the following [planets] are placed over the following Decans. The method by which, given any degree of a sign, one can determine by calculation, without the aid of a table, which Decan the given degree obeys, is as follows: Count the number of signs from the beginning of Aries up to the beginning of the proposed sign. Then multiply the number of complete signs by three. Finally, to the number that arises from the triplication of the whole dodecatemoria, add the complete Decans of the proposed sign, along with the incomplete Decan whose lord is sought. This done, divide the sum by 7. And from what remains after the division, the first unit is assigned to Mars, the second, if present, to the Sun, and so on according to the natural order of the planets, until the progression reaches the last unit, that is, the lord of the incomplete Decan in question. For example, if it is proposed to investigate which Decan rules the 15th part of a sign, 6 must be multiplied by 3 (for from the beginning to the beginning, six complete signs are counted), which makes 18. To this, add 2 for one complete Decan and one incomplete Decan, making 20 Decans, which, divided by 7, leaves 6. Now, if the first unit of the remaining number is assigned to Mars, the second to the Sun, the third to Venus, the fourth to Mercury, the fifth to the Moon, the sixth incomplete Decan will finally reach Saturn, which the table also shows. Here are assigned certain co-workers or benefactors, who are three deities applied to each Decan. Also, certain images that arise with each Decan, called by the Greeks 'planatellonta', of which Ptolemy makes no mention, and, as I believe, he counts all of this chapter among the 'irrational arrangements and unreasoned causes'. Therefore, leaving these aside, let us proceed to those things that are not prepared for the sake of vain ostentation, but are confirmed by physical causes. The first chapter is about the virtues of the planets. And because now, with God's approval, we have discussed the powers of the planets in the signs, which are house, exaltation, triplicity, term, and face: Now therefore let us discuss their virtues or strengths in these themselves. For the lord of the house has five strengths, and the lord of the exaltation four, the lord of the triplicity three, the lord of the term two, and the lord of the face one. Understand therefore from this number the strengths of the planets. For those who abound more in number, abound more in strength. And some prefer the term to the triplicity, that is, they want the lord of the term to be stronger than the lord of the triplicity. But we have already explained above, in what each is valid. Some also gave such a comparison concerning this matter, saying that when a planet is in its house, it is like a man in his house and in his dominion. And when it is in its exaltation, it is like a man in his kingdom and glory. And when it is in its term, it is like a man among his parents and relatives and people. And when it is in its triplicity, it will be like a man in his honor, and among his helpers and ministers. And when it is in its face, it will be like a man in his mastery. These are the universal essential powers of the planets in the signs, and the figures of the signs follow these. And because the author has now established the universal familiarities of the stars and places, which they call the essential powers or dignities of the planets in the signs, as five in number, namely house, exaltation, trigon, term, and face. Since we have explained what each of these is, it is next to be said how great the force and power of each is, that is, how much strength and power each power can or cannot confer on the planets. And this is what the author says, that he will now discuss the virtues or strengths of the planets placed in these dignities themselves. Since the house is the noblest of all dignities, he therefore assigns five strength points to it. Next to this is the exaltation, and for this reason it is endowed with four points of strength. Then follows the trigon, distinguished by three points of power. After that, the dignity of the term, marked by two points. The last is the face, and for this reason signified by one point. This order of dignities is arranged in numbers so that we can more easily estimate and judge the magnitude of the strength or weakness of the stars, which they acquire in any place of the sky by their 'synoikeiosis' (familiarity), and thus more correctly choose the victorious and predominant planet. For we can declare that a star which we find to collect a greater number of strengths or powers in some place of the sky, overcomes the other stars in dominion in that place. For example, in the first six parts of Libra, when Saturn obtains 5 powers for its house, 4 for its exaltation, 3 for its trigon, and two for its terms, and thus easily surpasses all other wandering stars with a total of 14 powers: therefore, there is no doubt that Saturn is the ruler and victor in such a place. This order of dignities is general, and for this reason it should not be thought that it is observed everywhere consistently. For these dignities are accustomed to change their strength and powers in many ways with respect to the matters to be explained. Indeed, in matters concerning life, the house has more power than others; in honors and magistracies, the exaltation; in fortune and friends and popular glory, the trigon; in singular and proper matters, the term. And with this admonition, we have also provided a clear solution to the hesitation that the author raises here about the prerogative of trigons and terms. Furthermore, what the author says at the end of the chapter, that these are the universal essential powers of the planets in the signs, agrees with Ptolemy's doctrine and words, if you remove the face which he despised. For he also, as soon as he familiarized the stars with the dodecatemoria,

title: Ptolemy on Dignities and Carpenta

Eæ verò funttres, quas ibidem ſtatim fubijcit, nempe carpentum, perſona & gaudium, quod ſupra cum de domiciliis planetarum ageretur, expofuimus. De perſona dicetur infrà in tertia differentia. Igitur Carpentum eft, cùm planeta quaſi in omni ſuo gaudio, & in omni ſua dignitate eſt. Nam natus tali ſignificatore, apud ſuos & apud alienos diligi tur, honoratur, omnia illi deferuntur, creduntur, vitam agit tra quillam, diſponit omnia cum facilitate quadam, vt verè fortunatus dici mereatur. At in domo planeta cùm fuerit, qui natus fuerit eo difponente, vim habebit perpetuam, & fine ſtrepitu ſpontè homines illi parebunt: vitam agut tranquillam, & quandam perpetuam efficaciam planetæ illius oftendunt:Si fit Mercurius, ſtudij & negotiationis: ſi ſit Venus, gratiæ & leporis: fi Iuppiter, probitatis & adminiſtrationis:fi Sol, dignitatis & majeftatis:fi Mars, audaciæ & promptitudinis, fortitudinis, iracun diæ:Si Saturnus, morofitatis, ftabilitatis,aſtutiæ, pertinaciæ, fo licitudinis:fi Luna, defiderij itinerum, & aliena negotia tractan di. At fi ad generales conſtitutiones referas, eandem videbis ſimilitudinem. Nam fi Saturnus fuerit in domibus fuis, & dominetur, erunt tempora generaliter frigida & ficca, cum frigiditate fixa & valida : & fi fuerit æftas, erunt tempora perpetud temperata.Si Iuppiter, erit tempus ferenum, iucundum, pulchrum, latum, cum ventis moderatis & gratis. Et fi mars, tempus aridisfimum & ficcifsimum:fi Sol, tempus calidiſsimum, cum paucis pluuijs tamen;ſi Venus, totum lene, ſuaue, pluuiofum:fi Mercu rius, totum varium & inconſtans & mutabile, vt nunquam tribus diebus perpetuis in eodem tenore conſtet:fi Luna, tempus nubiloſum, pluuioſum, inconſtans & graue. Is igitur cùm in Leone eſt, maximos facit feruores atque æqualiter dominatur. At Leo Solis domus eſt, imo carpentum & folium (ſed trigonorum ratio vix debet, inter carpenti partes numerari, cùm ſit à domo pendens & altitudine) ideo Solis vis in Leone à domicilio procedit. translation: He shows that the familiarity of the house, exaltation, trigon, and terms are to be reconciled, rejecting faces, dodecatemoria, and other such subtle and empty divisions of rulerships. He concludes this same treatise with similar words, saying: "The familiarities of the stars and the dodecatemoria are such," that is, the familiarities of the stars and places are almost in this manner. The difference, however, is in this, that the author said all, but Ptolemy said almost all, indicating by the particle "almost" that besides those already mentioned, there are still other familiarities of signs with stars. These are three, which he immediately adds in the same place, namely, the carpentum, the persona, and the joy, which we explained above when discussing the domiciles of the planets. The persona will be discussed below in the third difference. As for the carpentum, we read that Ptolemy wrote about it in this manner: "And they are said to be in their own splendors, and on thrones and in their own chariots, when two or more of the aforementioned modes of familiarity are found to be associated with the places in which they are found. For then their powers are especially increased for action, due to the similarity and cooperative nature of the dodecatemoria's innate familiarity, and they are said to be enthroned and to shine." That is, they are said to be placed in splendors, and as if carried in royal chariots and carpenta, and to sit on thrones, and to be adorned with such excellences, when they have a right to those places in which they are found, by two or more of the modes of familiarity already explained above. For when their powers are especially increased for action, due to the similarity of the dodecatemoria, as far as innate properties are concerned, and the cooperative action of the same, then they are said to sit on their own thrones and to shine. From these words of Ptolemy, it is clear that the carpentum is not a simple dignity, but one composed of other simple dignities. For he wants a planet to achieve carpentum only when it is in a place related by two or more modes of association. For example, when Mercury is within the first seven degrees of Virgo, it obtains three simple dignities, namely, house, exaltation, and terms. Therefore, placed in such a place, it is said to be carried in a carpentum and to sit on its own throne. Thus, Jupiter in the first twelve degrees of Sagittarius, where it possesses house, trigon, and terms, as if in its own throne or carpentum, triumphs, etc. Ptolemy says that the power of planets sitting on their own thrones, that is, their faculty of action, is excellently increased for two reasons. The first is the similarity of properties innate to both signs and planets, which doubles the planet's power of action, and this is the efficient cause. The second is the cooperative nature of the dodecatemoria, that is, the proper power of the signs, which helps to perfect the action of the present stars. And this is the material cause of the effects. From this, it follows that signs empty of planets are not idle, but by their inherent power, they foreshadow those effects which the stars related to them accomplish. But now let us conclude this section with a brief repetition of what we have said, namely, that according to Ptolemy, there are seven kinds of dignities, or modes by which stars are reconciled to places in the sky: namely, house, exaltation, trigon, and terms, persona, joy, and carpentum. Of these modes, the first six are pure or simple; the last, however, is composed of simple ones. Finally, of the simple ones, the first four, concerning whose prerogative among themselves we have already spoken, are principal. The other two are less principal, with persona preceding joy. From this, it follows that the carpentum is to be considered the first and most noble of all, while joy is the last and weakest of all. Concerning that similarity, by which the author, according to the common opinion of astrologers, institutes a comparison of the house to dominion, and of exaltation to kingship, etc., there exists not only the judgment of the most celebrated man Cardano, but also a most beautiful exposition of all these dignities, explaining what each can do in any matter. Therefore, we have taken care to include his entire passage on this matter here. He says, "It must be seen what each of these can do, and not like Haly, who compares house to house, exaltation to kingdom, and explains the powers of dignities in such a way that you know as much after reading what he writes about them as you did before. Therefore, one must pay attention, since this is a most excellent tradition, and discovered by us through long observation and subtle reasoning. Nor should we be less grateful to Haly, who, besides what he could do, provided us with the cause for inquiring into the truth. Therefore, a carpentum is when a planet is, as it were, in all its joy and in all its dignity. For one born with such a significator is loved and honored by his own and by strangers, everything is deferred to him, believed, he leads a tranquil life, arranges everything with a certain ease, so that he truly deserves to be called fortunate. But when a planet is in its house, one born under its disposition will have perpetual power, and without noise, people will spontaneously obey him: they lead a tranquil life, and show a certain perpetual efficacy of that planet: if it is Mercury, of study and negotiation; if it is Venus, of grace and charm; if Jupiter, of probity and administration; if the Sun, of dignity and majesty; if Mars, of audacity and promptness, fortitude, anger; if Saturn, of moroseness, stability, cunning, obstinacy, solicitude; if the Moon, of desire for journeys, and handling other people's affairs. But if you refer to general constitutions, you will see the same similarity. For if Saturn is in its houses and rules, the times will generally be cold and dry, with a fixed and strong coldness: and if it is summer, the times will be perpetually temperate. If Jupiter, the time will be serene, pleasant, beautiful, broad, with moderate and pleasing winds. And if Mars, the time will be very arid and very dry; if the Sun, the time will be very hot, with few rains however; if Venus, everything will be mild, sweet, rainy; if Mercury, everything will be varied and inconstant and changeable, so that it never remains in the same tenor for three continuous days; if the Moon, the time will be cloudy, rainy, inconstant and heavy. If anyone asks, whence is this from Ptolemy? I answer, Ptolemy taught us this expressly, that the action of the Sun is to be imitated in all things. Therefore, when it is in Leo, it causes the greatest heat and rules equally. But Leo is the Sun's house, indeed, its carpentum and throne (but the ratio of trigons should hardly be counted among the parts of the carpentum, since it depends on the house and exaltation), therefore the Sun's power in Leo proceeds from its domicile. By the same reasoning, exaltation itself is understood. For the dignity of exaltation in private nativities brings new and not perpetual powers, and more among strangers than among one's own, and produces a restless kind of life. Therefore, exaltation is not to be compared to the house in terms of tranquility, or duration, or ease. But because it is new and unusual: Defense

Quis enim nó ma gis miretur hæreditatem quatuor millium coronatorum ab ex terno acceptam, quàm quòd filius patri ſuccedit in triginta mil libus?Atque ea ratio eſt, quòd multi altitudinem domui præpo fuerint. Itaque in generalibus conſtitutionibus Sol cumintrat Arietem altitudinem fuam, totum commouet orbem, omnia pullulant, omnia exhilaratur, nec tamen calefacit vt in Leone, imo neque vt in Cancro nec Tauro. Et vt vno verbo dicam, do mus rem quietam & perpetuam facit, altitudo in mutatione & ſpecie maioré. Trigonus verò plus habet virtutis in diuidendo regiones terræ quàm in genituris. In genituris autem ſignificat robur & efficaciam, receptaq; apud homines mandata illius, fi præſit, vel confilia, ſi ſit ſocius. Fines declarant vires potentiores, ſed temporibus terminatas, veluti legationes, annuos magiftratus & negotia fingularia.In temporibus verò mutationes non generales, fed quæ paucis diebus aut menibus finiant. Vndefines, quòd finemaccipiant, diai. Qualitates autem cùm animi tum corporis expoliunt, non faciunt. Vnde qui in finibus martis aut Saturni morum dominum habuerit, poterit qui dem eſſe bonus alioquin, ſed moribus rudibus, agreſtibus & incomptis. In corporis etiam affe&ibus, mala lenit aut auget fi nis, non facit. Perſona facit, vt videatur & ſpeciem referat, non vt fit. Gaudium autem lenit aut exacerbat ſignificata, ſed vix ſentitur. translation: 72. DIFFERENCE. It brings unexpected and almost unhoped-for advantages, and is considered more illustrious by most people. For humans tend to admire the unexpected more, even if weaker and smaller, than the usual and greater things. Who would not marvel more at an inheritance of four thousand crowns received from a stranger, than at a son succeeding his father to thirty thousand? And this is the reason why many have preferred altitude to house. Therefore, in general constitutions, when the Sun enters Aries, its exaltation, it moves the whole world, everything sprouts, everything is cheered, yet it does not heat as in Leo, nor even as in Cancer or Taurus. And to say it in one word, the house makes a thing quiet and perpetual, the exaltation in change and a greater appearance. The trine, however, has more virtue in dividing regions of the earth than in nativities. In nativities, it signifies strength and efficacy, and the mandates of that person are received by people, if he presides, or his counsels, if he is an associate. The terms declare more powerful forces, but limited by times, such as embassies, annual magistracies, and singular affairs. In times, however, changes are not general, but those that end in a few days or months. Hence, terms, because they receive an end, are called days. Qualities, however, polish both mind and body, they do not create them. Hence, he who has the lord of manners in the terms of Mars or Saturn, may indeed be otherwise good, but with rude, rustic, and unkempt manners. In bodily affections too, the term alleviates or increases evil, it does not create it. The person makes it appear and represent a species, not that it is. Joy, however, alleviates or exacerbates the significations, but is barely felt. This is Cardan's view. CONCERNING THE ESSENCES OF THE SIGNS, AND what they signify in themselves. Because in the signs there are certain signs that are called rational, namely, Virgo, Gemini, Libra, and Aquarius, the first half of Sagittarius, because their images in the circle are figured as images of humans. These are also called signs having beautiful voices. These also thrive when they are in the East. And some are called winged, namely Gemini, Virgo, Pisces. And some are quadrupedal, like Leo, Sagittarius. Some of them are domestic, like Aries, Taurus, Capricorn, these thrive when they are in the South. Virgo, however, and Capricorn and Aquarius thrive when they are in the North. And among the signs, some are tortuous and vicious, namely, Aries, Taurus, Cancer, Scorpio, and Capricorn. And some of them are said to have many offspring, namely Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces, and these thrive when they are in the West. And some of them are sterile, like Gemini, Leo, and Virgo. And some have few children: like Aries, Taurus, Libra, Sagittarius, Capricorn, and Aquarius. And some are said to be very luxurious: namely Aries, Taurus, Leo, and Capricorn. And among the signs, some are said to have a medium voice, which are formed in the images of bleating, bellowing, and roaring animals, like Aries, Taurus, Leo, and Capricorn, the last part of Sagittarius. And some are voiceless, namely those formed in the images of voiceless animals: namely Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces. This title: Concerning the essences of the signs, and what they signify in themselves, seems to indicate that these differences of the signs, which are discussed in this chapter, should be referred to the simple and naturally inherent properties of the dodecatemoria. But if we wish to examine the matter more accurately, we will find that they are not entirely simple, but rather derived from the fixed stars, and from the connection of the images of the eighth sphere. And this is the reason why, at the end of the preceding chapter, after the completed discussion of the dignities of the planets in the signs, or rather of the reconciliation of the planets with the natural temperament of the dodecatemoria, it says that the figures of the signs follow, or as it is said more clearly in Greek, τὰ μορφώματα τοῦ ζῳδίου, that is, the appellations accommodated to the dodecatemoria from the adjacent forms of the signs. And immediately at the beginning of this chapter, it says that the dodecatemoria of Gemini, Virgo, Aquarius, and the first half of Sagittarius are called human because the images connected to these signs are figured in the likeness of a human image. This was especially true in the time of the ancients and Ptolemy, when, as has been copiously explained above, not only did the head of the starry Aries coincide with the vernal intersection, but also the entire image of the same Aries was joined to the Arian dodecatemorion, and in the same way the subsequent images in the eighth sphere coincided entirely with the dodecatemoria following in the outermost sphere. But in our time, since the first star of Aries has receded 17 parts and a little more from the vernal equinox, and all the other constellations have made similar progressions εἰς τὰ ἐπόμενα, it is clear that, having abandoned the dodecatemoria they once occupied, they have almost entirely migrated into the succeeding dodecatemoria. For example, the image or sign of Aries of the eighth sphere has for the most part been transferred into the dodecatemorion of Taurus of the outermost sphere, and similarly the Taurine image of the eighth sphere has proceeded into the dodecatemorion of Gemini of the outermost sphere, and the image of Gemini into the Cancerian dodecatemorion. Therefore, the dodecatemorion of Gemini, for example, which was once called a human sign with the added image of Gemini, can no longer be correctly called human, but rather quadrupedal; and the Cancerian dodecatemorion, once named reptilian from the connected image, will now be more correctly called human because of the ingress of the image of Gemini. For there is no doubt that such a translation of the things themselves must follow the translation of the appellations that the dodecatemoria assume from the images of the eighth sphere. This must be observed more diligently, lest by abusing these appellations as if they were constantly inherent in the dodecatemoria, we fall into a very gross error. Haly wrote these words to the same effect: Many things, he says, the ancients told you about the signs, but it is fitting that you accept from their sayings that which agrees with Ptolemy's opinion, because he alone is the one who follows the natural thing. For he holds that each sign has its separate power, because of the fixed stars existing in each sign. And for this reason, it is fitting that you inspect.

translation: FIRST. You will judge which stars belong to which sign, and according to this, you will judge. And when you have done this, you will naturally have followed this art, and you will be in agreement with Ptolemy's opinion. For it is fitting that you do this, and dismiss all other sayings, and not lose yourself, like one who wants to take many paths and does not know which is certain. And thus you will not go astray, like one who does not know what is truth and what is not. But Ptolemy's words, by which he teaches that the appellations of the dodecatemoria are derived from the adjacent figures of the fixed stars, are these: "But they also applied other appellations to the dodecatemoria, taken from the figures of the stars, for example, quadrupedal and terrestrial, and ducal, and fertile, and so named. Which appellations, since they are evident from their figures, we deem it superfluous to enumerate here. The qualities of such forms, insofar as their powers are useful in judgments, have been expounded by us." By this text of Ptolemy, we are usefully and very timely admonished that the enumeration of appellations, which the places of the heavens take from the adjacent images of living things, by which the entire machinery of the world is distinguished, is by no means necessary: since it is easy to understand the powers of each from its form or figure: especially, however, since, as he himself testifies here, insofar as they can or should be usefully considered in judgments, it has been sufficiently expounded in its proper places. Ptolemy did this in Book 1, Chapter on the powers of the fixed stars, and throughout all the books of the Apotelesmatics. Haly is of the opinion that it is superfluous to enumerate these differences of the signs, because they are inconstant, and are changed over longer periods due to the transit of figures into succeeding places. For the same reasons, the author also, as it seems, distinguishes at least twelve principal figures, which are interwoven with the zodiac, in this chapter as an example with various appellations. "Human or anthropomorphic signs" are those in which human effigies are found, such as Gemini, Virgo, Aquarius, and the middle part of Sagittarius. The author and Haly also include Libra among the human signs, because of its proximity to Virgo, I think. Why Gemini and Virgo are called "winged," that is, having wings, the painting of the globes sufficiently indicates. But the "gills" of Pisces are like wings. However, I do not remember having read in Ptolemy that the figures of Pisces and Gemini are called winged, who, when he speaks of winged creatures, for example, names Virgo and Sagittarius with a hen. "Quadrupedal" signs are Taurus, Leo, and the last part of Sagittarius. And two of these, namely Leo and Sagittarius, are "wild" or untamed: the rest, however, are "tame" or domestic. The author adds that human signs are also called vocal, and the same in the east: but quadrupedal signs exert greater power in the south than in other quadrants, with Virgo, Aquarius, and Capricorn being more in agreement with the North. In general, however, the figures as well as the appellations of all signs took their origin from their natural effects, which they impress upon the subjects and those things that particularly agree with them. As in general constitutions, human signs signify events that will turn out for the human race, and those of other figures for similar brutes and similar things: which Ptolemy expounded more fully in Book 3, Chapter 7, on the kind of events. In nativities also, human signs bestow sociability, gentleness, and civil custom upon the natives. On the contrary, those that have the names of wild beasts, such as Leo, engender inhospitality, savagery, insolence, and rapacity. But those that are called domestic, indeed add gentleness to the native, but with a certain humility and abjection. To this belongs that saying of Hermes: "Whoever does not have the rulers of his nativity or the ascendant in human signs, will be alienated from humanity even to himself." In the same way, Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces are called tortuous, vicious, and morbid signs, because they inflict upon the natives humps, lameness, dislocations, ulcers, and such calamities. Ptolemy calls them "afflicted signs": "especially," he says, "when the Moon is in the nodes or flexures, or in the afflicted signs, such as Cancer and Pisces, there occur bodily defects of humps, lameness, and dislocations." And there a little further down, where he speaks of judgments of diseases from the signs of the eighth sphere: "Specifically," he says, "Cancer, Capricorn, and Pisces, and generally all creeping and aquatic signs, particularly cause diseases of ulcers that consume flesh and creeping skin diseases, scrofula, fistulas, leprosy, and such diseases." Similarly, "fertile signs," that is, those having many offspring, such as Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces, have found their name from their effect. He says that these are strong in the west, while "sterile signs" are Aries and Leo. Furthermore, he seems to wrongly enumerate Gemini among the sterile signs, since their image is "two-bodied," and for this reason, according to Ptolemy in Book 4, Chapter on children, it multiplies the number of children. But the six remaining signs, namely Taurus, Virgo, Libra, Sagittarius, Capricorn, and Aquarius, since they have a middle nature in this matter, are not improperly called signs of few children. Similarly, since the signs of men are called vocal: but Cancer, Scorpio, and Pisces are mute: the rest, namely Aries, Taurus, Leo, Capricorn, and the last part of Sagittarius, are not undeservedly left to a middle nature. These are the differences of the twelve signs interwoven with the zodiac. The principal ones of the rest that are situated outside the Zodiac, whose use Ptolemy shows in their proper places, are almost these: "Signs of birds," such as the eagle and the hen: "Aquatic signs," such as the dolphin and Pisces: "Shipwrecked signs," such as Argo and the ship: "Poisonous signs," such as Scorpio and serpents: "Mutilated signs," such as Taurus and the head of Medusa: "Bound signs," such as Andromeda and Cepheus: "Ducal signs," such as both the northern and southern crowns. All these can be reduced to a compendium in this way. Some images are of animals, others of things. Of things, such as ducal, like both crowns: and shipwrecked, like Argo. Furthermore, of animals, some are flying in the air or winged, such as the eagle and the hen: others are swimming in the waters: and of these again, some are called marine, such as the dolphin: and others are called fluvial, such as the fish.

Sed Ptolemæŭ paulò aliter fentire, atq; à gra uioribus caufis de futurorű morborű vitiorúq; locis & qualitatibus coniectură facere, fatis cõftare nobis poteſt ex ſuis verbis, quæ extant lib. Apoteleſ. 3. capite de vitijs & morbis corporis. εὐταῦθα μὲν, inquit, πρὸς τὼ καθόλο διάληψιμ, αποβλέπομ δεῖ πρὸς τὰ τὸ ὁρίζοντα δύο κέντρα, τότισι, ὁ ἀνατολικόμ καὶ τό δια τικόμ μάλιςα δὲ πρός τετὸ διεύομ αὐτὸ καὶ πρὸς τὸ ἡγέμψομ,ὅ ἐσιμ ἀσιώδετομτῷ ἀνατολικῷ κέντρῳική πρατηρῶμ τὸς κακοποιός τη ἀς έρωμ, πῶς ἐληματισμένοι πρὸς αὐτό τυγχάνοσιμ.ἐὰμ γαὶ πρὸς τὰς ἐπαναφορομένας μοίρας ἢ εἰρημένωμ τόπωμώσιμ ἐςῶτες ἤτοι σωματικῶς ἤτοι τετραγωνικός. Η καὶ κατὰ διάμετρομ: Ητοιότοός πόρος αὐτὸν ἢ κὶ ἀμφότεροι : σίνω κὶ πάθη σωματικά ἐπὶ τὸς γλυ νωμένες ὑπονοκτέους μάλιςα δ' ἄμκὴ τὴν φωτῶν ἤτοι ὁ ἑτόρομ, κ᾿ ἀμφότερα κεκεντρωμένα καθ' ὅμ εἰρήκαμαν βόπομ τυγχάνη ἅμα, Η καὶ κατὰ διάμετρομ, τότε γαρ & μόνομἐαμἐπαναφόρηταί τις την κακοποιώμἀλλὰ καμπροαναφόρηται την φωτῶν αὐτὸς κεκενβωνε μένο, ἱκανός βι διατιθέναι τι τῶν ἐκκειμένων, ὁποῖον ἂν δι τε το ό -. βίζοντο τόποι κι ὁι την ζωδίων ὑποφαίνεσι σίνα Η πάθθ, κ) αἱ ἀτέρων φύσεις την τε κακούντων κὶ τ'κακομένων, κὶ ἔτι τῶν συλία ματιζομένων αὐτοῖς. τοί το γαρ μέρη τ' ζωδίων έκας τὰ ποριέχονο τα τὸ ἀδικόμενον μέρα το ορίζοντα, δηλώσει τὸ μέρα το σώμασ 5 S T
translation: and what follows, Mars the chest and what follows, the Sun the shins. Digitized by Google. FIRST. 85. And the shins and ankles, that is, the heels, Venus the neck, Mercury the head, Luna the thighs. Luna the thighs of a man in Cancer, Saturn the genitals and all that follows them, Jupiter the thighs, Mars the chest, the Sun the feet, Venus the arms and shoulders, Mercury the eyes and throat, Luna the head. In Leo, Saturn the private parts and what follows, Jupiter the thighs and knees, Mars the belly, the Sun the head, Venus the heart, Mercury the shoulders and throat, Luna the neck. In Virgo, Saturn the feet, Jupiter the knees and what follows them, Mars the belly, the Sun the neck, Venus the belly, Mercury the heart, Luna the shoulders. In Libra, Saturn the knees and what follows them, Jupiter the eyes and what follows them, that is, parts of the head, Mars the private parts and what follows them, the Sun the shoulders, Venus the head, Mercury the belly, Luna the heart. In Scorpio, Saturn the ankles, that is, the heels, and what follows them, Jupiter the feet, Mars the head, arms and thighs, the Sun the heart, Venus the private parts and what follows them, Mercury the back, Luna the belly. In Sagittarius, Saturn the feet, Jupiter the shins and head, Mars the feet and hands, the Sun the belly, Venus the thighs and arms, Mercury the private parts and heart, Luna the back. In Capricorn, Saturn the head and feet, Jupiter the knees and eyes, Mars the shins and shoulders, the Sun the back, Venus the thighs and heart, Mercury the private parts and what follows them, Luna the thighs. In Aquarius, Saturn the head and neck, Jupiter the shoulders, chest and feet, Mars the ankles and heart, the Sun the private parts and what follows them, Venus the knees and what follows them, Mercury the thighs and heart, Luna the private parts. In Pisces, Saturn the shoulders, arms and neck, Jupiter the heart and head, Mars the ankles and belly, the Sun the thighs and what follows them, Venus the neck and back, Mercury the shins and private parts, Luna the genitals. In the previous chapter, the agreement of the signs with the body parts was shown; in this one, it wants to indicate the parts of the body that the wandering stars affect as they pass through the signs. For example, if Saturn is placed in the sixth house of the nativity, which signifies illness, threatening infirmity to the native, then according to this rule, if Saturn is in Aries, the chest will be in danger; if in Taurus or Gemini, the belly; if in Cancer, the genitals and all that follows them, etc. For greater ease of use, we have included a summary in the following figure. ARIES, chest and belly. back. head and neck. thighs and knees. TAURUS, belly. GEMINI, belly. private parts and what follows. Chest and what follows. shins and ankles. feet. head. neck. shins. feet. head. knees. shins. thighs. CANCER, genitals and all that follows them. thighs. FIRST. LEO, private parts and what follows them. thighs and knees. VIRGO, feet. knees and what follows them. belly. chest. belly. feet. head. arms and shoulders. heart and belly. neck. eyes and throat. shoulders and throat. heart. neck. shoulders. LIBRA, knees and what follows them. eyes and what follows them. private parts and what follows them. shoulders. head. belly. heart. SCORPIO, ankles and what follows them. feet. head, arms and thighs. heart. private parts and what follows them. back. belly. SAGITTARIUS, feet. shins and head. feet and hands. belly. thighs and arms. private parts and heart. back. CAPRICORN, head and feet. knees and eyes. shins and shoulders. back. AQUARIUS, head and neck. PISCES. shoulders, chest and feet. shoulders, arms and neck. heart and head. ankles and heart. ankles and belly. private parts and what follows them. thighs and what follows them. thighs and heart. knees and what follows them. neck and back. thighs and heart. shins and private parts. genitals. private parts and what follows them. thighs. The reason for this division, by which parts of the human body are distributed to the planets as they pass through all the signs, appears to be as follows. To each planet, its own house, which first occurs after Aries in the subsequent signs, is assigned the first part of the human body, namely the head. If the same planet then passes through the sign next to that house in the subsequent signs, it will also affect the second part of the human body, and the part closest to the head, that is, the neck, and similarly, as it travels through the subsequent signs, it will take care of the subsequent parts of the human body, whose order has been explained above. For example: since Capricorn is the house of Saturn, and indeed the one that first occurs when counting from Aries in the subsequent signs, therefore Saturn, when placed in Capricorn, will take care of the head; in Aquarius, the sign immediately following Capricorn, the part closest to the head, namely the neck; in Pisces, the shoulders and arms; in Aries, the chest; and in such an order, in the subsequent signs, it will affect the subsequent parts of the human body. Not only to the twelve signs of the zodiac and the seven planets, but also to the twelve stations of the sky described below, some have been accustomed to distribute the parts of the human body in a completely similar manner, and just as Aries is the first among the signs, so the ascendant, the first of the twelve houses, is considered to occupy the first part of the body, namely the head. For this reason, to the second house, the second part of the body, namely the neck, is attributed, and similarly to the remaining subsequent houses, the remaining subsequent parts of the body. This seems to be derived from the 74th aphorism of the Centiloquium, whose words are these: "Whoever has Mars ascending will certainly have a scar on his face." Therefore, what the ascendant, the first house of the natal chart, can do for the head, the first part of the body, I believe it has been judged that the second house can similarly achieve and exercise for the neck, the second part of the body, and the subsequent houses for the subsequent parts. These are the reasons why we see most astrologers insist on detecting and pronouncing the places of diseases and defects determined by the stars. But Ptolemy, as we can clearly see from his words in Book 3, Chapter on bodily defects and diseases, of his Apotelesmatics, thought somewhat differently and made conjectures about the places and qualities of future diseases and defects from more serious causes. "Here," he says, "for a general understanding, we must look at the two centers of the horizon, that is, the eastern and western, but especially at the one that is itself moving and at the one that is not fixed to the eastern center, observing how the maleficent stars are configured to it. For if they are positioned in the ascending degrees of the aforementioned places, either bodily or quadratically, or diametrically, or if such a place or both are afflicted, then bodily affections are to be suspected in the natives, especially if either of the luminaries, or both, are angular in the manner we have said, or diametrically opposite. For then, not only if one of the malefics is ascending, but also if one of the luminaries is ascending, being angular itself, it is sufficient to dispose to one of the aforementioned conditions, whatever the places of the horizon and the signs indicate together with the affection, and the natures of the stars, both those that cause harm and those that are harmed, and also those that are configured with them. For each of the parts of the zodiac that contain the afflicted part of the horizon will indicate the part of the body." S T

translation: Thus far, he has explained those things which contribute to understanding the nature of the celestial spaces and the power of celestial bodies. In the following, he will enumerate all the names of astrologers, including the aphetic and anaretic places, and the profections, directions, and other revolutions of the aphetic places. The first chapter is named after the six great conjunctions, which, I believe, received their name from the rarity and magnitude of the accidents they produce, such as wars, plagues, earthquakes, floods, and similar effects, which are believed to occur with great delay and evidence. Indeed, the first and greatest of all, the conjunction of Saturn and Jupiter, celebrated at the beginning of Aries, is said to return every 960 years and generally dispose of the same times. Similarly, the second conjunction, which is the transit of the conjunction of Saturn and Jupiter from one trigon to another, occurs every 240 years. The third, which is the conjunction of Saturn and Jupiter at the beginning of Cancer, occurs every 30 years. The fourth, which is the conjunction of Saturn and Jupiter in any sign, occurs every 20 years. The fifth place is held by the true return of the Sun to the point of the vernal equinox, or, as they commonly say, the Sun's ingress into the first point of Aries. The last and least of these is the synod or diameter of the luminaries. Besides these, there are many other smaller and lesser conjunctions, nearly 120, a catalog of which Haly and Pontanus enumerate in the aforementioned 50th chapter of the Centiloquium. Furthermore, that the author says the greatest conjunction of Saturn and Jupiter returns every 960 years, and the second every 240 years, is very variable, and can only be true if we assume that the conjunction of Saturn and Jupiter returns every 20 years, and proceeds annually by 2425 parts, that is, by 8 signs and two and a half parts. For example, if the conjunction of Saturn and Jupiter first begins at the beginning of Aries, then after 20 years from the root, according to this thesis, the second conjunction of Saturn and Jupiter will be in 2 degrees, 30 minutes of Leo. The third, after 40 years from the root, in 5 degrees, 2 minutes. The fourth, after 60 years, in 7 degrees, 30 minutes. The fifth, after 80 years, in 10 degrees. The sixth, after 100 years, in 12 degrees, 30 minutes of Scorpio. The seventh, after 120 years, in 15 degrees of Sagittarius. The eighth, after 140 years, in 17 degrees, 30 minutes of Capricorn. The ninth, after 160 years, in 20 degrees of Aquarius. The tenth, after 180 years, in 22 degrees, 30 minutes of Pisces. The eleventh, after 200 years, in 27 degrees, 30 minutes of Aries. The twelfth, after 220 years, in 36 degrees, that is, in 6 degrees of Leo. Therefore, after 240 years from the root, this great conjunction will migrate from the fiery trigon to the earthy trigon, for it will be at the end of 30 degrees of Pisces, that is, at the beginning of Taurus, and likewise in this earthy, as they call it, triangularity, it will remain for 240 years, and in the same bodies, Saturn and Jupiter will similarly conjoin twelve times. Therefore, if you multiply 12 by 20, you will again get 240 years, after which the great conjunction will pass to the beginning of Gemini, and again, after exactly 240 years, it will come to the beginning of Cancer, and will remain in its trigon for 240 years; and finally, after a total of 960 years, it will return to the beginning of Aries, that is, to the fiery triangularity. It will therefore remain in each trigon for 240 years. And since there are 4 trigons, multiply 4 by 240, which will be 960 years, after which the greatest conjunction, changing from the watery trigon, will return to Aries, from where it first departed. But it appears that this hypothesis of astrologers is false. For the minor conjunction of Saturn and Jupiter occurs not every 20 years, but every 19 years and 315 days, 19 hours. And the process is by 243 degrees, 59 minutes, 18 seconds, 9 minutes, 19 seconds, that is, by 8 signs, 2 degrees, 59 minutes, 18 seconds, 9 minutes, 2 seconds. And so in one and the same nature of the trigons, as if from one to another, not, as the author seems to want, by 2 degrees, 30 minutes, 14 seconds, but proceeding by almost three parts. From which it is clear that the two superiors in one trigon do not, as is written here, meet twelve or thirteen times, but at least ten times, and thus that great conjunction in one and the same trigon does not persist for 240 common years, but at least 198 and a little less than one year. But we believe that a more extensive discussion and deeper inquiry into this matter neither pertains to our purpose nor to the true substance of the art; since the doctrine concerning great conjunctions, although both the learned and the unlearned vulgar equally rush into it, can be proven to be partly plainly erroneous, and partly not of such great importance as is thought, by arguments of fact and the authority of Ptolemy, who, at the beginning of the second book of the Apotelesmatics, explaining the general prognostic parts of astronomy, transferred both the foreknowledge of regions and cities, to which the significations of great events are directed, and their times, to the sole eclipses of the luminaries, making no mention of great conjunctions. His words are these: "For the general examination of cases, two things are especially taken into account: the dodecatemoria of the zodiac, and furthermore the familiarity and sympathy of the stars with each of the climes, and the significations of effects that occur in their proper parts at the proper time, according to the conjunctions of the Sun and Moon, and according to the transits of the wandering stars around their risings and stations. Therefore, before all things, we will explain the natural causes of the aforesaid sympathy, at the same time briefly recounting the bodily and moral peculiarities of all nations, which are not alien to the natural constitution of both the dominant stars and the places of the zodiac to which they are subject." And further down he says again: "There is no prior or more powerful cause of such cases than that which is derived from the eclipses of the Sun and Moon, and the transits of the stars at that time." But someone might say that this text of Ptolemy rather extols and strengthens the doctrine of great conjunctions than abolishes it, since the power of producing great and general events is attributed not only to the eclipses of the luminaries, but also to the transits of the wandering stars? I answer that, although Ptolemy, in general mutations, attributes power to the superior planets equally with the rest of the multitude of astrologers, he does not do so to the same extent, nor in the same way as they themselves think about their power. For they primarily observe the conjunctions of Jupiter and Saturn for discovering the times and places of great events, to which something is signified, and extend their effect for many centuries. But Ptolemy, on the contrary, considers the times and places.

Et duo gradus, qui fuerint vnius longitudinis à cao pite fignorum mobilium, dicuntur compotentes, id est, vnius fortitudinis, &confortes feu participes in vir tute, vt XX. gradus Capricorni vel Cancri cum X. gras du Sagittary vel Geminorum, XX. gradus Arietis wel Libre cum X.gradu Piſcium vel Virginis. translation: DIFFERENCE. Fortunate. Unfortunate. Fortunate. Unfortunate. 4 7 26 4 7 4 25 1 15 4 26 2 13 4 27 2 20 2 25 2 7 2 24 14 24 18 12 28 12 ++ 25 22 ++ 26 m 15 P 25 ++ 13 p 26 ++ 9 X 26 p X 28 21 14 X 9 X 13. ON COMPOTENT DEGREES. And two degrees, which are of the same longitude from the head of the movable signs, are called compotent, that is, of the same strength, and partners or participants in virtue, as the 20th degree of Capricorn or Cancer with the 10th degree of Sagittarius or Gemini, the 20th degree of Aries or Libra with the 10th degree of Pisces or Virgo. Parts equally removed from the four cardinal points, generally called by the common people compotent degrees, or degrees of the same strength and power, the Greeks were accustomed to call τὰ ἀντίσκια μέρη. These are distinguished by Ptolemy in such a way that parts equally removed from the tropical points on both sides are called βλέποντος καὶ ίime μoντo, that is, beholding and equally powerful: but parts receding equally from the equinoctial points are properly and learnedly called προςκατοντσο καὶ ἀκής or, that is, commanding and obedient. The words of Ptolemy concerning commanding and obedient places are these:

translation: FIRST. They are: προςάποντα καὶ ἀκέοντα Λέγονη τμήματα τὰ κατ' ἴσίω διάςασιμ ἀχὶ τῷ αὐτε όποτορα του ισημερινών σημείωμ ακμαν libr.t.capt Ειλόμλια, δια τὸ ἐμοῖς ἴσοις χρόνοις αναφορες δέτε᾽ ἴωμ πραλ πυρί προς λάλωμ. τότωμ δὲ τὰ μεν οὐ τους θερινῷ ἡμικυκλίῳ προς άοσοντα και σαυτόνο λίτη, τα δ' ἐμ το χειμερινῷ ὑπακοντα: 21ὰ τὸ κατ' ἐκεῖνο μή γινό τωμα- μενου τόμ Ο μείζονα ποιεῖμ τνυκτὸς τὸ ἡμέραμ, κατὰ το δεές καόντωμ λάζω: that is, Commanding and obedient places are said to be those which are at an equal distance from one or both of the equinoctial points, and this is because they rise in an equal space of time and describe the same parallel circles. Moreover, those which are in the summer part of the sphere are called commanding, and those which are in the winter half are called obedient, because the Sun makes the days longer there, and the nights longer here. In this text, Ptolemy teaches us both the causes of familiarity between commanding and obedient places, and why the latter are subject to the former. He states one cause of familiarity to be that such places, being at equal intervals from the equinoctial points, have equal anaphoras or ascensions, as they call them, which is clear and known from the first spherical institutions, and the use of the anaphora table described by us below easily indicates the same. For example, in our altitude, that is, of the city of Agrippina, which is 51 parts, both dodecatemoria, namely and X, when equally receding from the vernal intersection, in their rising draw with them from the equinoctial 13 parts, 21 scruples, which converted into time make 0 hours, 57 scruples. Thus and rise equally with 17 parts, 25 scruples, that is, one temporal hour, and 14 scruples. In the same way, we must judge concerning the remaining dodecatemoria equally removed from the intersection, whether vernal or autumnal. He states another cause of association between the said parts to be that they describe equal parallel circles, which is equally easily established. For if four points are taken equally removed from the equinoctial intersection on both sides, the lines connecting the assumed points will be both equal to each other and equally removed from the diameter of the ecliptic, and will be equidistant from it by the 14th of the third element of Euclid, and will delineate parallel circles entirely equal to each other during one revolution of the world. But why are the northern places more powerful and nobler than the southern?

translation: DIFFERENCE. are nobler than the southern? He answers that since day is nobler than night and more suitable for action, therefore the places of the zodiac signs which bring an increase of days are deservedly called commanding, and the remaining ones in which the diurnal light is overcome by diurnal darkness are called obedient. Thus Aries commands and is more powerful than Pisces, and Taurus than Aquarius, and the rest than the rest, which we have described in the following table. Commanding | | 4 | Obedient | X ). p 5 121 my ++ But Ptolemy wanted to indicate to us not only entire dodecatemoria, but also other minute parts of the sky, by means of προςάσοντα, καὶ ἀκόοντα τμήματα. For example, the first part of Aries, and the last of Pisces, the second part of Aries, and the penultimate of Pisces: Likewise the tenth part of Aries, and the twenty-first of Pisces, the twentieth part of Taurus and the eleventh of Aquarius, and so on with the rest, which are equally removed from the vernal or autumnal intersection, mutually command and obey each other. For such an association of such parts we can correctly begin either from Aries and Pisces, or from Virgo and Libra: so that you might understand this more clearly, we have also included a figure of this matter.

translation: FIRST. 30 20 10 30 20 10 30 4 20 LO COMMANDING. 20 10 HATEFUL 70 20 20 Kri 70 20 10 HATEFUL 2 70 20 10 2030 10 20 30 OBEDIENT S 101 20 30 10 20 30 10 20 30 10 20 30 x Lib.1. cap.. Ptolemy described to us places that are beholding and equally powerful with these words: ἰσοδιωαμεῖμ φασίμ ἀλλήλοις μέρη, τὰ τῷ αὐτῶ καὶ όπος. τορα την τροπικόμ σημείωμτὸ ἴσομἀφεςῶτα, λὰ τὸ καθ' έκατέρο πόντωρ. καὶ ἰσοδυ αὐτῶν τε ο γινομένα, τις τε ἡμορας ταῖς ἡμέραις κὶ τὰς νύκτας ταῖς νυξί, κή, το διας ήματα τῇ οικείωμ ὡρῶμ ἰσόχρονα ἀποτελεῖθη, ναμόντων ταῦτα δε καὶ βλέπαμἄλληλα λέγεται, διά τε τα προφρημένα, καί ἐπειδήπερ εκάτερομ αὐth in to thì cὐτῇ μέρωμα ορίζοντ ἀνατέλλει, καὶ εἰς τὰ αὐτὰ καταδύς, that is: They say that parts are of the same power which are equally distant from one or both of the tropical points, because when the Sun passes through those parts, days become equal to days, and nights to nights, and finally hours become equal to hours. These are also said to behold each other, both for the reasons we have just mentioned, and because both rise and set in the same parts.
