translation: DIFFERENCE. from both tropical points, because when the Sun passes through those parts, days become equal to days, and nights to nights, and finally hours become equal to hours. These are also said to behold each other, both for the reasons we have just mentioned, and because both rise and set in the same parts. From these words, three causes are evident, by which parts equally removed from the tropical points, beholding each other, acquire equal power. The first is that they make days and nights equal: the second, that they maintain equal times for the hours: the third, that they always rise and set in one and the same place of the horizon. For example, when the Sun is in the 27th part of Cancer, the same amount of light and darkness is produced as when the Sun is in the fourth part of Gemini, and this in any position of the sphere. Similarly, the temporal hours to be explained below, of the 27th part of Cancer, both diurnal and nocturnal, are equal to the diurnal and nocturnal temporal hours of the fourth part of Gemini. Finally, whether the Sun is in the 27th part of Cancer, or in the 4th part of Gemini, it rises and sets in one and the same point of the horizon, if indeed the said points are constituted in one and the same parallel circle. Now, as for what remains, we propose to examine the beholding and equally powerful signs in the table. (Beholding M I X ++ p καμμια ip) What we warned about a little earlier in the discussion of commanding and obedient places, we repeat here, namely that all these things which are said about the equipollence of entire dodecatemoria, are to be referred not so much to the dodecatemoria themselves, as to parts and scruples of parts, the grammatical ἀπόδαξιμ of which we have presented to the eyes. HATEFUL AND BEHOLDING. HATEFUL AND BEHOLDING.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Antiscia and Inconjunct Places
text: Ac antiſcia quidem locorum ſigniferiad hunc modum quem deſignauimus, ſeſe habent. Ad vfus autem facilitaté, vtq; omnis ſoluatur obſcuritas, deſcribemus deinceps partium ſcrupulorumq; tabulam, ex qua cùm imperantes obtemperantesq;,tum verò ſeſe mutud intuentes ſignorű partes partiúq; ſcrupula facilimè depromuntur. Exempli gratia, fi Saturnus fit in part.20 ſc.10.Arietis, fitq; eius locipars tamobediens quam intuens feu æquipollens inuenienda: primò ex prioribus deſcriptionibus figuorú perfpicui eſt, bgnu Arietis imperare figno Pifcit, eun 104 DIFFERENTIA eundemq; Arietemintueri Virgine. Iam porro vtpartes quoq partibus,in quibus cómoratur Saturnus, correfpódentes cognoſcas, intra primò cum p.20.in tabulá ſequenté partiū, & èregione p.20.reperies ſcriptas p. 11. Mox etiä ſcrupula 10. mittas in tabella ſcrupulorú, & offerent ſe ſc. 51. Pronuciabis itaq; h in p.20.fc.10.Vimperare p. 11.fc.11.X, intueri verò p.11.fc.1. my. Nam cognita parte vnius, pars alterius nungignorabitur, id quod eſt obferuatiõe dignissimi. Julius Firmicus & autor opinantur in partem 30. dodecatemorij nulla partem proijcere antiſcium, id quod veritati minimè conſentaneu viſum eſt. Quis enim eſt, qui no intelligat, primi gradus Geminorú punaumediŭ, tricefimi gradus Cancri mediú penepunaŭ refpicere.Itaq; vna parte mota, paulò aliter quam Firmicus partiŭ ἀντίσκια hic deſcripfi. TABVLA ANTISCIORVM. Partium. | Scrupulorum. ---|--- 1 | 30 | 1 | 60 | 16 | 45 2 | 29 | 2 | 59 | 17 | 44 3 | 28 | 3 | 58 | 18 | 43 4 | 27 | 4 | 57 | 19 | 42 5 | 26 | 5 | 56 | 20 | 41 6 | 25 | 6 | 55 | 21 | 40 7 | 24 | 7 | 54 | 22 | 39 8 | 23 | 8 | 53 | 23 | 38 9 | 22 | 9 | 52 | 24 | 37 10 | 21 | 10 | 51 | 25 | 36 11 | 20 | 11 | 50 | 26 | 35 12 | 19 | 12 | 49 | 27 | 34 13 | 18 | 13 | 48 | 28 | 33 14 | 17 | 14 | 47 | 29 | 32 15 | 16 | 15 | 46 | 30 | 31 Hoc quoq; obferuare tenemur, bina ſigna imperantium obedientiumq;, nempe ४ cum, & ṁ cum m dici odioſe imperantia & obedientia,quòd preter hoc copulationis teſtimonium quadrata radiatióe ſe- ſe mutuò percutiant. Simili excau- ſa duæ combina- 'tiones' intuentiú, nempe 8 cum &ṁcu odiofe ſeſe contueri dicuntur, quod obferuaſſe plurimum ad πρωγνωσική confert. Hinc enim patet ſub ſignis fixis exortos generaliter hoſtiles alere inimicitias, & eas longè intolePRIMA 105 intolerabiliores, ſo ſignia illa imperantia & obedientia ſint, nam hæc dissimilitudinem morum, hoc eſt, tyrannidem imperanti, & infinitam toletantiam obedienti ingenerare conſueuerunt. Hic tamen ſicut vbique conditio hominum perpendenda eſt. Nam ſi imperans fuerit nobilior, vi & conculcationibus apertè aggredietur obedientem: ſi fortunæ humilioris, idem tepabit. non violentia, ſed fraude & dolis, qualiter principes nonnunquam à ſucatis conſultoribus deceptos ſeductoſq; accepimus, Tolerabilior auté hoſtilitas futura eſt inter illos, quos intuentia figna, quæ fimilitudinem morum ſignificant, difiunxerint. Etenim ſi ſigna illa fuerint ambo æſtiualia, vtrunque natum iniuriarum impatientem imperioque gaudentem: ſihyemalia, vtrunque diſsimulatorem facient. His ita ſe habentibus,perſpicuumeſt, loca intuentia per ſe, neque concordiæ, neque diſcor diæ vinculo coniungi, ſed ſolummodo ſimilitudinem morum veluti tolerantiam vel impatientiam æqualem decernere.Nam ſi intuitus concordiæ eſſet vinculum, odio radiationis quadratz depulſo, aut certè mitigato, mediocritatem quądam conſen ſus faceret.Similitudinem ergo morum inter duos facit ſignorum intuitus: diſcordiam verò vel concordiam, locorum conueniens vel hoſtilis figuratio. Neq; verò id prætereundum eft, intuentia & imperantiacum obedientibus eſſe potentiora, virtutemque ſuam longè latiùs diffundere iuxta ambo ſolſticia, vbi declinatio Solis admodum paulatim variatur, quàm circa zquinoctia, vbi declinatio ſubitò & celeriter augetur vel minuitur: Nam vt Plinius loquitur, poſitio ſigniferi circa media ſui obliquior eſt, iuxta ſolſticia verò re&ior. Quapropter pars 10. Cancri etſi cum parte 21. II præciſe copulata fit, tamen cum parte eiuſdem ſigni Geminorum decima, & multò magis cum reliquis intermedijs fermè conuenit. Nam talium punctorum parallelos circulos penè vnico ſaltem gradu diſtare, ex figuris antiſciorum paulò antè à nobis delineatis æſtimare licet.Econtrariò iuxta æquinoctia vbi paralleli ſingularum partium latè diſiunguntur, nifi partes partibus reſpondeant, parŭ poſſunt. Eſtautem & inconiunctorum locorum manifeſtus in iudicijs vſus; quæ cùm ab autore plane relicta ſint, idcirco eorum quoque definitiones & notas ex Ptolemæoadferemus, ne aliquod 206 DIFFERENTIA quod præceptum ad plenam artis cognitionem nancifcondar neceffarium in his inftitutionibus defideretur.. Verba Prolemei, quibus inconiuncta & aliena loca nobis expofuit, ſunt hæc:ἀσιώδετα κα) απηλλοβιωμένα καλείται τμήματα, όσα μια δένα λόγομ ἁπλῶς ἔχὶ πρὸς ἄλληλα τον καταλεγμένωμ οικφεύς αωδέτων σεωμ. ταῦτα δὲ βλερ, ὁ μήτε τν προςαπόντων ή ακγόντωμ τύγε χάνο, μήτε τον βλεπόντωμ ισοσαναρμόντωμ. ἔτι δὲ καὶ την τεσ σάρων χηματισμών, τότε κατά διάμεβου, καὶ tỹ An Tỡ * ἐξάγωνο, κατὰ τὸ παντελὲς ἀμέτοχα καταλαμβανόμενα, καὶ ἤτοι διὰ ἑνὸς ἢ διὰ ποίτε γινόμλνα δωδεκατημορίωμ. ἐπιδήπερ τα Η διἑνὸς ἀπές ραπαι ὥσπερ ἀλλήλωμ,κὶ δύο αὐτὰ ὄντα ἑνὸς α έχφ γωνίαμ. τα δε 21ὰ πιτπεἰς ἄνισα διαίρει τομ ὅλομ κύκλου τη ἄλλωμχηματισμῶν εἰς ἴσα πω τποριμέβα χαίρεσιμποιγμένωμ, hoc eſt: Ea verò loca, quæ nullam plane enumeratarum ſocietatum habent rationem, inconiuncta & aliena nominantur. Ipſa verò funt, quæ neque imperant neque obediunt, neque ſe intuentur, neque idem valent, neque vllam quatuor figurarum, quas ſupră recenfuimus,id'eſt, non diametrum, non trigonum, non quandrangulu, non hexangulum deſcribunt,ſed quævel ſecunda vel ſexta numerantur. Nam duo ſigna continua auerfa ſuntà ſeſe, & mutuo contactu vnum potius augulum, quam vilam figuram efficiunt. Illa verd quæ ſexta numerantur, totum circulum in partes inæquales ſecant, cùm aliæ figuræ cum in par tes æquales diuidant. Hæc Ptolemæus, que vt recte intelligas, in promptu habere debes, nouem modos hactenus à nobis per capita expofitos, quibus ſigniferi partes inter ſeſe colligantur. Primo enim cohærent ea loca imperio, hoceſt, cùm in duabus lineis æquinoctio parallelis extiterint. Secundoκατὰ τὰ ἰσου διωαμίαμ, hoc eft, cùm vna eandemq; lineam æquinoctio paral Jelā terminauerint. Tertiò cùm per diametrű diſiūta fuerint. Quarto ti ex triangulo ſeſe complectantur. Quinto ſi quadratam figuram faciant. Sexto denique ſi hexagono conſocientur: quorum modorum tres poſteriorcs cùm numerum duplicent, vt ſuprà capite de aſpe&ibus oſtenſum eſt, efficiuntur nouem vincula, quibus vnusquifq; ſigniferi locus copulatur cum locis alijs, qualia in toto ſigniferi duodecim reperiútur. Quod cùm ita PRIMA 107 Italie, duo fattein reſtabunt loca dodira, hoc eft, ab omnico pula exclufa.Aries, exempli gratia,cùm imperet X,& intueatur up, & denique copuletur radiatione diametra, quadrata 20 d Motriquetra &,hexagona Geminis & Aquario, duo ſale tem facit reliqua ligna ασύδετα, id eft, inconiuncta, nempe ४. '& quibus nullo plane vinculo cohæret:Gmiliter de alijs omibus fignis mobilibus & communibus iudicandum eſt. Nam figna fixa cum ſaltem octo alijs ſignis copulari queant, à qua euoraliis planè deferuntur. Id verò ideo accidit, quòd bina ſigna geminato vinculo ſibi firmius connectentes duabus copulis priuentur. Veluti Taurus cum, & Aquarius cum geminato vinculo, nempe intuitu & quadrangulo cohærent. Similiter 8 cum, & cum i preter quadratam radiationem impesiali poteſtate connectuntur.Hac de cauſa illa magno cum odio ſeſe contientia, & hæc non fine indignatione præcipientia & obediétia nominari paulò antè dicebamus. Illa ergo ſigna, que muto plane vinculo, qualia nouem iam expofuimus, ſignis alijs coniunguntur,nominat Ptolemæus ἀστιίδεται καὶ ἀπαλλοβιω μένα, id eft, inconiun&a & aliena, qualia funt, quæ vel ſecunda vel ſexta numerantur, Nam ſecunda ad diuerſas partes tortuo- ſe diſiuntazatque vnum ſaltem angulum conſicientia, nullam figuram concludunt,neque ἐπ' ευθείας ficut in diametro poſicapr
translation: The antiscia of the zodiacal places are arranged in the manner we have described. For ease of use and to resolve all obscurity, we will now describe a table of parts and minutes, from which both commanding and obeying signs, as well as parts of signs and minutes of parts that mutually behold each other, can be easily derived. For example, if Saturn is in the 20th part, 10th minute of Aries, and its obedient and beholding or equivalent place is to be found: first, from the previous descriptions of the figures, it is clear that the sign of Aries commands the sign of Pisces, and the same Aries beholds Virgo. Now, to recognize the corresponding parts to the parts in which Saturn resides, first enter with part 20 into the following table of parts, and opposite part 20 you will find part 11 written. Then also, send the 10 minutes into the table of minutes, and 51 minutes will appear. Therefore, you will declare that in part 20, minute 10 of Aries, it commands part 11, minute 11 of Pisces, and beholds part 11, minute 1 of Virgo. For once the part of one is known, the part of the other will never be unknown, which is most worthy of observation. Julius Firmicus and the author are of the opinion that no part projects antiscia into the 30th part of the dodecatemory, which did not seem to be consistent with the truth. For who does not understand that the middle point of the first degree of Gemini almost beholds the middle point of the thirtieth degree of Cancer? Therefore, with one part moved, I have described the antiscia of the parts here a little differently than Firmicus. TABLE OF ANTISCIA. Parts. | Minutes. ---|--- 1 | 30 | 1 | 60 | 16 | 45 2 | 29 | 2 | 59 | 17 | 44 3 | 28 | 3 | 58 | 18 | 43 4 | 27 | 4 | 57 | 19 | 42 5 | 26 | 5 | 56 | 20 | 41 6 | 25 | 6 | 55 | 21 | 40 7 | 24 | 7 | 54 | 22 | 39 8 | 23 | 8 | 53 | 23 | 38 9 | 22 | 9 | 52 | 24 | 37 10 | 21 | 10 | 51 | 25 | 36 11 | 20 | 11 | 50 | 26 | 35 12 | 19 | 12 | 49 | 27 | 34 13 | 18 | 13 | 48 | 28 | 33 14 | 17 | 14 | 47 | 29 | 32 15 | 16 | 15 | 46 | 30 | 31 We must also observe that two commanding and obeying signs, namely Taurus with Scorpio, and Scorpio with Taurus, are said to command and obey hatefully, because besides this testimony of conjunction, they strike each other with a square radiation. For a similar reason, two combinations of beholding signs, namely Taurus with Leo and Scorpio with Aquarius, are said to behold each other hatefully, which observation contributes greatly to prognostication. From this, it is clear that those born under fixed signs generally harbor hostile enmities, and these are far more intolerable if those signs are commanding and obeying, for these are accustomed to generate dissimilarity of manners, that is, tyranny in the commander and infinite tolerance in the obedient. Here, however, as everywhere, the condition of men must be considered. For if the commander is nobler, he will openly attack the obedient with force and oppression; if of humbler fortune, he will attempt the same, not with violence, but with fraud and deceit, as we have sometimes heard of princes being deceived and seduced by their counselors. However, a more tolerable hostility will exist between those whom beholding signs, which signify similarity of manners, have disjoined. For if those signs are both summer signs, both will be born impatient of injuries and delighting in command; if winter signs, both will be dissimulators. These things being so, it is clear that beholding places by themselves are joined neither by a bond of concord nor discord, but only determine a similarity of manners, such as equal tolerance or impatience. For if intuition were a bond of concord, with the hatred of square radiation repelled or certainly mitigated, it would create a certain mediocrity of consent. Therefore, the intuition of signs creates a similarity of manners between two; but discord or concord, a suitable or hostile figuration of places. Nor should it be overlooked that beholding and commanding signs with obeying signs are more powerful, and diffuse their virtue much more widely near both solstices, where the Sun's declination varies very gradually, than around the equinoxes, where the declination suddenly and rapidly increases or decreases. For, as Pliny says, the position of the zodiac near its middle is more oblique, but near the solstices it is more direct. Therefore, the 10th part of Cancer, although precisely conjoined with the 21st part of Gemini, nevertheless almost agrees with the tenth part of the same sign of Gemini, and much more with the remaining intermediate parts. For it can be estimated from the figures of the antiscia delineated by us a little earlier that the parallel circles of such points differ by almost only one degree. On the contrary, near the equinoxes, where the parallels of individual parts are widely disjoined, they can do little unless parts correspond to parts. There is also a manifest use of inconjunct places in judgments; since these are plainly left by the author, we will therefore also adduce their definitions and notes from Ptolemy, lest any precept necessary for a full knowledge of the art be lacking in these instructions. The words of Ptolemy, by which he explained inconjunct and alien places to us, are these: "Unconnected and alienated sections are called those which have no simple relation to each other of the enumerated associations. These are those which neither command nor obey, nor behold each other, nor are of equal power, nor describe any of the four figures we have enumerated above, that is, neither diameter, nor trine, nor square, nor sextile, but those which are numbered second or sixth. For two continuous signs are averted from each other, and by mutual contact form one angle rather than any figure. But those which are numbered sixth divide the whole circle into unequal parts, while other figures divide it into equal parts." These are Ptolemy's words, which, to understand correctly, you must have at hand the nine ways we have hitherto explained chapter by chapter, by which the parts of the zodiac are connected to each other. For first, those places cohere by command, that is, when they exist in two lines parallel to the equinox. Secondly, by equal power, that is, when one and the same line parallel to the equinox terminates them. Thirdly, when they are disjoined by diameter. Fourthly, if they embrace each other by trine. Fifthly, if they form a square figure. Sixthly, finally, if they are associated by sextile: of which ways, the three latter, since they double the number, as shown above in the chapter on aspects, make nine bonds by which each place of the zodiac is coupled with other places, of which twelve are found in the whole zodiac. When this is so, two places will remain unconnected, that is, excluded from all coupling. Aries, for example, since it commands Pisces, and beholds Libra, and finally is coupled by diametrical, square, and trine radiation, and sextile to Gemini and Aquarius, it leaves at least two other signs unconnected, namely Taurus and Virgo, to which it coheres by no bond at all. Similarly, the same must be judged for all other movable and common signs. For fixed signs, although they can be coupled with at least eight other signs, are plainly deserted by the others. This happens because two signs, connecting themselves more firmly by a double bond, are deprived of two couplings. For example, Taurus with Scorpio, and Aquarius with Leo, cohere by a double bond, namely intuition and square. Similarly, Taurus with Leo, and Scorpio with Aquarius, are connected by imperial power besides square radiation. For this reason, we said a little earlier that the former are called mutually beholding with great hatred, and the latter commanding and obeying not without indignation. Therefore, those signs which are joined to other signs by no plain bond, such as the nine we have already explained, Ptolemy calls unconnected and alien, such as those which are numbered second or sixth. For the second are tortuously disjoined to different parts, and forming at least one angle, they enclose no figure, nor are they in a straight line as in a diameter.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Luminaries and Friendship
text: 15 dini, propter quadratam radiatiõem maximu ingenerant odin Si fortè & hoc teſtimonio familiaritatis deſtituamur, viderede bemus,an Sol cũ Luna loca ſua comutauerint, hoc eſt, an locus Solis in genitura Pauli, ſit locus Lunæ in genitura, Petri:& co trario locus Solis Petri fit locus Lunæ Pauli:id em ad beneuolé sizingenerandu vnice proficit. Veluti ſi paſcéte Paulo Sol per currat ♐, Lunam ♏, & naſcéte Petro Sol decurrat perm, Luna per ♐,inter Paulu & Petru ppter comutata luminarium figna arctissima familiaritaté futura iudicabimus, & quidé mirum in modú ſtabilé propter ſigna luminariú obedientia, quæ vtapho riſmus ifte fonat beneuolentia adaugent, &conuenientia quan dam morum ingenerant. Vtrunq; enim ſignú Piſciu & Scorpij eft obediens. Quòd ſi talis locorú Solis, vel locorú Lunæ aſpeAus, vel luminarium commutatio, fieret ex ♎ & ♏, fimiliter propter trigonam radiationem ſignificaretur, ſed tamen nő ita æqualis beneuolentia. Nam amor illius cuius ſignificatores Cancrum ſignú imperans occupant, cum imperio & faſtu quadam coniungitur:alterius verò ♏ fignificatores in ♏ſigneobedienti deprehenduntur cum ſubiectione & famulatu.Adeudem modum ſigna horoſcoporum ſi in vtraque genitura fuerint vel eadem, vel triangularia, vel ſexangularia, vel denique ſi loca commutauerint, amicitiam ſignificabunt. Sed cùm finguli naſcentes fingula ſigna afcendentia habeant, quomodo horofcoporum commutatio fieri poterit? Refpondeo, in tali com paratione aſſumendam eſſe partem fortunarum. Nam ficut af Ptol.lib.z. cendens Solis, ita fors lunaris eſt horoſcopus. Sicut autem amicitia ex dictorum locorum συμφονία, ita econtrariò odium ex eorundem locorum ἀσυμφονία dijudicatur. Nam ſi quadrata aut oppoſita, aut ασύνδετα ſeu inconiuncta ſigna abſque vlla commutatione luminarium poſſederit, hoftilitates, odia & fimultates denunciabunt. Ad vſum antiſciorum ſeptuagefimum octauum enunciatum etiam referunt: πολλάκις ἐνοργεῖ ὁ ἀς προ να τόπωυκ ἔχε εἰ οργφαμ, καρδθ ἀπροσδόκητη προσφορωμ. τους Hννωμοίῳ. hoc eft, exercet fæpè vires ſtella, quo in loco nullas. habet vires, lucrum inexpectatum nato adferens. Exempli gratia, ſi culminet pars 7 ♏, & 4 fit in parte 24 ♉. Quod fi ♉ antiſcium hoc Iouis hoftiliter feriat, adminiſtrationem magiſtratus interturbabit.Econtrà verò Ve nus Ioui auxiliabitur, fælicemq; adminiſtrationem efficiet, & antiſcio illi ex beneuolis locis fuffragium attulerit. Quapro pter ſi quando ſtellæ ſe non viderint, perpendendum eft, sa fibi per antiſcium ſociata radiatione iungantur. Quum enim antiſcium fic emiferit, vt per id trigona, quadrangula vel he xagona radiatione iungantur, fic ſtellæ ipfe decernunt, ac i fint ſimplici ordinatione conſtitutz. Quæ omnia ſi diligenter attendes, in fatis hominum explicans plurimum iuuaberis. DE ESSE CIRCVLI ACcidentali. Sed quia auxiliante Deo iam protulimus eſſe circuli Signorum eſſentiale, nunc proferamus accidentale.Nam circulus figuratur in omni omni hora hora tali tali figura, figura, quæ que diuidi. d tur in quatuor partes, quas diuidit circulus hemispha rý & circulus meridiei, id eſt, circulus medij cæli, qui facit medium diem. Et vnaquæque pars iſtarum partiu diuiditur intres partes inæquales, ſerundum aſcenſio. nes ſigni aſcendentis. Atque hoc modo diuiditur circu lus in duodecim partes, quæ vocantur domus:Nominan tur quoque & cuſpides & turres, cuius opus expofitum eſt in libro Ezibi, id eſt, in libro curſus ſiderum. Princi pium quoque diuifionis eſt horoscopus:hoc eſt aſcendens, cuius initium eſt ſuper circulum hemispherý orientalis, deinde ſecunda domus ſequitur & tertia, caterę, do mus vſque ad duodecimam. translation: 15. If they generate maximum hatred due to a square radiation. If by chance we are deprived of this testimony of familiarity, we should see whether the Sun and Moon have exchanged their places, that is, whether the place of the Sun in Paul's nativity is the place of the Moon in Peter's nativity, and conversely, whether the place of the Sun in Peter's nativity is the place of the Moon in Paul's. This uniquely promotes benevolence. For example, if when Paul was born, the Sun passed through Sagittarius and the Moon through Scorpio, and when Peter was born, the Sun passed through Scorpio and the Moon through Sagittarius, we would judge that there would be a very close familiarity between Paul and Peter due to the exchanged signs of the luminaries, and indeed, a wonderfully stable one due to the obedience of the luminary signs, which, as this aphorism states, increase benevolence and generate a certain conformity of manners. For both the sign of Pisces and Scorpio are obedient. But if such an aspect of the Sun's places, or the Moon's places, or an exchange of luminaries, were to occur from Libra and Scorpio, it would similarly be signified by a trine radiation, but not with such equal benevolence. For the love of him whose significators occupy Cancer, an imperious sign, is joined with a certain command and pride; but the significators of the other, Scorpio, are found in the obedient sign of Scorpio with subjection and servitude. In the same way, if the signs of the horoscopes in both nativities are either the same, or trine, or sextile, or finally if they have exchanged places, they will signify friendship. But since each native has a single ascending sign, how can an exchange of horoscopes occur? I answer that in such a comparison, the Part of Fortune must be assumed. For just as the ascendant is the Sun's, so the lunar lot is the horoscope. But just as friendship is judged from the συμφονία (harmony) of the said places, so conversely, hatred is judged from the ἀσυμφονία (disharmony) of the same places. For if square or opposite, or ἀσύνδετα (unconnected) or unconjoined signs are possessed without any exchange of luminaries, they will announce hostilities, hatreds, and enmities. They also refer the seventy-eighth statement to the use of antiscia: πολλάκις ἐνοργεῖ ὁ ἀς προ να τόπωυκ ἔχε εἰ οργφαμ, καρδθ ἀπροσδόκητη προσφορωμ. τους Hννωμοίῳ. That is, a star often exercises powers where it has no powers, bringing unexpected gain to the native. For example, if the 7th degree of Scorpio culminates, and Jupiter is in the 24th degree of Taurus. Here Jupiter, although it does not aspect Scorpio, nevertheless helps the native to attain magistracies through the hidden protection of the antiscium. For the 7th degree of Scorpio and the 24th degree of Taurus are of the same potency. But if Taurus hostilely strikes this antiscium of Jupiter, it will disturb the administration of the magistracy. Conversely, Venus will help Jupiter, and will bring about a successful administration, and will bring support to that antiscium from benevolent places. Therefore, if ever the stars do not see each other, it must be considered whether they are joined to each other by associated radiation through the antiscium. For when the antiscium has emitted in such a way that they are joined by trine, square, or sextile radiation through it, the stars themselves thus determine, as if they were constituted in a simple arrangement. If you diligently attend to all these things, you will be greatly helped in explaining the fates of men.

translation: CONCERNING THE ACCIDENTAL BEING OF THE CIRCLE. But since, with God's help, we have already set forth the essential being of the circle of Signs, let us now set forth the accidental. For the circle is figured at every hour with such a figure, which is divided into four parts, which are divided by the circle of the hemisphere and the circle of the meridian, that is, the circle of the midheaven, which makes midday. And each of these parts is divided into three unequal parts, according to the ascensions of the ascending sign. And in this way the circle is divided into twelve parts, which are called houses: they are also called cusps and towers, whose work is expounded in the book of Ezibi, that is, in the book of the course of the stars. The beginning of the division is also the horoscope: that is, the ascendant, whose beginning is above the circle of the eastern hemisphere, then the second house follows and the third, and the other houses up to the twelfth.

PRIMA. Ity quatuor partes æquales dirimit, vnde propter eius multiplicem vſum ſolus quaſi proprio nomine circulus verticalis nuncupatur. Ergo quoties nomea circuli verticalis abſolutum occurrerit, intelligas circulum verticalem, qui per ortum occaſumque & per verticem loci ducitur. Circulos verò altitudinum huiufmodi deſcriptione accipe:Cuiuſuis loci vertex diſtat à ſuo finiente P.90. Si ergo per fingularum partium difcriminaintelligantur ducta plana finienti æque diſtantia, conficientur circuli altitudinum, quorum primus omniumq; maximus eſt ὁρίζωμ, id eſt, finiens. Reliquorum verò quiſque tanto minor exiſtit, quanto vertici fuerit vicinior. Itaque omnes fimul ducati, meridianireliquorumq; verticalium quadrantes extantes in P.90. ſecantes, & ab ijſdem vicissim in P.360. ſecti, quaſi hemifphzFicum rete intexunt. Plerique peregrina voce hos circulos vocant almicantarach. Horum circulorum officium eſt, Solis aliarumqueſtellarum veram ab ortu aut occaſu diſtantiam & altitudinem in circulis verticalibus oftendere. Eſt enim altitudo ſtellæ vel cuiuſuis punti in cœlo notati arcus verticalis circuli percentrum ſideris ducti inter horizonta & ipſum aftrum cadés. Stella altitudinem meridianam ſortitur, cùm meridianum circulum attigerit:cùm verò à meridiano abfuerit, orientalem antemeridianam:ſi eundem præterierit, occidentalem pomeridianam altitudinem habere dicitur. Circulus poſitionis eſt, qui per centrum dati ſideris & per meridiani & horizontis commif Juras ducitur.Hicnonnunquam ſtellæ horizon ſeu finiens appellatur, & in progreſsionibus locorum Apheticorum non pe nitendum vfum habet. His ita præcognitis, ad deſcriptionem horoſcopi tranſeamus. Dicitur autem ὡροσκόπος proprièis, qui rationem horarum obferuat.Sed hic nominamus horoſcopum fidus natalicium, ſeu potiùs partem Zodiaci quæ conuerfione cali oritur co momento quo quis naſcitur, autalia quæpiam res capit principium.Sumitur nonnunquam vt hic, pro pictura cœli, quam græci σῆμα appellant, hoc eſt, capitur pro toto cœlo in forma plana figurato atq; diſtributo in partes ſeu loca duodecim, quæ varias appellationes acceperunt ab Aftrologis.Nomināt enim domicilia, habitacula, turres, partes, hofpitia, manGones, & vfitatè duodecim domos in figura conuerfione cæli. P2 1 Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 3== ==Start of OCR for page 4== 16 DIFFERENTIA Quamuis autem non vna ſed plures diuerſeque legantur apud aſtrologos rationes, quibus vniuerfum cælum in 12.partes variè diuiditur: Tamen omnes conueniunt in hoc, quòd imagi nantur ſex circulos magnos, in duobus locis oppofitis ſeſe ſecantes, quorum communis diameter ab auſtro verſus aquilo nem tendit, totum cælum mundique vniuerfam machinam in 12.partes diftinguere, ita vt quicquid in mundo eſt, id omnino in aliquaiſtarum 12. partium, quas domos nominamus, comprehendatur.Sed extrema diametri,in quibus di&i ſex circuli ſe mutuo ſecant, alia ab alijs conſtituta eſſe reperio. Nam autor vt apparet, & qui ante Campanum de domicilijs cæleftibus par tiendis præceperunt, putaueruntiſte duo puncta eſſe vertices mundi, in quibus cælum quotidie ſpatio 24.horarum inenarrabili celeritate reuolutum potifsima cauſa dictorum domici liorum exiſtit.Imaginentur maioris euidentię gratia duo circu li magni, quorum vnus fit meridianus, alter verò per punctum eclipticæ fupra finientem ſcandens, eique oppoſitum duâus, ita vt mutua ſectione in polis mundi, cælum in quatuor portiones dirimant. Poſthec, portionum equinoctialis fingulz,di Ais duobus magnis circulis intercepta trifariam æqualiterós ſecandæ funt,atque per pun&a fectionum & polos mundi ducendi circuli quatuor, qui cum prioribus duobus duodecim c li domicilia autoris conficiút. In hac ratione aquandi circulus finiens tantum in ſphæra recta, nunquam verò in ſphæra obliqua dictis ſex circulis annumeratur:quod & Abraam Auenezra annotauit. Preterea & hoc, vt autor admonet, efficitur, cofdem ſex circulos Eclipticam ſemper in duodecim partes inequa Ies dirimere, & non nifi oppoſita domicilia æqualia conficere, imò hoc modo ecliptica non nifi certa hora dici, nimirum cum principia Arietis & Libræ in horizonta procumbunt, in qua tuor quadrantes æquales fecernitur. Alij rurfus hec ſetionum puncta, quæ fex circulis communia funt, iudicauerunt effe meridiani & horizontis commiſſuras. Sed ne hic quidem omnes inter ſe conſentiunt. translation: From the division of the zodiac, which is perpetual and unvarying, we now pass to its accidental division. Hitherto, we have distinguished the zodiac, or rather the entire machinery of the world, into four quadrants perpetually congruent with the times of the year, into twelve regions of the signs, into ascending and descending halves, hot and cold, and many such stable parts, divided by circles or rather by hemicycles drawn through the pole of the zodiac. And finally, we have shown the familiarity and agreement of the zodiac's quadrants and signs with other things. Now, however, we must similarly distinguish the same machinery of the world into halves, quadrants, and twelve regions, but differently, that is, into inconstant parts, and continually variable due to the first motion, by the office of the meridional circle and the horizon and the hemicycles ending in the intersections of these circles, and dividing not the zodiac, but rather the equinoctial equally. And similarly, we must explain the familiarities or agreements of the quadrants and those twelve unstable stations of the heavens with the things subject to them. And this distinction agrees with Ptolemy's doctrine. For he also, having explained the natural texture of the times of the year, adds the nature of the accidental parts of the heavens and times according to the same example and manner: "Similarly," he says, "the four places and angles of the horizon, one having more of the dry towards the east," etc. These things being so, for the sake of learning and aiding memory, this part will be conveniently divided into two members, the first of which is where the author explains the accidental division of the zodiac, which continually varies with the diurnal revolution of the heavens; the second, where he enumerates the familiarities or significations of the quadrants and the twelve places of the heavens, which accompany this accidental, as he calls it, and mutable division of the zodiac. But the author very briefly touches upon the first member, which ought to contain the method of distributing the heavens, and refers the whole matter of constructing the celestial domiciles to the canons of the tables. Therefore, I think I shall do a worthwhile deed if I describe more fully in this place all the variety of this distribution for young students, and show the complete method of forming themes or pictures of the houses, and the way of extracting the places of the planets from the tables of ephemerides and motions. But before all else, certain circles omitted by the author of the Sphere must be described, the knowledge of which is not only necessary for the distinction of the celestial domiciles, but also for understanding the method of directing, as they call it. Vertical circles are those which, drawn through the vertex of the place and through each part of the horizon, divide it into 360 parts, and are called by some circles of rectitude, because they show in what region of the world any star rises or sets, and how far it is at any time from the east or west, from the south or north. Most people call them azimuth circles by an exotic name. It must be observed here that the meridian also belongs to the number of vertical circles, whose use is greatest in astrology above all others; but next to it in dignity among the vertical circles is that which, intersecting the meridian at right angles and drawn through the points of rising and setting, divides the horizon into four equal parts, whence, because of its manifold use, it is almost uniquely called the vertical circle by its proper name. Therefore, whenever the name of the vertical circle occurs absolutely, understand the vertical circle which is drawn through the rising and setting and through the vertex of the place. But understand the circles of altitude by this description: The vertex of any place is distant from its horizon by 90 degrees. If, therefore, planes equally distant from the horizon are understood to be drawn through the divisions of each part, circles of altitude will be formed, the first and greatest of all being the horizon itself. Each of the others, however, is smaller the closer it is to the vertex. Thus, all drawn together, intersecting the quadrants of the meridian and the other vertical circles at 90 degrees, and being intersected by them in turn at 360 degrees, they weave a kind of hemispherical net. Most people call these circles almicantarath by a foreign name. The office of these circles is to show the true distance and altitude of the Sun and other stars from the rising or setting in the vertical circles. For the altitude of a star or of any point noted in the sky is the arc of the vertical circle drawn through the center of the star, falling between the horizon and the star itself. A star obtains its meridian altitude when it reaches the meridian circle; but when it is absent from the meridian, it is said to have an eastern antemeridian altitude; if it has passed it, a western postmeridian altitude. The circle of position is that which is drawn through the center of a given star and through the intersections of the meridian and the horizon. This is sometimes called the horizon or boundary of the star, and has no small use in the progressions of Aphetic places. These things being known beforehand, let us proceed to the description of the horoscope. The horoscope is properly said to be that which observes the reason of the hours. But here we call the horoscope the natal star, or rather that part of the Zodiac which rises by the revolution of the heavens at the moment when someone is born, or when any other thing begins. It is sometimes taken, as here, for the picture of the heavens, which the Greeks call schema, that is, it is taken for the whole heaven figured in a flat form and distributed into twelve parts or places, which have received various appellations from astrologers. For they call them domiciles, habitations, towers, parts, hospices, mansions, and commonly twelve houses in the figure of the revolution of the heavens. Although not one but many and diverse methods are read among astrologers by which the entire heaven is variously divided into 12 parts, nevertheless all agree in this, that they imagine six great circles intersecting each other in two opposite places, whose common diameter extends from south to north, distinguishing the entire heaven and the whole machinery of the world into 12 parts, so that whatever is in the world is entirely comprehended in one of these 12 parts, which we call houses. But the extremities of the diameter, in which the said six circles intersect each other, I find to be constituted differently by different authors. For the author, as it appears, and those who preceded Campanus in teaching about dividing celestial domiciles, thought that these two points were the vertices of the world, in which the heaven, revolved daily with ineffable speed in the space of 24 hours, is the most potent cause of the said domiciles. For greater clarity, imagine two great circles, one of which is the meridian, and the other drawn through the point of the ecliptic ascending above the horizon and its opposite, so that by their mutual intersection at the poles of the world, they divide the heaven into four portions. Thereafter, each of the portions of the equinoctial, intercepted by the said two great circles, must be equally divided into three parts, and through the points of section and the poles of the world, four circles must be drawn, which, with the previous two, constitute the twelve domiciles of the heaven according to the author. In this method of equalizing, the horizon circle is counted among the said six circles only in a right sphere, never in an oblique sphere: which Abraham Avenezra also noted. Moreover, as the author warns, this also results in the same six circles always dividing the ecliptic into twelve unequal parts, and only making opposite domiciles equal; indeed, in this way the ecliptic is separated into four equal quadrants only at a certain hour of the day, namely when the beginnings of Aries and Libra lie on the horizon. Others again judged these points of section, which are common to the six circles, to be the intersections of the meridian and the horizon. But not even here do all agree among themselves. For Campanus and Gazalus distinguish the domiciles.

translation: In this manner, the quadrants of the vertical circle passing through the ascendant and descendant, intercepted between the horizon and the meridian, are divided into three equal portions. Then, through the points of the sections and through the common sections of the meridian and horizon, four circles of positions are imagined to be drawn, which, with the ascendant and meridian, divide the entire sky into twelve equal spaces, and this is according to the ratio of the parts of the vertical circle, and not according to the parts of the zodiac or the equator. Horontius, otherwise a distinguished artisan, defends this method of equalizing houses and thinks it should be retained. On the contrary, Regiomontanus, in Problem 14 of his tables, rejects it as alien to the mind of philosophers and constructs another fabric of celestial domiciles differing from the two preceding methods. First, he divides not the vertical circle, but the equinoctial itself into four equal parts by the meridian and the oblique ascendant, each of which quadrants he again cuts into three equal parts, and through the points of the sections he draws four circles of positions, which, meeting at the sections of the horizon and meridian, divide the entire sky into twelve houses of the sky. This method of Regiomontanus is, as it were, intermediate between the first and second. For in that he divides the equinoctial and not the vertical circle into twelve equal parts, he agrees with the first method and contradicts Campanus, but inasmuch as six circles meet and intersect not in the poles of the world, but in the common sections of the ascendant and meridian, to that extent he dissents from the first method and agrees with Campanus's reasoning. Finally, here the spaces of the sky are unequal in every oblique horizon, as in the first, but each house retains its invariable magnitude, as in the second. Therefore, he not unreasonably calls this method of equalization rational, because it rationally avoids all the inconveniences that could be objected to the extremes. Although this method of the most learned Regiomontanus is more erudite than all the others and simply more consistent with the truth, and finally approaches most closely to Ptolemy's opinion, there were nevertheless those who did not approve of it much, such as Johannes Schonerus and the other distinguished artisans of our age, who prefer to this and all other methods another, and in our order, fourth fabric of equal domiciles, as they call them, which is to be imagined in this way. First, the zodiac is separated by the ascendant and a great circle passing through both poles of the zodiac and the vertex of the place into four quadrants. Afterwards, each quadrant of the ecliptic is cut into three equal parts: which done, if you understand five circles to be drawn through the points of such sections and the poles of the zodiac, those very circles, with the one that passed through the vertex, will divide the entire zodiac and the whole world into twelve equal parts or houses, to which all astrologers are said by many to have accommodated their judgments. That this is true cannot be denied, if it is understood of Firmicus, Albohali, Albumasar, Albubater, Bonatus, and similar authors. But that the supreme Ptolemy, the prince of all astrologers, nay, among so many writers on celestial matters, the only astrologer, in his quadripartite construction approved this equal equalization of houses, it is certain that this is done with insufficiently firm conjectures, the principal of which I will recite here. And first, to begin with those things that are thought to be most firm, the figure of equal sides, which they think can happen nowhere else than in the equalization of houses, which is done by equal degrees, assigning 30 degrees of the zodiac to each house, scarcely supports their opinion. But this is not sagaciously enough, nor consistent with Ptolemy's opinion. For the very words of Ptolemy, which are extant in the third apotelesmatic, chapter on those who are not nourished, openly speak that the figure is to be understood otherwise. He says, "If one luminary is in an angle, and one of the malefics either conjoins or opposes it, and this partially and according to an isosceles figure, no benefic being configured, and the ruler of the luminaries being detained in the places of the malefics: what is born cannot be nourished, and will soon die." In these words, by an isosceles figure, he understands an exact conjunction of bodies or rays, both according to longitude and latitude, so that in conjunction the latitude is the same, but in opposition of the Sun, the malefic is below the Ecliptic, but in opposition of the Moon, the malefic has an equal latitude, but opposite. For example, let Saturn be in the 12th part, with a northern latitude of 3 parts, and the Moon opposite Saturn in the 12th part, with a southern latitude of 3 parts. It is now manifest that Saturn and the Moon with their places in the ecliptic make two isosceles and equal triangles, in which one side of the isosceles is the fourth part of the ecliptic, the other the fourth part of the great circle passing through the centers of the Moon and the malefic and the poles of the zodiac. Similarly, when the latitude is equal on the same side, we imagine an isosceles figure in the quadrants of the ecliptic and the circle of latitude because the arcs of the said circles drawn from their common intersection, and cut off by the circle passing through the poles of the zodiac and the point of conjunction, are equal to each other: which the annexed diagram clearly shows. Moreover, Schonerus wrote that an isosceles figure is formed if the Sun is in the ascendant, the Moon in the fourth house, the infortune in the seventh, striking the Sun by diameter, and the Moon by square. But Ptolemy did not speak of an equilateral triangle, that is, of a figure of three equal sides, such as is depicted in this configuration, but of an isosceles figure, of which only two sides are equal, such as we showed a little earlier. Moreover, Ptolemy in this place made no mention of the square configuration, which is added here by Schonerus. Finally, the following words in the same place clearly state that Ptolemy understood by an isosceles figure only an exact conjunction of opposite bodies or rays: "But if this position does not form a triangle of two equal sides, that is, if the malefics do not harm the luminaries by the same longitude and latitude, but only apply their rays to the places of the luminaries from nearby, etc." From these it can be clearly seen that the equal distribution of houses in the zodiac is by no means confirmed by an isosceles figure. It remains to be seen.

text: ==Start of OCR for page 4== IMA. 131 vealterius loci, ex quo idem autoritate Ptolemei defenditur, ſententiam proferamus. Extat autem hic locus lib.3.cap.delocis Apheticis, cuius verba hæc funt : πρώτομμὲν τόπος ἁγκτέομ ἀφετικός, ει δις εἶναι δεῖ πάντως τόμ μέλλοντα τιώκυρίαμ τ' ἀφές στως λαμβάνομ, τό, τε πολί γόμ ωροσκόπομ δωδεκατημόριομ ἀλό πάτε μερῶν την προαναφόρομοίωμ αὐτε ὡρίζοντο μεχεί την λοιπῶν καὶ ἐπαναφόρομοίωμ εἴκοσι πάτε καὶ τὸς ταύταις λ' ταῖς μοίραις δεξίας εξαγώνες, τι, τε ἀγαθοποις δαίμονο κὴ τε τραγώνος το ὑπὸὶ γῆμ μεσορανημάτο, καὶ τριγώνος τε καλομές να θεό, καὶ διαμέβος το διύοντο. hoceſt, Primum fanè exiſtimandum eft, eſſe Aphetica ſeu prorogantia loca, in quibus om nind neceſse eſt reperiri eum, qui velit vitæ gubernanda iurif ditione potiri, vt circa horoſcopum à quinis partibus prius ſu pra finitorem emergentibus vſque ad 25. reliquas ſuccedentes. Et qui has tricenas partes dextris hexagonis radijs contin git,qui boni dæmonislocus dicitur,& in ordine vndecimus exiſtit. Et qui tetragonis nimirum cæli ſupra terram faftigiű, & qui trigonis, qui Deus appellatur, & qui oppofitis, qui in oc cafum prouoluitur. Hactenus Ptolemeus, ex cuius verbis re- &è colligitur, quòd quinque gradus præcedentes cuſpidem & 25.mox ſequentes teneant virtutem talis domus:ſed quod additur de ſignifero, nempe, quòd quælibet domus contineat qualiter 30.partes ſigniferi, non procedit. Nam Ptolemeus hic pullam facit mentionem figniferi:neque de eius, ſed equinoAialis partibus loquitur, ita vt quęlibet domus partibus 30. quino&ialibus finiri intelligatur. Et hoc eft cauſa, cur ſtatim fubiungat, quòd vndecima domus ſexangulo, nona trigono, medium cæli quadrato aſpectu horoſcopum ſemper videant, quod eſſet abfurdum, ſi de æquali domiciliorum in ecliptica diftributione hic textus Ptolemei intelligatur.Nam in plerifq; locis aſcendente ſigno II, medium cœli occupat, atque ita horoſcopum non ex quadrangulo, ſed ex trigono reſpectu eclipticę refpicit.Econtrarid aſcendente, in nonam domum incidens,horofcopum non ex trigono, ſed quadrangulo videbit. Quapropter de ſententia Ptolemşi rectè docet Cardanus, duo genera aſpetuum eſſe intelligéda, vnum in zodiaco, qui ad vnguem in ecliptica fumi debent ex fimili parte & fcrupulo Digitized by Google 1 ==End of OCR for page 4== ==Start of OCR for page 5== DIFFERENTIA, pulo in partem & fcrupula confimilia, vt fupra dictum eft. After terum, quem hic intelligit Ptolemæus, in equinoctiali,in quo ficut in fignifero duodecim ſignorum configurationes imagi nandę funt. Cum hac fententia multi obſcuri eius loci congruunt, veluti quando dicit, quòd trigonus maleficarum è ognis breuium afcenfionum affli&us quoque perimat, hoc eft, quan. do per extremitates figuræ triangularis in eclipticam impacta circuli poſitionis duai, tantum interuallum ex zquinoctiali claudunt, quod in eodem æquino&iali formam aſpectus qua drati referat:tunc talis aſpectus triangularis maleficarum vita datori occurrens, natű perimit. Similiter etiam hæc verba, quz priorem locum antecedunt: ἐνίοτε δε καὶ ἀπὸ τὴν πολυχρονιές τωμ δωδεκατημορίωμκακοθεὶς ὁ ἐξάγωνο αναιρεί, de duplici radiatione atque de tranſformatione hexagoni afpe&usine cliptica in quadratum aſpectum in zquino&iali intelligenda funt, aliasenim nec trigonus breuium afcenfionum, nec hexagonus afcenfionum longarum interficerent. Hęc igitur omnia quz ex Ptolemeo adducuntur, ab æquali diſtributione domici liorum quæ in lignifero inſtituitur, aliena eſſe ſatis oftendunt, atquemonteregium in equatione domiciliorum cæleftium ad ſententiam Ptolemei proximè accedere apertè teſtantur, tantb abeſt, vt ad cuentendam eius ſentétiam accommodari pofsint. Illud quoque mihi mirum vifum eſt, quomodo circulum per verticem & polos ſigniferi ductum notari intelligantà Ptolemeo per hæc verba, quæ extant lib.4.capite de filijs: εί δε τῇ πολί γάμωμ λόγῳ καὶ ὁ πολί τέκνωμ ακολοθεῖ, σκοπεῖμ δείσι πάζει κατά κορυφήμ τότεῳ προσόντας, &c. vbiper τόπου κατά κυρυφώμ oftenditur μεσοράνημα, id eft, cæli faftigium feu do mus decima, & potius meridianus quàm circulus per polos zo diaci ductus indicatur. Non fecimus hic mentionem corum, quz adducuntur ex Centiloquio,cùm certum fit, id fcriptum Ptolemço falſo attribui, & plerifque in locis omnino pugnare cumipuus do&rina, quam nobis in Quadripartito ſubali ingenio expofitam reliquit. Sed iam à ſectione cæli ad canones fabricandorum domiciliorum accedam, poſtquam rudiorum gratia ordinem & nomina cuſpidum priùs expoſuerimus. Pri madomus eſt, cuius cuſpis ſeuinitium primum emergit, & fecundum Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 5== PRIMA. cundum modum Monteregij deinceps educitur per æquino Æ ialis partes tricenas, quas Firmic & reliqua turba in ſignifero numerat. Græci ἀνατολήν, id eft, partem exorientem vocant, quòd de inferiori ad ſuperius hemispherium ſcandat:vocante tiam horoscopum. Circulus poſitionis infra terram distansab horizonte 30. partes finit domum primam & fecundam inchoat. Quare eam domum, quæ primæ ſub horizonte fuccedit, ſecundam domum eſſe scias. Hanc ſequitur tertia, quæ ad angulum notis finitur, & sic deinceps contra motum primi mobilis eundo numerantur domus reliquæ, vſque ad domum duodecimam, quòd ſignorum ordo talis sit, & planetarum motus hoc modo procedat. Quatuor ex his duodecim domibus vocantur κάτρα, id est, cardines cæli, nempe ανατολή, orientalis cardo, Juris, occafus seu cardo occidentalis: μεσγράνημα, medium cæli seu domus decima: ὑόγομ, imum medium cæli seu domus quarta. Peculiariter verò Ptolemæus ſummitates primæſquelineas domicilia inchoantes, centra: Spatia verò præcipuas has quatuor ſtationes cœli complentia, angulos vocare ſolet. Quatuor quæ cardines ſequuntur, nempe ſecunda, quinta, octaua & vndecima, επανάφορα, id est, ſuccedentes: Reliquæ quatuor, nempe tertia, ſexta, nona, & duodecima, ἀποκλίνοντες, id est, cadentes vocantur. Quæ omnia oculis ſubijciuntur in cæli generali figura, cuius καταγραφήμ rudioru gratia adijcio:Aſſumpto primo quadrato iuſtæ magnitudinis, ſi inſcribatur quadratum ſecundum, cuius anguli attingant primi quadrati quatuor co- ſtarum media,efficientur quatuor triangulares figurę, quarum quælibet ſecetur bifariam rectis lineis ab angulis primi quadrati ad puncta media coſtarum ſecundi protenſis. Tandem tertium quadratum deſcribatur in ſecúndo non aliter quam ſecun dum in primum inſcriptum eſt, & apparebunt circa quadratum tertium duodecim triangulares formæ, quas domicilia cœle- ſtia vocamus. Simile DIFFERENTIA
translation: We will present the opinion of the other place, from which the same is defended by the authority of Ptolemy. This passage is found in Book 3, Chapter on Aphetic Places, whose words are these: "First, indeed, it must be considered that there are Aphetic or prorogating places, in which it is absolutely necessary for him to be found who wishes to obtain the jurisdiction of governing life, as around the horoscope from five parts previously emerging above the horizon up to the 25 remaining succeeding. And he who touches these thirty parts with right hexagonal rays, which is called the place of the good daemon, and is the eleventh in order. And he who touches with tetragonal rays, namely the zenith above the earth, and he who touches with trigonal rays, which is called God, and he who touches with opposite rays, which is cast into the setting. So far Ptolemy, from whose words it is rightly gathered that the five degrees preceding the cusp and the 25 immediately following hold the virtue of such a house: but what is added about the zodiac, namely, that each house contains qualitatively 30 parts of the zodiac, does not proceed. For Ptolemy here makes no mention of the zodiac: nor does he speak of its parts, but of the equinoctial parts, so that each house is understood to be bounded by 30 equinoctial parts. And this is the reason why he immediately adds that the eleventh house always sees the horoscope with a sextile aspect, the ninth with a trine, the midheaven with a square, which would be absurd if this text of Ptolemy is understood of an equal distribution of domiciles in the ecliptic. For in many places, with the sign Gemini ascending, the midheaven occupies, and thus it does not look at the horoscope from a square, but from a trine aspect of the ecliptic. On the contrary, with the ascendant falling into the ninth house, it will not see the horoscope from a trine, but from a square. Therefore, Cardanus rightly teaches concerning Ptolemy's opinion that two kinds of aspects are to be understood, one in the zodiac, which must be taken exactly in the ecliptic from a similar part and minute, as said above. The other, which Ptolemy understands here, is in the equinoctial, in which, as in the zodiac, the configurations of the twelve signs are to be imagined. Many obscure passages of his agree with this opinion, as when he says that the trine of malefics from signs of short ascensions also destroys when afflicted, that is, when the two circles of position, impacted into the ecliptic through the extremities of the triangular figure, enclose such an interval from the equinoctial that it represents the form of a square aspect in the same equinoctial: then such a triangular aspect of malefics, encountering the giver of life, destroys the native. Similarly also these words, which precede the former passage: "sometimes also the hexagon, afflicted by the long-lived parts of the dodecatemoria, destroys," are to be understood of a double radiation and of the transformation of the hexagonal aspect in the ecliptic into a square aspect in the equinoctial, otherwise neither the trine of short ascensions nor the hexagon of long ascensions would kill. All these things, therefore, which are adduced from Ptolemy, sufficiently show that they are alien to the equal distribution of domiciles which is established in the zodiac, and openly testify that Monteregius approaches most closely to Ptolemy's opinion in the equation of celestial domiciles, so far is it from being able to be accommodated to understanding his opinion. It also seemed wonderful to me how Ptolemy is understood to denote the circle drawn through the vertex and poles of the zodiac by these words, which are found in Book 4, Chapter on Children: "If the reason for marriage also follows the reason for children, it is necessary to observe those things that are present at the vertex," etc., where by "the place at the vertex" is shown the midheaven, that is, the zenith or the tenth house, and rather the meridian than the circle drawn through the poles of the zodiac is indicated. We have not made mention here of those things which are adduced from the Centiloquium, since it is certain that this writing is falsely attributed to Ptolemy, and in most places it entirely contradicts his doctrine, which he left to us expounded with his own genius in the Quadripartitum. But now I will proceed from the section of the sky to the canons for constructing domiciles, after having first explained the order and names of the cusps for the sake of the less learned. The first house is that whose cusp or beginning first emerges, and according to the method of Monteregius it is then drawn out through thirty equinoctial parts, which Firmicus and the rest of the crowd count in the zodiac. The Greeks call it "anatole," that is, the rising part, because it ascends from the lower to the upper hemisphere: also calling it the horoscope. The circle of position below the earth, distant from the horizon by 30 parts, defines the first house and begins the second. Therefore, know that the house which succeeds the first below the horizon is the second house. This is followed by the third, which is bounded at the angle of the notes, and so on, going against the motion of the primum mobile, the remaining houses are numbered up to the twelfth house, because the order of the signs is such, and the motion of the planets proceeds in this way. Four of these twelve houses are called "katra," that is, the cardines of the sky, namely "anatole," the eastern cardo, "dysis," the setting or western cardo: "mesouranema," the midheaven or tenth house: "hypogeion," the imum medium coeli or fourth house. Ptolemy, however, specifically calls the summits of the first lines initiating the domiciles, centers: but the spaces completing these four principal stations of the sky, he usually calls angles. The four that follow the cardines, namely the second, fifth, eighth, and eleventh, are called "epanaphora," that is, succeeding: The remaining four, namely the third, sixth, ninth, and twelfth, are called "apoklinontes," that is, cadent. All these are shown to the eyes in the general figure of the sky, whose "katagraphe" I add for the sake of the less learned: Having taken the first square of just magnitude, if a second square is inscribed, whose angles touch the midpoints of the four sides of the first square, four triangular figures will be formed, each of which is cut in half by straight lines extended from the angles of the first square to the midpoints of the sides of the second. Finally, a third square is described in the second no differently than the second was inscribed in the first, and around the third square will appear twelve triangular forms, which we call celestial domiciles.
