author: Valentin Naibod
title: Calculating Celestial Figures: Monteregius' Method
text: Simile idemque valens ſchema sit, cùm duo quadrata, quorum alterum alteri inſcriptum eſt, duabus diagonalibus ab angulis quadrati exterioris oppoſitis procedentibus dispeſcuntur sic, vt ſequitur. His PRIMA. His rationibus proijciendi cæli in plano nostro tempore plerique vtuntur.Sunt & aliæ complures depingendi cæli formæ non ita vfitatæ, quarum delineationem ob id omisimus. His ita expofitis ad ἀστρολαγίαν conſtituendorum cæli thematum ad quodcunque temporis momentum, & ad quamcunque regionis latitudinem, accedemus. Ac primò ad calculum reuocabimus modum Monteregij ,fiquidem is solus eſt, qui Ptolemæi sententiæ & veritati conſentit, atque idcirco imprimis studiosis cognoscendus. De constructione figuræ cœlestis iuxta modum rationalem Ioannis Monteregij ex tabulis directionum: - Admiranda eſt actio luminis cœlestis, quum non solum corpora in sublunarimundo iam nata abolutaque afficiat , regat & foueat:verumetiam rebus omnibus, quæ sub certa cæli habitudine DIFFERENTIA bitudine exoriuntur, statim ab initio cam vim conferat , quæ easdem ad finem durationis vſque gubernet atque conſeruet, ita vt rerum genitarum ab initio tantum durationis láncietur spatium, atque talis futurus euentus, qualis fuerit in earundem exordio cæli poſitus : vtque ab initio mouendi reliqua series futurorum motuum vſque ad finem, à naturæ peritis explicari posſit. Cum autem cælum ob inenarrabilem celeritatem singulis temporummomentis situm suum notabiliter permutet, facilè eſt iudicare, cur astrologi ex poſitu cæli exortarum rerú naturali ordine secuturos euentus explicaturi, momenta temporis, quibus ipſæ res initia sumpserunt , diligenter exquirant. Niſi enim de tempore exortus rerum nobis exactè cõtet, nunquam ad situm cæli verum, neque ad causam rebus eo tempore productis vim conſervatricem infundentem peruenire poterimus. In construendis igitur veris cæli thematibus prima cura fit, ne à vero tempore, hoc eſt, non saltém ab anno, mense, die & hora, verumetiam minuto temporis scrupulo, quo res ipsa cepit, aberremus. Cùm autem diuerfitas temporis ciuilis & mathe maticorum incautiores & rudiores animos hic implicitos tene re, atque in errorem ducere queat, idcirco primò præcipiemus de reducèndo tempore politico seu vulgari ad tempus Astronomicum, vt ad calculum tabularum congruat. Sumunt autem mathematici ob cauſas alibi explicandas initium diei à circulo meridiano, id eſt, ab eo puncto, quo Sol meridionalem circulú seu supra seu infra terram tenet , & inde 24. horas continuè, quoad Sol eundem locum repetat, numerant. In Ephemeridibus etiam hoc modo computati sunt motus, vt loca stellarum singulis diebus assignata, de diebus Mathematicis completis semper intelligenda sint .Exempli gratia, anno labente 1527. die Maij 21. hora 3.& sc.47. vnius horæ natus eſt quidã Vallidoleti, sub latitudine 41. part. longitudine verò par. 8. scr. 20. Oppositio luminarium proximè & immediatè genituram præcedens fuit die Maij 15. iuxta quem motus ☉ in Ephemeride Stoffleri assignatus est p.3. sc.6.11 Hic motus intelligendus eſt de die Mathematico completo, hoc eſt, quòd in eo puncto meridiei eius diei, qui etiam ciuiliter decimus quintus Maij dicitur, centrum Solis in dicto scrupulo signi Geminorum reperiatur, Nam PRIMA. Nem Stoflerus in Ephemeridibus stellarum situs non ad mediam noctem, sed ad meridiem seu medium diurnæ lucis suppu tauit. Porro tempus oppoſitionis luminarium, quæ in eundem diem incidit, notatur 11.H.11.sc.quod rursum de tempore com pleto intelligendum eſt, hoc eſt, diem hunc Mathematicum integrè eſsè completum, & insuper horas 11, cùm, scrupulis 11. præterijſsè. Quòd ſi eadem opposſitio fuiſset circa horam 23. intelligi debet, illam fuiſse futuram vna hora ante meridiem diei proximè ſequentis, qui ciuiliter ad diem 16. Maij refertur. Nam & ciuiliter seu in communi vita plerunque itidem à meridiano circulo dies inchoantur, verùm non à meridie, vt in ephemeridibus noſtris, sed ab hora mediæ noctis principium capiunt, atque ad noctis ſequentis medium protenduntur. Tales sunt er tiam supputationes motuum in tabulis Prutenicis, in quibus Epochæ Chriſti ad mediam noctem diei præcedentis Calendas Ianuarias statuuntur. Qui itaque Romanorum exemplo diem à media nocte exordiuntur, atque non integro horologio, sed horis duodecim bis repetitis vtuntur, horum horas ad tempus Astronomicum à meridie inceptum aptabis, ſi horis antemeridianis seu poſtmedium noctis elapsis adijcias horas duodecim pomeridianas, atque numerum die rum ciuilium vnione diminuas. Veluti si quis natus eſset salutiferi partus anno labente 1527. die 21. Maij, 9. H. 47.sc. ante meridié, tunc pro tali ciuili tempore ad tempus ephemeridum aptando, horis 9.& scupulis 47. adijciendæ sunt horæ 12. vt exeant 21.H.47.sc. quod tempus horarum scrupulorumq; vicesimum diem Maij completum sequitur. Idem eſt, si in tali casu ex vulgi seu politico tempore horas duodecim detrahas. Veluti si ex die 21. Maij, 9. horis, 47.scrup. detrahas horas 12. remanebunt vt prius dies 20. Maij, 21.H.47.sc. Cæterum si offerantur horæ à meridie ad mediam noctem numeratæ, ex cum horis Astronomicis congruunt, nec vllam commutationem vlteriorem admittunt. Veluti accidit in assumpta genitura, quæ vt tanto antè dicebamus, fuit anno 1527. die 21. Maij, hora 3; scrup.47. poſt meridiem. Id tempus noſtra reductione non indiget, offeruntur enim 21.dies Maij in meridie completi, & insuper horæ vnà cum scrupulis 47. Talis simplex temporis commutatio nó ==Start of OCR for page 1== 149 DIFFERENTIA tantùm ad cæli figuram extruendam ſufficit, ſed & ad excerpendos motus planetarum tum ex vſitatis ephemeridibus, tum verò ex pleriſque tabulis cæleftium motuum, quas reſolutas vocant, proficit. Sed tamen ſi ex tabulis Prutenicis atque ab Epochis Chriſti, quæ vt dixi, ad mediam noctem ſtatuuntur, motus ſtellarum colligere placet: tunc vice verſa horas Aſtronomicas à meridie inceptas, in horas à media nocte computatas conuertere debemus, à tempore ad hunc modum, quem iam expofui,ad meridiei principium transformato, duodecim horas ſubducentes, vt horæ aſtronomicæ à medietate noctis numeratæ relinquantur. Quòd ſi quâdo ſubtractio ab horis fieri nequeat, tunc integer dies naturalis, hoc eſt, horæ 24. ficut in calculo Phyſico conſuetum eſt, accommodandæ funt, numeruſq; dierum vnitate minuendus. Et vice verſa ſi tempus à me dia nocte ſupputatum offeratur, atque ad meridiei principium traducendum fit, tunc tempori propoſito horæ 12.adijciendæ funt, & ſi ex huiufmodi compoſitione plures quàm 24. horæ excreſcant, vigintiquatuor horæ abijciendæ, numeruſq; dieri vnione augedus. Quòd ſi forte horas ab occaſu numeratas, quæ libus olim Athenienſes vtebantur, in horas Aſtronomicas à meridie ſupputatas conuertere velis, tunc horas tuas ab occa- ſu ſupputatas cum tempore ſemidiurno, quod per doctrinam aſcenſionum eruendum eſt, coniunge, & conflabitur tempus Mathematicum à meridie inceptum. Et ſi ex tali additione plures quàm 24. horæ exiſtant, iterum memineris abijciendas eſſe ex aggregato horas 24. dierumque numerum vnione augen dum. Econtra verò, ſi tempus à meridie ſupputatum in tempus ab occaſu ſluens conuertendum fit, ab horis aſtronomicis aufer arcum temporis ſemidiurni, & quotieſcunque ſubtractio fieri nequiuerit, horas 24. aſſume, numerumque dierum vnitate minue, & reſtabit tempus ab occaſu numeratum. Ad extremum tempus ab ortu numeratum in dictum tempus no- ſtrum Aſtronomicum commutabis, ſi ipſi arcum ſemidiurnum vnà cum integris 12. horis adijcias, abiectis rurſus 24. horis, ſi opus fit, numeroq; dierum vnitate adaucto. At vice verſa ſi ille horæ aſtronomicæ in horas ab ortu numeratas traducendæ ſint, ſeminocturnuar temporis arcum vnà cum integris 12.horis Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 1==
translation: A similar and equally valid scheme is when two squares, one inscribed within the other, are divided by two diagonals proceeding from opposite angles of the outer square, as follows. These are the FIRST. Most people in our time use these methods for projecting the heavens onto a plane. There are also many other less common forms of depicting the heavens, whose delineation we have omitted for that reason. Having explained these things, we will proceed to the astrology of constructing celestial charts for any moment in time and for any latitude of a region. And first, we will bring back to calculation the method of Monteregius, since he alone is the one who agrees with Ptolemy's opinion and truth, and therefore must be known above all by students. On the construction of the celestial figure according to the rational method of John Monteregius from the tables of directions: - The action of celestial light is admirable, as it not only affects, governs, and nourishes bodies already born and perfected in the sublunar world, but also confers upon all things that arise under a certain celestial disposition, from the very beginning, that power which governs and preserves them until the end of their duration, so that from the beginning of generated things, the duration of their existence is cast, and such will be the future event as was the position of the heavens at their origin: and that from the beginning of motion, the remaining series of future motions until the end can be explained by those skilled in nature. Since the heavens, due to their ineffable speed, significantly change their position at every moment in time, it is easy to judge why astrologers, in explaining future events according to the natural order of things arising from the position of the heavens, diligently seek out the moments in time when these things began. For unless the time of the origin of things is accurately told to us, we will never be able to arrive at the true position of the heavens, nor at the cause that infuses a preservative power into things produced at that time. Therefore, in constructing true celestial charts, the first concern is that we do not deviate from the true time, that is, not only from the year, month, day, and hour, but also from the minute and second of time when the thing itself began. However, since the diversity of civil and mathematical time can entangle and mislead the incautious and less educated minds here, we will first prescribe how to reduce political or common time to Astronomical time, so that it agrees with the calculation of the tables. Mathematicians, for reasons to be explained elsewhere, take the beginning of the day from the meridian circle, that is, from that point where the Sun holds the meridian circle either above or below the earth, and from there they count 24 continuous hours until the Sun repeats the same place. In Ephemerides, motions are also calculated in this way, so that the positions of the stars assigned for each day are always to be understood as referring to completed Mathematical days. For example, in the year 1527, on May 21, at 3 hours and 47 minutes, someone was born in Valladolid, under latitude 41 degrees, and longitude 8 degrees, 20 minutes. The opposition of the luminaries immediately preceding the birth was on May 15, according to which the motion of the Sun in Stöffler's Ephemeris is assigned as 3 degrees, 6 minutes, 11 seconds. This motion is to be understood as referring to the completed Mathematical day, that is, that at that point of the meridian of that day, which is also civilly called the fifteenth of May, the center of the Sun is found in the said minute of the sign of Gemini. For Stöffler, in his Ephemerides, calculated the positions of the stars not for midnight, but for midday or the middle of the diurnal light. Furthermore, the time of the opposition of the luminaries, which falls on the same day, is noted as 11 hours, 11 minutes, which again is to be understood as referring to completed time, that is, that this Mathematical day is entirely completed, and moreover, 11 hours and 11 minutes have passed. If the same opposition had been around the 23rd hour, it should be understood that it would have been one hour before midday of the next day, which civilly refers to May 16. For civilly or in common life, days are often begun from the meridian circle, but not from midday, as in our ephemerides, but they begin at midnight and extend to the middle of the following night. Such are also the calculations of motions in the Prutenic Tables, in which the Epochs of Christ are set for midnight of the day preceding the Kalends of January. Therefore, those who, following the example of the Romans, begin the day from midnight, and use not a full clock, but twelve hours repeated twice, you will adapt their hours to Astronomical time begun from midday, if you add twelve post-meridian hours to the ante-meridian hours or those elapsed after midnight, and diminish the number of civil days by one. As if someone were born in the year of salvation 1527, on May 21, at 9 hours, 47 minutes before midday, then for such civil time to be adapted to the time of the ephemerides, 12 hours must be added to the 9 hours and 47 minutes, so that 21 hours, 47 minutes result, which time of hours and minutes follows the completed twentieth day of May. The same is true if in such a case you subtract twelve hours from the common or political time. As if from May 21, 9 hours, 47 minutes, you subtract 12 hours, there will remain, as before, May 20, 21 hours, 47 minutes. However, if hours counted from midday to midnight are offered, they agree with Astronomical hours and do not admit any further commutation. As happened in the assumed nativity, which, as we said so long ago, was in the year 1527, on May 21, at 3 hours, 47 minutes after midday. That time does not need our reduction, for May 21 is offered as completed at midday, and moreover, hours along with 47 minutes. Such a simple commutation of time is not only sufficient for constructing the figure of the heavens, but also useful for extracting the motions of the planets both from the usual ephemerides and from most tables of celestial motions, which they call resolved. But if it is desired to collect the motions of the stars from the Prutenic Tables and from the Epochs of Christ, which, as I said, are set for midnight: then, conversely, we must convert Astronomical hours, begun from midday, into hours calculated from midnight, by subtracting twelve hours from the time transformed to the beginning of midday in the manner I have already explained, so that the astronomical hours counted from the middle of the night remain. If, however, subtraction cannot be made from the hours, then an entire natural day, that is, 24 hours, as is customary in physical calculation, must be accommodated, and the number of days diminished by one. And conversely, if time calculated from midnight is offered, and it is to be translated to the beginning of midday, then 12 hours must be added to the proposed time, and if from such a composition more than 24 hours arise, twenty-four hours must be discarded, and the number of days increased by one. If by chance you wish to convert hours counted from sunset, which the Athenians once used, into Astronomical hours calculated from midday, then combine your hours calculated from sunset with the semi-diurnal time, which must be found by the doctrine of ascensions, and the Mathematical time begun from midday will be formed. And if from such an addition more than 24 hours exist, remember again that 24 hours must be discarded from the aggregate, and the number of days increased by one. Conversely, if time calculated from midday is to be converted into time flowing from sunset, subtract the arc of semi-diurnal time from the astronomical hours, and whenever subtraction cannot be made, assume 24 hours, and diminish the number of days by one, and the time counted from sunset will remain. Finally, you will convert the time counted from sunrise into our said Astronomical time, if you add to it the semi-diurnal arc along with a full 12 hours, again discarding 24 hours if necessary, and increasing the number of days by one. But conversely, if those astronomical hours are to be translated into hours counted from sunrise, you will subtract the semi-nocturnal arc of time along with a full 12 hours.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Calculating Astronomical Time
text: ==Start of OCR for page 2== E I PRIMA. 229 12.horis ab illis aufer, accommodatis iterum, ſi res ita poſtulauerit, 24.horis, numeroque dierum vnitate diminuto, & remanebit tempus ab occaſu ſupputatú. Quod ad cauſam taliú temporis commutationum attinet, ea in promptu eſt ijs qui intelligunt, eos qui à meridie motus auſpicantur, plus temporis retrò complecti, quàm qui vel ab occaſu, vel à media nocte, vel denique ab ortu principium capiunt. Ipſa verò ſeu vulgi, ſeu ciuilia tempora,cùm iuxta hæc præcepta in tempus aſtronomicum meridiei accommodatum traducta fuerint, nulla ampliùs correctione, quantum ad figuram cæli conſtruendam attinet, indigebunt. Nam ad tempus æquatum quod ciuili tempori cő- ſentit, figura cœli conformatur. Quòd ſi tempus aſtronomicum ex tabulis motuŭ aut ephemeridibus depromptum fit, id quod vſu venire ſolet, cúaſcendés alicuius Eclipſis, aut alteriùs luminarium vel reliquarú ſtellarú copulationis ſcire cupimus: tunc pro vera thematis cõſtitutione addenda eſt ei, vel ab eodem diminuenda dierũ naturaliŭ προθαφαίρεσις, ſeu vt vocat, æquatio. Similiter ratióe meridianorű eidé adijciendú eſt, vel ab eodem demendú tépus in tabula regionű repertú, prout expofcit notz A vel M ibidé aſsignatæ. Atq; hactenus de vero tépore con- ſtituendo, quod primò in conſtructione figuræ cæleftis obſeruandum eſt. Secundum præceptum eſt, vt ad hoc ipſum tempus aſtronomicú ex motuum vel ephemeridű tabulis, verú locum Solis eliciamus: vbi etiam ad meridianorú differentiam reſpici endú erit, ſi tuus & Ephemeridű loca, longitudine notatu digna diſsideant. Ea verò, vt infrà clariùs dicetur, addenda eſt tempori,ad quod locum Solis excerpere ſtatuiſti: ſi locus tuus loco ta bularum ſeu Ephemeridum fuerit occidétalior, aut ab eodem ſubtrahenda, ſi fit orientalior. Veluti in noſtro exemplo, quonia locus Vallidoleticus longitudo eſt p.8.ſcr.20. loco Vlmen ſi,ad quem Ephemerides Stoffleri accomodatæ ſunt, cuius lon gitudo eſt.par.33.ſcr.0.occidentalior eſt part.24.ſc.40.hoc eſt, hora 1.ſcr.38.idcircò hæc differétia ad horas 3.ſc.47.addenda eſt, vtproueniant horæ 5.ſcr.25.quod ſi ſcrupuloſius negotiú perſequi libeat, ratione æquationis dierum adhuc ſcr.9.ſunt addenda, vt fiant horæ 5. ſcr.353.quod eſt tépus ad locú Solis, imò verò ad loca omnium ſtellarum ex Ephemeridibus depromenda I R Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 2== ==Start of OCR for page 3== 230 DIFFERENTIA menda omnibus modis aptatum. Porrò locum Solis verű cum deprehenderimus, mox eidem arcum æquinoctialis congruen tem ex tabulis aſcenſionum rectarum excerpemus, idq; ſufficiet ſi Sol præcisè in meridiano fuerit, id eſt, ſi diebus in meridiem cõpletis nullæ horæ, nec vlla ſcru.téporis adhæſerint. Verùm ſi Sol à meridie aliquod tépus emenſus eſt, tunc aſcenſioni rectæ loci Solis adijciendus eſt arcus æquinoctialis, quem horæ horarumq; ſcrupula pomeridiana in gradus æquinoctialis graduumq; ſcrupula conuerſa conficiunt, vt numerus graduum & ſcrupulorum ex huiufmodi additione proueniens, medij cæli ſeu cuſpidis decimæ domus aſcenſioné rectă oſtédat. Ceterum horæ horarumq; ſcrupula per 15.gradus multiplicata in tépera æquinoctialis tranſeunt. Singulis enim horis æqualibus vbiuis locorum 15.gradus æquinoctialis peroriuntur. Id auté de phy- ſica multiplicatione intelligendum eſt. Quòd ſi vulgarem ſupputādi rationem ſequi malis, horas quidem per 15.gradus multiplica, ſcrupula verò horarum prima per 4. partiaris, vt grad æquinoctialis tali tempori congruentes prodeant:ſiquidé,vt di Aú eſt, vni horæ téporis ex æquinoctali 15.tépora, & ob id quatuor téporis ſcrupulis primis vnus gradus æquinoctialis correſpondet. Veluti in noſtro exemplo, cùm tépus horarũ 3.ſcru. pulorumq; 47.in gradus æquinoctialis traſmutare debeamus, ducemus horas 3.in 15.& fient par.45.ſcru.verò 47. partiemur per 4.& prodibunt part.11.fcr.45.quæ compoſita cum partibus 45.producunt partes 56.fcr.45. qui eſt arcus æquinoctialis horis 3.& temporis ſcrupulis 47.correſpondés. Tertiò reliquorũ quinq; domiciliorú orientalium, quæ decimæ domui ad quartam vſque ſuccedunt,aſcenſionem obliquam colliges, ſi part.30. aſcenſioni medij cæli quinquies ordine adijcias. Veluti ſi aſcen ſioni M C,adijcias partes 30.congeries talis erit aſcenſio obliqua domus vndecima. Huic iterum ſi addas partes 30.aſcenſionem obliquã domus duodecimæ colliges, & ſic deinceps conti nua additione partium 30.aſcenſiones obliquæ cuſpidű ſequetium eliciendæ funt. quartò loca Eclipticæ, quæ his aſcenſionibus reſpondent, ex tabulis aſcenſionu, quarú peculiares ſingulis penè locis deſeruiunt, per nonũ problema Monteregij elici enda funt. Sed decimæ domus cuſpis perpetuò ex tabulis aſcen. ſionum Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 3== ==Start of OCR for page 4== = 7 E = = i : PRIMA. 139 ſionũ rectari ſumiť, na meridionalis circulus vice recti horizó tis fungit. Cardini verò exorientis cæli ſeruit tabula cõdita ad latitudiné loci propoſiti. Deniq; tabulas cuſpidibus reliquarú domorú eliciédis in ſeruiétes, indicat rationalis domorú tabella, circa finé tabularű monteregij poſita, in cuius area duo nume ri diſtin&i ſunt, quorú prior eleuationé poli, ſupra circulú pofitionis vndecime & tertię domus, poſterior eandé ſupra circulú poſitionis duodecime & ſecunde cómonſtrat. quere ergo nu merű eleuatióis poli, ſeu quod idé eſt, numerű latitudinis dati loci in ſiniſtro tabellæ rationalis latere, & è regione in area deprehendes numeros, qui monét, ex quib' tabulis & ad quá latitudiné códitis principia reſtantiú domorú depromé da ſint.Ex. empli gratia, loci geniturę, qua in exéplari expoſitione vtimur, latitudo eſt partiŭ 41.idcirco vndecime & tertię cuspidi ſeruiet tabula aſcenſionú ad obliquarú latitudiné partiú 23.& ſc.29.có poſita. Duodecima verò & ſecúdę aptanda erit alia tabula facta ad latitudiné partiŭ 36.ſc.58. Sed cùm tabulę aſceſionú obliqua rumad integros gradus ſaltem ſint conditę,idcirco pro partib⁹ 23.& ſcrup. 29.fimpliciter part.23.vel 24.& pro partibus 36.ſcr. 58.ſimpliciter integras partes 37.propter vicinitaté numerorú, ad vſus facilitatem ſine manifeſto errore aſſumemus. quòd ſi omnia diligenter exequi, atque ad vnguem elaborare velimus, finem noni problematis tabularů monteregij euoluétes cogno ſcemus, quid facto opus ſit ſupputatori, calculum ſuum ad poli altitudinem non precisè in gradus, ſed in graduŭ ſc. deſinétem accommodanti. Sed hęc diligentia no nifi ad exactè conſtitué- dam partem horoſcopantem neceſſaria eſt, cùm decima domus nullum planè exaltati verticis numerű annexű habere confueuerit, & reliquis cuſpidibus extra cardines poſitis ſupputatio ciπλάθη facta abundè ſatisfaciat. Cognitis autem initiis ſex do morum orientalium, principia reliquarű ſex domorú occidentalium nó ampliùs latebunt, ſiquidem vnaquæq; domus orientalis ſuam comparem in parte occidentali ex diametro reſpicit. Quapropter ſi in oppoſita domicilia oppoſitorú ſignorum fimiles partes & ſcrupula deſcripſerimus, integrű cæli thema per ficiemus. Verű hęc omnia vt exemplo planiora fiant, ad tempus geniturę aſſumptę, nempe ad diem 21. Maii, hora tertiam pome R2 ridiaDigitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 4==
translation: From these, subtract 12 hours, adjusting again, if the matter so demands, by 24 hours, and with the number of days diminished by one, the time calculated from sunset will remain. As for the reason for such time commutations, it is readily apparent to those who understand that those who begin their movements from noon encompass more time backward than those who begin from sunset, or from midnight, or finally from sunrise. However, the common or civil times, when they have been translated into astronomical time adjusted to noon according to these precepts, will no longer require any further correction, as far as constructing the figure of the heavens is concerned. For the figure of the heavens is conformed to the equated time that agrees with civil time. But if astronomical time is taken from tables of movements or ephemerides, which usually happens when we wish to know the ascendant of an eclipse, or of another luminary or the conjunction of other stars: then for the true constitution of the chart, the natural days' prothaphairesis, or as it is called, the equation, must be added to it or subtracted from it. Similarly, for the meridians, the time found in the table of regions must be added to it or subtracted from it, as indicated by the A or M assigned there. And so far concerning the determination of true time, which must first be observed in the construction of the celestial figure. The second precept is that for this very astronomical time, we derive the true place of the Sun from the tables of movements or ephemerides: where also the difference of the meridians must be considered, if your location and the locations of the Ephemerides differ by a notable longitude. This difference, as will be explained more clearly below, must be added to the time for which you have decided to extract the Sun's place: if your location is more westerly than the location of the tables or Ephemerides, or subtracted from it if it is more easterly. As in our example, since the location of Valladolid is longitude 8 degrees 20 minutes, it is 24 degrees 40 minutes, that is, 1 hour 38 minutes, more westerly than the location of Ulm, for which Stoffler's Ephemerides are adapted, whose longitude is 33 degrees 0 minutes. Therefore, this difference must be added to 3 hours 47 minutes, so that 5 hours 25 minutes result. If one wishes to pursue the matter more scrupulously, 9 minutes must still be added due to the equation of days, so that 5 hours 35 minutes result, which is the time adapted in all ways for the Sun's place, indeed, for the places of all the stars to be derived from the Ephemerides. Furthermore, when we have found the true place of the Sun, we will immediately extract the corresponding arc of the equinoctial from the tables of right ascensions, and this will suffice if the Sun is precisely on the meridian, that is, if no hours or minutes of time are attached after the completion of the days at noon. But if the Sun has passed some time from noon, then the arc of the equinoctial, which the hours and minutes of the afternoon hours converted into degrees and minutes of the equinoctial make up, must be added to the right ascension of the Sun's place, so that the number of degrees and minutes resulting from such an addition shows the right ascension of the Midheaven or the cusp of the tenth house. Moreover, the hours and minutes of hours multiplied by 15 degrees pass into equinoctial times. For in every equal hour, 15 degrees of the equinoctial rise everywhere. This, however, is to be understood as physical multiplication. But if you prefer to follow the common method of calculation, multiply the hours by 15 degrees, and divide the first minutes of the hours by 4, so that the degrees of the equinoctial corresponding to such time result: since, as has been said, 15 times correspond to one hour of time from the equinoctial, and for this reason, one degree of the equinoctial corresponds to four first minutes of time. As in our example, since we must transform 3 hours and 47 minutes into degrees of the equinoctial, we will multiply 3 hours by 15, and 45 parts will result. We will divide 47 minutes by 4, and 11 parts 45 minutes will result, which, combined with 45 parts, produce 56 parts 45 minutes, which is the arc of the equinoctial corresponding to 3 hours and 47 minutes of time. Thirdly, you will collect the oblique ascension of the remaining five eastern houses, which succeed the tenth house up to the fourth, if you add 30 parts to the ascension of the Midheaven five times in order. For example, if you add 30 parts to the ascension of the MC, the sum will be the oblique ascension of the eleventh house. If you again add 30 parts to this, you will collect the oblique ascension of the twelfth house, and so on, by continuous addition of 30 parts, the oblique ascensions of the succeeding cusps are to be derived. Fourthly, the places of the Ecliptic, which correspond to these ascensions, are to be derived from the tables of ascensions, of which peculiar ones serve almost every place, by the ninth problem of Monteregius. But the cusp of the tenth house is always taken from the tables of right ascensions, for the meridional circle acts as the right horizon. The table constructed for the latitude of the proposed place serves the cardine of the rising heaven. Finally, the rational table of houses, placed near the end of Monteregius' tables, indicates the tables serving to derive the cusps of the remaining houses. In its area, two distinct numbers are found, the former showing the elevation of the pole above the circle of position of the eleventh and third houses, the latter showing the same above the circle of position of the twelfth and second houses. Therefore, seek the number of the elevation of the pole, or what is the same, the number of the latitude of the given place in the left side of the rational table, and opposite it in the area you will find numbers that indicate from which tables and for what latitude they were constructed, the principles of the remaining houses are to be derived. For example, the latitude of the place of birth, which we use in the exemplary exposition, is 41 parts. Therefore, the table of oblique ascensions constructed for the latitude of 23 parts and 29 minutes will serve the eleventh and third cusps. The twelfth and second will be adapted to another table made for the latitude of 36 parts 58 minutes. But since the tables of oblique ascensions are constructed only for whole degrees, therefore for 23 parts and 29 minutes, we will simply assume 23 or 24 parts, and for 36 parts 58 minutes, simply 37 whole parts, due to the proximity of the numbers, for ease of use without manifest error. If we wish to execute everything diligently and elaborate it to the letter, by consulting the end of the ninth problem of Monteregius' tables, we will know what the calculator needs to do, adjusting his calculation to the altitude of the pole not precisely in degrees, but in minutes of degrees. But this diligence is necessary only for accurately constituting the horoscoping part, since the tenth house is not accustomed to having any number of the exalted vertex attached, and for the remaining cusps placed outside the cardines, a calculation made approximately abundantly suffices. However, once the beginnings of the six eastern houses are known, the principles of the remaining six western houses will no longer be hidden, since each eastern house looks diametrically opposite to its counterpart in the western part. Therefore, if we describe similar parts and minutes in the opposite houses of the opposite signs, we will complete the entire celestial chart. But all these things, so that they may become clearer by example, for the time of the assumed birth, namely, for the 21st day of May, at the third hour after noon.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Aspects and Their Nature
text: PRIMA. 53. tatur vt vides in ſchemate fic, ſex videlicet lineis ex vno cen tro exeuntibus, quæ in circulo deſcriptę dirimunt eum in fex partes æquales. Hic aſpectus mediocriter bonus céſetur, quòd ſigna hoc interuallo ſe contuentia ſexu & natura conueniant. 2 Hunc proximè ſequitur τετράγων, hoc eft, quadratus af. Et afpicit pectus:fic dictus, quòd quartam partem Zodiaci, hoc eft, tria fi- quartam, gna fcu P.90.comprehendat, quem in figura vides deſcriptum &c. per rectam AD, notatumque tetragono, qui in circulo de- ſcriptus in quodlibet latus tria ſigna ſuſcipit. Hic aspectus mediocriter malus iudicatur, quòd duo ſigna tali interuallo difiű &a, ſexu & natura diſsideant. 3 Tetragonum ſequitur τρίγων, id eft, trinus aſpe&us,qui tertiam partem Zodiaci, hoc eft, P.120.continet: & defcribitur Et afpicit in ſchemate per rectam A E,ſignaturque æqualium laterum tri quoque gono A, qui in interiorem circulum translatus, in quodlibet &c. latus 4. figna recipit. Hic quoque aſpectus bonusiudicatur, par tim propter efficaciam, partim quòd ſigna tria tanto interuallo diſtantia ſexu & natura conueniant. 4 Triangulum ſequitur διάμετρg, hoc eft, aſpectus diametralis vel oppoſitus, qui medietatem circuli, id eſt, ſex figna, P. Afpicit au tem ſeptio 180.continet:& defcribitur in ſchemate per rectam A F, nota- mum, &c.. turque duobus orbibus oppoſitis .Hic aspectus egregiè malus cenfetur, non propter pugnam naturarum in ſignis, ſed potiùs ratione oppoſitionis: etenim quæ oppoſita ſunt atque maximè diſtant, maximè ſibi inimicantur, & in vnoquoque genere ſunt ſibi contraria. Sunt ergo duę cauſe diſconuenientiz in aſpectibus: vna & nimis principalis eſt pugnantia ſignorum in natura & fexu:altera & principalis eſt diſtantia maxima. Sic in quadrato radio, quoniam diftantia mediocris eſt, & tantùm in natura & ſexu ſignorum contrarietas, diſconuenientia minor exiſtit. Et & & * radiationes cùm non maximè diſtent, nom diſconueniút, ſed mediocriter conueniunt propter ſimilitudiné naturarú & ſexus in dodecatemorijs: fed oppoſita figna,cùm maximè diſtét per 15.tertij elemétorű Euclidis, ideo, etli natura & ſexu cóueniat, maximè tamé propter ſumma diſtantia diſcor dant:veluti & funt ambo masculina & diurna, & &m, ambo ſunt feminina & no&urna,&tamé funt oppoſita & difc6- uenientia, G3 - Digitized by Google 54 DIFFERENTIA uenientia, cuius diſcóuenientię cauſa eſt maxima corú diſtátis, & non diuerſitas ſeu contrarietas in ſexu & natura, id quod om nino verum eft:fiquidem ſigna oppoſita, que ſexu & natura có uenire manifeſtum eſt, maximè & plus quam quadrata inter ſe pugnare credere oportet. Quod verò nonnulli tradiderunt,figna oppoſita ideo inter ſe diſcordare, quòd ex duobus quadra. tis aſpe&ibus componantur,quo pacto & diameter ex duobus quadratis radiationibus, nimirum &5, deinde & conftat, non procedit. Nam quæ vni diſconueniunt, illa potiùs inter ſe conueniunt quam diſconueniunt. Prætereà cú oppoſita radiatio etiam ex duabus conuenientibus, nempe hexagona & trigona conſtet: ſecundum hanc rationem ſignorum oppofitio maximè iudicanda eſſet ſymphora, quod eft ab- ſurdum. Haly putat, ſigna oppoſita propter duas caufas effea- ſymphona. Prima eft, quòd vno afcendente alterum deſcendat, & econtra:ſecunda, quòd quando eſt in vno eorum, faciat tempus contrarium ei quando eſt in relique: veluti quandoeſt in, facit tempus humidum, in ficcum. Sed expo- ſteriore cauſa ſequeretur hoc abſurdum, quòd & eflent aſymphona,ſiquidem non minus eſt ſignum vernale quàm ſignum II, cui opponitur. Quapropterratio prius expoſita, ad radiationum do&rinam firmandam potior eft, & Ptolemzi ſententiæ conſentit. Verba eius de hac re ſunt iſta: τότωμ μέρ τοιχηματισμώμ οι μαντρίγωνοι καὶ εξάγωνοι σύμφωνοι και λένται, πατὸ ἐξ ομογενῶμ συγκεῖθαι δωδεκατημορίωμ, ήτοι ἐκ πάντωμθηλυκῶμ ἢ αρσενικῶμ. ἀσύμφωνοι δὲοι τετράγωνοι, καὶ οι κατὰ διάμετρομ, δίοτι κατὰ αὐτίθεσιμ την ομογενώμ τὸ σύςα- σιμ λαμβάνεσι, hoc eft: Exiſtimantur autem natura congruentes trianguli & ſexanguliaſpectus,proptereà quòd tunc cognafigna feminina aut masculina conferuntur:inconuenientes verò figuræ habentur quadrata & oppoſita, quod illæ per diſsimilia loca conſtituantur. Ex quibus verbis etiam illud colligitur, quòd ſignorum conuenientia in hexagonis & triangulisa- ſpectibus, & diſconuenientia in quadratis & oppoſitis radiatio nibus non de crafi & temperatura, vt volunt Arabes, ſed tátum de ſexu & natura fignorum intelligenda fit,vt fignar & II etfa hexagona radiatione iunguntur, non tamen funt ejufdem tem perisia Digitized by Google PRIMA. 55 perici, ſed alterum humidum, alterum ficcum exiſtit. Similiter ſigna trigona non temperie, ſed natura & ſexu, vt ſuprà dictum eft, conueniunt. Vt & omnia maſculina diurnaq; funt, ſed ſignum humidum eſt & calidum, & calidum & ficcum, →→ ficcum & frigidum, &c. Quòd ſi aliquis Ptolemæo obijciat, quòd quæ figna maſculina diurnaque ſunt, eadem quoque calida & ficca: & quæ fœminina & nocturna, eadem etiam frigida & humida non immeritò putari? Ad hanc obiectionem reſpondere debemus, talem conſecutionem minimè eſſe neceſſariam, quandoquidem conditio temporis & ſexus ex pri mis qualitatibus non naſcitur. Etenim diurnus nocturnusque ſtatus à ſexu maſculino proueniút: Ipſe verò ſexus maſculinus & fœmininus à magis efficaci velinefficaciactione deriuatur. Efficacia denique hæc manat à principio motus robuſtiſsimi, qui iuxta æquino&ialem exiſtit. Teſtatur id Ptolemeus libr.. cap. de mafcul, & fœmin. ſignis, his verbis: ὁ ἰσημερινός κύκλος λλὰ τὸν δωδεκατημόριομ, καὶ ἔτι τὸ τὴν χηλωμγραφόμενο, τιὼ πρώτιὼ καὶ ἰαυροτάτω την όλωμ φοράμἀοτελῶ, hoc eft,a quino&ialis circulus per V & deſcriptus, prima & validif ſimam omnium agitationem efficit. Ab his igitur continua ſerie maſculinis fœminina ſubijciuntur, hoc eft, Aby&vir tus hæc communicatur II &. Nam hæc fica ab vtro- . que principio, nempe & conuenienti aſpectu, id eft, * & etiam a ſeſe aſpiciunt. His ita ſe habentibus, perſpicuum eft, primò quòd ſexus & conditio diurna nocturna ſignorú, à motu & non à qualitatibus ducat originem. Secundò,quòd non omniamaſculina diurnaq; dodecatemoria, rec&è dicantur calida & ficca, nec fœminina noturnaque, humida & frigida. 1 Aſpectibus connumeratur coniun&io, od, quæ notatur vnico orbe ſico, vt duorum corporú in eadem linea coitus fignificetur. Ex his quæ dicta funt, etiam id liquet, noxios & mi Etſiduos naces aſpectus eſſe quadrangulares & diametros: Beneuolos &c. verò triquetros & hexagonos: conjun&ionem deniq; eſſe indifferentem, hoc eſt, cum bonis bonam, cum malis malá. Quod Ad robur eorum attinet, coniunctio omnium eſt robuſtiſsima & efficacifsima, poſt oppoſitio, quòd sicut in cauis ſpeculis apparet, lines per centrum cuntes, omnium fint validiſsimæ, & quòd Digitized by Google 56 DIFFERENTIA quòd ſemper à paucioribus lateribus procedentia ſint effica ciora ijs, quæ à multis lateribus funt. Tertium locum habent quadratus & trigonus, qui poteſtate penè equales cenfentur. Vltima & debiliſsima radiatio eſt, que fit ex hexagono. Ptole meus radiationum duas alias adducit rationes, alteram Geome tricam, alteram Muſicam, quas ſtudio breuitatis iam omitto,& quòd radiationibus magis ab experimento,quam propter vllas rationes fides adhibeatur. Omnes prætereà radij pręter oppoſitos, duplices ſunt, ſiniſtri & dextri.Siniſtri funt, qui ante lignum à quo incipimus, exiſtunt, id eft, qui tendunt ſecundum ordinem ſignorum: Vt Hexagonus AC, & quadratus AD, & tri gonus A E.Dextri, qui ab eo figno à quo incipimus retrò funt, id eft, qui tendunt contra ordinem ſignorum: Vt ſextilis AB, & reliqui qui dextrorfum deſcedunt. Hinc patet primò, quòd radius A C dicitur ſiniſter reſpectu A,& dexter refpe&u B. Secundò,quòd ſtellæ poſitæ in ſuperiore hemiſphærio radiatione ſiniſtra:in inferiore verò radiatione dextra horoſcopum illuminent, fiue hunc ex ſexageno, fiue quadrato, fiue triangulo videant. Hicqueſtio exoritur, an radij dextri cum ſiniſtris eiufdem fint poteſtatis,an verò altera forma, viribus ſuperet alteram? Reſpondeo per diſtributione. Radij aut ad
translation: FIRST. 53. as you see in the diagram, with six lines emanating from one center, which, when drawn in a circle, divide it into six equal parts. This aspect is considered moderately good, because the signs contemplating each other at this interval agree in sex and nature. 2. This is closely followed by the square, that is, the square aspect. And it looks at the breast: so called because it comprehends the fourth part of the Zodiac, that is, three signs or P.90., which you see described in the figure, etc., by the straight line AD, and marked with a tetragon, which, inscribed in a circle, receives three signs on each side. This aspect is judged moderately bad, because the two signs at such an interval disagree in sex and nature. 3. The square is followed by the trine, that is, the trine aspect, which contains the third part of the Zodiac, that is, P.120.: and it is described in the diagram by the straight line AE, and marked by an equilateral triangle A, which, when transferred to the inner circle, receives 4 signs on each side. This aspect is also judged good, partly because of its efficacy, partly because the three signs distant by such an interval agree in sex and nature. 4. The trine is followed by the diameter, that is, the diametral or opposite aspect, which contains half of the circle, that is, six signs, P. 180.: and it is described in the diagram by the straight line AF, and marked by two opposite orbs. This aspect is considered egregiously bad, not because of the conflict of natures in the signs, but rather by reason of opposition: for those things which are opposite and most distant, are most inimical to each other, and in every genus are contrary to each other. There are therefore two causes of disagreement in aspects: one, and most principal, is the conflict of signs in nature and sex: the other, and principal, is the greatest distance. Thus in the square ray, since the distance is moderate, and the contrariety is only in the nature and sex of the signs, the disagreement is less. And when the radiations do not differ greatly, they do not disagree, but agree moderately because of the similarity of natures and sex in the dodecatemoria: but opposite signs, since they differ most by 15. of the third element of Euclid, therefore, even if they agree in nature and sex, they nevertheless disagree most because of the greatest distance: just as both are masculine and diurnal, and both are feminine and nocturnal, and yet they are opposite and disagreeing, G3 - Digitized by Google 54 DIFFERENCE disagreeing, the cause of which disagreement is their greatest distance, and not diversity or contrariety in sex and nature, which is absolutely true: for it is manifest that opposite signs, which agree in sex and nature, ought to be believed to fight most and more than squares. What some have handed down, that opposite signs disagree with each other because they are composed of two square aspects, in which way the diameter also consists of two square radiations, namely and, then and, does not proceed. For what disagrees with one, rather agrees with it than disagrees. Moreover, since the opposite radiation also consists of two agreeing ones, namely hexagonal and trigonal: according to this reasoning, the opposition of signs ought to be judged most harmonious, which is absurd. Haly thinks that opposite signs are harmonious for two reasons. The first is, that when one ascends, the other descends, and vice versa: the second, that when it is in one of them, it makes a time contrary to that when it is in the other: just as when it is in, it makes a humid time, in dry. But from the latter cause, this absurdity would follow, that and would be disharmonious, since is no less a vernal sign than the sign II, to which it is opposed. Therefore, the reason previously explained, for strengthening the doctrine of radiations, is more potent, and agrees with Ptolemy's opinion. His words on this matter are these: "The trine and sextile aspects are considered harmonious, because they are composed of homogeneous dodecatemoria, that is, either all feminine or all masculine. But the square and opposite aspects are disharmonious, because they are constituted by dissimilar places." From these words it is also gathered, that the agreement of signs in hexagonal and trigonal aspects, and the disagreement in square and opposite radiations, is not to be understood of temperament and mixture, as the Arabs wish, but only of the sex and nature of the signs, so that signs and II, although joined by hexagonal radiation, are not of the same temperament, Digitized by Google FIRST. 55 temperament, but one is humid, the other dry. Similarly, trine signs agree not in temperament, but in nature and sex, as said above. For all are masculine and diurnal, but the sign is humid and hot, and is hot and dry, is dry and cold, etc. But if someone objects to Ptolemy, that signs which are masculine and diurnal, are also hot and dry: and those which are feminine and nocturnal, are also cold and humid, is not this rightly thought? To this objection we must answer, that such a consequence is by no means necessary, since the condition of time and sex does not arise from the primary qualities. For the diurnal and nocturnal state proceeds from the masculine sex: but the masculine and feminine sex itself is derived from a more efficacious or inefficacious action. Finally, this efficacy flows from the principle of the most robust motion, which exists near the equinoctial. Ptolemy testifies to this in book 1, chapter on masculine and feminine signs, with these words: "The equinoctial circle, described through V and, produces the first and most powerful motion of all." From these, therefore, in continuous series, feminine signs are subjected to masculine, that is, and this virtue is communicated to II and. For these thus look at each other from both principles, namely and a convenient aspect, that is, * and also from themselves. These things being so, it is clear, first, that the sex and diurnal nocturnal condition of the signs derive their origin from motion and not from qualities. Secondly, that not all masculine and diurnal dodecatemoria are rightly said to be hot and dry, nor feminine and nocturnal, humid and cold. 1. Conjunction is numbered among the aspects, which is marked by a single orb, so that the coitus of two bodies in the same line may be signified. From what has been said, it is also clear that the harmful and threatening aspects are the square and opposition: the benevolent ones are the trine and sextile: and the conjunction is indifferent, that is, good with good, bad with bad. As for their strength, the conjunction is the most robust and efficacious of all, after the opposition, because, as appears in concave mirrors, lines passing through the center are the most valid of all, and because Digitized by Google 56 DIFFERENCE that those proceeding from fewer sides are always more efficacious than those from many sides. The third place is held by the square and trine, which are considered almost equal in power. The last and weakest radiation is that which comes from the sextile. Ptolemy adduces two other reasons for radiations, one Geometrical, the other Musical, which I now omit for brevity's sake, and because more faith is given to radiations from experience than for any reasons. All rays, moreover, except the opposite ones, are double, sinister and dexter. Sinister are those which exist before the sign from which we begin, that is, which tend according to the order of the signs: As Hexagon AC, and square AD, and trine AE. Dexter are those which are behind the sign from which we begin, that is, which tend against the order of the signs: As sextile AB, and the rest which descend to the right. Hence it is clear, first, that ray AC is called sinister with respect to A, and dexter with respect to B. Secondly, that stars placed in the upper hemisphere illuminate the horoscope with sinister radiation: but in the lower with dexter radiation, whether they see it from a sextile, or a square, or a trine. translation: Let us establish a celestial figure for the 47th minute of the 3rd hour after noon on May 21, 1527, for a region with a latitude of 41 degrees. First, from what has been said, it is clear that the political time defined by us requires no reduction, since, besides the 21 completed days of May, it counts 3 hours and 47 minutes from noon, and therefore it perfectly agrees with the astronomical time sought. With these things thus established, the next step is to extract the Sun's position corresponding to the said time from the tables of motions or ephemerides, and its right ascension from the tables of right ascensions. Having done this, we will find the Sun to be at 8 degrees, 53 minutes, 28 seconds of Gemini, and its right ascension to be 67 degrees, 10 minutes. To this, we now add the arc of the Equator corresponding to the 3 hours and 47 minutes elapsed from noon, namely 56 degrees, 45 minutes. By doing this, we will produce the right ascension of the Midheaven or the Tenth House, approximately 123 degrees, 55 minutes. Continuously adding 30 degrees five times to this, we will find the oblique ascensions of the remaining five eastern houses as follows: Midheaven: 123 degrees, 55 minutes, Pole 39 degrees, 30 minutes. Eleventh: 153 degrees, 55 minutes, Pole 24 degrees, 30 minutes. Twelfth: 183 degrees, 55 minutes, Pole 37 degrees, 30 minutes. First: 213 degrees, 55 minutes, Pole 41 degrees, 30 minutes. Second: 243 degrees, 55 minutes, Pole 32 degrees, 30 minutes. Third: 273 degrees, 55 minutes, Pole 24 degrees. Now, what remains is to extract the parts of the ecliptic corresponding to these house ascensions from specific latitude tables that serve each. First, by entering the right ascension of the Tenth House, having 123 degrees, 55 minutes, into the area of the table of right ascensions, we will find that it corresponds to approximately 1 degree, 39 minutes of Leo on the left side of the table, assuming a proportional part, which should always be done whenever the ascension number is found exactly in the table area. Similarly, by entering the oblique ascension of the First House, having 213 degrees, 15 minutes, into the table of oblique ascensions constructed for the natal location, that is, for a latitude of 41 degrees, we will find that it corresponds to 26 degrees, 44 minutes of Libra, which should be written on the eastern horizon or on the cusp of the First House. Furthermore, we have previously warned that in this example, the table constructed for a latitude of 24 degrees serves the ascensions of the Eleventh and Third Houses without obvious error. Therefore, in it, for the oblique ascension of the Eleventh, which is 153 degrees, 55 minutes, you will find 6 degrees, 17 minutes of Virgo corresponding. And for the oblique ascension of the Third House, which is 273 degrees, 55 minutes, in the same table, 23 degrees, 26 minutes of Sagittarius correspond. Finally, since the table of oblique ascensions constructed for a latitude of 37 degrees serves the oblique ascensions of the Second and Twelfth Houses, you will find in it, first, for the oblique ascension of the Twelfth House, which is 183 degrees, 55 minutes, 3 degrees, 13 minutes of Libra corresponding. And finally, for the oblique ascension of the Second House, which is 243 degrees, 55 minutes, in the same table, 21 degrees, 55 minutes of Scorpio correspond. You now have calculated the beginnings of the six eastern houses originating from the Midheaven, to which, in the corresponding parts of the opposite signs, the beginnings of the remaining six western houses must be placed. For example, in our case, since 1 degree, 39 minutes of Leo falls on the Midheaven, the beginning of the Fourth House will be placed at 1 degree, 39 minutes of Aquarius. And since we found 26 degrees, 44 minutes of Libra on the cusp of the First House, the Seventh House, which is opposite the ascendant, will begin at 26 degrees, 44 minutes of Aries. Similarly, the beginnings of the remaining four houses, that is, the Fifth, Sixth, Eighth, and Ninth, must be established diametrically opposite the Eleventh, Twelfth, Second, and Third, as is evident in the following description of the celestial chart, to which we have also added the Part of Fortune and the longitudes of all wandering stars. How these are to be extracted from ephemerides for the meridian of any city, we will discuss shortly. Although we should have omitted the more exact circle of tables here, as in the first rudiments of calculations, we will briefly touch upon it, since it is absolutely necessary for establishing exact stellar epochs.

Secundæ verò domui adſcribe ſimilem gradum ſigni ſequentis, & fic deinceps nullo ſigno omiffo donec figura com pleatur,procedas. Sed per aſcenſione rectam medij cæli ex ta bula aſcenſionŭ rectarú locus Eclipticæ correſpondens excer pēdus eſt, qui in faſtigio figuræ deſignatus cor cæli vocať. Idé thema cœli atq; ad eundé locú extrudú iuxta modú equalem. 123.54. 26.44. 28.21 20 25.за Cor cali. 1.39.2. 26-44m 27,53 4.27.12 44 25.30 6.44 Digitized by Google Lits.8.0 4. ==End of OCR for page 3== ==Start of OCR for page 4== : 136 DIFFERENTIA 'DE CONSTRUCTIONE FIGURAE COEleftis iuxta modum rationalem Montere gij ex tabulis æquationis duodecim domorum. PRIMO videndum eſt, vt fi tempus propofitum ciuileſsit, in aſtronomicum tempus modo paulò antè expofito transformetur. Secundò ad meridiem diei completi locus Solis ex tabulis motuum vel Ephemeride excerpendus eſt. Tertiò diaŭ locu, ſeu potiùs gradú Solis completű in tabula domorum cæli ad eleuatiõem tui loci, vel ad proximè minoré maioremúe tuo loco latitudinem condita, fub titulo decima domus inquire. Quo facto deprehendes ſitum cæli, quæq; ſigniferi partes fint in ſingularum domorum oriétalium initis tempore meridiei. Quocirca ſi tempus tuum præcisè in meridiem congruit, ſtatim initia dictarum ſex domorú verſus dextram ſe offerent, nec quicquam amplius ex tabula inueſtigandum reſtabit. Verùm ſi diei ad meridié completa horę velhorarum ſcrupula adhæſerint, tum in eodem loco tabulæ tempus ad latus ſiniſtrum poſitum deſcribe, cui ſemperpro ſcrupulis graduum 15. Solis gradibus adhærentibus, fingula horarum Icrupula(nam 15.fcrupula equatoris exoriuntur ſpatio temporis vnius ſcrupuli)adijcias, Quartò tempori ex tabula deſcripto iungas tempus tuum in horis & fcrupulis, ad quod cæli domos fabricare conſtitui- ſti: id diligenter obſeruas, vt pro fingulis ſex adiectis horis, ſemper fingula temporis ſcrupula inſuper adijcias æquationis dierum gratia: nam vna cœli reuolutio horis 24. ſcrup.4. ferè conficitur.Obferuabis etiam regulam calculi Aſtronomici, quæ monet, quandocunque ex huiusmodi horarum compoſitione emerferit tempus, quod 24.horas excedat, eafdem 24. horas ex tota congerie eſſe remouendas, & omnino abijciendas, & reliquum feruandum. Quinto has horas & fcrupula, quæ ex coitu duorum tempo. rum exiſtunt, aut poſt remotionem 24. horarum reſtant,quzre in tabula domorum ad latitudinem regionis tuæ condita in margiDigitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 4== ==Start of OCR for page 5== 1 ! PRIMA. 137 margine eius finiftro, nam è regione verſus dextram offerent ſe initia ſeu cuſpides ſex domiciliorum cæli, nempe decima, vndecimz, duodecime,prima, ſecuda & tertiæ domorum. Hoc autem modo hemiſpherio orientali deſcripto, ſex ſtationes occidentales ex oppoſitis ſignis cognofcuntur. In propoſito exemplo locus Solis eſt part.8.fcru.54.II, qui in hoc negotio pro nono gradu completo tutoæſtimatur. Quero ergo hunc nonum gradum II in tabula ad latitudinem part.42.condita(nam viciniorem Ephemerides vulgate non habent) ſub titulo decimz domus, & reperio ad ſiniſtram partem tabulæ tempus à me ridie elapfum correſpondens, nempe horas 4.fc.29.quziuncta noſtro tempori pomeridiano, hoc eſt, horis 3.fc.47.efficiuntho ras 8.fcru.16.qui numeru
translation: 1527. May 21, 3 hours, 47 minutes after noon. Pole 41. Deviation of Planets from the Ecliptic: Sun 19 minutes, 40 seconds. Moon 4 degrees, 26 minutes, 40 seconds. Mercury 28 minutes. Jupiter 4 minutes. Saturn 2 minutes. Mars 52 minutes. Venus 1 degree, 46 minutes. Dragon's Head 1 degree, 25 minutes, 2 seconds. Dragon's Tail 6 degrees, 5 seconds. Part of Fortune 1 degree, 4 minutes, 46 seconds. Ascendant 23 degrees, 5 minutes.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Erecting a Celestial Figure
text: ==Start of OCR for page 5== 132 DIFFERENTIA ridianam, ſcrupulum 47. anni 1527. labentis figură cæli in latitudine regionis partium 41.conſtituamus. Primò ex his quæ die &a ſunt manifeſtum eſt, tempus politicú à nobis definitú nulla egere reductione:ſiquidem pręter dies 21. maii completos, horas 3.ſc.47.à meridie numerat, atque ob id planè cum tempore aſtronomico quod queritur, conuenit. His ita ſe habentibus, proximű eſt vt locum Solis dicto tempori congruenté ex tabulis motuum aut Ephemeridibus, eiuſdemq; loci Solis aſcenſioné rectă ex tabulis aſcenſionú rectarú depromamus: quo facto, deprehendemus Solem habere p. 8.ſc.53.28 38. II, eiuſq; aſcen- ſionem rectam eſſe part.67.ſc.10. Huic iam adiiciemus arcum Equatoris, qui horis 3. ſc. 47. à meridie elapſis correſpondet, nempe p.56.ſc.45.quo facto producemus aſcenſione rectã medii cæli ſeu decimæ domus par.123.fcr.55.fere. Huic continenter quinq; vicibus gradus 30.adiicientes, reliquorú quinq; orientaliű domiciliorú aſcenſiones obliquas deprehendemus ſic: Aſcenſio Grad. Scrup. Poli ἔαρμα. Partes Eclimedii cæli 123 55 9 pticæ l. 39. 30 82 Vndecima 153 55 24 30. Duodecimæ 183 55 37 30 Prima 213 55 41 30 Secunda 243 55 32 30 Tertia 273 55 24 Iam quod reliquum eſt, partes eclipticæ his aſcenſionibus domorum correſpondétes ex certis latitudinű tabulis, quæ ſingulis feruiűt, depromere debemus. Ac primò aſcenſione rectã decimæ domus, habente part.123.fcr.55.in aream tabulæ aſcenſionum rectarú mittentes, deprehendemus ipſi in ſiniſtro tabulæ latere correſpondere grad.1.ſc. 39. Leonis ferè, aſſumpta parte proportionali, id quod ſemper fieri debet, quotieſcunq; nume rus aſcenſionis in area tabulæ ad vnguem reperitur. Similiter aſcenſionem obliquam primæ domus.habenté par.213.ſc.15.in tabulam Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 5== ==Start of OCR for page 1== : 1 1 コ F PRIMA. :או tabulam aſcenſionum obliquarŭ ad ſitum natalicijloci, hoc eſt, ad latitudinem part.41. conditam mittentes, deprehendemus ei correſpondere part.26.fc.44.Libræ, quæ in horizonte orien tali ſeu in cuſpide primæ domus ſcribēda ſunt.Porrò ſuprà mo nuimus,in hoc exemplo aſcenſionibus vndecimæ & tertiæ do mus ſine errore patente ſeruire tabulam ad latitudinem par.24 conditam.In ea itaq; aſcenſioni obliquæ vndecima, que eſt par tium 153.fcr.55.inuenies correfpondere part.6.fc.17. Virginis. Et aſcenſioni obliquæ domus tertiæ, quæ eſt partium 273.fcru. 55.in eadem tabula correſpondent part.23.fc.26.Sagittarij.Ad extremum cùm aſcenſionibus obliquis ſecundæ & duodecimę domus ſeruiat tabula aſtéfionum obliquarumad latitudinem partium 37.códita, inuenicin ea primò afcenfioni obliquæ do mus duodecimæ, quæ eſt partiú 183.fc.55.correſpondere par.3. fc.13.Libre. Et deniq; aſcenſioni obliquz domus ſecúdz, que eſt part.243.fc.55.correſpondent in eadem tabulapart.21.fcr. 55.Scorpij. Habes ia ſupputata principia ſex domorú orientalium à medio cœli originem trahétium, quibus è regione in ſignorum oppoſitorum partibus confimilibus initia reliquarú ſex domorum occidentalium ſtatuenda funt. Veluti in noſtro exemplo cùm in culmen cœli gradus 1. fcrup. 39. Leonis incidant, erit principium quartæ domus ſtatuendum in gradu 1.fc. 39.Aquarij. Et cùm in cuſpide primę domus deprehenderimus part.26.fc.44.Libra, ſeptima domus, que eſt oppoſita horofca po,inchoenda erit à grad.26.fc. 44.Arietis:&militerq; initia reliquarum quatuor domorum, hoc eſt, quinte, ſexte, octauz & nonæ ex diametro vndecima, duodecima, ſecunde & tertię ſta tuenda funt, ficut patet in ſequenti thematis cæli deſcriptione, cui etiam fortem & loca longitudinum omnium errantium fiderum inferuimus. Ea verò quemadmodum ex Ephemeridi bus ad meridianum cuiufuis ciuitatis depromenda ſint,pauld poſt dicemus.Exatiorem circulum tabularum, etſi hic veluti in primis ſupputationum rudimentis meritò diſsimulare debe bebamus: tamen eum quoque, ſiquidem ad exactas ſtellarum epochas conſtituendas planè eft neceſſarius, breuifsimè per- ſtringemus. : 1 R3 Deuia : Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 1== ==Start of OCR for page 2== 213 345 8625.3014 tato DIFFERENTIA 123.55 25.53. 15.42854 1527. translation: On erecting a celestial figure from the tables of Monteregius' directions according to the equal house system, from which the judgments of the Arabs are said to depend. You should have the right ascension of the Midheaven from the preceding doctrine, and to it add 90 degrees, and you will complete the oblique ascension of the rising cardinal point, from which, from the table of your climate, extract the corresponding degree of the ecliptic, as has been said, and assign it to the cusp of the First House. To the Second House, assign a similar degree of the following sign, and so on, proceeding without omitting any sign until the figure is completed. But from the right ascension of the Midheaven, the corresponding place of the Ecliptic must be extracted from the table of right ascensions, which, designated at the top of the figure, is called the Heart of Heaven. The same celestial chart and for the same place to be erected according to the equal house system.

Solis gradibus adhærentibus, fingula horarum Icrupula(nam 15.fcrupula equatoris exoriuntur ſpatio temporis vnius ſcrupuli)adijcias, Quartò tempori ex tabula deſcripto iungas tempus tuum in horis & fcrupulis, ad quod cæli domos fabricare conſtitui- ſti: id diligenter obſeruas, vt pro fingulis ſex adiectis horis, ſemper fingula temporis ſcrupula inſuper adijcias æquationis dierum gratia: nam vna cœli reuolutio horis 24. ſcrup.4. ferè conficitur.Obferuabis etiam regulam calculi Aſtronomici, quæ monet, quandocunque ex huiusmodi horarum compoſitione emerferit tempus, quod 24.horas excedat, eafdem 24. horas ex tota congerie eſſe remouendas, & omnino abijciendas, & reliquum feruandum. Quinto has horas & fcrupula, quæ ex coitu duorum tempo. rum exiſtunt, aut poſt remotionem 24. horarum reſtant,quzre in tabula domorum ad latitudinem regionis tuæ condita in margiDigitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 4== ==Start of OCR for page 5== 1 ! PRIMA. 137 margine eius finiftro, nam è regione verſus dextram offerent ſe initia ſeu cuſpides ſex domiciliorum cæli, nempe decima, vndecimz, duodecime,prima, ſecuda & tertiæ domorum. Hoc autem modo hemiſpherio orientali deſcripto, ſex ſtationes occidentales ex oppoſitis ſignis cognofcuntur. In propoſito exemplo locus Solis eſt part.8.fcru.54.II, qui in hoc negotio pro nono gradu completo tutoæſtimatur. Quero ergo hunc nonum gradum II in tabula ad latitudinem part.42.condita(nam viciniorem Ephemerides vulgate non habent) ſub titulo decimz domus, & reperio ad ſiniſtram partem tabulæ tempus à me ridie elapfum correſpondens, nempe horas 4.fc.29.quziuncta noſtro tempori pomeridiano, hoc eſt, horis 3.fc.47.efficiuntho ras 8.fcru.16.qui numeru
translation: ON THE CONSTRUCTION OF A CELESTIAL FIGURE according to the rational method of Monteregius from the tables of the equation of the twelve houses. First, it must be seen that if the proposed time is civil, it should be transformed into astronomical time in the manner explained a little earlier. Second, the Sun's place for the completed day's meridian must be extracted from the tables of motions or the ephemeris. Third, inquire about the said place, or rather the completed degree of the Sun, in the table of the houses of the sky for the elevation of your location, or for the nearest lesser or greater latitude to your location, under the title of the tenth house. Having done this, you will find the position of the sky and which parts of the zodiac are at the beginnings of the individual eastern houses at the time of the meridian. Therefore, if your time precisely coincides with the meridian, the beginnings of the said six houses will immediately present themselves to the right, and nothing further will remain to be investigated from the table. But if hours or minutes of hours are attached to the completed day at the meridian, then in the same place in the table, describe the time placed on the left side, to which you always add individual minutes of hours for every 15 minutes of degrees of the Sun (for 15 minutes of the equator rise in the space of one minute of time). Fourth, to the time described from the table, add your time in hours and minutes, for which you have decided to construct the houses of the sky: diligently observe this, that for every six added hours, you always add individual minutes of time for the sake of the equation of days, for one revolution of the sky is completed in approximately 24 hours and 4 minutes. You will also observe the rule of astronomical calculation, which warns that whenever, from such a composition of hours, a time emerges that exceeds 24 hours, those same 24 hours must be removed from the total aggregate and completely discarded, and the remainder preserved. Fifth, these hours and minutes, which result from the combination of the two times, or remain after the removal of 24 hours, look for them in the table of houses constructed for the latitude of your region in its left margin.

translation: In its left margin, for the regions to the right, the beginnings or cusps of the six houses of the sky will present themselves, namely the tenth, eleventh, twelfth, first, second, and third houses. However, with the eastern hemisphere thus described, the six western stations are known from the opposite signs. In the proposed example, the Sun's place is 8 degrees, 54 minutes of Gemini, which in this matter is safely estimated as the completed ninth degree. I therefore look for this ninth degree of Gemini in the table constructed for a latitude of 42 degrees (for the common ephemerides do not have a closer one) under the title of the tenth house, and I find on the left side of the table the corresponding time elapsed from noon, namely 4 hours, 29 minutes, which, added to our afternoon time, that is, 3 hours, 47 minutes, makes 8 hours, 16 minutes, which number...

translation: In the left part of the table, the time is found not exactly, but approximately greater, that is, 28 hours, 17 minutes. From this region, the eastern hemisphere is described, namely, the cusp of the tenth house is 2 degrees, 2 minutes; the eleventh house is 7 degrees, 0 minutes; the twelfth house is 7 degrees, 0 minutes; the first house is 27 degrees, 2 minutes; the second house is 22 degrees, 0 minutes; the third house is 24 degrees, 0 minutes. The remaining six western houses are known from the opposite signs, as I said. The method of erecting a figure of the heavens according to the author's opinion follows. At the given time, first find the right ascension of the Midheaven, which, as we said, is composed of the right ascension of the Sun's place and the degrees of the equinoctial generated by the time elapsed from noon. Then, to this right ascension, add a quadrant of the circle, that is, 90 parts, and the oblique ascension of the Ascendant will result. From the table constructed for the latitude of your place, extract the corresponding part of the ecliptic, and then calculate the semi-diurnal and semi-nocturnal arcs of that already found part of the ecliptic, and take the third part of each calculated arc. Now, to establish the cusps of the six eastern houses, first, to the right ascension of the Midheaven, add in order three times the third part of the semi-diurnal arc, so that the right ascensions of the eleventh, twelfth, and first houses are produced. Then, to the right ascension of the first house, add in order twice the third part of the semi-nocturnal arc, and you will establish the right ascensions of the second and third houses. Finally, since the horoscoping part is known from the preceding, it remains for us to extract the appropriate parts for the remaining five houses, for all of which the table of right ascensions serves. Having found these, we will have the centers of the eastern houses readily available, and we will establish the remaining six western houses through the opposite points as before. As in our example, the right ascension of the Midheaven is 123 parts, 55 minutes. If we add a quadrant of the circle to this, that is, 90 parts, we will find the oblique ascension of the Ascendant to be 213 parts, 55 minutes. To this, in the table constructed for a latitude of 41 parts, corresponds 26 parts, 44 minutes of Libra. The semi-diurnal arc of this horoscoping part is 80 parts, 52 minutes, and its third portion is approximately 26 parts, 57 minutes. And the semi-nocturnal arc is 99 parts, 8 minutes, and its third portion is approximately 33 parts, 3 minutes. These being so, if you add in order to the right ascension of the Midheaven 26 parts, 47 minutes, which is the third portion of the semi-diurnal arc of the horoscoping part, you will produce the right ascension of the eleventh, twelfth, and first houses. And indeed, for the eleventh, 150 parts, 42 minutes; for the twelfth, 178 parts, 29 minutes; and finally, for the first, 205 parts, 16 minutes. Now, if the third part of the semi-nocturnal arc, containing 33 parts, 3 minutes, is added twice in order to the right ascension of the first house, the right ascension of the second and third houses will result. And indeed, for the second, 238 parts, 19 minutes; for the third, 271 parts, 22 minutes. Moreover, in the table of right ascensions, to the right ascension of the eleventh station corresponds 28 parts, 45 minutes of Gemini; to the twelfth, 28 parts, 40 minutes of Virgo; to the second, 0 parts, 30 minutes of Cancer; and finally, to the third, 1 part, 14 minutes of Leo. The culminating and horoscoping parts of the Ecliptic never change, but are always found and remain the same in all methods, that is, those of Regiomontanus, the Arabs, the author, and Campanus. I omit here the method of constructing figures of the heavens according to Campanus and Gazulus because it is erroneous. I could have omitted the author's method for a similar reason, had it not been necessary for the exposition of the text. For here we only approve the opinion of Regiomontanus, which is also Ptolemy's, or certainly approaches his opinion as closely as possible. If there is anyone who also wishes to reduce Campanus's speculation on the twelve houses to numbers, let him consult problem 16 of Regiomontanus. The first figure of the author. May 21, 3 hours, 47 minutes past noon. Pole 41. Regarding the motions of the seven planets and the head and tail of the Dragon, to be found for any hour of the day and placed in the figure of the heavens. A complete figure of the heavens consists, first, of the description of the houses, namely, the 4 cardinal, 4 succedent, and 4 cadent houses. Second, of the inscription of the true places of the seven wandering stars, also the head and tail of the Dragon. Third, the notation of fixed stars of the first and second magnitude, whose latitude from the Ecliptic is not great. Fourth and finally, of the inscription of the lots and other parts. And thus far it has been said how, for any moment of time, the figure of the heavens, according to the opinion of both Regiomontanus and other astrologers, should be formed, so that it may be seen which signs are above the earth and which lie below the earth, and which parts of the dodecatemoria fall on the cusps of the individual houses. It remains, therefore, for us to briefly complete the remaining members of the celestial figure. It has been said above that all motions written in the usual ephemerides are calculated for the meridian of the day to which their number adheres. However, since the same motions vary at every moment of the days due to the continuous motion of the spheres, it is necessary to present a method by which the motion of any planet for any moment of time should be calculated from the ephemeris. Therefore, take the motions of the planets that are assigned opposite your proposed day, and write them out in order. Then, immediately place above them the motions of the same planets for the immediately following day, and subtract the smaller from the larger, for the excess or the difference of the numbers will show the diurnal motions of the planets, as is evident in the following example of the proposed nativity. May 22, 9 degrees, 46 minutes, 9 degrees, 10 minutes, 28 seconds, 23 degrees, 27 minutes, 29 seconds, 29 degrees, 1 minute, 27 seconds, 504, 21 degrees, 25 minutes, 22 seconds. May 21, 8 degrees, 44 minutes, 25 seconds, 6 degrees, 28 minutes, 16 seconds, 27 degrees, 16 minutes, 29 seconds, 21 degrees, 26 minutes, 38 seconds, 3 degrees, 12 minutes, 25 seconds. Super. or 0 degrees, 57 minutes, 14 seconds, 14 degrees, 40 minutes, 40 seconds, 4 degrees, 7 minutes, 0 seconds, 13 degrees, 0 minutes, 2001, 12 degrees, 1 minute, 9 seconds, 0 degrees, 3 minutes, 1 second. Diurnal motion. After this, one must proceed according to the rule of proportions in this way: In the first place, set 24 hours, into which the entire natural day is divided. In the second place, place the differences of the motions or the diurnal motions. The third place should be occupied by the hours elapsed from noon, together with the minutes, for which the motion is proposed to be sought. The fourth number, which is elicited from the progression of the rule of proportions, is to be added to the motion of your complete day if the planet is direct, that is, when its motion written in the ephemeris increases. But if it is retrograde, that is, if its motion written in the ephemeris decreases, it must be subtracted from the same, so that the motion corresponding to the proposed time may result. Here it must be observed that the Sun and Moon are swifter than the other wanderers, and therefore never retrograde. Likewise, the Head of the Dragon always progresses in antecedents.
