translation: Having observed all these things, add the complete month of May, 3 hours, 47 minutes. You will collect the true longitudes of the planets from Stoffler's ephemerides, calculated as follows: Sun 8 degrees, 58 minutes; Moon 27 degrees, 19 minutes; Mercury 28 degrees, 17 minutes; Venus 27 degrees, 18 minutes; Mars 29 degrees, 18 minutes; Jupiter 26 degrees, 49 minutes; Saturn 3 degrees, 23 minutes; Dragon's Head 25 degrees, 25 minutes. Lords: Aquarius, Aries, Gemini, Scorpio, Gemini, Cancer, Sagittarius. However, these motions, collected in this manner, still need to be corrected for two reasons. One is the distance between meridians, the other is the equation of days. But just as both are necessary for scrutinizing the true motion of the Moon, so in collecting the motions of the other planets, they are safely neglected due to their slow progression, especially when we extract the motions of the stars from ephemerides not adapted to the Prutenic but to the Alphonsine calculation, and therefore showing the positions of the stars not precisely, but approximately. Therefore, leaving aside the six planets, you must correct the Moon's course as follows: First, in the tables of regions, which are commonly added to ephemerides, under the character (region), the minutes of degrees are noted for any given place, by which the Moon's motion, taken from the ephemeris for the proposed time, must be augmented or diminished due to the meridians or the position of the place, as indicated by the letters A and M added to these minutes. The table of provinces and more recent towns, which precedes Stoffler's ephemerides, does not have our example's location inserted. Therefore, we must refer to a place in Spain whose longitude agrees with or at least does not differ much from the longitude of Valladolid, such as the longitude of the city of Cordoba, from whose region under the character (we find) 42 minutes, which must be added to the Moon's true motion, because the meridian described through Cordoba is so much more western than the Ulm meridian, for which Stoffler compiled the ephemerides. Finally, the minutes, which must always be subtracted from the same motion due to the equation of days, will be indicated by the common angle of the following table, if in its left margin you take the Sun's position for the proposed day, and in the head of the table the sign in which the Sun is located. As in the previous example, ten minutes are indicated, which, being subtracted from the Moon's motion, and those which the difference of meridians produced for us being added to it, the Moon's motion, corrected in all ways, becomes 27 degrees, 51 minutes of Aquarius. The table of the Moon's motion follows. 2437 hours must be subtracted, so that the time starting from midnight remains. For example, in our case for the year 1527, 21 days are proposed as completed at noon, with 3 hours, 47 minutes elapsed from noon. Therefore, if you subtract twelve hours from this, you will have the remaining time starting from midnight: 20 days of May, 15 hours, 47 minutes. And this is the first thing worthy of consideration in our calculation. Secondly, this time must be corrected by the difference of meridians, that is, it must be translated to the meridian of the place for which the motions are calculated. You will achieve this if you subtract the difference of longitude or meridians converted into time from the proposed time, when your meridian is more eastern than the meridian of the tables, or if you add it to the same when it is more western, because eastern places count more hours, and therefore they must be subtracted; western places, however, count fewer, and therefore they must be added, so that the time prepared for the meridian of the tables exists. As in our example of Königsberg in Prussia, for whose meridian the Prutenic tables were compiled, the longitude is 46 degrees, 45 minutes, 1 second. The longitude of Valladolid, however, is 8 degrees, 20 minutes. The difference of meridians is therefore 38 degrees, 25 minutes, 1 second, which converted into time makes two hours, 33 minutes, 3 seconds, to be added to the proposed time, because Valladolid, a city in Spain, is so much more western than Königsberg in Prussia. If you wish to use the table of regions for brevity's sake, and immediately calculate the positions of the stars for a given meridian other than the meridian of the tables, which is what we are doing here, look for your location in the catalog of places, or one closer to it, and add the ascribed time difference to your time if the letter S is present, or subtract it if A is present, so that in this matter of ours you do the opposite of the notes themselves. The location of our example is not noted in the Prutenic catalog, but nearby locations, such as Toledo, Cordoba, Compostela, are, for which a nearly prior time difference is found with the letter S indicating that such a difference must be added to our time for the hours to be adapted to the meridian of Königsberg, contrary to what the notes actually signify. For in the catalog of notable places, the more eastern locations of the tables have the note of addition A, while the more western ones have the note S. For these notes are specifically intended for such calculations, so that the same motion calculated for the meridian of the tables corresponds to different hours of another meridian. Thirdly, by the Sun's position, which the ephemerides will always easily show you, the prothaphaeresis or equation of days should be instituted. As in our example, because the ephemerides showed the Sun's position to be almost in 9 degrees of Gemini, therefore from the first Canon of the Equation of Natural Days, the prothaphaeresis of 9 minutes of days is collected, designated by the title S indicating subtraction. For these titles A and S have a place when apparent time is converted into equality, but the opposite must be done with these titles when equal time is in turn to be converted into apparent. Therefore, the time of our example, corrected both for the meridians and for the equation of days, is as follows: For the complete year 1526, 4 complete months, 20 days, 18 hours, 11 minutes, 4 seconds. Finally, the Julian time, reduced and conformed to astronomical equality by the methods we have discussed, must be adapted to the Alphonsine Abacus from the congruent table, which is found on folio 7 and following. For the Alphonsine method, in which, for collecting the equal motions of the stars, Julian time is reduced to sexagenaries of days and minutes, is shorter and, as it seems, more suitable than others. However, you should seek fuller instruction for this part from the third Prutenic precept, according to whose tenor we have preferred to describe only the diagram of our example here, rather than obscure the matter with a verbose narration in an unsuitable place. Sexagenaries 3, 2, 1. Days 1, 2. Year 1500: 2, 32, 11, 15, 2, 6, 8, 38, 16. Common months 4: 3, 0. Days 20: 20. Hours 18: 45 minutes, 1 second. 11 minutes, 27 seconds, 30 degrees. 2, 10, 250. Sum: 2, 34, 51, 5, 45, 27, 55. Therefore, the time suitable for Alphonsine calculation will be 2 third sexagenaries, 34 second sexagenaries, 51 first sexagenaries, 5 days, 45 minutes, 27 seconds, 15 thirds. In this way, given the time for use.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Calculating Equinoctial Precession
text: PRIM A. 145 vfum calculi æqualium motuum accommodato, Alphonfinam formam colligendi medios motus ſtellarum vinere debes. Eam verò hic non defcribo, cum dilucidè & exemplis ſit demonſtra ta in octauo præcepto Prutenico. Sequitur ἐπιλογισμός τ' ἰσημβρίας προηγήσεως, id eft, verę præceſsionis æquino&ij ſupputatio. Alphonſus deriuat ſhas ſupputationes ab interſectione verna, quam omninò mutari notius iam eſt, quàm vt verbofiùs hic probari debeat. Hinc eſt cernere cauſam, ob quam Copernicus & Eraſmus puncta æquino&lorum, ficut nodum Lunæ Anabibazonta & Catabibazonta in præcedentia agi ſtatuunt, & imaginantur duo æquinoctia, medium videlicet & verum: illud quidem quod regulariter, hoc verò quod ἀνομάλωςà capite Arietis ſtellati retrocedat. Eſt igitur præcefsio equinoctiorum arcus Eclipticæ interceptus inter duos circulos magnos, quorum vterque quidem ducitur per polos Zodiaci, ſed alter per prima ſtellam Arietis ſtellati,alter verò per punctum equinoctii feu veri ſeu medii ducitur. Differentia aut autem inter medium & apparens æquinoctium vocatur προθαφαίρεσις equinoctiorum, quæ maxima eſt 71. fcrupulorum primorum, 22 ſcru. ſecund. vt videre eſt in Canone Proſtaphærefeon octaurſphæra.Cùm itaque puncta equinoctiorum non ſint fixa, rectius motus exor diuntur à capite Arietis ſtellati, principio videlicet certo & in octaua ſphera fixo. Deinde verò precessionem equinoctiorum ad motus ſtellarum inuentos addunt, vt vera ipſarum loca ab æquino&io verno colligant. Vnde perfpicuum eſt, ante omné errantium ſtellarum fupputationem neceſſarium eſſe, vepriùs inueniamus, quot partibus equino&ium apparens à primaſtella Arietis in precedentia recefferit.Calculi autem forma eſt talis:Ad datum tempus rectè preparatum collige mediiæquinoAii verni motum equalem, feu æqualem pręceſsionem addendo eam cum ſua Epocha. Nam in colligendis motibus æqualibus ſemper Epoche Chriſti Dei, vel Alexandri, vel Ceſaris, aut -alterius initii, à quo motus equales deriuantur,premittenda funt. Deinde codem labore excerpe ſimplicem equinoctiorum ἀνομαλίαμ, qua duplata mox ex cóuenienti Canone ProT ſtaphę- 146 DIFFERENTIA ſtaphæreſin equinoctiorum collige, eandemque ſi opus fit per partem proportionalem corrige. Et fi Anomalia duplex ſemicirculo, hoc eſt, partibus 180.minor fuerit, tunc Proſtapheresis ab ęquali motu pręceſsionis fubtrahatur, fi maior, eadem Pro- ſtaphęrefis cum equali motu pręceſsionis equinoctiorum coniūgatut, vt in aggregato reſiduoue vera & apparens equinoctio rum pręceſsio ad datum tempus exeat. Regula ergo Anoma. lię equinoctiorum additionis vel ſubtra&ionis conſentit cum regula additionis & fubtractionis Anomalia Solaris. Iuxta hęc ſupputatio noſtri exempli erit talis, vt ſequitur: Sexag. 1. P. 12. 22. Simplex Aequinoctiorum precessio. o. 26. 50. 37. Anomalia equinoctiorum fimplex. 2. 46. 48. 3. Anomalia duplicata.. 5. 33. 36. 6. Proſtaphęreſis correcta. 0. 0. 31. 44- Ea verò cùm Anomalia duplex ſemicirculum excedat, fimplici equinoctiorum preceſsioni iungenda eft, quo facto, prodit vera equinoctiorum precefsio ſex..Part.27.22 12,21 22 : ἐπιλογισμός τ' ήλιακῆς ψηφοφορίας, id eſt, ſupputatio veri ſeu apparentis motus Solaris. Duos modos fupputandi verum motum Solis tradidit Erafmus in ſuis tabulis. In altero vtitur motu Solis ſimplici, quię- qualiter deriuatur à primo puncto Arietis ſtellati:In altero verò aſſumit motum Solis ab æquinoctio medio deriuatum, qué vocat motum Solis compofitum, eò quòd ſimplici motui tantum adiiciat, quanta eſt equalis equinoctiorum proceſsio. Verùm nos alterum duntaxat modum in quo equales motus à pri ma Arietis ſtella fluunt, eò quòd cum ſequentium ſiderum alculo communis fit, hic breuiter perſtringemus. Eft autem modus calculi talis.. PRIMO habeas in promptu hec quatuor.. 1.. Veram equinoctiorum pręceſsionem.. 2. Anomaliam equinoctiorum fimplicem, quorum vtrunque ex precedenti precessionis equino&iorum fupputatione affumitur.. 3.Acqua PRIMA. 147 3. translation: For the calculation of equal motions, you must use the Alphonsine method for collecting the mean motions of the stars. I do not describe it here, as it is clearly demonstrated with examples in the eighth Prussian precept. There follows the calculation of the true precession of the equinox, that is, the computation of the true precession of the equinox. Alphonsus derives these computations from the vernal intersection, which is now known to have changed completely, more than needs to be proven here at length. Hence it is to be seen the reason why Copernicus and Erasmus state that the points of the equinoxes, as well as the nodes of the Moon, Anabibazonta and Catabibazonta, are in precession, and they imagine two equinoxes, namely the mean and the true: the former indeed which recedes regularly, but the latter which recedes anomalously from the head of the starry Aries. Therefore, the precession of the equinoxes is the arc of the Ecliptic intercepted between two great circles, each of which is drawn through the poles of the Zodiac, but one through the first star of the starry Aries, and the other through the point of the equinox, whether true or mean. The difference between the mean and apparent equinox is called the prothaphaeresis of the equinoxes, which is at most 71 first minutes, 22 second minutes, as can be seen in the Canon of Prosthaphæreses of the eighth sphere. Since therefore the points of the equinoxes are not fixed, motions more correctly begin from the head of the starry Aries, that is, a certain and fixed beginning in the eighth sphere. Then, however, they add the precession of the equinoxes to the discovered motions of the stars, so that their true places from the vernal equinox may be collected. Hence it is clear that before any computation of the wandering stars, it is necessary that we first find out by how many parts the apparent equinox has receded in precession from the first star of Aries. The form of the calculation is as follows: For the given time, correctly prepared, collect the equal motion of the mean vernal equinox, or the equal precession, by adding it with its Epoch. For in collecting equal motions, the Epochs of Christ God, or Alexander, or Caesar, or another beginning from which equal motions are derived, must always be prefixed. Then, with the same effort, extract the simple anomaly of the equinoxes, which, when doubled, you then collect the prosthaphæresis of the equinoxes from the convenient Canon of Prosthaphæreses, and correct it by the proportional part if necessary. And if the double anomaly is less than a semicircle, that is, 180 parts, then the Prosthaphæresis is subtracted from the equal motion of precession; if greater, the same Prosthaphæresis is combined with the equal motion of the precession of the equinoxes, so that in the aggregate or remainder the true and apparent precession of the equinoxes for the given time emerges. The rule for the addition or subtraction of the anomaly of the equinoxes therefore agrees with the rule for the addition and subtraction of the Solar Anomaly. According to these, the computation of our example will be as follows: Sexagesimal 1. P. 12. 22. Simple precession of the equinoxes. 0. 26. 50. 37. Simple anomaly of the equinoxes. 2. 46. 48. 3. Doubled anomaly. 5. 33. 36. 6. Corrected Prosthaphæresis. 0. 0. 31. 44. But since this exceeds the semicircle of the double anomaly, it must be added to the simple precession of the equinoxes, after which, the true precession of the equinoxes comes out as six parts, 27.22.12.21.22. The calculation of the solar motion, that is, the computation of the true or apparent solar motion. Erasmus, in his tables, provided two methods for computing the true motion of the Sun. In one, he uses the simple motion of the Sun, which is equally derived from the first point of the starry Aries; in the other, he assumes the motion of the Sun derived from the mean equinox, which he calls the composite motion of the Sun, because he only adds to the simple motion as much as the equal precession of the equinoxes. But we will briefly touch upon only the other method, in which equal motions flow from the first star of Aries, because it is common to the calculation of the subsequent stars. The method of calculation is as follows: FIRST, have these four ready: 1. The true precession of the equinoxes. 2. The simple anomaly of the equinoxes, both of which are taken from the preceding computation of the precession of the equinoxes. 3. The equal motion of the Sun. 4. The motion of the annual anomaly (with their respective established Epochs added in both cases). The motion of the Sun, which depends on the Apogee. SECOND, by means of the simple anomaly of the equinoxes, two things are drawn from the canon of the Prosthaphæreses of the Sun, namely, the prosthaphæresis of the center, and the proportional minutes. This prosthaphæresis of the center, or as it is commonly called, the equation of the argument, is nothing other than the difference between the mean and true Apogee, which, just as it is added before the semicircle of the annual anomaly of the Sun, so it is subtracted from it after the semicircle, to obtain the true and corrected anomaly of the Sun. THIRD, by means of the fourth anomaly, with one and the same entry into the table of the Prosthaphæreses of the Sun, these two are sought: namely, the equation or prosthaphæresis of the orb and the subsequent excess, of which the part corresponding to the proportional minutes reserved a little earlier must always be added to the prosthaphæresis of the orb, so that the absolute or corrected equation may emerge. Finally, if the equated anomaly of the Sun is less than a semicircle, then the corrected equation of the orb is subtracted from the equal motion of the Sun, so that the true place of the Sun from the head of the starry Aries may emerge. To this, if the true precession of the vernal equinox is finally added, discarding the entire cycle if necessary, the true place of the Sun from the apparent equinox will appear in signs, parts, and minutes. As in our assumed example: Sexagesimal P. 1a 2a 3a 4a True precession of the equinoxes 0 27 22 21 Simple anomaly of the equinoxes 2 46. 48 3 51.3 Equal simple motion 0 40.39 27 21 321 Annual anomaly 5 28.58 7 16:53: Prosthaphæresis of the center added 0 0 55 25 33.48. Proportional minutes 0 0 54.35.36 Equated anomaly 5:30 53 32 50 41 T2. The first equation of the orb added 0 0 52 21 38 49. Subsequent excess. 0 0 14 55 0 41. Proportional part of the excess 0 0 13 34 21 to be added. Equated equation of the orb added 0 0 52 34 13 10. Hence the true place from the head 0 41 32 1 34 2. And the true place from the equinox, 1 8 54 22 34 2 apparent. Therefore, the Sun at the time of the proposed nativity falls in 8 degrees, 54 minutes, 22 seconds of Gemini, which motion differs from the motion of Stoffler's ephemerides by almost 5 first minutes. The calculation of the lunar motion. First, these two are assumed from the preceding: 1. True precession of the equinox. 2. Equal simple motion. 1. Elongation of the Moon. Then, from the appropriate tables, two others are extracted: 1. Anomaly of the Moon, or as they call it, the mean argument. (1. Of the second epicycle, or as it is commonly called, the center of the Prosthaphæresis A or S. With these established, the longitude of the Moon from the Sun is immediately doubled, and by that double, that is, by the distance of the center of the epicycle from the Apogee, two things are sought: 2. Proportional minutes. Less is added. And if the doubled elongation, that is, if the center of the Moon is a semicircle, then the Prosthaphæresis of the second epicycle is subtracted from the lunar motion, so that the true motion may be obtained.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Placement and Latitude
text: 158 DIFFERENTIA 'ple&untur, obſervata tamen graduum ſerie & multitudine. Vi luti in noſtro exemplo locus Solis eſt p. 8.fc.54 ra Geminorú. Id dodecatemorion cùm inueniatur in domo octaua & nom, & pars eius octona ſeu potius nouena ſequens cufpidem ods uæ & antecedens cuspidem nonę, incidat in domum o&auam, idcirco locum Solis quoque in o&auam domum inſerere debe mus.Et eadem ratione reliquæ ſtella ſtationibus cæli reliquis in quas incidunt, ſunt inſerende,prout videre eſt in figuris ſoprà deſcriptis. Alij ſubtilius hæc perſequuntur, habita ratione non faltem ſtellarum longitudinis, fed & latitudinis. Hoc quoque modo ſtellę latitudine aliqua præditę ſi in partibus fignorum in medio vel circa medium alicuius domus exiſtentibus reperiantur, non aliter ac ſi latitudine carerent, in eafdem do mos rectè collocabuntur. Quòd ſi planeta habeat effatu digní latitudinem, & præterea prope cuſpidem alicuius domus frrit, fieri poſſe oſtendunt artifices, vt alias in domum ſequentem,alias in præcedentem propter ipſius ad latera diſceſſum incidat. Sed huius rei exactam dijudicationem petere debent qui volent, ex problemate tabularum 21. Monteregij. Mihihęc cura ſuperflua, & quod magis eſt, à ſententia Ptolemzi aliena eſſe videtur.Nam Ptolemeus cùm latitudines planetarum in di `re&ionibus neglexerit, conſentaneum eſt, eum multo magis poſthabuiſſe eaſdem in diſtributione ſtellarum per domesti gurę celi, præfertim cùm vim ſtellis tribuat, vtinfra latiùs pa tebit, non ex loco latitudinis, ſed ex partibusEcliptice, que de finiuntur per lineas, per centra ſtellarum,& per vtrunque polum Eclipticæ tranfeuntes. Veluti Palilicium habet vim circa medietatem quartæ partis Geminorum, non quòd orbis radio rum eius eò vſque ſe extendat, ſed quia linea per centrum ſtel- 12 & polos Zodiaci ducta per dictum punctum Ecliptica tran feat. Atque hæc de conſtitutione figuræ cæleſtis hic dixiffe ſuf ficiat. Nam de forte & alijs partibus inueniendis infra in differentia quinta dicetur. I Quarta autẽ pars quæ est ab aſcendente vſq; ad mo dium cali, quæ est domus duodecima, vndecima & de cima, dicitur quarta pars orientalis, masculina & ad. ueniens, Digitized by Google ! 3 F PRIMA. 159 1 - weniens:fignificat initium vitæ: vocatur puerilis, fan guinea, vernalis. Et pars alia quæ eſt à medio cæli vſque ad gradum occidentalis ſigni, id est, ſeptime, qui est fu per circulum hemifphæry occidentalis , que est domus nona, octaua & ſeptima, est meridiana,fœminea, rece dens: fignificat mediam etatem : & vocatur profectio iuventutis, æstiualis, cholerica. Tertia quoque pars, quæ eſt ab occidente vſq; ad gradum quartæ domus, qui est ſuper circulum meridiei fub terra, quæ est domus fexta, quinta & quarta, eſt occidentalis, maſculina, adueniens: fignificat finem vitæ: & vocatur autumnalis, melancholica, & est fenilis: & eſt mediocris 'etatis. Quar= "hoc eſt, ſe ta pars, que est à quarta domo vsque ad aſcendens, nondu de que domus est tertia, ſecunda, aatque atque aſcendens aſcendens, eſt eſt ſe= les fi ptentrionalis, fæminina,recedens.Significat quod acci dit homini poſt mortem eius, & ad quid deueniet cor. pus eius, vel eius diſpoſitio de dimißa ſubſtantia, aut quid dicetur de eo, ſcilicet vtrùm laudetur vel vitupere tur:Et vocatur hac pars ſenilis, defectiua, flegmatica, hyemalis. 2 Et ille due partes, quæ ſunt à medio cæli vſque ad aſcendens,& ab aſcendente vfque ad quartam domum, faciunt medietatem, quæ vocatur medietas aſcendens. Relique partes, quæ ſunt à quarta domo vsque ad ſeptio mam,& inde ad medium cæli, conſtitumt medietatem deſcendentem vocatam. - 3 Et quicquid fuerit ſuper terram ex circulo, id est, pars nii primi : : Digitized by Google &c. 160 DIFFERENTIA pars circuli ſuperior, dicitur dextra: trque ſub terra la tet, id est, pars inferior,dicitur ſiniſtra. 4 Et aſcendes quarta & ſeptima & decima ala med, id est, quas nos angulos vocam, vt pulchrius fonet. Et ſecunda domus, octaua & quinta & vndecima, fuc. cedentes angulis vocantur. Tertia autem & ſexta,nons & duodecima cadentes ab angulis dicuntur. 5 Cùm ergo Planeta in angulis vel eius ſuccedentibus fuerit, dicitur proficere:& ſi fuerit in cadentibus ab an gulis, dicitur deficere. 6 Et vnaquæq; iſtarum domorum ſignificat aliquid de eſſe hominum. I Quarta Hoc eſt alterum membrum diſtributionis cæli fortuitz. auté pars, Poſtquam enim breuiter attigit cæli partitionem in partes in- ſtabiles, iam earundem partium cum ſubiectis rebus (υνοικείως σιμ ſeu familiaritatem deſcribere intendit. Acorditur hoc negotium à cœli quadrantibus, qui horizonte & meridionali cireulo diſtimguuntur, atque eofdem, vt ſuprà in expofitione parpartiú cæli ſtabiliú fecit, téporibus anni, plagis mundi , ſexui, ata tibus & quatuor humoribus comparat. Sicut autem diurnus ſeu ipſius equinoctialis motus, qui harum inſtabilium partium effector eſt & commutator aſsiduus, motui alteri, ſtellis perfigniferum diſcurrentibus proprio aduerſatur: ita quoque nuTà, meratio feu ſeries ſeu ordo quartarum cæli inſtabilium ſtabilibus opponitur, ind; aduerſam partem mundi protéditur.E- ρώμτες tenim quadrantes ſtabiles, de quibus ſuprà diximus, quos duo ταρτημό circuli per polos figniferi & puncta cardinalia tranſeuntes fa- ρια. ciunt, numerantur per figna conſequentia: hi verò quos meri- τε ό 20, dionalis & finiens complectuntur, per præcedentia.Hinc effiTO' To citur, vt quadras qui eſt ab ortu ad meridiem, primus ſit:qui in ποι καλ ter horizonta occidentalem & cæll medium ponitur, ſecunyeria dus:qui inter occaſum,&noctem mediam, tertius:denique qui imo cæli & finiente orientali concluditur, quartus. Quodad ſexum Digitized by Google ! ! PRIMA. HE ſexum quartarum attinet, concordat hic autor cum Ptolemeo, vterque enim quadrantem primum & tertium maſculinum, ſecundum & quartum fœmininum facit. Verba auté Ptolemzi de hac re ſunt hæc: καὶ ἔτι λέγουσι τού αςέρας θηλαύεθαι ἀρ ξενέθαι πλὰ εσύ πρός τόμ ὁρίζοντα χηματισμός, οἱ μὲν γαρ οῖς ἀλὸ ἀναβολῆς μέχρι μεσγρανήματα ἢ καὶ ἀλὸ δύσεως μέχρι πάς λιμτε αντικομένο μεσγρανήματα ακμαλισμοῖς, ὡς ἀπκλιωλικός ἀῤῥενδθαι: ἐμδιοῖς λοιποῖς δυσὶτεταρτημορίοις, ὡς δυτικές θη λαύεθαι, hoc eſt, Stellas prætereà maſculeſcere & effœminari aiunt pro eo atq; erga horizontis figurationes ſeſe habuerint. Maſculæ enim ſunt veluti orientales in quadrante ab ortuad medium cæli, & ab occaſu ad imum medium cœli: In reliquis verò duobus quadrantibus fœmineæ ſunt tanquam occidenta les. Hinc manifeſtú eſt, artifices velle iuxta mundi ad meridionalem circulum aſcenſum vel ab eodem deſcenſum mutarienergiam agendi & ſexum Planetarum. Primò enim ab horosco po ad medium cæli vſque ſemper altiùs ſubeunt, mox deinde à meridiano cæli faſtigio vergentes ad occaſum deſcendunt:& rurfus ab occaſu imum meridianum accedentes aſcendunt, & denique hinc donecad ortiuum horizonta peruadant, deſcendunt. Hac ratione autor quoque primum & tertium quadrantes aduenientes, reliquos verò duos recedentes nominauit. Hanc viam veram eſſe clarè nobis oftendit opus Lunæ,quæad vtrumuis meridianum ſcandens magno impetu marinarum aquarum affluxus ciet: & econtrariò à meridiano ad occidentalem vel orientalem horizonta delabens, eaſdem remittit atque refluxus efficit. His ita ſe habentibus, perſpicuus eſt vulgatus error, duos cótinuos quadrantes orientalem medietatem con- ſtituentes, maſculos, reliquos verò duos quòd in occidentali medietate ponantur, fœmineos nominantium. Nam medietas mundi ortiuo horizonte diſtincta, nominatur medietas aſcendens, & huic oppoſita deſcendens, reſpectu ſolius punti verticalis, & non ratione vtriuſque meridiani, ſuperioris videlicet & ſubterranei, quod hic potiſsimum ſpectamus. Vtilis eſt huius rei noticia cùm ad cognofcendum robur ſtellarum, tum ve rò ad dijudicandos maſculorum & fœmineorum fœtuú ſignificatores. translation: 158. The difference is filled, however, by observing the series and multitude of degrees. For example, in our case, the Sun's place is 8 degrees, 54 minutes of Gemini. Since this dodecatemorion is found in the eighth house, and its eighth or rather ninth part, following the cusp of the eighth and preceding the cusp of the ninth, falls into the eighth house, we must therefore also insert the Sun's place into the eighth house. And by the same reasoning, the other stars are to be inserted into the remaining stations of the heavens into which they fall, as can be seen in the figures described above. Others pursue these matters more subtly, taking into account not only the longitude but also the latitude of the stars. In this way, if stars endowed with some latitude are found in parts of the signs existing in the middle or around the middle of any house, they will be correctly placed in the same houses, just as if they lacked latitude. But if a planet has a significant latitude, and is moreover near the cusp of some house, artificers show that it can happen that it falls sometimes into the succeeding house, sometimes into the preceding one, due to its deviation to the sides. But those who wish to, should seek an exact judgment of this matter from the 21st problem of Monteregius's tables. To me, this care seems superfluous, and what is more, alien to Ptolemy's opinion. For since Ptolemy neglected the latitudes of the planets in directions, it is consistent that he much more disregarded them in the distribution of stars through the houses of the figure of the heavens, especially since he attributes power to the stars, as will appear more widely below, not from the place of latitude, but from the parts of the Ecliptic, which are defined by lines passing through the centers of the stars and through both poles of the Ecliptic. Just as Palilicium has power around the middle of the fourth part of Gemini, not because the orb of its rays extends so far, but because the line drawn through the center of the star and the poles of the Zodiac passes through the said point of the Ecliptic. And let what has been said here about the constitution of the celestial figure suffice. For concerning the Part of Fortune and other parts to be found, it will be said below in the fifth difference. The fourth part, which is from the ascendant to the midheaven, which is the twelfth, eleventh, and tenth house, is called the eastern, masculine, and approaching fourth part. It signifies the beginning of life; it is called puerile, sanguine, vernal. And the other part, which is from the midheaven to the degree of the western sign, that is, the seventh, which is above the circle of the western hemisphere, which is the ninth, eighth, and seventh house, is southern, feminine, receding; it signifies middle age; and is called the progression of youth, aestival, choleric. The third part also, which is from the west to the degree of the fourth house, which is above the meridian circle below the earth, which is the sixth, fifth, and fourth house, is western, masculine, approaching; it signifies the end of life; and is called autumnal, melancholic, and is senile; and is of middle age. The fourth part, which is from the fourth house to the ascendant, that is, the third, second, and ascendant house, is northern, feminine, receding. It signifies what happens to a person after their death, and to what their body will come, or its disposition from the substance left behind, or what will be said of them, namely, whether they will be praised or blamed. And this part is called senile, defective, phlegmatic, hiemal. And those two parts, which are from the midheaven to the ascendant, and from the ascendant to the fourth house, make up the half which is called the ascending half. The remaining parts, which are from the fourth house to the seventh, and thence to the midheaven, constitute the descending half. And whatever is above the earth from the circle, that is, the upper part of the circle, is called right; whatever lies below the earth, that is, the lower part, is called left. And the ascendant, fourth, seventh, and tenth are called angles, as it sounds more beautiful. And the second, eighth, fifth, and eleventh houses are called succedent to the angles. But the third, sixth, ninth, and twelfth are called cadent from the angles. Therefore, when a planet is in the angles or their succedents, it is said to prosper; and if it is in the cadent houses from the angles, it is said to decline. And each of these houses signifies something about human beings. This is the other member of the distribution of the fortuitous heaven. For after briefly touching upon the division of the heaven into unstable parts, he now intends to describe the familiarity of these parts with the subject matters. He begins this matter with the quadrants of the heaven, which are distinguished by the horizon and the meridian circle, and compares them, as he did above in the exposition of the stable parts of the heaven, to the seasons of the year, the regions of the world, sex, ages, and the four humors. Just as the diurnal or its equinoctial motion, which is the effector and constant commutator of these unstable parts, is opposed to the other motion proper to the stars traversing the zodiac: so also the numbering or series or order of the unstable quadrants of the heaven is opposed to the stable ones, and thence extends to the opposite part of the world. For the stable quadrants, of which we spoke above, which two circles passing through the poles of the zodiac and the cardinal points make, are numbered by consequent signs: but these, which the meridian and the setting encompass, by preceding ones. Hence it results that the quadrant which is from the east to the south is the first: that which is placed between the western horizon and the midheaven, the second: that which is between the west and the middle of the night, the third: finally, that which is concluded by the imum coeli and the eastern setting, the fourth. As for the sex of the quadrants, this author agrees with Ptolemy, for both make the first and third quadrants masculine, the second and fourth feminine. But Ptolemy's words on this matter are these: "And they also say that the stars are feminized and masculinized according to how they are configured with respect to the horizon. For they are masculine as if eastern in the quadrant from the east to the midheaven, and from the west to the imum coeli: but in the other two quadrants they are feminine as if western." Hence it is clear that artificers wish the energy of acting and the sex of the planets to change according to the ascent of the world to the meridian circle or its descent from the same. For first, from the ascendant to the midheaven, they always ascend higher, then from the meridian zenith, inclining towards the west, they descend: and again from the west, approaching the imum coeli, they ascend, and finally from here until they reach the eastern horizon, they descend. By this reasoning, the author also named the first and third quadrants approaching, and the other two receding. The work of the Moon clearly shows us that this way is true, for when it ascends to either meridian, it excites a great influx of marine waters: and conversely, when it descends from the meridian to the western or eastern horizon, it remits them and causes reflux. These things being so, the common error is clear, of calling the two continuous quadrants constituting the eastern half masculine, and the other two feminine because they are placed in the western half. For the half of the world distinguished by the eastern horizon is called the ascending half, and the opposite of this the descending, with respect to the vertical point alone, and not with respect to both meridians, namely the upper and the subterranean, which we particularly consider here. The knowledge of this matter is useful both for recognizing the strength of the stars and for judging the significators of masculine and feminine births. The first quadrant is eastern, the second southern, the third western.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Ptolemy's Quadrants and Qualities
text: 162 DIFFERENTIA tortius occidentalis, quartus borealis etiam à Ptolemezo dicitur. Ac de orientali & occidentali plaga nullum eſt dubium. Sedcurimum cæli perpetuò latens ſeptentrioni perpetuò apparenti, & vice verſa, medium cæli perpetuò apparens plagz meridionali perpetuo latenti tribuitur?Id fit propter fimilitudinem qualitatum.Nam vt medius dies extremi caloris eſt initium, ita à parte auſtrali & à zona torrida ſummus calor procedit. Et medium noctis initium eſt maximi frigoris, cuius cauſa etiam boreas exiſtit. Cæterùm autor eofdem hos quadrantes ad tempora anni, ætates & humores comparans Ptolemæum re linquit, & communem vulgatamque de hac re opinionem ſequitur. Nam primam partem mundi inter orientalem horizon ta & medium cæli poſitam veri, ſecundam æſtati, tertiam autumno, quartam hyemi aſsimilat. At Ptolemæus contrà primé quadrantem ficcum, ſecundum calidum, tertium humidum, quartum frigidum facit. Sanè in ſecundo & quarto quadrante fermè cóueniunt, in primo verò & tertio diſcrepant. Vult enim Ptolemeus partem ortiuam non inſtar veris humidam, ſedipotius ficcam, & partem oppoſitam quæab occaſuad cæli imum protenditur, non inſtar autumni ficcam, ſed potius humidam Lib.1. cap. exiftere, vt totus dies ficcus, totaq; nox humida conſtituatur. Verba cius hæc funt: ὁμοίως δε και την δ' το ορίζοντο τόπωμα ὡρῶμα δ' γωνιῶν, ἀφ᾿ ὡμκαλοικαθ' ὅλα μέρα πνέοντος ἄνιμοι τὰς αρχὰς γωνιῶν ἔχοσι, ὁ μὲν πρὸς τὰς ἀναβολὰς αὐτός το γὸ πλίσμίλει ἐμ τους ξαρῷ, διωάμος στὰ τὸ κατ' αὐτὸμ γινομένο το Θ, τὰ ἀπὸ τ' νυκτός υγροθίντα τότι ως, id est, πρῶτομαρχεπαει ξηραίνεθαι, οἱ δ' ἀπ᾿ αὐτό πνέοντος ἄνεμοι, ὃς De anni κοινότδρομἀπαλιώτας καλῶσιμἄνικεμοί τι εἰσίμκαὶ ξκραντικοι. όδι téporibus πρὸς μεσημβείαμ τόπος, αυτός τί ότι θερμότατος διά τι τὸ πυρος & quatu- δου την τε ο μεσυρανίσιωμ καὶ λλά το ταύτας κατὰ τὸ ση' έμετέ forumina ρας οἰκομένης έγκλισιμ, πρὸς μεσημβρίαμ μᾶλλομἀποκλίνει: οἶτα pa ἀπ᾿ αὐτό πνέοντσο ἄνεμοι ὃς κοινῶς νότας καλόμαν, θερμοί τείσι καὶμανωλικοί. ὁ δὲ πρὸς τοῖς δυσμαῖς τότος, αυτός τι όζιμ υγρός 21ὰ τὸ κατ' αὐτόμ γινομοίο πουλία, τὰ ἀλο πἡμέρας αναποθέντα τότε πρῶτομαρχεθη διυγραίνεοθη: οίτε ἀτε᾿ αὐτό φερόμενοι ἄνες μοι, ὃς καινότόρομ ζεφύρος καλόμθν, νιαροί τι είσι καὶ ύγραντικοί. ὁ Η πρὸς ταῖς αρκοις τόπο, αυτός τε όξι ψυχρότατα, λλά το κατά gura. الرسه Digitized by Google
translation: The fourth is western, the fourth is northern, as Ptolemy also says. And there is no doubt about the eastern and western regions. But why is the perpetually hidden part of the sky attributed to the north, and the perpetually apparent part to the south, and vice versa, the perpetually apparent midheaven to the southern region, and the perpetually hidden part to the north? This is due to the similarity of qualities. For just as midday is the beginning of extreme heat, so from the southern part and from the torrid zone, the greatest heat proceeds. And midnight is the beginning of the greatest cold, the cause of which is also the north wind. However, the author, comparing these same quadrants to the seasons of the year, ages, and humors, leaves Ptolemy and follows the common and vulgar opinion on this matter. For he likens the first part of the world, situated between the eastern horizon and the midheaven, to spring, the second to summer, the third to autumn, and the fourth to winter. But Ptolemy, on the contrary, makes the first quadrant dry, the second hot, the third humid, and the fourth cold. Indeed, in the second and fourth quadrants, they almost agree, but in the first and third, they differ. For Ptolemy wants the eastern part to be not humid like spring, but rather dry, and the opposite part, which extends from the west to the imum coeli, not dry like autumn, but rather humid, so that the whole day is dry and the whole night is humid. His words are these: "Similarly, the fourth part of the horizon, the fourth of the angles, from which the winds blowing throughout the parts have their origins in the angles, the one towards the east is itself dry, because of the heat that occurs there, drying up the parts moistened by the night; and the winds blowing from it, which are commonly called Apeliotes, are dry and drying. The place towards the south is itself very hot, because of the fire of the midheaven and the inclination of our inhabited world towards the south; and the winds blowing from it, which are commonly called Notus, are hot and rarefying. The place towards the west is itself humid, because of the rain that occurs there, moistening the parts dried by the day; and the winds blowing from it, which are commonly called Zephyrus, are moist and humidifying. The place towards the north is itself very cold, because of the cold that occurs there."

author: Valentin Naibod
title: The Four Angles and Their Qualities
text: ==Start of OCR for page 1== コ 1 PIT PRIMA. 163 Ηώ η ἡμετέρας οἰκομένως ἐγκλιουμτὰς τῆς θερμότητα αἰτίας 18 Ο μεσγρανέντα πλέομ αὐτε διεςάναι, ὥσπόρτα Ο αντιμε συρανόντα: οἵτε ἀπ᾿ αὐτό πνέοντου ἄνεμοι οἱ καλόμενοι κοινῶς Βορέαι ψυχροί τε εἰσι καὶ πυκνωτικοί, hoceſt: Ad eundé modum diftribuuntur etiam quatuor finientis anguli, ex quibus venti totas illas partes occupantes nafcuntur. Sed Ptolemæus hanc legem non tantum negligit, ſed & omninò inuertit. Nam Hippocrates, καὶτπᾶς ὁ στην αυτου χόρθ τὴν ἀσκληπιαδῶν, diei quadrantem primum, qui orientali horizonte & finiente continetur, veri& humori ſanguineo:ſecundum, æſtati & bili: tertium, autumno & humori melancholico:quartum, hyemi & pituita comparat Ad hæc interalia multa, quæ Ptolemzo obijci poſſunt, hoc quoque eſt grauifsimum, quòd his partibus diei vnam folam qualitatem præficit,&quidem taliter, vt vbiuis ſeriem qualitatum numerare incipias, ſemper conijciaris in ordinem actionibus naturalibus repugnátem, nempè a calido ad ficcum, à ficco ad humidum,ab humido ad frigidum, à frigido ad ficcum: qui proceſſus abſurdè contra legem a&ionum naturalium inſtitui tur. translation: In the same way, the four terminating angles are also distributed, from which the winds occupying those entire parts arise. The East indeed has a great deal of dryness, because when the Sun is there, the humidity excited during the night begins to dry up. And the winds blowing from there, which are commonly called subsolani, are dry and desiccating. But the southern region is very hot, both because of the Sun's inflammations in the middle of the sky, and because the Sun's culminations in the location of our regions incline most towards the south. The winds also blowing from there, which we generally call nothos, are hot and rarefying. However, the western place is humid, because when the Sun illuminates it, the humidity exhausted during the day then first begins to spread. The winds also erupting from there, which they call favonios, are soft and moist. But the northern place is cold, because, due to the position of our earth, it is very far from the Sun's culminations that produce heat, just as the Sun, while ascending the subterranean south, is very far from us. And the winds that blow from there, commonly called aquilones, are cold and congealing. From all these, both the tradition of the Arabs and Ptolemy concerning the nature of the four quarters of the world, as well as their agreement or disagreement, can be made manifest. But how this diversity of opinions is to be reconciled, or if it cannot be reconciled at all, how one opinion is to be rejected and the remaining one approved and defended by reasons consistent with nature, is not so clear or readily apparent; indeed, it seems very difficult. For it is not probable that Ptolemy, in these suddenly changeable parts of the sky, which are distinguished by the southern and terminating circles, departed without some grave cause from the usual order received from nature, which he faithfully observed not only in the four quarters of the year, but also in the configurations of other stars to the Sun. For just as he made spring hot and humid, summer hot and dry, autumn dry and cold, winter cold and humid, in the same way he afterwards taught that the stars from the first emergence of the Sun to the quadrangular configuration with the Sun are humid like spring; from the first quarter to the opposition, hot like summer; from the opposition to the second quarter, dry like autumn; and finally from there to the occultation, cold. This natural order plainly seems to require that in the same way the first of the four angles of the world be believed to be humid and hot; the second, hot and dry; the third, dry and cold; and finally the fourth, cold and humid. But Ptolemy not only neglects this law, but completely inverts it. For he makes the East, which according to this reasoning ought to be humid, dry, and he says the West, which by common tradition is dry, is humid. On the contrary, the Arabs seem not only to observe the customary order here, but also to be able to confirm their opinion by the authority of men most skilled in nature. For Hippocrates, and all his school of Asclepiads, compares the first quadrant of the day, which is contained by the eastern horizon and the terminator, to spring and sanguine humor; the second, to summer and bile; the third, to autumn and melancholic humor; the fourth, to winter and phlegm. To these, among many other things that can be objected to Ptolemy, this is also very serious, that he assigns only one quality to these parts of the day, and indeed in such a way that wherever you begin to enumerate the series of qualities, you are always thrown into an order repugnant to natural actions, namely from hot to dry, from dry to humid, from humid to cold, from cold to dry: which process is absurdly instituted against the law of natural actions. For we see matter first dissolved and slightly heated by ordered heat; then it becomes hot and dries out, and finally, having dried out, it becomes cold. Thus, bodies do not dry out because of cold, but they become cold because of dried-out humidity. And children do not become humid because they are hot, but because they are humid, they therefore become hot. These individual points can so disturb an unperturbed reader that he always doubts whether Ptolemy or the Arabs are more to be believed. Pontanus also, in his copious exposition of this passage, brought forward nothing but a bare exposition of the words. And Hali noted nothing worthy of observation here, except that he gathers from Ptolemy's words that the qualities of the southern region are changed into the qualities of the northern part by reason of the inhabitants. "Ptolemy," he says, "said very well when he said with respect to our dwellings: Because if we suppose that there is some population in the southern part, the north will be to those who dwell there in the manner that the south is to us. Because to us the south is hot and burning with respect to our dwellings, because the Sun is in the direct part of that region all year, and it makes many reverberations of rays." Only Cardanus do I find to have truly looked into the matter in this place. "The meaning of Ptolemy's words," he says, "is that such a time, such a part of the sky, and such a wind, which has its origin from the east to the south, is dry in quality and action, not in substance, nor does it couple another quality to it. The reason is that in things of short duration, one must consider actions, which are vehement, and these produce qualities, not substances. For substance requires concoction, concoction requires time, but time is short and very small: Therefore, planets and fixed stars change qualities, not substances, in parts of the day and sky. Therefore, it is not inconvenient for an action to pass from dry to hot, or from hot to humid. But here perhaps someone will doubt how primary qualities, insofar as they are substances, are to be distinguished from primary qualities, which are accidents and impressions, that is, how, for example, hot is sometimes transferred to a quality, and sometimes to a body or substance. I answer that the names of qualities are sometimes transferred anomymously or denominatively to the whole body from inherent qualities. For we call a body hot according to substance, a subject body receiving alternately and by turns contrary qualities, that is, heat, coldness, humidity, and dryness. Hence, when we name much or little hot, we refer to the quantity of the substance. For example, if there are two equal vessels, but filled with unequally tempered liquid of water and wine: then we would rightly say that one vessel contains more wine and more heat, and the other more water and less heat. For wine is hot by its nature. It is similar if two vessels are unequal in magnitude, but nevertheless are supposed to be filled with equally tempered liquid. But indeed, when we look not at the abundance of substance or subject, but only at the quality, we say that there is more heat in the former, and less in the latter, which must be understood differently. For example, if two equal vessels are filled with unequally heated liquid, then we would say that the hotter one contains more heat, and the colder one less. And here there is a clear catachresis in the word 'more', if indeed properly and better 'more' or 'less', 'more vehemently' or 'more weakly', and similar words are accommodated to qualities: but 'little' and 'much' are rightly observed only in the quantity of the substance. Similarly, it is manifest that both substances and qualities are subject to alterations. For to speak first of qualities, the substance of oil now receives heat infused from continuous motion or elsewhere; now again, due to rest or otherwise, the quality of heat is expelled and it becomes cold. This same substance, when it consists of a vaporous, oily, and well-concocted air, if subjected to fire, feeds a tranquil flame. But if, striving to preserve the entire quantity of the substance, one restores the parts of the oil consumed by heat through continuous addition of watery parts, that substance will always become worse, and the flame, due to the impure and crude matter acceding, will multiply its crackling, and finally

author: Valentin Naibod
title: The Nature of Heat and Cold
text: cruditate partes concoctas vnicente extinguetur. Hæc actio naturalis in noftris quoque corporibus cernitur. Subſtantia vitalem calorem ſuſti nens, ab exordio puriſsima exiſtit, conſtat enim ex ſemine atreo & menſtruo aquoſo benè concocto, & ob id paucifsimam omnino terrena ſubſtantiam participat. Cùm aut partes vitali calore depaſtas continenter inſtaurari neceſſum fit, imò verò ipſam ſubiecta ſubſtantia amplificari oporteat, & partes ille per nutritionis organa quotidie cófetę atq; corporibus, vt deperditi fiat inſtauratio adiecte, ſint impuriores &magis terreftres, perspicuú eſt, ſubſtantia ſeu fubie&ú vitalis illius & innati calo ris continenter attenuari, donec tadem plane terreſtre factum & deficcatum, flamma vitali alimentum neget, ipſumq; corpus Aphorif, morti obnoxium faciat. Vnde rectè Hippocrates enunciauit, me 14.li. pueros caloreinnato eſſe calidiores ijs qui in iuuenta vigore prime. conſiſtunt, aut ad ſeniŭ prolapſi ſunt. Lycus ergo voce calidi ad qualitatem transferens falſo calumniabatur Hippocrate, ætati conſiſtenti vehementiorem calorem quàm ætati puerili ineffe oftendere contendens. Nam id quoq; hic non negauit Hippocrates, qui no ad qualitatem, ſed ad ſubſtantia caloris refpexit, stque ==Start of OCR for page 5== : 1 1 R 1 1 1 E PRIMA. 267 atq; puerorű corpora nő acutioré caloris qualitate, ſed maiore copia ſubſtătiz innatæ, atq; ad vitale calore ſuſtinédū aptę pof. ſidere voluit.na vt modò dicebamus, qui in coſiſteti ętate funt, habent magis terreſtre corpus, & licet valdè veheméter caleat, ſubſtantia tamé caloris innati habet paucifsima,alioqui febrili aſtu correptos diceremus plurimű habere caloré innatú, cùm nó modò plus, fed ne priori quidé habeat equalé. Sicut auté in noſtris, ita etiam in cæleftibus corporibus tá ſubſtantiarú quá qualitatű has alterationes cogitare & intelligere debemus.Ac ſubſtátię quidé corporú cæleſtiú ficut ex multitudine, déſitate & puritate luminis conſtant, ita eadé luminis differétia variantur. Qualitates aŭt proficiſcútur aut à robore debilitateq; ſub- ſtätiaru, aut à qualitate cõſimili: veluti calor à multo calido aut à caliditate proficifcitur:frigus verò à paruo calore vel frigiditate.Sed dicat aliquis, quomodo calor frigiditaté pariet? Qui Ariftotelis de rerű natura doctrină mordicus retinere ſtuduerunt, nunquá ex his ambagibus eluctari potuiſſe optimè credo Cardano, qui ob ea cauſam choro Ariftoteleorű relito qualitatű natură aliter prodidit: Duæ funt, inquit, qualitates tátum: vna agens cæleftiſq;, & vocatur calor, ſiquide in ſtellis præter calore nil exiftit, cui opponitur frigiditas, que nil aliud eft qua caloris priuatio. Altera qualitas, nepe humiditas, nő in cælo, ſed in globo elementorum eft. Nam humidu & ficcum ſubiectorű 3 corporum τάπαθι ſunt. Humiditati auté ficcitas tanqua priuatio opponitur. Ergo calidum corpus eſt, quod ex copiofo lu minecopactum eft:frigidum verò vbi parum de lumine & calo re exiſtit.Et elementorú corpus humidŭ eſt, quod vapore benè cocoto abundat: & ficcu, quod ſubſtantia illa humida deſtitui tur.Omneauté aftrú ſuo lumine in ſubiecta materia caliditaté & tenuitatem, ſeu humiditate efficit, fic vt cogites & intelligas, frigidum & ficcu in aſtrorum actionibus non eſſe calidi & ficci qualitates contrarias, ſed nomina meſuræ calidi & humidi. Exempli gratia, Sol copiofo, denſo & claro ſuo lumine infigniter calefacitacré. La ſi cu lumine lumen i fortiter cómifceat, - noamplius aër nobis incalefcere, ſed poti infrigidari videbit, perinde vt aqua ferués aquę tepide fortiter admixta intéſioné caloris remittit. Ex his intelligere potes, nature no repugnare, ντα 5 ==Start of OCR for page 1== 160 DIFFERENTIA vt à paruo calore oriatur frigus. Sed humiditas à multo humido aut ab humida qualitate prouenit. Et ficcitas denique à pauco humido in ſubſtantia vel à qualitate ficca deriuatur. Ex his que di&a funt,intelligi potest,quomodo ſubſtantia ſtellarum, cognata temperatura ſignorum, in quibus diutiùs hærent,con fortetur, diluatur craſi contraria: & quomodo ezdem ſtellz in mundi angulis, quos ob celerem mundi cõuerſionem aſsiduò mutant, non ſubſtantiam alterare, ſed ſolam qualitaté aliquam intendere queant aut remittere. Hinc ſequitur, vnam eandemqueſtellam, vno eodemque tempore diuerſo reſpectu read poſſe dici humidam & ficcam. Huius rei ponit Cardanus exem plum tale. Taurus eſt ſignum temperatifsimum, plurimum habens calidi & humidi, quia eſt ſignũ vernale & mediű: planeta tamen in eo exiſtens dicetur ſiccus, ſi in parte cœli orientalire periatur, fed tamen ficcus ſecundum qualitatem, id eft, natus • ſub tali planeta, licet habeat plurimum de calore naturali & hu mido vitali, quod ſubſtantia ipſi idé eſt: illud tamen humidum erit ficcius & non ita purum neq; tam feruens ſecundum qualitaté, vt eſſet humidum illius qui natus eſſet Tauro occidente. Econtrariò natus ſub planeta in Libra occidente, humidumha bet purius & fincerius ſecundum qualitatem, non erit tamen ita den ſum neque multum, imò vt ita dicam, aqueum.Atque ad hunc modum dignoſcendas eſſe vires planetarum à ſignorum angulorumque familiaritate, indicat Ptolemeus his verbis, qui bus caput de generali conſtitutione ſignorum, & quatuor angulorum natura claudit : χρησίμα διἑτότωμ διάληψις, πρὸς τὸ τὰς συγκράσφς παίτα τρόπομ εκάςοτε 2/ακρίναμ. σύκα τανόπτομ γαρότι καὶ πλατας οιαύτας κατας άσος ήτοιτν αρωμ, την άλω κιών, ἢ τὴν γωνιώμ τρίπεται πῶς ὁ ποιητικόμ τ' την αςέραρδυν νάμεως. καὶ ει μεν ταῖς οἰκείοις καταςάσεσιμ, ακρατωτέρεμ έχοσι τώ ποιότητα, καὶ τιὼ ἐνοργφαμ ίχυροτέραμ, οἷομ έμ ταῖς θερμαῖς οἱ θερμαντικοί τώ φύσιμ, καὶ ἐμταῖς ὑγραῖς οἱ ὑγραντικοί, ἐμ δε ταῖς ἐναντίαις, κεκραμένημ καὶ ἀθενες έραμ, ὡς ἐμ ταῖς ψυχαῖς οἱ θερμαντικοί, καὶ ἐμταῖς ξηραῖς οἱ ὑγραντικοί, καὶ ἐν ταῖς ἄλλαις δὲ ὡσαύτως κατὰ τὸ ἀνάλογομ καί του λάτ' μίξεως συγκιρναμέν νιποιότα. hoceſt, Vtiliselt harum rerum noticia ad dijudi. candas Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 1==
translation: The concocted parts will be extinguished by the crudity of the unconcocted parts. This natural action is also observed in our bodies. The substance sustaining vital heat exists in its purest form from the beginning, for it consists of well-concocted seminal and watery menstrual fluid, and for this reason, it participates in very little earthly substance. But since it is necessary for the parts consumed by vital heat to be continuously restored, and indeed for the underlying substance itself to be amplified, and those parts, daily consumed through the organs of nutrition and added to the bodies, so that the lost may be restored, are more impure and more earthy, it is clear that the substance or subject of that vital and innate heat is continuously attenuated, until at last, having become completely earthy and desiccated, it denies nourishment to the vital flame, and renders the body itself subject to death. Hence Hippocrates rightly stated, in Aphorism 14, that children are warmer with innate heat than those who are in the vigor of youth or have fallen into old age. Lycus, therefore, by transferring the word 'hot' to quality, falsely slandered Hippocrates, contending to show that a more vehement heat is present in a consistent age than in childhood. For Hippocrates did not deny this here either, who did not refer to the quality, but to the substance of heat, and wished the bodies of children to possess not a sharper quality of heat, but a greater abundance of innate substance, and one apt to sustain vital heat. For, as we just said, those who are in a consistent age have a more earthy body, and although it may be very vehemently hot, it has very little of the substance of innate heat; otherwise, we would say that those seized by a feverish heat have a great deal of innate heat, when they not only do not have more, but not even an equal amount to the former. Just as in our bodies, so also in celestial bodies, we must consider and understand these alterations of both substances and qualities. And indeed, the substances of celestial bodies, just as they consist of the multitude, density, and purity of light, so they are varied by the same difference of light. Qualities, however, proceed either from the strength or weakness of substances, or from a similar quality: just as heat proceeds from much heat or from hotness; cold, however, from little heat or coldness. But someone might say, how can heat produce coldness? I believe that those who diligently adhered to Aristotle's doctrine of the nature of things could never extricate themselves from these ambiguities, especially Cardano, who for that reason, having abandoned the chorus of Aristotelians, presented the nature of qualities differently: There are, he says, only two qualities: one active and celestial, and it is called heat, since in the stars nothing exists besides heat, to which coldness is opposed, which is nothing else than the privation of heat. The other quality, namely humidity, is not in the heavens, but in the globe of the elements. For humid and dry are the affections of the subject bodies. To humidity, however, dryness is opposed as a privation. Therefore, a hot body is one that is compacted from abundant light; a cold one, however, where there is little light and heat. And the body of the elements is humid, which abounds in well-concocted vapor; and dry, which is deprived of that humid substance. But every star, by its light, produces heat and tenuity, or humidity, in the subject matter, so that you may consider and understand that cold and dry in the actions of the stars are not qualities contrary to hot and dry, but names of the measure of hot and humid. For example, the Sun, with its abundant, dense, and clear light, significantly heats the air. If it mixes its light strongly with the light of the Moon, the air will no longer seem to us to grow warmer, but rather colder, just as hot water strongly mixed with lukewarm water reduces the intensity of heat. From these things, you can understand that it is not contrary to nature for cold to arise from little heat. But humidity comes from much humid or from a humid quality. And finally, dryness is derived from little humid in the substance or from a dry quality. From what has been said, it can be understood how the substance of the stars, by the cognate temperament of the signs in which they remain longer, is strengthened, diluted by a contrary crasis; and how the same stars in the angles of the world, which they constantly change due to the rapid conversion of the world, can intensify or remit not the substance, but only some quality. Hence it follows that one and the same star, at one and the same time, can be said to be humid and dry with respect to different aspects. Cardano gives such an example of this. Taurus is a very temperate sign, having much hot and humid, because it is a vernal and middle sign: yet a planet existing in it will be called dry, if it is found in the eastern part of the sky, but dry according to quality, that is, one born under such a planet, although it may have much natural heat and vital humidity, which is the same as its substance: yet that humid will be drier and not so pure nor so fervent according to quality, as would be the humid of one born in Taurus setting. On the contrary, one born under a planet in Libra setting has purer and more sincere humid according to quality, yet it will not be so dense or abundant, but, so to speak, watery. And in this way, Ptolemy indicates that the powers of the planets are to be distinguished by the familiarity of the signs and angles, in these words, with which he concludes the chapter on the general constitution of the signs and the nature of the four angles: "The understanding of these things is useful for judging the temperaments in every way. For it is clear that such positions, whether of the signs, or of the Moon, or of the angles, somehow affect the productive power of the stars. And if they are in their proper positions, they have a more unmixed quality and a stronger inherent power, as those that are hot by nature in hot places, and those that are humid in humid places; but in contrary positions, mixed and weaker, as those that are hot in cold places, and those that are humid in dry places, and similarly in others according to analogy, and the quality is mixed with a little mixture." That is, the knowledge of these things is useful for judging.
