translation: 208. The Hare, with a southern latitude of 53 parts. The rest are Saturnine. IV. Lepus. V. Canis. VI. Procyon. VII. Argo. Canopus. VIII. Hydra. IX. Crater. X. Corvus. XI. Centaurus. XII. Boötes. XIII. Thuribulum. XIV. Corona Australis. XV. Piscis Austrinus. The Hare, under the feet of Orion, has Mercurial stars. Canis Major has all Venusian stars, except for the immense and largest star that shines in its mouth, and Canicula, also accustomed to be called Sirius, which is Jovial and also has a little of Mars. Its longitude is 98 parts, with a southern latitude of 39 parts. This star, rising in the morning, is seen even at midday in a mirror, with water placed against the Sun, and wonderfully increases the Sun's heat. Procyon, the Lesser Dog or Antecanis, is so called because it rises before Sirius. Its bright star is of the first magnitude, of the nature of Mercury and also has a little of Mars, and has a longitude of 109 parts with a southern latitude of 164 parts. The bright stars of Argo, which they also call Jason's ship and Noah's ark, are of the nature of Jupiter and Mars. Furthermore, the star of the first magnitude at the top of Argo's rudder is called Canopus, but it does not rise for us, for it has a southern latitude of 75 parts. The bright stars of Hydra or the Serpent are Saturnine and partly Venusian. Some of the more recent authors have called this constellation magnanimous. Crater, the cup or urn, has Venusian stars and partly Mercurial. Corvus has Martian and Saturnine stars. Centaurus, Chiron, who is also called the beast-bearer. The stars located in the human part of the body have the efficacy of Jupiter and Venus. The splendid stars in the equine part are of the nature of Jupiter and Venus. The beast, or wolf, or monster that the Centaur carries, also named Leopard by some, has bright stars, partaking of the nature of Jupiter and a little of Mars. The Thuribulum, that is, the altar or censer, has Venusian stars, and also a little of Saturnine. The bright stars in the Southern Crown imitate the quality of Jupiter and Venus. This constellation is also called the Raven and Ixion's Wheel. Piscis Austrinus, the marine or great fish, which is said to have been translated to the heavens because it once received Venus in her flight from Typhon, has in its mouth a bright star of the first magnitude, of the nature of Jupiter and Mercury, and its longitude is 327 parts, with a southern latitude of 23 parts. So much for the fixed stars: now the forces of the planets are to be revealed. But first, the hesitation that arises here must be recited and examined. For it may reasonably be doubted whether stars that are said to have mixed qualities of malefics and benefics retain both simultaneously, even if contrary, or whether, one quality being abolished, they follow the other. For example, Basiliscus is said to have the forces of Jupiter and Mars. If, therefore, the Heart of Leo ascends in a nativity, it may be doubted whether it makes people sometimes just, as Jupiter usually does, sometimes unjust, as Mars, or whether it makes them simultaneously just and unjust, and finally, whether it makes them intermediate between just and unjust? The answer does not seem absurd to me if we say that these constellations reflect the qualities of that planet with which they are conjoined, that is, joined by body or aspect. In this matter, he who is found present in the asterism, or who sees it more closely, will always be preferred. But if neither of the cognate planets, but some third foreign planet, is joined to the constellation, the constellation seems to defend the nature of that planet to which it is friendly by aspect, and with which it agrees in nature. So if neither Jupiter nor Venus, but Mars, who abounds in Martian quality because of heat, sees Basiliscus, it will not unreasonably seem to retain only the qualities of Mars, the Jovial nature being suppressed. But Cardano has another opinion, who thinks that the mixed natures of the planets should be retained in the constellations, because Ptolemy also used to mix the forces of the planets, so that similar things are increased by similar things, but from contrary qualities, either both are removed, if they are equal, or the stronger is weakened by the weaker, if qualities of unequal forces are compared. According to this method, the star in the tail of Ursa Major, of the nature of Saturn and Venus, will be very humid, because both of these planets are very humid: but moderate in heat, warmer than the Moon and colder than Venus. In the same way, stars of the nature of Mars and Saturn will be very dry, for both planets are dry. But in heat, as it were, tempered, but they will incline to cold, because the cold of Mars is greater than its heat. They will be angry because of Mars, and tenacious in anger because of Saturn, and fierce and audacious in that anger, and finally, timid because of the Sun when it is fervent. Finally, we must also observe that fixed stars, some of which have receded to the south, and some further to the north from the Ecliptic, do not rise or set in every climate in the same way with the Sun or another part of the Ecliptic. For example, a curved line extended from the vernal intersection to both poles of the world, although in a right sphere, in which both poles touch the horizon, it ascends entirely in one moment, yet for us northerners, it does not all rise at once, but where the northern part has risen, the southern will still lie hidden depressed. Therefore, when the Sun or another star rises in the vernal intersection, that is, in the first part, a fixed star placed in the same line to the south will not rise with it, but some degrees later. On the contrary, a star situated to the north, having risen earlier, will precede the Sun, and will neither rise nor set simultaneously with the Sun. Therefore, lest the positions of the stars deceive you, you must diligently provide for them through a solid sphere, or through the tables of Regiomontanus. In the books of Ephemerides also, tables are arranged for some elevations of the pole for this purpose, which show which more unworthy stars rise, set, and culminate with which parts of the Ecliptic. The text follows concerning the power, authority, and signification of the seven wandering stars. 1. Saturn is masculine, malefic, diurnal. 2. And it is the significator of fathers, if the child's nativity is at night. 3. And it signifies extreme old age if it is occidental: but the beginning of old age if it is oriental. And it signifies the gravity of cold and dryness. 4. And it signifies, that is, in the complexion of bodies, melancholic, its increase and distillation. And perhaps it will sometimes be a cold, humid, ponderous, and fetid complexion, and much eating and true love. 5. And it signifies depth of counsel, and multitude of silence. 6. And from the arts, ancient precious things. Indeed, the two members which you see enclosed in parentheses, if they are joined by translation, the reading will be more orderly and arranged: since in both the quality of the art is noted. Hence it will also follow that the paragraph: And it signifies depth of counsel, etc., will be correctly continued with the paragraph: And if he is fortunate, there will be true love, etc. For in both, the quality of the soul is discussed. If he is fortunate, he signifies the cultivation of fields, the devastation of lands and rivers. On the contrary, however, vile and laborious things, such as rubbings in baths, fullers, and sailors, if he is malefic. And if he is fortunate, he will be spacious and patient in true love, if he is malefic, he will be indiscreet, stable, sad and mournful, prone to evil suspicion, very suspicious, and moving men to whispers. (In addition, if he is the sole significator without the complexion of any other planet, he signifies works of leather or his own. But if Jupiter is conjoined with him, he signifies the work of parchment, in which divine books are written. And if Mars is conjoined with him, he signifies the soles of shoes and their preparations. And if the Sun is conjoined with him, he signifies the work of shoemakers' leathers. And if Venus is conjoined with him, he signifies the work of leathers from which drums and other instruments that we use in games are made. And if Mercury is conjoined with him, he signifies the work of leathers in which testaments and numbers of stipends are written. And if the Moon is conjoined with him, he signifies the preparation of leathers of wild animals and mountain animals, and similar things.) 7. And if he is fortunate, he signifies from the substance ancient durable inheritances, and cultivated land, Almauerith, that is, the substances of the dead. And if he is

Iupiter fortuna, masculinus, diurnus:tò eſt ſignifi- 4 cator substantie, & operatur calorem & humiditatem temperatam, aeream, sanguineam,& de ætatibus figni ficat iuuentutem vsque ad perfectionem ætatis: et ex magiſterijs que pertinent ad legem: vt iusta iudicia iu dicare,& pacem inter homines mittere, & in bonis ftudere. Et fubstantiæ fignificat abundantiam, & ex nego tijs illa quæ fiunt absque seductione, & fignat animam & vitam, lætitiam & veritatem, & religionem & pa tientiam,& omne præceptum pulchrum & pretiofum, & abundantiam Veneris: & ex infirmitatibus quæ fi unt ex sanguine per augmentum quantitatis, quæ non fuerit superflua extranaturam , nec ex sanguine vſto & conuertibili: est planeta sapientiæ & intellectus & vfus.Cui si complectitur Saturnus, signat necroman tiam & incantationes & exorcismos, &c. Et si com plectitur ei Mars, significat scientiam medicinæ. Et si complectitur ei Sol, significat scientiam sectarum, & prudentiam in contentionibus & disputationibus. Et si complectitur ei Venus, signat compositionem sonorum & aliarum delectabilium. Et si complectitur ei Mercu rius, signat scientiam Arithmetica & scribendi: Astro nomiam quoque & Philoſophiam & Geometriam. Et Dd 3. fi com- 274 DIFFERENTIA Ficomplectitur ei Luna, signat scientiam dispositiones aquarum & menfuræearum, nec non terrarum. Et ex qualitate animi signat largitatem & verecundiam atq; iuftitiam, & ex sectis pluralitatem atq; simu lationem. Quidam autem dixerunt, quòd significat hepar & Stomachum, & aurem sinistram & brachia atque ven trem: inferiora quoque pectinis & intestina: & ex coloribus colorem cineritium & viridem, & horum simi les: & ex saporibus habet dulcem : & quantitas orbis eius est graduum IX. & ex diebus habet diem Iouis,& ex noctibus noctem diei Lunæ: & anni fridariæ eius,id est, dominationis eius,funt XII. & anni maximi sunt CCCCXXVIII.maiores LXXIX.medij XLV. dimidium:minores verò XII. & fortitudo eius ex pla gis circuli est in occidente. Et dixit Messahala, quòd ex hominibus signi ficat hominem album, habentem ruborem in facie: habentem oculos non prorsus nigros , naves non æquales & breves, caluum: in aliquo dentium habentem nigredinem, pulchra stature, boni animi, bonis moribus, pulchri corporis. Et dixit Dorotheus, quòd signifi cat hominem habentem magnos oculos: & pupillam la tam,barbam crispam. Et habet ex regionibus Alchi rath & Babyloniam & Asen & Persidem, & Alaormes & Archadiam. Et ex partibus habens partem habitudi nis profectus, & significat fidem & appetitum in bonis: & ex operibus salubritatem, securitatem & partici pationem. Mars SECVNDA. 215 - i - Mars masculinus, nocturnus, malus, operatur calo rem & siccitatem. Et est significator fratrum & pere grinationum: & habet ex ætatibus iuuentutem vsque in fine iuventutis: & natura eius cholerica amari saporis, Et ex magisterijs habet omne magisterium igneum, & quod fit per ferrum & ignem, sicut est percussio gladio rum cum martellis. Cumque ei complectitur Saturnus, signat percussionem ferri: Si Iuppiter, significat per cussionem æris: Si Sol, significat percussionem num morum aureorum: Si Venus, significat magisterium ornamentorum: Si Mercurius, percussionem acuum: Si Luna, significat percussionem lancearum & li brorum. Significat etiam , cùm solus suscipit signifi cationem, opus medicinæ: Si nullus Planeta ei com plectitur, significat minutionem, & vulnerationem, & apertionem, & horum similia. Etsi complectitur ei Saturnus, signat opus medicinæ & vulnerum: & si Iuppiter, signat opus naturarum : & si Sol, si gnat curam oculorum : si Venus, signat opus orna menti, vt tonsuram crinium & barbarum, & absci sionem vngularum : & si Mercurius, signat abscisio nem venarum:si Luna, significat eradicationem den tium & purgationem aurium. Et per se signat in iurias miserorum,reffusionem sanguinis, & oppressio nes per vim, & abscisiones viarum & iracundiam, & ductum exercitus, & festinationem & inuerecundi am,& peregrinationem extra patriam, & fæditatem coitus, & casus puerorum,id est,abortiuorum:& fratres medios. ٠١ DIFFERENTIA medios atque sorores, scientiam ac discretionem curæ bestiarum. Et ex infirmitatibus febres calidas et san guineas, et pustulas sanguineas, alabraha, quæ est rubedo corporis cum asperitate et fæditate et commestione car nium cum putredine: et dimidy capitis dolorem,et ig nem sacrum et pauorem, et cogitationes horribiles, quæ inquietant homines et commouent et impediunt atque inanes reddunt , et quicquid fuerit cum inflammatione caloris. Et ex qualitate animi, commotionem scilicet a nimi et conturbationem, si aspexerit eum Saturnus, sig nificat odium et maximam inuidiam: et ex sectis eam in qua bellum fuerit: et vnitatem et celeritatem muta tionis à fide, et multitudinem hesitationis, et mutatio nem de testamento in testamentum : erunt tamen hec omnia sub vnitate. Dixerunt quidam quòd habet ex membris fel,renes, et venas, et decursum spermatis et dor sum:et habet ex coloribus rubedine, et ex saporibus ama rum:et quãtitas orbis eius est VIII.gra.et ex diebus habet die Martis:et ex noctibus nocte Sabbati:et anni fridariæ eius sunt septe, et anni eius maximi sunt CCLXIIII.ma iores sunt LXVI.medij autem XL. & dimidiu: & mi nores XV. & fortitudo eius in plagis circuli meridie. Et dixit Messahala: quòd significet de imaginı bus hominum, hominem rubeum, habentem capillos ruf fos, et faciem rotundam leuiter homines dehonestan tem, habentem oculos croceos, horribilis aspectus, auda cem , alias citum habentem in pede signum vel macu lam. Et dixit Dorotheus, quòd significat habentem acu tum 1 A.
translation: If it is malefic, it signifies dirty and bad-tasting waters, old and changeable. And from infirmities, phlegmatic and melancholic diseases, viscous, congealed, and acute, such as leprosy, morphea, gout, cancers, and other similar things. And it signifies distant peregrinations, prisons, chains, labor, slowness, and affliction. And it signifies among sects that which confesses unity, if it is fortunate; and if it is malefic, it signifies the faith of unity, yet with much hesitation or doubt. And Messahala, whom God willed to be exalted, who was an Astrologer clear in the science of judgments, whence he was so called, said that it signifies the Jewish faith, because it is from the most ancient, and because all confess it, and it confesses no other. Just as Saturn, to whom all are joined, and he to none. It also signifies black garments. And some others said that Saturn signifies the inner parts of the ear, the spleen, and the stomach. And it has blackness from colors; and from tastes, astringent and sour; from days, Saturday; finally, from nights, that which precedes Wednesday. And the quantity of its orb is nine degrees. And the years of its fridaria are XI, the greatest are CCCCLXVI, the major LVII, the medium XLIII, and the minor XXX. Its strength in the regions of the circle is in the right of the north. And Messahala said that it signifies among human figures, a man between black and yellow, who when he walks casts his eyes to the ground, heavy, joining his feet in his gait, thin, stooped, having small eyes, and dry skin, veiny, having a sparse beard on his jaws, thick lips, cunning, ingenious, a seducer, a killer. And Dorotheus said: This signifies a man with a very hairy body, with joined eyebrows. And it has from regions Ascine and India, all the land of black people. And from parts, having a part of strength and stability. And it signifies lands or earthly things and inheritances: and those who are in charge of works, and audacity and labor, talents and causes of death: and it has from the parts of the world Achiud and Alchiut, the confines of the guilty, and the lands of black people and their mountains.

translation: Jupiter, fortunate, masculine, diurnal: it is a significator of substance, and it operates heat and temperate humidity, airy, sanguine, and from ages it signifies youth up to the perfection of age: and from crafts that pertain to the law: such as judging just judgments, and bringing peace among men, and striving for good things. And of substance it signifies abundance, and from businesses those that are done without deception, and it signifies the soul and life, joy and truth, and religion and patience, and every beautiful and precious precept, and the abundance of Venus: and from infirmities that arise from blood by an increase of quantity, which is not superfluous beyond nature, nor from burnt and changeable blood: it is the planet of wisdom and intellect and use. If Saturn is conjoined with it, it signifies necromancy and incantations and exorcisms, etc. And if Mars is conjoined with it, it signifies the science of medicine. And if the Sun is conjoined with it, it signifies the science of sects, and prudence in contentions and disputes. And if Venus is conjoined with it, it signifies the composition of sounds and other delightful things. And if Mercury is conjoined with it, it signifies the science of Arithmetic and writing: also Astronomy and Philosophy and Geometry. If the Moon is conjoined with it, it signifies the science of the dispositions of waters and their measure, as well as of lands. And from the quality of the soul it signifies generosity and modesty and justice, and from sects plurality and simulation. Some, however, said that it signifies the liver and stomach, and the left ear and arms and belly: also the lower parts of the chest and intestines: and from colors it has ash color and green, and similar to these: and from tastes it has sweet: and the quantity of its orb is IX degrees. And from days it has Thursday, and from nights the night of Monday: and the years of its fridaria, that is, of its dominion, are XII. And the greatest years are CCCCXXVIII, the major LXXIX, the medium XLV and a half: and the minor XII. And its strength from the regions of the circle is in the west. And Messahala said that among human images it signifies a white man, having redness in his face: having eyes not entirely black, uneven and short ships, bald: having blackness in some teeth, of beautiful stature, good spirit, good manners, beautiful body. And Dorotheus said that it signifies a man having large eyes: and a wide pupil, a curly beard. And it has from regions Alchirath and Babylon and Asen and Persia, and Alaormes and Arcadia. And from parts, having a part of the habit of progress, and it signifies faith and desire for good things: and from works, health, security and participation.

Et per se signat in iurias miserorum,reffusionem sanguinis, & oppressio nes per vim, & abscisiones viarum & iracundiam, & ductum exercitus, & festinationem & inuerecundi am,& peregrinationem extra patriam, & fæditatem coitus, & casus puerorum,id est,abortiuorum:& fratres medios. ٠١ DIFFERENTIA medios atque sorores, scientiam ac discretionem curæ bestiarum. Et ex infirmitatibus febres calidas et san guineas, et pustulas sanguineas, alabraha, quæ est rubedo corporis cum asperitate et fæditate et commestione car nium cum putredine: et dimidy capitis dolorem,et ig nem sacrum et pauorem, et cogitationes horribiles, quæ inquietant homines et commouent et impediunt atque inanes reddunt , et quicquid fuerit cum inflammatione caloris. Et ex qualitate animi, commotionem scilicet a nimi et conturbationem, si aspexerit eum Saturnus, sig nificat odium et maximam inuidiam: et ex sectis eam in qua bellum fuerit: et vnitatem et celeritatem muta tionis à fide, et multitudinem hesitationis, et mutatio nem de testamento in testamentum : erunt tamen hec omnia sub vnitate. Dixerunt quidam quòd habet ex membris fel,renes, et venas, et decursum spermatis et dor sum:et habet ex coloribus rubedine, et ex saporibus ama rum:et quãtitas orbis eius est VIII.gra.et ex diebus habet die Martis:et ex noctibus nocte Sabbati:et anni fridariæ eius sunt septe, et anni eius maximi sunt CCLXIIII.ma iores sunt LXVI.medij autem XL. & dimidiu: & mi nores XV. & fortitudo eius in plagis circuli meridie. Et dixit Messahala: quòd significet de imaginı bus hominum, hominem rubeum, habentem capillos ruf fos, et faciem rotundam leuiter homines dehonestan tem, habentem oculos croceos, horribilis aspectus, auda cem , alias citum habentem in pede signum vel macu lam. Et dixit Dorotheus, quòd significat habentem acu tum 1 A.
translation: Mars, masculine, nocturnal, malefic, operates heat and dryness. And it is a significator of brothers and peregrinations: and it has from ages youth up to the end of youth: and its nature is choleric with a bitter taste. And from crafts it has every fiery craft, and that which is done by iron and fire, such as the striking of swords with hammers. And when Saturn is conjoined with it, it signifies the striking of iron: If Jupiter, it signifies the striking of bronze: If the Sun, it signifies the striking of gold coins: If Venus, it signifies the craft of ornaments: If Mercury, the striking of needles: If the Moon, it signifies the striking of spears and books. It also signifies, when it alone takes on the signification, the work of medicine: If no planet is conjoined with it, it signifies diminution, and wounding, and opening, and similar things. And if Saturn is conjoined with it, it signifies the work of medicine and wounds: and if Jupiter, it signifies the work of natures: and if the Sun, it signifies the care of the eyes: if Venus, it signifies the work of ornament, such as cutting hair and beards, and cutting nails: and if Mercury, it signifies the cutting of veins: if the Moon, it signifies the eradication of teeth and the cleaning of ears. And by itself it signifies the injuries of the miserable, the effusion of blood, and oppressions by force, and the cutting off of roads and anger, and the leading of an army, and haste and shamelessness, and peregrination outside the homeland, and the foulness of coitus, and the falling of children, that is, abortions: and middle brothers and sisters, the knowledge and discretion of the care of beasts. And from infirmities, hot and sanguine fevers, and sanguine pustules, alabraha, which is redness of the body with roughness and foulness and consumption of flesh with putrefaction: and headache of half the head, and sacred fire and terror, and horrible thoughts, which disturb men and move and impede and render them empty, and whatever is with inflammation of heat. And from the quality of the soul, namely commotion of the soul and disturbance, if Saturn aspects it, it signifies hatred and greatest envy: and from sects that in which there is war: and unity and swiftness of change from faith, and multitude of hesitation, and change from testament to testament: yet all these will be under unity. Some said that it has from the members the gall, kidneys, and veins, and the flow of semen and the back: and it has from colors redness, and from tastes bitter: and the quantity of its orb is VIII degrees. And from days it has Tuesday, and from nights the night of Saturday: and the years of its fridaria are seven, and its greatest years are CCLXIIII, the major are LXVI, the medium XL and a half: and the minor XV. And its strength in the regions of the circle is in the south. And Messahala said that among human images it signifies a red man, having reddish hair, and a slightly round face dishonoring men, having yellow eyes, of horrible aspect, audacious, sometimes having a mark or spot on his foot. And Dorotheus said that it signifies one having a sharp.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Mercury's Significations
text: itinerantium extra domum.Et fignat de infirmitatibus infirmitates ſpirituales anima, id eft, cogitationes horribiles, & inquietudinem mentis id eft,c atque dubitationes, & cætera ſimilia. Significat etiam animi qualitate,& cætera fimilia fecundú complexionē Suam & coniunctionem:cum fuerit fortunatus, erunt bo fortunatus erunt bo nitates ſecundum genus fortune quæ facit eum fortuna. tum, & ſecundum locum in quo fuerit fortunatus Mercu rius: & cùm fuerit malus, erunt iniquitates fecundum malum quod eum facit malum, & ſecundum locum in quo factus est malus. Et fignificat ex ſectis culturam vnitatis, & horum ſimilia, Ghoc fecretò cum hypocrifia & fimulatione. Et dixerunt quidam, quòd fignificat fes mora, vmbilicum & pectinem & crura, neruos atque ve nas. Et ex coloribus habet omnem colorem commixtum atque variatum, & alexmenium, qui eſt color floris lilij agreftis: & ex saporibus acetofum. Et quantitas orbis eius est VII.graduum, & habet ex diebus diem Mercu rij,& ex noctibus noctem diei dominica. Anni fridaria eius funt XIII.15 anni eius maiores LXXVI.maximi CCCCLX. medij XLVIII. minores verò XX. Forti tudo eius in plagis circuli eft in feptentrione. Et dixe Tunt Digitized by Google SECVNDA 223 runt quidam, Mercurius à medio retrogradationis eius vſque ad ſtationem fecundam, fignificat pueritiam: &à ſtatione ſecunda vſque ad coniunctionem Solis, iuuentu tem: &à cõiunctione Solis vſque ad ſtationem primam, mediam etatem:& à ſtatione ſua prima vsque ad mediu retrogradationis, fignificat ſenectutem & contrarieta tem:& in coniunctione ſua per directionem vfq; ad ſta= tionem primam,fignificat dilectionem Gamicitiam, amoris imitationem: & ex ſtatione ſua ſecunda ad coniunctionem iens, ſignificat inquifitionem dilectionis concordiam: Gà ſtatione ſua prima iens iterum ad retrogradationem, fignificat inquifitionem contrarietatis & & dißimulationis, tarditatem verecundiam paruam = & occupationem & ſtuporem in rebus: & in medio retrogradationis,fignificat celeritate temperată propter ingený debilitatem: apud ſtatione fecundam fignifie cat ftuporem occupationem ingeniy tarditatem egreffus:ad hæc celeritatem apertionem ingeniorum: coniunctione fua Soli per directionem, fignificat ce leritatem & paßionem ingeniorum & latitudinem vel augmentationem eorum.Similiter Veneri accidit in his locis & planetis altioribus. Et dixit Meſſahala: Mercu rius fignificat ex figuris hominum, bomine non multum album neq; nigrum colore, frontem habente eleuată longa, in facie lõgitudine, nafum longũ, rara habente barba in maxillis,& oculos pulchros, no ex toto nigros, logos quoq; habente digitos. Et ex partibus habet parte negotiationis, & fignificat timore & infestationem bellum 224 DIFFEREΝΤΙΑ bellum et inimicitias et feductiones & contrarietates: fignificat quoque perfectum magisterium & fubtilis tatem in opere &inquifitione, & in cateris quæ fiunt in hominibus exactis & contentionibus. Et ex terris habet Adelech, Alcuiden & maximè in meridie, 17 ter. ras Indorum. Luna fortuna, fœminina, nocturna, operatur frigidi tate humiditate. Et eſt ſignificatrix matrű, fi fuerit natiuitas nocturna: est in ea phlegma temperatu. Es fignificat atate puerile initiu crefcendi. Et ex operis bus habet bet legationes mandata, opera aquaru atq; terrarum fecundu quantitate fortunj vel infortunij vel mali:quòd fi fuerit bona, ſignificat bonum, & è conuer fo. Significat etiam principatum, ſi præfuerit vel fuerit dominās aliquibus rebus ad regem pertinentibus, fi fue rit fortunata à Sole in bono aſpectu & in bono loco. Et de ſubſtantia fignificat argentum, et terre cultum, fi habuerit autoritatem in quarta. Et ex fide religionem. Et ex irfirmitatibus epilepfiam, paralyfin, guttam ca ducam, vultus torſionem, enodationem membrorum commotionem, & quicquid fuerit in ſimilitudine frigoris & humiditatis. Et qualitatem animi fecun. dum coniunctionem fuam cum planetis. Cui ſi com plectitur fortuna, & fuerit fortuna ipfa Venus, fig. nificat curialitatem & benignitatem, & animi sua uitatem, morum honeftatem, et motus celeritatem, et diuinum obfequium:fi fuerit Iuppiter, ſignificat bone ſtatem, cautelam et focietate et benignitate et vita pul chritu- : 225 SECVNDA. chritudinem:& fi coplexa fuerit malis, fuerit Mars, fignificat ftudium fufurrationis inter homines vsum in talibus: fi Saturnus, odium, fimulationem ac inui» diam:fi fuerit Sol ab afpectu laudabili, ſignificat difpo. fitionem re regalem:fifuerit Mercurius, fignificat Rhe toricam, id est facundiam & bonitatem lingue & fcri pturam. Et dixerunt quidam quòd fignificat cogitatio nem nouitatem nouitatem animi & debilitatem fenfus, & gra uitate lingue, & mulieres honeftas nutritiones par. auloru, matres & materteras, preparationes cibo rum. Et dixerut alij, quòd fignificat cerebrum propriè et pulmonem. Et ex coloribus habet croceum, & ex fa poribus falfum. Et quantitas orbis eius est XII.graduum. Et fignificat in viris oculum finiſtrum, et in mu lieribus dextrum. Et ex diebus habet diem Luna, et ex noctibus noctem Veneris. Et anni fridarie eius funt no uem: maiores autem CVIII. maximi CCCCCXX. medij LXVI. dimidium, & fecundum quofdam XXXIX. dimidum:minores verò XXV. Et for. titudo eius ex plagis circuli in dextra occidentis. Et dixit Meßa Meßahala, quòd fignificat de figuris homi num hominem album, confectum rubore, iunctis fuperci lijs, beneuolum, habentem oculos non ex toto nigros, fas ciem rotundam, pulchram ſtaturam: & in facie eius figni. Et ex terris habet Arcoch & Timana et Aldeilam. 18 Et quidam dixerunt, quòd fignificat pueritiam ab initio menfis vsque ad VII. dies, & à VII.diebus vfq; Ff ad : 226 DIFFERENTIA ad XIIII. dies iuuentutem: & fignificat etatem per fectionis, id eft, medire cetatis,vfque ad. XXI. dies noctes: Senectutem vsque ad coniunctionem, id eft, of que in finem menſis, quando coniungitur foli. Et cùm fuerit fub radys Solis, ſignificat ſecreta & res occultas: fimiliter omnis planeta.Et in initio menſis quando crefcit fignificat omne quod faciendum eft:& in imple tione sua quod deftruendum est, quia decreſcit: & in ini tio menfis ipfa & Solſignificant accufatores:& in fine menfis accufatos: & fignificat in initio menfis lucrum continentia, in fine menſis ſtipendia er difperfiones. Et fignificat in oppofitione contrarietatem, et in initio mutationis ad oppofitionem, id eft, cùm fuerint inter eam et oppofitionem XV.gradus, fignificat initium con trarietatis et eius caufam: et cum feparata fuerit ab op. pofitione, fignificat caufas exceptionis à contrarietate: et in exitufuo de fub radys Solis, fignificat exitu ab oa cultatione, et his fimilia:et in introitu fubradijs, signat aptationem ad occultandum: et hora diuifionis à Sole, fignat aptationem exitus fui ab occultatione: et hora exitus fui de fub. radijs, fignificat apparitionem ad. uentum ab abſentia:et dum fuerit in quarto afpectu So lis fignificat diminutionem rei feu defcenfionem ab ah to in infimum. Similiter in quarto afpectu fecundo,fed in quarto afpectu primo ſignificat apparitionem re rum, et lucrum & augmentum continentie: et in quare to afpectu Solis fecundo, ſignificat horum oppoſita. Et
translation: of travelers away from home. And it signifies spiritual infirmities of the soul, that is, horrible thoughts, and restlessness of mind, that is, doubts, and other similar things. It also signifies the quality of the mind, and other similar things according to its complexion and conjunction: when it is fortunate, there will be good things according to the kind of fortune that makes it fortunate, and according to the place in which Mercury is fortunate: and when it is evil, there will be iniquities according to the evil that makes it evil, and according to the place in which it was made evil. And it signifies from the sects the worship of unity, and similar things, secretly with hypocrisy and simulation. And some have said that it signifies the thighs, the navel, the comb, the legs, the nerves, and the veins. And from colors it has every mixed and varied color, and alexmenium, which is the color of the wild lily flower: and from tastes, sour. And the quantity of its orb is 7 degrees, and it has from the days the day of Mercury, and from the nights the night of Sunday. Its Fridaria years are 13. Its greater years are 76, its maximum 460, its medium 48, its minor 20. Its strength in the regions of the circle is in the north. And some have said that Mercury from the middle of its retrogradation until the second station signifies childhood: and from the second station until the conjunction of the Sun, youth: and from the conjunction of the Sun until the first station, middle age: and from its first station until the middle of retrogradation, it signifies old age and contrariety: and in its conjunction by direction until the first station, it signifies affection, friendship, imitation of love: and from its second station going to conjunction, it signifies the inquiry of affection and concord: and from its first station going again to retrogradation, it signifies the inquiry of contrariety and dissimulation, slowness, little shame, and occupation and stupor in things: and in the middle of retrogradation, it signifies moderate speed because of the weakness of the intellect: at the second station it signifies stupor, occupation, slowness of intellect, egress: to these, speed, openness of intellects: its conjunction with the Sun by direction, signifies the speed and passion of intellects and their breadth or augmentation. Similarly, it happens to Venus in these places and to the higher planets. And Messahala said: Mercury signifies from the figures of men, a man not very white nor black in color, having a high, long forehead, a long face, a sparse beard on the cheeks, and beautiful eyes, not entirely black, also having long fingers. And from the parts it has the part of negotiation, and it signifies fear and infestation, war and enmities and seductions and contrarieties: it also signifies perfect mastery and subtlety in work and inquiry, and in other things that are done in exact men and contentions. And from the lands it has Adelech, Alcuiden and especially in the south, the lands of the Indians. The Moon is fortunate, feminine, nocturnal, operating with coldness and humidity. And it is the significator of mothers, if the nativity is nocturnal: there is in it a temperate phlegm. And it signifies the infantile age, the beginning of growth. And from its works it has embassies, mandates, works of waters and lands according to the quantity of fortune or misfortune or evil: if it is good, it signifies good, and vice versa. It also signifies principality, if it presides or is dominant over some things pertaining to the king, if it is fortunate from the Sun in a good aspect and in a good place. And from substance it signifies silver, and cultivated land, if it has authority in the fourth. And from faith, religion. And from infirmities, epilepsy, paralysis, falling sickness, facial distortion, dislocation of limbs, commotion, and whatever is similar in coldness and humidity. And the quality of the mind according to its conjunction with the planets. If fortune embraces it, and fortune itself is Venus, it signifies courtesy and kindness, and sweetness of mind, honesty of manners, and swiftness of movement, and divine service: if it is Jupiter, it signifies honesty, caution, and society and kindness and beauty of life: and if it is embraced by evils, if it is Mars, it signifies the study of whispering among men, use in such things: if Saturn, hatred, simulation and envy: if it is the Sun from a praiseworthy aspect, it signifies a royal disposition: if it is Mercury, it signifies rhetoric, that is, eloquence and goodness of tongue and writing. And some have said that it signifies thought, novelty of mind and weakness of sense, and heaviness of tongue, and honest women, nourishment of children, mothers and aunts, preparation of foods. And others have said that it signifies the brain properly and the lung. And from colors it has saffron, and from tastes, salty. And the quantity of its orb is 12 degrees. And it signifies in men the left eye, and in women the right. And from the days it has the day of the Moon, and from the nights the night of Venus. And its Fridaria years are nine: its greater years are 108, its maximum 520, its medium 66 and a half, and according to some 39 and a half: its minor years are 25. And its strength from the regions of the circle is in the right of the west. And Messahala said that it signifies from the figures of men a white man, tinged with redness, with joined eyebrows, benevolent, having eyes not entirely black, a round face, a beautiful stature: and in his face a sign. And from the lands it has Arcoch and Timana and Aldeilam. And some have said that it signifies childhood from the beginning of the month until 7 days, and from 7 days until 14 days youth: and it signifies the age of perfection, that is, middle age, until 21 days and nights: old age until the conjunction, that is, until the end of the month, when it conjoins the sun. And when it is under the Sun's rays, it signifies secrets and hidden things: similarly every planet. And at the beginning of the month when it grows, it signifies everything that is to be done: and in its fullness what is to be destroyed, because it decreases: and at the beginning of the month it and the Sun signify accusers: and at the end of the month the accused: and it signifies at the beginning of the month gain and continence, at the end of the month stipends and dispersions. And it signifies in opposition contrariety, and at the beginning of the change to opposition, that is, when there are 15 degrees between it and the opposition, it signifies the beginning of contrariety and its cause: and when it is separated from the opposition, it signifies the causes of exception from contrariety: and in its exit from under the Sun's rays, it signifies exit from concealment, and similar things: and in its entrance under the rays, it signifies adaptation to concealment: and the hour of division from the Sun, it signifies adaptation of its exit from concealment: and the hour of its exit from under the rays, it signifies appearance and arrival from absence: and while it is in the fourth aspect of the Sun, it signifies diminution of a thing or descent from high to low. Similarly in the second fourth aspect, but in the first fourth aspect it signifies the appearance of things, and gain and increase of continence: and in the second fourth aspect of the Sun, it signifies the opposite of these.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Qualities and Ages
text: i SECUNDA. 227 Et dixerut quidam, quòd Luna à coniunctione quide vfque ad dimidium luminis sui primum,erit natura eius humida: à medietate luminis ſui vfque ad impletio nem, erit natura eius calor : & ab impletione vfque ad dimidium sui luminis fecundum, erit natura eius ficci. tas: Gà dimidio luminis sui secundi vſque ad-coniun. Etionem, erit natura eius frigus. Reliqui verò planeta ab ortu suo vsque ad ſtationem suam primam, in natura humiditatis erunt, & ſignificant pueritiam : Gaſtatione sua prima vsque ad oppoſitionem Solis, erit natura eorum calor, & fignificant iuuentutem: & ab hoc loco vfque ad ſtationem secundam, erit natura corum Ique ad ftationem secundam, ficcitas, & fignificant perfectam ætatem: Gà ſtatio ne sua secunda vsque ad occultationemfuam primam, erit natura corum frigus, & fignificant senectutem fris gidam. 19. Et dixerunt quidam, quòd Sol ſignificat ſpiritum, idest, animam vitalem: Luna cogitationem fenfum: Saturnus mærorem & tristitiam,vilitatem & malum: Iuppiter sapientiam & rationem: Mars iram, suriam & celeritatem: Venus ludum gaudium. Mercurius rationalitatem seu dialecticam difciplinam. 20. Et quia diximus in hoc capitulo de ſignifica tione planetarum, dum complectuntur alteri planete: fimiliter etiam est confiderandum in cæteris rebus que fignificant planeta. : Ff 2 Potes ==Start of OCR for page 1== 283 DIFFERENTIA Poteſtas erraticarum ſtellarum quatuor præcipuis cognofci tur modis. Primò cùm inſita vis & prima ipfarum qualitas ex. ploratur. Secundò cùm loca cœli, quæ ratione longitudinis & laritudinis occupant, perpenduntur. Tertiò, quando ipſas aut ad ſuas adidas & epicyclos, aut ad luminaria atque inuicem có paramus. Denique cum ad ſitum alicuius regionis referuntur. Camenim in vna regione in decima apparent, fieri poteft vt ab alijs in vndecima, vel duodecima, prima, ſecunda, aut alia domo cernantur. De modo ſecundo & quarto, hoc eſt, de fortitudine planetarum in dodecatemoriis, ded; ſignificatione eorundem In cufpidibus ex parte in prima differentia dixit: fed modum primum in hac ſecunda:tertium in tertia proſequitur differentia, Cæterum modum primum trifariam commodè partiemur, nimirum in ſexum, qualitatem & naturam. Nam his tribusre Aè perceptis, maxima & neceſſaria pars huius ſecundæ differen. tiæ fiet perſpicua.. SEXVS PLANETARVM.. In omni genere mares fœminis ſunt.efficaciores,idcirco planetas potentiores maſculos nominant. Cæterum ſexus plane. tarum, vt Ptolemæus tradidit, trifariam diſtinugitur. Primò na tura:qua rationeij,in quorum υγρῷ humiditas caloré non fuperat, vt in 4o &, masculini:ij verò, in quorum.temperatura calor ab humido vincitur, vt in) & &,feminini dicun tur.Sed & ficut alibi, ſic hic ancipitis naturæ eſſe cenfetur, cùm ex æquali ficcitates & humiditates efficiat. Secundò collatione ad. Nam orientales maſculi, occidentales fœminini vocan. tur:hoc eſt, planeta qui Sole horoſcopante ſupra terram verſatur, maſculinus eft:qui infraterram commoratur,fœmininus. Tertiò comparatione ad mundum. Nam commorantes in prima aut tertia quadra, maſculini: in ſecunda verd & quarta fiunt fœminini. SECUNDA. : 229 τὸς πρὸς Ο ληματισμός, ἑσίας μεν γαρ ὄντας κή προπγομένος αρα ρενδοθη, εστποείος δὲ καὶ ἑπομένες θηλυύεθα. κὴ ἔτι πρὰ τὸς πρὸς τὸν ὀρίζοντα. οί μεν γαρ τοῖς ἀλὸ ανατολῆς μεχρί μεσκρανήματα, κ᾿ ἀπὸ δύσεως μεχρί πάλιμ το αντικομένο μεσορανήματα ματισμοῖς, ὡς ἀπηλιωτικός αρρενδοθη. οἱ δὲ τοῖς λοιποῖς δυσὶ τὸ ταρτημορίοις, ώς δυτικός θηλαύεθς. QUALITAS PLANETARVM L Qualitatem hic naturam planetz tempori congruentem intelligimus, atque planetam per diem gaudentem, diurnum:per noctem gaudentem nocturnum vocamus. Qua quidem in reid præcipueſpectandum eft, quòd vt Ptolemæus docet, tempera ti planetæ tempori eiſdem qualitatibus abundanti attribuana tur: Vt & 4 propter temperatam crafin, in qua calor domi natur humido, diurnæ luci accommodantur: fic) & & attri buuntur nocti non ob aliam cauſam, nifi quòd conſtant tempe ratura, in qua vt in noctis craſi, humidum calorem facilè ſupe. rat.Ex intemperatis verò Saturno dies ad.temperandum frigus, nox Marti ad temperandam ipfius ficcitatem, accommodatura: ſiquidem in mala mixtura ſemper deteriores qualitates contra rijs emendare oportet. Hinc ſequitur, neque Saturnum nimis refrigeraturum, nec Martem præter modum deficcaturum, ſi hunc de die, illum per noctem dominari cótingat. Idcircò neuter tantum.vt.folet, nocebit. Ex his perſpicuum eft, quòd tres: fint planetæ diurni, nempèluminare diurnum cũ 1 & 4,quorum:vterque. eſt potentiſsimus, & obid hic infortuna, ille fortuna magna vocantur. Totidemq; reperiuntur nocturni,nempeluminare.nocturnum cum & & &, quorum prior infortuna, poſterior fortuna minor dicitur. At Mercurius quoniam ad vtramque qualitatem ſe.accommodat, ideo modò diurnus eft, præfertim in matutino exortu, modò nocturnus,& imprimis heſperius, tum enim fit frigidior minuſq; calet. Iuxta hac ergo rationem optimè diſponuntur planetæ, diurni quidem, fi de die fint ſupra terram orientales:no&urni verò ſi de nocte itide fint, ſupra terram orientales. Vniuerfaliter enim ficut &), ita quæuis aliarum ſtellarum ſupra quam infra terram eſt effica dior, id quod negauerüt arabes, qui planetas nocturnos de die, Ff. 3 diurnos Digitized by Google ==Start of OCR for page 3== ! 230 DIFFERENTIA diurnos verò de nocte infra terram efficaciores eſſe, quam ſupra terram diſputauerút. Sed abſurdu videbitur fortaſsè alicui etiam illud, quòd dicitur diurnorum planetarum vim in die ſu pra terram magis excitari, cùm in confeſſo ſit, omnia aſtra diur paluce fieri languidiora, quando per præſentiam radiorum ſolarium vis ipforum diſsipatur, atque diffunditur? Ad hanc hæſi tationem Cardanus de ſententia Ptolemæi reſpondet, quòd hæc omnia non ad Solis præſentiam, fedad temporis naturam potifsimum referantur, ita vt planetæ diurni, in die per ſe & ex hac conditione temporis optimè temperari intelligantur, Gue fupra, fiue infra terram reperiantur, fiue fint matutini, fiue vefpertini. Quòd fi Solem interim præſentem habuerint, nihil abſurdi admittetur, ſi eundem ſua præſentia ſtellarum conftitutionem & robur non promouere, ſed potiùs conturbare cre damus. Exempli gratia, ſtella Iouis diurna luce optimè temperatur.Sed hæcipſius efficacia adhuc maior futura eſt, & prater iſtam conditionem fupraterram feratur,præſertim verò, ſi ſit oriétalis liber à radijs ſolaribus. Et vice verſa turbabitur ipfius conditio, fi infra terram lateat, & multò plus, ſi ſit occidentalis, radijſúe ſolaribus inuolutus. Eodem modo reliquorum condi tionem iuuari & minui cogitandum est. Ad extremum id ob- ſeruatione dignum eſt, planetas qualitate ſeu conditione temporis conſentientes vocari eiuſdem αἱρέσεως ſen ſetz, & id ma ximè ſi prætereà etiam ſexu conueniant. translation: And some have said that the Moon, from conjunction up to the first half of its light, will have a humid nature; from the middle of its light up to its fullness, its nature will be hot; and from its fullness up to the second half of its light, its nature will be dry; and from the second half of its light up to conjunction, its nature will be cold. The other planets, from their rising until their first station, will be of a humid nature and signify childhood; from their first station until opposition to the Sun, their nature will be hot and signify youth; and from this point until their second station, their nature will be dry and signify perfect age; and from their second station until their first occultation, their nature will be cold and signify cold old age. And some have said that the Sun signifies the spirit, that is, the vital soul; the Moon, thought and sensation; Saturn, sorrow and sadness, vileness and evil; Jupiter, wisdom and reason; Mars, anger, fury, and swiftness; Venus, play and joy; Mercury, rationality or dialectical discipline. And because we have spoken in this chapter about the signification of the planets when they embrace another planet, similarly, it must also be considered in other things that the planets signify. The power of the wandering stars is known in four principal ways. First, when their inherent force and primary quality are explored. Second, when the places of the heavens, which they occupy by reason of longitude and latitude, are considered. Third, when we compare them either to their epicycles and deferents, or to the luminaries and to each other. Finally, when they are referred to the position of a particular region. For when they appear in the tenth house in one region, it is possible that they are seen by others in the eleventh, or twelfth, first, second, or another house. Concerning the second and fourth modes, that is, concerning the strength of the planets in the dodecatemoria, and concerning their signification in the cusps, he spoke in part in the first difference; but he pursues the first mode in this second difference, and the third in the third difference. However, we will conveniently divide the first mode into three parts, namely, into sex, quality, and nature. For once these three are rightly understood, the greatest and most necessary part of this second difference will become clear. The Sex of the Planets. In every genus, males are more efficacious than females; therefore, they call the more powerful planets masculine. However, the sex of the planets, as Ptolemy taught, is distinguished in three ways. First, by nature: by which reasoning, those in whom humidity does not overcome heat, as in Saturn and Jupiter, are masculine; while those in whom heat is overcome by humidity, as in Venus and the Moon, are called feminine. But Mercury, as elsewhere, is considered to be of an ambivalent nature here, as it produces equal dryness and humidity. Second, by comparison to the Sun. For oriental planets are called masculine, occidental feminine: that is, a planet that is above the earth when the Sun is rising is masculine; one that is below the earth is feminine. Third, by comparison to the world. For those dwelling in the first or third quadrant are masculine; in the second and fourth, they become feminine. Ptolemy's words concerning masculine and feminine stars are these: "It is fitting that they have given the Moon and Venus as feminine stars, because they have more humidity in their essence; and Saturn, Jupiter, and Mars as masculine. Mercury is common to both sexes, as it is equally productive of dry and humid essence. They say that stars are made masculine and feminine by their positions relative to the Sun; for those that are oriental and preceding are made masculine, while those that are occidental and following are made feminine. And also by their positions relative to the horizon. For those in the positions from the east to the midheaven, and from the west to the opposite midheaven, are made masculine as oriental; while those in the other two quadrants are made feminine as occidental." The Quality of the Planets. Here we understand quality as the nature of the planet congruent with the time, and we call a planet that rejoices by day diurnal, and one that rejoices by night nocturnal. In this matter, it is especially to be observed, as Ptolemy teaches, that the temperaments of the planets are attributed to the time abounding in the same qualities: so that Jupiter and the Sun, because of their temperate mixture, in which heat dominates humidity, are accommodated to diurnal light; thus, the Moon and Venus are attributed to night for no other reason than that they consist of a temperament in which, as in the mixture of night, humidity easily overcomes heat. From the intemperate, however, day is accommodated to Saturn to temper his cold, and night to Mars to temper his dryness; since in a bad mixture, it is always necessary to correct worse qualities with their opposites. Hence it follows that neither Saturn will be too cold, nor Mars excessively dry, if the former happens to rule by day, and the latter by night. Therefore, neither will harm as much as usual. From these, it is clear that there are three diurnal planets, namely, the diurnal luminary with Jupiter and Saturn, both of which are most powerful, and for this reason, one is called a malefic, the other a great benefic. And there are found as many nocturnal planets, namely, the nocturnal luminary with Mars and Venus, of which the former is called a malefic, the latter a lesser benefic. But Mercury, since it accommodates itself to both qualities, is sometimes diurnal, especially in its morning rising, and sometimes nocturnal, and especially in the evening, for then it is colder and less hot. According to this reasoning, therefore, the planets are best disposed: the diurnal ones, if they are above the earth and oriental by day; the nocturnal ones, if they are likewise above the earth and oriental by night. For universally, just as the Sun and Moon, so any other of the stars is more efficacious above the earth than below it, which the Arabs denied, who argued that nocturnal planets are more efficacious below the earth by day, and diurnal planets above the earth by night. But it will perhaps seem absurd to someone also that it is said that the power of diurnal planets is more excited above the earth by day, when it is acknowledged that all stars become more languid in diurnal light, when their power is dissipated and diffused by the presence of the Sun's rays? To this hesitation, Cardan responds, according to Ptolemy's opinion, that all these things refer not so much to the presence of the Sun, but rather to the nature of the time, so that diurnal planets are understood to be best tempered by themselves and by this condition of time during the day, whether they are found above or below the earth, whether they are morning or evening stars. But if they have the Sun present in the meantime, nothing absurd will be admitted if we believe that the Sun's presence does not promote but rather disturbs the constitution and strength of the stars. For example, the star of Jupiter is best tempered by diurnal light. But its efficacy will be even greater, and beyond that condition, if it is carried above the earth, especially if it is oriental and free from the Sun's rays. And conversely, its condition will be disturbed if it lies hidden below the earth, and much more so if it is occidental and enveloped in the Sun's rays. In the same way, the condition of the others is to be considered as being helped or diminished. Finally, it is worthy of observation that planets agreeing in quality or condition of time are called of the same sect, and this especially if they also agree in sex. It remains for us to transcribe Ptolemy's words concerning diurnal and nocturnal: "Similarly," he says, "since of the times, these two most manifest intervals occur, that of day, being more masculine because of the heat and activity in it, and that of night, being more feminine because of the humidity and rest in it: they have accordingly given the Moon and Venus as nocturnal stars, and the Sun and Jupiter as diurnal. Mercury is common to these. And if it is a morning star, it is diurnal; if an evening star, it is nocturnal. We have added, however, that the principle of the two sects is not of a corrupting essence. For it is not according to the same natural causes, but according to the opposite ones. For to those of a good temperament, similar things mixed with it produce greater benefit. But to the corrupting ones, unsuitable things mixed with them greatly weaken and disturb the temperament."

Quia longiſsimè vt videtur, cùm à calore Solis, tum verò ab humidis terræ vaporibus diſtat. Cùm autem ſtellæ duplici lumine luceant, proprio videlicet ipſarumq; ſubſtantiæ inharente, & deinde lumine Solis, quod à corpore aſtri terſo ſolidoq; reflectitur, quorum vtrumque ex illuminationibus & defectibus Lunz eſt euidentiſsimum: Idcirco perſpicue ſunt cau- ſe Ptolemæi, ob quas Saturnum frigefacere & aliquantulum de ſiccare dixit. Nam cùm propria ſubſtantia Saturni impurior mi nuſque tenuis fit,& ob magnam diſtantiam ſuam à Sole minùs cæteris tenuiſsimo illo & clarifsimo penetrantiſsimoq; lumine illuſtretur, admodum parum calefacere poteft, & parum calefaciendo refrigerat. Probamus enim hic ſententia Cardani, qui in libris de rerum ſubtilitate, & in commétarijs in Ptolem. ſcriptis, contra vulgares Aſtrologos,&vniuerfam turbam Peripateticorum, omnia cœleftia corpora non τῷ διωάμd ſaltem, vt Ariſtoteles voluit, fed & actu calere, prodidit. Sed Saturni calorem, quia fit languidior, metallis, herbis, arboribus & quibufdam tantùm animalibus, vt ſcorpionibus, conuenire.Eundem membra hominum grauiter lædere ait, non quidem per ſe ſeu ſimpliciter, ſed quia ipſius calor minor fit, quam quæ congruat intenſiori calori, qualem temperatura humana defiderat. Vt enim ſi tepidam aquam feruenti immiſceas, conflabis aquam feruente minùs calidam: ſic calorem exiguum Saturni iunctum calori hominis copioſiori, infrigidare docet.Eodem modo Saturnum, & præfertim hyberno tempore Lusę copulatum, quòdeius calor Lunæ calore fitremifsior,caufam frigoris fieri intelligimus. Adextremum, ſi quando ſolum Saturnum abſque alijs ſtellis dominari contingat, frigus nihilominus augebit.Nam aér ſua natura non eft, vt exiſtimatur,calidus, fed frigidiſsimus,& qualemcunque caliditatem conſequitur, eam ab aſtris mutuatur.Sunt enim duæ tantum qualitates, calor videlicet cæleftis,& humidum elementorum. Frigiditas autem nihil aliud eſt, quam caloris cœleftis ab elementis abſen tia, ficut ficcitas humiditatis eſt priuatio. Saturnus itaque cùm omni aſtro minus caleat, ſemper cauſa frigoris exiſtit, quotiefcunque ipſe potens factus, ſe aliorum actionibus caliditatem efficienDigitized by Google SECUNDA. 233 efficientibus admiſcet. Vt enim vinum aéreum copia vini aquoſi dilutum, ad deteriorem, hoc eſt, vicinioré aquoſo quàm aéreo formam traducitur:ita Saturni vis exuberans aliorum vi ribus minus efficacibus admixta,pariet in aëre effectus maximè ſuæ naturæ congruentes, hoceſt, parum calefacietaërem, qui cùm ſua natura æterno gelu rigens omni calore priuetur, ideo parum calefactus, ingens adhuc frigus retinebit. Ex his perfpicua eſt cauſa, ob quam Saturni qualitatem Ptolemæus fri gidificam eſſe dixit. Ad huncmodum etiam reliqua tam meantia quàm cælo inherentia ſidera ſuum calorem habere, ſed calida, frigida aliaúe qualitate prædita propter effectus in homine nominaridicimus. Reliquum eſt vt oftendatur cauſa, ob quam Saturni qualitatem aliquantulum ficcam eſſe dixit. Siccum & humidum oriuntur ab vna qualitate, hoc eſt, à cæleſti calore, ſed tamen cùm materia, tum verò quátitate differente. Vt enim in igne noſtro tenues eius ſpiritus teſtas penetrantes calorem vaporarijs inducere, craſſam verò eiufdem fuliginem non penetrando, ſed teſtis faltem adhereſcendo calefacere notamus: ita eodem modo differentia cæleſtis caloris maximi, parui, ze nuis craſsique cogitanda eſt. Hic ſi ſit maximus & tenuis, facili. mehumidum aéreæ regionis penetrabit, & velociteraérom pe netrando non deficcabit eum, ſed potius in aqueos vapores re- ſoluet & humectabit. Et vice verſa ſi calor craſſus ſit, ægrè aë- rem penetrat, prefertim verò ſi ſit exiguus. Quapropter cùm ocyùs aérem penetrare nequeat, non diſſoluet aërem ſed cogendo deficcabit ſimulatque calefaciet. Saturni itaque calor cùm. ſit exiguus, vt diximus, & ſubſtantia eius impurior &ad pene. trandum inepta:meritò leuiter exiccare dicitur. Martis autem ſubſtantia cum ſit adhucimpurior, minus penetrabit, plus tamé propter copiam caloris deficcabit. Iouis denique & Veneris, So lis & Lunæ corpora propter tenuitatem multitudinemque lu minis calefaciunt & humectant, ſed inæqualiter. Nam Sol omnium calorem ſuperat.Hinc Iuppiter Venere, Venus Luna eſt calidior:& vice verſa Luna Venerem, & Venus Iouem humidi tate facilè excedit. translation: This means: Since there are two evident distinctions of times, namely day and night, and day is more congruent with masculine nature, both because there is greater heat during the day and because of the opportunity for action; and night is more congruent with feminine nature, because of humidity and suitability for rest: Therefore, they appropriately handed down that the nocturnal stars are Mars and Saturn, and the diurnal stars are Jupiter and the Sun. Mercury is of both conditions, diurnal when eastern, and nocturnal when western. Finally, they attributed one of the malefics to each condition, following not the similarity of quality, but diversity. For when similar qualities are familiarly coupled with stars of good temperament, they increase their goodness. But dissimilar qualities mixed with destructive ones restrain the vehemence of their malice. Hence, they joined Saturn, who is cold, to diurnal heat, and Mars, who is dry, to nocturnal humidity. In this way, each, having attained a symmetry of qualities from a contrary constitution, achieves a temperate condition. CONCERNING THE NATURE OF THE PLANETS. In explaining the nature of the planets, two things are especially to be observed. First, the primary qualities, that is, which planets are hot, cold, humid, or dry. Second, how, from the measure of the primary qualities, proceeding from the magnitude of heat, the genera of consonant effects are distributed to individual planets. Pliny and Ptolemy are authorities that the star of Saturn is of a cold and rigid nature: hence it has the power of cooling and slightly drying, and as the author says, it signifies the gravity of cold and dryness, which is sufficiently confirmed by experience, that when placed in a hot sign it diminishes heat, and in a cold sign it increases frost, condenses the air, and fills it with pestilential clouds. Ptolemy's words about its nature are these: 'The quality in the star of Saturn is cooling and somewhat drying. Because it is, as it seems, very far both from the heat of the Sun and from the moist vapors of the earth.' Since stars shine with a double light, namely their own inherent substance, and then the light of the Sun, which is reflected from a polished and solid astral body, both of which are most evident from the illuminations and defects of the Moon: Therefore, Ptolemy's reasons are clear, why he said Saturn cools and somewhat dries. For since Saturn's own substance is more impure and less subtle, and because of its great distance from the Sun it is less illuminated than others by that very subtle, clear, and penetrating light, it can heat very little, and by heating little, it cools. For we prove here the opinion of Cardano, who in his books on subtle matters, and in his commentaries on Ptolemy, written against common astrologers and the entire crowd of Peripatetics, declared that all celestial bodies are hot not only potentially, as Aristotle wished, but also actually. But Saturn's heat, because it is weaker, is suitable only for metals, herbs, trees, and certain animals, such as scorpions. He says that it severely harms human limbs, not indeed by itself or simply, but because its heat is less than what is suitable for the more intense heat that human temperament desires. For just as if you mix lukewarm water with hot, you will produce water less hot than the original hot water: so the small heat of Saturn joined to the more abundant heat of man, teaches to cool. In the same way, we understand that Saturn, and especially when coupled with the Moon in winter, because its heat is weaker than the Moon's heat, becomes a cause of cold. Finally, if it ever happens that Saturn alone dominates without other stars, it will nevertheless increase cold. For air by its nature is not, as is supposed, hot, but very cold, and whatever heat it acquires, it borrows from the stars. For there are only two qualities, namely celestial heat and elemental humidity. Coldness, however, is nothing other than the absence of celestial heat from the elements, just as dryness is the privation of humidity. Therefore, since Saturn heats less than any other star, it is always a cause of cold, whenever it becomes powerful and mixes itself with the actions of others that produce heat. For just as wine diluted with an abundance of watery wine is reduced to a worse form, that is, closer to watery than to airy: so the exuberant force of Saturn, mixed with the less effective forces of others, will produce effects in the air most congruent with its nature, that is, it will heat the air little, which, since by its nature it is rigid with eternal frost and deprived of all heat, therefore, being heated little, will still retain immense cold. From these, the reason is clear why Ptolemy said Saturn's quality is cooling. In this way, we also say that the other stars, both wandering and fixed in the sky, have their heat, but are named hot, cold, or endowed with other qualities because of their effects on man. It remains to show the reason why he said Saturn's quality is somewhat dry. Dry and humid arise from one quality, that is, from celestial heat, but differing both in matter and in quantity. For just as in our fire we note that its subtle spirits penetrating the vessels induce heat into the vapors, but its thick soot, not penetrating but merely adhering to the vessels, heats them: so in the same way, the difference of celestial heat, whether great, small, subtle, or thick, must be considered. If it is very great and subtle, it will most easily penetrate the humid air of the region, and by quickly penetrating the air, it will not dry it, but rather resolve it into watery vapors and moisten it. And conversely, if the heat is thick, it penetrates the air with difficulty, especially if it is small. Therefore, since Saturn's heat is small, as we said, and its substance is more impure and unfit for penetration: it is rightly said to dry slightly. But since Mars's substance is still more impure, it will penetrate less, but will dry more due to the abundance of heat. Finally, the bodies of Jupiter and Venus, the Sun and the Moon, due to the subtlety and multitude of light, heat and moisten, but unequally. For the Sun surpasses all in heat. Hence Jupiter is hotter than Venus, and Venus is hotter than the Moon: and conversely, the Moon easily exceeds Venus, and Venus exceeds Jupiter in humidity. Therefore, Jupiter, or as we should rather recite Ptolemy's words in Greek, 'the star of Jupiter has a temperate productive power, being in the middle of its motion, and that according to the times.'

Etenim propter vicinitatem ad Solem aliquantulum faltem calidifica eſt, ſed vehementer quemadmodum & Luna humectat, propter amplitudinem cor poris ſui, attrahens ex locis terræ vicinis humidam exhalationem.Plinius lib.2.cap.8.fcripfit, huius ſideris natura cuncta generari in terris. Namque in alterutro exortu genitali rore con- ſpergens, non terræ modò conceptus implet, verùm animantium etiam omnium ſtimulat. Et in cap. 16. eiuſdem libri dicit, Veneris ſtella ab Ecliptica partibus 8. euagantem efficere, vt quædam animantia & in deſertis mundi naſcantur. Hæc quando manè ante exortum Solem conſpicitur, εωσφόρο & φωσφό Platine lucifer, quaſi lucem prænuncians accelerantq;:quando verò poſt occaſum Solis fulgens lucem prorogat atque poſt eum occidit, o, id eft, veſper nominatur, quă vocem Virgilius vfurpauit verſuiſto: Venit Heſperus, ite Capellæ, id eſt, cerni incipit, non oritur. Nam Heſperus nunquam in oriente conſpicitur, neclucifer in occidente. Dicitur etiam veſperugo. Vitruuiuslib.9. Veneris ſtella quum Solem ſequitur poſt occafum eius apparens in cælo clariſsimeque lucens, veſperugo vo citatur. Mercuriú à radiando ſeu corruſcatione σίλβοντα nominant. Hic naturam habet ita verſatilem, vt imitetur qualitazes earum ſtellarum cum quibus copulatus fuerit: veluti fi auto iungatur, ficcus erit & maleficus:fit familiaritatis teſtimonio conſentiat, frigidus futurus eft, & intemperatus.Sed ſi 4 & aut copuletur, calidus humiduſque, & ob id temperazus & beneficus efficietur. Hæc res occaſionem præbuiſſe videtur poëtis, vt eum nuncium atque miniſtrum deorum eſſe docerent:quandoquidem à luminarium & reliquorum planeta. rum omnium,imò verò etiam à maiorum ſtellarum inerran. tium viribus vincitur & mutatur, inque ipforum naturam à quibus ſuperatur, tráſit. Id verò accidit propter tres cauſas.Pri ma eſt, quòd & inter omnes ſtellas ſit minimus, adeo vt ſenſu vix percipiatur. Secunda, quòd inter planetas ſit leuiſsimus,& pcyſsimè circumductus efficiat influxum minus efficacem,qui S Gg 2 tardio- 246 DIFFERENTIA tardiorum aſtrorum influxu validiori facilè ſuccumbat. Deni que quòd fidus & taliſitpręditum ingenio, quòd in vtramque naturam tranſire & mutari paratum fit. Ptolemæus tradit eum deficcare & hume&are æqualiter.Ac deficcare quidem, quòdà Sole non longè, id eſt,amplius p.28.nunquam abſcedat:Hume- &are verò, quòd humidum Lunæcælum contingat, Subitas ve rò mutationes efficere in vtranque partem inflatus ſeu incitatus quaſi celeritudine, qua circa vehitur.Idem ventorú maximusautor eſt, cùm quòd circa Solem verſetur, tu verò quòd in ſuo circulo breui, quem ἐπίκυκλου nominant, veluti virga quædam ſtridens ocyſsimè circúductus aërem concutiat. Verba Ptolemæi hæc funt: ὁδιτ8 ἔρμο, ὡς ἐπίπαμ ἐξ ἴσο πότε μπά ξηραντικός καταλαμβάνεται. κὶ τὴν ὑγρῶμαὐαποτικός, αλά το με δέποτε πολύ το θερμασίας κατὰ μῆκθ ἀφίς απς, πόδια υγραντικός, Αλὰ τὸ τῇ ποριγφοτάτη σφαίρα τ) ἐπικεῖθη: τας χείας δὲ ποιεῖθη τὰς ἐπαμφότερα μεταβολάς πνευματόμενο ὥσπερ ὑπὸ τ' ἐπι αὐτομ Ο ὀξυκινησίας. Ad extremum Lunam vehementer humectare & calefacere leniter indicium eſt, quòd dormientes ſub dio humido alieno repleantur. Et carnes lumi ni Lunæ expofitæ humido & calido auto, humiditate tamen calorem vincente ſtatim putrefcut. Hinc Ptolemæus ipſam cor pora imprimis putrefacere dixit, & rectè: Nam cùm putredo prohibeatur,aut cùm calor dominatur humido, aut cùm deficit humidum, aut denique quando deficit calidum: Luna autem dominante nihil horum poſsit fieri: ſequitur Lunam nimia ſua humiditate & exiguo calore cauſam præcipuam eſſe pu eredinis. Tradit autem eam ideo abundare humore, quòdad ſedem elementorum proximè accedat: exiguo verò calore effe praditam, quòd alienis, hoc eſt, Solis radijs accenſa luceat. Verbacius hæc funt: 2 τὸ Η πλέομ ἔχει το διυάμεως οι τῷ υγραί ναμ, σχοὰ πιώ τουλιγφότητα δηλονότι,κή τιὰ τὴν ὑγρῶν αἰαθυμίαση, κή διατίθκουμ ὅτω αντικρυς τὰ σώματα πεπαίνεσα καί διαστής πόσα τὰ πλεῖςα, κεκοινώνηκε δι κρέμα κλ το θερμαίνομλλὰ τὸς ἀλό Η κλία φωτισμός, hoc eſt, Luna proptereà quòd proximèterram fertur, vnde humidę exhalationes exeűt, plurimum madefacit. Atque ita prorſus ſubiecta corpora & mollia reddit & pu trefacit. Habet tamen propter illuſtrationem Solis etiam cale faciendi
translation: Jupiter's star is of a temperate nature, as it is carried between Saturn, which cools, and Mars, which burns. It both heats and moistens. Because the power of heating prevails due to the underlying spheres, it is productive of fertile spirits. And it could not be otherwise than that Jupiter would be called fertile. For where the warm dominates the moist, there is temperance, fertility, and the best life, which seems to be the reason why the ancients named this star Ziva from living, and Jupiter as if a helping father. From the same source, it can be understood that he is the author of winds. For warmth joined with moisture, with the heat prevailing, as we note in 4, generates spirits; with the moisture prevailing, as in f, vapors. Mars' star both dries and burns, just as it corresponds to its fiery color and its proximity to the Sun, since the solar sphere is subject to it. The Sun's heat is very moist and very subtle. Therefore, by its nature, it heats more languidly, and at least dries according to quality. For Ptolemy says: The Sun is found to have the nature of heat and gradually of dryness. We perceive this much more certainly than in other stars, both because of its magnitude and the evident changes of the seasons: for the closer it approaches our vertical place, the more we experience what we have said. Venus's star, according to Ptolemy's opinion, has the same nature as Jupiter, but conversely. For it gently heats and excellently moistens. But Venus, he says, is the same as Jupiter in its temperate nature, but conversely, it is productive, for it heats gently due to its proximity to the Sun, but it moistens most vehemently, like the Moon, due to the magnitude of its own light, attracting moist exhalation from the surrounding waters of the earth. Pliny, in book 2, chapter 8, wrote that by the nature of this star, all things are generated on earth. For in either of its risings, sprinkling with generative dew, it not only fills the conceptions of the earth but also stimulates all living beings. And in chapter 16 of the same book, he says that Venus's star, wandering 8 parts from the Ecliptic, causes certain animals to be born even in the deserts of the world. When it is seen in the morning before sunrise, it is called Eosphoros and Phosphoros, the morning star, as if announcing and accelerating the light; but when it shines after sunset and prolongs the light and sets after it, it is called Hesperos, that is, the evening star, which word Virgil used in his verse: Hesperus comes, go, Goats, that is, it begins to be seen, it does not rise. For Hesperus is never seen in the east, nor Lucifer in the west. It is also called Vesperugo. Vitruvius, in book 9, says that when Venus's star follows the Sun, appearing after its setting in the sky and shining very brightly, it is called Vesperugo. They call Mercury Silbonta from radiating or flashing. This star has such a versatile nature that it imitates the qualities of the stars with which it is conjoined: as if it is joined with Saturn, it will be dry and malefic; if it agrees with the testimony of familiarity, it will be cold and intemperate. But if it is conjoined with Jupiter or Venus, it will be warm and moist, and therefore temperate and benefic. This seems to have given occasion to poets to teach that he is the messenger and minister of the gods: since he is overcome and changed by the forces of the luminaries and all other planets, and indeed even by the greater fixed stars, and passes into their nature by which he is overcome. This happens for three reasons. The first is that among all stars, he is the smallest, so much so that he is barely perceived by the senses. The second is that among the planets, he is the lightest, and being carried around very quickly, he produces a less effective influx, which easily succumbs to the stronger influx of slower stars. Finally, because the star is endowed with such a nature that it is ready to pass into and be changed into either nature. Ptolemy states that it dries and moistens equally. And it dries indeed because it never departs far from the Sun, that is, more than 28 degrees: but it moistens because it touches the moist sphere of the Moon. But it produces sudden changes in both directions, inflated or excited as if by the speed with which it is carried around. It is also the greatest author of winds, both because it revolves around the Sun, and because in its short circle, which they call epicycle, it shakes the air very quickly, like a whistling rod. These are Ptolemy's words: Mercury is generally found to be equally drying and moistening, but never far from the heat, and moistening because it is placed in the outermost sphere: and it makes quick changes in both directions, as if animated by the swift motion around it. Finally, the Moon vehemently moistens and gently heats, an indication of which is that those sleeping outdoors are filled with foreign moisture. And flesh exposed to the light of the Moon, being moist and warm, but with moisture overcoming heat, immediately putrefies. Hence Ptolemy said that it primarily causes bodies to putrefy, and rightly so: For since putrefaction is prevented either when heat dominates moisture, or when moisture is deficient, or finally when heat is deficient: and when the Moon dominates, none of these can happen: it follows that the Moon, by its excessive moisture and slight heat, is the principal cause of putrefaction. He states that it abounds in moisture because it is closest to the seat of the elements: but it is endowed with slight heat because it shines illuminated by foreign, that is, the Sun's rays. His words are these: The Moon has more power to moisten, obviously because of its proximity, and because of the moist exhalation, and thus it softens and putrefies most of the bodies subject to it. But it also partakes of heating due to the Sun's illumination.

In hoc textu quatuor infignia capita diligenter obſeruanda perfectè nobis oftendit Ptolemæus. Primò enim declarat materiam ſeu ſubiecta, quibus leſiones morbique imprimi ſolent, nempe domum ſeptimam, inde primam, deinde ſextam,quæ horoſcopo inconiuncta eſt. Rationi autem conſentaneum eft, atque obferuatum eſſe dicit Cardanus, primam & ſeptimam olm vitia, ſextam verò τὰ πάθη,id eft, morbos fufcipere.Secundò deſcribit talium peftium cauſas effectrices, nempe malefico rum corpora, & hofticos radios corundem. Quum itaque hæ cauſe cum fubiecta materia concurrunt, natorum corpora mul tipliciter offendunt:pręſertim verò ſi luminaria fimul prehendant, vel oppoſita radiatione, aut etiam quadrata pulſent. Tersid docet id, de quo principaliter hoc loco agimus, nempe natu ram & qualitatem egritudinum eruendam eſſe ex quatuor potiſsimum circunftantijs. Primò ex finitoris ſeu locorum mundi natura, nempe domus primæ vel ſeptimæ, ſextæ vel duodecimæ, has enim morbos, illas verò vitia ſeu leſiones plerunque oſtendere paulò antè dicebamus. Inde ex natura duplicilocorum in ſignifero, nempe à ſiderum imaginibus,quas μορφώμα τὼ vocant, & à dodecatemorijs, que ab interfectione vernali nu merantur.
