translation: TRANSFER of nature from a lighter to a heavier planet. 6. If, however, neither of these is joined to the other, but each is joined to a third, then if that third planet aspects some place in the circle, it returns their light to that place: this is called the return of light. 7. Again, if one planet is not joined to another, but a third planet has transferred light to both, then this conjunction is also called the return of light. 8. Next follows prohibition, and it occurs in two ways. One, namely, by conjunction, that is, when there are three planets in one sign, but in different degrees: if the heavier planet has more degrees, then the one in the middle prohibits the former, that is, the one with fewer degrees, from joining the heavier until it passes it. In the second way, if two planets are in one sign, and the lighter joins the heavier, and another also joins the same heavier planet by aspect: then the one that is with it in one sign prohibits the aspecting planet from conjunction with the heavier, if the degrees of the one joining and the one aspecting are equal, that is, of the same number. But if the one aspecting is closer in degrees to the heavier, it will be the conjunction of the aspecting planet. 9. And if a planet is joined to the ruler of the sign in which it is, or to the ruler of its exaltation, or to the ruler of its other dignities, it is said to 'strike', that is, to send the nature of that ruling planet, namely, of the same dignity, to it. 10. And if a planet is in one of its dignities, and is joined to another planet which also has a part of dignity in the same place: it also sends to it both natures, its own and that of the one to which it is joined: and this entire sending is called Alcochod, that is, reception. 11. Now follows the return, that is, when a planet returns virtue to another planet that is combust, that is, under the Sun's rays, or retrograde, because of its own weakness, which it cannot retain. Then if both planets are in an angle or succedent houses, the return will be with profit: similarly if the one joining it is only in an angle, and the one to which it is joined receives it. But if the planet that joins is cadent from an angle, and the one to which it is joined is in an angle or succedent house, or if both are cadent, the return will be with detriment. 12. Next follows Almenen, that is, refranation, which occurs when a planet wants to join another, but before it joins, retrogradation occurs, and thus its conjunction is destroyed. 13. This is followed by Alichorad, that is, accidental contrariety. This occurs when a light planet has many degrees in a sign, and another, heavier than it, has fewer degrees, and a third, lighter than the first, wants to join the heavier: but before it joins it, the lighter one with more degrees becomes retrograde, and joins and applies to that heavy one, and passing it, also joins another lighter planet: and thus the conjunction of the former with the heavy one is destroyed. 14. Next follows Alfazim, that is, frustration. This also occurs when a planet seeks the conjunction of another planet, but before it reaches it, this one changes into another sign, and there will be some planet in few degrees aspecting that sign, and its rays will be at the beginning of the sign. And when the following planet leaves the first sign, it joins this aspecting one, and the conjunction it had with that one, namely with the first, is annulled. 15. Hence follows the cutting off of light, that is: when a planet seeks the conjunction of another, and is in the second sign from the sign of the other planet to which it is joined: but before it joins it, the one in the second sign first becomes retrograde, and joins it and cuts off its light from the planet that previously wanted to join. 16. Similarly, if a light planet is applying to the conjunction of another planet, and that other planet to which it wants to join seeks the conjunction of another planet heavier than itself: but before the light planet reaches the degrees of the heavier one, the heavier one joins another planet heavier than itself, and cuts off its light from the first lighter planet. 17. Next follows the fourth kind of passions that happen to planets when compared to each other. The first event of this kind is syzygy, or conjunction: which occurs when stars are either in one region of the sky, or in the radiation of fire, or finally, when they aspect each other by antiscion. For the word syzygy is general, and signifies not only application or coition, but also testimony by aspect. And Ptolemy's phrase in the fourth book of his Apotelesmatics sufficiently indicates that these words are to be distinguished in this way, where, speaking of the geometricians, he wrote: 'nor do the rhomboidal ones, which are distant from the anaphas, receive good wives by conjunction and by testimony, if the malefics are contrary.' Furthermore, of conjunctions, some are partile or perfect, others are platical or imperfect. But of those, etc. imperfect. And of partile conjunctions, some are of longitude, others of latitude, the most absolute of all conjunctions. And in a partile conjunction of longitude, stars are joined when they are found either in one circle described through the points of the zodiac, or in different ones, but nevertheless aspecting each other partilely through parts of the ecliptic, even if they are separated by latitude towards the sides of the world. As if the Sun is at the beginning of Leo, and Mars is there with its maximum southern latitude, then Mars is said to be partilely joined to the Sun according to longitude, even if they are separated by a space of seven degrees of latitude in the sky. Similarly, one should judge concerning the conjunctions of aspects. But stars cohere in a partile conjunction of latitude when, besides the agreement of longitude, they also agree in latitude. This is observed by Ptolemy, neglecting aspects in the bodily conjunctions of the Sun and stars, and also, as it seems, in diametrical oppositions, whence it deserves to be called a bodily syzygy. And although the bodies of the stars pass to opposition in at least one case: nevertheless, it is manifest that they can meet in bodies in several ways. First, if, as the author says, in the application of conjunction, tending simultaneously to one side of the world, what latitude they acquire. As if the Moon and Mars, agreeing in minutes of longitude, and tending simultaneously to the maximum northern limit of latitude, or thence to the ascending node, have equal latitude, they will conjoin most perfectly both in longitude and in latitude. Then stars can be corporally and most perfectly conjoined, even if they travel to different limits or nodes, provided their latitudes are of the same form, that is, northern or southern and equal: and this is what the author says, that in this case it must happen that the latitude of one is ascending in the north, and that of the other descending in the north: or of one ascending in the south, and of the other descending in the south. For example, if the Moon and Saturn agree in parts and minutes according to longitude such that Saturn is near its ascending node, and having entered the north, has already acquired a northern latitude of three minutes: and the Moon, descending from the northern limit, is approaching its descending node, decreasing its northern latitude, and is similarly distant from the Ecliptic by three minutes, a bodily syzygy of the Moon and Saturn will be effected, that is, a most perfect conjunction of bodies, as far as longitude and latitude are concerned, so much so that the Moon in such a meeting of those stars would deprive us of their sight, unless a very large parallax, or, as they call it, diversity of aspect, prevented it. Then, from the bodily coition, beyond and on this side, forwards and backwards, by the space that the apparent semidiameters of both luminaries compose, receding, they maintain a platical conjunction as long as, as will be said presently, their rays are mixed. From what I have said, what a bodily syzygy of stars is, is clear. It remains for us to explain the bodily configuration, that is, the bodily conjunctions of aspects, of which the diametrical one not only has diverse and equal, but also contrary latitudes, that is, one northern, the other southern.

Quòd verò in aſpe Aibus latitudines obferuare non conueniat, caufam dicit effe, quòd non conſiderentur radij planetarum, vt exvno in extremitate mundi perueniunt ad alterum, sed vt ambo radii perueniuntad centrum terræ,& in ipfis angulum faciunt: verbacius In fine libri primi Apoteleſmaticorum hæc sunt: καὶ καθόλο δὲ συνάπλμ μὲν λέγονται δῖς ἑπομοίοις οι προηγόμονοι. translation: 2. A table explaining the latitude of the trigon and hexagon of stars more than 8 parts distant from the Ecliptic. This is a clear ratio of bodily conjunctions, not involved in any obscure ambiguities, and if you consider the matter correctly, it almost agrees with Ptolemy's tradition concerning the equicrural figure explained above. However, Blanchinus and many other astrologers, by no means common, apply this theory not only to the conjunctions of bodies, but also to all other aspects and simple radiations, and they direct such empty places for stars, just as they observe the latitude of stellar bodies significantly distant from the Ecliptic. And since the circles of latitudes passing through the poles of the Ecliptic and the limits of radiations having latitude do not coincide at the limits of radiations simply calculated on the Ecliptic in the manner described above, but extend beyond the hexagon and within the trigon, therefore, in order to establish some equation of aspects, they use "prothaphairia" (scruples), the multitude of which varies according to the latitude of the planets whose aspects are being considered, as the series of numbers described here indicates to you. Latitude of planet. Scruples of prothaphairia. Latitude of planet. Scruples of prothaphairia. These scruples are added to hexagons and subtracted from trigons. For example, Mars, located at 15 degrees 49 minutes 1 second of Capricorn, with a southern latitude of 7 parts, has a sinister sextile aspect falling at 15 degrees 49 minutes 1 second of Pisces. If you add 111 scruples, corresponding to seven parts of latitude, you will find the equated sinister aspect of Mars falls exactly at the end of 16 degrees of Pisces, with a southern latitude of 3 degrees 29 minutes 19 seconds 37 parts 28. However, the sinister trine radiation of the same Mars, which, simply calculated by longitude, falls at 15 degrees 49 minutes 1 second of Taurus, when it omits the same 111 scruples due to the equation, ends at 15 degrees 48 minutes of Taurus, with a northern latitude of 3 degrees 29 minutes 1 second 37 parts 2. The same judgment applies to dexter radiations. I, however, could find no cause worthy of mention that could or should have impelled practitioners to follow this laborious and troublesome operation, which seems to display much ostentation and no fruit. Some have presented it as fortified by the authority of Monteregius. But not carefully enough. For Monteregius rejects this method of operation as weak and useless, so far is he from approving it, and teaches how to translate the bare and simple radiations of stars endowed with latitude to the Ecliptic without any observation of latitude, in this way: Every star, like all others, also emits those most efficacious rays which we observe in an orbicular manner, so that for a hexagonal radiation we have the periphery of that circle, which is described by one side of a large celestial hexagon inscribed in such a way that one extremity of the said side, ending at the center of the star, remains fixed, and the side itself, together with the limiting extension, is rotated so that it returns to the place from which it began to move. Similarly, one must judge concerning all other radiations, except that Monteregius, no differently than Blanchinus, wished the place of a square radiation to always be distant from the place of the planet's longitude by a quadrant of the Ecliptic, and the opposite radiation to end at the diameter's terminus. These things being so, it is clear that a hexagonal ray does not penetrate the Ecliptic in any part of itself if the star's latitude exceeds 60 parts; but it intersects the Ecliptic in two places if the star's latitude is less than 60 parts. Example: Let it be decreed to explore to what parts of the Ecliptic Mars projects its sextile rays, being placed at 15 degrees 49 minutes 1 second of Capricorn, with a southern latitude of 7 parts. If you now wish to clearly experience this on a celestial globe, first of all find the place of Mars, both in longitude and latitude, on the instrument. This done, then with a compass, opened to the interval of the side of the hexagon calculated on the Ecliptic, or what is the same, on the equinoctial, describe from the place of Mars as a center the periphery of a circle, representing the hexagonal radiation of Mars, which periphery, intersecting the Ecliptic in two places equally distant from the star on either side, that is, 59 degrees 45 minutes 1 second, will define to the left the limit of the sinister sextile radiation, and to the right the terminus of the dexter sextile radiation. In the same way, one must think about the termini of both trine and square radiations. The table of rays inscribed here, which defines the places of both trine and sextile radiations, explains to you the variety of these intervals, which different stellar latitudes create. TABLE OF RAYS for the hexagonal and trine radiation of Monteregius. Arc of Latitude, Ecliptic. If, therefore, according to Monteregius's opinion, you wish to equate the radiations or aspects of stars endowed with noteworthy latitude, and to prepare them for deductions, which they call directions, within the table of rays with the planet's latitude, an arc of the Ecliptic will immediately present itself to you, defining the longitude of the dexter and sinister hexagonal radiation to be calculated from the place of the star's longitude. When this is subtracted from 60 parts, the scruples of equation will remain to be subtracted from the longitude of both sextile radiations, but to be added to the longitude of both trine radiations, so that the equated longitude of all these aspects may appear. According to this method, therefore, Mars, constituted at 15 degrees 49 minutes 1 second of Capricorn, with a northern latitude of 7 parts, extends its dexter hexagonal ray to 15 degrees 34 minutes 18 seconds of Scorpio, and its sinister ray to 15 degrees 34 minutes 1 second of Pisces. But it projects its dexter trine radiation to 16 degrees 41 minutes of Virgo, and its sinister to 16 degrees 41 minutes of Taurus. The longitudes of square radiations, whose dexter extends to 15 degrees 49 minutes 1 second of Libra, and sinister to 15 degrees 49 minutes 1 second of Aries, as has been said, require no such equation. Similarly, neither does the longitude of the diameter, which ends at 15 degrees 49 minutes 1 second of Cancer. However, stars lacking latitude, or rather, not more than 1 degree 4 minutes 1 second distant from the Ecliptic, universally maintain the longitude of all radiations without any equation. From these, the difference between the operations of Blanchinus and Monteregius is clear. For in one, the longitude of the hexagons is increased, while in the other, it is diminished. A similar contradiction is observed in the equation of trine radiations. Leaving it open which of these opinions is truer, for two main reasons it seemed best not to approve either at present: first, because however great the latitude, it can make little difference to the aspects, whether taken from the Ecliptic or by the said methods, so much so that eight parts of latitude make scarcely 20 scruples of difference in places in the ray. The second and most important reason is the authority of Ptolemy, who stated that latitudes should be observed not in the aspects of rays, but in the bodily conjunctions of stars, and then only if the stars meet in one sign. Example: If the Moon is 16 parts distant from the Sun, having a latitude of 7 or 8 parts, it will certainly be outside the Sun's rays. The reason why it is not appropriate to observe latitudes in aspects, he says, is that the rays of the planets are not considered as they come from one to the extremity of the world to the other, but as both rays come to the center of the earth, and form an angle in them. His words at the end of the first book of Apotelesmatics are these: "And generally, those that precede are said to be conjoined with those that follow, and those that follow are said to be separated from those that precede, when there is a great distance between them. And this is taken easily, especially if it happens according to the given calculations, except that for the bodily conjunctions and emanations themselves, and latitudes, one must observe."

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Latitudes and Aspects
text: ὐτὴν χρήσιμομ, εἰς το μονας, τὰς τι τὰ αὐτὰ μείρα τα λλὰ μέσομ ευρισκομοίας πρόδος πρας δέχεσθα,. : Digitized by Google ==Start of OCR for page 1== 328 DIFFERENTIA δέχεθζ. πρὸς δὲ γόμ γινόμλνομ αιμαλισμόμτποπιπόμ ἔςι το διστομο πασῶν ἀεὶ τὴν ἀκτίνωμ ἐπι ταυτά, τοτέςιμ ἐπὶὸ κάτρομη γῆς φερομοίωμ, καλόμοίως πανταχόθεμ συμβαλοσῶμ,hoceft: Vaiuerfaliter autem præcedentes ſequentibus dicuntur applicare. Sequentes verò à precedentibus defluere, quæcunque longo interuallo minimè diſtiterint, id quod tam de corporeis illarum congreſsibus, quam configurationibus intelligédum eſt, nifi quòd in corporum applicationibus & defluxibus etiam latitudines earum obferuare vtile eſt: fiquidem non nifi ij congreſſus admittuntur, qui ad eafdem partes Eclipticæ reperiuntur. In configurationibus autem huiufmodi curare ſuperuacaneum eft, cùm radij vniuerfi eodem, id eſt, ad centrum terrz Ferantur, atque ita vndecunque emiſsi ſemper concurrant Ex his verbis Ptolemzi ſequi videtur, quòd in Selis coniun&ionibus latitudo obfervanda fit,& in nullis alijs aſpectibus. Et quanquam hunc textů ita exponat Cardanus, &res illud ipſum habeat, non enim poteſt ſtellarum corporea efficiconiun&io, niſi latitudines habeant æquales, & eas non tantùm in vno circulo latitudinis, ſed etiam ad eafdem partes Eclipticæ ſitas: nihilominus tamen arbitramur, in alijs quoque aſpettibus ob- ſeruationem ſtellarum latitudinis, ſi non ad varietatem interuallorum, ſed robur afpectuum referatur, plurimum habere momenti.Sienim, exempli gratia, Martis & Veneris fiat oppoſi tio, cui dubium eſſe poteſt, talem oppoſitionem longè fore efficaciorem potentioremque, ſi Mars & Venus contrarias latitudines habeant, quam ſi ſimul ſeptentriones vel meridiem lu- ſtrarent, alias nullum locum habere poſſent ea, quæ fuprà ex ſententia Ptolemzi de figura æquicruria dicta funt. Et vttriquetram radiationemiam filentio præteream, non dubium eft, alios corporales intuitus omnes robur aſſumere, ſi ab vna parte Eclipticæ ferantur, & econtrarid fieri imbecilliores, ſi latitudines contrarias habeant. Hanc ob cauſam ſcriptum eſt apud Pto lemzum, vnam caufarum, quæ efficiunt, vt loca Aneretica ſemperin vaiuerfum vim interficiendi non habeant, effe corporé Aphetz & Ancrete contrariam latitudinem, εμποδίζεται γαρ ἡ αἰαιρετικά μοῖρα, inquit, ἐαμ βμάτωρ όντων ἀμφοτόρωμ τ ἀφιοίτο καὶ τῷ ὑπαντέντο, μια ανού πλάτο ἱ ἑκατέρων, id eſt, pars Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 1== ==Start of OCR for page 2== TERTIA. 329 eſt, pars interimens impeditur, ſi corpus vtrumque dum eft, & quod occurſat & quod moderatur, non ſit vtriuſque eadem ſen ad eafdem partes latitudo. Vnde conſtat, fortiorem fieri occurfum, ſi Apheta & Angreta latitudine meridionali vel ſeptentrio nali concordent, languidiorem verò, ſi eadem diſſentiant. Hinc patet, Ptolemæum hic non reijcere omninò ſtellarum congref- ſus in vtraque parte Ecliptice factos, ſed maiorem potioremá; vim & perfectionem tribuere coniunctionibus ad eafdem partes Eclipticæ factis. Imò verò ſi latitudo ex eadem parte Ecliptice, fed tamen inæqualis fuerit, minorſequetur effectus,propterea quòd corpus ad corpus nunquàm queat peruadere. Sed xalis obferuatio latitudinum nõ interualla, vt dixi, radiationú, ſed robur ſtellarum mutat. Nam ficut inerrantes, ſicetiam planetæ quantò Eclipticę propinquiores funt, eò magis euidentiores effectus pariunt: quantò remotiores, tantò obſcurius agunt, ſemper in vniuerſum ſiue prope Eclipticam fuerint, fiue ab eadem longiſsimè diſtiterint, vnum finem actionis, vnumq; genus effectuum intendentes, fiquidem omnium cæleſtium corporum naturalis actio, ad orbitam Solis, qui rectorOlympi, vitz luciſque fons exiſtit, meritò refertur. Sed cur ſtellarum latitudines, interualla configurationum in Ecliptica non mutát? quia, vt inquit Ptolemæus, vniuerfi radij ad centrum terræ feruntur, atque ita vndecunque emiſsi, ſemper cocurrunt.Sit ma ioris euidentiæ gratia Iuppiter in par.15.Cancricum latitudine meridionali par.2.& in par. 15. Scorpijcum latitudine meridionali par.7. dico radios Martis & Tours corporeos in intimo terræ punto concurrentes, vndique æquali diſtantia,hoceft, ſpacio vnius anguli re&i & parte eiuſdem tertia, ſeu quod idem eſt, gradibus 120. diſtare. Id vt penitiùs intelligas, cogita vtriufque planetę orbicularem radium, ac vnum quidem per lineam à centro terræ ad Martis corpus protenſam, atque per vtrunq; ſigniferi polum integrè reuolutam: alterum verò per lineam alteram ſimiliter à centro terrę ad corpus Iouis procurrentem, & pereofdem ſigniferi polos circumductam, delineatum. His ita ſe habentibus, manifeſtum eſt, duo magnorum circulorum hemicyclia ſecare ſeſe mutuò in axe Eclipticę centrum terra tranfeunte, & quauis parte vltra citraque Eclipticam obferTt uata • Digitized by Google : ==End of OCR for page 2== ==Start of OCR for page 3== 1350 DIFFERENTIA nata æqualitate anguli à ſe inuicem diſtare. Similiter planè de omnibus alijs radiationibus cogitandum eſt, nempè quèd ſine hemicyclia in polos ſigniferi deſinentia.Haec cum ita ſe habe. ant, perfpicuum eft, nec Blanchini, aſpectus in peripheria cuiufdam circuli ad Eclipticam inclinati imaginantis, nec Monteregij, qui radios à corporibus ſtellarum tanquam centris effufos orbes effe cogitat, opinionem concordare cum ſententia Ptolemzi, qui vt oftenfum eft, radios omnes transfert in bemicyclia in polis ſigniferi interfe&a, quorum interualla in Eeliptica ſupputata planè fimilia funt,quibuſuis interuallis ver- ſus vtrumuis ſigniferi polum procedentibus. Eſtque hæc ſummalaus huius ſententiæ Ptolemei, quòd per eam planetz non tantum ſtellis ſignifero propè adiacentibus, ſed & omnibus a lijs quotquot vniuerſum cælum intexunt, vno circuitu conjunguntur, id quod aliarum imaginationum nulla præſtare poteſt. Exempli gratia, ſtella in ſiniſtra manu Perſci & capite Me duſe lucens, γοργόνιομ & κεφαλή τ' γοργόνης appellata, habet la titudinem borealem par.23. quam Mars nunquam conſequitur, & tamen iuxta hanc rationem rectè dicentur hæ ſtelle conjungi, tum cùm Mars iter facit per par.20f Fauri: tanta enim ferè eft noftro tempore ab equino&io apparente Gorgonis remotio. Talis autem coniunctio longè potentior futura eft, ſi Mars in ſeptentrione cum maxima quam poteft conſequi latitudine verſetur. Et Ptolemæum tales coniunctiones probare, plurimis eius dictis probari poteſt. Exempli gratia, in loco paulò antè citato,vbi de numero vxorum conijcere docet, fic ſcribit:ἐὰν ἡ) οι' διατύμῳτύχη ζωδίω, ἢ καὶ πολυμόρφω, π πλείοσιμ εἰ τῷ αὐτῷ ζωδίῳ τώ συνάφλαμ ἔχασα, πολυγάμος mai,hoceft: Si Luna ſit in ſigno bicorporiaut multiformi, aut pluribus ſtellis in tali ſigno exiſtens applicet, viros ſæpiùs coniugium iteraturos notat. Hic ſigna δίωμα & πολύμορφα difcerni debent. Nam ſigna numerum conduplicantia alia infigniforum cadunt, vt funt Gemini, Piſces & Sagittarius, & vocantur δίωμα: alia verò extra zodiacum commorantur, vt funt Centaurus, Auriga, Bootes, Hercules, hydra, & vocantur propriè πολύμορφα,id eft, multiformia ſeu multiplicia, quorum nullú propter magnam ab Ecliptica diſtantiam, Luna partes quinque latitu Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 3==
translation: However, universally, preceding planets are said to apply to succeeding ones. Succeeding planets, however, are said to flow from preceding ones, provided they are not separated by a long interval. This applies to both their bodily conjunctions and configurations, except that in the applications and flows of bodies, it is useful to observe their latitudes. For only those conjunctions are admitted which are found in the same parts of the Ecliptic. In configurations, however, such care is superfluous, since all rays are carried to the same point, that is, to the center of the earth, and thus, emitted from anywhere, they always concur. From these words of Ptolemy, it seems to follow that in lunar conjunctions, latitude must be observed, but not in any other aspects. And although Cardan explains this text in this way, and the matter itself holds true (for a bodily conjunction of stars cannot be effective unless they have equal latitudes, and not only in one circle of latitude, but also situated in the same parts of the Ecliptic), nevertheless, we believe that in other aspects too, the observation of stellar latitude, if it refers not to the variety of intervals but to the strength of the aspects, has great importance. For if, for example, an opposition of Mars and Venus occurs, who can doubt that such an opposition will be far more effective and powerful if Mars and Venus have contrary latitudes, than if they both traversed the north or south? Otherwise, what was said above from Ptolemy's opinion about the figure of the equicrural triangle could have no place. And to pass over in silence the trine radiation, there is no doubt that all other bodily aspects gain strength if they are carried from one part of the Ecliptic, and conversely become weaker if they have contrary latitudes. For this reason, it is written in Ptolemy that one of the causes why anaretic places do not always universally have the power to kill is the contrary latitude of the Apheta and Anareta's body. "For the anaretic part is impeded," he says, "if both bodies, that which meets and that which governs, do not have the same latitude or latitude in the same parts." Hence it is clear that the encounter becomes stronger if the Apheta and Anareta agree in southern or northern latitude, but weaker if they disagree. From this, it is evident that Ptolemy here does not entirely reject the conjunctions of stars made in both parts of the Ecliptic, but attributes greater and more powerful force and perfection to conjunctions made in the same parts of the Ecliptic. Indeed, if the latitude is from the same part of the Ecliptic, but unequal, a lesser effect will follow, because body can never penetrate body. But the exact observation of latitudes changes not the intervals, as I said, of radiations, but the strength of the stars. For just as the fixed stars, so also the planets, the closer they are to the Ecliptic, the more evident effects they produce; the more remote, the more obscurely they act, always universally, whether they are near the Ecliptic or very far from it, intending one end of action and one kind of effects, since the natural action of all celestial bodies is rightly referred to the orbit of the Sun, who is the ruler of Olympus, the source of life and light. But why do the latitudes of the stars not change the intervals of configurations in the Ecliptic? Because, as Ptolemy says, all rays are carried to the center of the earth, and thus, emitted from anywhere, they always concur. For greater clarity, let Jupiter be in 15 degrees of Cancer with 2 degrees of southern latitude, and in 15 degrees of Scorpio with 7 degrees of southern latitude. I say that the bodily rays of Mars and Jupiter, concurring at the innermost point of the earth, are equidistant from all sides, that is, by the space of one right angle and a third part of it, or what is the same, by 120 degrees. To understand this more deeply, imagine the orbital radius of each planet, one drawn through a line extended from the center of the earth to the body of Mars, and completely revolved through both poles of the zodiac; the other drawn through another line similarly extending from the center of the earth to the body of Jupiter, and circumducted through the same poles of the zodiac. These things being so, it is manifest that the two hemicycles of the great circles intersect each other on the axis of the Ecliptic passing through the center of the earth, and that in any part beyond or within the Ecliptic, observed equality of angle, they are distant from each other. Similarly, one must think about all other radiations, namely, that they are hemicycles ending at the poles of the zodiac. Since these things are so, it is clear that neither the opinion of Blanchinus, who imagines aspects on the periphery of a circle inclined to the Ecliptic, nor that of Monteregius, who thinks that rays diffused from the bodies of stars as centers are orbs, agrees with Ptolemy's view. As has been shown, Ptolemy transfers all rays into hemicycles intersected at the poles of the zodiac, whose intervals calculated in the Ecliptic are plainly similar to any intervals proceeding towards either pole of the zodiac. And this is the greatest praise of Ptolemy's opinion, that through it, planets are connected not only to stars closely adjacent to the zodiac, but also to all others that weave the entire sky, in one circuit, which no other imagination can achieve. For example, the star shining in the left hand of Perseus and the head of Medusa, called Gorgonion and the head of the Gorgon, has a northern latitude of 23 degrees, which Mars never reaches. And yet, according to this reasoning, these stars will rightly be said to be conjoined when Mars travels through 20 degrees of Taurus, for such is approximately the distance of the Gorgon from the apparent equinox in our time. Such a conjunction will be far more powerful if Mars is in the north with the greatest latitude it can attain. And that Ptolemy approves of such conjunctions can be proven by many of his sayings. For example, in the place cited a little earlier, where he teaches how to infer the number of wives, he writes thus: "If the Moon is in a bicorporeal or multiform sign, or applies to several stars existing in such a sign, it indicates that men will frequently repeat marriage." Here, the signs δίσωμα (bicorporeal) and πολύμορφα (multiform) must be distinguished. For some signs that double the number fall within the zodiac, such as Gemini, Pisces, and Sagittarius, and are called δίσωμα. Others, however, reside outside the zodiac, such as Centaurus, Auriga, Bootes, Hercules, Hydra, and are properly called πολύμορφα, that is, multiform or multiple, none of which, due to their great distance from the Ecliptic, can have the Moon's five parts of latitude.

text: ==Start of OCR for page 4== TERTIA. 338 latitudinis nanquam excedens, intrare poteſt, & tamen dicitur commorari in illis, cùm in eadem longitudine fuerint.In hæc prolixius nos diuertiſſenemini videri debet alienum,propter afsiduam artificum diſceptationem decapiendis radiorum interuallis, quorum per dicta hemicyclia diſcretio, ſi ita veritati Gicut ſententiæ Ptolemæi conſentit, certè Bianchini & Monte, regij omniumque aliorum quoquo modo longitudinis aſpe, Aus æquantium opiniones, non modò ad artis exercitium mi nimè neceſſariz, ſed & erronez non immeritò cenferi pote runt. Adiuuat nos ipſa dirigendi, vt vocant,ratio à Ptolemzo lib.3.cap.de vitæ ſpacio propoſita,vbi difertè tradit, ratione latitudinis vires ſeu potentiam ſtellarum faltem mutari, & non variari tempora, quæ per progreſsionem definiunt. Etenim quamuis ſtella ſeu paruam ſeu magnam latitudinem habeat, tamen pro directione perficienda ſemper aſſumit tempora horaria partis Ecliptica longitudinis, hoc eſt, eius partis Ecliptica, quam hemicyclium per polos ſigniferi & ſtellam tranſmif ſum definit, perinde ac ſi ſtella Eclipticam luſtraret. Nam ſi ſtel larum latitudines temporibus per dire&ionem definiendis ali quam varietatem poſſe adferre putaſſet, rem tantam tantique momenti, vir ille immeaſe diligentiæ haud dubiè ſilentio non præterijſſfet. Sed iam reliquas ſpecies copulationum exponere tépus eft. Platice copulationes ſunt, quæ ſimiliter vel in vno ſigno vel in ſignis coniun&is exiſtunt, ſed tamen iuxta latioré cohærentiam radiorum accidere dicuntur. Hæc ſubdiuiditur in κόλλησιν καὶ ἀπόρροια, id eft, glutinationem ſeu applicatio- κόλλησις nem & defluxum,quem ſeparationem nominant. Nam vt Cardanus ſcripfit, vires aſpectuum ſunt veluti la bor fle&endi baliſtam. Nam coniunctio velaſpectus ex duabus partibus eſt decuplus illi qui fit cum diftantia partium decem: & qui ex eadem parte, decuplusei, qui fit cum diſtantia duarum partium: & qui per ſex partes minutas, decuplus ei, qui fit per diſtantiam fermè vnius partis, vnde maximi euentus. Item ob ſerua, vt applicatio leuioris ad ponderofiorem fiat, neceffe effe ponderofum plures p. in ſuo ſigno confeciſſe, leuiorem verò pauciores. translation: It can enter, never exceeding 338 degrees of latitude, and yet it is said to reside in them when they are in the same longitude. It should not seem strange to anyone that we have dwelt at length on this, because of the constant dispute among practitioners regarding the measurement of ray intervals, the distinction of which through the said hemicycles, if it truly agrees with Ptolemy's opinion, certainly the opinions of Bianchini and Monte, and all others who in any way equate longitude, can not only be considered unnecessary for the practice of the art, but also erroneous. The very method of directing, as they call it, proposed by Ptolemy in Book 3, Chapter on the Length of Life, helps us, where he explicitly states that the strength or power of the stars changes only by reason of latitude, and that the times defined by progression do not vary. For although a star may have little or great latitude, yet for the completion of a direction, it always assumes the hourly times of the Ecliptic part of longitude, that is, of that part of the Ecliptic which the hemicycle passing through the poles of the zodiac and the star defines, just as if the star were traversing the Ecliptic. For if he had thought that the latitudes of the stars could bring any other variation to the times to be defined by direction, that man of immense diligence would undoubtedly not have passed over so great and important a matter in silence. But now it is time to explain the remaining types of conjunctions. Platic conjunctions are those which similarly exist either in one sign or in conjoined signs, but are said to occur according to a broader coherence of rays. This is subdivided into κόλλησιν καὶ ἀπόρροια, that is, agglutination or application and defluxion, which they call separation. Application occurs when the lighter planet follows the heavier, and approaches a partial conjunction with it. Defluxion or separation occurs whenever the lighter planet, existing in more parts, recedes from the heavier: it is sufficient, however, that the lighter, if it is not in more parts, at least recedes by one scruple or more, since their bodies very rarely come together due to the dissimilarity of latitudes: as if it is in 28 degrees 6 minutes, and in 28 degrees 7 minutes of the same sign, the Moon is said to recede from the Sun. But judges disagree as to how much space the mixed force of the rays coheres. The author believes that application and defluxion last as long as the body or primary ray of the lighter planet is 6 degrees distant from the heavier or its ray. The common opinion is that the conjunction of planets lasts as long as their rays are mixed, that is, cohering by touching each other by half of both orbs. In this way, existing in 5 degrees 2 minutes, applies to Saturn traversing 29 degrees of the same sign, and thus in 6 degrees, applies to Saturn residing in 27 degrees, for the difference is 21 degrees, which is made up of the semidiameters of the composite orbs, whose spaces are indicated above: the semidiameter of the Sun is 15 degrees, of the Moon 12 degrees and 49 minutes, of 8 degrees and 7 degrees. Therefore, if is in 3 degrees, and in 26 degrees, then the Sun will be said to apply and go to Mars, and this continues until, by its faster motion, it reaches a part similar to the 5th part of Leo in which Mars is located, and joins it partially. Haly wished that applications occur from three degrees before, and separations one degree beyond the heavier, yet he did not neglect the application according to the quantity of the orbs. Guido says: When one planet projects its rays upon the rays of another, and is distant from it by the space that the semidiameters of both orbs make up: then the lighter is said to seek the conjunction of the heavier, and signifies the effect of the matter, unless another planet destroys such a conjunction. But if they are distant by half of the orb of the other, the conjunction already completed will likewise signify the effect of the matter, unless it is impeded elsewhere. Finally, if the half of the orb of the faster touches the body of the heavier, then the heavier is said to be in the parts of the lighter. It is undoubtedly true that Cardan weighed these limits more diligently and defined them more accurately and truly according to Ptolemy's opinion: therefore, I think he, as an expert, should be followed here above all. Furthermore, the spaces of the sky in which the bodies of the stars and their rays cohere, as Ptolemy indicates, are in 17 degrees, Moon 12 degrees, 4 degrees, 12 degrees, 10 degrees, 7 degrees, 8 degrees, and 7 degrees. Therefore, when the planet to which the application is made, or from which the defluxion occurs, is not more distant from the one applying or separating than the parts assigned in the table, it is called an application or defluxion, so that those parts are understood of the planet to which the application is made, or from which the defluxion occurs, and not of the one that flows away or applies: diligently observing that the application is stronger than the defluxion, and the closer the application is by aspect or body, and the less the separation has receded, the more effective the conjunction is to be judged. For as Cardan wrote, the forces of aspects are like the labor of bending a ballista. For a conjunction or aspect from two parts is ten times that which occurs with a distance of ten parts: and that which is from the same part, ten times that which occurs with a distance of two parts: and that which is by six minutes, ten times that which occurs by a distance of almost one part, whence the greatest events. Also observe that for the application of the lighter to the heavier to occur, it is necessary for the heavier to have completed more degrees in its sign, and the lighter fewer. The heavier or grave planets are called such by the slowness of their motion, such as the three superiors: the lighter ones by their speed, such as the three inferiors. From this it follows that a retrograde or diminished in course lighter planet becomes heavier by nature. In this way, retrograde Mercury is considered heavier than Venus, and this slow in course is considered heavier than the Sun. From what has been said, the first type of application and separation is the simplest and easiest. The second type of application is when both planets are retrograde, but the lighter is in more parts, and by moving faster, overtakes the heavier, as if is in 9 degrees retrograde, and in 8 degrees retrograde. Here, by retrograding faster, pursues Saturn, who is retrograding more slowly and tardily, and finally joins him. But if, with both retrograding, the lighter is in fewer parts, a violent or averse separation occurs, as if is in 8 degrees retrograde, and in 9 degrees retrograde. Here Venus separates from by contrary motion, because she moves faster than Saturn into the preceding signs. The third type of application and defluxion is called mutual. For mutual application occurs when two planets, one which is anterior by direct motion, and the other which follows by retrograde motion, meet each other, as if is in 1 degree.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Difference in Obstruction
text: DIFFERENTIA cipio ſigni ſequentis,&reliqui duo circa finem figni anteceden tis. In contrarietate verd, de qua paulò antè dixit, omnes tres planetzin codem figno exiftunt, & planeta setrocedens non folum ponderoſo, fed etiam leuiori occurrit, neque in alia re hz duz forma differunt, quare vtramque ſub vno nomine vel contrarietatis, vel perturbationis, vel interpoſitionis rectè con eluferis. Similiter 16 Quando leuis petit & ponderoſi, ſed antequam eum affefi fue. &c. quatur ponderoſus, iungitur alteri ſe ponderofiori, & ita ponderoſiſsimus abſcindit lumen mediocris à leui, vt fi fu hin parte 10. 4 in parte 9.V. in parte 4. V. Hic of petit & 4, ſed antequam ei coniungatur Iuppiter co pulatur cum Saturno, qui abſcindit lumen Iouis à Marte.Sed hocaccidens fuprà prohibitionem vocauit, nullum enim difcrimen inter hæc duo apparet. Quare horum caſuum cùm alter ad perturbationem,alter ad prohibitionem pertineat, abfcifio luminis rectius & propriè definienda adhuc relinquitur. Eft igitur abſciſio luminis, quando tres planetæ fuerint intra metas, & leuiſsimus fit in paucioribus partibus, ſed curſu reliquos vincens prætergrediatur medium, atque attingat illum qui eft in pluribus partibus antequam medius illi coniungatur, tunc ille leuis & curſu velox dicitur abſcindere lumen medij à poAremo, qui eſt in pluribus partibus:veluti fi fit 4. in parte 28. ४. in parte 14.४. in parte 7.४. Similiter autem per radios alterius planetz abſciſio luminis, fed quæ longè ſit de bilior, intelligenda eft:veluti ſi fit.. ! 4 in TERTIA. 4 in parte 1.3.II. in parte 8.11. DE LOCIS FORTUNATIS & malis.. I Sunt quoque his planetis loca in quibus confortan tur, & in quibus debilitantur, & loca in quibus fiunt fortune, & loca in quibus fiunt mali. Sed loca in quibus fiunt fortune, funt hæc, vt fcilicet fint in afpectibus boo norum, id eft, in aliquo afpectu vtili, ſcilicet fextili aut in trino afpectu, quia quibufdam ita nominatur hexa. gona radiatio quoque ac trigona, & vt fint mali cadentes ab eis finta separati ab infortuna &iuncti fortuna: aut fint obsesi à fortunis vel radijs eorum, id eft, vt fint inter duas fortunas vel inter radios fortunarum. 2 Hec eft enim obſeßio, vt habeat planeta fortunam velradios eius ante ſe, & aliam fortunam, vel eius ras dios poſt ſe. Et quidam vocant boc altifert, id eft, vene ratio: aut fint vniti cum Sole in vno gradu, aut fint in afpectu eius fextili, ſive trino, vel confimili aspectu Luo ne, Luna tunc fit fortunata, & vt curſu fint veloces, aucti lumine & numero, aut fint in dignitatibus fuis vel in fuo Haim, id est, in ſua ſimilitudine, vt fit videlis cet planeta mafculinus in ſigno masculino, & fæmini. nus in ſigno fæminino, & diurnus in die fuper terram, in nocte fub terra, nocturnus in nocte fuper tere ramier Digitized by Google 344 DIFFERENTIA' ram, & in die fub terra, & reliqua: vt fint in ſignis in quibus habuerint dignitates feu poteftates fuas, vel in Loca forti gaudijs fuis, vel in gradibus lucidis, recepti. Et ex for tudinum. titudine eorumeft, vt fint afcendentes in feptentrione, aut fint feptentrionales, vel fint afcendentes in circulo augis fue, aut in ſtatione fecunda, hoc est, quando fue rint in ſtatione vbi dirigantur à retrogradatione, aut fint exeutes de fub radijs Solis, aut fint in angulo, vel in Jequenti eius, aut fint tres altiores orientales à Sole: quòd ficum afpexerint fextili, aut trino afpectu, erit illis hoc augmentum fortitudinis, aut fuerint in quartis mafculinis. Et Solcùm fuerit in illis quartis mafcu. linis, aut in fignis masculinis, erit fortis etiam, niſi ſit in Libra, quia ibi cadit. Et ex fortitudine trium inferios cadit. rum eft,vt fint occidentales à Sole, aut in quartis fæmi. ales à Sole,aut in quartis fem ninis,& ex fortitudine Lunæ, vt fit in nocte ſuper ter ram,& in die fub terra, in loco fæminino, vel in ſigno faminino, cùm fuerit in exaltatione Solis. De infortunio planetarum. Ex infortunio verò planetarum & deftructione eos Loca in fortuna rum est, vt fint in coniunctione malorum, aut in oppofis tia. tione eorum, vel in eorum tetragona, aut trigona, aut hexagona radiatione: aut fifuerit inter eos & corpus mali vel radios eius minus termino planeta, aut fuerint in terminis malorum, aut domibus eorum, aut fint mali eleuati ſuper eos à X.vel XI. à locis eorum, & multò deteriùs fi non receperint eos. Aut fuerint in coniunctione So Digitized by Google TERTIA 349 ne Solis, vel oppofitione eius, vel in tetragona radiatio. ne, aut fuerint in capitibus fuorum Genzahar, aut cum candis fuis, aut cum capite Draconis vel cauda, fintque inter eos & inter aliquem iſtoriſcilicet locorú XII. gradus vel infrà : & maximè fi fuerit Luna in aliquo Luminaeorum. Similiter & Sol tunc magis impeditur ab eis, ria maxi. id eft, à capite vel cauda, cùm fuerint inter ipſum & me impe. vnum eorum etiam III grad. ante vel retro. Ant fuerint fuer diuntur planete obſeßi inter duos malos, hoc eft, vt fit planeta in propterea ſigno aliquo & cum eo malus, vel radij eius ante fe, & quòd in matus vel radij eius poſt ſe; aut ſeparetur à malo per cons foleat iunctionem, vel per afpectum, & iungatur tali modo al. deficere. teri malo: aut fuerit malus, vel radij eius in figno quod eft ante eum, id eft, in ſecundo ab eo: & in ſigno quod eft post eum, id eft, in duodecimo ab eo fuerit alter malus, velrady eius. Similiter dicitur de ſignis quòd fint obfef. Ja. Quodfi afpexerit fortuna vel Sol eundem planetam obseffum, vel fignum obſeſſum à trino, vel à ſextili afpea Etu: fuerita inter cum 'coniunctionem minus ſeptem gradibus, foluitur ipſa malicia vel obſeßio. Et ex hoc vt *id eft, in fit-planeta retrogradus, vel fub radijs Solis combuftus, fortunio ant cadens ab afcendente. etiam eſt. Et ex debilitate eorum eft, vt fint tar di curſu, aut in Loca debi ſtatione prima, boc est, quando ſtant vt xetrogradi f aut fint in gradib' tenebrofis: aut fint masculini in ſignis famininis: & gradibus fœmininis, in die sub terra, & in nocte ſuper terram, aut fint feminini in ſignis mafculi. nis:& in gradibus mafcutinis,in nocte ſub terra, & in Xx die ſuper Digitized by Google
translation: The difference is that in the first case, the lightest planet is at the beginning of the following sign, and the other two are near the end of the preceding sign. In the case of contrariety, which was mentioned a little earlier, all three planets are in the same sign, and the retrograde planet encounters not only the heavier but also the lighter one. These two forms do not differ in any other respect, so you may correctly conclude both under one name, either contrariety or perturbation or interposition. Similarly, when the light planet seeks the heavier, but before it reaches it, the heavier planet joins another still heavier one, and thus the heaviest planet cuts off the light of the medium from the light one. For example, if Mars is in the 10th degree of Aries, Jupiter in the 9th degree of Aries, and Saturn in the 4th degree of Aries. Here Mars seeks Jupiter, but before it can join Jupiter, Jupiter conjoins Saturn, who cuts off the light of Jupiter from Mars. But this occurrence was called a prohibition above, for no distinction appears between these two. Therefore, since one of these cases pertains to perturbation and the other to prohibition, the cutting off of light still remains to be defined more accurately and properly. Therefore, the cutting off of light is when three planets are within their bounds, and the lightest is in fewer degrees, but by its course, surpassing the others, it overtakes the middle one and reaches the one in more degrees before the middle one joins it. Then that light and swift-moving planet is said to cut off the light of the middle one from the last, which is in more degrees. For example, if Jupiter is in the 28th degree of Taurus, Mars in the 14th degree of Taurus, and the Moon in the 7th degree of Taurus. Here Mars was going to Jupiter, but the Moon, with swifter motion, passing Mars, reaches Jupiter before Mars does, and cuts off the light of Mars from Jupiter. For although the Moon has not yet reached Jupiter, nor is it interposed between Mars and Jupiter, yet it must be judged as if it had already reached it, because everything that is potentially such obtains such power in actuality, from which source all these precepts have flowed. Similarly, the cutting off of light by the rays of another planet, but which is much weaker, is to be understood. For example, if Jupiter is in the 1st degree of Gemini, Mars in the 8th degree of Gemini, and the Moon in the 6th degree of Virgo, casting a ray into the 6th degree of Gemini. Here Mars was going to seek a conjunction with Jupiter, but the Moon, moving faster, passing Mars, having fallen from a square aspect to Jupiter, cuts off the light of Mars from Jupiter, etc.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Fortunate and Unfortunate Places

In contrarietate verd, de qua paulò antè dixit, omnes tres planetzin codem figno exiftunt, & planeta setrocedens non folum ponderoſo, fed etiam leuiori occurrit, neque in alia re hz duz forma differunt, quare vtramque ſub vno nomine vel contrarietatis, vel perturbationis, vel interpoſitionis rectè con eluferis. DE LOCIS FORTUNATIS & malis.. I Sunt quoque his planetis loca in quibus confortan tur, & in quibus debilitantur, & loca in quibus fiunt fortune, & loca in quibus fiunt mali. Sed loca in quibus fiunt fortune, funt hæc, vt fcilicet fint in afpectibus boo norum, id eft, in aliquo afpectu vtili, ſcilicet fextili aut in trino afpectu, quia quibufdam ita nominatur hexa. gona radiatio quoque ac trigona, & vt fint mali cadentes ab eis finta separati ab infortuna &iuncti fortuna: aut fint obsesi à fortunis vel radijs eorum, id eft, vt fint inter duas fortunas vel inter radios fortunarum. 2 Hec eft enim obſeßio, vt habeat planeta fortunam velradios eius ante ſe, & aliam fortunam, vel eius ras dios poſt ſe. Et quidam vocant boc altifert, id eft, vene ratio: aut fint vniti cum Sole in vno gradu, aut fint in afpectu eius fextili, ſive trino, vel confimili aspectu Luo ne, Luna tunc fit fortunata, & vt curſu fint veloces, aucti lumine & numero, aut fint in dignitatibus fuis vel in fuo Haim, id est, in ſua ſimilitudine, vt fit videlis cet planeta mafculinus in ſigno masculino, & fæmini. nus in ſigno fæminino, & diurnus in die fuper terram, in nocte fub terra, nocturnus in nocte fuper tere ramier Digitized by Google 344 DIFFERENTIA' ram, & in die fub terra, & reliqua: vt fint in ſignis in quibus habuerint dignitates feu poteftates fuas, vel in Loca forti gaudijs fuis, vel in gradibus lucidis, recepti. Et ex for tudinum. titudine eorumeft, vt fint afcendentes in feptentrione, aut fint feptentrionales, vel fint afcendentes in circulo augis fue, aut in ſtatione fecunda, hoc est, quando fue rint in ſtatione vbi dirigantur à retrogradatione, aut fint exeutes de fub radijs Solis, aut fint in angulo, vel in Jequenti eius, aut fint tres altiores orientales à Sole: quòd ficum afpexerint fextili, aut trino afpectu, erit illis hoc augmentum fortitudinis, aut fuerint in quartis mafculinis. Et Solcùm fuerit in illis quartis mafcu. linis, aut in fignis masculinis, erit fortis etiam, niſi ſit in Libra, quia ibi cadit. De infortunio planetarum. Ex infortunio verò planetarum & deftructione eos Loca in fortuna rum est, vt fint in coniunctione malorum, aut in oppofis tia. tione eorum, vel in eorum tetragona, aut trigona, aut hexagona radiatione: aut fifuerit inter eos & corpus mali vel radios eius minus termino planeta, aut fuerint in terminis malorum, aut domibus eorum, aut fint mali eleuati ſuper eos à X.vel XI. à locis eorum, & multò deteriùs fi non receperint eos. translation: There are also places for these planets in which they are strengthened, and in which they are weakened, and places in which fortunes occur, and places in which misfortunes occur. But the places in which fortunes occur are these: that they are in the aspects of benefics, that is, in some useful aspect, namely a sextile or a trine aspect, because some call the hexagonal and trigonal radiation thus. And that malefics are cadent from them, separated from misfortune and joined to fortune; or that they are besieged by benefics or their rays, that is, that they are between two benefics or between the rays of benefics. This is indeed obsession, that a planet has a benefic or its rays before it, and another benefic or its rays behind it. And some call this 'altifert', that is, veneration. Or that they are united with the Sun in one degree, or that they are in its sextile or trine aspect, or a similar aspect of the Moon. The Moon is then fortunate, and that they are swift in course, augmented in light and number, or that they are in their dignities or in their Hayz, that is, in their similitude, so that a masculine planet is in a masculine sign, and a feminine planet in a feminine sign, and a diurnal planet in the day above the earth, and in the night below the earth, a nocturnal planet in the night above the earth, and in the day below the earth, and so on. That they are in signs in which they have their dignities or powers, or in their joys, or in bright degrees, received. And from their fortitude it is that they are ascending in the north, or are northern, or are ascending in the circle of their apogee, or in the second station, that is, when they are in the station where they are directed from retrogradation, or are emerging from under the Sun's rays, or are in an angle, or in its succedent. Or that three are higher and oriental from the Sun: for if they aspect with a sextile or trine aspect, this will be an increase of strength for them, or if they are in masculine quarters. And the Sun, when it is in those masculine quarters, or in masculine signs, will also be strong, unless it is in Libra, because there it falls. And from the strength of the three inferior planets it is that they are occidental from the Sun, or in feminine quarters. And from the strength of the Moon, that it is in the night above the earth, and in the day below the earth, in a feminine place, or in a feminine sign, when it is in the exaltation of the Sun. Regarding the misfortune of planets. From the misfortune and destruction of planets it is that they are in conjunction with malefics, or in opposition to them, or in their square, or trine, or sextile radiation. Or if there is between them and the body of a malefic or its rays less than the term of the planet, or if they are in the terms of malefics, or their houses, or if malefics are elevated above them from the 10th or 11th house from their places, and much worse if they do not receive them. Or if they are in conjunction with the Sun, or its opposition, or in a square radiation, or if they are in the heads of their Genzahar, or with their tails, or with the head of the Dragon or the tail, and there are between them and any of these places 12 degrees or less: and especially if the Moon is in any of them. Similarly, the Sun is then more impeded by them, that is, by the head or tail, when there are between it and one of them even 3 degrees before or after. Or if planets are besieged between two malefics, that is, that a planet is in some sign and with it a malefic, or its rays before it, and a malefic or its rays behind it; or it is separated from a malefic by conjunction, or by aspect, and joins another malefic in such a way: or if a malefic, or its rays, are in the sign that is before it, that is, in the second from it: and in the sign that is after it, that is, in the twelfth from it, there is another malefic, or its rays. Similarly, it is said of signs that they are besieged. But if a benefic or the Sun aspects the same besieged planet, or besieged sign, with a trine or sextile aspect: and there is between them a conjunction of less than seven degrees, that malice or obsession is resolved. And from this, that is, in misfortune, it is also that a planet is retrograde, or combust under the Sun's rays, or cadent from the ascendant. And from their debility it is that they are slow in course, or in the first station, that is, when they stand as if retrograde, or if they are in dark degrees: or if they are masculine in feminine signs: and in feminine degrees, in the day below the earth, and in the night above the earth, or if they are feminine in masculine signs: and in masculine degrees, in the night below the earth, and in the day above the earth.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Strength and Weakness
text: 348 DIFFEREΝΤΙΑ funt, Ecótrarid combustrogradi, tardiq;, peregrini,minaci bus radijs maleficarú oppagnati, & deniq; ab angulis cadentes, infortunati debileſq; cenfendi.Quòd fi ex vtraque parte eque lia fuffragia adepti fuerint, hoc eſt, ſi ſint partim fortitudine & prærogatiuis nonnullis prædici: partim verò infortunijs deip çiantur, indifferentes ſeu mediocres effe putantur. Similis breuitas huius loci eft apud Prolemeum, cuius verba in prin.lib.z. ἢ ἀποτελεσμάτωμ hæc funt: σκοπᾶμδα, πότερομοναρχῶς τυγ χάνεσι Λακείμενοι, κατοί τε αυτό το κοσμικόμ, καὶ τὸ κατά τώ γέα νεσιμ, ἐτὸ εναντίομ. δραστικόταδι μεν γαρ εἰσιν, ὅταμκοσμικός μπὺ οἱ οικείοις, ἢ καὶ οἱ ἰδίοις ὦσι τόπους, καί πάλιμ όταν ανατολι καὶ τυγχάνωσι πας προθετικοί δῖς αριθμοῖς· κατάλλύεσις δε, ό ταν ἐπὶ τα κοίτρωμη την επαναφορών προδέσωσι, και μάλισα τν πρώτωρ, λέγω δὲ ἢ κατὰ τὰς αναφοράς καὶ τὰς μεσορατά · σις. ἀδρανές αβι δὲ κοσμικός μεν, ὅταν οἱ ἀλλοτρίοις τόποις, Η αί κείοις, καὶ δυτικοί, ἢ αὐαποδυτικοί δῖς δρόμοις ὦσι: κατὰ γεύεσιρ δὲ, ὅταμ ἀποκλίνωσι την κάτρων. Robur ergo planetarum & debilitas ſecundum Ptolemæum dijudicatur ex quatuor circun tantijs. Primò comparatione ad mundum, cùm videlicet fue rint in locis οικίοις, οι' ιδίοις, ἀλλοτρίοις, οι αἰοικείοις, học eſt, domefticis aut proprijs, in alienis fiue non domeſticis.Se cundò comparatione ad Solem. Nam αναβλικοί validi funt, Sulexei infirmi. Tertiò comparatione ad ſuos orbes. Nam velo τις προθετικοί δῖς ἀριθμοῖς robur fumunt.Econtrario tardi,re trogradi καὶ βῖς ἀριθμοῖς ἀφαιρετικοί, idem deperdunt.Quartò comparatione ad geneſin ſeu figuram cæli. Nam in centris, & præcipuè in afcendente & MC. item in fuccedentibus funt validi, in cadentibus verò debiles. Id quoque hic adiiciendum putaui, Iouem & Venerem potentes & fortunatos maximam fortiri efficaciam:imbecilles verd & infortunatos mediocribus zquiparari, parumque aut omninò nihil preſtare. Rurfum & forlices atque in ſplendoribus fuis collocatos, minùs nocere, quàm cùm extra prærogatiuas infortunati debileſquere perti fuerint.Mediocres autem & & fortes atque fortu natos beneficis æquiparari, infortunatos debileſque non miAus ipfis maleficis detrimentum portendere. Cùm o afcendentis domino corpore iungitur in Leone, niff Mars ipſe aliquam in aſcendente dignitatem habuerit, vel noo etiam in octauo loco, qui natus eft cremabitur. Ex quo patet, quòd planetz non noceant tantùm in locis, vbi dominantur. Idem notamus in malis principibus, qui non tam malè tractant fuos proprios ſubditos quàm alienos. Ad extremum loca duodecim cufpidum, planetarum ſortiumq; fortunantur per pres fentiam & omnes afpectus bonorum, & bonos afpectus mediocrium. Damnantur verò & infortunantur per 8, 8 & malorum,& & mediocrium,& hoc fi fiant aſpectus intra me tas, vel ve plerique tradiderunt, ex medietate, & intra medieta rem ſuorum orbium. Veluti ſit cuſpis ſeptimæ par. 12. X,& in par.19. X. Hic & fua præſentia ex medietate orbis fui, qui eſt par.7.fortunatcuſpidem domus ſeptimæ. Idem præſtatgradus intermedij. Et dicitur in planetis quòd fint quidameorum ſe in wicem diligentes & odientes : nam de diligentibus quie dam antiquorum dixerunt,quòd Iupiter diligit omnes planetas & fit amicus corum, & ipfi illius, prater Mar tem. Tentinantem coniunctio eft Sa turni Martis in initio Cancri, que fit in omnibus XXX. annis. Quarta quoq; eft coniunctio Louis et Sa turni in vnoquoque ſigno, quæfit in omnibus XX. ano nis. Quinta eft defcenfioluminaris maioris in punctum aquinoctiý vernalis temporis, fcilicet ingreßus Solis in capite Arietis, que fit in omni anno. Sexta eft coniun Etio luminarium & oppoſitio eorum, que fit in dimidio cuiufq; menſis Lunaris. 2 Et quando dicitur afcendens coniunctionis, dicitur vel fignificatur afcendens mundi, ideft, fignum quod afcendit hora introitus Solis in prædictum punctum a quinocty vernalis, in initio ſcilicet illius anni in quo de, bet fieri coniunctio predicta. translation: Planets that are combust, retrograde, slow, peregrine, afflicted by the malefic rays of malefics, and finally, cadent from the angles, are considered unfortunate and weak. If they have received equal votes from both sides, that is, if they are partly endowed with strength and certain prerogatives, but partly depressed by misfortunes, they are considered indifferent or mediocre. A similar brevity of this passage is found in Ptolemy, whose words in the beginning of Book 2 of the Apotelesmatics are these: "We consider whether they are ruling monarchically, both cosmically and natally, or the opposite. For they are most active when cosmically they are in their own or proper places, and again when they are oriental and in direct motion; but natally, when they precede the center in their return, and especially the first, I mean those in the ascensions and midheaven. But they are cosmically inactive when they are in alien places, or their own, and occidental, or in retrograde motion; and natally, when they decline from the centers." Therefore, the strength and weakness of planets according to Ptolemy are judged from four circumstances. First, in comparison to the world, when they are in their own, proper, alien, or non-domestic places. Second, in comparison to the Sun. For oriental planets are strong, occidental ones weak. Third, in comparison to their orbits. For swift planets in direct motion gain strength. Conversely, slow, retrograde planets lose it. Fourth, in comparison to the genesis or figure of the heavens. For in the centers, and especially in the ascendant and Midheaven, and also in succedent houses, they are strong, but in cadent houses, they are weak. I also thought it necessary to add here that Jupiter and Venus, when powerful and fortunate, have the greatest efficacy; but when weak and unfortunate, they are equated with mediocre planets and achieve little or nothing. Again, even fortunate planets placed in their splendors do less harm than when they are outside their prerogatives, unfortunate and weak. But mediocre and strong and fortunate planets are equated with benefics, while unfortunate and weak ones portend no less detriment than the malefics themselves. This opinion is confirmed by the torment of the 7th and 8th houses, he says: "When the lord of the ascendant is conjoined with the body in Leo, unless Mars himself has some dignity in the ascendant, or not even in the eighth house, the native will be burned." From this it is clear that planets do not only harm in places where they rule. We note the same in evil princes, who do not treat their own subjects as badly as they do strangers. Finally, the twelve cusps, planets, and lots are made fortunate by the presence and all good aspects of benefics, and by the good aspects of mediocre planets. But they are condemned and made unfortunate by the conjunctions and bad aspects of malefics, and of mediocre planets, and this if the aspects occur within the limits, or as most have taught, from the midpoint, and within the midpoint of their orbs. For example, if the cusp of the seventh house is at 12 degrees of Aries, and Jupiter is at 19 degrees of Aries. Here Jupiter, by its presence from the midpoint of its orb, which is 7 degrees, makes the cusp of the seventh house fortunate. The same is achieved by intermediate degrees. Nor is there any other judgment concerning aspects, as if Jupiter projects a ray to 21 degrees of Aries, there from the midpoint of its orb it would make the same cusp of the seventh house fortunate. Concerning the fortune and misfortune of the Sun and Moon, it has been said above. Concerning the friendship and hatred of the planets. And it is said among the planets that some of them love and hate each other: for concerning those who love, some of the ancients said that Jupiter loves all planets and is their friend, and they are his, except Mars. The friends of Venus are all planets, and all love her except Saturn. But the friends of Saturn are Jupiter, the Sun, and the Moon; his enemies are Mars and Venus, and she herself hates him more. And the friend of Mars is Venus, the other planets hate him, and Jupiter and the Sun more. But the friends of the Sun are Jupiter and Venus, and his enemies are Mars, Mercury, and the Moon. The friends of Mercury are only Jupiter, Venus, and Saturn, and his enemies are the Sun, Moon, and Mars. But the friends of the Moon are Jupiter, Venus, and Saturn, and her enemies are Mars and Mercury. The friends of the Head of the Dragon are Jupiter and Venus, and its enemies are Saturn and Mars. But the friends of the Tail are Saturn and Mars, and its enemies are the Sun, Moon, Jupiter, and Venus. And there are other kinds of enmity: when two planets have their houses opposite, like Mars and Venus: when they are conjoined, it is called the opening of the gates. A third kind of enmity is when the exaltations of two planets are opposite. But the stronger friendship of planets is when a planet agrees with a planet in nature, quality, substance, and power, just as Mars agrees with the Sun, because both agree in heat, dryness, sharpness, and swiftness, and Mars is the lord of its exaltation, in which its strength appears: and just as the Moon and Venus agree in coldness and humidity, and Venus is the lady of its exaltation. But when two planets agree in nature and substance, like Jupiter and Venus, they are friends. Jupiter's friends are all except Mars. Venus's friends are all except Saturn. Saturn's friends are Jupiter, Sun, Moon; enemies are Mars and Venus, and Venus hates him more. Mars's friend is Venus; enemies are all others, especially Jupiter and Sun. Sun's friends are Jupiter and Venus; enemies are Mars, Mercury, Moon. Mercury's friends are Jupiter, Venus, Saturn; enemies are Sun, Moon, Mars. Moon's friends are Jupiter, Venus, Saturn; enemies are Mars, Mercury. Head of the Dragon's friends are Jupiter and Venus; enemies are Saturn, Mars. Tail of the Dragon's friends are Saturn, Mars; enemies are Sun, Moon, Jupiter, Venus. Enemies by reason of opposite houses. Enemies by reason of opposite exaltations. Friends by reason of agreement in nature, quality, substance, and power. End of the third difference.
