translation: DIFFERENCE & places of effects collected from the defects of the luminaries of the Sun. However, in another circumstance, that is, in the judgment of places, besides the Eclipse, it seems to take the stations and heliacal risings of the three superior planets, and indeed at least those which fall into the time of the Eclipse, and of those planets which preside over the defect. This seems to be able to be elicited from his own words: “The first chapter of this prediction contains the foreknowledge of places and cities, to which, besides or particularly, both the defects of the luminaries and the concurrent monthly constitutions of the wandering stars, such as exist in stationary Saturn, Jupiter, and Mars, bring significations of events.” From this it seems to follow that places subject to future events are to be found from the dodecatemoria containing the defects, and also from the stations of the three superior planets when they preside over the defective places, that is, when they are lords of the place of defect or placed in the place of the Eclipse, or aspect it, or finally ascend the horizon at the hour of the Eclipse. But neither is this a sufficiently firm consequence deduced from Ptolemy's words: For the causes of great events are to be taken both from defective places and from the stations and risings of the wandering stars, it follows that both of these indicate to us places subject to such effects. And what is more, Ptolemy, in the consideration of the regions to which the effects pertain, expressly makes no mention of the wandering stars, and accommodates the significations of defects at least to those places which are coupled with the defective dodecatemorion. Hence it is clear that, when we think that places, which by sympathy or affinity of the dodecatemoria containing the stars dominating the defect, are in some part subject to the significations of the Eclipse, he either completely disregarded them, or at least insinuated them to us in passing. As for the words, “τὰς ἔμμένους στάσεις” we believe to mean the same as the Latin “menstruas constitutiones”. There are those who read “ἐπιμένεις στάσεις”. Both readings proceed well. For monthly stations are long-lasting, and familiar to the three superior planets, and alien to the inferior ones, who, since they scarcely consist due to the swiftness of their motion, are very far from menstruation. However, that the times of great events are not to be calculated from the conjunctions of Saturn and Jupiter, but rather from the defects, can be established from this, that the effects of the former, extended to very long times, must be changed and varied almost infinitely by the transits of so many of them and other stars through different regions of the sky. But the decrees of these, since they can never exceed two and a half years, remain simpler, and usually expire before the heavier planets significantly change their places in the sky. Moreover, Ptolemy in this matter considers not the inherent power of the three superior planets, but that which is derived from the defects and brought to effect. For he says that the “κατὰ καιρὸν ἐμμήνους στάσεις” of the wandering stars, that is, the concurrent monthly constitutions, are to be observed. And if you wish to pursue the conjunctions of the heavier planets in the same way that Ptolemy treats the defects of the luminaries, the very difficulty of the matter, since the human intellect cannot comprehend the exact time of the conjunction of the superior planets, will easily repel you from your purpose. For even if they move with the swiftest motion they can have, it has already been shown by experts that Jupiter departing from Saturn never exceeds a difference of six first scruples in diurnal motion. Since this distance is smaller than can be detected by calculation or any instrument, such a conjunction will be so far from a certain hour and figure of the sky that not even its day can be considered certain. When the doctors of great conjunctions noticed this, they were therefore forced to devise another remedy for this ailment, which the author mentions in this place. For when the ascendants of great conjunctions are named, they mean those signs which the Sun, having reached the vernal intersection, or as they say, entering the beginning of Aries, and that in that year in which that conjunction is to occur, is observed ascending. For example: In the year of Christ our Lord 1563, a great conjunction of Jupiter and Saturn will be celebrated, and according to the calculation of the Alphonsine tables, on the 26th day of September, around 6 hours after noon, in the 4th part, 8th scruple, 1st degree of Leo; but according to the Prutenic calculation, on the 25th day of August, around the tenth hour after noon, in the 29th part of Cancer. Furthermore, the horoscoping part in the annual revolution, or as they call it, the part at the moment of time, when the Sun reaches the first point of Aries in that year, the ascendant, will be, according to their calculation, the 16th part of Sagittarius. For this reason, Sagittarius will be called the ascendant of that great conjunction by them, although there is a similar uncertainty about the ascending sign of the annual revolution, and no less disagreement in calculations. But it is also established by many arguments that this has no semblance of truth and is completely empty and without reason. For the beginning of the natural year, which is certainly accommodated to the Sun's entry into the beginning of Aries, is simply uncertain. For although it is consistent with truth that the Sun's return to the cardinal points of the zodiac constitutes the beginning of the natural year, nevertheless it brings with it no small hesitation as to which of these should be used as the primary principle, especially since each of these parts has something excellent, why it seems to claim the beginning of the year or the new moon by right for itself. Hence it happened that of those who commented on these matters, one established one beginning of the year, another another, as each was impelled by reasons or his own nature to establish this or that. Ptolemy also, since he could not resolve this hesitation, therefore transferred the discussion of the causes making the variety of individual years, not to one, but to all four, so that the vernal equinox varies spring, the summer solstice summer, the autumnal equinox autumn, the winter solstice winter. Therefore, since all doubt about the beginning of the natural year, how can we be certain about the sign ascending at its beginning? Finally, even if the Sun's return to the vernal intersection made the new moon of that year, nevertheless the signs ascending at the Sun's return to that beginning would still escape us, because that time can hardly be calculated so exactly as to serve the perfection or structure of the celestial theme without gross error. And this is the reason why Ptolemy prescribes to consider for the change of the four seasons of the year, not the Sun's return to the cardinal points, but rather the conjunctions or oppositions of the luminaries immediately preceding the Sun's entry into the cardinal points: whose times, since they can be calculated most exquisitely, provide us with the most perfect fabric of the celestial theme.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: The Vanity of Vulgar Astrologers
text: QVARTA. ? 839 Hæccuni ita fe habeant, manifeſta eſt vanitas hominum noſtro zuo vulgi diaria condentium, qui omiſſa doctrina vera, ex folo Solis ad interfectionem vernam tranfitu diluuia regionum, excidia vrbium, bella, famem, terræmotus, ſterilitatem,& tales publicas calamitates in publicum effutientes fuis mendaeijs & meris nugis mundum replent, atque ipſam omnium præ ſtantissimam profeſsionem reddunt infamem, ing; maximam vituperationem etiam eorum, quibus veritas curæ eft,traducunt. Sed adhuc ad maiorem feu focordiam ſeu impudentiam accedunt, cùm ne abſq; cauſa fabulofam de futuris hiſtoriam contexuiffe videantur, per magnarum coniunctionum annuas progreſsiones, paulò infra etiam ab Alcabicio deſcriptas, aut per commutationem nefcio cuius magni orbis, nihilò certius magno anno Platonis in verrum natura exiſtentis, vaticinia ſua communire nituntur. Quapropter aut nulla aut magis erudita de caufis maiores illos in mundo effectus producentibus, coniectatio in rebuſpub non immeritò optari poterat. Ea verò eſt quz de caufis naturalibus primis & omnium potentiſsimis per omnia media ad maximè ſeu propria feu particularia perua dit. Non eft autem, vt paulò antè de ſententia Ptolemæi diceba mus, prianneque potior cauſa huiufmodi cafuum, quam quæ à luminarium defectiuis copulationibus ducitur. Hanc ſequitur ratio, qua per συζυγίας luminum Solis in puncta cardinalia tranfitum antecedentes, vniufcuiufque quadrantis anni generalis conftitutio eruitur. Hincafpicienda eft ἔφοδο μπα νιαῖα, id eſt, ratio menstrua, qua fingulorum anni menfum futura conftitutiones produntur. Inde ſequitur ſubtiliorum quarundam fignificationum ad quadrantes vniufcuiufq; menfis pertinentium o. Reliquis inferior eft illa ratio, qua quocunque die qualitatem aëris ex illuſtrium ſtellarum or tu & occafu colligere docemur. Poftremum locum habet ea ratio, quz per diei fingulas horas ex luminarium per cardidines tranfitu qualitatem in aére conftitutionum venatur. Hic eſt Ptolomai de aéris conſtitutionibus cõiectandi modus, à maximis ad minima tradu&us, qui &plus laboris, & longè ma iorem rerú peritia eruditionemq; requirit, quam quę omnibus noßris Calendariorü conditorib in vná maſſam cófatis ineft. De -360 DIFFERENTIA DE ANNIMODAR, ID EST, GRADVS A cendentis natiuitatum inueftigatione. Et ex hoc Annimodar, quod est inueftigatio gradus afcedentis alicuius natiuitatis. 2 Et putant multi Aftro logorü, quòd inueniatur per eundem idem gradus afcen dentis natiuitatis alicuius:fed hoc falfum est, boc iam dent patefeci in libro meo quem feci de annimodar:fed inueni tur per eunde dignior omnibus gradibus circuli postgra dum afcendentis in eadem hora fecundum curfum natu ralem:& concordat multoties,id eft, accidit aliquando quòd inueniatur per eum gradus afcendentis.Cuiusrei cognitio eft, vt confideretur gradus cõiunctionis velgra dus prauentionis quæ fuerit ante ipsam natiuitate: fi fuerit cõiunctio illi natiuitati proprior, vocatur ipsa na tiuitas coniunctionalis: &fifuerit preventio illi pro pior, dicitur preuētionalis.i. in qualicunque corŭ fuerit natiuitas, in coniunctione ſcilicet, vel prauentione, eode modo & nomine natiuitas prædicta nucupatur: & quia gradus cõiunctionis est ide gradus in quo inguntur lumi naria, nulla est inquifitio, nec indiget ex poſitiõe in pra uentione aut quia vnumquodque luminariu est in gradu fuo.i. in diuerfis gradibus. Neceffe eft nobis ſcire, quem horum graduŭ velit intelligi gradum tintelligi gradum preventionis. Et iam dixit Ptolemæus:quia gradus illius luminaris quod fuerit fuper terram, est gradus præuctionis. Et quidam ſapientum dixerunt, quòd fi euenerit in prevêtiõe, quòd vnum luminarium fit in gradu orientis, alteram in gradu occidentis, tunc gradus orientis erit gradus pras quod uentionis ا 4 í ہے QUARTA wentionis. Et dixit Vellias, quia preventionis est gra dus in quo fit impletio, volens intelligi gradum Lune: fed quia hoc eft opus Ptolemai, oportet in hoc nos redive ad eius fententiam. Cum ergogradus cõiunctionis vel gradus preventionis certißimè patuerit, conſtitues gras dumaſcendentem per aftimationem bora natiuitatis quatuor angulos & reliquas domos: deinde afpicies gra cõiunctionis, vel gradum preuentionis quæ fuerit ante natinitatem:& quis planetarum in eo fuerit dignior vel fortior in eadem bora cõiunctiouis vel preventionis per multitudinem dignitatum feu poteftatum, id eft,confide rabis quis planetarum habeat maius dominium in eode loco. Hunc ergo planetam æquabis ad horam opinatam natinitatis. Deinde afpicies vtrum fit gradus illius plan neta in ſigno in quo eſt propior gradui quo eft propior gradui X.domus aut gra dai afcendentis, & cui borum propior fuerit:facies bunc mum propter fuerit: facies bui angulam ad inftar gradus ipfius planeta & eius minutie diuides XII.domus per eum. Dixit Ptolemeus: fi pla nete plures conuenerint in dominio ciufdem loci, er fue rint equales in fortitudine, conftitues dominam loci ib tum qui fuerit dominus baim:quod si in hoc etiam conue nerint, conſtitues.eum qui velocius debet mutari ab effe fuo ad id quod fuerit melius, id est, ex qui voluerit exire de aliquo figno in quo fuerit, & intrare alia din quo ba buerit plures dignitates,vel si fuerint orientales à Sole, eliges eum qui Solo fuerit propinquior, Co non fuerit sub radijs. Si verdin angulo fuerint, ille est eligedus qai gra dui eiufdem anguli fuerit propinquior. & hoc intellige 03.10 Zz C 365 DIFFERENTIA Et ex hoc 1. Nam gradus cardinis exorientis in natiuitatibus fumma diligentia estperueftigandus, ea emim rectè cognito, quicquid in future fato naſcétis celatur, à nature ſtudioſis præpideri poteſt & præ dici: Econtrario mala eiufdé cognitio occafione errorú falfarunque præuifionum prebet, & manifeſta cauſa eſt hallucinan diin locorum Apheticorum dedu&ionibus, quibus ad cognitionem corum, qua-nato in vita obutatura funt, & quo etiam tempore eidem accident, inueftigantur. Cæterùm modi haben di verum afcendens plures funt. Primus & firmissimas eſt per ἀπρόλαβομ. Verum enimươrò cùm fieri nequeat, vt ſingatis pa rientibus adfintartifices, qui idoneis inſtrumentis Solisalte riuſúeaſtri notata altitudine tempus exitus infantis verum ex. acte metiantur: idque obſtetrices ob imperitiam obferuare atque referit nequeantidenique cum horologia & reliqua pre Lidia qua plerunque adhibentur, multipliciter fallant, idcirco conati funt veteros ſapientes oblatum tempus, quopuerum na tum faltem exiftimant, ad præcifionem arte quadam atq; alijs coniecturis ex ipfamtura peritis reducere. Eſtorgo fecundus modus habédi veruafcendens, qui fumitur ex ijs qui con ſoqué tur effe&ibus, quem autor & Ptolemaus pratermiserunt, quia præfupponunt natiuitatem elle proximam, nec quidquam ſcire Mtrologum de nato, non in masculinus fit, an femella, vinus nean marmus, tan sama baft, vt cum hire putent duz ili con tigerint. translation: Since these things are so, the vanity of those men of our time who compile vulgar calendars is manifest. Neglecting true doctrine, they babble publicly about floods in regions, destruction of cities, wars, famine, earthquakes, sterility, and such public calamities, solely from the Sun's passage to the vernal intersection. They fill the world with their lies and mere trifles, and render this most excellent profession infamous, bringing great opprobrium even upon those who care for truth. But they proceed to even greater folly or impudence when they attempt to strengthen their prophecies, as if they had not woven a fabulous history of the future without cause, by means of the annual progressions of great conjunctions, described a little below even by Alcabitius, or by the change of some great orb, no more certain than Plato's great year existing in the true nature of things. Therefore, either no, or a more learned, conjecture about the causes producing these greater effects in the world could not undeservedly be desired in republics. This is that which penetrates through all media from the first and most powerful natural causes to the most proper or particular. It is not, as we said a little earlier concerning Ptolemy's opinion, a prior or more potent cause of such events than that which is derived from the defective conjunctions of the luminaries. This is followed by the method by which the general constitution of each quadrant of the year is derived through the syzygies of the luminaries preceding the Sun's passage into the cardinal points. From this, the annual ephemeris, that is, the monthly method, by which the future constitutions of individual months of the year are produced, must be considered. Then follows the consideration of certain more subtle significations pertaining to the quadrants of each month. Inferior to these is the method by which we are taught to gather the quality of the air on any given day from the rising and setting of illustrious stars. The last place is held by the method which, through each hour of the day, hunts for the quality of the air's constitutions from the passage of the luminaries through the cardines. This is Ptolemy's method of conjecturing about the constitutions of the air, translated from the greatest to the least, which requires both more labor and far greater skill and erudition in things than is contained in all our calendar makers combined into one mass.

translation: Next, calculate the years since birth during which adverse bodily events occurred. Having found these in the Ephemeris, if any misfortunes, such as bodily ailments, occurred on a certain day when the native's body or a malefic ray struck the ascendant degree itself or near it, these will prove the true ascendant of the nativity. Observe diligently that Saturn, passing through the ascendant with its body or malefic ray, causes melancholic infirmities of both mind and body, while Mars causes anger of the soul, wounds, burns, and similar Martial infirmities. Similarly, the transits of the lord of the ascendant and the lord of the geniture through the bodies and noxious rays of malefics must be attended to. Since such accidents occur not only through transits over the ascendant but also through other progressions of the Apheta to anaretic parts, for this reason, in the second place, it teaches how to find the true ascendant from such effects by means of profections, in this manner: See whether the annual profection of the ascendant, about which more below, can reach the bodies or noxious rays of malefics. If so, calculate the mean days between the day of birth and the day of the accident, and multiply them by six, so that the sixths of the days emerge, which, divided by 73 sixths of days (for 73 sixths of days make one degree in annual profections, when they progress by 30 degrees per 365 days), will yield the remaining degrees and minutes. If you subtract these from the place of the promissor of the accident, you will obtain the true degree and minute of the ascendant. For greater clarity, let's take an example. Schonerus was born in the year 1477, on January 16th, at 11 hours after noon, in latitude 50 degrees. In his 58th year, on the first day of January, he writes that he fell from a height, the cause of which was retrograde Saturn in 20 degrees 32 minutes of Scorpio in the tenth house. Through this significant fall, he thus examined the ascendant degree in his geniture. He first noted that in that year, the annual profection of the ascendant had reached 20 degrees 32 minutes of Scorpio, and before the completion of the year, it had reached 21 degrees 42 minutes of Scorpio, where Saturn was in the radix. He counted 349 days from the nativity to the time of the accident, that is, the first of January, which, multiplied by 6, yielded 2094 days. These, divided by 73 days, yielded 28 degrees 40 minutes, which he then subtracted from the place of the promissor, that is, Saturn, which was 20 degrees 32 minutes of Scorpio, and there remained 21 degrees 42 minutes of Scorpio, which are almost the degrees of the true ascendant, namely Libra, which ascends in Schonerus's geniture, as follows: 20 degrees 32 minutes of Scorpio. 1477. January 16th: 11 hours. Plus 30 degrees. 20 degrees 32 minutes of Scorpio. Finally, by directions of the ascendant to unfortunate parts, the ascendant degree is to be proceeded with thus: From the oblique ascension of the promissor of the accident for the given latitude of the region, subtract the degrees and minutes due to the completed years and days from birth up to the year and day of the accident (for each year makes one degree, and six days make one minute in primary directions, as will be explained below). What remains will be the oblique ascension of the ascendant of the same region, which, if you put it into the table of oblique ascensions, you will obtain the true horoscoping degree. The same Schonerus narrates that when he was 40 years old, with 192 days completed, he was afflicted with scabies, ulcers, and various melancholic disturbances, which are manifest effects of Saturn. Therefore, by diligent calculation, it was found that around these days, the direction of the ascendant applied to the square of Saturn, falling in 20 degrees 32 minutes of Scorpio, and inflicted such evils on his body. Knowing these things beforehand, it is not difficult to obtain the true ascendant degree by backward calculation. For if from the oblique ascension of the said square aspect of Saturn, which is 250 degrees 46 minutes, you subtract 40 degrees 32 minutes, which correspond to 40 years and 192 days, there remains the oblique ascension of the true ascendant, namely 210 degrees 14 minutes, to which, in the table of ascensions constructed for latitude 50 degrees, corresponds approximately 21 degrees 40 minutes of Scorpio as the true ascendant of the nativity. Nor indeed are the other significators and promissors with their accidents to be treated differently, so that they may yield the truly horoscoping part. The third method of finding the true ascendant is Ptolemy's, which the author calls Annimodar, that is, which is taken from the dominion of the conjunction or opposition, which they call prevention, immediately preceding the nativity, and is explained here by the author. Here, however, before explaining the method of operation, he raises a very difficult question for us, namely, whether the number of degrees that the planet ruling in the place of the luminaries has made in the sign can truly show the ascendant degree. For if this method, by which the ascendant degree is said to be found by numbers, is universally true when it agrees with the ascendant degree taken by the Astrolabe, why is there a need for observation by the astrolabe or other examinations? But if these two do not generally agree, and yet both are to be trusted, it seems to follow that the birth of a human has two ascendants and twin decrees of the stars, differing in kind, quality, magnitude, and time, which cannot be. Before answering this hesitation, it must first be judged what the principle of human nature is. For man is not born in one moment, as many have thought, but first, turning in the womb, he breaks the cotyledons and the chorion, that is, the membrane in which the infant is enveloped, commonly called the secundine, and opens it. Then, if he is born naturally, he first comes out headfirst, then the middle, and finally the whole. Finally, the umbilical cord is cut or broken during birth, all of which actions from beginning to end never conclude in less than three hours; often, one and sometimes three days are involved. Now, therefore, will all these have force, or only one? If all, we will have nothing certain; if only one, it can rightly be doubted whence it should be taken. For when the head emerges, he is not yet born, and for the whole to emerge seems more voluntary than natural; indeed, it depends on the will of the midwife. Finally, the space from the emergence of the head to the emergence of the whole is too long to be considered the beginning. But here, according to Cardano's opinion, we must respond that the time in which an infant is produced is not a moment, but just as in the art of healing diseases and in all other human cases there is a certain known latitude, so also the nativity has some delay, distinguished by three principal moments, namely, access, consistency, and end, in which whole space the infant receives power from the stars, it must be believed. Access is the first principle, Consistency the second, which, since it is unequally distant from the extremes, and sometimes approaches very close to the end or the beginning, cannot properly be called the middle. The third moment is the end of the whole nativity. But these principles are to be taken from the infant's principal action, namely, respiration, so that the beginning of birth is established when the infant begins to breathe. The middle and principal beginning is when he has breathed so that he can live by himself. The end is when he has already drawn in so much air that he spontaneously ceases to breathe and the umbilical arteries move. Therefore, each principle acquires the powers of the whole nativity, but not equally, but so that each part has its own signification, and the beginning signifies about the beginning, the middle about the middle, and finally the end about the end. These things thus explained and understood, the answer to the question proposed at the beginning will also be clear, namely, that the time which must be taken by the astrolabe is that time which we have said to be principal and almost middle. But the time which the author and Ptolemy teach to find by numbers sometimes coincides with this principal time, from which the most true prognostics of astrologers are taken; sometimes, however, it coincides with the beginning by which wills and the beginnings of things are signified, which usually follows the effects; sometimes, finally, it falls into the end of the nativity, from which the quality of the end of the matter is gathered. And this is what the author says, that by Annimodar the true ascendant degree is not always found, but the most dignified of all degrees of the circle after the ascendant degree at the same hour according to the course of nature.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Two Corollaries from Cardano
text: turalem. Satius eft & tutius per aftrolabum notare principium principale, verùm cùm id plerunque non habeatur, capiendum eſt per Annimodar naturale principum, quod etfi aliquando fit imperfectius, tamen cùm vt oftenfum eft, pars to tius natiuitatis ſemper exiftat atque nunquam otiofum autina ne eſſe poſsit, pro vero principio natiuitatis rectè aſſumitur. :: CONSECTARIA DVO EX EOdem Cardano. Primò ex dictis conſequitur, quòd quibus aſcendit numerus confimilis numero inuento per viam Ptolemæi vel ſaltem valde proximus, vtin exemplo à nobis aſſumpto accidit, tales habeant principia, media & fines valde ſimilia & conformia:quibus verò aſcendens verum plurimum diſtat ab afcendente quæfito per Annimodar, in his principia, media & fines fore valde diſsimilia,præfertim ſi multum diſtiterint. Talesenim aliquádo è faſtigio ad eiuſdem rei ima repentè caſuri funt, atque econtrarid aliquando in profundo demerſi atque omni Ype deſtituti, præter omnium expectationem quaſi numineadiuti ſubitd emergent. Secundò liquet, quòd de fententia Ptolemæi & autoris, qui hunc locum ex Ptolemeo defcripfit, oporteat vtrumque principiűconfiderare, vaŭ quod per aſtrolabum eſt inuentum, & naturale. Nam etſi peraſtrolabum verum principium habere nequeas, illud tamen per confenfum plurimarum progrefsio num, quas veras cum experimento deprehenderis, inuenire po teris, vt fuprà oſtendimus. Sed iam ipfam ephodum, quæ totaà fyzygijs luminarium immediatè genituram præcedentibusde pendet, explicare tempus eft. Si genituram proxime pracefsit coniunctio luminarium, per quem gradum operandum fit, nemo non videt. At finatiui tatem oppofitio luminarium proximè præcefferit, ibi quia lus minaria gradus oppofitos tenent, dubitari poteft, vter gradus Solis ne, an verò Luna locus præuentionis tit. Ad hanc hæfitationem autor de ſententia Ptolemai refpondet, maiorem ele prerogatiuam gradus cius luminaris, quod tempore oppoſitio nis fu Digitized by Google DIFFERENTIA nis ſupra terram fuerit. Quod fi in & vnum luminareorienta Dem horizonta, alterum verò occiduum cardinem ſtringat, tunc gradum in oriente, gradui occidentali præferendum eſſe ait. τίαμ οἱ πείτε τόβις θεωρομία, οι τριγώνῳ τε καὶ δικῳ καὶ να ζώμαζι καὶ ὁρίῳ καὶ φάσφ, ἢ συληματισμῷ : τοτέςι, ὅταμοι Εκαὶ πλείονα τότωμή καὶ παίτα ὁ ζητάμενο ἔχει τόπο πρὸς ομμέλλοντα οικοδεασοτήσφμ. ἰαμμαὶ ἐν ἕνα πρὸς ταῦτα παίτα τὰ πλεῖσα οικείως διακείμλνομ ευρίσκωμεν, ἅμαμ ἐπέχη μοῖραν ὑτῷ ἀκριβῶς καθ' ὁ προδένει δωδεκατημόριομ οἱ τῷ τ' ἐκτροπῆς χρόνῳ, τω ἰσάριθμομ αὐτῆ κρινομθύ ανατέλλομ οι τῷ λλά την αναφορῶμ πραγματείας ευρημονῳ ἐγγυτέρω δωδεκατημορίῳ· ἐαμ δε δύο ἢ καὶ πλείας συνοικοδεασοτέντας δηλονότι ευρίσκωμαν, ε ἂν αὐτὴν ἡ κατὰ τὰ ἐκτροπήν μοιρική πρόδο ἐγγύτερομ ἔχα γόμ ἀριθμὸμ τῇ κατὰ τὰς αναφορὰς αὐατελλόσι, τότο τῇ ποσότηι ἢ μοιρώμ καταχρησόμεθα. εἰ δὲ δύο ἢ καὶ πλείος ἔχοια γόμαρίθο μόμἐγγὺς, τῷ μᾶλλομ ἔχοντι λόγῳ πρὸς τε τοὶ κίτρα καὶ τιώ αίρα σιμκατακολυθήσμαι.ἰαμμοί οι πλείωμ μ ἱδιάςασις τον τοικοι δεασοτίας μοιρῶν πρὸς τὰὸ κατὰ ὁ ὀλοχερές ὡροσκόπιος, ἤπο αρὸς τὸ κατὰ τὸ ὅμοιομ μεσορανήματι : τοῦ αὐτῷ αριθμῷ πρός τω μεστραντζαν μᾶραμ καταχρησάμενοι, λλά ταύτης καὶ τὰ λοιπά τε κάτρο κατας ησόμεθα, hoc eft:Eam itaque quæ proximè geni turam antecefsit copulationem, fiue illa interlunij fuerit fiue plenilunij,capere debemus, & in interlunio quidem parte veri pſque luminaris, in plenilunio verò eius quod fupra torram extiterit partem exacte ſupputatam ſpectantes, confiderare opor set ſtellas in partem illamius dominationis exercentes, quod quidem in his quinque conſtit, triangulo videlicet & domicilio, altitudine, termino & irradiatione ſou configuratione, hoc eſt, cùm vnum aut plura horum aut etiam vniuerfa diAus ille locus erga potituram dominatione tellem obtineri itaqué = QUARTA. 369 itaque ſtellam vnam his omnibus aut pluribus inſtructam repe riemus, tunc parte, quam ille tempore genituræ occupat, in eo ſigno in quo commoratur, ad vnguem notata, iudicabimus par tem numero Hli confimilem exoriri in eo ſigno, cuius partes proximæ maximeque dominatoris partilis loco ſimiles per πραγματᾶαμ feu fupputationem aſcenſionum ſurſum ferri de prehenfum eft. Quòd ſi duos pluresúe ad principatum domina tionis aſpirare deprehendamus, cuius partilis locus tempore genituræ fimilior, ſeu potius numero fuerit vicinior partiiuxta regulam afcenfionum ſcandenti, huius partium numero vtemur. At fi duo plurefúe propinquitate illa numeri conſentiant, tunceum, qui & angulis & conditione téporis magis con- ſentit, ſequemur. Verùm enimuerò vbi huius dominatus partium differentia ratione horoſcopi ad tempus æſtimatum poſiti, maior fuerit,quam reſpectu cæli culminis, tunc illo ipſo eodemque partium numero ad partem culminantem conſtituendam vtentes, fecundum eius ductum reliquos cardines conſtituemus. Senfus ergo huius loci eſt hic: Si erecta figura cœli ad rempus eſtimatum (quod eft quo puerum editum effe putant, ſi per correctifsima inſtrumenta obſeruatum non fit) iam certd cognouiſti, quod fignum (nam niſi de ſignoconstet, hacregula te in craſsum errorem facilè ducere poſſet) hora natiuitatis orientalem horizontaluſtrauerit, atque faltem de gradu horof copante hæſitaueris, deq; eo certior reddi velis: Tum,inquit, fi horam natiuitatis proxime praceſsit & coniunctio, vide diligenter, quis inter planetasinloco coitus plures ac maiores prærogatiuas, quæ ex ſententia Ptolemæi, vt patuit, triangulo, domicilio, altitudine, termino,na) φασφ Η συληματισμῷ, id eft, irradiatione ſeu configuratione continentur, habuerit, atque motum eiuſdem verum ad tempus natiuitatis æſtimatuın diligentiſsimè exquire: quo facto, aſpicias thema cæli ad horam natiuitatis eſtimatam conformatum, & perpende vtrum locus dominatoris interlunij gradui horoſcopati, an vero medio ce li conſtituto ſmilior fit feu propinquior numero, hoc eft, ſecundum quantitatem deiuncta ſeu Arithmeticam. Nam fi propinquior fuerit cardini orientali, ea pars ſigni exorientis in ho roſcopum promouenda eft, quz cöfentit cum gradibus & foru a pulis 370 DIFFERENTIA pulis à planeta dominatore interlunij in ſuo ſigno emenfis. Quòd fi idem planeta dominator propinquior medio cæli fue Fit, totidem partes ſigni ibidem verſantis in culmine cœli col. locandæ funt, atque zandem ad verum vel horoscopi vel medij cæli ſitum reliqua loca conformanda, co modo quem paulò infra exponam.Neque verò aliter examinatur horofcopus geniturarum præuentionalium, vt vocant, per gradum luminaris in fuperiore hemiſphærio commoratis: proinde iam exemplo. rum fupputatione rem intellectu faciliorem reddere debemus. Ac vt primo loco dicatur de genitura à nobis in toto hoc opere exempli gratia deſcripta, manifeſtum eſt eam confecu tam eſſe luminarium oppoſitionem,&quidem talem, in qua fupraterram reperiretur Luna, recens ingreſſa quartam partem. ' Sagittarii, vbi Juppiter ob domum, trigonum & fines habet Car pentum.Et quoniam Iuppiter tempore geniturz deprehenfus eſt in parte 27. Geminorum, qui numerus longiſsimè à parte culminante difcedens, proximè ad numerum horoſcopantium partium accedit, atque ob id in horizonta orientalem partem 27. Libræ iuxta regulam noſtram promouendam eſſeindi cat, id quod planècongruit cum afcendente temporis,fiue illud per ἀρόλαβομ obferuatum, fiue alio modo correptum eſt. Duos quoque potentiores copulationis luminarium antecedentis dominatores, fi cum cufpidum præcipuarum altera con. gruant, regulæ intentionem efficacius perficere eſt conſentaneum. Hic tamen ficut vbique alibi primatum obtinet potentior, & maximè ſiis idem, vt hic accidit, conditionem temporis ſequatur, & ius trianguli, quod in loco copulationis luminarium exercet, hæreticum fuerit.Idem valens efficitur, ſi duorum potentiorum il
translation: It is better and safer to note the principal beginning by astrolabe, but since this is often not available, the natural beginning of the princes must be taken by Annimodar, which, although sometimes imperfect, yet, as has been shown, always exists as part of the whole nativity and can never be idle or useless, and is rightly assumed as the true beginning of the nativity. TWO COROLLARIES FROM THE SAME CARDANO. First, it follows from what has been said that those for whom the ascending number is similar to the number found by Ptolemy's method, or at least very close, as happened in the example we assumed, have very similar and conforming beginnings, middles, and ends: but for those where the true ascendant differs greatly from the ascendant sought by Annimodar, their beginnings, middles, and ends will be very dissimilar, especially if they differ greatly. For such people will sometimes suddenly fall from the summit to the lowest depths of the same thing, and conversely, sometimes, having been plunged into the depths and deprived of all hope, they will suddenly emerge as if aided by divine power, contrary to all expectation. Secondly, it is clear that, according to the opinion of Ptolemy and the author who described this passage from Ptolemy, both beginnings must be considered, the one found by the astrolabe and the natural one. For even if you cannot have the true beginning by astrolabe, you can nevertheless find it by the consensus of many progressions, which you will find true by experience, as we showed above. But now it is time to explain the ephod itself, which depends entirely on the syzygies of the luminaries immediately preceding the geniture. If the conjunction of the luminaries immediately preceded the geniture, no one fails to see by what degree one should operate. But if the opposition of the luminaries immediately preceded the nativity, there, because the luminaries hold opposite degrees, it can be doubted which degree, whether of the Sun or the Moon, is the place of prevention. To this hesitation, the author, according to Ptolemy's opinion, responds that the degree of that luminary which was above the earth at the time of the opposition has greater prerogative. But if one luminary touches the eastern horizon and the other the western cardinal point, then he says that the degree in the east is to be preferred to the western degree. Ptolemy's words on this matter are these: We must take the syzygy that immediately preceded the geniture, whether it was a conjunction or a full moon; and having accurately determined the degree, in the case of a conjunction, of both luminaries, but in the case of a full moon, of that one which was above the earth, we must consider the stars that exercise dominion over that part, which consists in these five: namely, the trigon, domicile, exaltation, term, and irradiation or configuration, that is, when one or more of these, or even all, are held by that place with respect to the ruling star. Therefore, if we find one star suitably disposed to all or most of these, then, having accurately noted the part which it occupies at the time of the geniture, in the sign in which it dwells, we shall judge a part similar in number to it to rise in that sign whose parts, by computation or by the calculation of ascensions, are found to be most similar to the part of the ruler. But if we find two or more aspiring to the principality of dominion, whose part at the time of the geniture was more similar, or rather closer in number, to the part ascending according to the rule of ascensions, we shall use the number of its parts. But if two or more agree in that proximity of number, then we shall follow the one who agrees more with both the angles and the condition of the time. But indeed, where the difference of the parts of this dominion, with respect to the horoscope placed at the estimated time, is greater than with respect to the culmination of the heavens, then, using that very same number of parts to constitute the culminating part, we shall constitute the remaining cardinal points according to its guidance. The meaning of this passage, therefore, is this: If, having erected the figure of the heavens for the estimated time (which is when they think the child was born, if it was not observed by very accurate instruments), you have already certainly known which sign (for unless the sign is certain, this rule could easily lead you into gross error) touched the eastern horizon at the hour of birth, and you have at least hesitated about the horoscoping degree, and wish to be more certain about it: Then, he says, if the conjunction immediately preceded the hour of birth, diligently see which among the planets in the place of conjunction had more and greater prerogatives, which, according to Ptolemy's opinion, as appeared, are contained in the trigon, domicile, exaltation, term, and irradiation or configuration, that is, irradiation or configuration, and most diligently seek out its true motion for the estimated time of birth: having done this, look at the theme of the heavens conformed to the estimated hour of birth, and consider whether the place of the ruler of the interlunium is more similar or closer in number, that is, according to the quantity of the disjunction or arithmetic, to the horoscoping degree or to the midheaven. For if it is closer to the eastern cardinal point, that part of the rising sign is to be advanced into the horoscope which agrees with the degrees and parts measured by the ruling planet of the interlunium in its sign. But if the same ruling planet is closer to the midheaven, so many parts of the sign dwelling there are to be placed in the culmination of the heavens, and then the remaining places are to be conformed to the true position of either the horoscope or the midheaven, in the manner which I will explain a little below. Nor indeed is the horoscope of the preventional genitures, as they call them, examined otherwise, by the degree of the luminary dwelling in the upper hemisphere: therefore, we must now make the matter easier to understand by the calculation of examples. And so, to speak first of the geniture described by us as an example throughout this work, it is manifest that it followed the opposition of the luminaries, and indeed such an opposition in which the Moon was found above the earth, having recently entered the fourth part of Sagittarius, where Jupiter has the carpentum by reason of domicile, trigon, and terms. And since Jupiter at the time of the geniture was found in the 27th part of Gemini, which number, departing farthest from the culminating part, approaches closest to the number of the horoscoping parts, and on account of this indicates that the 27th part of Libra is to be advanced into the eastern horizon according to our rule, which plainly agrees with the ascendant of the time, whether it was observed by astrolabe or corrected in some other way. It is also consistent that the two more powerful rulers of the preceding conjunction of the luminaries, if they agree with one of the principal cusps, more effectively fulfill the intention of the rule. Here, however, as everywhere else, the more powerful one takes precedence, and especially if it also follows the condition of the time, as happens here, and the right of the trigon, which it exercises in the place of the conjunction of the luminaries, was heretical. The same effect is produced if two more powerful ones

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Determining the Ascendant and Midheaven
text: lorum gubernatorum alter quidem cuſpidi domus decima, alter verò prima congruat, cuius rei cuiden. tia perſpicua eſt in noſtro exemplo, ſi in præuentione loco Lunæ præferas aut faltem ſimul conferas locum Solis principium. Geminorum luftrantis, cui Saturnus & Mercurius magna cum potentia imperantes, officere videntur, vt numerus partium illius quidem cuspidi domus primæ, huius verò ſecundæ congruat. Aliud exemplum de duobus ad principatum afpiranti. bus: Tempore aliquo aſtimato culminet pars 18. Cancri, &a ſcendas QVARTA 378 ſcendat pars 14. Libra, & præcedat immediatè hancgenituram luminarium coniunctio in parte 10. Libræ, vbi prærogatiuam primam habet Saturnus in parte 15. Capricorni commorans. Secundam verò prærogatiuam habet Venus luftrans partem vndecimam Scorpionis. Nam vterque iftorum duorum planetarum in dicto loco coniunctionis præcedentis carpentum habet, Saturnus quidem propter exaltationem & trigonum,& quia eundem locum ex parte 15. Capricorni quadrangulo radio illuminat: Venus verò propter domum & fines. Sed quia ipſa eſt valde remota à ſimilitudine numeri angulorum, idcirco præferenduseft ei Saturnus, qui & Venere potentior eft, & numerum partium partibus aſcendentis viciniorem in ſuo figno confecit. Sit rurfus in genitura culmen cæli in parte 15. Aquarij, horoſcopus verò in par.7. Geminorum, & locus coniun&ionis proximè genituram præcedentis in parte 22.Librz, in qua cùm primam prærogatiuam habeat Saturnus orientalis luftrans partem 13. Virginis, ſecundam verò Mercurius percur rens partem 5.Scorpij,patet quòd numerus Mercurij ad numerum afcendentis, & numerus Saturni ad numerum medij cœli zqualiter accedant:quia tamen Saturnus potentior eſt tupropter prærogatiuas in loco coniunctionis immediatè præceden tis, tum verò propter conditionem diei, ideo pars 15. Aquarii in medium cæli potius quàm parss. in afcendentem cardinem promouendaeft.Autor videtur ſentire accipiendam eſſe dominatoris ad angulos propinquitatem fecundum quantitatem continuam. Veluti ſi ſit horoscopus genituræ æſtimatæ par. 5. , & M.C.par.4. ४, & dominator planeta fit Sol, tranfiens per part.14. Arietis, tunc iuxta quantitatem continuam Sol propior eſt medio cæli quam horoſcopo, maior enim eſt diſtantia ab Ariete in Capricornum, quam in Taurů, & obid gradus 14. Tauri in cufpidem medii cæli eſſetpromouendus, atque ad fitum eiuſdem medii cœli reliqua loca.conformanda.Sed-quantitas diſcreta protrudit ad horofcopum p.14.Capricorni, quod numerus 14.propius accedat ad s.quam ad 4. Verùm enimuerd hoc negotium intelligendum eſſe de quantitate diſcreta & non continua, ipſa verba Ptolemai paulò antè à nobis recitata apersè loquuntur. Traditur aut vulgò alius quartus modus de inue niendo 2 1 ٢٠ : 372 DIFFERENTIA De truti-, niendo gradu horoſcopante per moram nati in vtero materno, na Herme quem trutinam Hermis, nominant. Hermes enim rerum cæletis.. ſtium antiquiſsimus ſcriptor, ex caſu ſpermatis in matricem té pora geniturarum æſtimata emendat, atque verum gradum exorientem exquirit, quòd illelocus cæli,in quo) eſt tempore conceptus,in cardinem exorientem tempore genituræ incidat. Voluit enim Lunam ad hominis natiuitatem tantam habere có formitatem,vt locus Lunæ in caſu ſpermatis aſcendat tempore genituræ,& vice verſa Lunæ locus tempore natiuitatis ſitaſcen dens caſus ſpermatis in matricem : vt fi fuerit in part.3.४. fœmina concipiente fætum, eundem gradum oriri ſupponit ipſa pariente. Rurfus_fi conceptus tempore emergat primus gradus Scorpij, puerum non nasciturum niſi cùm Luna ad primam partem eiufdem venerit. Nonnunquam tamen non idem fignum, ſed oppoſitumaſcendere prodidit, quod tunc verum eſſe volunt, quando tempore concubitus in occiduo cardine reperitur. Verba eius hæc funt:cθα ὀξίμ εν τε καιρῷ το eve Enuncia- τάσεως ἡ) ἐκεῖνο τὸ ζώδιομ εἰ τῷ απορᾷ ὡροσκότοκομικας οἴθα ἐπὶ επιστ... Σποράς ϊτυχεῳ, ἐκεῖνο οι' τῷ ἀποκυήση ὡροσκόπιμο το τεκα τὰ διάμετρομ, id eft, in quo ligno eſt genituræ tempore, illud in conceptu fac afcendens, & in quo figno inuenta fuerit in conceptu, illud aut eius oppoſitum fac afcendens in partu. Hocalphoriſmo poſito & coceſſo, per altitudinem, quam Luna tempore partus in noſtro vel το ανεχθόνωμ hemiſperio habet, gradű tépore natiuitatis alicuius exorienté venant in hunç mo dum;Primò conſtruatur thema coeli, & inueniatur locus Lunz verus ad tempus quo infantem natum eſſe exiftimant.Secundo inueniatur dſtantia Lunæ ab horoscopo vel cardine occidentali:ab horoscopo quidem ſi infra terram, à ſeptima verò ſi ſupra terram commoretur. Talem verò diſtantiam ſupputabis hocmodo. Signa & partes anguli à ſignis & partibus Lunæ fubtrahe, accommodata, vt calculus aſtronomicus exigit, integra revolutione, quotiefcunque inferior à ſuperiore deduci nequi uerit: quo-facto,remotio Lunæ à cardine relinquetur.. Tertiò remotionem Lunse à cardine repertam quære in tabella mora foctus in vrero materno, & deprehendes dies more in prioxi quidem ſcric.aumerorum, ſi Lunatempore genituræ fubter: ra fue : QUARTA.. 373 rafuerit:in-poſteriore verò,ſiin ſuperiore hemiſphærio ambuJauerit. Quartò vt tempus conceptionis inuentum habeas, moram inuentam à tempore natiuitatis retrò numera, vel quod idé eſt, eandem à tempore natiuitatis, quod principio anni Romanorum & punto natiuitatis intercipitur, ſubtrahe, accommodata integra reuolutione anni præcedentis, ſi quando ſubtratio fieri nequeat, vtrobique diligenter ad biſſextum animaduertens, & relinquetur numerus dierum conceptionis anni eiufdem aut præcedentis, quando videlicet vnam anni præcedentis ſeu communis ſeu biſſexti periodum accommodaueris. Vt in noſtro exemplo, motus Lunæ eſt ſignorum 10. par.27.ſc. 52.11,& horoſcopus ſignorum 6.part.26.ſcr.44.1,quibus à motu Lunæ ſublatis, relinquitur Lunæ ab aſcendente diſtantia, nempèſignorum 4. part. 1. ſcr.8.1, cui in prima ſerie tabellæ reſpondent dies 283. quibus natus in vtero materno detentus fuit. Cùm autem à principio anni 1 127. labentis ad tempus genituræ elapfi fint dies ſaltem 1417.qui pauciores ſunt, quam vt dierum mora in vtero ſubtractionem ferant, idcircò adijciam eis reuolutionem integram præcedentis & comunis anni, hoceſt, dies 365. & producam in ſumma dies 5064. à quibus mora in in vtero dierű 283. ſubtracta, reliquos facit dies 223. cum quibus, vt menſis diem anni præcedentis agnoſcas, intra in tabellam menſium anni communis,in qua è regione Iulij offerent ſe dies 2 12. proximè minores, quos à diebus 223. ſubtrahe,& remanebunt dies 11. Quodfi) motu bidui vel infra à dictis ſignis abfuerit, motus eius ad dies proximè ſequentes capiendus eſt: Et econtrariò ſi Luna eadem loca excef- ſerit, motus Lunæ dierum antecedentium tantiſper ſpectandi, quoad locum Lunæ proximè minorem cardinis loco perueniatur:ſic enim fiet, vt vera genitura, ficut rationi conſentaneum eſt,ſem.
translation: The two rulers of the house, one of the cusp of the tenth house, the other of the first, agree. The evidence for this is clear in our example, if in the prevention you prefer the place of the Moon to the place of the Sun, or at least compare it simultaneously with the beginning of Gemini, which Saturn and Mercury, ruling with great power, seem to obstruct, so that the number of parts of the former agrees with the cusp of the first house, and that of the latter with the second. Another example concerns two aspirants to leadership: At some estimated time, let the 18th degree of Cancer culminate, and the 14th degree of Libra ascend, and let the conjunction of the luminaries immediately precede this nativity in the 10th degree of Libra, where Saturn, residing in the 15th degree of Capricorn, has the first prerogative. Venus, traversing the eleventh degree of Scorpio, has the second prerogative. For each of these two planets has a chariot in the said place of the preceding conjunction, Saturn indeed because of exaltation and trine, and because he illuminates the same place from the 15th degree of Capricorn with a square ray: Venus indeed because of house and bounds. But because she is very remote from the similarity of the number of angles, therefore Saturn is to be preferred to her, who is also more powerful than Venus, and has made the number of parts closer to the parts of the ascendant in his sign. Let the Midheaven again be in the 15th degree of Aquarius in the nativity, and the ascendant in the 7th degree of Gemini, and the place of the conjunction immediately preceding the nativity in the 22nd degree of Libra, in which, since the oriental Saturn, traversing the 13th degree of Virgo, has the first prerogative, and Mercury, traversing the 5th degree of Scorpio, has the second, it is clear that the number of Mercury approaches the number of the ascendant, and the number of Saturn approaches the number of the Midheaven equally: however, since Saturn is more powerful, both because of the prerogatives in the place of the immediately preceding conjunction, and because of the condition of the day, therefore the 15th degree of Aquarius should be promoted to the Midheaven rather than the 5th degree to the ascendant. The author seems to think that the proximity of the ruler to the angles should be taken according to continuous quantity. As if the ascendant of the estimated nativity is the 5th degree of Taurus, and the Midheaven the 4th degree of Capricorn, and the ruling planet is the Sun, passing through the 14th degree of Aries, then according to continuous quantity the Sun is closer to the Midheaven than to the ascendant, for the distance from Aries to Capricorn is greater than to Taurus, and therefore the 14th degree of Taurus should be promoted to the cusp of the Midheaven, and the other places conformed to the position of the same Midheaven. But discrete quantity pushes the 14th degree of Capricorn to the ascendant, because the number 14 is closer to 5 than to 4. But indeed, that this matter is to be understood of discrete and not continuous quantity, the very words of Ptolemy, recited by us a little earlier, speak openly. Another fourth method is commonly handed down for finding the ascendant degree by the duration of the native in the mother's womb, which they call the balance of Hermes. For Hermes, the most ancient writer on celestial matters, corrects the estimated times of nativities from the moment of the sperm's fall into the womb, and seeks the true rising degree, because that place of the sky, in which it is at the time of conception, falls into the rising angle at the time of birth. For he wanted the Moon to have such a conformity to the birth of man, that the place of the Moon at the fall of the sperm ascends at the time of birth, and vice versa, the place of the Moon at the time of birth, if it is the ascendant of the fall of the sperm into the womb: so if it was in the 3rd degree of Taurus when the woman conceived the fetus, he supposes the same degree to rise when she gives birth. Again, if at the time of conception the first degree of Scorpio emerges, the child will not be born unless the Moon comes to the first part of the same. Sometimes, however, he stated that not the same sign, but the opposite, ascends, which they want to be true when at the time of coitus it is found in the western angle. His words are these: “In what sign is the nativity at the time of conception, make that the ascendant; and in what sign it was found at conception, make that or its opposite the ascendant at birth.” This aphorism being posited and conceded, by the altitude which the Moon has at the time of birth in our hemisphere, they find the rising degree at the time of someone's birth in this way: First, construct the celestial chart, and find the true place of the Moon for the time when they estimate the infant to have been born. Second, find the distance of the Moon from the ascendant or the western angle: from the ascendant if it is below the earth, but from the seventh if it is above the earth. You will calculate such a distance in this way: Subtract the signs and parts of the angle from the signs and parts of the Moon, adjusted, as astronomical calculation requires, by a full revolution, as often as the lower cannot be subtracted from the higher: this done, the removal of the Moon from the angle will remain. Third, seek the found removal of the Moon from the angle in the table of the duration of the fetus in the mother's womb, and you will find the days of duration in the first series of numbers, if the Moon was below the earth at the time of birth; but in the latter, if it was in the upper hemisphere. Fourth, to have the found time of conception, count back the found duration from the time of birth, or, which is the same, subtract it from the time of birth, which is intercepted between the beginning of the Roman year and the point of birth, adjusted by a full revolution of the preceding year, if subtraction cannot be done, diligently observing the bissextile year in both cases, and the number of days of conception of the same or the preceding year will remain, when namely you have adjusted one period of the preceding year, whether common or bissextile. As in our example, the Moon's motion is 10 signs, 27 degrees, 52 minutes, 11 seconds, and the ascendant is 6 signs, 26 degrees, 44 minutes, 1 second. Subtracting these from the Moon's motion, the Moon's distance from the ascendant remains, namely 4 signs, 1 degree, 8 minutes, 1 second, to which in the first series of the table correspond 283 days, during which the native was detained in the mother's womb. However, since from the beginning of the year 1127, at least 1417 days have elapsed until the time of birth, which are fewer than to allow subtraction of the days of duration in the womb, therefore I will add to them a full revolution of the preceding and common year, that is, 365 days, and I will produce a sum of 5064 days, from which, subtracting the 283 days of duration in the womb, 223 days remain. With these, to recognize the day of the month of the preceding year, enter the table of months of the common year, in which opposite July, 212 days, the next smaller number, will present themselves. Subtract these from 223 days, and 11 days will remain. Therefore, the estimated day of conception according to this method is found to be the twelfth of August, at the end of which the Moon traversed the 20th degree, 16 minutes, 1 second of Libra, as the Ephemerides tables show you. Middle. Below earth from ascendant. Above earth from seventh. Whether the found day is the true day of conception or not? If on the estimated day of conception you find the Moon's place in the sign, at the time when the infant is said to have been born, rising, as happened in our example, or even in its opposite, know that you have reached the true goal with a certain calculation. For, as was said a little earlier, in what sign it was found at conception, that same or its opposite ascends at birth. But if the Moon was absent from the said signs by a two-day motion or less, its motion for the next following days must be taken: And conversely, if the Moon exceeded the same places, the Moon's motion of the preceding days must be observed until the Moon's place reaches the next smaller place of the angle: for thus it will happen that the true nativity, as is consistent with reason, always...

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Moon in Sun's Rays
text: ==Start of page 2== QUARTA. * Et cum hoc fi fuerit Luna in his locis ſub radijs So lis, non erit apta bylech. 4 Afpicies autem bylech in angulis & ſuccedentibus eorum ſecundum quòd æquantur XII.domus circuli per gradus horarum aſcendentis, ſecundum quòd exponitur eius opus in canone planetarum, idest, in libro curſuum planetarum. 5 Cumque æquaueris domos hoc modo: omnis planeta qui fuerit ante gradum ſigni aſcendentis, vel cuiustibet domus per V.gradus, vel infrà,erit fortitudo eius va lida in domo quæ ei ſuccedit. 6 Et omnis locus ex locis quæ prædiximus, eſt aptus hylech:ſi aſpexerit eum aliquis dominorum illius qui fit. dominus domus, aut dominus exaltationis aut dominus: terminis aut triplicitatis: aut dominus faciei : & ſi non. non erit aptus hylech. De Alcochoden. Et ex hoc Alcochoden, qui est ſignificator vitæ,id est minus annorum vel dans annos:quem cum volueris ſci re, & cùm prænoſti hylech:eo ordine quem prædixi,aſpi cies dominum domus hylech, aut dominum exaltationis eius, aut dominum terminieius:aut dominum triplicitatis: aut faciei eius, quis horum fortior fuerit & autentior in loco hylech : & ſi aſpexerit hylech, erit dignior alcochoden: quòd ſi non aſpexerit hylech, qui fuerit pluris auctoritatis : aſpicies eum qui fuerit auctoritatis minoris, donec inuenias aliquem ex illis qui aſpiciat: quòd ſi non aſpexerit hylech, nõ poterit ille ba locus Digitized by Google ==End of page 2== ==Start of page 3== 388 DIFFERENTIA locus hylech: & inter-quæres ab alio hylech, & iterum quæres ab eodem ſecundo hylech alcochoden eo ordine quo prædiximus: quòd ſi æquales fuerint duo planetæ veteres in auctoritate gradus hylech, & aſpexerint hylech, erit dignior alcochoden qui fuerit fortior lo co.Si verò fuerint æquales in fortitudine loci,ſcilicet vbi fuerint ipſi planetæ, erit ille alcochoden qui propior fue rit gradui hylech. Quibuſdam autem videbatur melius inſpicere à domino domus: qui ſi aſpexerit hylech, ponebant eum alcochoden, & non conſiderabant alterum: & ſi non inueniſſent dominum domus aſpicientem hylech, accipiebant dominum exaltationis : quem ſi aſpicere viderent hylech, ponebant alcochoden eum, & non conſiderabant alium: & ſimiliter faciebant de domino triplicitatis terminis atq faciei per ordinem: & erat Dorotheus præponens dominum termini in hoc domino domus: Et quidam dixerunt, quòd ſi æquales fuerint duo planetæ, vel tres, vel plures in dignitate, & in propinquitate aſpectus gradui hylechs ille erit alcochoden, qui erit in loco laudabili à Sole: ideſt; qui fuerit cum Sole in vno gradu per XVI. minuta, aut infrà: vel propinquior gradui anguli : aut in initiis ortus ſui, id est, cum mane apparuerit: aut fuerit in ſtatione ſua ſecunda; vel in aliquo eſſe ſcilicet laudabili à Sole, ſicut prædiximus, ipſe erit alchocoden. Si verò gradus Solis fuerit in hylech, & fuerit in Ariete vel in Leone, erit Sol hylech, & alcochoden fimul: & non conſiderabitur alius alcochoden præter eñ. ſimi. Digitized by Google ==End of page 3== ==Start of page 4== 1 QVARTA ſimiliter fi fuerit gradus Lunæ hylech & fuerit in Tau ro vel in Cancro, habebit eam pro hylech & alcochoden ſimul, & non conſiderabis alcochoden aliũ præter eam. 7. Almut est qui præeſt natiuitati: ab eo ſignificatur eſſe nati post hylech & alcochoden, & ipſe planetis cæ- teris est pluris auctoritatis, in aſcendente & in locis luminarium atqne in loco partis fortunæ, in loco quoque coniunctionis vel prænentionis, quæ eſt ante natiuitate. Quod ſi præfuerit aliquis planeta duobus locis aut tribus, aut quatuor, aut pluribus pre multitudine poteſta tis fuæ, erit ipſe Almut,id est, auctor & ſignificator vitæ post hylech & alcochoden, & per eum ſignificatur eſſenati. & quidam habent eum pro alcochoden ad dan dum vitam. { Sequentia duo capita ad maximam & præcipuam Geneth, Et ex hoc lialogica quæſtionem, qua vitæ ſpacium nati exponitur,perti, hylech&c. nent. Etſi veroteſte Ptolemæo, huius quæſtionis non ſimplex aut leuis nec facilis fit expoſitio, & deniq; magni laboris fit pru dentiæque non vulgaris videre, quando vitæ datoris vis extinguatur,& quæ ſtella ſit angong seu vitæ nati interemptrix, perſpicere: Tamen præcepta quæ hic traduntur,nec adeo obſcu ra ſunt, nec difficilia,& tribus potissimum locis concluduntur, Omnino enim hæc tractatio dependet ex Apheticorum locorum, ijsque dominantium ſtellarum,atque denique aſtrorum locorumque.interimentium deprehenſione, quorum vnum, quodque quomodo ex traditione Ptolemæi dijudicandum fit, primò capitulatim indicabo, poſteà verò his ſententiam autoris aliorumque Arabum attexam. ἐπεί τόπων ἀφεζικοςμο De locis Apheticis. ) De his ſic dicit Ptolemæus: τόποι ἀφελικοὶ εἰσὶμ οι οἷς εἶναι δε πείτως τόμ μέλλονται τὸ κυείαν γ'αφέσεως λαμβάνω, hoc est: b3 ! Loca Digitized by Google ==End of page 4== ==Start of page 5== १. DIFFERENTIA Loca Aphetica ſeu loca prorogantia ſunt, in quibus omninò neceſſe eſt reperiri cum qui velit vitæ moderandæ iuriſdictione potiri. Numerat autem ficut prorogatores, ita loca in quibus prorogatores poſiti vitam decernunt, quinque, quorum ſe ries hæc eſt:Primus locus, quique cæteris potentia & auctoritate hicpræfertur, est medium cæli. Secundus & primò poteſtate proximus, est aſcendens. Tertius eſt domus vndecima, ἀγαθος δαίμων. Quartus eſt ſeptima domus. Quintus & reliquis debilior, eſt locus nonus θεός. Reliquæ ſeptem domus funt inutiles. Primò enim omne quod ſubterraneum eſt, id Ptolemæus in hoc præclaro imperij genere meritò poſthabendum docet, eo tantum excepto, quòd cùm horoſcopo in lucem prodit, hoc est, gradus 25! qui cardinem orientalem conſequuntur. Deinde ex ijs locis qui ſupra terram extant, nec octauum τόπον ἀργὸν, id est, locum otioſum dictum, qui nullo familiaritatis vinculo cùm aſcendente coheret, accipere conuenit, nec duodecimum, qui κακὸ δαίμων, id eſt, mali dæmonis locus appellatur. Nam præterquam quòd ab angulo præcipitatur, horoſcopoque eſt ἀσιώδες, fumoſa tenebricoſitas ex terræ vaporibus nata, aſtrorum ibi commorantium effluxus conturbat ac veluti abolet; qua de cauſa ſuis neque coloribus, neque magnitudinibus apparent. Locaautem quæ ponit eſſe Aphetica omnia præter 25. partes centro orientis ſuccedentes, ſupra terram extant, & horoſcopo in æquinoctiali, vndecimus quidem ſexangulo, decimus quadrato, nonus trigono, ſeptimus denique aſpectu oppoſito configurantur. Ad extremum id diligenter hic obſeruandum est, ſingula loca tricenas æquinoctialis partes comprehendere, quarum quinæ cuſpidem antecedunt, & 25. ſuccedentes eandem ſequuntur. Quapropter quamuis locus vndecimus vſque ad duodecimum per 30: gradus æquinoctialis extendatur: tamen quinque illi gradus vltimi vndecimæ, vt non omnino virtute careant, eam herclè vim non habent, vt ſtellam ibi commorantem dominam vitæefficiant. Quòdo verò locus idoneus,idoneo loco ſuccedit, tuncilli quinque gradus vltimi nõ auferútur, quia quamuis ratione loci cuius ſunt pars, boni non ſint, tamen ratione loci ſequentis idonei cenſentur. Veluti 5. partes vltimæ loci noni, licet apæ ſint bonæ ratione loci propi, 4 L ſunt Digitized by Google ==End of page 5==
translation: And if the Moon is in these places under the Sun's rays, it will not be suitable as Hyleg. You will look for the Hyleg in the angles and their succedent houses, according to how the twelve houses of the circle are equalized by the degrees of the ascending hours, as its operation is explained in the canon of the planets, that is, in the book of the courses of the planets. And when you have equalized the houses in this way: every planet that is five degrees or less before the degree of the ascending sign, or of any house, its strength will be valid in the house that succeeds it. And every place among the places we have mentioned is suitable for Hyleg: if any of its lords, who is the lord of the house, or the lord of its exaltation, or the lord of its terms, or of its triplicity, or the lord of its face, aspects it; and if not, it will not be suitable for Hyleg. Regarding Alcochoden. And from this Alcochoden, who is the significator of life, that is, the giver of years or the lesser giver of years: when you wish to know him, and when you have known the Hyleg in the order I have described, you will look at the lord of the Hyleg's house, or the lord of its exaltation, or the lord of its term, or the lord of its triplicity, or of its face, which of these is stronger and more authoritative in the place of the Hyleg: and if he aspects the Hyleg, he will be more worthy of being Alcochoden: but if he does not aspect the Hyleg, whoever has more authority: you will look at the one with less authority, until you find one of them who aspects it: but if he does not aspect the Hyleg, that place will not be able to be the Hyleg. And you will seek another Hyleg, and again you will seek the Alcochoden from the same second Hyleg in the order we have described: but if two planets are equal in authority to the degree of the Hyleg, and aspect the Hyleg, the one who is stronger in place will be more worthy of being Alcochoden. But if they are equal in strength of place, that is, where the planets themselves are, the one who is closer to the degree of the Hyleg will be the Alcochoden. To some, it seemed better to look at the lord of the house: if he aspected the Hyleg, they would make him Alcochoden, and would not consider another: and if they did not find the lord of the house aspecting the Hyleg, they would take the lord of the exaltation: if they saw him aspecting the Hyleg, they would make him Alcochoden, and would not consider another: and similarly they would do with the lord of the triplicity, terms, and face in order: and Dorotheus preferred the lord of the term over the lord of the house in this matter: And some said that if two planets, or three, or more, are equal in dignity, and in proximity of aspect to the degree of the Hyleg, the one who is in a laudable place from the Sun will be the Alcochoden: that is, the one who is with the Sun in one degree by sixteen minutes, or less: or closer to the degree of the angle: or in the beginnings of its rising, that is, when it appears in the morning: or is in its second station; or is in some laudable state from the Sun, as we have said, he will be the Alcochoden. But if the degree of the Sun is in the Hyleg, and it is in Aries or Leo, the Sun will be Hyleg and Alcochoden together: and no other Alcochoden will be considered besides it. Similarly, if the degree of the Moon is Hyleg and it is in Taurus or Cancer, you will take it as Hyleg and Alcochoden together, and you will not consider any other Alcochoden besides it. Almut is he who presides over the nativity: from him is signified the being born after the Hyleg and Alcochoden, and he is of greater authority than the other planets, in the ascendant and in the places of the luminaries and in the place of the Part of Fortune, and also in the place of conjunction or prevention, which is before the nativity. But if any planet presides over two places or three, or four, or more by the multitude of its power, it will be the Almut, that is, the author and significator of life after the Hyleg and Alcochoden, and through him is signified the being born. And some consider him as Alcochoden for giving life. The following two chapters pertain to the greatest and principal astrological question, by which the lifespan of the native is explained. And although, according to Ptolemy, the explanation of this question is not simple or easy, nor is it easy, and finally it requires great labor and uncommon prudence to see when the power of the giver of life is extinguished, and which star is the destroyer of life or the killer of the native, to perceive: Nevertheless, the precepts taught here are neither so obscure nor difficult, and are concluded in three principal places. For this treatise depends entirely on the apprehension of the Aphetic places, and the stars ruling them, and finally the stars and places that destroy. One of these, and how each is to be judged from the tradition of Ptolemy, I will first indicate chapter by chapter, and afterwards I will add the opinion of the author and other Arabs. Regarding Aphetic places. Concerning these, Ptolemy says thus: Aphetic places are those in which it is absolutely necessary for him to be found who wishes to obtain jurisdiction over the moderation of life. He enumerates, just as he does prorogators, so also the places in which prorogators, when placed, determine life, five, whose series is this: The first place, and which is preferred to the others in power and authority here, is the Midheaven. The second, and first in proximity of power, is the Ascendant. The third is the eleventh house, the Good Spirit. The fourth is the seventh house. The fifth, and weaker than the others, is the ninth house, God. The remaining seven houses are useless. For first, Ptolemy rightly teaches that everything that is subterranean is to be postponed in this excellent kind of dominion, with the sole exception that when it comes to light with the horoscope, that is, the 25 degrees that follow the eastern cardinal point. Then, from those places that are above ground, it is not appropriate to accept the eighth place, called idle, which is not connected to the ascendant by any bond of familiarity, nor the twelfth, which is called the place of the evil daemon. For besides being cast down from the angle, and being smoky and dark to the horoscope, born from earthly vapors, it disturbs and as it were abolishes the efflux of the stars dwelling there; for which reason they do not appear in their colors or magnitudes. But the places which he posits as Aphetic, all except the 25 parts succeeding the center of the east, are above ground, and to the horoscope in the equinoctial, the eleventh is configured by a sextile, the tenth by a square, the ninth by a trine, and the seventh by an opposite aspect. Finally, it must be diligently observed here that each place comprises thirty equinoctial parts, of which five precede the cusp, and 25 succeeding ones follow it. Therefore, although the eleventh place extends up to the twelfth by 30 degrees of the equinoctial: nevertheless, those last five degrees of the eleventh, although they are not entirely devoid of virtue, do not have the power to make a star dwelling there the lord of life. But when a suitable place succeeds a suitable place, then those last five degrees are not removed, because although they are not good by reason of the place of which they are a part, nevertheless they are considered suitable by reason of the succeeding place. As the last 5 parts of the ninth place, although they are good by reason of the proper place, they are not.

title: Aphetic Places and Alcocoden
