Hoc modo dirigen dos eſſe Aphetas exiſtentes in-cardinibus, omnes probati magi ſtri conſentiunt, & fæpius Ptolemæus admonuit.Item Hermes enunciato 25.mid, inquit, όμποείπαμ τε ἐπικρατήρα, ὅτι ἐδὶ οὐ τῷ μεσγρανήματι λλά την αναφορῶμ το σφαίρας, ότε δὲ όζιμ οι τῷ ὡροσκόπω, λλά την αναφορῶμ το κλίματα,hoceft:Dominatoris progreſsionem cùm in medio cæli poſitus eſt, per ſphæ ræaſcenſiones,cùm verò in aſcendente conſtitutus eft, per cli matis afceniones facito. 2. Hactenus de modo dirigendi ſignificatore in angulis con- ſtituto, in ſequentibus exponit, quid ſequendum fit ſi extraan gulos reperiatur, nimirum vtræque tam climatis quam ſphæræ aſcenſiones capiende ſunt, fimulque miſcendæ inuicem temperando:vbi ante omnia explorandum eſſe monet, an ſignificator ſupra,an verò infra terram commoretur, quo facto, diſtantia fr gnificatoris à M.C.vel I.M.C. perueftiganda eſt in hunc modú: Si apheta ſupra terram exiſtens fuerit in quarta orientali, id eft, fi nondum attigit meridianum, auferas afcenfionem rectam me dij cœli ab afcenfione recta Aphetæ : Siautem fuerit in quarta occidentali, id eſt, ſi meridianum tranfierit, fupra terram tamen adhuccommorans, aufer afcenfionem rectam Aphetæ ex aſcen Hone recta M. C. & relinquetur diſtantia Aphetæ à meridiano feu M.C.diurna, quæ vtautor præcipit, per horaria tempora Aphete & quidem diurna partienda eſt, vt temporales hore Aphe se quibus à M. C.diſtat exeant. Similiter fi Apheta infra terram fuerit, pari facilitate diſtantiam eius à meridiano ſeu I.M.C ſup putabis. Si enim in quarta medietatis deſcendétis, hoc eft, fian se meridianum ſub terra fuerit, aſcenſionem rectam anguli terrz,ex afcenfione recta Aphetæ minue:Si verò in quarta medietatisafcendentis fuerit, hoc eſt, ſi angulum terre tranfierit, tunc afcenſionem rectam Aphetz aufer ex aſcenſione recta angulis, & relinquetur no&urna eius à meridiano diftantia, quz per ho saria tempora nocturna ſignificatoris partienda eft, vt numesus ſectionis oftendat elongationem Aphetæ ab angulo terra in horis temporalibus. Ad eundem verd ſcopum, & fortaffe fa eilius peruenies, fi per oppoſitapuncta opereris. translation: The difference here is that he did not provide the rationale for the operations, which we see in Alfonso's tables. Consequently, unless one is a very skilled mathematician, one must perpetually adhere to Regiomontanus's rule and tables, which he deliberately did so that people would be servants of his book, for which he was justly criticized by my father. For a person is liable to error in three ways. First, if some table is faulty in part. Second, if you somehow misunderstand the rule, which easily happens amidst so many variations of northern latitude, southern latitude, right ascension, oblique ascension, nocturnal or diurnal arcs, of the whole or of the half in the descending or ascending half, above or below the earth. For this reason, in my judgment, the best is a large solid sphere, or a large planisphere, made precisely from brass, etc. Thus, Cardan, though many praised the method of directing by the most learned Regiomontanus, scarcely approved of it due to the difficulty of operation, and in his later years, being confirmed in the truth of the art, he seems rarely or never to have used it. But to discuss these matters at greater length is not our purpose, for we aim not so much to explain what is true in this matter as to provide a clear method of directing. First, then, to begin with the author's method of directing, we are admonished here, above all, to diligently ascertain in which part of the sky the significator planet is situated, whose varied position in different places causes us to attend now to the ascensions of the sphere, now to those of the climate in which we are situated, when collecting times. For if the significator is placed in the eastern angle, according to the ascensions of the climate; if it holds the western angle, according to the descensions of the climate; if finally it occupies the midheaven or the imum coeli, the times of future events must be measured and calculated according to the right ascensions of the sphere. Therefore, if you wish to direct the significator located in the ascendant to some place of the subsequent promittor, subtract the oblique ascensions of the significator from the oblique ascensions of the promittor, that is, subtract the beginning of the arc from the end: for what remains will show the arc of direction. For example, if the Sun from Capricorn makes a square aspect to the 12th part of Aries, and Jupiter is placed in the ending degree of Cologne, holding the fifth part of Pisces, and is to be directed to the square rays of the Sun, first you will seek the oblique ascension of the promittor, that is, the 12th part of Aries, from the table calculated for latitude 51 degrees (for such is the altitude of the north pole of Cologne), and you will find it to be 5 degrees, 8 minutes. Then in a similar way, take the oblique ascension of the significator, namely the 5th part of Pisces, which is 349 degrees, 2 minutes. Third, subtract the latter ascension, namely that of the significator, which is 349 degrees, 2 minutes, from the former ascension, that is, of the promittor, which is 5 degrees, 8 minutes, accommodating a full revolution or 360 degrees; for this must always be done whenever in physical calculation the lower exceeds the higher and therefore cannot be subtracted from it. This done, there will remain 16 degrees, 6 minutes of the sought direction. But if the significator was in the angle of the west, subtract the oblique descension of the significator from the oblique descension of the promittor, and the times of direction will remain. Nor is it otherwise what the author prescribes, that the oblique ascension of the point diametrically opposite the place of the significator (for the Arabs call this the nadir) should be subtracted from the oblique ascension of the point which is diametrically opposite the place of the promittor. For in an oblique sphere, the ascension of a sign is the descension of the opposite sign, and conversely, the descension of a sign is the ascension of the opposite sign, as in the previous example: If the Sun is in the western angle, and is to be directed to a ray: first, the descension of the promittor, that is, the 12th part of Aries, must be sought, or what is the same, the oblique ascension of the 12th part of the opposite sign, that is, Libra, must be found, which is 196 degrees, 55 minutes. Then similarly, the descension of the significator, that is, the 5th part of Pisces, must be extracted, that is, the ascension of the opposite sign, the 5th part of Virgo, which is 144 degrees, 40 minutes, which, subtracted from the former, leaves the sought times of direction, 52 degrees, 15 minutes. Finally, if the significator is in the midheaven or imum coeli, the right ascension of the significator must be subtracted from the right ascension of the promittor, so that the sought times of direction emerge. Example: In the same altitude of the region, 51 degrees, the culminating point of a certain nativity was 16 degrees of Scorpio, the significator of honor and mastery, which I wish to direct to the Sun, that is, to the promittor, which was in 9 degrees of Leo. Therefore, subtract the right ascension of the significator, which is 107 degrees, 22 minutes, from the right ascension of the promittor, which is 131 degrees, 27 minutes, and there remain 24 degrees, 14 minutes, the sought direction of the Midheaven to the trine of the Sun. The operation is to be performed in precisely the same way whenever the significator holds the Imum Coeli. All approved masters agree that Aphetas existing in the angles are to be directed in this way, and Ptolemy has often admonished this. Likewise, Hermes in his 25th enunciation says: "The progression of the ruler, when it is placed in the midheaven, make it by the ascensions of the sphere; but when it is constituted in the ascendant, by the ascensions of the climate." 2. So far concerning the method of directing the significator situated in the angles. In the following, he explains what should be followed if it is found outside the angles, namely, both the ascensions of the climate and of the sphere must be taken, and at the same time mixed together by tempering. Here, above all, he warns that it must be ascertained whether the significator is above or below the earth. This done, the distance of the significator from the Midheaven or Imum Coeli must be investigated in this way: If the Apheta, being above the earth, is in the eastern quadrant, that is, if it has not yet reached the meridian, subtract the right ascension of the midheaven from the right ascension of the Apheta. But if it is in the western quadrant, that is, if it has passed the meridian, yet still remaining above the earth, subtract the right ascension of the Apheta from the right ascension of the Midheaven, and the diurnal distance of the Apheta from the meridian or Midheaven will remain, which, as the author prescribes, must be divided by the temporal hours of the Apheta, and indeed diurnal, so that the temporal hours of the Apheta by which it is distant from the Midheaven emerge. Similarly, if the Apheta is below the earth, you will calculate its distance from the meridian or Imum Coeli with equal ease. For if it is in the quadrant of the descending half, that is, if it is before the meridian below the earth, subtract the right ascension of the angle of the earth from the right ascension of the Apheta. But if it is in the quadrant of the ascending half, that is, if it has passed the angle of the earth, then subtract the right ascension of the Apheta from the right ascension of the angle, and its nocturnal distance from the meridian will remain, which must be divided by the nocturnal temporal hours of the significator, so that the number of the section shows the elongation of the Apheta from the angle of the earth in temporal hours. To the same end, and perhaps more easily, you will arrive if you operate through opposite points. For the distance of a place from the Imum Coeli is equal to the distance by which the point diametrically opposite the said place...

translation: Let the apheta be constituted in the 9th part, and the square rays of Saturn, to which it is to be directed, be in the 6th part. The angle of the Midheaven is 15 degrees. The right ascension of the apheta or Sun is 311 degrees, 27 minutes. The right ascension of the Midheaven is 279 degrees, 48 minutes, which, subtracted from the right ascension of the apheta, leaves its distance from the Midheaven as 31 degrees, 39 minutes, which was to be sought first. Then I want to inquire into the hourly times of the apheta or Sun traversing the 9th part of Aquarius, and I see in the table of the quantity of days under the altitude of the pole of 51 degrees, directly opposite the 9th part, 7 hours, 35 minutes, which I multiply by 24, making the hourly times 18 hours, 58 minutes, and indeed nocturnal, because it is from the southern signs. But because we need diurnal hourly times, I subtracted these nocturnal times from 30 parts, and immediately the diurnal times of 11 degrees, 2 minutes appeared, by which I divided the distance of the apheta from the Midheaven, that is, 31 degrees, 39 minutes, and I extracted 2 temporal hours, 52 minutes, by which the apheta is distant from the Midheaven. Now, in order to have the significator of the direct circle, from the right ascension of the rays of Saturn, namely, striking the sixth part of Pisces, which is 337 degrees, 48 minutes, I subtracted the right ascension of the apheta, which is 311 degrees, 27 minutes, and there remained the significator of the direct circle, 28 degrees, 21 minutes. For the significator of the oblique circle, from the oblique ascension of the rays of Saturn, which is 349 degrees, 30 minutes, I subtracted the oblique ascension of the apheta, which is 335 degrees, 10 minutes, and there remained the significator of the region, 14 degrees, 20 minutes. Therefore, the difference of the significators is 3 parts, which, divided by 6, yielded 0 parts, 30 minutes, or half of one part, to be multiplied by the temporal hours by which the significator is distant from the meridian, that is, by 2 hours, 52 minutes. This done, the hourly times of 1 degree, 26 minutes resulted as the equation, to be subtracted from the significator of the direct circle, for the reason that it is greater than the significator of the climate. This done, the sought direction remained 27 degrees, 54 minutes. This process of the author plainly depends on the rule of proportions: For that he orders the difference of the significators to be divided by 6, and the number of the section to be multiplied by the number of temporal hours by which the significator is distant from the Midheaven, is the same as if he had proposed these numbers in the rule of proportions in the usual way, thus: In six temporal hours the direction varies (to repeat the same example) by 3 parts, how much will the same direction vary in 2 temporal hours, 52 minutes, by which the apheta recedes from the meridian? Temporal hours: 6. Difference of significators: 3 parts. Temporal hours by which apheta is distant from Midheaven: 2 hours, 52 minutes. Furthermore, since in the process of the rule of proportions we not only divide the number generated from the mutual product of the second and third by the first, but also divide the second by the first, and correctly multiply the number of the section by the third, so as to arrive at the fourth and unknown, it is clear why the author orders the difference of the significators to be divided by 6, and the number of the section to be multiplied by the temporal hours by which the apheta is distant from the cardine, so that the equation of the direction may result. But you will elicit this same equation more certainly and much more expeditiously if, in the rule of proportions, you place the semi-diurnal or semi-nocturnal arc of the significator in the first place, and in the second place you place the difference of the significators. Finally, in the third place, you establish the distance of the significator from the cardine. For with the operation of the rule completed, the fourth number will show the equation to be added to or subtracted from the significator of the sphere, as the ratio of the calculation itself demands. As in the example above, from the tables of ascensions, or even, if you please, from the table of the quantity of days, you will find the semi-diurnal arc of the apheta to be 66 degrees, 4 minutes. Say then: in the space of 66 degrees, 4 minutes, the direction varies by 3 parts; how much will it vary in 31 minutes, by which the place of the apheta is removed from the apex of the sky? It makes 1 degree, 26 minutes, as above. But if the significator is between the horoscopus and the Midheaven, the operation must be performed in exactly the same way, except that in such a case, for the apheta, just as nocturnal local hours are used, so also are semi-nocturnal arcs. Therefore, I believe no example is needed here. From these, the author's opinion regarding the direction of the significator residing in the ascending hemisphere is clear. The method of directing the apheta or significator residing in the descending hemisphere, which is the arc from the angle of the earth to the Midheaven, calculated through the seventh house, follows. The canon of operation here does not differ from the former, except that, as is also clear from the text, where we used ascensions in the ascending hemisphere, here in the descending hemisphere, descensions must be used, or, which is the same, the work must be instituted precisely by the ascensions of the opposite points. For example, let the Imum Coeli be 15 degrees, 0 minutes, and the apheta or significator be in 15 degrees, constituted to be directed to the body of Mercury placed in 15 degrees, Virgo. And since the apheta is found below the earth and in the descending hemisphere, therefore, by operating through opposite points and nocturnal hourly times, we will reach the true goal. The right ascension of the apheta is 137 degrees, 29 minutes. The right ascension of the Imum Coeli is 106 degrees, 17 minutes. The distance of the apheta from the cardine is 31 degrees, 12 minutes. But the nocturnal semi-arc of the apheta (for the apheta resides below the earth) is 68 degrees, 45 minutes, and its nocturnal hourly times are 11 degrees, 28 minutes, or in total 688 minutes, by which I divided the distance of the apheta from the Imum Coeli, which is 31 degrees, 12 minutes, that is, 1872 first minutes, and I produced 2 hours, 43 minutes, by which temporal hours the apheta is removed from the cardine. From this, I sought the difference of the significators in the following way. The right descension of the promissor, that is, the right ascension of 5 degrees, is 346 degrees, 12 minutes. The right descension of the apheta, that is, the right ascension of 15 degrees, is 317 degrees, 27 minutes. The significator of the sphere is 28 degrees, 43 minutes. The oblique descension of the promissor is 353 degrees, 34 minutes. The oblique descension of the apheta is 338 degrees, 46 minutes. The significator of the climate is 14 degrees, 48 minutes, which I subtracted from the significator of the sphere, and there remained the difference of the significators, 13 degrees, 55 minutes, which I divided by 6, and I extracted the number of the section, 2 degrees, 19 minutes, which I multiplied by the hours by which the apheta is distant from the Imum Coeli, that is, by 2 hours, 43 minutes, and I produced 6 degrees, 18 minutes, which is the equation to be subtracted from the significator of the sphere exceeding the significator of the climate. This done, the sought direction remained 22 degrees, 25 minutes. The same goal is reached by operating through the nocturnal semi-arc of the apheta. Arrangement of numbers for the Rule of Proportion: 1. The semi-nocturnal arc of the apheta is 68 degrees, 45 minutes. The difference of the significators is 13 degrees, 55 minutes. Or thus: 13. The distance of the apheta from the cardine is 31 degrees, 12 minutes. 31. 684. The second number multiplied by the third, yields 4345908, divided by 684, yields the equation of 6 degrees, 18 minutes, to be subtracted, as said, from the significator of the sphere, so that the direction of 22 degrees, 25 minutes, which we proposed to find, remains. Finally, the converse direction, or in the preceding, is done in the same way by which the direct is perfected, with this one observation: that ascensions and descensions are calculated in the circle of position of the promissor and not of the significator. For just as the promissor is carried to the place of the significator, so here the significator is understood to be led to the place of the promissor. Therefore, if the promissor is placed in the Midheaven, we will subtract the right ascensions of the promissor from the right ascensions of the significator. But if the same has been the horoscopus, we will subtract the ascensions of the climate of the promissor from the ascensions of the climate of the significator. Finally, if the promissor has held the angle of the west, we will deduce its descensions from the descensions of the significator, so that the sought direction remains. What, however, should be followed if none of the cardines holds the promissor, but the same is constituted between the said places, can be clear from those canons which are set forth in the exposition of the direct direction, namely, both ascensions or descensions are to be taken, and at the same time proportionally mixed, as we have said. From these, the author's opinion regarding the deduction of Aphetic places is clear, in which, like Ptolemy, he despised

translation: Planetary latitudes, which are more observed in this matter, have accustomed to evidently vary the times of directions. Therefore, if anyone, following this author's method of directing, also wishes to consider latitudes, they must use the tables of Johannes Blanchinus, by the aid of which the ascensions of all places of the sky deviating from the Ecliptic are most easily found. The tables of directions by Regiomontanus also provide the same and more copiously, but their use is more difficult and admits only a well-practiced calculator. Blanchinus's tables were also composed in Italy. For other regions, as far as the right sphere is concerned, it satisfies all. As for the oblique sphere, everyone must construct one for themselves with the aid of Regiomontanus's tables for the altitude of their region. The author's method of directing is not very dissimilar from the tradition of Ptolemy, according to whose opinion the times of directions are collected in this manner. First and foremost, the Apheta and the remaining significators, whose progressions principally signify the times of events, must be observed. Similarly, the places of the planets or promissors, that is, the radiations of the stars occurring, must be described and noted. For the sake of avoiding obscurity, it was pleasing to use these words very generally in this place, so that whatever is said here about the deduction of the Apheta or the lord, you may also understand it concerning all other significators of events, so that by the name Apheta, we understand every significator, and by the name Anareta, every promissor. These things being so, then the distance of both the Apheta and the promissors from the midheaven must be taken, if the Apheta is above the earth; or their difference from the imum coeli must be investigated, if the Apheta is found below the horizon. After this, the horary times of individual places, diurnal if the place of the Apheta is above the earth, but nocturnal if below the earth, must be collected, and then the distance of the Apheta from the cardine, just found by the horary times of the Apheta, must be divided by the horary times, so that the temporal hours by which the Apheta is removed from the cardine may result: all of which will be accomplished according to the method abundantly explained in the exposition of the text. Finally, the horary times of the Anareta must be multiplied by the hours by which the Apheta is distant from the cardine of the sky, and what is produced, according to the following cautions, must be added to or subtracted from the difference or distance of the Anareta from the cardine of the sky. The number that results from this will be the number of parts and minutes of the direction, or it will be the number of years, months, and days, after which the promissor will come to the significator: indeed, each degree thus computed counts as a year, and each minute as a month, and each single second as six days and two hours. Rules of Prohairesis: If the Apheta is above the earth, subtract the produced number from the direct difference of the promissor (here we call the difference of the place from the cardine the distance) if the Apheta is between the ascendant and the Midheaven. If both are between the Midheaven and the seventh house, the direct difference of the promissor must be subtracted from the produced number. But if the Apheta is below the earth between the imum coeli and the seventh house, then, as in the first case, the product must be subtracted from the difference of the promissor. But if the Apheta is between the imum coeli and the horoscope, the difference of the promissor, as was done in the second case, is subtracted from the product. Finally, if the Apheta is in the eastern quadrant, and the promissor is in the western quadrant, whether above or below the earth, then the difference of the Apheta must be added to the product. From these, it is clear that the opposite quadrants of the sky correspond to each other completely. Therefore, when the Apheta is found below the earth, with opposite points, just as if the Apheta were above the earth, you will perform the operation to the same end, perhaps with less effort. And this is the reason why Cardanus, in this whole matter, teaches that no more than 4 or rather 3 differences, which follow, are found, which is true, if, as I said, the opposite points are attended to. If the Apheta is in the Midheaven, nothing is added to the difference of the ascensions, but simply, as has been said, the direction is made by the ascensions of the sphere. 2. Apheta between the Midheaven and the horoscope: the product is subtracted from the difference of the promissor. 3. Both Apheta and Anareta in the western quadrant: then the difference is subtracted from the product. Apheta in the eastern quadrant, Anareta in the western quadrant: then the difference is combined with the product. Example of a significator between the Midheaven and the horoscope. I wish to direct the Moon first to the trine radiation of Mars, and then to the body of Saturn, so that the Moon, initially inimical to Mars, has the first place or the place of the significator, and the rays of Mars and Saturn have the place of the promissor. Since the Moon is below the earth, we will therefore operate more conveniently through opposite places falling into the eastern quadrant of the world, that is, we will make the place of the Moon in 27 degrees 52 minutes of Leo, and the rays of Mars in 28 degrees 21 minutes of Virgo, and finally the place of Saturn in 26 degrees 42 minutes of Libra. And since the place of the Moon is understood to be between the horoscope and the Midheaven, I therefore first subtracted the right ascension of the Midheaven from the right ascension of the place which we have for the Moon's place, and there remained a difference of the Moon of 26 degrees 11 minutes, which I divided by the diurnal horary times of the Moon's place, which are 18 degrees 21 minutes, and the number of the section showed me 1 hour 27 minutes of the Moon's preceding distance from the Midheaven. Then I subtracted the right ascension of the Midheaven from the right ascension of the malefics, and there remained a direct distance, of Mars's trine 54 degrees 35 minutes, and of Saturn 80 degrees 52 minutes. Finally, I multiplied the diurnal horary times of the trine radiation of Mars, that is, 15 degrees 7 minutes, by the temporal hours 1 hour 27 minutes, by which the Moon's place is distant from the Midheaven, and the number produced was 21 degrees 57 minutes, which, since the Moon's place is above the earth in the eastern quadrant, I subtracted from the difference of Mars's trine radiation, that is, from 54 degrees 35 minutes, and there remained 32 degrees 38 minutes, that is, 32 years, 7 months, and 18 days: after which time the body of the Moon passes through the trine of Mars. We produced the times of direction of 59 degrees 34 minutes, by which the Moon moves to Saturn, by exactly the same form of computation. This is Ptolemy's formula for directing, which, somewhat changed and with one operation removed, we will achieve the same goal, and we will perform all directions most easily in the following way. To all Aphetas and Anaretas, first assign the difference of the right ascension from the right ascension of the Midheaven, from the table of right ascensions placed below. Then, similarly, add to these, opposite them, the horary times of each place from the table of the quantity of days: diurnal, if the Apheta is above the earth; but nocturnal, if the Apheta is below the earth. These things being thus arranged, multiply the direct difference of the Apheta or significator by the horary times of the Anareta or promissor, and then divide what is produced by the horary times of the significator, and subtract the number of the section from the direct difference of the promissor, or you will subtract this same from that, according to the fourfold.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Calculating Directional Numbers
text: DIFFERENTIA plicem illam diftinctionem fuprà expoſitam. Numerus enin qui hinc felt aumerus directionis quaite. Repetamus maioxis euidentiæ gratia exemplum proximum, in quo Lunaad Mar tis triquetrum & corpus Saturni ducitur. Primò ergo è regione horum cæli locorum appofui differentias aſcenſionum à medio cæli, & deinde quod locus ſignificatoris fupra terram verfetur, adieci ſingulis ſua tempora horaria diurna, vt in præſenti defcriptione vides. : Differentiz... : 1 Σ Par.26.fcrup.11 1. Par.54.fcrup.35 12. Horaria tempora. Par.18.fcrup.2 1. 1 • Par.80.fcrup.521. Par. 15-fcrup.71. Par. 14. fcrup.40 1. His ita poſitis multiplicaui horaria tépora triquetre radiationis Martis p.15.fc. 71, in differentia retaloci,quæ eft p.26.fc.11 1,& pduri ſexag.61, p.35.fc.45 1ª, vel ſi mauis,p.395. fcr.45 1, quz partitus ſum per horaria tépora loci (népèp.18.fc.218,& eduxi numerú ſectionis p.2r. ſc. 571. Et quia Aphetæ & Angre tæ loca inter horoscopú &medium cæli verſantur, ideo produtum hoc à differentia recta triquetrorum radiorum Martis, quzelt par.54.fcr.35 1ª ſuſtuli,& remanferit p.32.fc.38 1*,qui. bus congruunt anni 32.menſes 7.dies 18. non aliter quàm in ſu periore lupputatione. Ad eundem modum differentiam Lunarem in tempora horaria Saturni multiplicantes, produximus ſexag.6 1,p.24.fcr. 11, quæ ſecta per horaria tempora loci Lune,pepererunt p.21.fc.18.à differentia eiuſdem Saturni auferen da, vt exeat numerus directionis, nempèp.59. fcr.33. 12. Aliud exemplum. In eadem genitura, Saturni quadraturam ad Solem deducere velis, tunc horum locorum differentijs & hora rijs temporibus fic, vt prz ceptum eſt, deſcriptis, mul tiplica differentiam Solis in horaria tépora quadratę radiationis, Saturni, & Differentię ὡριαίοι χρόνοι P. 56. fcrup. 581 P.18.fcr.221 P.37. fcr.321 P.18.fcr.421 produces ſexag. 171, p.45.fc.16 18 que per horaria tépora Solis, hoceſt, perp. 18. fer.22.1ª fi partiaris, & elicies p. 58.ad vnguć, quibus differentia Saturni, quod vterq; locus inter finitorem occidentalem & culmen coli contineatur, ſubtrahenda eft, vt . relin: QUARTA 438 relinquatur numerus directionis p.20.fcrup.28.14. 4 Ex his exéplis perfpicua eſt ratio dirigédi, fi vterq; ſignifica quòd ſi tor & promiſſorin hemicyclio ad culmen cæli ſcandente repe- fuerit ſiriať: tuc em dire&io tota fit per afcenſiones'in femicircuio poſignificator tionis fignificatoris. Omnes enim circuli pofitionis in medie tate aſcendente ſunt aſcenſionum:Similiter fi vterque hemicyclium mundi ad ſubterraneam cufpidem delabente teneat, tota directio per aſcenſiones in hemicyclio Aphetæ ſeu ſignificatoris pérficitur: omnes enim femicirculi poſitionis in medietate defcendente ſunt deſcenſionum. Sed quid faciendum eſt, ſi af cenfio in defcenfionem, aut deſcenſio in aſcenſionem commu tatur? Veluti ſi Apheta inter ſeptimam & medium cæli verſans, ad promiſſoré inter horoſcopum & medium cæli herentem de ducendus ſit? Refpondeo ex ſententia Ptolemei & autoris, in tali caſu primò medium cæli ad Aphetam per deſcenſiones circuli poſitionis Aphetę effe ducendum:deinde verò promiſſore ad culmen medij cœli per aſcenſiones Sphere recte promouendum:& denique duos hos arcus dire&ionis iungendos, vt nu merus totius directionis prodeat. In aſſumpta genitura, Solem in quadrante medietatis mundi deſcendentis pofitum, ad qua dratam radiatione Martis, que in p.28.fc.21 1ª Leonis, & in qua drantem mundi medietatis aſcendentis incidit, dirigentes,primò vtriusque loci tam à medio cæli differentiam, quam hora Differentie ωριαίοι χρόνοι P.36. feru. 181 P.18.fcr.22 14 P.26.fcr.361 P.16.fcr.2018 ! riatempora defcripfimus fic, vt à latere vides. Deinde Differentiam Solis in horaria tempora quadrature Martis multiplicates, pro duximus fexag. 1 519, p.58.fcr.5612, que ſecta per horaria tempora Solis, prodiderunt quadraturę Martis in locum Solis traductę a medio cœli diftantiam part. 52. fcr.121, que quando cũ eiufdem quadraturæ Martis diſftantia prima, quæ eft p.26.fc.36 coniungitur, facit numerum directionis par.78.fcr.481. 2 Sequitur alter huius capitis locus, de inuenienda parte figni gni Oudici feri, ad quam directio Aphetæ, in dato anno ætatis peruadit. fciueris Veluti in noſtro exemplo, in quo finiens orientalis in part.26.&c. ſcrup. 441 Libre incidit, fi inueniendum proponatur,ad quem gradum DIFFERENTIA kc gradum ſigniferi dire&io horoſcopi anno ætatis nati 40.men. ibus.s.diebus 6.peruadat, capias primò aſcenſionem obliquam fignificatoris, id eft, horoſcopantis partis 26.fcr.44 14, quæ eft partiuin 213.fcrup.55 14, atque eidem adiunge partes 40.fcr.26 1*, ratione temporis à radice elapſi, & colliges partes 254. ſcrup. 21 14, cum quibus in aream tabula obliquz ingreflus, (nam barbara vox arcuare id hic ſignificat) reperies eifdem refpondere part.28.fc.21 14 Scorpij, vbi martis eſt epocha. Ad eum igitur pun&um ſigniferi pertinget directio horoscopi, annis 40. menfibus 5. diebus.6.completis. Reliqua huius loci ſunt facilia, atque ijs quæ di&a ſunt, non diſsimilia. Et locus 6 Sequitur, quomodo Catholici temporum arbitri eligendi ad quem ſint. Videtur autem hic diuiſorem, hoceſt, dominum termini præferre domino radiorum, quod eſt contra Ptolemæum, qui præcipuum dominium ſtelle vel radio, Aphetz occurreni tribuit, eiuſq; dominis participem facit dominum termini. Verba eius de hacre extant circa finem quadripartita con- ſtructionis: καὶ πρωομ μli, inquit, δοτέομ εσύ χρόνος καθεκοίς τρ ἄφεσιμ τῷ κατ᾿ αὐτό το αφελικής μοίρας τυχόντε ή συληματιθέντις δαμδίμὴ ὅτως ἔχῃ, δ τιὼ ἔγγυςα προήγησιμ ἐπιλαβόντι, μέχρι το τω ἐξῆς εἰς τὰ ἑπόμπια μοτραμ ἐπιθεωρήσαντο, εἶτα τότῳ μέν χρι το εξῆς, καὶ επί τὴν ἄλλων ομοίως, πραλαμβανομένων εἰς τὸ οἰκοδιασοτίαμ καὶ τῇ τὰ ὅεια ἐπεχόντωμάς όρωμ, hoc eft: Acpri mum fanè tempus in ſingulis moderationibus illi dabitur, qui Apheticum locum corpore occupat vel radijs. Quòd fi nullus fuerit in loco Aphetico preciſe, aſciſcemus proxime antece dentem planetam vel radium eum vfq;,qui proxime ſuccedentem afpicit partem. Deinde hoc vtemur vfque ad proximum, atquein alijs fimiliter, aſſumentes in gubernandi communica tionem terminorum dominos planetas. Exemplari diſcurſu res fiet clarior. In noſtro exemplo orientalé horizonta circun dat pars 26.fcr.441ª Libre, cui cùm ad vnguem penè infideat radius oppoſitus Saturni, idcircò Saturnum primum temporá arbitrum conftituimus, participe imperij Marte, de ſententia Ptolemei terminorum domino. Sed paulo poſt in parte 27.fcr. 21vbi diametro Saturni ſuccedit wiquetraradiatio Louis & E ९ Lunz
translation: The difference is that distinction explained above. For the number here is the number of the fourth direction. Let us repeat, for greater clarity, the previous example, in which the Moon is led to the trine of Mars and the body of Saturn. First, therefore, opposite these celestial places, I placed the differences of ascensions from the midheaven, and then, because the place of the significator is above the earth, I added to each its diurnal hourly times, as you see in the present description: Differences: 1. Part 26, scrup. 11 1. Part 54, scrup. 35 12. Hourly times: 1. Part 18, scrup. 2 1. Part 80, scrup. 52 1. Part 15, scrup. 7 1. Part 14, scrup. 40 Having thus set these, I multiplied the hourly times of the trine radiation of Mars, p.15.sc.7, by the difference of the rectified place, which is p.26.sc.11, and produced sixty 61, p.35.sc.45, or if you prefer, p.395.sc.45, which I divided by the hourly times of the place (namely p.18.sc.2), and extracted the section number p.21.sc.57. And because the places of Apheta and Angreta are between the ascendant and the midheaven, therefore I subtracted this product from the direct difference of the trine rays of Mars, which is p.54.sc.35, and there remained p.32.sc.38, to which correspond 32 years, 7 months, 18 days, no differently than in the above calculation. In the same way, multiplying the lunar difference by the hourly times of Saturn, we produced sixty 61, p.24.sc.11, which, divided by the hourly times of the Moon's place, yielded p.21.sc.18, to be subtracted from the difference of the same Saturn, so that the number of direction, namely p.59.sc.33, comes out. Another example: In the same nativity, if you wish to deduce the square of Saturn to the Sun, then, having described the differences of these places and their hourly times as instructed, multiply the difference of the Sun by the hourly times of the square radiation of Saturn, and the Differences of the hourly times: P. 56. scrup. 58 P.18.sc.22 P.37.sc.32 P.18.sc.42 produces sixty 171, p.45.sc.16, which, if you divide by the hourly times of the Sun, that is, by p.18.sc.22, you will elicit p.58 exactly, from which the difference of Saturn must be subtracted, because both places are contained between the western horizon and the culmination of the sky, so that the number of direction p.20.scrup.28 remains.

translation: From these examples, the method of directing is clear, if both the significator and the promissor are found in the hemicycle ascending to the culmination of the sky: then the entire direction is made by ascensions in the semicircle of the significator's position. For all circles of position in the ascending half are of ascensions. Similarly, if both hold the hemicycle of the world descending to the subterranean cusp, the entire direction is completed by ascensions in the hemicycle of Apheta or the significator: for all semicircles of position in the descending half are of descensions. But what is to be done if ascension is changed into descension, or descension into ascension? As if Apheta, being between the seventh house and the midheaven, is to be led to the promissor, which is between the ascendant and the midheaven? I answer, according to the opinion of Ptolemy and the author, that in such a case, first the midheaven must be led to Apheta by the descensions of Apheta's circle of position: then the promissor must be moved to the culmination of the midheaven by the ascensions of the right sphere: and finally, these two arcs of direction must be joined, so that the number of the entire direction comes forth. In the assumed nativity, directing the Sun, placed in the quadrant of the descending half of the world, to the square radiation of Mars, which falls in p.28.sc.21 of Leo, and in the quadrant of the ascending half of the world, we first described both the difference of each place from the midheaven and the hourly times, as you see on the side. Then, multiplying the difference of the Sun by the hourly times of the square of Mars, we produced sixty 1519, p.58.sc.56, which, divided by the hourly times of the Sun, yielded the distance of the square of Mars translated to the Sun's place from the midheaven, part 52.sc.12, which, when joined with the first distance of the same square of Mars, which is p.26.sc.36, makes the number of direction part 78.sc.48.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Finding the Degree of the Zodiac
text: DIFFERENTIA plicem illam diftinctionem fuprà expoſitam. Numerus enin qui hinc felt aumerus directionis quaite. Repetamus maioxis euidentiæ gratia exemplum proximum, in quo Lunaad Mar tis triquetrum & corpus Saturni ducitur. Primò ergo è regione horum cæli locorum appofui differentias aſcenſionum à medio cæli, & deinde quod locus ſignificatoris fupra terram verfetur, adieci ſingulis ſua tempora horaria diurna, vt in præſenti defcriptione vides. : Differentiz... : 1 Σ Par.26.fcrup.11 1. Par.54.fcrup.35 12. Horaria tempora. Par.18.fcrup.2 1. 1 • Par.80.fcrup.521. Par. 15-fcrup.71. Par. 14. fcrup.40 1. His ita poſitis multiplicaui horaria tépora triquetre radiationis Martis p.15.fc. 71, in differentia retaloci,quæ eft p.26.fc.11 1,& pduri ſexag.61, p.35.fc.45 1ª, vel ſi mauis,p.395. fcr.45 1, quz partitus ſum per horaria tépora loci (népèp.18.fc.218,& eduxi numerú ſectionis p.2r. ſc. 571. Et quia Aphetæ & Angre tæ loca inter horoscopú &medium cæli verſantur, ideo produtum hoc à differentia recta triquetrorum radiorum Martis, quzelt par.54.fcr.35 1ª ſuſtuli,& remanferit p.32.fc.38 1*,qui. bus congruunt anni 32.menſes 7.dies 18. non aliter quàm in ſu periore lupputatione. Ad eundem modum differentiam Lunarem in tempora horaria Saturni multiplicantes, produximus ſexag.6 1,p.24.fcr. 11, quæ ſecta per horaria tempora loci Lune,pepererunt p.21.fc.18.à differentia eiuſdem Saturni auferen da, vt exeat numerus directionis, nempèp.59. fcr.33. 12. Aliud exemplum. In eadem genitura, Saturni quadraturam ad Solem deducere velis, tunc horum locorum differentijs & hora rijs temporibus fic, vt prz ceptum eſt, deſcriptis, mul tiplica differentiam Solis in horaria tépora quadratę radiationis, Saturni, & Differentię ὡριαίοι χρόνοι P. 56. fcrup. 581 P.18.fcr.221 P.37. fcr.321 P.18.fcr.421 produces ſexag. 171, p.45.fc.16 18 que per horaria tépora Solis, hoceſt, perp. 18. fer.22.1ª fi partiaris, & elicies p. 58.ad vnguć, quibus differentia Saturni, quod vterq; locus inter finitorem occidentalem & culmen coli contineatur, ſubtrahenda eft, vt . relin: QUARTA 438 relinquatur numerus directionis p.20.fcrup.28.14. 4 Ex his exéplis perfpicua eſt ratio dirigédi, fi vterq; ſignifica quòd ſi tor & promiſſorin hemicyclio ad culmen cæli ſcandente repe- fuerit ſiriať: tuc em dire&io tota fit per afcenſiones'in femicircuio poſignificator tionis fignificatoris. Omnes enim circuli pofitionis in medie tate aſcendente ſunt aſcenſionum:Similiter fi vterque hemicyclium mundi ad ſubterraneam cufpidem delabente teneat, tota directio per aſcenſiones in hemicyclio Aphetæ ſeu ſignificatoris pérficitur: omnes enim femicirculi poſitionis in medietate defcendente ſunt deſcenſionum. Sed quid faciendum eſt, ſi af cenfio in defcenfionem, aut deſcenſio in aſcenſionem commu tatur? Veluti ſi Apheta inter ſeptimam & medium cæli verſans, ad promiſſoré inter horoſcopum & medium cæli herentem de ducendus ſit? Refpondeo ex ſententia Ptolemei & autoris, in tali caſu primò medium cæli ad Aphetam per deſcenſiones circuli poſitionis Aphetę effe ducendum:deinde verò promiſſore ad culmen medij cœli per aſcenſiones Sphere recte promouendum:& denique duos hos arcus dire&ionis iungendos, vt nu merus totius directionis prodeat. In aſſumpta genitura, Solem in quadrante medietatis mundi deſcendentis pofitum, ad qua dratam radiatione Martis, que in p.28.fc.21 1ª Leonis, & in qua drantem mundi medietatis aſcendentis incidit, dirigentes,primò vtriusque loci tam à medio cæli differentiam, quam hora Differentie ωριαίοι χρόνοι P.36. feru. 181 P.18.fcr.22 14 P.26.fcr.361 P.16.fcr.2018 ! riatempora defcripfimus fic, vt à latere vides. Deinde Differentiam Solis in horaria tempora quadrature Martis multiplicates, pro duximus fexag. 1 519, p.58.fcr.5612, que ſecta per horaria tempora Solis, prodiderunt quadraturę Martis in locum Solis traductę a medio cœli diftantiam part. 52. fcr.121, que quando cũ eiufdem quadraturæ Martis diſftantia prima, quæ eft p.26.fc.36 coniungitur, facit numerum directionis par.78.fcr.481. 2 Sequitur alter huius capitis locus, de inuenienda parte figni gni Oudici feri, ad quam directio Aphetæ, in dato anno ætatis peruadit. fciueris Veluti in noſtro exemplo, in quo finiens orientalis in part.26.&c. ſcrup. 441 Libre incidit, fi inueniendum proponatur,ad quem gradum DIFFERENTIA kc gradum ſigniferi dire&io horoſcopi anno ætatis nati 40.men. ibus.s.diebus 6.peruadat, capias primò aſcenſionem obliquam fignificatoris, id eft, horoſcopantis partis 26.fcr.44 14, quæ eft partiuin 213.fcrup.55 14, atque eidem adiunge partes 40.fcr.26 1*, ratione temporis à radice elapſi, & colliges partes 254. ſcrup. 21 14, cum quibus in aream tabula obliquz ingreflus, (nam barbara vox arcuare id hic ſignificat) reperies eifdem refpondere part.28.fc.21 14 Scorpij, vbi martis eſt epocha. Ad eum igitur pun&um ſigniferi pertinget directio horoscopi, annis 40. menfibus 5. diebus.6.completis. Reliqua huius loci ſunt facilia, atque ijs quæ di&a ſunt, non diſsimilia. Et locus 6 Sequitur, quomodo Catholici temporum arbitri eligendi ad quem ſint. Videtur autem hic diuiſorem, hoceſt, dominum termini præferre domino radiorum, quod eſt contra Ptolemæum, qui præcipuum dominium ſtelle vel radio, Aphetz occurreni tribuit, eiuſq; dominis participem facit dominum termini. Verba eius de hacre extant circa finem quadripartita con- ſtructionis: καὶ πρωομ μli, inquit, δοτέομ εσύ χρόνος καθεκοίς τρ ἄφεσιμ τῷ κατ᾿ αὐτό το αφελικής μοίρας τυχόντε ή συληματιθέντις δαμδίμὴ ὅτως ἔχῃ, δ τιὼ ἔγγυςα προήγησιμ ἐπιλαβόντι, μέχρι το τω ἐξῆς εἰς τὰ ἑπόμπια μοτραμ ἐπιθεωρήσαντο, εἶτα τότῳ μέν χρι το εξῆς, καὶ επί τὴν ἄλλων ομοίως, πραλαμβανομένων εἰς τὸ οἰκοδιασοτίαμ καὶ τῇ τὰ ὅεια ἐπεχόντωμάς όρωμ, hoc eft: Acpri mum fanè tempus in ſingulis moderationibus illi dabitur, qui Apheticum locum corpore occupat vel radijs. Quòd fi nullus fuerit in loco Aphetico preciſe, aſciſcemus proxime antece dentem planetam vel radium eum vfq;,qui proxime ſuccedentem afpicit partem. Deinde hoc vtemur vfque ad proximum, atquein alijs fimiliter, aſſumentes in gubernandi communica tionem terminorum dominos planetas. Exemplari diſcurſu res fiet clarior. In noſtro exemplo orientalé horizonta circun dat pars 26.fcr.441ª Libre, cui cùm ad vnguem penè infideat radius oppoſitus Saturni, idcircò Saturnum primum temporá arbitrum conftituimus, participe imperij Marte, de ſententia Ptolemei terminorum domino. Sed paulo poſt in parte 27.fcr. 21vbi diametro Saturni ſuccedit wiquetraradiatio Louis & E ९ Lunz
translation: The other place of this chapter follows, concerning finding the degree of the zodiac to which the direction of Apheta reaches in a given year of age. As in our example, where the eastern horizon falls in part 26.scrup. 44 of Libra, if it is proposed to find to what degree of the zodiac the direction of the ascendant reaches in the 40th year, 5 months, 6 days of the native's age, first take the oblique ascension of the significator, that is, of the ascendant part 26.sc.44, which is 213 parts, 55 scrup., and to this add 40 parts, 26 scrup., according to the elapsed time from the root, and you will collect 254 parts, 21 scrup., with which, entering the area of the oblique table (for the barbaric word 'arcuare' signifies this here), you will find that 28 parts, 21 scrup. of Scorpio correspond to them, where the epoch of Mars is. Therefore, the direction of the ascendant will reach that point of the zodiac when 40 years, 5 months, 6 days are completed. The rest of this place is easy, and not dissimilar to what has been said.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Choosing Rulers of Time
text: DIFFERENTIA plicem illam diftinctionem fuprà expoſitam. Numerus enin qui hinc felt aumerus directionis quaite. translation: It follows how the Catholic arbiters of times are to be chosen. It seems, however, that here the divider, that is, the lord of the term, is preferred to the lord of the rays, which is contrary to Ptolemy, who attributes the principal dominion to the star or ray meeting Apheta, and makes the lord of the term a participant in its dominion. His words on this matter are found near the end of the Quadripartite construction: 'And first, indeed, time in each moderation will be given to him who occupies the Aphetic place by body or by rays. But if there is no one precisely in the Aphetic place, we will take the planet or ray immediately preceding it, until it looks at the next succeeding part. Then we will use this until the next, and similarly in others, taking into communication for governing the planetary lords of the terms.' The matter will be clearer with an exemplary discussion. In our example, the eastern horizon encompasses part 26.sc.44 of Libra, to which, since the opposite ray of Saturn almost exactly rests, therefore we constitute Saturn as the first arbiter of times, with Mars participating in the rule, according to Ptolemy's opinion, as the lord of the terms. But a little later, in part 27.sc.21, where the trine radiation of Jupiter and the Moon succeeds the diameter of Saturn.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Algebugthar: Direction of the Ascendant
text: 438 DIFFERENTIA De Algebugthar. Et ex hoc ſequitur Algebugthar in natiuitatibus: est directio gradus afcendentis, hoc eft, vt afpicias gra dum afcendentis in termino cuius fit planeta, minuas aſcenſiones afcendentis de afcenfionibus, quæ funt in die recto finis termini planete per afcenfiones regionis, & quod remaferit, accipies vnicuiq; gradui vnum annum, & omnibus quinque minutis menſem : & omni minuto VI. dies:& erit dominus termini dispoſitor annorũ, qui dicitur diuiſor. Poſt hoc accipies gradus termini,qui eu fuccedit, vertes eos etiam in gradus afcenfionum, & quod fuerit, accipies vnicuique gradui annum vnum, & omnibus V. minutis menfem, & omni minuto ſex dies, eritq; dominus termini diuiſor & diſpoſitor illorum an norum. Similiter intrabis cum omni termino domini per fucceßionem vsque in finem vite: & ſi fuerit in termino alicuius planete:planeta aut radij eius erut participes in diuifione: hac diuifio dicitur Algebugthar. De directioneaſcendentis ad planetarum fines, quod Arabes vocant Algebuthar. Hic perpende gradum horoſcopátem, & obferua,ad cuius planetæ fines referatur, & quouſque ſeſe fines iſtius planetæ extendant. Deinde & gradus afcendentis & extremæ partis finiŭ aſcenſione climatis reperta, ſubtrahe prio rema poſteriore,& remanebit quæfita directio horoſcopi ad terminum propoſitu, Verbi gratia, in aſſumpta genitura, in qua eleuationem verticis ſupponimus part. 41. horoscopus eſt par.26.fcrup.44 1* Libra, cui iuxta ordinationem finium Ptolemzi Mars præſidet. Idem Mars habet imperiŭ finium in principio Scorpij,atq; id ad finem ſextæ partis eiuſdem Scorpij exrendit. Afcenſio climatis horoscopi eſt part. 213. fcrupu.55 1. Afcenſio climatis in cadem eleuatione extreme partis finium MarDigitized by Google i QUARTA. 439 Martis,hoceft,par.6.Scorpij eſt part.225.fcrup.46 14,à qua ſub lata aſcenſione obliqua horoscopi, remanent part.11.fcrupu. 5114,hoc eft,anni 11.menfes 10.dies 6. cuius temporis Mars eſt dominus.Immediatè ſequentes fines Ioui aſsignati, ad part. 14, Scorpij porriguntur, quorum aſcenſio obliqua reperitur part. 236.fcrup.3 1,à quibus fi auferas aſcenſionem extreme partis proximè præcedentium finium Martis, reſtabunt par. 10.fcrup. 117.hoc eft, anni 6. méſes 3.dies 12.tempus gubernationis Iouię, quod cum tempore paulò antèMarti aſsignato cõiunctum, efficit ætatis nati annos 32.menſem 1.dies 18. Hocmodo ad finem vitæ aſcendentis directio ad preuidendas infirmitates at que impedimenta nato in corpore obuentura cótinuanda eſt. Poteris autem tibi ſi velis formare tabellam, ex qua progreſsio afcendentis ad planetarum fines ſingulis annis facilimè depro mi queat, hoc modo: Pro principio primi anni natiuitatis aſsigna in capite tabellę gradum horoſcopantem. Poſted capias horoſcopi in climate aſcenſionem, cui pro ſingulis annis ſequentibus ordine fingulos gradus adijcias, & quod hinc fit, in area eiuſdem tabule aſcenſionum perquire, accui ſigniferi loco re- ſpondeat, obferua:is enim eo anno ius diuifionis ſeu diſpoſitio nis ſibi vendicabit. In noſtro exemplo primo anno currenti af ſignanda eſt pars 26.fcrup.44 1 Libra, cuius aſcenſioni climatis, hoc eft, part.213.fcrup.55 13,fi&quino&ialis vnum gradum adijcias, afcenfionem obliquam par.214.fcrup.55 1,pro ſecundo anno conflabis, cui reſpondent in tabula partes 27. fcr.31 18 Libra. Tali continua æquinoctialis partium ad aſcenſioné horoſcopi adiectione,yniuerfum ordinem partium pro fingulis annis nati tabelle inferendarum,præfidio tabulæ tui climatis vſque ad finem vitæ nati facilè extendes. Ad extremum partibus ſigniferi hoc pacto repertis, domini finiŭ, qui diuiſoris ap pellationem ſuſtinent, ex tabella terminorum Ptolemæi, vel & placet, Aegyptiorum adfcribendi funt, ficut in ſequenti exemplo patet. Hæc autem de horofcopo præcepta ſimili ratione ad reliquorum fignificatorum in ſuis circulis pofitionum deduAionem poffunt & debent referri. Diuifor Anni ætatis Partes diuifionis grad.fc. Σ. Et ex hoc duodenarie planetarum aut domorum: hoc eft, vt afpicias quantum ambulauerit planeta in fi. gno fuo in quocunque fuerit, aut grad domus quem volue ris ex gradibus & minutis: multiplicabisque hoc in 12. post hoc addes defuper ipfos grad.atque minuta, que mul tiplicafti in 12. & quod collectum fuerit, ex co proijce ab initio Digitized by Google QUARTA. ح initio grad.eiufdem figni: dans vnicuique ſigno XXX. grad. & vbi fuerit finitus numerus, ibi erit duodenaria planetarum ac domorum. De nouenariis eorundem. 2. Et Etexhoc Anaubarach, quæ funt nouenaria. Cus ius ſcientia eſt, vt ſcias quantum ambulaverit planeta in ſigno ſuo ex gradibus & minutis, aut grad. domus cuias volueris ſcire nouenarium. Poſt hoc diuide ſignum in nos nem diuifiones, & vnaquæque diuifio eſt ex tribus gradi bus & tertia parte vnius gradus. Poſt hoc aſpicies in quo nouenario ex ex nouer nouenarys ceciderit gradus planetie ani. domus. Post hoc dabis primum nonenarium ex fignis domino ſigni mobilis eiuſdem triplicitatis, & ſecundu nouenarium domino ſigni, cuius eſt ipſum ſignum quod fuccedit: donec venias ad aliquem nonerarium,ex quo est gradus, eritque planeta dominas eius novenary. Verbigratia: fignum ex quo fuerit gradus ille, erat ex triplicitate Arietis: erit ergo primum novenarium eiufdem figni Martis domini Arietis, & ſecundum nouenarium Veneris domina Tauri, & nouenarium tertium Mercurij domini Geminorum. Similiter vf. que ad nouenarium nonum. Et ſi fuerit ſignum ex tri plicitate Cancri, erit primum nouenarium eius Luna, fecundum nouenarium Solis domini Leonis: &ter. tium Mercuriý domini Virginis. Et ſi fuerit ſignum ex triplicitate Libre,erit primum nouenarium eius Vene ris domini Libre, & ſecundum Martis domini Scorpio nis, & tertium Ionis domini Sagittariý. Similiter in tri k plicitaDigitized by Google 44 DIFFERENTIA plicitate Capricorn, primum nouenarium eius, eſt Satur ni domini Capricorni:15 ſecundum fimiliter Saturni do mini Aquarij,& tertium Iouis domini Piscium, deinde que ſequitur ſecundum ordinem ſucceßionis ſignorum. Verbi gratia:planeta vel aliqua domus ex domibus XII. erat in XIX.gradu Aquarij, ſi diuidatur ſignum per no uem diuifiones, erit gradus XIX. in diuifione ſexta,& quia ſignú Aquarij eſt in triplicitate Libre:ponitur pri mum nouenarium eius Veneris domine Libra: ſecudum Martis domini Scorpionis, & tertium Iouis domini Sagittariy. quartu Saturni domini Capricorni, & quin tum etiam Saturni domini Aquarij, &ſextum Iouis do mini Piſcium, eritque Naubahar, id eſt, novenarium XIX.grad. Aquary Iouis. Modus inueniendi dominum decani. 3 Et ex hoc Adorogen, hoc eſt, vt diuidas afcendens in tres partes, & fit omnis diuifio X. graduum:dabisque di uiſionem primam domino afcendentis, & ſecundam do mino quinti ſigni ab eo: & tertium domino noni:nam a- [cendes V. IX.vna ſemper funt triplicitas.Verbi gra tia:ab initio Arietis vfque in in decimum decimum gradum gradum eius, eius, est e Dorogen, id eſt, decanatus decanatus Martis. Martis. Et Et ſi ſi fuerit fuerit ex X. gradu vſque in XX.gradu,erit eius Dorogen Sol domi nus Leonis. Et fi fuerit à XX.vſque in finem eius, erit Dorogen Iupiter dominus Sagittarý. 4 Et ex hoc dominus orbis in natiuitatibus, hoc eſt, vt afpicias dominum hora in qua oritur natus, dabisque afcendens & dominu hora anno primo natiuitatis eius, & ſigni Digitized by Google
translation: 438. DIFFERENCE. Concerning Algebugthar. And from this follows Algebugthar in nativities: it is the direction of the ascendant degree, that is, that you look at the degree of the ascendant in the term of which planet it is, you subtract the ascensions of the ascendant from the ascensions which are in the direct day of the end of the planet's term by the ascensions of the region, and what remains, you will take for each degree one year, and for every five minutes a month: and for every minute six days: and the lord of the term will be the disposer of the years, who is called the divider. After this, you will take the degrees of the term which succeeds it, you will also convert them into degrees of ascensions, and whatever it is, you will take for each degree one year, and for every five minutes a month, and for every minute six days, and the lord of the term will be the divider and disposer of those years. Similarly, you will proceed with every term of the lord by succession until the end of life: and if it is in the term of any planet: the planet or its rays will be participants in the division: this division is called Algebugthar. Concerning the direction of the ascendant to the planetary terms, which the Arabs call Algebuthar. Here consider the horoscoping degree, and observe to which planet's terms it refers, and how far the terms of that planet extend. Then, having found the ascensions of the climate for both the ascendant degree and the extreme part of the terms, subtract the former from the latter, and the sought direction of the horoscope to the proposed term will remain. For example, in the assumed nativity, in which we suppose the elevation of the vertex to be 41 parts, the horoscope is 26 parts, 44 minutes of Libra, which, according to Ptolemy's arrangement of terms, Mars presides over. The same Mars has dominion over the terms at the beginning of Scorpio, and extends to the end of the sixth part of the same Scorpio. The ascension of the climate of the horoscope is 213 parts, 55 minutes. The ascension of the climate at the same elevation of the extreme part of Mars's terms, 439. that is, 6 parts of Scorpio, is 225 parts, 46 minutes, from which, if the oblique ascension of the horoscope is subtracted, there remain 11 parts, 51 minutes, that is, 11 years, 10 months, 6 days, of which time Mars is the lord. The immediately succeeding terms assigned to Jupiter extend to 14 parts of Scorpio, whose oblique ascension is found to be 236 parts, 3 minutes, from which if you subtract the ascension of the extreme part of the immediately preceding terms of Mars, there will remain 10 parts, 11 minutes, that is, 6 years, 3 months, 12 days, the time of Jupiter's governance, which, combined with the time assigned to Mars a little earlier, makes the native's age 32 years, 1 month, 18 days. In this way, the direction of the ascendant to the end of life is to be continued to foresee illnesses and impediments that will occur to the native in the body. You can, if you wish, form a table from which the progression of the ascendant to the planetary terms can be most easily derived for each year, in this way: For the beginning of the first year of the nativity, assign the horoscoping degree at the head of the table. Then take the ascension of the horoscope in the climate, to which for each succeeding year you add single degrees in order, and what results from this, search for it in the area of the same table of ascensions, and observe to which place of the zodiac it corresponds: for that will claim the right of division or disposition for itself in that year. In our example, for the first current year, 26 parts, 44 minutes of Libra are to be assigned, to whose climate ascension, that is, 213 parts, 55 minutes, if you add one degree of the equinoctial, you will form the oblique ascension of 214 parts, 55 minutes for the second year, to which correspond in the table 27 parts, 31 minutes of Libra. By such a continuous addition of equinoctial parts to the ascension of the horoscope, you will easily extend the entire order of parts to be inserted into the table for each year of the native's life, with the help of your climate table, until the end of the native's life. Finally, to the parts of the zodiac found in this way, the lords of the terms, who bear the appellation of divider, are to be ascribed from Ptolemy's table of terms, or, if you prefer, from the Egyptians, as is evident in the following example. These precepts concerning the horoscope can and should be referred in a similar way to the deduction of the positions of the other significators in their circles. 440. Table of division or direction of the horoscope, which the Arabs call Algebuthar. Years of age, Parts of division, deg. min., Divider. Years of age, Parts of division, deg. min., Divider. Years of age, Parts of division, deg. min., Divider. 8 126 44 07/15/7 401 129 18 34 2 27 31 168 27 3019 21 13 28 18 179 21 31/20. 71 1429 6 18/10 01 530m 19/10 47 32/20 48211 33/21 41 60 40% 2011 341 34/22 29 17 261 21/12 28 135/23 161 82 13 22/13 7 3624 3 19 13 12313 47 137/24 461 10/3 47 124/14 41 12/38/25 401 114 344 62515 27 3926 26 12/5 20 2616 15 140/27 141271 13/6 15 2717 01 4128 147 8 28 17 47 42 28 46. Concerning the Duodenaries of Planets and Houses. 1. And from this the duodenaries of planets or houses: that is, that you look at how much the planet has traveled in its sign, in whatever it may be, or the degree of the house you wish, in degrees and minutes: and you will multiply this by 12. After this, you will add to it those degrees and minutes which you multiplied by 12. And what is collected, from it you will cast off from the beginning of the degrees of the same sign: giving to each sign 30 degrees: and where the number is finished, there will be the duodenary of the planets and houses. Concerning their Novenaries. 2. And from this Anaubarach, which are the novenaries. The knowledge of which is, that you know how much the planet has traveled in its sign in degrees and minutes, or the degree of the house whose novenary you wish to know. After this, divide the sign into nine divisions, and each division is of three degrees and a third part of one degree. After this, you will look in which novenary out of the nine novenaries the degree of the planet or house falls. After this, you will give the first novenary from the signs to the lord of the movable sign of the same triplicity, and the second novenary to the lord of the sign whose sign it is that succeeds: until you come to some novenary, from which the degree is, and the planet will be the lord of its novenary. For example: if the sign from which that degree was, was from the triplicity of Aries: then the first novenary of the same sign will be Mars, lord of Aries, and the second novenary will be Venus, lady of Taurus, and the third novenary will be Mercury, lord of Gemini. Similarly up to the ninth novenary. And if the sign is from the triplicity of Cancer, its first novenary will be the Moon, the second novenary will be the Sun, lord of Leo: and the third will be Mercury, lord of Virgo. And if the sign is from the triplicity of Libra, its first novenary will be Venus, lady of Libra, and the second will be Mars, lord of Scorpio, and the third will be Jupiter, lord of Sagittarius. Similarly in the triplicity of Capricorn, its first novenary is Saturn, lord of Capricorn: and the second similarly Saturn, lord of Aquarius, and the third Jupiter, lord of Pisces, then what follows according to the order of succession of the signs. For example: a planet or some house out of the twelve houses was in the 19th degree of Aquarius, if the sign is divided into nine divisions, the 19th degree will be in the sixth division, and because the sign of Aquarius is in the triplicity of Libra: its first novenary is placed as Venus, lady of Libra: the second Mars, lord of Scorpio, and the third Jupiter, lord of Sagittarius. The fourth Saturn, lord of Capricorn, and the fifth also Saturn, lord of Aquarius, and the sixth Jupiter, lord of Pisces, and Naubahar, that is, the novenary of the 19th degree of Aquarius, will be Jupiter. Method of finding the lord of the decan. 3. And from this Adorogen, that is, that you divide the ascendant into three parts, and each division is of 10 degrees: and you will give the first division to the lord of the ascendant, and the second to the lord of the fifth sign from it: and the third to the lord of the ninth: for the ascendant, 5th, and 9th are always one triplicity. For example: from the beginning of Aries up to the tenth degree of it, is Adorogen, that is, the decanate of Mars. And if it is from the 10th degree up to the 20th degree, its Adorogen will be the Sun, lord of Leo. And if it is from the 20th up to its end, its Adorogen will be Jupiter, lord of Sagittarius. 4. And from this the lord of the orb in nativities, that is, that you look at the lord of the hour in which the native is born, and you will give the ascendant and the lord of the hour in the first year of his nativity, and of the sign.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Rulerships and Dignities

Venerem confortem Imperij patietur: & in tertia parte ſeprena Sol dimiſſa Venere Merctriem collegam, & fic deinceps alios ſequentes aſſumet, quemadmodum id videre eſt in tabulis, tum in Ephemeridum, sum verò paſsim in libris Arubum deſcriptis. Porrò hæc diſtinGio Arabum confita eſſe videtur ad ſimilitudinem prima tem porum diftinctionis Ptolemaica, quarum quatuor extant circa Anem quadripartita conſtructionis. I Et ex hoc Almanar, id est, ſupereminentia, qua dici tur, quòd planeta vadit fuper planetem: hoc eft, vt afpi cias curfum planete medium & locum eius æquatum: fi fuerit locus eius aquatus minus medio curfu eius, erit afcendens à medio circuli fui,vfque ad fummitatem circuli: & fi fuerit locus eius equatus plus medio curfuerit defcendens à medio circuli ſui vfque ad infimum eius. 2. Et fifuerit locus eius aquatus equalis medio curſai, erit in medio circuli fui. Deinde locum planeta minues de medio curfu fuo. 3 Poftquam hoc certißimè cognoueris, id eft, locum Poftquam hoc aquatum:velè conuerfo minorem de maiori corum ſubtrahendo:& multiplicabis quod remanferit per ſeptem, diuides per XXII. & quod de diuiſione exierit, es rit boe omni planeta, pro quantitate eins afcenfionis, vel defcenfionis. De Venere & Mercurio accipimus, quod est inter vtraq loca, id eft, quod est inter locum So lis & ==Start of OCR for page 2== i QVARTA 449 lis & Veneris & Mercury, & facimus vt fupra: quia per hoc fcimus quis corum fortior fit in circulo augis:fed Venus & Mercurius cùm fuerit aliquis corum orientas lis, & fuerit locus eius aquatus minus loco Solis : accipies refiduum, quod est inter locum Solis, Glocum eius: faciesa cum eo quemadmodum feceris, vt dictum est in planetus altioribus ex multiplicatione & diuifione. 4 Et propriorac fortior fit fignificatio planetarum dumabierint fuper feinuicem: idest, dum abierit alter fuper alterum in coniunctione: in oppofitione autem in quarto afpectu, erit fignificatio eorum minus appares i debilior. 5 Et tamen cum vnus illorum fuerit afcendens & al ter defcendens: tunc afcendens corum vadit it fup fupra deſce. dentem. Cùm verò ambo fuerint defcendentes: ille qui fuerit minoris defcenfionis, vadit fuper illum qui fuerit maioris vel pluris deſcenſionis. Si autem ambo fuerint afcendentes: ille qui est maioris aſcenſionis, vadit ſuper illum qui fuerit minoris afcenfionis. 6 Dicitur quoque & alio modo, id est, vt ſcias latitu dinem viroruma planetarum: quia ſeptentrionalis corú vadit fuper meridianum. Et fi fuerint ambo ſeptentrionales: ille qui fuerit pluris latitudinis, vadit ſuper eum, qui fuerit minoris latitudinis. DIFFFRENTΙΑ li, nullam latitudinem habens: tunc aftendens ſeptentrio nalis vadit fuper eum, quicaret latitudine:vadit ipfe qui fine latitudine eft, fuper defcendentem meridianum., 7 Et ex hoc apertio portarum dicitur, cum iungitur planeta inferior planete fuperiori, et fuerint cum boc do mus corum oppofita. Et ex hoc I De eminentia, ſuperlatione ſeu ſublimitate planetarum,que Almanar, vniuerfaliter cum reſpectu accipiendaeft. Eſt enim eminentis planetæ, quum in ſummo apice circuli breuioris, qui Graco no mine Epicyclus dicitur, planeta ipſe fertur, altero planeta partes demiſsiores tenente. Cum enim planeta in culmine ſui epicycli tenetur, videtur illic quodammodo regnare, ſedensque in folio ius dicere:alter verò qui ab Apogeo epicycli remotior eft, magisque à circuli ſummitate declinat, ad pedes illius ſedere ſu perioréq; venerari. Hancpoteſtatem graci nominant καθυπέρ- τέρησιμ, recétiores verò eleuationem, ſed ineptè planè:nam ver Bum eleuare Latinis deprimere vel deiicere fignificat,& opponitor verba extollere: Voluti eleuare res aduerfarii, eſt eas verbis minuendo deprimere, deprimendoque contemptiores reddere. At καθυπόρῶμ Græcis idem eſt quod latinis ſuperare, ſuperiorem eſſe atque extolli. Primò autem docemur hic, quaratlone alterum planetam ſuperiorem atque eminentiorem eſſe cognofcamus, idque ex tabulis motuum, ſed quodamodocon. fufe ve apparet:fiquidem curſum planete medium nominat mo tum centri epicycli verum, quæ hodie in Theoriis planetarum fic diftinguuntur. Porro tota zquatio argumenti lineaque veri motus,planetæ lineani veri motus epicycli præcedit, cùm planeta à ſummo apice epitycliad imum fertur, ſequiturque eandem cùm in altera medietate ab imo epicycli ad faſtigium ciufdem contendit. translation: Thus, these rulerships are exchanged, so that it is very easy to describe all these in a table. For the dodecatemoria of the planets, the decan lords, and the 84 salia are much more easily drawn from tables, which, as to how they should be composed by anyone, since we are sufficiently clearly admonished here, their description seemed superfluous to me, especially since the tradition of such things, as we have forewarned, differs greatly from the precepts of true art. This last chapter also appears to be such, containing nothing else but a very easy precept for constructing the tables of Ferdaria, that is, the years or times by which, according to the doctrine of the Arabs, the planets generally govern and rule the life of the native in order. The full years of Ferdaria for each planet are these: 111, 412, 07, 010, 8, 513, 9, 003, 2. The beginning of the rulership, however, is from the luminaries. For in a diurnal nativity, the Sun will principally govern for 10 years, to whom the next planet in order succeeds in rulership, that is, Venus, and she presides from the tenth to the eighteenth year of the native: after which Venus succeeds in rulership for 13 years. Then Mercury for 9 years. Then Saturn for 11 years. Then Jupiter for 12 years. Then Mars for 7 years. Then the Moon for 3 years. Finally, the Ascendant for 2 years. This is the complete circuit of planetary power in a diurnal nativity, containing 75 years, after which the rulership will return to the beginning and dominion of the Sun. Furthermore, in nocturnal nativities, the nocturnal luminary will assume rulership for the first 9 years, to whom the other succeeding planets will follow in series and order, as we have said. But truly, both in diurnal and nocturnal nativities, the rulership is distributed among these planets in such a way that each one governs alone for the seventh part of its years, but in the remaining seven parts of the rulership, it admits the six immediately succeeding planets as colleagues. For example, in a diurnal nativity, the first ten years are ascribed to the rulership of the Sun, but the Sun itself will govern the native for the seventh part of these years, that is, one solar year and approximately 156 days. In the second seventh part of its years, that is, from the 116th day of the native's first year to the completion of two years and 313 days, it will allow Venus as a partner in rulership: and in the third seventh part, the Sun, having dismissed Venus, will take Mercury as a colleague, and so on for the others that follow, as can be seen in tables, both in Ephemerides and widely described in Arabic books. Furthermore, this distinction of the Arabs seems to have been established in imitation of the primary Ptolemaic distinction of times, of which four exist concerning the four-part construction of the year. And from this Almanar, that is, pre-eminence, by which it is said that a planet goes above another planet: that is, so that you may observe the mean course of the planet and its equated place: if its equated place is less than its mean course, it will be ascending from the middle of its circle to the summit of the circle: and if its equated place is more than its mean course, it will be descending from the middle of its circle to its lowest point. And if its equated place is equal to its mean course, it will be in the middle of its circle. Then you will subtract the planet's place from its mean course. After you have most certainly known this, that is, the equated place: or conversely, subtracting the smaller from the larger of them: and you will multiply what remains by seven, you will divide by XXII, and what comes out of the division will be for every planet, according to the quantity of its ascent or descent. For Venus and Mercury, we take what is between both places, that is, what is between the place of the Sun and Venus and Mercury, and we do as above: because by this we know which of them is stronger in the circle of the apogee: but Venus and Mercury, when one of them is oriental, and its equated place is less than the Sun's place: you will take the remainder, which is between the Sun's place and its place: you will do with it as you have done, as was said for the higher planets by multiplication and division. And the signification of the planets will be more proper and stronger when they go above each other: that is, when one goes above the other in conjunction: but in opposition and in the fourth aspect, their signification will be less apparent and weaker. And yet when one of them is ascending and the other descending: then the ascending one goes above the descending one. But when both are descending: the one with the lesser descent goes above the one with the greater or more descent. But if both are ascending: the one with the greater ascent goes above the one with the lesser ascent. It is also said in another way, that is, so that you may know the latitude of the planets: because the northern one goes above the southern one. And if both are northern: the one with the greater latitude goes above the one with the lesser latitude. But if both are southern: the one with the lesser latitude goes above the one with the greater latitude, and therefore it is said that the Moon goes above Jupiter in these two operations. Similarly, if one of the two planets is in the middle of the circle, having no latitude: then the ascending northern one goes above the one that lacks latitude: the one that is without latitude goes above the descending southern one. And from this, the opening of the gates is said, when an inferior planet joins a superior planet, and their houses are opposite. And from this, concerning the eminence, superiority, or sublimity of the planets, which is universally to be understood with respect. For a planet is eminent when it is carried at the highest point of the shorter circle, which is called an Epicycle by the Greek name, while the other planet holds lower parts. For when a planet is held at the culmination of its epicycle, it seems to reign there in a certain way, sitting on a throne and dispensing justice: but the other, which is further from the apogee of the epicycle, and declines more from the summit of the circle, seems to sit at its feet and venerate the superior one. The Greeks call this power καθυπέρ-τέρησιμ, but more recent authors call it elevation, but quite ineptly: for the word 'elevare' in Latin means to depress or cast down, and the opposite word is 'extollere': to 'elevate' the affairs of an adversary is to depress them by diminishing them with words, and by depressing them, to render them more contemptible. But καθυπόρῶμ in Greek is the same as 'superare', 'to be superior', and 'to be extolled' in Latin. First, however, we are taught here by what reason we recognize one planet to be superior and more eminent than another, and that from the tables of motions, but somewhat confusedly as it appears: if indeed it calls the mean course of the planet the true motion of the center of the epicycle, which today in planetary theories are distinguished as follows. The mean motion of a planet or epicycle is the arc of the zodiac from the beginning according to succession, up to the line of the mean motion of the planet, which, drawn from the center of the world to the zodiac, is equally distant from the line extending from the center of the apogee to the center of the epicycle. The true motion of the epicycle is the arc of the zodiac calculated from the beginning up to the line of the true motion of the epicycle, which the author here calls the mean course of the planet. Finally, the true motion of the planet is the arc of the zodiac, which, beginning from the beginning, extends up to the line of the true motion of the planet, which goes from the center of the world through the body of the planet to the zodiac. They call the mean anomaly the arc of the epicycle calculated from its mean apogee to the body of the planet. But its true and equated anomaly is the arc of the epicycle, which extends from the true apogee of the same epicycle to the body of the planet, to which the corresponding arc in the zodiac is called προσθαφαίρεσις or the equation of the anomaly, to be added to the true motion of the epicycle, if the line of the true motion of the planet precedes the line of the true motion of the epicycle: or to be subtracted, if it follows the same, so that finally the true motion of the planet is elicited. Furthermore, the entire equation of the anomaly and the line of the true motion of the planet precedes the line of the true motion of the epicycle, when the planet is carried from the highest point of the epicycle to the lowest, and follows the same when in the other half it strives from the lowest point of the epicycle to its summit. With these things known, as they can be most easily recognized by inspecting the figures of the Theories, the author's meaning is clear. If, he says, the equated place of the planet is less than its mean course, that is, if the arc of the true motion of the planet is smaller.

translation: FIRST. 149 so that the true anomaly of the Moon may emerge. For, as it is said in the Theory of the Moon, when the center of the Moon is less than six common signs, then the true argument is greater than the mean, and vice versa. 1. Equation of the first epicycle. These things being so, 2. The following excess, a smaller part of which, corresponding to the proportional anomalies of the Moon, is added to the equation of the first epicycle, so that its true equation may emerge. It is subtracted from the mean motion. To the extreme minor, if the anomaly of the Moon is equated to a semicircle, the equation of the first epicycle is added to the longitude, so that the true distance of the Moon from the mean place may emerge. For since in the upper part of the epicycle it proceeds against the succession of the signs, it happens that when the true argument is less than six common signs, the line of mean motion precedes the line of true motion, and vice versa. Furthermore, the true distance of the Moon having been explored, the simple motion from the first star of Aries must be added to it; this done, the true distance of the Moon from the first star of Aries will emerge. To this, if you finally add the true precession of the vernal equinox, the true distance of the Moon from the vernal equinox in signs and parts of signs will emerge. There follows for a given time the diagram of the lunar motion. True precession of the equinox. Simple equal motion of the Moon from elongation. Anomaly of the Moon. Elongation from duplicated. Prosthaphaeresis of duplicated added. Proportional scruples. Anomaly of the Moon equated.
