Its prosthaphaeresis added. 2 58 53 Following excess. 1 50 6 Part corresponding to proportional scruples. 0 1 43 2 Absolute prosthaphaeresis of the first epicycle added. 0. 4 41 55 True elongation. 4 19 50 25 And true place from the head. 5 29 50 Finally, the true place from the equinox. 5 27 52 13 parts. 27. sc. 52 12, of Aquarius. Thus, the Moon has at the given time, according to this calculation, the true places of the limits of the nodes. What the nodes and limits of the Moon are has been sufficiently explained above. The form of calculation, however, given in the 26th Prutenic precept, which we must now show, is this. For a given time, you will find these four things. 1st. The equal simple motion of the Sun. 2nd. The equal simple motion of the Moon from the Sun. 3rd. The true precession of the equinoxes. And these three can be assumed from the preceding. 4th. The equal motion of the Moon's latitude, which for the proposed time is found to be 5 sexagesimal parts, 27 scruples, 38.1, 42.23. These having been found, cast into one sum the simple motion with the elongation of the Moon from the Sun, and the equal longitude of the Moon from the first star of Aries will emerge, 4 sexagesimal parts, 55 scruples, 47.18.57.28. From this, subtract the equal motion of the Moon's latitude, so that you may make the remaining distance of the northern limit from the head of the starry Aries 5 sexagesimal parts, 28 scruples, 9.18.15.24. From this, again subtract a quadrant of a circle, that is, 90 parts, so that the distance from the beginning of the starry Aries may emerge, 3 sexagesimal parts, 58 scruples, 9.14.15.22. Finally, if you add the true precession of the equinox to both of these distances, you will have the distance of both from the apparent equinox, the node carrying the Moon, 4 sexagesimal parts, 25 scruples, 31.12.36.22. The northern limit, however, 5 sexagesimal parts, 55 scruples, 31.14.36.2. Therefore, the head of the Dragon of the Moon is in part 25, almost of Sagittarius: the northern limit, however, in part 25+ of Pisces. Moreover, the tail and the southern limit are always found in diametrically opposite places. 6. Digitized by Google. 1. 1: : FIRST. THERE FOLLOWS THE SUM OF THE 28TH AND 29TH PRUTENIC PRECEPTS, concerning finding the true motion of the Moon's latitude from the northern limit, and this having been found, how the true latitude of the Moon is to be further explored. First, for the assumed time, find the equal motion of the Moon's latitude. Then, have the absolute prosthaphaeresis of the Moon's first epicycle, which, as instructed in the calculation of the Moon, you will subtract from the motion of latitude if the equated anomaly of the Moon is less than a semicircle, or add to it if it exceeds a semicircle. For in this way you will complete the true motion of the Moon's latitude, which the said tables, in imitation of Ptolemy and Copernicus, deduce not from the ascending node, as in the Alphonsine Abacus, but from the northern limit. Therefore, if you were to add a quadrant of a circle to this true motion of the Moon's latitude, you would produce that arc which the Alphonsines call the argument of the Moon's latitude, truly deduced from the head of the Dragon, as they call it, through consequent signs, which the Prutenic calculation certainly does not need, by which we are taught to hunt for the true latitude of the Moon from the ecliptic through the arc of the true motion of the Moon's latitude from the northern limit. Example: for our given time, the mean motion of the Moon's latitude was found to be 5 sexagesimal parts, 27 scruples, 38.11.42.22. And the absolute prosthaphaeresis of the first epicycle, 4 parts, 42 scruples, 12 55. 2nd to be added, because the equated anomaly is greater than a semicircle. Therefore, the true motion of latitude or of the northern limit is 5 sexagesimal parts, 32 scruples, 20.137.2, by the aid of which we calculate the true latitude of the Moon from the ecliptic in the following way. When the true motion of latitude is either nothing or a full semicircle, then the Moon's latitude is maximal, namely 5 parts, indeed northern when that true motion of the Moon's latitude is nothing: but southern when that same motion completes a semicircle. But when it equals a dodrant, the Moon is entirely devoid of latitude, and holds the ascending node, from which it gradually rises northward. But when it equals a quadrant, then the Moon is similarly without latitude, and holds the descending node, from which it gradually declines southward from the solar plane. Outside these places, when the motion of the Moon's latitude is found, then the canon (Digitized by Google) 1. rya DIFFERENCE. not the full latitude of the Moon, which is found on folio 135, second column, will easily show you both the quantity and quality of the Moon's latitude. As in our example, since the true motion of latitude is 5 sexagesimal parts, 32 scruples, 20.137.22, that is, 11 dodecatemoria, 2 parts, 20 scruples, 137.28. Therefore, to this number of dodecatemoria and parts in the said Canon corresponds the northern latitude of the Moon, ascending 4 parts, 25 scruples, 18.39.2. Calculation of the true motion of the five wandering stars, namely Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Venus, and Mercury. Before all else, these four things should be at hand. 1st. The true precession of the equinoxes. 2nd. The simple equal motion: These two are assumed from the preceding. 3rd. The planet's 'allaxis', that is, its commutation, or as the Alphonsines call it, the mean argument of the planet, that is, that arc of the epicycle which extends from the mean apogee to the planet through consequent signs. 4th. The apogee of the planet in the second sense, as it is commonly spoken of, which is numbered from the first star of Aries. Therefore, if you were to add the precession of the vernal equinox to this, the apogee from the vernal equinox for the given time would become known. However, the apogee of Venus always occupies one and the same place under the fixed sphere, and is distant from the first star of Aries by 0 sexagesimal parts, 48 scruples, 21.1, according to Copernicus. These four things provide us with the full calculation of the five wandering stars. For since both the motion of the anomaly of commutation and of longitude agree with the motion of the Sun by a certain ratio and harmony, it is not necessary that, besides the simple motion of the Sun, both the motion of anomaly and of longitude of the planet be laboriously sought from tables, since one of these is sufficient. For the motion of anomaly subtracted from the mean motion of the Sun leaves the planet's equal motion in longitude, and this in the three superior planets. But the simple motions of Venus and Mercury perpetually agree with the simple motion of the Sun: for they are 'sun-runners' and 'equal-runners' and 'same-runners', as these things are copiously explained in the elements and hypotheses of motions.

Talis enim ſitus eius efficit æquationem argumenti ſequentem lineam veri motus epicycli, auferendam à vero motu epicycli, vt verus motus planetæ remaneat. Quare 'etiam tum diminutus numero dicitur. Quòd fi locus æquatus plus fuerit medio curſu, id eſt, ſi motus planetę verus maior fue rit vero motu centri epicycli, erit deſcendens à medio, id eft, fa- ſtigio ſeu Apogeo rotz ſuz breuis vſque ad eius infimum. Quapropter tum auctus numero dicitur, &c. 2 Quando inter motum planetę verum & motum verum cen Et fi fuetri epicycli nullum eſt diſcrimen, tum etiam nulla eſt æquatio rit locus argumenti, & lineæ veri motus planete & veri motus centri epifcius,&c. cycli vniuntur: quod non fit, nifi planeta Apogeum aut Perigeum verum ſui epicycli permeante. 3. Fieri autem poteft vt planetæ pari vel impari ſublimitate Poftquam ferantur, idcirco fubiungitiam regulas, quarum obferuatione hoc,&c. vtrfuſque planetæ vires altitudineſque in breui circulo examinari atque penfitari debent, vtvtrius fit maior potentia atque 12 maios ==Start of OCR for page 5== 452 DIFFERENTIA maior autoritas, planè intelligamus.Ac quis inter tres ſuperio res alius aliq fuperiorem inferioremúe locum teneat, vt conije ciamus, iubet inquirere differentiam inter verum motum pla netz & verum motum centri epicycli, auferendo minorem maiori,atq; differentiam repertam multiplicare per.7. & quod hinc naſcitur, partiri per 22.& denique numerum le&ionis pro ſublimitate quando afcendit, aut deieétione quando deſcen dit, feruare; quem operandi modum ex habitudine ambitus cir culi ad ſuam diametrum autorem ſumpſiſſe, haud est dubium. Nam cuius circuli peripheria habet partes 22. talium partium 7.colligit eiuſdem circuli dimetiens. Veneris & Mercurii ſtelle in exortu matutino parui circuli culmen, veſpertino verò imf partem eiufdem petunt. Vtra autem iſtarum ſtellarum in circu lo breui magis polleat, ex differentia inter verum motum & verum motum & aut & iam iam expofito modo conijciēdum præcipit. Sed quali ratione ſeu caufa huiufmodi altitudinum perfcrutatio conftet, non abſque re dubitamus, fiquidem hinc ſequitur, planetam in alterutram mediarum longitudinum po fitum extolli ſuprareliquos, ſummitates ſuorum epicyclorum occupantes. Nam cùm circa longitudines indicis verſantur, maximè à centro epicycli recedunt, & in fine huius operationis maximum numerum exaltationis oftendunt. Econtrariò in apogeis vel perigeis epicyclorum collocati, cu inter verum motum corporum & verum motum centri epicycli,nullam differentiam faciant, juxta hanc operationem, neque altitudinis neque deprefsionis numerum vilum conſequi poterunt. Proinde omnibus his rationem fuprà à nobis,cum de afcenfu & deſcenfu planetarum ageremus, expofitam,tanquam veriorem & faciliorem preferendam cenfemus. Hinc enim ſatis vnicuique.con- ſtare poterit, illum planetam, cuius Anomalia coæquata, ſeu ve loquuntur,argumétum verum numero ſuperabitur, potiorem in hoc negotio atque ſuperiorem effe illis, quorum Anomalis ſen argumenti conqustinumerus maior fuerit, & hoc vſque ad ſex figna, id est, vſque ad perigeum epicycli.Porro in altera epicycli medietate, quz ab imo ad faftigium porrigitur, ille planeta, cuius argumentum verum maius fuerit, altior ac eminentior indicandus eft illo, cuius argumentum veramminus fuerit. In ſummay ==Start of page 1== QVARTA 443 ſamma, planeta ille qui propius ad culmen circuli breuis admp petur, ſublimior iudicatur illo, qui ab hoc faftigio magis deli pat. Quum autem planetæ non tantùm in epicyclis, verùm eti am in fuis abidibus ſcandant & deprimantur, ſublimitas hac pon tátum ratione ſuorum epicyclorum, verùm etiam ratione ſuarum abſidum eis competere videtur, ita vt is planeta, qui à fa ſtigio ſuæ abſidis minus diſtat, illo qui ab eodem magis remozuseft, ſublimior cenfeatur. 4 Superlationis planetarum in epicyclo maximam potefta- Et propritem eſſe in ponderoſorum coniunctionibus, atteſtatur enun- or ac for. giatu 63. Hoc modo etiam Sol nubibus, Luna, Mercurio & Venere eſt ſublimior.Altera ſublimitatum ſpecies, eſt corporum à finiente circulo ſubleuatio, qua ratione fieri poteſt, vt Sole altior ſit Luna, i videlicet oriente eo, ipfa in culmine cœli cernatur. De priore modo altitudirum hucufque egimus, ſequitur iam altera ſublimitatum ſpecies, quam latitu do & obliquitas ſigniferi efficit. Nam in coniun&ionibus plaBeta, qui habet latitudinem ſeptentrionalem, ſublimior acpatentior eſt planeta habente atitudinem auſtralem. Quòd ſi ambo fuerint præditi latitudine boreali, ſublimior indicandus eſt is, qui propiùs ad boream acceſſerit, illo, qui longius ab eadem plaga abeſt. Si vterque auſtrali latitudine fit præditus, is qui minorem latitudinem habuerit, præferédus erit ei, qui ma iorem.Si alter habeat latitudinem borealem, altero in Eclipti sacollocato, borealis eminentior iudicatur carente latitudine, : : 13 & hic Digitized by Google ==End of page 1== ==Start of page 2== : 454 DIFFIKINTIA & hic omnes meridionalem latitudinem habentes ſibi ſubiſcie Ad extremum ſi vterque in medietate zodiaci aſcendente vel deſcendente verſetur, eandemq; latitudinem ſeptentrionalem vel meridionalem ſimul habuerint, ſublimior cenſetur is, qui magis ab equinoctiali ad ſeptentrionem declinauerit. Ex his omnibus manifeſtum eſt, erraticas ſtellas quinque modis ſcandere & deſcendere. 1 Nam terra ſurſum ſcandunt dupliciter. I. Quando ad ſupremam partem circuli breuis feruntur. II. Quando ad apogeum abſidis Seu Tockxirga promouentur. I. Cùm ad motum vniuerſi mun di ab oriente ad meridianum circulum vehuntur. II. Cùm in aliquo Ecliptice cir- à finiente ad ver) culo poſiti, per Capricornum atq; ticé attolluntur) interſe&ionem vernalem ad artribus modis. Goam partem pelluntur. III. Cùm ratione latitudinis ſigniferi ab auſtro ad boream protruduntur. Ptolemæus huius dominationis atque excellentiæ frequentif- ſimè in Apoteleſmaticis meminit, ſed nuſquam quid per na θυπερτορισιμ intelligat, ab ipſo definitum reperio. Sententia ta men ipſius wiτ καθυπόρτόρήσεως, vt Cardano placet, talis eſt. Planeta dicitur exaltatus ſuper alium, quando fuerit ponderoſior & occidentalior: Veluti ſi Saturnus ſit in parte 20. Librz, Sol verò in eiuſdem ſigni Libræ principio: & hoc fit quadrifariam, ſicut eodem exemplo Saturni & Solis oſtendemus. Primò cùm Saturnus fuerit in angulo, & Sol extraangulum, & hoc eſt pessimum. translation: If the true motion of the epicycle is such that the planet ascends from the middle, that is, from the lowest point of its small circle or epicycle to its highest point, such a position causes the argument's equation, which follows the line of the epicycle's true motion, to be subtracted from the epicycle's true motion, so that the planet's true motion remains. Therefore, it is then said to be diminished in number. But if the equated place is greater than the mean course, that is, if the planet's true motion is greater than the true motion of the epicycle's center, it will be descending from the middle, that is, from the apex or apogee of its small wheel down to its lowest point. Such a position of the planet in the descending half of the epicycle causes the argument's equation, which precedes the line of the epicycle's true motion, to be added to its true motion, so that the planet's true course emerges. Therefore, it is then said to be augmented in number, etc.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Weakness and Elevation
text: ==Start of page 3== i 1 QUARTA. 455 rantur imbecilles planetæ, & qui ſunt extra dignitates, inq; locis ſuæ naturæ diſsimilibus poſiti. Hinc Ptolemæus Apotelef matico tertio, de nati futuris moribus diſſerens, ait, extolli pla. netas ſuper dominos qualitatum animæ principaliter, cùm illi domini fuerint imbecilles aut extra proprios diſsimileſq; 104 cos. Tertiò cùm Saturnus in quadratura vel etiam triangulo aut hexagono Solis fuerit, ita vt Sol ad dextram, Saturnus verò ad ſmiſtram ponatur: aliâs enim Saturnus Sole occidentalior eſſe non poſſet. Veluti ſi Sol ſit in Libra, Saturnus verò in Capricorno.Simile efficietur ſi Sole per ſignum obediens iter facien te, Saturnus in gno imperate reperiatur. Vt ſi itin p.10. & Sol in p.20. X. Quartò ſi Saturnus ad abſidem ſui Eccentri aſcendat, Sole in ſua abſide deſcendente.Maximè autem aſcen- ſus vel deſcenſusin epicyclis obſeruatur, vt diximus. Porrò planeta ponderoſus occidentalis ideo dicitur extolli ſupra Sojem aut Lunam, aut alium planetam ſe leuiorem, quia Sol vel. Luna in tali ſtatu vadunt ad illum planetam, & nunquam ſupertor planeta vadit ad eos. Et ſicut homo qui alios expectat, domi videtur nobilior ijs qui vadunt ad domum eius: ita planeta ponderofior cùm fuerit occidétalis, dicitur elatus ſuper alium, maximè ſi adfuerit ei vna vel plures iam dictarum conditionum,quæ ſunt præcipuæ. Non prætermittas tamen rationem. declinationum, latitudinum, & denique motum vniuerſi mundi, quo ſtellæ ab oriente ad meridianum locum ratione horizontium eminentiſsimuin feruntur. Semper autem declinatio & latitudo borealis efficacior eſt & altior auſtralibus. His ita ſe habentibus, non poterit conſtare amplius de ſupereminentia ſtellarum traditio Porphyri), qui orientales occidentalibus altiores eſſe prodidit: Verba eius ίκαθιπορτορήσεως hec ſunt: καθυπερτορεῖ πᾶς ἀςήρ, ἐμ του δεξιῷτριγώνῳ ἢ τετραγώνω κείμες *, τόμ ἐμ του συωνύριο, σχετη δε ύπὸ αὐτόμ, οίομ, ὁεμαίγος πέρωτι καθυπερτερείτη ποτέμ, καί υπό το έμ πν, και Η σκορπίκλέγοσι δε διωαντωτέρας εἶναι τὰς καθυπόρτερήσφς,εί τι Δ μεμ, είτε. ὁ γὰρ ὅτω καθυπορτορῶμας ἐρ ἰχυρότερο ὀλιμαν τε ἀγαθοποιός καντε κακοποιός, ἶδε καὶ ἅμα ἐπίκενβθ. ἀγαθος πιός Άγαρ ὠμ ἐπίσημομτκὸ γεύεσιμ δείκνυσι, φθυρυποιός δὲ ἄσια μομο ۱ Digitized by Google ==End of page 3== ==Start of page 4== &c. 456 DIFFERENTIA μομ, καὶ καθόλο τσᾶς ἀτἰρ ἐμ Τους δεξιῷ καθυπερτερεί τε ἐν τῷ an σύμῳ ἐφ' ὅμφερέται, hoc eſt:Omnis ſtella in aut * poſita, ſiniſtram & ad quam accedit ſuperat. Veluti ſtella in Capricorno poſita ſuperatur à ſtellis Libram, Virginem & Scorpium Laſtrantibus.Porrò ſoperationes, quæ ex & vel contingunt, teliquis potentiores eſſe aiunt. Nam ſtella ad hunc modú ſublata, robur aſſumit, ſiue ea benefica ſiue malefica fuerit, & maximè ſi cum hoc in angulo reperiatur. Acſi benefica quidem fuerit, illuſtrem genituram oftendit:ſi malefica, obſcuram. In vaiperfum vnaquæq; ſtella dextrorfum poſita, ſiniſtram ad quam fertur, ſuperat. 7. Vitimus locus eſt de apertione portarum, quas fic à maxiEt ex hoc, mis & euidentibus in circumfuſo cælo effe&ibus nominant. Eſt autem apertio portarum duplex, magna ſcilicet & parua. Parua apertio portarum efficitur fic vt hic dicitur, nempe cùm inferior ſuperiori coniungitur, & fuerint cum hoc domus corum oppoſitę. Tales ſyzygiæ ſi Lunam negligas, tresinueniuntur: Prima eſt Saturni & Solis, quzaérem valde turbidum & frigidum efficit: Secunda Iouis & Mercurij, quæ ventos commouet: Tertia Martis & Veneris, imbres & fulgura procreans. Harum trium copulationum ſingulæ tres pariunt formas, nempe coniun&ionem, oppoſitionem & quadraturam, quarum validissima eſt oppoſitio, inde coniuctio, quę plus roboris habet quam tetragonus. カ 824 후우 Apertiones portarum. カ 624 4 2 Apertionem portarum magnam ſic definiunt:Quando Luns ſeparata ab inferiore, applicatur ſuperiori coniun&ione, oppo ſitione vel quadrato, fic vt planeta illi, quos Luna coniungit,ha beant domus oppoſitas, fit apertio porta vel valuz magnæ, in qua aëris maxima & euidens fit commutatio, quz naturam ſupe riotis refert, & præfertim ſi ſuperior & inferior ſeſe reſpiciant: veluti & ſit O in parte 29.Scorpij. hin parte 29. Aquarij. Din parte 27 Leonis. Digitized by Google Hic ==End of page 4== ==Start of page 5== TQVARTA 457 Mic Sol Saturnum de quadrato aſpicit,eidemque applicat:Luna veroà Solis recedens, atque ad diametrum Saturni proximè accedens, ex Sole in Saturnum maximas portas aperit, facitque tempus nebulofum & frigidum,& potiſsimùm ijs locis, quorum cardines Luna luftrauerit. Talis conſtitutio ſi aquea ſigna occupauerit, maximos imbres pariet, præfertim ſi Saturnus dominetur cardinibus ad momentum quadrate radiationis con- ſtitutis. Similis pene conſtitutio efficitur, ſi Luna à Saturno de Auens, ſeſe Soli adiungat. Vniuerfaliter enim planeta cui Luna primdadiungitur, dicitur aperire portam ſuper illum, ad quem defluens ipſa ſecundò copulatur, ſi modò domus habeant oppofitas: ſed tamen maior eſt ſignificatio, ſi fiat ab inferiore ad ſuperiorem:ea enim demum elt vera valuarum apertio, minor verò & breuñor permutatio in aëre ſit, atque turbulentia breuiuſculo temporein ſtatum tranquillum permutatur, cum Lunaà ponderoſo defluens leuioriapplicat. Apertio valuarum magnarum. 50 recedens li Da, vel feratur ad 45 adi 4 vel econtrarið. In omni autem defluxu Lunæ peculiariter eſt reſpiciendum ad Mercurium:nam ſi planetis, quoram vnus alteri valuas aperit, iungatur aut eoſdem videat, aeris inſtabilitate & ventos faciet. E: ex hoc albuzic, & eſt res quam valde feruabant Indi. Nam ipſi numerant poſt coniunctionem Solis & Lunæ XII.horas, & applicant eas Soli, & diuidunt XII.horas trinas, id eſt, in tres partes, ſcilicet in haim Solis, & iudicant ſuper omnes quatuor horas ſecundum iudicium dominorum triplicitatis Solis hora coniundlio nis: deinde dant Veneri poſt XII. boras, & diuidunt eas iterumtrinas,& iudicant ſuper omnem trinam diui ſionem ſecundum dominos triplicitatis Veneris horacojunctionis: post hocfaciunt ſimiliter cum Mercurio & alijs m . 1 Digitized by Google ==End of page 5== ==Start of OCR for page 1== 458 DIFFERENTIA QUARTA alijs planetis per fucceßionem eorum, donec reuertitur orbis ad Solem poft LXXXIIII.horas, & non ceßant fic facere frequenter, donec venerit coniunctio fecunda. Et quidam dicunt, quòd Albuzic fit hoc quòd post cons iunctionem fint XII.hora inæquales, quæ vocantur cos buſta: & non oportet aliquem in his incipere aliquod opus.Et post horas XII.funt LXXII. hora incombu- ſte: in quibus est vtilis operum inceptio. Et post has LXXII. boras incobustas funt XII.combuste fimiliter vfque ad coniunctionem qua fuccedit. poft hoc diuidunt has horas XII. in tres diuifiones, id eft, in partes. Dixerunta cùm quis cœpit in quatuor boris primis deambulare vel bellare: timenda erit perditio anime fue: & in quatuor horis fecundistimendum erit detrimentum corporis fui sine amiſstone anima: & in quatuor bo ris vltimis timendum est detrimentum fubftantiæ fuæ ſuorum & omniumque poßidet. De horis fortunatis & infauftis iuxta opinionem gentis Indorum.Indi,inquit, periodicum menfem in horas diſtribuunt, ac primas duodecim horas, quæ principiumà coniun&ioneluminarium capiunt, horas Solis ſer combuftas nominat, quibus surfum dominos triangularitatis loci coitus luminarium præ ficiunt.Primus trienti primo, fecundus ſecundo, tertiustertio præeft.Sequentes horas 1.2.afſcribunt Veneri, quæ Solem in or dine fequitur, ijfdemque eodem modo, vt de Sole diaŭ eſt, pre faciunt dominos triangularitatis loci, quem Venus in ſynodo luminarium tenuit. Et fic deinceps horæ ſequentes ſequentibus planetis diſtribuuntur, donecpoſt horarum menfis 84.la pfum, Solpriorem ambitum imperij repetat. Reliqua funt fa cilia Hora combuſtæ funt, qua Solem dominum ſortiuntur. Item perditionem anima ſuo more amiſsionem vicz nomi sat, &c. : DIFFE ==End of OCR for page 1==
translation: Planets are rendered weak and outside their dignities when they are placed in locations dissimilar to their nature. Hence Ptolemy, in the third book of his Apotelesmatics, discussing the future manners of nativities, says that planets are elevated above the lords of the soul's qualities primarily when those lords are weak or outside their proper and dissimilar places. Thirdly, when Saturn is in a square, or even a trine or sextile to the Sun, such that the Sun is on the right and Saturn on the left; otherwise, Saturn could not be more occidental than the Sun. For example, if the Sun is in Libra and Saturn in Capricorn. A similar effect occurs if, while the Sun travels through an obedient sign, Saturn is found in an imperious sign. For example, if the Sun is in 10 degrees of Aries and Saturn in 20 degrees of Capricorn. Fourthly, if Saturn ascends to the apogee of its eccentric while the Sun descends to its apogee. However, ascent or descent is most observed in epicycles, as we have said. Furthermore, a ponderous occidental planet is said to be elevated above the Sun or Moon, or another lighter planet, because the Sun or Moon in such a state moves towards that planet, and the superior planet never moves towards them. And just as a man who awaits others at home seems nobler than those who come to his house, so a more ponderous planet, when occidental, is said to be elevated above another, especially if one or more of the aforementioned principal conditions are present. However, do not neglect the reason for declinations, latitudes, and finally the motion of the entire world, by which stars are carried from the east to the most eminent meridian place by reason of the horizons. Always, northern declination and latitude are more efficacious and higher than southern ones. Given these conditions, Porphyry's tradition regarding the supereminence of stars, who stated that oriental stars are higher than occidental ones, can no longer hold. His words on supereminence are these: "Every star placed in the right trine or square, or in the left, and to which it approaches, overcomes. For example, a star placed in Capricorn is overcome by stars traversing Libra, Virgo, and Scorpio. Furthermore, they say that supereminences, which occur from trine or square, are more powerful than others. For a star thus elevated gains strength, whether it be benefic or malefic, and especially if it is found in an angle with this. And if it is benefic, it shows an illustrious nativity; if malefic, an obscure one. In general, every star placed to the right overcomes the left to which it is carried." This means: "Every star placed in the right trine or square, or in the left, and to which it approaches, overcomes. For example, a star placed in Capricorn is overcome by stars traversing Libra, Virgo, and Scorpio. Furthermore, they say that supereminences, which occur from trine or square, are more powerful than others. For a star thus elevated gains strength, whether it be benefic or malefic, and especially if it is found in an angle with this. And if it is benefic, it shows an illustrious nativity; if malefic, an obscure one. In general, every star placed to the right overcomes the left to which it is carried."

Nam ipſi numerant poſt coniunctionem Solis & Lunæ XII.horas, & applicant eas Soli, & diuidunt XII.horas trinas, id eſt, in tres partes, ſcilicet in haim Solis, & iudicant ſuper omnes quatuor horas ſecundum iudicium dominorum triplicitatis Solis hora coniundlio nis: deinde dant Veneri poſt XII. boras, & diuidunt eas iterumtrinas,& iudicant ſuper omnem trinam diui ſionem ſecundum dominos triplicitatis Veneris horacojunctionis: post hocfaciunt ſimiliter cum Mercurio & alijs m . 1 Digitized by Google ==End of page 5== ==Start of OCR for page 1== 458 DIFFERENTIA QUARTA alijs planetis per fucceßionem eorum, donec reuertitur orbis ad Solem poft LXXXIIII.horas, & non ceßant fic facere frequenter, donec venerit coniunctio fecunda. Et quidam dicunt, quòd Albuzic fit hoc quòd post cons iunctionem fint XII.hora inæquales, quæ vocantur cos buſta: & non oportet aliquem in his incipere aliquod opus.Et post horas XII.funt LXXII. hora incombu- ſte: in quibus est vtilis operum inceptio. Et post has LXXII. boras incobustas funt XII.combuste fimiliter vfque ad coniunctionem qua fuccedit. poft hoc diuidunt has horas XII. in tres diuifiones, id eft, in partes. Dixerunta cùm quis cœpit in quatuor boris primis deambulare vel bellare: timenda erit perditio anime fue: & in quatuor horis fecundistimendum erit detrimentum corporis fui sine amiſstone anima: & in quatuor bo ris vltimis timendum est detrimentum fubftantiæ fuæ ſuorum & omniumque poßidet. De horis fortunatis & infauftis iuxta opinionem gentis Indorum.Indi,inquit, periodicum menfem in horas diſtribuunt, ac primas duodecim horas, quæ principiumà coniun&ioneluminarium capiunt, horas Solis ſer combuftas nominat, quibus surfum dominos triangularitatis loci coitus luminarium præ ficiunt.Primus trienti primo, fecundus ſecundo, tertiustertio præeft.Sequentes horas 1.2.afſcribunt Veneri, quæ Solem in or dine fequitur, ijfdemque eodem modo, vt de Sole diaŭ eſt, pre faciunt dominos triangularitatis loci, quem Venus in ſynodo luminarium tenuit. Et fic deinceps horæ ſequentes ſequentibus planetis diſtribuuntur, donecpoſt horarum menfis 84.la pfum, Solpriorem ambitum imperij repetat. Reliqua funt fa cilia Hora combuſtæ funt, qua Solem dominum ſortiuntur. Item perditionem anima ſuo more amiſsionem vicz nomi sat, &c. : DIFFE ==End of OCR for page 1==
translation: The seventh topic is about the opening of the gates, which are so named from their great and evident effects in the surrounding heavens. There are two types of gate openings: great and small. A small opening of the gates occurs as described here, namely when an inferior planet conjoins a superior one, and their houses are opposite. Such conjunctions, if you disregard the Moon, are found to be three: The first is of Saturn and the Sun, which causes very turbulent and cold air. The second is of Jupiter and Mercury, which stirs up winds. The third is of Mars and Venus, which produces rain and lightning. Each of these three conjunctions produces three forms, namely conjunction, opposition, and square, of which opposition is the most powerful, then conjunction, which has more strength than the square. The great opening of the gates is defined thus: When the Moon, separating from an inferior planet, applies to a superior one by conjunction, opposition, or square, such that the planets to which the Moon conjoins have opposite houses, a great opening of the gate or valve occurs, in which there is a great and evident change in the air, which reflects the nature of the superior planet, and especially if the superior and inferior planets aspect each other. For example, if the Sun is in 29 degrees of Scorpio, Saturn in 29 degrees of Aquarius, and the Moon in 27 degrees of Leo. Here the Sun aspects Saturn by square and applies to it. The Moon, receding from the Sun and approaching the diameter of Saturn, opens the greatest gates from the Sun to Saturn, and makes the weather cloudy and cold, especially in those places whose cardines the Moon traverses. If such a configuration occupies watery signs, it will produce great rains, especially if Saturn dominates the cardines established at the moment of the square radiation. A nearly similar configuration occurs if the Moon, separating from Saturn, joins the Sun. For universally, the planet to which the Moon first applies is said to open the gate over it, to which the Moon, flowing away, secondly conjoins, provided they have opposite houses. However, the significance is greater if it occurs from an inferior to a superior planet, for that is truly the opening of the great valves. A smaller and shorter change in the air occurs, and the turbulence quickly changes to a tranquil state when the Moon, flowing from a ponderous planet, applies to a lighter one. The great opening of the valves occurs when the Moon separates from a planet and applies to another, or vice versa. In every lunar flow, Mercury must be particularly observed, for if it joins the planets, one of which opens the gates to the other, or if it aspects them, it will cause instability in the air and winds. And from this, Albuzic, and it is a thing that the Indians greatly observed. For they count 12 hours after the conjunction of the Sun and Moon, and apply them to the Sun, and divide the 12 hours into three parts, namely into the haim of the Sun, and they judge over all four hours according to the judgment of the lords of the triplicity of the Sun's hour of conjunction. Then they give to Venus after 12 hours, and divide them again into three, and judge over every three-part division according to the lords of the triplicity of Venus's hour of conjunction. After this, they do similarly with Mercury and other planets in succession, until the orb returns to the Sun after 84 hours, and they do not cease to do so frequently until the second conjunction arrives. And some say that Albuzic is this: that after the conjunction there are 12 unequal hours, which are called combust; and no one should begin any work in these. And after the 12 hours, there are 72 hours incombust; in which the beginning of works is useful. And after these 72 incombust hours, there are 12 combust hours similarly until the conjunction that follows. After this, they divide these 12 hours into three divisions, that is, into parts. They said that when someone began to walk or wage war in the first four hours, the loss of his soul would be feared; and in the second four hours, the loss of his body without the loss of his soul would be feared; and in the last four hours, the loss of his substance, his possessions, and everything he owns would be feared. Regarding fortunate and unfortunate hours according to the opinion of the Indian people. The Indians, he says, divide the periodic month into hours, and name the first twelve hours, which begin from the conjunction of the luminaries, the combust hours of the Sun, to which they assign the lords of the triplicity of the place of the luminaries' coitus. The first lord presides over the first third, the second over the second, and the third over the third. The following 12 hours they assign to Venus, who follows the Sun in order, and to these they assign the lords of the triplicity of the place that Venus held in the synod of the luminaries, in the same way as was said for the Sun. And so on, the following hours are distributed to the following planets, until after the lapse of 84 hours of the month, the Sun repeats its former circuit of dominion. The rest are easy. The combust hours are those that have the Sun as their lord. Likewise, they call the loss of the soul the loss of life in their manner, etc.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Parts of the Houses and Other Parts
text: QUINTA. 465 • Octaua domus est pars mortis, & accipitur in die ac nocte à Luna in gradū minuti domus octaua: ade ditur defuper quòd ambulauerit Saturn. in signo fuo, projcitur ab initio figni Saturni. Pars planete qui in. terficit, accipitur in die à domino grad.afcend.in gradu Luna:& in nocte econuerfo, Fc. Pars anni, in quo time tur nato mors & paupertas, impedimentum ac deftrua Etio, accipitur in die ac nocte à Saturno in gradum domi ni domus coniunctionis vel preventionis, que fuit ante natiuitatem &c. Parsligationis carceris:& vtrum liberetur ab eo velnō, accipitur in die à Saturno in par tem fortune, & innocte in nocte econuerfo. ? Nona domus est pars peregrinatiõis:accipitur in die ac nocte à domino domus none in grad.et minutú domus none, & proijcitur ab afcend. Pars itineris per aquam, accipitur in die à Saturno in XV.gradum figni Cancri: & in nocte econnerſo, &c. Quòd fi fuerit Saturnus in XV. gradu Cancri, erit gradus partis grad. afcedentis. Pars religionis accipitur in die à Luna in Mercurium, Gin nocte econuerfo, et proijcitur ab afcendente. Domus decima pars regionis et regni : et quid operis faciat natus: et accipitur in die ac nocte à Saturno in Lunam, et proijcitur ab aſcend. Pars regis atq; regni ac cipitur in die à Marte in Lunam: innocle econuerfo, et proijcitur ab afcendente. Et est alia pars fimilis pas tri patrum. Pars operis et regni fecundum Vellium aca cipitur in die ac nocte à Sole in gradum medý cæli, et projcitur ab afcen. Pars fignificans vtri fit caufa regni aut non, Digitized by Google DIFFERENTIA aut non, accipitur in die ac nocle à Sole in gradumedij cæli, & proiicitur a loue. Pars Martis accipitur in die à Venere in Lunam, & in nocle è conuerfo: & proiicitur ab afcendente. Vndecima domus est pars amicorum, Gaccipitur in die ac nocte à Luna in Mercurium, & proiicitur ab afcendente. Et dixit Alendez god, accipitur in nocte è cos werſo. Pars fignificans effectum amicorum & amicitia, accipitur in die à parte fortune in partem futurorum,et in nocte è conuerfo,et catera. Duodecima domus est pars inimicorum fecundum Hermetem, et accipitur in die ac nocte à domino domus inimicorum, vſque in gradum et minutum domus inimis corum, et proiicitur ab afcendente. Et fecundum quofdam antiquorum accipitur in die et in nocle à Saturno in Martem etc. Et he funt partes XII. domorum etc. He funtalice partes quibus vtuntur magiftri iudiciorum aftrorum multum. Ex quibus est pars quæ fignificat mendacium rumorum, quando ceciderit in fignum tortuofum vel mobile:aut dominus domus fuerit retrogradus vel impeditas:aut afpexerint eum mali vel ei fint coniuncti. Et fignificat veritatem rumorum corum, fice ciderit cum fortunis aut in domibus feu terminis earū, vel afpexerint eum aut fuerint in ſigno directo: et accipi tur in die à Mercurio in Venerem, iu nocte è conuerfo, et proiicitur, etc. Et fecundum Albumazar in die à Mer curio in Lunam et in nocle è conuerfo, etc. Pars rationis profunditatis fenfus accipitur in die à Saturno in Lunam, Digitized by Google QUINTA. 465 Lunam: & in nocte è conuerfo. &c. Pars fapientice difciplinæ accipitur in die ac nocte à Saturno in Ioue. projcitur &c. Pars guerra & præliorum accipitur in die ac nocte à Saturno in Luna:&proijcitur að afce. Pars pacis & concordiæ exercituum accipitur in die ac notte à Luna in Mercurii, & proijcitur ab afcendente. Pars afpiciēdi in reuolutiõe anni: accipitur in die à Lu na in Venere:& in nocte è cõuerſo: & proijciturà Sole. De partibus reuolutionű in caufis regnorum. Et quia auxiliate Deo introduximus has partes que conueniūt in rebus particularibus: profequamur partes qua conueniunt in reuolutione annorum mundi & coniunctionum fignificantium cauſas regni, ſtabilitate feu duratione Feius. eius. Ex Ex quibus quibus est eft pars pars que dicitur pars regni ſeu imperij, que exercetur in reuolutione mundi: accipiturque à Marte in Lunam, & projcitur ab afcen dente coniunctionis, quæ ſignificat mutationem regni. Sic quoque alio modo accipitur.f.à gradu afcendentis cõ iunétionis in gradum coniunctionis:& projcitur à gra du afcendentis revolutionis. Item fit alio modo: accipia tur à gradu medij cali reuolutionis vſq; ad Sole.& pro jcitur à Ioue.i.à gradu Iouis. Pars temporis regis:id eft, quadiu debet durare:accipitur hora electiõis regis à Sole in XV gra.figni Leonis, &proijcitur à Luna:deinde accipitur à Luna in XV.grad.figni Cacri, projcitur Sole. Alia pars de tempore electiõis regis:accipitur ho ra electionis regis in die à Loue in Saturni, & in nocte è converso: proijcitur ab afcendete revolutiõis anni, in quo - Digitized by Google 466 DIFFERENTIA in quo furrexerit Rex. Quòd fi fuerit Iupiter in figno co muni, fuerit reuolutio diuina:fueritq; lupiter cadens ab angulis:tunc accipitur à Saturno in Ioue: & addun tur defuper XXX.grad.& proijcitur ab afcedente.Si verò Saturn. & Iupiter fuerint fibi oppofiti, fuerint ambo cadētes ab afcendente:mediatur quod exierit inter eos, i. accipitur medietas graduŭ qui funt inter eos: proijcitur ab afcendente.Et fi fuerit Iupiter in exal tatione fua:& fuerit reuolutio in nocle: numeratur ab eo in Saturnum, & proijcitur ab afcendente. Duce partes maximè funt ex qbus extrahetur tepus electiõis regis & eius durationis. Prima earu eft, vt afpi cias horă electiõis Etidis regis, regis, qua qua peruenit peruenit pfectus pfectu anni à co iunctione triplicitatis, quæ fignificauit ſectaex numero quo datur omnib.XXX.grad.annus: & omnib.grad. duob. cũ dimidio meſis vnus.& cufciueris in quo gradu vel figno fit, hic ferua en:quia hic eft locus à quo aquabis prima parte: cũ volueris aptare eŭ, aptabis afcendes reuolutionis anni in quo furrexit ipfe electus. Poft hoc afpice à planeta Orientali à Sole ex Saturno Gloue in ipso anno vfq; ad gradu æquationis partis prime quam feruafti: proijce ab afcedente revolutiõis anni:& quo peruenit numerus, ipſe eſt locus primæ partis. Pars fecu. da fic inuenitur:accipe in cõiunctione in qua furrexerit Rex vel cœpit regnu: ad quod fignu & gradu peruenit profectio anni ex numero quo datur oibus XXX. gra. annus,& II.gradibus cũ dimidio mēſis vfq; ad die quo Currexerit Rex: ipfe erit locus equationis fecunde partis, 1 Digitized by Google QUINTA. 467 partis, ferua quoq; eu. Poft hoc afpice à planeta occidēta lià Sole ex Saturno & Lone, Ioue, vſq; v/q; ad ad locu locu equatiõis equatiois par- partis fecuda qua feruaſti, pijce en ab aſcédente reuolu. tionis:quo puenerit, erit locus partis ſecude. Ha funt partes qfignificat fortitudine regis & eius duratione. Item funt aliæ partes quib. vtūtur in revolutionibus annoru mundi: ſcitur p eas quid graue de reb.in foro, qd erit leue in pretio:quidue pretiofum feu vile:mula tū feu paru:hoc eft, vt afpicias vbi ceciderit pars, id eft, in cuius planeta domo, exaltatione,termino, vel triplici tate: qui Planeta fi fuerit retrogradus, vel combuftus, vel in malo loco:vilefcit illa res & erit parui pretij. Si verò fuerit in loco fortitudinis, aut in angulo & maximè in medio cali,grauabitur illa res, Gerit magni pre tij: fi peruenerit dominus domus ad locum deſcenſionis fux: vilefcit illa res. Et afpice afpectus fortunarum planetarū malorum ad ea, nec noi ad Luna do minueius:et vide quo modo et quis afpiciat ipfam partë: ſi fortuna et Luna afpexerint illa parte, multiplicabi tur illa reset ſi afpexerint ea mali, detrimentű patietur. Pars diſpoſitionis anni à Luna in Mercuriū. Pars aque à Luna in Venere. Pars hordei, à luna in Ioue. Pars cice ris quod est quodda genus leguminis: à Venere in Sole. Pars lentis, à Marte in Saturnu. Pars fabaru, à Saturno in Marte.Pars almusi,id eft, milij à Saturno in Marte. Pars ceparu à Saturno in Marte. Pars tritici à Solé in Martem. Pars nucu à Mercurio in Martem, Pars zac cari à Mercurio in Venerem, Pars dactylorum à Sole in n2 Venerem. Digitized by Google
translation: The eighth house is the part of death, and it is taken day and night from the Moon to the degree and minute of the eighth house. It is added from above that Saturn has walked in its sign, projected from the beginning of Saturn's sign. The part of the planet that kills is taken by day from the lord of the ascendant degree to the degree of the Moon, and by night, conversely, etc. The part of the year in which death, poverty, impediment, and destruction are feared for the native is taken day and night from Saturn to the degree of the lord of the house of conjunction or prevention that occurred before the nativity, etc. The part of imprisonment and whether one will be freed from it or not is taken by day from Saturn to the Part of Fortune, and by night, conversely. The ninth house is the part of peregrination; it is taken day and night from the lord of the ninth house to the degree and minute of the ninth house, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of a journey by water is taken by day from Saturn to the 15th degree of the sign of Cancer, and by night, conversely, etc. If Saturn is in the 15th degree of Cancer, the degree of the part will be the degree of the ascendant. The part of religion is taken by day from the Moon to Mercury, and by night, conversely, and it is projected from the ascendant. The tenth house is the part of region and kingdom, and what work the native does; it is taken day and night from Saturn to the Moon, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of the king and kingdom is taken by day from Mars to the Moon; by night, conversely, and it is projected from the ascendant. And there is another part similar to the father of fathers. The part of work and kingdom according to Velius is taken day and night from the Sun to the degree of the Midheaven, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part signifying whether there is a cause for a kingdom or not is taken day and night from the Sun to the degree of the Midheaven, and it is projected from Jupiter. The part of Mars is taken by day from Venus to the Moon, and by night, conversely, and it is projected from the ascendant. The eleventh house is the part of friends, and it is taken day and night from the Moon to Mercury, and it is projected from the ascendant. And Alendez said that it is taken by night, conversely. The part signifying the effect of friends and friendship is taken by day from the Part of Fortune to the part of future things, and by night, conversely, etc. The twelfth house is the part of enemies according to Hermes, and it is taken day and night from the lord of the house of enemies, up to the degree and minute of the house of enemies, and it is projected from the ascendant. And according to some ancients, it is taken day and night from Saturn to Mars, etc. And these are the parts of the 12 houses, etc. These are other parts that the masters of astrological judgments use frequently. Among these is the part that signifies the falsehood of rumors, when it falls in a crooked or mutable sign, or if the lord of the house is retrograde or impeded, or if malefics aspect it or are conjoined with it. And it signifies the truth of rumors if it falls with benefics or in their houses or terms, or if they aspect it or are in a direct sign; and it is taken by day from Mercury to Venus, by night, conversely, and it is projected, etc. And according to Albumazar, by day from Mercury to the Moon, and by night, conversely, etc. The part of the reason for the profundity of sense is taken by day from Saturn to the Moon, and by night, conversely, etc. The part of wisdom and discipline is taken day and night from Saturn to Jupiter, projected, etc. The part of war and battles is taken day and night from Saturn to the Moon, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of peace and concord of armies is taken day and night from the Moon to Mercury, and it is projected from the ascendant. The part of looking in the revolution of the year is taken by day from the Moon to Venus, and by night, conversely, and it is projected from the Sun. Concerning the parts of revolutions in matters of kingdoms. And because, with God's help, we have introduced these parts that are relevant in particular matters, let us proceed to the parts that are relevant in the revolution of the years of the world and the conjunctions signifying the causes of the kingdom, its stability or duration. Among these is the part called the part of the kingdom or empire, which is exercised in the revolution of the world; and it is taken from Mars to the Moon, and it is projected from the ascendant of the conjunction that signifies the change of the kingdom. It is also taken in another way, namely, from the degree of the ascendant of the conjunction to the degree of the conjunction, and it is projected from the degree of the ascendant of the revolution. Likewise, it is done in another way: it is taken from the degree of the Midheaven of the revolution up to the Sun, and it is projected from Jupiter, i.e., from the degree of Jupiter. The part of the king's time, that is, how long it should last, is taken at the hour of the king's election from the Sun to the 15th degree of the sign of Leo, and it is projected from the Moon; then it is taken from the Moon to the 15th degree of the sign of Cancer, projected from the Sun. Another part concerning the time of the king's election: it is taken at the hour of the king's election by day from Jupiter to Saturn, and by night, conversely; it is projected from the ascendant of the revolution of the year in which the king arose. If Jupiter is in a common sign, and the revolution is divine, and Jupiter is falling from the angles, then it is taken from Saturn to Jupiter, and 30 degrees are added from above, and it is projected from the ascendant. But if Saturn and Jupiter are opposite each other, and both are falling from the ascendant, what comes between them is mediated, i.e., half of the degrees between them are taken; it is projected from the ascendant. And if Jupiter is in its exaltation, and the revolution is at night, it is counted from it to Saturn, and it is projected from the ascendant. There are two main parts from which the time of the king's election and its duration will be extracted. The first of these is to look at the hour of the king's election, when the perfection of the year arrives from the conjunction of the triplicity that signified the sect, from the number by which 30 degrees are given for each year, and for every two and a half degrees, one month. And when you know in what degree or sign it is, keep it here, because this is the place from which you will equate the first part: when you want to adapt it, you will adapt the ascendant of the revolution of the year in which the elected one arose. After this, look from the oriental planet from the Sun, from Saturn and Jupiter in that year, up to the degree of the equation of the first part that you kept; project it from the ascendant of the revolution of the year; and where the number arrives, that is the place of the first part. The second part is found thus: take at the conjunction in which the king arose or began to reign, to what sign and degree the progression of the year arrives from the number by which 30 degrees are given for each year, and two and a half degrees for each month, up to the day on which the king arose; that will be the place of the equation of the second part, also keep it. After this, look from the occidental planet from the Sun, from Saturn and Jupiter, up to the place of the equation of the second part that you kept; project it from the ascendant of the revolution: where it arrives, that will be the place of the second part. These are the parts that signify the strength of the king and his duration. There are also other parts that are used in the revolutions of the years of the world: by these, it is known what is heavy in the market, what will be light in price, or what is precious or vile, much or little; that is, you look at where the part falls, that is, in whose planetary house, exaltation, term, or triplicity: if that planet is retrograde, or combust, or in a bad place, that thing will become vile and of little value. But if it is in a place of strength, or in an angle, and especially in the Midheaven, that thing will become heavy and of great value; if the lord of the house reaches the place of its fall, that thing will become vile. And look at the aspects of the benefics and malefics to these, and also to the Moon and its lord; and see how and who aspects that part: if benefics and the Moon aspect that part, that thing will multiply, and if malefics aspect it, it will suffer detriment. The part of the disposition of the year from the Moon to Mercury. The part of water from the Moon to Venus. The part of barley from the Moon to Jupiter. The part of chickpeas, which is a kind of legume, from Venus to the Sun. The part of lentils from Mars to Saturn. The part of beans from Saturn to Mars. The part of millet from Saturn to Mars. The part of onions from Saturn to Mars. The part of wheat from the Sun to Mars. The part of nuts from Mercury to Mars. The part of sugar from Mercury to Venus. The part of dates from the Sun to Venus.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Calculating Planetary Positions
text: PRIMA. 153 plicantur. His itaq; quatuor conſtitutis, aufer æqualem commu rationis planetæ motum ab æquali Solis motu fimplici, vtaqualis longitudinis planetæ motus à capite Arietis ſtellati prodeat, idque tantùm in tribus ſuperioribus.Nam & & &,vimodò dicebamus, æqualem motum longitudinis ſemper eundem ſeu communem cum Sole obtinent. Hincvero à motu longitudinis iam deprehenſo, mox aufer Apogeon planetę in ſecunda ſignificatione, & remanebit Anomalia Eccentri, ſeu vt vulgò vocant, centrum medium. 1. Proſtaphærefis ſeu æquatio Eccentri, hoc eft, arcus inter lineam medij & Deinde per Anoma-veri motus epicycliiacens. Hæcprofeliam Eccentri quæ- to nulla eſt centro epicycli Apogeum runtur duo vel Perigeum luſtrate, maxima verò per longitudines medias eunte. (2. Scrupula proportionalia. ſtam medio moaufert tui longitudinis, Minor Et fi Anoma qua lia Eccentri æquatio femicirculo Eccétri fuerit Maior adijcit quam Anomalie Eccentri,vt vtrű- que coæquetur, quemadmodú ip fi quoq; tituli ad- ſcripti huic ordiIni Proſtaphereſe- (on per ſe indicat Contrarium autem faciendum eſt pro habenda vera Anoma lia commutationis. Alternatim enim pariter ſeſe excedunt & exceduntur, cùm arcus æquationis Anomaliæ Eccentri & com mutationis epicycli inter ſe fint proportionales & fimiles,atq; ob id ex tabulis deprompto vno habeatur & reliquus. Loca denique vera fixarum tam longitudinis quam latitudinis ſic venabere: E canonica ipſarum deſcriptione excerpenda eſt ſtella longitudo à capite Arietis ſtellati, ac mox deinde huic longitudini adijcienda eſt equinotij verni pręceſsio vera, vt longitudo ſtellæ ab apparenti æquinoctio in partibus exeat. Has partes i per 30. ſecemus, facilè deprehendemus, in quo dodecatemorio & in qua parte eiuſdem ſtella verſetur. Exempli gratia, fit inuenienda cœli pars, in qua ad datum geniturz tempus hæret λαμπαδίας,palilicium Romanis dicta. Longitudo eius à capite Arietis in Aſteriſmo Tauri annotatur part.36. Idcirco hęc coniuncta cum longitudine ſtellæ efficit part.63.fcru.22 12, 212a, quæ per 30.fectę produnt duo ſigna cum par.3. ſcru.22 12, 2122. translation: FIRST. 153 are applied. These four being thus constituted, subtract the equal common motion of the planet from the equal simple motion of the Sun, so that the equal motion of the planet's longitude from the head of the starry Aries may emerge, and that only in the three superior planets. For Mars, Jupiter, and Saturn, as we just said, always obtain the same or common equal motion of longitude with the Sun. From this longitude motion, now ascertained, immediately subtract the Apogee of the planet in the second signification, and there will remain the Anomaly of the Eccentric, or as it is commonly called, the mean center. 1. The Prosthaphæresis or equation of the Eccentric, that is, the arc between the line of the mean and the true motion of the epicycle. This, indeed, is nothing at the apogee or perigee of the epicycle's center, but is greatest when passing through the mean longitudes. (2. Proportional minutes. If the equation of the Anomaly of the Eccentric is less than a semicircle, it subtracts from the mean motion of longitude; if greater, it adds to it, so that both may be equalized, as the titles themselves indicate. The opposite, however, must be done to obtain the true Anomaly of commutation. For they alternately exceed and are exceeded by each other, since the arcs of the equation of the Anomaly of the Eccentric and the commutation of the epicycle are proportional and similar to each other, and therefore, one being taken from the tables, the other is also obtained. Afterwards, by the commutation of the planet, 1. The Prosthaphæresis or equation of the orbit. 2. The subsequent excess, whose part corresponding to the proportional minutes must always be added to the equation of the orbit, so that the parallax or absolute equation of the orbit may emerge. And if the Anomaly of commutation is less than a semicircle, subtract the Prosthaphæresis of the orbit from the co-equated longitude, so that the true place of the star from the horn of Aries may emerge. If you add the true precession of the equinoxes to this, the true place of the planet from the apparent equinox will emerge. There follows for a given time a diagram of calculation. 12 21 True precession of the equinox. 0 27 22 21 Simple equal motion of the Sun 4° 39 27 Parallax or Anomaly of the orbit. 0 39 46 33 Apogee 4 0 33 58 Equal motion of the center of the epicycle 0 52 54 Anomaly of the eccentric 2 0 19 3 Prosthaphæresis of the anomaly of the eccentric, subtract. 5 46 49 Proportional minutes 0 415 Equated Anomaly of the Eccentric 1 54 32 14 True motion of the center of the epicycle 5 55 6 5 Co-equated Anomaly of the orbit 0 45 33 22 First Prosthaphæresis of the orbit, add. 0 3 55 58 Subsequent excess 0 26 13 Part corresponding to proportional minutes 0 0 17 57 Absolute Prosthaphæresis of the orbit, add. 0 4 13 55 Therefore, the true place from the head of Aries is 5 20 0 59 And its true place from the apparent equinox is 0 26 42 20 It therefore falls into the 26th part, 42 minutes, 1,212 seconds of Aries. Calculation of Mars. 1 2 Anomaly of the orbit. 2 52 20 39 Apogee. 1 59 57 16 Equal motion of Mars 3 48 18 48 : Anomaly of the eccentric 1 48 21 32 Prosthaphæresis of the anomaly of the eccentric, subtract. 0 10 496 L Proportional minutes 1 : 29 56 Equated Anomaly of the eccentric 1 37 32 26 True motion of the center of the epicycle 3 37 29 42 Co-equated Anomaly of the orbit 3 3 9 45 Parallax of the orbit, subtract. 0 4 43 42 Subsequent excess 0 3 35 31 Part corresponding to proportional minutes, add. 0 1 47 31 Absolute parallax of the orbit, subtract. 0 6 31 13 i Distance from the head 5 3 30 58 29 And its distance from the true equinox 3 58 20 50 : Therefore, according to this calculation, it falls into the 28th part, 24 minutes, 1 second of Scorpio, approximately. Absolute parallax of the orbit, add. 17 36 7 Distance from the head 0 58 31 27 Its distance from the equinox 1 25 53 48 Therefore, it holds the 25th part, 13 minutes, 1.48 seconds of Gemini. Calculation of Mercury. 1 2 Commutation. 2 6 27 13 Simple equal motion 0 40 39 27 Apogee 3 317 21 Anomaly of the eccentric :, .. 3 9 326 Prosthaphæresis of the anomaly of the eccentric, add. 0 3 2 58 Proportional minutes 1 52 48 True motion of the epicycle 41 12 18 Co-equated Anomaly of the eccentric 3 10 4 57 Co-equated Anomaly of the orbit 2 5 54 22 Parallax of the orbit, add. 0 18 6 26 Subsequent excess 0 5 8 14 Part corresponding to proportional minutes 0 4 31 14 Absolute parallax of the orbit. 22 37 40 Distance from the head 1 3 49 58 Its distance from the equinox 1 31 12 19 • Therefore, it has the 1st part, 12 minutes, 14 seconds of Cancer, approximately, which motion differs by more than two whole parts from the calculation of the ephemerides derived from the Alphonsine tables. We could have omitted the co-equation of the anomaly of the eccentric when calculating the stars' motion in longitude. However, we have everywhere added this co-equation because, through the co-equated anomaly of the eccentric and the absolute anomaly of commutation, besides many other things to be explained elsewhere, the latitudes of the five wandering stars must also be calculated according to precepts 81 and 82 of Prutenic. Thus, in our example, the latitude is calculated in this way: Saturn's southern latitude, descending from the apogee of the eccentric, is approximately 2 parts, 4 minutes, 12,20,22 seconds. Jupiter's southern latitude, ascending, is 0 parts, 28 minutes, 12,272 seconds. Mars' southern latitude, descending, is 0 parts, 52 minutes, 12,17,22 seconds. Venus' northern latitude, ascending, is 0 parts, 39 minutes, 12,14,3 seconds. Mercury's northern latitude, descending, is 1 part, 46 minutes, 12 seconds. Finally, you will find the true places of the fixed stars, both in longitude and latitude, as follows: From their canonical description, the star's longitude from the head of the starry Aries must be extracted, and then the true precession of the vernal equinox must be added to this longitude, so that the star's longitude from the apparent equinox may emerge in parts. If we divide these parts by 30, we will easily discover in which dodecatemorion and in which part of it the star is located. For example, let us find the part of the sky in which Lampadias, called Palilicium by the Romans, is located at a given time of birth. Its longitude from the head of Aries in the constellation Taurus is noted as 36 parts. Its southern latitude is 5 parts, 10 minutes, 1 second, which never changes in fixed stars, and therefore needs no further correction. But the true precession of the equinox, found above, is 27 parts, 22 minutes, 12,21,22 seconds. Therefore, this, combined with the star's longitude, makes 63 parts, 22 minutes, 12,21,22 seconds, which, divided by 30, yields two signs with 3 parts, 22 minutes, 12,21,22 seconds. Therefore, Palilicium falls into the 3rd part, 22 minutes, 12,21,22 seconds of Gemini. Likewise, Basiliscos, that is, Regulus, has a longitude from the head of Aries of 115 parts, 50 minutes, 1 second, with a northern latitude of 0 parts, 10 minutes, 12 seconds. Adding the true precession of the equinox to the star's longitude, you will produce its distance from the apparent equinox of 143 parts, 12 minutes, 12,21,22 seconds, which, divided by 30, makes 4 dodecatemoria, with 23 parts, 12 minutes, 12,21,22 seconds remaining. Therefore, Regulus falls at the given time into the 23rd part, 12 minutes, 12,21,22 seconds of Leo. Similarly, the true longitudinal places of the other fixed stars can be calculated. Finally, when you insert the exactly calculated places of the stars into the celestial chart, consider which celestial stations complete the dodecatemoria in which the stars are located. For each star must be described in the houses whose cusps embrace the parts of the dodecatemoria in which it is located.

Dodecatemorijs, Nouenarijs, Partibus, & id genus alijs non naturalibus, fed comméticijs ſigniferipartitiõibus Ptolemæus effectus naturales adimit. Vocat enim ἀριθμὸς καὶ αναιτιολό- γητα. Et admodum eſſe debilem ſignificationem partium,ipſe quoque Alcabicius teſtatur. Expoſitis ergo ordine, vt promiſerat, omnibus neceſſarijs præceptis, quibus partium cæli naturæ,ſtellarum crafes, & aliz affectiones continentur, omniſque huius diuinæ artis varietas & vis vocum explicatur: nunc in vltima parte fuæ Iſagoges vniuerfarum partium ſeu ſortium principia,à quibus manant, & fines, in quos deſinunt, ordine & ſtritim numerat, ne aliquid quod ad elementa huius Matheſeos partis attinet, reliquiſſe alicui videatur. Primò autem geniturarum, deinde verò Reuolutionum annorum mundi partes colligit. Sed ante omnia generalé regulam tradit, cuius ductu omnis cæli thematis cunctas fortes facilè licet cognofcere. Cognitis,inquit, locis, hoc eft, principio & fine cuiuſuis ſortis, arcum ſeu gradus intermedios inquire: quibus repertis eofde ab afcendente alijſue locis quæ indicabuntur proijce, hoc eft, ab aſcendente alteriuſúe loci gradu exorſus tamdiu ſubſequentes'gradus numera, donec ſumma illa interceptorum graduum finiatur. Nam in quo illa finita fuerit, is gradus locum fortis quzſitæ oftendet. Hanç regulam declarat exemplo ſortis fortunarum, quam die à loco Solis in Lunam, noſtu verdà in, colligendam effe precipit. Verbi gratia:in diurna genitura aſcé- dat Arietis gradus nonus Sole in p.5. 8, Luna verò in 24. có- morantibus. Sià in numerando per inſequentia progref- ſus fueris, deprehendes à abeſſe gradib. 169. Iam porro ab horoſcope, hoc eft, à 9.gradu per inſequentia figna numerando progrediaris, donec ſumma 169. graduum definat:definet autem in 28.mp, qui partis fortunarum locus erit. Proinde & in eodem ſcrupulo cœlicommorantibus, fortem fortunarum horoſcopus fortietur. Sit rurfus nocturna genitura, in qua loca luminarium eundemque gradum 9. Ex his verbis etiam cauſa patet, ob quam parti fortunarum effectio meritò à Ptolemeo tribuitur, quia videlicet ipfa fit quafi lunaris horofcopus, atque quo radio O, qui εſt fons vitalis, potentię horoſcopum illuminat, co quoque vel ſimili radio) fons potentiæ naturalis illuſtrat partem fortunarum: quæ res externas externaq; accidentia ſignificat, vnde & pars fortune dita eſt. Hæceſt ratio ſortis, quæ in alijs planetis nullum locum habere poteſt, quia nec luminaria funt, nec lumen Solis reflectunt.
