translation: The Part of Honey from the Moon to the Sun. The Part of Rice (which is a kind of grain) from Jupiter to Saturn. The Part of Olives from Mercury to the Moon. The Part of Grapes from Saturn to Venus. The Part of Cotton from Mercury to Venus. The Part of Alonica (a kind of white seed, similar to flaxseed, used by doctors to make a useful ointment) from Mercury to Jupiter, or from Saturn to Venus. The Part of Melons (which are large and yellow) from Mercury to Saturn. The Part of Sour Foods from Saturn to Mars. The Part of Sweet Foods from the Sun to Venus. The Part of Foods with the taste of celery or similar herbs from Mars to Saturn. The Part of Bitter Foods from Mercury to Saturn. The Part of Sharp Medicines from Saturn to Jupiter. The Part of Salty Medicines from Mars to the Moon. The Part of Poisonous Medicines from Genzahar (that is, from the Head of the Dragon) to Saturn. And all these are to be cast from the Ascendant of the year's revolution. These are all the Parts that have come down to us. We have also introduced these very last Parts, even if their narration is weak, so that we might not omit anything that could be introductory to the mastery of astrological judgments. Friends: Table of friendship and enmity of the signs. 447 2 x II Enemies. Digitized by Google. FIFTH. 149. ON THE FIFTH DIFFERENCE. Ptolemy removes natural effects from dodecatemoria, novenaries, Parts, and other such non-natural but artificial divisions of the zodiac. For he calls them 'arithmos kai anaitiologeta' (numbers and uncaused). And Alcabitius himself testifies that the signification of the Parts is very weak. Having therefore set forth in order, as he promised, all the necessary precepts by which the natures of the parts of the sky, the temperaments of the stars, and other affections are contained, and all the variety and force of the terms of this divine art are explained: now in the last part of his Isagoge, he enumerates in order and strictly the principles of all the Parts or Lots, from which they originate, and the ends into which they terminate, lest it seem to anyone that he has left anything pertaining to the elements of this part of Mathematics. First, he collects the Parts of nativities, and then those of the Revolutions of the years of the world. But before all else, he delivers a general rule, by the guidance of which one can easily know all the Lots of any celestial chart. Having known, he says, the places, that is, the beginning and end of any Lot, inquire into the arc or intermediate degrees: having found these, cast them from the Ascendant or other places that will be indicated, that is, starting from the Ascendant or the degree of another place, count the subsequent degrees until that sum of intercepted degrees is finished. For in whatever degree it finishes, that degree will show the place of the sought Lot. He explains this rule with the example of the Part of Fortune, which he instructs to be collected by day from the place of the Sun to the Moon, but by night from the Moon to the Sun. For example: in a diurnal nativity, let the ninth degree of Aries ascend, with the Sun in 8 degrees 54 minutes of Leo, and the Moon in 24 degrees of Capricorn. If you proceed by counting through the subsequent signs from the Sun to the Moon, you will find that the Moon is 169 degrees distant from the Sun. Now, proceed from the horoscope, that is, from the 9th degree of Aries, counting through the subsequent signs until the sum of 169 degrees ends: it will end in 28 degrees of Virgo, which will be the place of the Part of Fortune. Therefore, if the luminaries are in the same degree of the sky, the horoscope will share the Part of Fortune. Let there be again a nocturnal nativity, in which we imagine the places of the luminaries and the same 9th degree of Aries to ascend. If you now proceed from the Moon to the Sun through the succeeding signs, you will find the Moon to be 131 degrees distant from the Sun, which if you extend from the Ascendant counting through the subsequent signs, you will find the place of the Part of Fortune to be in the 20th degree of Libra. Digitized by Google. 470. DIFFERENCE. So that this calculation of Parts may be easier and more expeditious, resolve the places from which and to which the Parts extend, such as the places of the seven planets, the Ascendant, the second, eighth, ninth, midheaven, twelfth houses; also the places of the Part of Fortune and the Part of Spirit, and the Sun and Moon, which immediately preceded the figure, into numbers of signs, degrees, and minutes: having done this, subtract the place from which the author instructs the progression to begin from the place to which it is to be continued, accommodating a full revolution or 12 signs, as often as the subtraction cannot be performed, and add what remains to the signs, degrees, and minutes of the place from which it is to be cast, so that the true place of the sought Lot may emerge. In this way, we will subtract the place of the Sun from the place of the Moon in diurnal nativities, and what remains of the signs, degrees, and minutes, we will add to the Ascendant, and we will produce the true place of the Part of Fortune. As in our example of a diurnal nativity, the place of the Ascendant is 6 signs, 26 degrees, 44 minutes. The place of the Sun in 8 degrees, 54 minutes of Leo, resolved, makes 2 signs, 8 degrees, 54 minutes. The place of the Moon in 27 degrees, 52 minutes of Capricorn, resolved, makes 10 signs, 27 degrees, 52 minutes. From these, the motion subtracted leaves 8 signs, 18 degrees, 58 minutes, which joined to the eastern cardinal point, which has 6 signs, 26 degrees, 44 minutes, produce 3 signs, 15 degrees, 42 minutes, and demonstrate the place of the Part of Fortune to fall in 15 degrees, 42 minutes of Cancer. Conversely, if it had been a nocturnal genesis, then the place of the Moon, that is, 10 signs, 27 degrees, 52 minutes, would have to be subtracted from the place of the Sun, that is, 2 signs, 8 degrees, 54 minutes: which done, there would remain 3 signs, 11 degrees, 2 minutes, which joined with the horoscope, that is, 6 signs, 26 degrees, 44 minutes, make 10 signs, 7 degrees, 48 minutes, and project the place of the Part of Fortune into 7 degrees, 48 minutes of Aquarius. Ptolemy instructs that the place of the Part of Fortune is always to be collected by day and night from the Sun to the Moon. His words in Book 3, Chapter 'Of the Times of Life', are these: "The Lot of Fortune, the number collected from the degrees, both by day and by night, from the Sun to the Moon, and subtracting the same from the horoscope according to the succeeding signs of the zodiac, where the number falls, that degree and dodecatemoria, and that place, we say, holds the Lot of Fortune, so that it has the same ratio and configuration to the ascending horizon as the Moon has to the Lot of Fortune, and is as it were a lunar horoscope." From these words, the reason is also clear why the Part of Fortune is deservedly attributed an effect by Ptolemy, because, namely, it is as it were a lunar horoscope, and with the same or a similar ray with which the Sun, which is the vital source, illuminates the horoscope, so also the Moon, the source of natural power, illuminates the Part of Fortune: which signifies external things and external accidents, whence it is also called the Part of Fortune. This is the reason for the Lot, which can have no place in other planets, because they are neither luminaries nor do they reflect the light of the Sun. Therefore, in the examination of Lots, it seemed to me neither necessary nor useful to dwell here any longer. Glory to God. Errors are thus corrected. Fol. 3, line 32, read 'tosanikō'. Fol. 4, line 3, read 23. 1. Fol. 5, line 20, read 'Lioti'. Fol. 6, line 17, 'trigono, sexagono': and line 19, 'seorsim'. Fol. 8, last line, 'sex sunt'. Fol. 12, line 34, for 'dextra' read 'sinistra', and subsequently for 'sinistra', 'dextra'. Fol. 13, line 17, 'quinque'; and line 30, for 'uni', read 'uno'. Fol. 15, line 2, 'dilaniat': and line 29, 'hēgemonika', and subsequently 'Asterismi'. Fol. 16, line 6, read 'denu quater': and line 11, 'Cassiope': and 33, read 'rota'. Fol. 17, where the characters of the spaces of the sky are explained, for these numerical notes 1.2.3.4. placed to the right and to the left, read 12.22.34.48. And line 29, read 'hypo gōn'. Fol. 20, line 29, these words (capite de signis competentib.) should be omitted or transferred to the margin. Fol. 21, line 28, 'synezetazomenōn'. Fol. 26, line 13, for the character of Aquarius, read Capricorn. Fol. 27, line 19, read 'Lunae interuentu Solem occultari'. Fol. 28, line 12, 'quae est 100. ad 1'. Fol. 35, line 28, 'veluti Mars in Capric.': and line 31, 'leuiusculu'. Fol. 38, line 6, 'hypsōma': and line 31, for the sign of Leo, read Cancer. Fol. 41, in the scheme of dignities under the title Detrimenta, from the rule of the sign Capricorn for Saturn, read Luna. And there under the title Casus, the character of Saturn is to be depressed in one place, and placed from the rule of the sign Aries. Fol. 52, the scheme of aspects will be completed by describing the opposite sign next to the letter F, and by drawing triangular radiations C.R. and E.B. For example, read 'qui p quae'. Fol. 53, line 27, read 'minus'. Fol. 55, line 12, 'a sexu mascul. & foeminino'. Fol. 56, line 26, 'pponendi': and line 33, read 'partilis'. Fol. 59, line 6, 'iokratōra'.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Powers and House Meanings
text: ==Start of OCR for page 5== PRIMA. 177 ſint digna, niſi apud borealiores. Secundò conſideramus non lumen domibus proprium, ſed vires quas ſtellar in ſingu. lis ſtationibus conſequuntur, de quibus ſcripſit Ptolemæus in fine Apoteleſmatici quarti. Vult autem eo in loco, ſicut ſuprà annotauimus, dijudicari poteſtatem atq; qualitatem ſtellarum ex his quatuor. Primò ἐκ τ' ἰδίας τὴν αὐτὴν ἀς δρωμ φυσικῆς ἰ διοβοσίας, hoc eſt, ex propria cuiufq; ſtellæ & inſita natura. Secundò ἐκ τ ἰδιοβοπίας την ποριεχόντωμ δωδεκατημορίωμ id eſt, ex inſita natura dodecatemoriorum in quibus verſantur. Tertiò ἐκ τῆς φυσικῆς ἰδιοζοπίας τῇ πρὸς τομ ο χηματισμῶμ, hoc eſt, ex configuratione ad, an videlicet fint orientales vel occidentales, item an ſint veloces vel tardi curſu, quod quidem accidens nos ſemper ad proprios ipforum orbes quàm ad Solem retulimus. Quarto cκ τ φυσικῆς ἰδιοτροπίας τοῦ πρὸς τὰς γωνίας χηματισμῶp,hoc eſt, ex natura configurationum ad angulos. De tribus prioribus modis poteſtatum alibi dicetur.Solus autem quartus modus ad præſentis loci declarationem facit, & de eo iudicandum præcepit Ptolemæus his verbis: ποιόμ έκαςο την ας βρωμέκ τε πῶς ἔχειμ πρὸς τὸμ ὁρίζοντα στπες πέομμεσγρανόντου μεν γαρ ἢ ἐπιφερόμενοι του μεσορανήμαλιμάς λισα εἰσὶ δυναμικοί, δεύτερου 5, ὅταμ ι' αυτό το ὁρίζοντο ὠσικές παναφόρονται, καὶ μᾶλλομ ὅταμἐπὶ τὸ ἀναβλικό: ἦλομ δὲ ὅα ταμὑπὸ γῆμμεσγρανῶσιμ ἢ ἄλλως συληματίζωνη του ἀνατέλα λονι τόπω. μὲ ὅτω δὲ ἔχοντος, ἀδιύαμοι παντελῶς τυγχάνεσι. hoc eſt, Habitudines ſeu vires vniufcuiuſque ſtellarum ex po- ſitu ſuo, ſeu potius reſpectu erga finitorem, ad hunc modum ex quirendæ ſunt.Nam in medio cœlo, aut in ſuccedenti illi loco, maximè ſunt potentes. Secundum ſtatum potentiæ habent in finitore orientali & loco illi ſuccedente. Verùm tamé plus poffunt in finitore, minùs verò in imo cœlo, aut aliter finitori orientali configurato loco. Sed extra hæc omnia reperti, imbecilles &prorfus infirmi habétur. Quæ verba Cardanus ita expo nit. Primum locum obtinet cor cæli, inde vndecima ſeu prima &ſuccedens ſeu ſecunda, inde ſeptima, poſt octaua. Videtur autem, inquit,tribuere vires imo cœli & quinto loco & nono.Mihi verò id non ſolùm videtur, ſed & pro certo ſtatuo, ſiquidem Z id ipſum ==Start of OCR for page 1== 178 DIFFERENTIA id ipſum verba ſonant. Loca verò ab orientali horizonte exclu ſa intelligit locum duodecimum, ſextum & tertium quoq;, pro pterea quòd fit infra terram & cadens. Sunt etiam ab aſcenden te excluſa locus duodecimus & octauus: fed iſti per ſe vt dicta eſt, propter copiam luminis vim habent, etiamſi aſcédenti non configurentur.Imò verò octauus poteſtate ſemper nonum fuperat, pręterquam in vitę caufa, in qua apud Ptolemeum, vt ſuo loco videbimus, nonus precedit octauum, quòd in trigona radiatione aſcendentis fit & ſublimis,&Sol ibi exiſtens maximos calores efficiat. Poſt hos ordinat Cardanus locum ſextum, inde quartum, qui etſi nullam vim habeat per ſe, vt ſuprà dictu eſt:planetæ tamen ibi conſtituti, cùm omnes cardines radijs va lidilsimis illuminent, magnas adipiſcuntur vires. Ex his intelligi poteſt, locum quartum cùm omni luce deſtituatur, per ſe quidem eſſe imbecilliorem duodecimo vel ſexto, ſed eſſe robuftioré ijſdem ſi ad planetas fiat collatio,&c. Tertiò confiderantur loca cæli, prout familiaritate & συμπαθείᾳ quadam coniunguntur cum rebus inferioribus. Nam quemadmodum in eſſentiali ſigniferi ſeu potius totius cæli ſubſtantiali diſtributione dodecatemorion Arietis Germaniæ, Leonem Italiæ, &c.& Saturnum ſpleni, Venerem genitalibus, Mercurium lin guæ,&c.peculiariter conciliari diximus:Ita in hac fortuita quo que ſigniferi partitione certa loca certis rebus inferioribus fa. miliaritate quadam peculiari conſentire dicimus. Exempli gra tia, vitæ fauent tantum loca lumine illuſtria, vt infrà videbitur, nempe locus decimus, primus, ſeptimus, vndecimus & nonus. Nam etiamſi locus duodecimus & octauus ſint in lumine & vitæ fignificent, non tamen tantam vim planetæ ibi conſtituto communicare poſsunt, vt vitam geniti moderari queat, multò minùs herclè id ipſum locus ſecundus præſtare poterit, cùmta men in alias cauſas præcipuam vim & poteſtatem exerceat: veluti ratione diuitiarum ſecundus locus, non tantùm octauo& duodecimo, ſed & omnibus alijs præfertur. Et idem in honoribus potior eſt ceteris, pręter angulos & vndecimam ac nonam. Similiter reliquarum domorum omnium ſingulæ ſingulas res in corpore vel animo hærentes, vel aliunde accedentes reſpiciunt, Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 1== ==Start of OCR for page 2== コ Π E い ! = کے ک • PRIMA. 179 ciunt, atq; ob coſenſum peculiariter afficiunt. Cæterùm in harum rerum ad domicilia cœli accommodatione, Autor & reliqui Arabes per omnia Ptolemæo non conſentiunt, ſed in plerifq; diſſentiunt.Exempli gratia, ſtatum patris dijudicat Ptolemæus principaliter à poſitu &, matris verò à poſitu) & f. Contrà Arabes de rebus patris principaliter conijciunt ex domo quarta & domino eius. Huic deinde adiiciunt in diurna genitura conſiderationem Solis, in nocturna verò conſideracionem Saturni, & præter hæc planetam vel planetas in quarta domo poſitos. Item fortem patris & dominum eius contempla ripræcipiunt. Similiter de rebus matris principales coniecturas ſumuntà medio cœli & domino cius. Hinca (vel & habita ratione diurnæ vel nocturnæ conditionis. Denique à planetis decimam domum occupantibus, & à ſorte matris eiufq; gu bernatoribus: qualia loca omnia in tractatione de parentibus Ptolemæus contempfit,eorumque nullam mentionem fecit, & totam hanc coniecturam in luminaria Saturnum & Venerem tranftulit.Sanè Ptolemæus Apoteleſmatico tertio capite wi διδυμογόνωμ ſcripſit, Geminos vel plures naſci, ſi luminaria & horoſcopus duum corporű ſigna poſsideant, imò verò ſi cum luminaribus non horoſcopiangulus, ſed eius loco medij cæli culmen in bicorpora ſigna incidant, tales matres plerunque ge minos aut plures edere. Ex quo dicto Ptolemai ſequi videtur, quòd decimam domum voluerit eſſe matris. Hinc ergo imum medium cœli patriadiudicaſſe videntur. Id verò non ſatis reAd:neque enim fi decima matri conueniat, ſtatim conſequitur quartam ſub terra conuenire patri. Ad hæc cùm in dicto loco Ptolemæus de filiorum numero conijciat, longè rectius ex tali cauſa decima domus filijs quàm matri accommodabitur, præ- ſertim cùm ipſe Apoteleſmatico quarto decimum locum filijs attribuerit: in tractatione verò de matre loci decimi nullam lijs. mentionem fecerit. Similiter reliquis domibus Arabes plæ- raque præter ſententiam Ptolemæi, & multa etiam ad vanas quæſtiones pertinentia adſcripferunt, quorum commemoratione atque ad Ptolemaicam doctrinam collationem breuitatis gratia iam omittemus,atque ſaltem exemplo declarabımus, Z2 quemadeap. de fo Digitized by Google ==End of OCR for page 2==
translation: 177 are worthy, except among the more northerly. Secondly, we consider not the light proper to the houses, but the powers that the stars acquire in their individual stations, about which Ptolemy wrote at the end of the fourth book of his Apotelesmatics. In that place, as we noted above, he wants the power and quality of the stars to be judged from these four things. First, from the star's own inherent nature. Secondly, from the inherent nature of the dodecatemoria in which they are located. Thirdly, from the natural disposition of their configuration, that is, whether they are oriental or occidental, and whether they are swift or slow in their course, which accident we have always referred to their own orbits rather than to the Sun. Fourthly, from the natural disposition of their configurations to the angles. The three former modes of powers will be discussed elsewhere. Only the fourth mode contributes to the explanation of the present topic, and Ptolemy commanded judgment concerning it in these words: "The qualities of each star are to be sought from its position, or rather its relation to the horizon, in this manner. For in the midheaven, or in the place succeeding it, they are most powerful. They have a second degree of power in the eastern horizon and the place succeeding it. But they are more powerful in the horizon, and less so in the imum coeli, or in a place otherwise configured to the eastern horizon. But found outside all these, they are considered weak and utterly infirm." Cardan thus explains these words: "The first place is occupied by the heart of the sky, then the eleventh or first and succeeding or second, then the seventh, then the eighth." He seems, he says, to attribute powers to the imum coeli and the fifth and ninth places. To me, however, this not only seems so, but I also state it for certain, since the words themselves mean the same thing. He understands the places excluded from the eastern horizon to be the twelfth, sixth, and also the third, because it is below the earth and cadent. The twelfth and eighth places are also excluded from the ascendant: but these, as has been said, have power by themselves due to the abundance of light, even if they are not configured to the ascendant. Indeed, the eighth always surpasses the ninth in power, except in the matter of life, in which, according to Ptolemy, as we shall see in its proper place, the ninth precedes the eighth, because it is in a trine radiation of the ascendant and is sublime, and the Sun existing there produces the greatest heat. After these, Cardan orders the sixth place, then the fourth, which, although it has no power by itself, as stated above, yet the planets constituted there, when they illuminate all the cardines with very strong rays, acquire great powers. From this it can be understood that the fourth place, being deprived of all light, is indeed weaker by itself than the twelfth or sixth, but is stronger than them if a comparison is made to the planets, etc. Thirdly, the places of the sky are considered as they are connected by a certain familiarity and sympathy with inferior things. For just as in the essential distribution of the zodiac, or rather of the entire sky, we said that the dodecatemorion of Aries is particularly associated with Germany, Leo with Italy, etc., and Saturn with the spleen, Venus with the genitals, Mercury with the tongue, etc., so in this fortuitous partition of the zodiac, we say that certain places agree with certain inferior things by a peculiar familiarity. For example, only places illuminated by light favor life, as will be seen below, namely the tenth, first, seventh, eleventh, and ninth places. For even if the twelfth and eighth places are in light and signify life, they cannot communicate so much power to a planet constituted there that it can moderate the life of the native, much less, by Hercules, will the second place be able to accomplish this, although it exercises principal power and authority in other matters: as in the matter of riches, the second place is preferred not only to the eighth and twelfth, but also to all others. And the same is more potent in honors than the others, except for the angles and the eleventh and ninth. Similarly, each of the remaining houses respects individual things inherent in the body or mind, or coming from elsewhere, and affects them peculiarly by consent. Moreover, in the accommodation of these things to the domiciles of the sky, the Author and the other Arabs do not agree with Ptolemy in all things, but dissent in many. For example, Ptolemy judges the status of the father principally from the position of the Sun, and that of the mother from the position of Venus. On the contrary, the Arabs principally infer matters concerning the father from the fourth house and its lord. To this they then add, in a diurnal nativity, the consideration of the Sun, and in a nocturnal nativity, the consideration of Saturn, and besides these, the planet or planets placed in the fourth house. Likewise, they command the Part of Fortune of the father and its lord to be contemplated. Similarly, they take their principal conjectures concerning the mother's affairs from the midheaven and its lord. Hence, also taking into account the diurnal or nocturnal condition. Finally, from the planets occupying the tenth house, and from the Part of Fortune of the mother and its rulers: all such places Ptolemy scorned in his treatise on parents, and made no mention of them, and transferred this entire conjecture to the luminaries, Saturn, and Venus. Indeed, Ptolemy wrote in the third book of his Apotelesmatics, chapter on twins, that twins or more are born if the luminaries and the ascendant possess bicorporeal signs, or indeed if, with the luminaries, not the angle of the ascendant, but in its place the culmination of the midheaven falls into bicorporeal signs, such mothers usually give birth to twins or more. From this statement of Ptolemy, it seems to follow that he wanted the tenth house to be that of the mother. Hence, they seem to have judged the imum coeli to belong to the father. But this is not sufficiently clear: for if the tenth belongs to the mother, it does not immediately follow that the fourth, below the earth, belongs to the father. To this, since Ptolemy in the said place conjectures about the number of children, the tenth house will be much more rightly accommodated to children than to the mother for such a reason, especially since he himself in the fourth book of the Apotelesmatics attributed the tenth place to children: but in the treatise on the mother, he made no mention of the tenth place. Similarly, the Arabs ascribed many things to the other houses contrary to Ptolemy's opinion, and many things also pertaining to vain questions, the commemoration of which and comparison to Ptolemaic doctrine we will now omit for the sake of brevity, and will at least explain by example, as in the chapter on children.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Dignities and Almuten
text: 192 DIFFERENTIA eilij: Saturnus verò, quia ratione ſuę exaltationis quatuor ha bet ibidem poteſtates, & ratione triangulitres: denique ratio, ne finium duas,in ſummanouem poteſtates colligit,atque hac prerogatiua non tantum Venerem, fed & omnes reliquos pla netas ſuperat. Eſt igitur cardinis exorientis in hoc exemplo κύριο Γευ οἰκοδεσπότης Venus,& επικρατήτωρ Saturnus, qui idem autori dominator rei erit precipuus, ſi gradus iſte in quo planeta ἐπικράτησιμ obtinet, ad domum que ſignificat rem de qua quęritur, pertineat, prefertim ſi inſuper eandem quoque epicrateſin in locis alijs eandem rem ſignificantibus obtineat. Quicunque enim planeta in locis alicuius rei omnium ſignificatorum maiorem numerum dignitatum fimul collectarum obtinuerit, is dominator rei principalis & Almutes ſignificatorum ab Arabibus dicitur. Participem autem in ſignificatione autor facit illum planetam, qui naturam rei de quaagitur,continet. Quamobrem ſi (vt exéplo autoris vtamur) in cufpidem domus ſecundę geniturę diurne incidat pars 5. Arietis, & pars ſubſtantię cadat in p. 10. Leonis,& pars fortune deniq; incidat in par. 6. Sagittarii:tunc Sol, quòd in cufpide eiufdem fecunda. domus exaltationem atque ius trianguli habeat, in loco partis, ſubſtantię verò domum & trigonum: & denique in partis fortunæ loco trigonum obtineat, multitudine ac ſumna teftimoniorum,quę in di&is locis colligit, omnes reliquos planetas ſuperans, erit ſubſtantie ſignificator precipuus, & eius partiteps in fignificatione Iuppiter, qui fua natura ſubſtantiam & lucra licita fignificat. Sic fignificator regni eſt, qui plures potio refúe dignitates in medio cœli aliifque locis idem ſignificanti bus habet:confignificator autem eius Sol, quòd fua natura regnum fignificet. Nam res ſingulę in natura, vt fupra oftenfum eſt, non tantùm lignis zodiaci & domiciliis cæleſtibus ſignificantur, fed etiam fingulis planetis diſtribuuntur. Hocmodo Sa turnus regit patres, ſenes, monachos, agriculturam, the ſauros abfconditos, quęque longis itineribus & cum difficultate acquiruntur. Iuppiter autem habet religiofos prelatos, reges & duces, ſubſtantiam &lucra: Mars tonſores, chirurgos, medicos, lictores, carnifices, macellarios, furnarios, piſtores, milites, qui paſsim mauortii dicuntur. Similiterque alig ſtelle ſua in his aliifque ==Start of OCR for page 1== P PRIMA. 1 alijfq; cópluribus perfonis &rebus fignificata habétsprout her ab autore differentia ſequenti deſcribunt. Verum pleriq domi natore, ſeu vt Arabes vocant, Almutes colligunt, no tantű ex do minio in loca ſigniferi, familiaritate cõiuncta generi queſtióis, verú etiá his adiungunt dominia locorű, in quibus coſignifica tores verſant. In his locis omnib numero ſuffragiorű reliquos planetas ſuperates vocăt ſignificatorű alicuius rei Almutes. Ve luti, exépli gratia, ſi ſit Almutes ſignificatorum ſubſtantie ſen opum inueftigandus, confideranda erunt hæc loca ſigniferi, Locus ſecundæ domus, & locus domini eiuſdem:locus.con- ſignificatoris opum naturalis, qui ſemper eſt luppiter: locus partis fortunæ,& locus domini eiufdem: locus partis ſubſtantiz, & locus domini eiuſdem:denique tria loca triagulatorum domus fecundæ:In his locis omnibus planetam multitudine te ſtimoniorum reliquos omnes excellentem Almutes ſignificatorum ſubſtantiæ vocant,eiq; adiunguntalium in ſignificatione participem, qui etſi ſit robore inferior, præ cæteris tamen omnibus numero teſtimoniorum proximè ad Almutes accedit.Sed nos hicapprobamus methodum Ptolemæi, cuius in ſe cunda differentia oportuno in loco mentionem faciemus,cùm in expoſitione huius loci fatis prolixi fuerimus. DE POTESTATIBVS ACCIDENTALI bus planetarum. Ex poteftatibus quoq; planetarū accidētalibus eft Alhaiz.i. fimilitudo. Et hoc cũ fuerit planeta diurnus in die fupra terra, & in nocte fub terra: & planeta nocture nus in nocte ſuper terram, & in die ſub terra, et cũ hoc fi fuerit masculinus planeta in ſigno masculino, et plane ta fæmininus in ſigno fœminino, dicitur eſse in fua fimili tudine.i.in ſuo Alhaiz. Et erit fortitudo eiusvt viri forti tudo in loco eius profectus.i. acquifitionis atq; fortune. Caput vltimum Autoris, quod inſcribitur: De poteſtatibus planetarum accidentalibus, non eſt principium narrationis de viribus ſtellarum aduenticijs, cùm ea quoque de ſtellarum in ſtatiq- вь Digitized by Google ==Start of OCR for page 2== 1 194 DIFFERENTIA PRIMA.. 1 ſtatiõibus cœli vario poſitu q dita ſunt, ad poteſtates planetarú accidentales re&è referantur: ſed eſt potiùs huiufmodi narrationis continuatio, id quod verba ipſa quoque ſonant.Ex po teſtatibus quoque, inquit, planetarum accidentalibus eſt Haiz, quaſi dicat, ad alias poteſtates ſtellarum accidentales ſuprà ditas, refertur etiam illa ſtellarum proprietas que tempori con- ſentit. Nam ſtella tempori conſentiens Arabibus Haiz dicitur. Exempli gratia: Duo loca quorum alter eft Solis, alter Saturni, dicuntur patri familiares, ſeu patris fignificatores. Si itaque ſit diurna genitura, τ' αἱρέσεως παβικός τόπο, id eſt, locus tempori congruens, ſeu Haiz, erit Sol:ſi verò nocturua, Saturnus. Dicit planetam trifariam in ſuo Haiz, ſeu in ſua ſimilitudinereperiri. Primò ratione ſexus ſignorum, dum videlicet planeta maſculinus fuerit in ſigno maſculino, & fœmineus in figno fæ minino. Secundò ratione quartarum fœmininarű vel maſculinarum. Tertiò iuxta temperaturarum temporis & ftellará con- ſentientiam. Sed quòd in nocte planetæ diurni ſitum fub terra prefert pofitu eiuſdem ſupra terram:& quòd in die planetæ no Aurni ſitu ſub terra præfert poſitu eiufdé ſupra terra, omni ratione caret, & repugnat veriſsimæ ſententiæ Ptolemai, qui vni uerfaliter ſtellas, tam diurnas quàm nocturnas ſupra terra collocatas ſemper efficaciores eſſe voluit, ita vt etiam ftellæ diurnanocturno tempore ſupra terram conſtitutæ maiorem vim accipiant, quam ſi eodem tempore per inferius hemiſphærium ferantur. Vnumquodque enim fidus gaudet ſuo ſimili, quan tum ſcilicetattinet ad naturam ipſi congruentem, vtnocturnz ſtellæ noctis, diurnæ dici conſtitutione rectius temperantur. Itaque diurnus de die melior erit quàm de nocte, & nocturnus cõueniétiorem temperatura conſequit de nocte quàm de die. Talis autem temperaturarŭ συμπάθļa vel augmentatio ex moraſtella ſupra vel infra terrá exoriri non poteft. Omnes enim loco fupra terra ſemper gaudet, atq; in eo fiunt meliores & vali diores. Imò nec illud, quod dicit, maſculinos planetas in ſignis & quartis masculinis: fœmininos verd in ſignis & quartis fæmi ninis robur aſſumere, veritati coſentaneű eſt, cú omnes tā ratio one Solis a mudi & fignorű locis maſculinis debilitétur effici anturg; fortiores. Finis Differentiæ prima. DIFF Digitized by Google
translation: DIFFERENCE 192: Saturn, however, because of the reason of his exaltation, has four powers there, and by reason of his triplicity, three; finally, by reason of his terms, two, collecting in total nine powers, and by this prerogative he surpasses not only Venus but also all other planets. Therefore, in this example, the Lord of the Ascendant is Venus, and the Ruler is Saturn, who will be the principal dominator of the matter for the author, if that degree in which the planet obtains rulership pertains to the house that signifies the matter in question, especially if it also obtains the same rulership in other places signifying the same matter. For whatever planet obtains the greatest number of dignities collected together in the places of all the significators of a certain matter, is called the principal dominator of the matter and the Almuten of the significators by the Arabs. The author makes that planet a participant in the signification which contains the nature of the matter in question. Therefore, if (to use the author's example) the Part of Aries 5 falls on the cusp of the second house of a diurnal nativity, and the Part of Substance falls on Leo 10, and finally the Part of Fortune falls on Sagittarius 6: then the Sun, because it has exaltation and the right of triplicity on the cusp of the same second house, in the place of the Part of Substance, and indeed the house and trigon; and finally obtains the trigon in the place of the Part of Fortune, surpassing all other planets by the multitude and sum of testimonies it collects in the said places, will be the principal significator of substance, and its participant in signification will be Jupiter, who by his nature signifies substance and lawful gains. Thus, the significator of a kingdom is he who has more powers or dignities in the Midheaven and other places signifying the same thing; its co-significator is the Sun, because by its nature it signifies a kingdom. For individual things in nature, as shown above, are signified not only by the signs of the zodiac and celestial domiciles, but are also distributed to individual planets. In this way, Saturn rules fathers, old men, monks, agriculture, hidden treasures, and things acquired through long journeys and with difficulty. Jupiter, however, has religious prelates, kings and leaders, substance and gains: Mars has barbers, surgeons, doctors, lictors, executioners, butchers, bakers, soldiers, who are commonly called martial. Similarly, other stars have their significations in these and many other persons and things, as described by the author in the following difference. But most collect the dominator, or as the Arabs call it, the Almuten, not only from dominion in the places of the zodiac, familiarity connected to the type of question, but also they add to these the dominions of the places in which the co-significators are found. In all these places, they call the planet that surpasses the other planets in the number of votes the Almuten of the significators of a certain matter. For example, if the Almuten of the significators of substance or wealth is to be investigated, these places of the zodiac must be considered: the place of the second house, and the place of its lord; the place of the natural co-significator of wealth, which is always Jupiter; the place of the Part of Fortune, and the place of its lord; the place of the Part of Substance, and the place of its lord; finally, the three places of the triplicities of the second house. In all these places, they call the planet that excels all others in the multitude of testimonies the Almuten of the significators of substance, and they add to it as a participant in signification another planet which, although inferior in strength, approaches the Almuten most closely in the number of testimonies among all the others. But here we approve the method of Ptolemy, of which we will make mention in the second difference at an opportune place, since we have been sufficiently prolix in the exposition of this place. CONCERNING THE ACCIDENTAL POWERS of the planets. Among the accidental powers of the planets is also Alhaiz, that is, similarity. And this is when a diurnal planet is above the earth by day, and below the earth by night; and a nocturnal planet is above the earth by night, and below the earth by day, and with this, if a masculine planet is in a masculine sign, and a feminine planet in a feminine sign, it is said to be in its similarity, that is, in its Alhaiz. And its strength will be like the strength of a man in the place of his progress, that is, of acquisition and fortune. The last chapter of the Author, which is entitled: On the accidental powers of the planets, is not the beginning of the narration concerning the adventitious strengths of the stars, since those things which are said concerning the stars in their various positions in the sky are also rightly referred to the accidental powers of the planets: but it is rather a continuation of such a narration, which the words themselves also indicate. Among the accidental powers of the planets, he says, is Haiz, as if to say, to the other accidental powers of the stars mentioned above, that property of the stars which agrees with the time is also referred. For a star agreeing with the time is called Haiz by the Arabs. For example: Two places, one of which is of the Sun, the other of Saturn, are called family fathers, or significators of the father. If, therefore, it is a diurnal nativity, the place congruent with the time, or Haiz, will be the Sun; but if nocturnal, Saturn. He says that a planet is found in its Haiz, or in its similarity, in three ways. First, by reason of the sex of the signs, namely, when a masculine planet is in a masculine sign, and a feminine planet in a feminine sign. Secondly, by reason of the feminine or masculine quarters. Thirdly, according to the agreement of the temperatures of the time and the stars. But that in the night diurnal planets prefer a position below the earth to a position above the earth; and that in the day nocturnal planets prefer a position below the earth to a position above the earth, lacks all reason, and contradicts the most true opinion of Ptolemy, who universally wished stars, both diurnal and nocturnal, placed above the earth to always be more efficacious, so that even diurnal stars constituted above the earth during nocturnal time receive greater strength than if they are carried through the lower hemisphere at the same time. For every star rejoices in its like, as far as it pertains to its congruent nature, so that nocturnal stars are better tempered by the night, and diurnal stars by the day. Therefore, a diurnal planet will be better by day than by night, and a nocturnal planet obtains a more convenient temperament by night than by day. Such a sympathy or augmentation of temperatures cannot arise from the star's stay above or below the earth. For all rejoice in the place above the earth, and in it they become better and stronger. Indeed, neither is it consonant with truth what he says, that masculine planets assume strength in masculine signs and quarters: and feminine planets in feminine signs and quarters, since all are weakened and made stronger by the Sun and the masculine places of the signs. End of the First Difference.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Fixed Stars in Aries and Taurus

Quòd fi itaque πλεί : δωμ Digitized by Google i SECVNDA. 199 Sap manipulus ſtellarum hora geniture cum horoscopaue rit,natum & magnum & clarum faciet, præfertim fi eas 4 ir radiauerit: tum enim principibus gratus futurus eſt, exerci tumque ducet, fed tamen denique offenſo principe mala morte peribit.Stellæ enim fixæ infignes habent ſignificationes fortunę in genituris hominum, fiquidem in cardinibus & pręcipuè in horoſcopo aut M C. cum aut), aut cum parte fortunarum partiliter repertæ maximam fœlicitatem decernunt, ſed plerunque cum exitu calamitoſo. Quapropter ſtellarum fixa rum grandia dona, neque ſatis ſtabilia eſſe, neque fatis fidelia, niſi ſuffragio errantium ſtellarum confirmentur, monet Her. mes enunciato 29.οι ἀπλανεῖς ἀς έρες πέχοσιμ ευτυχίας αλός γος καὶ πραδόξος, ἀλλ' ὡς ἐπιτοπλᾶςομ ἐπισφραγίζοσι ταύτας δυσυχίαις, εἰ μὴ καὶ οἱ πλανήτες τῇ ἐυτυχία στ'αδοσιμ, hoceſt: ſtellę fixę irrationabiles atque admirabiles fœlicitates adferunt, quas tamen plerunque calamitatibus infigniunt, nifi & planetę ad fœlicitatem conueniant. Porrò vt de Pleiadibus vtpote notioribus ſtellis plura referam, ex quamuis ſunt beneficę, tamen qui ipſas ortus fui tempore in occiduo cardine à maleficis percuffas habuerit, naufragus morietur. quòd fi ita con- ſtitutas beneficę ftellæ intuebuntur, nihilominus nato repentinam mortem ex intemperantia & pituitę affluxu minabuntur. Item fi quis) lumine vacuam in Pleiadibus habeat à velhoftiliter percuſſas, ita natum oculis priuandum ferunt. Sed iam reliquas quoque ſideris ſtellas percurramus. Stellę circa Pleiades funt de natura & parum . In facie ſeu capite Tauri collocatur alius manipulus ſtellarum, quas latini veteres fucculas dixerunt, üs enim idem quod ſus valet. Sed Gręci nominarunt ύάδας ἀπὸ τεύξμ, quòd non finepluuia exorjantur. translation: The other's longitude is 28 parts with a half, and its northern latitude is 8 parts. There are also two stars in the head of Aries of the fifth magnitude, of the nature of Venus and a little Saturn, of which the more northern's longitude is 31 parts with a half, having a northern latitude of 7 parts. The more southern's longitude is 23 parts and a half, with a northern latitude of 6 parts. Those located in the hind foot are: in the tail, Venus. The constellation of Taurus consists of notable stars. And those four stars which are in the section or cutting off of Taurus, are all of the fourth magnitude, and have a similar mixture to Venus and a little Saturn, that is, they are cold and moist. Of these, the most northern today is precisely 47 parts distant from the vernal intersection, with a southern latitude of 6 parts. The next after it has a longitude of 46 parts, 40 minutes, 13 seconds, with a southern latitude of 7 parts, 15 minutes, 1 second. The third's longitude is 45 parts, its southern latitude is 8 parts, 30 minutes, 1 second. The fourth's longitude is 45 parts, 10 minutes, 1 second, with a southern latitude of 9 parts, 15 minutes, 1 second. On the back of Taurus, which are called Atarage by the Babylonians, in Greek Pleiades from 'plein' (to sail), because by their rising they indicate the time for navigation: in Latin they are called Vergiliae, because they rise at the end of spring when summer begins. They are also called Atlantides, because they are the seven daughters of Atlas, among whom Maia is the mother of Mercury, and not rarely is taken for all of them. Virgil in the Georgics, concerning the sowing of wheat: "Many began before the setting of Maia, but the expected harvest deluded them with empty ears." Six stars of this constellation are seen, but the seventh, because one of the daughters of Atlas mingled with a mortal, is scarcely perceived by the senses, as the German verses of Caesar in the interpretation of Aratus warn: "Seven are said to be in number, but one is snatched away, the eye failing to distinguish small bodies." Of these, the preceding has a longitude of 52 parts, 50 minutes, 1 second, with a northern latitude of 4 parts, 30 minutes, 13 seconds. The middle one's longitude is 53 parts, 10 minutes, 12 seconds, northern latitude 4 parts, 40 minutes, 1 second. But the following and last has a longitude of 54 parts, 20 minutes, 1 second, with a northern latitude of 5 parts, 20 minutes, 1 second. All are of the fifth magnitude, and are benefic stars: indeed, as Ptolemy relates, they follow the nature of the Moon and Jupiter, that is, they are very humid and hotter than the Moon, but colder than Jupiter. If therefore a cluster of stars at the hour of birth should appear with the ascendant, it will make the native great and famous, especially if it radiates to them: for then he will be pleasing to princes, and will lead an army, but nevertheless will finally perish by a bad death, having offended a prince. For notable fixed stars have significant indications of fortune in human nativities, if they are found in the angles and especially in the ascendant or Midheaven, with the Sun or Moon, or with the Part of Fortune, they decree the greatest felicity, but often with a calamitous outcome. Therefore, the great gifts of the fixed stars are neither sufficiently stable nor sufficiently reliable, unless they are confirmed by the suffrage of the wandering stars, as Hermes warns in his 29th statement: "The fixed stars bring irrational and admirable felicities, which nevertheless they mostly mark with calamities, unless the planets also agree to the felicity." Furthermore, to relate more about the Pleiades as more notable stars, although they are benefic, nevertheless whoever has them struck by malefics in the western angle at the time of their rising, will die by shipwreck. But if benefic stars look upon them thus constituted, they will nevertheless threaten the native with sudden death from intemperance and an afflux of phlegm. Likewise, if anyone has the Moon void of light in the Pleiades, or hostilely struck by them, they say the native will be deprived of sight. But now let us also examine the other stars of the constellation. The stars around the Pleiades are of the nature of the Moon and a little Jupiter. In the face or head of Taurus is located another cluster of stars, which the ancient Latins called Succulae, for 'sus' (pig) means the same to them. But the Greeks named them Hyades from 'hyein' (to rain), because they do not rise without rain. Of these, one huge and reddish star shining in the very eye of Taurus, called Aldebaran in Chaldean, Lampadias in Greek, and Palilicium in Latin, is of the nature of Mars and of the first magnitude. It belongs to the third part with a third of the dodecatemory of Gemini. Its southern latitude is 5 parts, 10 minutes, 1 second. Finally, in the highest horns are Martian stars. Castor and Pollux are thought to be Gemini. Some wish them to be Apollo and Hercules. Thus, the bright star in the head of the preceding Gemini, called the star of Apollo or Castor, has a longitude of 104 parts, that is, it belongs to the 14th part of the dodecatemory of Cancer. Its latitude is 9 parts with a half, and it is of the second magnitude, of the nature of Mercury. The other star shining in the head of the following Gemini is named Hercules, also Pollux. It has a longitude of 107 parts, 10 minutes, 13 seconds, with a northern latitude of 6 parts, 15 minutes, 1 second, and is of the second magnitude and of the nature of Jupiter. Ptolemy's words about these stars are these: "The two bright stars in the heads, the one in the preceding has a similar appearance to Mercury, and is also called Apollo, and the one in the following is called Hercules. Of the rest, those in the feet have shared a quality similar to Venus and a little Saturn, and the bright ones in the thighs are like Jupiter." In the breast of Cancer is found a nebulous cluster, called Praesepe. This was once in the dodecatemory of Cancer, but today its middle has advanced to the end of the first part of the dodecatemory of Leo. It has a northern latitude of 0 parts, 40 minutes. And it is nebulous, of the nature of Mars and the Moon. Of the two following and placed on either side, which are called Asini, the northern one has a longitude of precisely 121 parts, like Praesepe, with a northern latitude of 2 parts, 40 minutes, 1 second. But the southern Asinus has a longitude of 122 parts, with a southern latitude of only ten minutes. Both are of the fourth magnitude, of the nature of Mars and the Sun. The stars in the eyes of Cancer are like Mars and Venus, and a little Mercury. Those which are in the arms or claws resemble the nature of Saturn and Mercury. In the head of Leo are two stars of the third magnitude, of the nature of Jupiter and a little Mars, but the more northern has a longitude of 135 parts, with a northern latitude of 12 parts. The southern one has a longitude of 134 parts, 50 minutes, 12 seconds, that is, it falls into 14 parts, 50 minutes, 1 second, with a northern latitude of 9 parts, 30 minutes, 12 seconds. Three in the neck are Saturnine and a little Martian. Of these, the first has a longitude of 140 parts, 50 minutes, 1 second, with a northern latitude of 11 parts, of the third magnitude. The middle one is of the second magnitude, and has a longitude of 142 parts, 50 minutes, 1 second, with a northern latitude of 8 parts, 30 minutes, 1 second. The third has a longitude of 141 parts, 20 minutes, 1 second, with a northern latitude of 4 parts, 20 minutes, 1 second, and is of the third magnitude. In the middle of the constellation of Leo shines a bright star of the first magnitude, of the nature of Mars and Jupiter, which is commonly called Cor Leonis and Regulus, the Latins call it Regia, the Greeks Basiliskos, and it is in the 23rd part, 10 minutes, 18 seconds of the dodecatemory of Leo, with a northern latitude of only ten minutes. This is believed to be the most efficacious of all stars in the sky, because existing near the Ecliptic, it is greatly aided by the light of the Sun. Therefore, those who have it in the ascendant or placed in the Midheaven at the time of birth with the waxing Moon, will be promoted to great wealth and great empires. However, whenever the Sun and Moon reach it, it will stir up quarrels and contentions and serious wars for him. Those in the loins, and the bright one in the tail, are like Venus and Saturn. Those in the thighs are Venerean and a little Mercurial. Moreover, the one that shines at the end of the tail is of the first magnitude, and has a longitude of 165 parts, 10 minutes, 1 second, that is, it is in the 15th part, 10 minutes, 1 second of the dodecatemory of Virgo, with a northern latitude of 11 parts, 50 minutes, 19 seconds. Among the unformed stars around Leo, between its extremities and the Great Bear, the nebulous involutions which they call Coma Berenices are to be diligently noted. For the Moon, when it conjoins with these, is accustomed to wonderfully afflict the eyes of natives, as Ptolemy wrote in Book 3 of his Apotelesmata, chapter on injuries and bodily affections. Their position in our age is as follows: The luminous one, which looks most to the north, has a longitude of 165 parts, 30 minutes, 1 second, with a northern latitude of 30 parts. Of the obscure ones...

translation: SECOND. 204 of the second magnitude, having a longitude of p.215.30 with a northern latitude of p.44.36. VII. The Kneeler (Hercules) contains stars similar to Mercury. The Lyra, which is obscured by various names: for they call it Chelin, Fidicula, Tympanum, Falcon, Vulture falling, and the Chaldeans call it Asange, has stars of the nature of Venus and Mercury. VIII. The Bird, swan or hen, in Greek nun or oenis, has stars of the nature of Venus and Mercury. Some of our people call this constellation IX. the stork. The Bird. X. Cassiopeia contains stars of the nature of Venus and Saturn. XI. Perseus has stars of the nature of Jupiter and Saturn. That Gyrus in the hilt of the sword is Martian and Mercurial. In Perseus' left hand and in Medusa's head is a shining star worthy of observation, of the second magnitude, of the nature of Jupiter and Saturn, in the part of the dodecatemory of Taurus 205 with a northern latitude of p.13, which by the Babylonians is called Algol, by the Greeks Gorgonion, by us the Head of the Gorgon or Medusa, and because of its misfortune and destruction it is called Kakodaimon. It seems, however, that the fable, which says that Medusa's head was cut off by Perseus with a sword, is not inappropriately applied to this constellation, because, as Hermes declared in proposition 73, whoever is born with the Sun conjoined to the Gorgon's head, either by body or aspect, will be struck by an axe or sword, and his head will be cut off, if this dire conjunction of the Gorgon and the Sun is not aspected by a benefic star, nor do benefics protect the eighth house, which is of death, and the lord of the sign in which the conditional luminary is found is opposed to Mars, or strikes him from a square. Some understand this statement about Mars, that is, that Mars is found with the Gorgon, which also does not displease Pontano. For they are the authors of cruel and violent deaths. Moreover, it is almost an aphorism in Astrology, that whoever has Mars and Saturn in the eighth house, especially if a bad aspect of Saturn is added, or if they are with the head of Medusa, usually perish by violent death. Finally, the Greek text also names not the Sun, but Mars. If Mars is with the head of the Gorgon, not aspected by a benefic, nor is the benefic in the eighth house, and the lord of the conditional light is opposed to Mars, he will be beheaded; if the Moon is with the head of the Gorgon, his body will be burned; if it is conjoined to the tenth house, his hands or feet will be cut off. XII. Auriga. Hemochus or Auriga, also Erichtonius: In his left shoulder is a bright star of the first magnitude, called Capella, also by authors Capra Olevia from Olevus, a city of Arcadia, where they believe she was nourished. Ovid. The rainy sign of Olevia, Capella, is born. The Kids are referred to the 15th part of the dodecatemory of Cancer with a northern latitude of p.221. And in the palm of his right hand are two stars of the fourth magnitude, which are called Phoi, that is, the Kids, of which the preceding is in the 12th part of the dodecatemory of Gemini. The following is in the 13th part of the same. The northern latitude of both is part 18. The Kids are frequently mentioned by authors. Theocritus in Hodoiporia: "May there be a good voyage for the noble lord to Mitylene, even when the South Wind drives the moist waves at the setting of the Kids, and when the season is on the ocean." He wishes his friend a happy voyage to Mitylene even in an unfortunate and very turbulent sign, when, namely, the South Wind harasses the sea at the cosmic setting of Orion and the achronical rising of the Kids, who rise with the beginning of Taurus in Alexandria. Therefore, in October in the evening, as the Kids descend from the region, they emerge achronically: and therefore the poet calls them asterias, that is, appearing in the evening. As for the temperature of the bright stars of this constellation, it is of the nature of Jupiter and Mars. XIII. Serpens. Ophiuchus, the Serpent-bearer, has stars of the nature of Saturn. The serpent of Ophiuchus itself has stars of the same nature as XIV. Sagitta. Hercules' Arrow has stars imitating the nature of Mars and Saturn. XVI. Aquila. The Eagle, or as later authors called it, the flying Vulture, has Martian and Jovian stars. XVII. Delphinus. To this they adapt the fable or history of Arion the Lyric Poet. XVIII. Equisectio, or the first horse, protome, equuleus, which has bright stars, are of the nature of Mars and Venus. Pegasus, the winged horse, or the second horse, or the greater, has the same nature. Ovid in his Fasti relates that this horse was born from the blood that flowed from Medusa's severed head: "It is believed that from the blood of the slain Medusa's pregnant neck, Pegasus sprang forth from her scattered hair." He also writes that it was winged, and finally placed among the stars. XX. Andromeda, daughter of Cepheus and Cassiopeia, consists of Venus's stars. Some have called her chained: whose liberator Perseus holds the Gorgon's head in his left hand, and in his right. Finally, the Triangle has stars that are the same as XXI. the tenth. XV. Southern Asterisms. I. Cetus, hostile to Andromeda, which some call the marine Lion, some the bear, and some finally the Fish and Whale, is composed of Saturnine stars. II. The ancient Latins call Orion Lingula, and some call him the strongest. His stars are located in his shoulders and are similar. Moreover, the reddish star that shines in his right shoulder is of the first magnitude, and belongs to the 22nd part of Gemini with a southern latitude of p.17. That which is in his left shoulder is of the second magnitude, and belongs to the 11th part of Gemini with a southern latitude of p.174. The other bright stars of this constellation imitate Jupiter and Mars. In the belt or girdle of Orion, three notable stars of the second magnitude shine: the longitude of the preceding is p.76 with a southern latitude of p.24. The longitude of the middle is p.78 with a southern latitude of p.245. Finally, the longitude of the following in a straight line is p.80 with a southern latitude of p.25. In the left foot shines a bright star of the first magnitude, having a longitude of p.694 with a southern latitude of p.311. III. Eridanus or the river, also called the Nile, has at its extremity a bright star of the first magnitude, and is of the nature of Jupiter, and falls into the 20th part of the dodecatemory of Aries or the Nile.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Lunar Qualities and Malefics/Benefics
text: SECVNDA 237 faciendi communicationem.Neque verò fieri poterat, quin in Luna humiditas caliditatem infigniter excederet, cùm humi dum à duplici calore, proprio videlicet & Solis, calidum veròà Solis calore tantùm deijciat. Sed quærat aliquis, cùm Luna luminibus impleta frigus hyemis non mitiget, ſed maximè inten dat, quomodo fieri poſsit, vt lumine à Sole mutuato calefaciat? Exempli gratia: Facta ſynodo luminarium in Capricorno, Lu na difcedens à coitu, continenter à Sole magis illuſtratur,atque ad noſtros vertices magis accedit, donec penè ſemicirculo,hoc eſt, partibus 185 confectis tota accendatur, atque Cancrum percurrens noſtris fedibus fiat proxima.lbi Lunam hoc modo Solis lumen haurientem, noſtriſque verticibus magis magiſó appropinquantem, non tantum noſtrum aërem non calefacere, ſed & magis infrigidare notamus. Quare dubitari poteſt, an › lumine à Sole hauſto calefaciat? Ad häc hæfitatione reſpõde re debemus,præſentia illuminate Lunę nobis frigus nõ per ſe, ſed alio caſu adducere. Etenim vt plurimŭ fit, vt id caufam ali cuius effectus, quod cauſa minimè eſt, putent hoſes. Luna enim creſcens vapores plurimos è terra leuat, qui cùm ſint frigidiſsı- mi, non mirum eſt, ſi aërem rigidiorem faciant, præfertim cùm calor Lunæ ſemper maneat languidior, quàm vt copiam vapo rum queat abfumere. Ex eadem cauſa Sol à tropico Capricorni recedens, magis infrigidat, & fæuiorem facit hyemem. Poſteà verò à Capricorno progrediens, altior factus,rectiores & ob id fortiores radiores ad nos proijcit, qui edutos vapores paulatim magis magiſq; abfumunt, & ita calorem efficiunt. Hæcde qualitatibus ſtellarū primis dicta ſint. Sequitur vt quæ ex qualitatibus his naturaliter conſequantur, oftendamus. Acprimò κράσις feu mixtura ſtellarum facit, vt quædam ſtellæ dicantur beneficæ,quædam maleficæ, ſeu vt hodie loquuntur, fortunan tes & infortunantes. Quæ enim præter modum funt frigidæ, vt , & quz immodice calent,vto, intemperatæ, maleficæ & infortunantes vocantur. Sed Saturnum astrologi propter peſsimam eius temperaturam infortunam maiorem: Martem verd ob minorem dyſcraſian infortunam minorem nominant. Illæ verò ſtella, in quarum craſi calor dominatur humido, beneficæ humaneq; naturæ fauentes vocantur.Verùm vtcrafium, ita beneficarum Gg 3 1 138 DIFFERENTIA neficarum gradus ſunt. Nam in quibus calor humido perfecte dominatur, vt in 4, propter optimam mixturam maximè beneficæ:in quibus verò calor humorem mediocriter continet, vt in &,mediocriter temperatæ & beneficæ : in quibus deniq; humorexundans ob paucitatem caloris minimè cohibetur, vt in), parum temperatæ & beneficæ iudicantur. Ex his perfpicuum eſſe poteſt: Primò,cur 4 fortuna maior, & verò fortuna minor nominata fit. Deinde quòd (pleno orbelucens, fiquidem tum ratione humidi ſatis caloris acquirit, maximas adducat fœlicitates: vacua verò debiliterq; calefaciens, calamitates atque miferias portendat. Cæterùm & ficopuletur h,immodicum frigus, ſi o aut, immodicam ficcitatem acquirit. Qua re ita affe&us cùm fuerit, intemperatus atq; maleficus iudicandus eſt. Sed idé & cũ copulatur 4 & aut), calidus & humidus efficitur. Itaque tunc temperatus & beneficus cenſendus. Sol dux, princeps ac moderator reliquorú luminű, mens muadi & temperatio, etſi ob ingentia beneficia & temperaturam optimam omnium quæ in cælo cernuntur, maxima ſit fortuna: tamen vt 'inter ancipitis naturæ ſidera, & ob id nõnunquàm inter maleficas & infortunantes numeratur. Quæ fanè nomina ipfi ex vera cauſa nunquam competunt, ſed precipue caufæ pro pter quas ita aliquando nominetur, quatuor funt, vt refert Car danus. Prima, quòd lumine ſuo reliquas ſtellas occultet impediatque. Plin.lib.2.cap.18. inquit: Sol atque commiſſurz alidum extremæque orbitæ atram in obfcuritatem. E quibus ver bis colligimus tres modos quibus ſtellæ occultantur. Primùm Solem, qui in quacunque parte mundi fuerit, folus cernitur, vnde & nomen accepit: & de hoc modo hicloquimur. Deinde pun&a Ecliptica, ſeu nodi, in quibus & deficiunt. Poftremò circulorum adidas extremas, quæ quamuis ſtellas non omnind occultent, ob diſtantiam tamen magnam reddunt obſcu. riores. Secúda cauſa eſt, quòd copulatus maleficis vires corum foueat, vt infrà de locis Anæreticis dicetur. Tertia, quòd aſtris fixis iuntus, nonnunquam quodammodo fuam naturam immutare foleat. translation: It could not be otherwise than that in the Moon, humidity would significantly exceed heat, since the humid is cast down by a double heat, namely its own and the Sun's, while the hot is cast down only by the Sun's heat. But someone might ask, since the Moon, when full of light, does not mitigate the cold of winter, but rather intensifies it, how can it be that it heats by borrowed light from the Sun? For example: When the luminaries conjoin in Capricorn, the Moon, departing from the conjunction, is continuously more illuminated by the Sun and approaches closer to our zenith, until, having completed almost a semicircle, that is, 185 parts, it is fully ignited, and traversing Cancer, it becomes closest to our dwellings. There, we observe that the Moon, absorbing the Sun's light in this manner and approaching closer and closer to our zenith, not only does not warm our air but even cools it more. Therefore, it can be doubted whether it heats by light drawn from the Sun. To this hesitation, we must respond that the presence of the illuminated Moon brings us cold not by itself, but by another circumstance. For it very often happens that people consider that to be the cause of some effect which is by no means the cause. For the waxing Moon raises many vapors from the earth, which, being very cold, it is not surprising if they make the air colder, especially since the Moon's heat always remains weaker than to be able to consume the abundance of vapors. For the same reason, the Sun, receding from the Tropic of Capricorn, cools more and makes winter more severe. Afterwards, however, advancing from Capricorn, having become higher, it projects straighter and therefore stronger rays towards us, which gradually consume more and more of the exhaled vapors, and thus produce heat. Let these things be said about the primary qualities of the stars. Next, let us show what naturally follows from these qualities. And first, the krasis or mixture of the stars causes some stars to be called benefics, and some malefics, or as they are called today, fortunates and infortunates. For those that are excessively cold, like Saturn, and those that are excessively hot, like Mars, are called intemperate, malefic, and unfortunate. But astrologers call Saturn the greater infortune because of its very bad temperament, and Mars the lesser infortune because of its lesser dyscrasia. Those stars, however, in whose krasis heat dominates humidity, are called benefics and favorable to human nature. But just as there are degrees of krasis, so there are degrees of benefics. For those in which heat perfectly dominates humidity, as in Jupiter, are most benefic because of their excellent mixture; those in which heat moderately contains humidity, as in Venus, are moderately temperate and benefic; finally, those in which overflowing humidity is minimally restrained due to the scarcity of heat, as in the Moon, are judged to be little temperate and benefic. From these, it can be clearly seen: First, why Jupiter is called the greater fortune, and Venus the lesser fortune. Secondly, that the Moon, shining with a full orb, if it then acquires enough heat in proportion to its humidity, brings the greatest felicities; but when empty and weakly heating, it portends calamities and miseries. Moreover, if Saturn is conjoined with Mars, it acquires excessive cold; if with the Sun or Mars, excessive dryness. When thus affected, it is to be judged intemperate and malefic. But the same Saturn, when conjoined with Jupiter and Venus or the Moon, becomes hot and humid. Therefore, it is then to be considered temperate and benefic. The Sun, leader, prince, and moderator of the other luminaries, the mind of the world and its temperance, although it is the greatest fortune of all that are seen in the sky because of its immense benefits and excellent temperament, is nevertheless numbered among the stars of ambiguous nature, and therefore sometimes among the malefics and infortunates. These names, indeed, never truly apply to it from a true cause, but there are four principal reasons why it is sometimes so named, as Cardan relates. First, because it obscures and impedes the other stars with its light. Pliny, Book 2, Chapter 18, says: "The Sun and conjunctions of the extreme orbit into dark obscurity." From these words, we gather three ways in which stars are obscured. First, the Sun, which is seen alone wherever it may be in the world, from which it also received its name; and of this mode, we speak here. Secondly, the points of the Ecliptic, or nodes, in which the luminaries fail. Lastly, the extreme additions of the circles, which, although they do not entirely obscure the stars, nevertheless render them more obscure due to the great distance. The second reason is that, when conjoined with malefics, it fosters their powers, as will be said below concerning the Anaretic places. The third is that, when joined with fixed stars, it sometimes tends to change its nature in some way. For example, who is unaware that the Sun's vapors are ignited at the rising of the Dog Star? The effects of which star are felt most amply on earth. The seas boil at its rising, wines ferment in cellars, things move.

Et cùm ſeparatur vnus planeta ab alio nulli plinetarum iungitur, quandiu in codem figno fuerit, dicitur curſu vacuus: 1'. 3. Et cùm fuerit planeta in aliquo figno, & aliquis pla Mege talis netanonafpexerit boc fignum, alter planeta quandiu in pre alter. codem fuerit; dicitur feralis vel agrestis. 4. translation: The difference is that the Sun, revolving from the Sun to the very bottom, can be separated by a space of unknown things, and in that interval of time, when the epicycle once permeates, they are twice conjoined with the Sun, first by direct motion in the highest apogee of the epicycle, then by retrograde motion in the lowest apogee, all these four forms receive their risings and settings. First, hence, as the Moon shines forth in its evening rising, when it has proceeded a little from the conjunction of the Sun and from the highest apogee of the epicycle, and moves towards its evening station, from which afterwards, when it is about to seek another conjunction at the lowest apogee of the epicycle, it is hidden by the Sun's rays not long before the perigee of the epicycle, in its evening setting. Therefore, in the eastern semicircle of the epicycle, where they descend from its apogee to its perigee, they are called Hesperian and following, contrary to the three superior planets. Then, ascending from this place to the morning station, and not far from the perigee of the epicycle, they rise in the morning rising, having left the Sun's rays: finally, having passed the morning station, when they are again going to the highest apogee and seeking the Sun's conjunction again, they are a little distant from the apogee of the epicycle, they are hidden in the morning setting, and afterwards they proceed to the same turns. Therefore, in the meantime, while they appear through the western semicircle in the morning before the Sun's rising, they are called preceding, like the three superior planets. In an exemplary discussion, we will make the otherwise obscure matter much easier. Let the epicycle be AC EG: The highest apogee or its longest distance from the center of the world is A, where the middle of progression or direct motion is, and the first conjunction of the Sun and Venus: Evening rising. B, from where Venus descends in the epicycle, and diminishes its progression. The first or evening station C, from which it begins to retrograde. The evening setting occurs at point D. The middle of regression and the lowest point of the small circle of the apogee shows point E, under the 15th part of Taurus, where the star is again with the Sun. The morning rising is F. And the morning or second station occurs at G, from where it begins to progress. Point H is the morning setting, from which it proceeds most rapidly to the Sun and to repeat the said turns. The center of the world is T. The body is placed on the epicycle, and the quadrant of the zodiac rests on it, under which circle the center of the epicycle K, together with the line of the Sun's mean motion, revolves from west to Aries, the star running through the ambit of the epicycle. Two lines from the center of the world T through points G and G, erected to the zodiac, touching both epicycles, indicate the stations, as has been said. While the star descends from point B to point D, it is called Hesperus: while it ascends from sign F to sign H, it is called Lucifer. In the remaining spaces around the apogees, having lost both surnames, the name Venus is simply used. In the Ephemerides, the syllables Or. or Occ. are written under the notes of the five planets in the right column of the Ephemerides, showing when the planet is oriental or occidental. What position the planet has in the epicycle cannot be better judged than from the corrected anomaly of commutation, which in the five wandering stars is always counted from the top of the epicycle towards the east or through the consequent signs. For if it is nothing, it will indicate the planet culminating in the middle of its progression: if it has completed the semicircle exactly, it will indicate it pushed down to the bottom of the epicycle. Finally, it will show that it is lingering in the eastern semicircle of the epicycle: if greater, in the western. The knowledge of this matter contributes greatly to knowing whether the planet is free from the Sun's rays, or whether it is oppressed by them, as we taught a little while ago. The planet appearing before sunrise, oriental dexter, is called so not by reason of its region, but because it exists on the right side of the Sun. Similarly, in the evening, setting after sunset, it is called occidental sinister with respect to the Sun, because it sets after the Sun, and is found on the left side of the world from the Sun. Here you must observe which parts of the world are right, and which are left: concerning which distinction there are three opinions of authors. The first is that of the author and astronomers, who call the eastern part of the world left, and the western right. Pliny, book 2, chapter 8, concerning the world running to the west, says: "But the paths of all wandering stars, and that they move contrary to the world, that is, to the left, while it always rushes to the right." For astronomers, contemplating the sky, turn their faces to the south, where the course of the stars is most evident; for the motion of the stars near the poles is scarcely felt. Contrary to this is the opinion of geographers, who call the east right, and the west left. These, looking at the sky, turn their feet towards the pole, whose altitude they scrutinize. The last is that of the poets, who call the entire hemisphere from the equinox to the north right, and the other left. In the second of the Metamorphoses, Sol instructs Phaeton with these verses: "Let not the right turn to the twisted serpent: Nor let the left lead to the pressed altar." Thus Lucan writes that the southern shadows of those who came from Arabia to Pompey's aid were changed into northern ones: "Arabs, you come into an unknown world, marveling that the shadows of the trees do not go to the left." For before they had left the torrid zone, the shadows followed them, but after leaving it, they preceded them. What he says, that the two inferior planets, when they are occidental, assume three superior ones, and thus become stronger, seems to contradict Ptolemy, who taught that the five oriental planets are universally stronger. Even if this is true, nevertheless it is consistent that the inferior planets also sometimes rejoice in their setting. For since they have a mean motion common with the Sun, it can happen that they are faster than the Sun and by their nature become occidental: for this reason, the occidental Moon universally rejoices, but the three superior planets are always weakened by their setting due to the slowness of their motion. CONCERNING WHAT HAPPENS TO PLANETS TOWARDS EACH OTHER. Their being towards each other, that is, the same as conjunction, that is, when two planets are in two signs aspecting each other, and the lighter one is in its sign with fewer degrees than the heavier one is in its sign: if there are six degrees or less between them, then it is said that the lighter one goes to the conjunction of the heavier one: and when their degrees are equal, their conjunction is perfected: when it has passed it, it will be separated from it: this conjunction is called a conjunction of longitude. A conjunction of latitude, however, is when two planets are joined by latitude. And if there is an application of conjunction, their latitude must be equal in one part: and if there is a conjunction from opposition, their latitudes must be equal, so that the latitude of one is ascending in the north, and that of the other descending in the north: or one ascending in the south, and the other descending in the south: or if there is an application from any of the other aspects, that is, that the latitude of one is northern ascending, and that of the other southern descending: and vice versa in the south: and this is the application of latitude that has been mentioned. 2. And when one planet separates from another and is not joined to any other planet, as long as it is in the same sign, it is said to be void of course. 3. And when a planet is in a sign, and no other planet aspects this sign, as long as the other planet is in the same sign; it is called feral or wild. 4. And when a lighter planet separates from a heavier planet, and is joined to another, it transfers the nature of the first to the second. 5. A planet also transfers nature in another way, that is, when a lighter planet is joined to a heavier planet, and that heavier planet is again joined to another heavier planet, then the middle one transfers.

Primò perpendit, an planeta ſit cobuſtus vel non: Secu dd,an fit directus, velox aut rardus: Tertid, an fit in ſplédoribus ſuis, vt domicilio,altitudine, trigono, termine aut decuria col locatus: Quarto, an fit in aliquo aſpeſtu in fortunarú, vel in bo no aſpectu fortunarú: Quintò & vltimò, quomodo fit colloca eus in figura cæli, ſit ne in cardine ſuccedenti vel cadete. Plane. ez ergo a radijs ſolaribus liberi, directi, veloces, in ſplédoribus Cuis poſiti, bonis aſpectibus & beneficis aſſociati, in angulis de aiq; aut ſuccedentibus reperti, fortunati & potentes iudicandi '. translation: 346. Or they may be in opposition to their dignities or powers, or descending in the south or meridian; or falling from angles, or from succeeding angles, or in houses, or in cadent houses. Or they may be in the via combusta, which is the middle of the 15th degree of Libra and the first half of Scorpio, or they may be joined to a retrograde, impeded, or cadent planet, even if received. Or they may be the three superior planets occidental to the Sun, or in feminine quarters. And the debility of the Sun is when it is in feminine signs, or in feminine quarters, unless it is in the ninth house. And the debility of the three inferior planets is when they are oriental, or in masculine quarters. I. This describes a catalog of places in which planets increase or decrease their strength, are fortunate or unfortunate. And what it means can be clearly understood from the text. This astrological phrase (that they are cadent from them) should be noted here. A planet with any star, if it occupies the same sign, or is distant from it by 4, 7, or 10 signs (in the order of the signs), is said to be in an angle from it. But if it has traversed the second, fifth, eighth, or eleventh sign in order from it, it is in a succedent house; if it occupies the 3rd, 6th, 9th, or 12th, it is said to be cadent from it. Likewise, in this place, a superior planet is said to be more eminent, from which it is distant by 10 or 11 signs according to the order of the signs; as in my example, it is exalted above and traversing the sign. For my example is the tenth sign from Sagittarius. In this way, the MC angle is exalted above the ascendant. We will see the remaining forms of exaltations, or as they are called, elevations, below at the end of the following difference. This is the 2. Obsession is when two stars or two rays in some way enclose one star, with no other ray intervening, as if it is in the 10th part, 4th in the 11th, in the 12th. Jupiter is afflicted by being obsessed by two infortunes. Likewise, if Saturn is in the 6th part of Cancer, in the 8th part of the same Cancer, in the 10th part. The Moon will be said to be surrounded by the body of Saturn and the square ray of Mars. Likewise, if it is in such a figure: the Moon in, and in, which transmits its trine radiation into Leo, and its diameter into Libra, surrounds the Moon with its evil rays. In the same way, signs are also said to be obsessed, when, namely, one malefic is found at the beginning and another at the end. But when the Sun or a benefic aspects a planet or sign that is obsessed with a sextile or trine aspect, so that the aspect is within the orbs, then through such an aspect the obsession is resolved. Therefore, when malefics have obsessed the Moon or the horoscope, with no benefic shining upon the obsessed, they say that the native will have a short life. That it says nocturnal planets are stronger below the earth during the day than above the earth, we showed above in the first difference that this is contrary to Ptolemy's opinion. 3. As for the via combusta or Milky Way, Cardan in our century determines it to be in the northern part from the 19th degree of Gemini to the 30th degree of the same. And in the southern half from the 5th degree of Sagittarius to the 15th degree of the same. And again, because it is divided there from the 23rd degree of Sagittarius to the 4th degree. Of the great stars outside the zodiac, 4 are found in the vicinity of the Milky Way, namely the right side of Perseus, Capella in Auriga, Vega, the tail of Cygnus, and that which is in the 2nd degree, 18th minute, 12th second of Sagittarius with a southern latitude of 4 degrees, of the second magnitude, called Antares or the heart of Scorpio, which is important to observe. For every star in the Milky Way, or in its vicinity, somewhat disturbs the intellect and causes many other things. And so much for the text. Hyspalensis, whose opinion many follow, in examining the strengths of planets, observes only the principal places of this catalog. First, he considers whether the planet is combust or not: Second, whether it is direct, swift, or slow: Third, whether it is placed in its splendors, such as domicile, exaltation, trigon, term, or decan: Fourth, whether it is in any aspect of benefics, or in a good aspect of benefics: Fifth and finally, how it is placed in the figure of the heavens, whether it is in an angular, succedent, or cadent house. Therefore, planets free from solar rays, direct, swift, placed in their splendors, associated with good aspects and benefics, found in angles or succedent houses, are to be judged fortunate and powerful.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Aphetic Places and Anareta
text: QVARTA. 383 P ! ۱ funt tamea apte ratione loci decimi. Et quinque gradus vltimidecime, quamuis reſpectu eiufdem fint impotentes & inutiles, idonei tamen iudicantur ratione ſequentis domus vnde cimę.
