Nam propter fuam : イ 1 : QUARTA. 385 fuam præfentiam non patitur ſibi eripi ſuum priuilegiű, quod eſt, vt in diurna oppoſitione præferatur locus eius loco), niſi in occidente ad vnguem reperiatur. Ad extremum illud hic repetatur, quod per 5. dignitates apud Ptolemæum intelligantur domus, trigonus, altitudo,finis,coniunctio vel qualiſcunq; alius aſpectus, imò etiam intuentia imperantiaque, dummodo congruunt ad vnguem, aliàs non. Cautela:Siambo luminaria & propriæ conditionis gubernator(is verò eſt, qui in diurnis genituris in locis Solis, coniun- &ionis præcedentis & aſcendente, in nocturnis verò in & pra cedente & horoscopo dominatur) in locis Apheticis fuerint, luminum id præferendum eſt, quod maioris virtutis ac in loco aptiore fuerit. Veluti in nocturnis genituris ſi ſit) in no na vel ſeptima, Sol verò in aſcendente:tuncSol præferendus eft Lunæ,& maximè ſi iuxta gradum aſcendentis vel in dignitate ſua, fed tamen intra 25.gradus infra horizontem fuerit. In diurnis genituris, fi fit) in decima, in9.tunc ) præponenda erit Soli.Sed fi fit in decima & in II. erunt penè ratione locorum pares:quare illud præferendum iudicabis, quod maiore prærogatiua fuerit præditum. Quòd fi adhuc non ſatis notabiliter differre videbuntur, perpede, an alicui ipforum dominus loci corpore aut benigno aſpectu applicet.Nam fi dominus loci Aphetæ potens, eundem Aphetam & præcipuè bono aſpectu videat, prorogatorem multò robuftiorem efficiet. Atque hæc fanè eſt cauſa, ob quam Arabes ius moderandæ vitæ nunquam ſoli alicui planeta attribuant, niſi etiam accedat alter, quem vo cant Alcocodes, hoc eſt, qui dignitatem aliquam in loco futuri Aphetæ obtinens, eundemAphetam infuper refpicit.Cæterùm gubernator tum demum veriſque luminibus præferendus eſt, quando duas habuerit conditiones. Prima eſt, vt ipſa ſit in meliore loco Aphetico quam luminaria. Secunda, vt ex quinque prærogatiuis colligat tres in locis tribus, nimirum in loco, preced.& afcendente: Et alias tres in alijs locis tribus, nempeloco), præced. & horofcopo, & tunc dicitur gubernator vtriufque temporis. Hæc eſt prorogatorum lex: reſtat vt Anzretas fimili ordine recenſeamus. De Anzretis. í - 2 C Anz- 386 DIFFERENTIA Anerete ſeu μοίραι αναιρετικαί, hoc eft, partes interimentes, funt corpora atque hoftiles radij maleficorum, qui in progref- ſionibus vitæ datori obuiantes vitam nati incidunt,aut faltem eidem magna pericula imminere portendunt. Nam verbum αναιρέω idem eft quod occido, & αναpetisidem eſt quod occifor ſeu vitam incidens. Vnde etiam vulgò non vfque adeo inepte, ficut putatur, abſciſſorem ab abſcindenda vita vocarunt. Cæterùm interimentium partium apud Ptolemæum duoprima genera reperio: Vnuſa eſt eius, quæ verſus præcedentia figna fit moderationis, & habet partem interimentem tantùm vnam, nimirum cardinem finitoris occidentalis, eò quòd domi num vitæ abſcondat. Siderum verò vel occurſantium vel teſtificantium partes, adimunt ſaltem vel adijciunt aliquid ijs annis, quià vitæ moderatoread occaſum vſque deducto colligeban tur, & non interimunt, quòd non in locum Apheticum hæ impingant, ſed contrà Apheta ad Anæretam feratur. Ac quantùm adijciant adimantúe, rem alioqui nonnihil obſcuram infrà exemplo aliquo declarabimus. Alterum genus Anæretarum eſt eius dire&ionis,quæ verſus confequentia figna perficitur, & habet Anæretas præcipuos 16. quorum priores ſex per ſe in. terficiunt, funtque maleficarum duo corpora, corumque duo aſpectus oppoſiti, & alij duo quadrati. Reliquorum decem Anæretarű, qui generaliter non per ſe, ſed cum aliqua conditione interficiunt, primus cæteriſque validior eſt, tetragonus ipfius Aphetæ fecundum ſucceſsionem ſignorum procedens Conditio verò ſeu potius cauſa propter quam hic tetragonus interimat eſt, quòd hæc loca, quæ ab initio repugnabant, naturam ha beant contrariam. Ex hocliquet, quòd maleficæ ab initio quadrangulo Aphetam pulſantis occurrens trigonus, ipſo per ſe quadrato certiùs perimat. Arabes neque luminarium,neq; maleficorum corpora, aut vllos eorum radios perimere tradide. runt, quando vitez dominium ſuſceperunt. Deinde ſequuntur alij quatuor Anzrete, & o è ſignis di&o corum audientibus aut aſpicientibus ſecundum æquipollentiam: Veluti fi tem pore natiuitatis Apheta fit in parte ſecunda Geminorum, Anzreta verò in parte 29. Cancri: Hacconditio, nempe vt ab initio it anereta in intuente cum locoAphetico, hic eft neceſſaria, nó autem QVARTA 389 tatem in aſpectibus, quos fuprà per ſe interimere diximus: ve lutiſi ſit Apheta luſtrans Arietem, Saturnus verd Anæreta fit in Virgine proijciens radios ſuos
translation: The fourth section. 383. P. However, they are suitable by reason of the tenth house. And the five degrees of the ultimate tenth, although they are powerless and useless with respect to the same, are nevertheless judged suitable by reason of the following eleventh house. Therefore, we must consider the 9th and 10th houses without subtracting the five parts following their end, and subtract them only from the first, seventh, and eleventh, whose subsequent houses, namely the second, eighth, and twelfth, are not Aphetic. These are Ptolemy's Aphetic places. On Apheta: The Chaldeans call the significator and ruler of life to be moderated 'leech', but Ptolemy calls it 'kyrion zoes kai apheten', that is, the 'dimissor', from 'aphetes', because it possesses, as it were, a task from which the threads, once drawn and released, measure the fatal span of human life. Indeed, I believe that the poets' image of the three Fates, which Varro thinks are derived from 'partus' (birth) by a change of one letter, pertains here: for they are thought to bestow good or evil upon men at birth, and to moderate their lives as if spinning them. Lachesis, the spinner, provides the task, from which Clotho draws and twists the threads, which Atropos finally breaks. Therefore, I thought Lachesis signifies the Apheta, Clotho the procession of the Apheta, and Atropos the anaretic place. But as to whom this rulership and dispensation of life should be attributed, the Arabs differ much less from Ptolemy than in the constitution of the Aphetic places. The Arabs transfer the moderation of life to one of these six, namely, to the Sun or Moon, or to the degree of conjunction or prevention, or finally to the Part of Fortune or the Ascendant. But Ptolemy orders five, namely, the Ascendant, the Part of Fortune, and the ruler of these places, which of them it is convenient to choose as the rulers of life according to his opinion, the following descriptions will show clearly enough. In diurnal nativities, look at the Sun, if it is in any of the five said Aphetic places, let it be the Apheta above others. But if the Sun seems unsuitable for prolonging life due to the unsuitability of the place, then consequently, look at the Moon. And if the Moon cannot be attributed this rulership, then... 384. 3. Take the ruler, who has three of the dignities in the three places, namely: 1. The preceding conjunction, which is in the three places. DIFFERENCE: For if such a planetary ruler is in the Aphetic places, it will be given to the luminaries. But if this ruler is found in the Aphetic places, namely in the 3rd place of the Ascendant, then the Aphesis, or as they call it, the 'legium', devolves to the Ascendant. Observe that in diurnal nativities, attention must be paid to the place of the preceding conjunction, even if it immediately preceded and was closer to the nativity. For the Sun is more powerful than the others. In nocturnal nativities: 1. Make the Moon the Apheta, if it is in the Aphetic places. 2. Take the Sun, if it is in those 25 degrees that follow the center of the Ascendant. And if neither luminary can prolong life, then further: 3. Take the ruler, who has three of the five dignities in the three places, namely: 1. The place of the Ascendant. For if such a planet is in the 2nd Aphetic place, it will moderate life; if not, then in the 3rd place. 4. If the conjunction immediately preceded the nativity, take the Ascendant, because it favors the Ascendant. But if the opposition immediately preceded, take the Part of Fortune if it is in an Aphetic place; if not, then the rulership must again be deferred to the Ascendant. It should be noted that in nocturnal nativities, the opposition must be attended to, even when the conjunction was closer to the nativity, because it is sufficient, and has no power. Moreover, the place, as we have warned above, is the place of the luminary which is found above the earth at the time of the opposition. For by its 385. presence, it does not suffer its privilege to be taken away, which is that in a diurnal opposition, its place is preferred to the place of the Sun, unless it is found exactly in the west. Finally, let it be repeated here that by 5 dignities, Ptolemy understands houses, trigon, exaltation, bounds, conjunction or any other aspect, indeed even intuition and rulership, provided they agree exactly, otherwise not. Caution: If both luminaries and the ruler of their own condition (who is he who in diurnal nativities rules in the places of the Sun, the preceding conjunction, and the Ascendant, but in nocturnal nativities rules in the Moon, the preceding conjunction, and the Ascendant) are in Aphetic places, that of the luminaries is to be preferred which is of greater virtue and in a more suitable place. As in nocturnal nativities, if the Moon is in the ninth or seventh, but the Sun in the Ascendant: then the Sun is to be preferred to the Moon, and especially if it is near the degree of the Ascendant or in its dignity, but nevertheless within 25 degrees below the horizon. In diurnal nativities, if the Sun is in the tenth, and the Moon in the ninth, then the Moon will be preferred to the Sun. But if the Sun is in the tenth and the Moon in the eleventh, they will be almost equal by reason of their places: therefore, you will judge that to be preferred which is endowed with a greater prerogative. But if they still do not seem to differ notably enough, consider whether the lord of the place applies to any of them by body or by a benevolent aspect. For if the powerful lord of the Apheta's place sees the same Apheta, and especially with a good aspect, he will make the prolonger much stronger. And this is certainly the reason why the Arabs never attribute the right of moderating life to any single planet, unless another also accedes, whom they call Alcocodes, that is, one who, holding some dignity in the place of the future Apheta, moreover regards the same Apheta. However, the ruler is then finally to be preferred to both luminaries when he has two conditions. The first is that he himself is in a better Aphetic place than the luminaries. The second is that he collects three of the five prerogatives in three places, namely in the place of the Sun, the preceding conjunction, and the Ascendant: And another three in other three places, namely the place of the Moon, the preceding conjunction, and the Ascendant, and then he is called the ruler of both times. This is the law of the prolongers: it remains for us to enumerate the Anaretas in a similar order. On Anaretas: 386. DIFFERENCE. Anaretas, or 'moirai anairetikai', that is, destroying parts, are the bodies and hostile rays of malefics, which, encountering the giver of life in the progressions, cut off the life of the native, or at least portend great dangers to it. For the word 'anaireo' means the same as 'to kill', and 'anairetis' means the same as 'killer' or 'life-cutter'. Hence, they are also commonly, not so ineptly as is thought, called 'abscissor' from 'abscinding life'. However, among Ptolemy's destroying parts, I find two primary kinds: One is that which is of moderation towards the preceding signs, and has only one destroying part, namely the cusp of the western terminator, because it hides the lord of life. But the parts of the stars, either encountering or testifying, at least subtract or add something to those years which were collected by the moderator of life up to the setting, and do not destroy, because they do not impinge on an Aphetic place, but on the contrary, the Apheta is carried to the Anareta. And how much they add or subtract, a matter otherwise somewhat obscure, we will explain below with some example. The other kind of Anaretas is of that direction which is perfected towards the consequent signs, and has 16 principal Anaretas, of which the first six kill by themselves, and are the two bodies of the malefics, and their two opposite aspects, and another two squares. Of the remaining ten Anaretas, who generally do not kill by themselves, but with some condition, the first and most powerful is the square of the Apheta itself proceeding according to the succession of the signs. But the condition or rather the cause why this square kills is that these places, which were initially repugnant, have a contrary nature. From this it is clear that the malefic, encountering the Apheta striking with a square from the beginning, kills more certainly by itself than by a square. The Arabs have taught that neither the bodies of the luminaries nor of the malefics, nor any of their rays, kill when they have taken over the dominion of life. Then follow another four Anaretas, and from the signs hearing or aspecting according to equivalence: As if at the time of birth the Apheta is in the second part of Gemini, but the Anareta is in the 29th part of Cancer: This condition, namely that from the beginning the Anareta is in aspect with the Aphetic place, is necessary here, but not 389. power in the aspects, which we said above kill by themselves: as if the Apheta is transiting Aries, but Saturn the Anareta is in Virgo casting its rays.

title: Annual Profection Table and Calculation

V. fuerit- rit an. &c. que inter ipſum & fimilem gradum figni fuccedentis, hoc eft, par.17.8, quæ cundem annum finit, atque ad qua eadem pro greſsio ſequentis anni deuoluetur corpus vel radij planetaalicuius, & placeat cognofcere diem, quo profetio dictum plane tam vel radios eius fit confecutura; tunc partes & fcrupula, que inter gradum,ad quem annua profectio peruenit, & planetam radio fúe eius cadunt, multiplica per 127, hoc eft, dabis vnicuilibet gradui 12. dies & 4. horas, & proueniét dies, qui ſunt à prin cipio illius anni, vfque ad tempus quo profectio applicabit ad planetam aureius radios. Verbi gratia, in eodem exemplo ſint Saturni quadrangulares radij in 5. part. ४, arcus qui inter partem primam quæfitę profe&ionis, hoc eft, part. 17., & radios quadratos Saturni intercipiuntur par. 18.quæ per 12+ multiplicate proferunt dies 219.reuolutionis, quibus exactis progref fio radios Saturni ingredietur. Porro diftantiam feu arcum ab initio annuz profe&ionis ad initium loci ſequentis, de quo eſt quæſtio, in partibus atque ſcrupulis, ſi adfuerint, fic computabis. Cape aſpectum aut alium locum, de cuius applicatione ad annuam profectionem quæritur, atque ab eodem auferannua profectionis initium, & remanebit differentia inter principium annuæ profectionis & locum propoſitum.Exempli gratia,epocha profectionis horoſcopi in aſſumpta genitura anno Chriſti Dei 1567. ætatis verò nati 41. perueniet, vt paulò antè dicebamus, ad partem 26.fcrup.44 14. Aquarii. Duciam 1214 in diſtantie p.1.ſcr.37 14, & prouenient dies 19. quibus emen- ſis progreſsio horoſcopi in hac reuolu tione Martis quadraturam tranſibit. Aliud exemplum: Placet ſcire, quo die dicti anni profe&io conſecutura ſit quadratum radium Solis, qui in par.8. ſcrupu. 54 14 Piſcium incidit. Subtracto annuæ profectionis principio à quadratura Solis, reſtant par.12.ſcrup. 10 14, quæ multiplicata per 12. produnt dies 148. quibus exactis profectio horoſcopi in dicto anno ætatis quadratū Solis aſſequetur. Sed tempus progref- ſionum annuarum in diebus ope appo ſitæ tabellæ adiutus, faciliùs ſupputare poterunt rudiores. Cognitis enim par tibus atque ſcrupulis, quibus initium profectionis & locus ſequens diſsidět, ingredere tabellam cum partibus inte gris,nam è regione offeret ſe tempus progreſsionis quæſite in diebus, horis & ſcrupulis, cui pro quibuslibet ſcru. pulis primis quinque, ſi vnum diem adiicias,verum tempus profe&ionis conſtabis. Vtin priore exé plo, partibus 12. reſpondent dies 146. horæ 2.ſcrup.1914, quibus ſi dies 2. ratione ſcru. 10 14 adiicias, habebis tempus verum dieDigitized by Google rum QUARTA 399 rum 148.hor.2.ſcru.1919. Iam vt anni menſem & diem eius,in quem reperta applicatio incidit, queas agnofcere, inuentis diebus profe&ionis, adde dies qui numerantur à principio anni Chriſti, ad punctum natiuitatis ſeu potius reuolutionis, quæ ni hil aliud eft, quàm Solis ad ſua in genitura ετοχων reditus, nam numerus dierum, qui hinc exurgit,in tabella menſium vſualiú, quam tibi hic & fupra in tractatu ωριμοίρας ὡροσκοπόσης ordinaui, facilè oftendet, in quem diem meſis reperta proſectio nis applicatio incidat. In noſtro exemplo à principio anni Chri ſti ad radicem geniturę, elapſi ſunt dies 141.horæ 3.ſcru.47 14. Quòd ſi adhuc exactius hunc numerum ſcire velis, tunc ratione reuolutionis anni 41.auferre debes à prędicto tempore ho ras 21.ſcrup.12 14, quo facto relinquetur reuolutionis tempus exactum, nempe dies 140. horæ 6.ſcrup.35 14, quod tempus inuento tempori profectionis, hoc eft, diebus 148.iungentes, pro ducemus dies 288.hor.6.ſcrupu.35 14, à quo numero dierum proximè minor numerus, nempe 274. qui è regione Septembris in tabella anni biſſextilis ponitur, ablatus, relinquit dies cõpletos 14.ho.6.ſcr.35 14 Octobris, in quorũ fine horoſcopus ad quadraturam Solis prouoluetur. Obſerua ſi ex additione duorum temporum excreſcant dies plures, quàm vnus annus Solaris continet, tunc ſi annus labens fuerit communis, dies 365. ſi biſſextilis, dies 366. reiiciendos eſſe, atque id quod re- ſtat, ad annum Chriſti immediatè ſequentem referendum. Cæterum Ptolemæus circa finem quadripartitæ conſtructionis docet, ſignificatores cuiuslibet geniturę no tantùm per annos, ſed etiam per menſes & dies progredi. Ac annuarum proſectionum ratio dilucidè iam declarata eft. In proſectione auté men- ſurna vnicuique menſium proſectionalium, qui funt 13. numero, datur ſignum vnum, eiuſque dominus preſes, vt diximus, ita quòd ſignum cuiuslibet proſectionis annuæ, fit ſignum primi menſis proſectionalis eiusdem anni. Vnus ergo annus in 13. par tes dirimendus eſt, quarum quęlibet dicitur menſis proſectionalis. Hoc pacto efficietur, vt in ſuccedenti reuolutione tam in anno, quàm in menſe ad ſignum ſequens proſectione peruenia mus. Veluti in ſuperiore exemplo anno 41. proſectio annua horoscopi peruenit ad partem 26. ſcrupu.44 14. Aquarij, quod idem Digitized by Google
translation: By means of this table, the less experienced will be able to easily determine to which sign any annual profection applies in any given year of age. For example, in our case, in the 41st year of age, which falls in the current year of Christ 1567, the annual profection of the Ascendant will reach 26 degrees 44 minutes of Aquarius, the Moon to 27 degrees 52 minutes of Gemini, the Sun to 8 degrees 54 minutes, the Midheaven to 1 degree 39 minutes, the Part of Fortune to 15 degrees 42 minutes of Scorpio, etc. Next, concerning the finding of the annual progression of aphetic places in days: If, he says, the year of profection reaches the degree of any sign and place, for example, to 17 degrees of Taurus, and if between it and a similar degree of the succeeding sign, that is, 17 degrees of Virgo, which ends the same year, and to which the same progression of the following year will devolve the body or rays of any planet, and it is desired to know the day on which the profection will reach the said planet or its rays; then multiply the degrees and minutes that fall between the degree to which the annual profection arrives and the planet or its rays by 12, that is, you will give to each degree 12 days and 4 hours, and the days will result, which are from the beginning of that year, until the time when the profection will apply to the planet or its rays. For example, in the same example, let the square rays of Saturn be in 5 degrees of Taurus, the arc intercepted between the first part of the sought profection, that is, 17 degrees, and the square rays of Saturn is 18 degrees, which multiplied by 12 produce 219 days of the revolution, at the end of which the progression will enter the rays of Saturn. Furthermore, you will thus calculate the distance or arc from the beginning of the annual profection to the beginning of the succeeding place, about which the question is, in degrees and minutes, if they are present. Take the aspect or other place, about whose application to the annual profection it is asked, and from it subtract the beginning of the annual profection, and the difference between the beginning of the annual profection and the proposed place will remain. For example, the epoch of the profection of the Ascendant in the assumed nativity in the year of Christ 1567, and the 41st year of the native's age, will reach, as we said a little earlier, 26 degrees 44 minutes of Aquarius. It is now asked on what day of the same year this progression will apprehend the square ray of Mars, which falls in 28 degrees 21 minutes of the same Aquarius. First, subtract the beginning of the annual profection from the place of Mars' square, and the middle distance will remain: 1 degree 37 minutes. Mars' square: 3 signs, 28 degrees, 21 minutes. Beginning of annual profection: 3 signs, 26 degrees, 44 minutes. Distance between the beginning of profection and Mars: 0 signs, 1 degree, 37 minutes. Now multiply 12 by the distance of 1 degree 37 minutes, and 19 days will result, at the end of which the profection of the Ascendant in this revolution will pass the square of Mars. Another example: It is desired to know on what day of the said year the profection will reach the square ray of the Sun, which falls in 8 degrees 54 minutes of Pisces. Subtracting the beginning of the annual profection from the Sun's square, 12 degrees 10 minutes remain, which multiplied by 12 produce 148 days, at the end of which the profection of the Ascendant in the said year of age will reach the Sun's square. But the less experienced will be able to calculate the time of annual progressions in days more easily with the help of the appended table. For having known the degrees and minutes by which the beginning of the profection and the succeeding place differ, enter the table with the whole degrees, for opposite it will present itself the time of the sought progression in days, hours, and minutes, to which for any first five minutes, if you add one day, you will have the true time of the profection. As in the previous example, 12 degrees correspond to 146 days, 2 hours, 19 minutes, to which if you add 2 days for 10 minutes, you will have the true time of 148 days, 2 hours, 19 minutes. Now, in order that you may know the month and day of the year into which the found application falls, having found the days of profection, add the days that are counted from the beginning of the year of Christ to the point of nativity or rather of revolution, which is nothing other than the Sun's return to its place in the nativity, for the number of days that arises from this will easily show in the usual table of months, which I have arranged for you here and above in the treatise on the horoscopic apheta, into which day of the month the found application of profection falls. In our example, from the beginning of the year of Christ to the root of the nativity, 141 days, 3 hours, 47 minutes have elapsed. If you wish to know this number more exactly, then by reason of the revolution of the 41st year, you must subtract 21 hours, 12 minutes from the aforesaid time, which done, the exact time of the revolution will remain, namely 140 days, 6 hours, 35 minutes, which time, joining to the found time of profection, that is, 148 days, we will produce 288 days, 6 hours, 35 minutes, from which number of days the next smaller number, namely 274, which is placed opposite September in the table of the bissextile year, being subtracted, leaves 14 complete days, 6 hours, 35 minutes of October, at the end of which the Ascendant will revolve to the square of the Sun. Observe that if by the addition of two times more days arise than a solar year contains, then if the current year was common, 365 days, if bissextile, 366 days, are to be rejected, and what remains is to be referred to the immediately succeeding year of Christ. Furthermore, Ptolemy, near the end of the Quadripartite Construction, teaches that the significators of any nativity progress not only by years, but also by months and days. And the method of annual profections has now been clearly explained. In the monthly profection, however, to each of the profectional months, which are 13 in number, one sign is given, and its lord is the ruler, as we said, so that the sign of any annual profection is the sign of the first profectional month of the same year. One year, therefore, is to be divided into 13 parts, each of which is called a profectional month. In this way, it will happen that in the succeeding revolution, both in the year and in the month, we arrive at the next sign by profection. As in the example above, in the 41st year, the annual profection of the Ascendant reached 26 degrees 44 minutes of Aquarius, which is the same.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Profectional Months and Days
text: 409 DIFFERENTIA idem eſt ſignum primi menſis profe&ionalis eiufdem anni: ☿ ſecundi; ☿ tertii; ☿ quarti: ♊ quinti: ♌ ſexti: ♏ ſeptimi: ♐ ogaui: ♑ noni: ♒ decimi: ♓ vndecimi: ♈ duodecimi: ♉ decimitertii: ♊ decimaquarti, qui menſis profe&ionalis fimul eſt initium anni ſequentis. Ex quo patet, menſtruam proſectioné ex vno in aliud ſignum detuliſſe annuum dominatum per vnú ſignum. Cum igitur annus diuiditur in 13. menſes, fit vt vnus menſis proſectionalis contineat dies 28. horas 2. ſcrupu.17 14, 37 24. Sed Ptolemæus prætermittit horas & ſcrupula, & quia pa rum erroris adducere poterant, & quia facilè ſe infinuabant có ſtructi tabulas. Similiter etiam pro profectione diurna, menſis vnus profectionalis partiēdus eſt in partes 13. æquales, quarum quælibet habebit dies 2.horas 3.& ſcrupu.52 14 ferè. Cæterùm vt cognofcas quo pertingat proſectio tam menſurna quàm diurna ad quodcunque tempus in anno propoſitum, ſupputa té- pus elapſum à principio annuæ conuerſionis anni currentis, vfque ad tempus propoſitum vel aſſumptum, hoc modo: Numera primò dies & reliquas partes temporis, ſi adſint, comprehenſas inter principium menſis currentis vſualis, & epocham natiuitatis, ſeu quod idem eſt, pūtum reuolutionis, & tempus hoc adde numero dierum reperto è regione menſis vſualis immediatè præcedentis poſito, in prima quidem ſerie, fi fuerit annus có munis, in ſecunda ſi biſſextilis, tempus quod hinc exoritur (eſt autem id quod inter principium anni romanorum ſeu Chriſti, & epocham Solis in natiuitate comprehenditur)ſerua per totũ annum Solarem. Deinde eodem modo, quem diximus, addiſce, quantum tempus effluxit ab initio anni Romanorum vfque ad tempus propoſitum: Tandem ſubtrahe prius à poſteriore, & relinquetur tempus tranſactum à radice conuerſionis vſque ad tempus propoſitum. Id emenſum tempus iam inuentum quæ- rein tabula menſium proſectionalium: veluti fieri ſolet, quando per medium motum cuiuſcunque planetę quæritur tempus ei motui correſpondens. Ac ſi præciſe in area tabellæ inueneris tempus, numerus ſiniſter oftendet tibi numerum menfium profe&ionalium traſactorum ab initio anni Solaris currentis. Si autem tuum tempus non præciſe inueneris in area tabellæ, cape tempus, exactum proximè minus, atque idipſum à tempo re, quod Digitized by Google QVARTA 407 re, quod in tabulam mittere non potuiſti, ſubtrahe, vt ſuperflui dies cum ſcrupulis relinquantur: 'Dabis autem cuilibet menſi profectionali ſignum vnum, incipiédo à ſigno profectionis an nuæ. Dies verò ſuperfluos cum ſcrupulis adhærentibus mitte in tabulam profe&ionis menſurnæ, & è regione offerent ſe gradus & ſcrup. addenda ſignis & gradibus priùs notatis, & ſic perduceris ad locum zodiaci, quo peruenit proſectio in fine temporis tranſacti. Quòd ſi modo prædicto cum diebus ſuperfluis ingreſſus fueris tabulam profectionis diurnæ, elicies numerum ſighorum & graduum computandorum à ſigno profectionis menſurnæ: quibus cognitis loca vniuerſarum profectionũ inuenta habebis. Exemplari diſcurſu rem planiorem faciemus. In aſſumpto exemplo atque in reditu anni 41. oſtenſum eſt, annuam profectionem horoscopi attigiffe p. 26.ſcr.44.13. Aquarij, idq; die Maij 20.completo, horis 6.ſcr.35 14. Nam à tempore radicis ratione præſentis reuolutionis horas 21.ſc.12. 14, auferemda eſſe, paulò antè dicebamus. Sint iam exempli gratia inue- ſtiganda loca proſectionum ad 9. diem Iulij eiusdem anni currentis. Primò tempus quod ab initio anni Romanorum ad pun ctum reuolutionis effluxit, reperitur eſſe dierum 141.hor.6.ſc. 35.14, eſt enim currens præſens annus biſſextilis. Deinde fimiliter idem tempus, quod ab eodem initio ad tempus propoſitum protenditur, eſt dierum 191. à quibus ſi ſubtrahatur tempus prius, nempè dies 141.horæ 6.ſcrup.35.14, remanebūt dies 49.horæ 17.ſcrup.25.14, quod eſt tempus à radice conuerſionis ad limitem temporis propoſiti emenſum. Hunc dierum ſcrupu lorumq; temporis numerum ſi mittas in aream tabellæ iam iá à nobis deſcriptę, inuenies ei correſpondere partes 5. ferè, quas iunge cum partibus 26.ſcr. 44. 1ª Aquarij, fiunt p. 1.ſcr.44.14 Piſcium. Quapropter pronunciabis annuam progreſsioné horoſcopi ad datum tempus proceſsiſſe ad p. 1. ſcrup.44.1 Piſ- cium. Cæterum pro profectione meſtrua habéda, tempus illud, nempè dies 49. horas 17.ſcrup.25.14, in area ſequentis tabellæ menfium porſectionalium quærendum eft. Id verò cumibi nó reperiatur, ſubtrahe ab eo numerum proximè minorem ibidé repertum, nempè dies 28. hor. 2. ſcrup. 17.14,37.24, quo facto remanebút dies 21, horæ 15.ſcr.7.14 23.24. vltra vnum menſem profeDigitized by Google TABELLA MENSIVM PROFECTIONAlium & vſualium. translation: The sign of the first profectional month of the same year is ♈; of the second, ♉; of the third, ♊; of the fourth, ♋; of the fifth, ♌; of the sixth, ♍; of the seventh, ♎; of the eighth, ♏; of the ninth, ♐; of the tenth, ♑; of the eleventh, ♒; of the twelfth, ♓; of the thirteenth, ♈; of the fourteenth, ♉, which profectional month is also the beginning of the following year. From this it is clear that the monthly profection has carried the annual rulership from one sign to another through one sign. Since the year is divided into 13 months, it follows that one profectional month contains 28 days, 2 hours, 17 scruples, 14, 37, 24. But Ptolemy omits hours and scruples, both because they could cause little error and because they were easily incorporated into constructed tables. Similarly, for diurnal profection, one profectional month must be divided into 13 equal parts, each of which will have approximately 2 days, 3 hours, and 52 scruples, 14. Moreover, to know how far the profection, both monthly and daily, extends to any given time in the year, calculate the time elapsed from the beginning of the annual revolution of the current year up to the proposed or assumed time, in this manner: First, count the days and the remaining parts of the time, if present, comprised between the beginning of the current usual month and the epoch of the nativity, or, which is the same, the point of revolution, and add this time to the number of days found opposite the immediately preceding usual month, placed in the first series if it was a common year, in the second if it was a bissextile year. The time that arises from this (which is that comprised between the beginning of the Roman or Christian year and the epoch of the Sun in the nativity) keep for the entire Solar year. Then, in the same way we said, learn how much time has elapsed from the beginning of the Roman year up to the proposed time: Finally, subtract the former from the latter, and the time elapsed from the root of the revolution up to the proposed time will remain. This measured time, now found, is to be sought in the table of profectional months, just as is usually done when the time corresponding to the mean motion of any planet is sought. And if you find the time precisely in the area of the table, the number on the left will show you the number of profectional months elapsed from the beginning of the current Solar year. If, however, you do not find your time precisely in the area of the table, take the exact next smaller time, and subtract it from the time you could not put into the table, so that the superfluous days with scruples remain. You will assign one sign to each profectional month, starting from the sign of the annual profection. The superfluous days with the adhering scruples, put into the table of monthly profection, and opposite them will appear the degrees and scruples to be added to the signs and degrees previously noted, and thus you will be led to the place of the zodiac where the profection arrives at the end of the elapsed time. If, in the manner described above, with the superfluous days, you enter the table of diurnal profection, you will extract the number of signs and degrees to be computed from the sign of the monthly profection: knowing these, you will have found the places of all profections. We will make the matter clearer with an exemplary discussion. In the assumed example and in the return of the 41st year, it was shown that the annual profection of the horoscope reached 26 degrees, 44 scruples, 13 of Aquarius, and that on May 20th completed, at 6 hours, 35 scruples, 14. For, from the time of the root, by reason of the present revolution, 21 hours, 12 scruples, 14 had to be removed, as we said a little earlier. Let us now, for example, investigate the places of the profections for the 9th day of July of the same current year. First, the time that elapsed from the beginning of the Roman year to the point of revolution is found to be 141 days, 6 hours, 35 scruples, 14, for the present year is a bissextile year. Then, similarly, the same time that extends from the same beginning to the proposed time is 191 days, from which if the former time is subtracted, namely 141 days, 6 hours, 35 scruples, 14, there will remain 49 days, 17 hours, 25 scruples, 14, which is the time measured from the root of the revolution to the limit of the proposed time. If you put this number of days and scruples of time into the area of the table already described by us, you will find that it corresponds to approximately 5 parts, which you join with 26 degrees, 44 scruples, 14 of Aquarius, making 1 degree, 44 scruples, 14 of Pisces. Therefore, you will declare that the annual progression of the horoscope has proceeded to 1 degree, 44 scruples, 14 of Pisces at the given time. Moreover, for obtaining the monthly profection, that time, namely 49 days, 17 hours, 25 scruples, 14, must be sought in the area of the following table of profectional months. But since it is not found there, subtract from it the next smaller number found there, namely 28 days, 2 hours, 17 scruples, 14, 37, 24, after which there will remain 21 days, 15 hours, 7 scruples, 14, 23, 24, beyond one profectional month. author: Valentin Naibod
title: Planetary Rule and Aphetic Places
text: QVARTA 433 Lunz, imperium illud à Saturno transfertur in Iouem & Lunam mox ſequentem, quæ vfque ad finem Libra, & par.1.fcrupu.12 1ª Scorpij dominatur, Marte vtrobique ratione finium participe. Nam Mars tam ex fententia Ptolemæi, quàm Aegyptiorum in Scorpij prioribus ſex partibus terminorum domiaium obtinet.Hinc verò paulò poſt partem primam Scorpijim perium radiorum à Luna transfertur in mercurium, qui triquetram fuam radiationem in partem 1.fcrupu.121* Scorpis proijcit, atque ratione horum radiorum fuum imperium vfque ad partem 28. Scorpij extendit, participante propter dominiŭ terminorum primò Marte, deinde loue, hinc Venere, & denique Mercurio.Hincin parte 28. fcrup. 211, Scorpij, potentissimum omnium imperium ſuſcipit Mars: Nam preterquam quòd corporedi&um locum occupat in propria domo atque trigono,& inimicus aſcendentis exiftens, in figno fixo & venenofo retrod cedit, cum teftimonio eiuſdem martis & Saturni domini fi nium illius directionis, principium anni 41. attingentis, cuius annuz fignorum commutationes ſtellarumque πρόδοι tales fu ture ſunt, vt ſequitur. : Progrefsiones annuę. par.fcr.1. Horoscop. 26 44 ... 27 52 Sors 15 42 M.C. 1 39 Π πρόδοι ſtellarum. par.fcru.r. 8 54 カ20 3 08 54 Ⅱ 1158 44 46 retroce. 22 20 2 55० ४ 21 44 ४ 13 18 m Aliud exemplum: Sit horoſcopans pars ſexta Tauri, vbi nullus fit planeta nec vllus radius(nam fi parti horoſcopanti infideret aliquis planeta vel radius, is effet primus temporum arbiter) ſed tamen in præcedenti loco, hoc eft, in parte 4. Tauri fit corpus Iouis, & in ſequente parte 12. fit trigonus Luna, quem proxime ſequatur corpus Martis in parte 12. Geminorum, dii co quòd DIFFERENTIA 434 co, quòd Iuppiter primò gubernabic viram à partè ſexta Fauri, vſque ad partem 12.eiufdem, vbi funt radij triquetri Lunz: particeps autem eius erit & finium domina, ſed tamen etiam ipſa potens, nam in eodem loco habet trigonum & domum. Damus autem primum annum parti ſextæ, & non quarta Tauriy quam occupauit Iuppiterapte aſcendens, quia vt di&um eft, diretio & dire&ionis gubernatio, debet inchoari à locoAphete, & non à loco gubernatoris. Porro à parte 12. Tauri, vſque in finem Tauri,imò vſque ad partem 12. Geminorum,cùm nulli alij radij nec planetæ fint medij, gubernabit Luna eum finium do minis, fed multifariam & non vno modo. Nam à parte 12.8. vſque in partem 244.eiuſdem, plus poterit quam domini fi nium, quia tanta eſt magnitudo luminis Lunæ, que quantò ple nior erit,eò maior eius efficacia iudicanda.Sed à parte 24.in finem Tauri, cùm ) in 8 altitudinem habeat, æquales fermè erunt vires cum domino finis illius partis. Denique ab ini tio II ad partem eius duodecimam, propter longinquitatem ra diorum trigonorum,& mutationem ſigni,vbiLuna nullam habet poteftatem, finis domini maiorem habebit vim quàm D.. Hunc igitur in modum generales temporum arbitros creaSignifica- bimus. sores quo 7. Loca veròAphetica, ſeu vt vocant, ilegialia, hoc eft, Joca que que, &c. progrediuntur, ſunt quinque, ex quorum proceſſu de iis quz nato in vniuerſa vita obuentura ſunt,colligimus. Afcendentis ficut Aphete progreſsionem perpendimus, ad percipiendam nati valetudinem, quæque corpori obuentura fint, & quo tempore peregrinationes vtiles vel inutiles fit fufcepturas. Quando itaque ad afcendens benefica vel eius bonus radius deducuntur, bonum corporis habitum & fœlices peregrinationes nato decernemus. At fi eadem horoscopo occurrat malefice vel eorum noxij radij, diſcrafiam corporis & infelices peregripationes nato prædicemus. Dereliquorum locorum Aphetico rum progreſsionibus, eodem modo in ijs quæ fortunamaut infortunium fignificant, pronunciabimus. Hæcautem loca qua hic recenfentur, cum omnium bonorum fubie&a fint, ſequitur quòd non alia præter hec quinque in genituris procedere fit necellarium. Nam omnia bona sut ad animum aut ad corpus, autad QVARTA. 435 ut ad fortunam referuntur. Bona animia decernuntur.Bo. na corporis ab afcendente dependent. Quod ad fortuna bona attinet, eorum quæ ſunt magis principalia, vt dignitates,à: Modiocria verò ab horoscopo & M.C.defumuntur. Totidem loca numerantur etiam à Ptolemeo & Hermete enunciato 77. ποGp,inquit, ταπίπαομτε ὡροσκόπο, λὰ τὰ βωμαλίκα τόμ δικλῆρομη τύχης, λλὰ τὰ ἰτπίκτητα: πιὸ δι), λλά τιώ συνα ·φήμ το σώματος πρὸς τὴν ψύχαμ: τόνδε μεσημβρινομ, 21ὰ τὰς πράξης, hoc eft: Afcendentis progref fionem pro ijs, que corporis ſunt facito, partis fortuna pro iis, quz funt externa, Luna pro corporis animæque connexione, medium coe li,pro actioaibus. ۱ ; 1 2 Tabula DIFFERENTIA Tabulrafcenſionum rectarum G.G. m. translation: FOURTH 433. The rule of the Moon is transferred from Saturn to Jupiter and the Moon immediately following, which dominates up to the end of Libra, and 121st part of the 1st scruple of Scorpio, with Mars participating in the bounds in both places. For Mars holds the dominion of the bounds in the first six parts of Scorpio, both according to Ptolemy and the Egyptians. From here, a little after the first part of Scorpio, the rule of the rays is transferred from the Moon to Mercury, who projects his trine radiation into the 121st part of the 1st scruple of Scorpio, and by reason of these rays extends his rule up to the 28th part of Scorpio, with Mars participating first on account of the dominion of the bounds, then Jupiter, then Venus, and finally Mercury. From here, in the 28th part, 211th scruple of Scorpio, Mars assumes the most powerful rule of all: For besides the fact that he corporeally occupies the said place in his own house and trine, and being an enemy of the ascendant, he retrogrades in a fixed and venomous sign, with the testimony of the same Mars and Saturn, lords of the bounds of that direction, reaching the beginning of the 41st year, whose annual changes of signs and stellar progressions will be as follows: Annual Progressions. part.scr.1. Horosc. 26 44 ... 27 52 Lot 15 42 M.C. 1 39 Π Stellar progressions. part.scr.r. 8 54 20 3 08 54 Ⅱ 1158 44 46 retro. 22 20 2 550 21 44 13 18 m Another example: Let the horoscoping part be the sixth of Taurus, where there is no planet nor any ray (for if any planet or ray were in the horoscoping part, it would be the first arbiter of times), but nevertheless in the preceding place, that is, in the 4th part of Taurus, is the body of Jupiter, and in the following 12th part is the trine of the Moon, which is immediately followed by the body of Mars in the 12th part of Gemini, because DIFFERENCE 434 Jupiter will first govern life from the sixth part of Taurus, up to the 12th part of the same, where the trine rays of the Moon are: and the lord of the bounds will be its participant, but also powerful herself, for in the same place she has a trine and a house. We assign the first year to the sixth part, and not the fourth of Taurus, which Jupiter occupied near the ascendant, because, as has been said, the direction and the governance of the direction must begin from the place of the Apheta, and not from the place of the governor. Furthermore, from the 12th part of Taurus, up to the end of Taurus, indeed up to the 12th part of Gemini, since there are no other rays or planets in between, the Moon will govern with the lords of the bounds, but in many ways and not in one way. For from the 12th part up to the 24th part of the same, she will be more powerful than the lords of the bounds, because such is the magnitude of the Moon's light, which, the fuller it is, the greater its efficacy is to be judged. But from the 24th part to the end of Taurus, since the Moon has altitude in 8, her forces will be almost equal to the lord of the end of that part. Finally, from the beginning of Gemini to its twelfth part, on account of the distance of the trine rays, and the change of sign, where the Moon has no power, the lord of the end will have greater power than the Moon. In this way, therefore, we will create general arbiters of times. Signifiers. 7. The Aphetic places, or as they are called, illegal places, that is, places which progress, are five, from whose progression we gather what will happen to the native in his entire life. We consider the progression of the Ascendant as the Apheta, to perceive the native's health, what will happen to the body, and at what time useful or useless journeys will be undertaken. Therefore, when benefics or their good rays are led to the ascendant, we will decree good bodily habit and fortunate journeys for the native. But if malefics or their harmful rays occur to the same horoscope, we will predict bodily dyscrasia and unfortunate journeys for the native. Regarding the progressions of the other Aphetic places, we will pronounce in the same way concerning those things which signify fortune or misfortune. These places which are enumerated here, since they are the subjects of all goods, it follows that it is not necessary to proceed with any others besides these five in nativities. For all goods are referred either to the mind, or to the body, or to FOURTH. 435 or to fortune. Goods of the mind are decreed. Goods of the body depend on the ascendant. As for the goods of fortune, those which are more principal, such as dignities, are from: The mediocre ones, however, are taken from the horoscope and the M.C. The same number of places are also enumerated by Ptolemy and Hermes, enunciated 77. Ptolemy says: "The progression of the ascendant for those things which pertain to the body, the part of fortune for those things which are external, the Moon for the connection of the body and soul, the midheaven for actions." 1 2 Table of right ascensions G.G. m. author: Valentin Naibod
title: Lord of the Hour and Annual Predictions
text: QVARYA 445 fignificabitur per effe eius,id eft, dominum hora, fa nitas vel infirmitas corporis nati, ficut ſignificatur per dominum afcendentis radicis, id eft,natiuitatis:dabis dominum ſecunda hora ab ea, ſecunda domui ab aſcen dente in ſecundo anno, & fignificabitur per eum eſſe ſun in codem anno eſſe ſubſtantia, &c.ficut ſignificatur per eſſe domini domus ſubſtantiæ, & dabis dominum tertie horæ ab ea tertia domui ab afcendente tertio anno, & ſignificabitur per eſſe illius in eodem anno eſſe fratrum fororum & cæterorum, ficut ſignificabitur per eſſe domini tertiæ domus: dabis dominű horæ quartæ domui quartæ ab aſcendente in anno quarto, & ſignificabitur per eſſe ſuum in eodem anno eſſe patrum, & eorum qui ſignificantur per domum quartam. Similiter facies de dominis horarum per ſücceßionem, dabis ſcilicet do. minum vniufcuiusque horæ domui ex domibus radicali. bus, eritq; domino horæ XH,ab hora natiuitatis domus XII.ab aſcendente radicis: & annus XII. & dominus XIII.horæ à natiuitate radicis aſcēdētis: anni Xiij. & dominus XIIII. horæ ab ea domus ſubſtantiæ XIIII. anni.Et nominatur dominus horæ primæ domi. nus aſcendentis horæ, dominus II.horæ dominus horæ domus ſubſtantiæ,et dominus horæ tertiæ dominus horæ domus fratru: & fic in alijs ſimili dominus vniufcuiusq; harum horarū nominatur nomine domus, & nominatur etia orbis dominus, & ſignificatur p en omni anno ſicut ſignificatur p Alcochodě, hoc eſt-p dominu anni:et quida Aſtrologorú ponit dominü orbis,ſcilicet aſcēdētis radi. ka çis anno. DIFFERENTIA cis anno primo o anno planetam qui huic ſuccedit, Gin tertio anno planetam qui huic ſuccedit: ficut dic Ełum est in domino horæ. 5. Et ex hoc Alfridarie diſpoſitio, hoc eft, cùm natiui tas fuerit diurna, preerit in initio vitæ diſpoſitioni eius fridarie Sol, ſecundum quantitatem annorum fridaria eius, qui ſunt decé. Poſt hoc planeta qui ſuccedit Soli, qui eſt Venus, cuiusfridaria anni ſunt octo, & poſt Venerem planeta qui ſuccedit ei, & eſt Mercurius, & anni eius funt tredecim. Deinde Luna: anni fridaria eius funt nouem:deinde Saturnus, & anni fridaria eius funt XI. deinde Iuppiter,& anni fridaria eius funt XII.deinde Mars, & anni fridariæ eius ſunt VII.deinde caput dra conis, & anni fridariæ eius ſunt II I. poſt hoc cauda, & anni eius fridariæ funt duo funt ant anni collecti fimul LXXV.anni. Poſt hoc rexertitur diſpoſitio ad Solem: & ſimiliter vfque ad vltimum planetarum. Si autem fue rit natiuitas nocturna, incipit diſpoſitio à Luna, diſponetq; annos fridariæeius, qui ſunt nonem. Similiter pla neta poſt planetam, ſicut prædiximus, in Sole. Cumq; di. ſpoſuerit planeta annos fridariæ fuæ: diſponet propriè primam ſeptimam folum, id est,ſeptimam partem frida. ria ſui, deinde participatur ei in ſecunda ſeptima planeta qui eum ſuccedit: poſt hoc participatur ei in tertia ſe. ptima planeta tertius, qui ſuccedit ſecundum: & ita do nec participatur ei planeta, qui eſt ante eum in vltima ſe ptima ex annis fridariæ eius: & eſt vnicuique iudicium cum participatur alteri ſuper natiuitate. ExpoſtIQVARTA - Expofitaiamſunt elementa Aſtrologica autoris ferè otania, sque ad veearn antem nancifcendam pertinet. Reliqua verò quæ ſequitur, excepto capite de planetarum ſupereminentia, Lunariq; horoſcopo ſeu parte fortunarum, à natura diſſentire, & magis fortilegijs numerorumq; fortuitis caſibus, & id genus nugis alijs, quam veris & phyſicis rationibus conſentanea, atq very articurioſeaffida eſſe videntur, & non immeritò. Nam & Ptolemeus ea ipſahot nominepaucis attingit, atque mox tan quam otiofa & inaniareijcit: ταῦτα μενει, inquit, πιθανόμ, καὶ ὐ φυσικόμ, αλλα κεκοδόξομἔχοντα λόγομ Gulr. Et circa initiu libri tertij Sophiſticam & ſuperſtitioſam huiufmodi Aſtrolo giam grauitor reprehendit, his verbis: κους τα ή πολιώργοςτο τν πολλώμ φλυαυρύμπνα, καί μισά τσιθανόμ ἔχοντα λόγομ πρός τὰς ἀλὸ τ' φύσεως αἰτίας ἀποπεμτσόμανοι: τὰ δὲ οὐδεχομεναμ ἐὰ χοντα πώ κατάλυψις & Αλά κλήρους καὶ ἀριθμῶμ αναιτιολογή των, ἀλλὰ δι' αυτη τη χηματισμών πρός αύ οἰκείος τόπος θεω είας ἐπισκεπόμθνas, hoc est: Queq; multi curioſe & petulanter hugati funt, neque vllam verifimilitudinem pre ſe ferentia, ad ea que naturæ non conuenienter dicta ſunt, relegabimus: quæ verò naturæ deprehendi poſſunt, non per ſortes & numeros, quibus à naturæ rationes nullę inditæ ſunt, ſed contuitus conſigurationum ad familiaria loca obſeruantes. Hec ſequentia igitur, cùm ita quoque ſe habeant, leuiſſimo brachio attingentes perſtringemus. 1 δυοδεκατημόριa planetarum inquifiturus ſpacia planetaru Et ex hoc in fignis, in quibus deprehenduntur, emenſa attendat, quibus duodenanotatis p.& fcr.confecti itineris per 1 z. multiplicet, numeroq; rio &c. ex multiplicatione genito, id totu quod planeta in ſigna emen- ſus est, adijciat. Summá verò collectaab initio ſigni, in quo planetaçõmoratur, dando vnicuiq; figno p.30. proijciat, nam finis proiectionis ſeu numerationis planete quelicum διοδεκατημός Cop oftender. Verbigratia, fit querenduα δυοδικάτεμόριου Sa lis part.8.fcr.541 II luftrantis, multiplicap.8.fer.541 per 12, & efficientur ſexag. 1*part.46. fcr.48 1*, hoc est,figna 3.grad. 16.fcru.48 18, quibus fi adijcias part. 8. ſcru. 54 1,fient ſignaz part.25. fcrup.42 1. Atque hæ partes planetis ita diuiduntur, vt prime nouena parti præfideat dominus ſigni mobilis triplicitatis eiufdem; ſecundæ dominus ſigni, fignum mobile mox ſequentis, & fic conſequenter ſecundum planetarum dominium in ſignis ipforumq; fignorum naturalem ſeriem: Veluti prima parti nouenz & dominatur, nam pertinet ad igneum, vt vocant, trigonum, cuius fignum mobile eſt Aries & eius dominus Mars. Si prima pars Arietis horizontsorientalem circundet, tunc figni aſcendentis dominus decanus eius futurus eft : & fi eiufdem Arietis ſecunda de. curia aſcendat, erit eius decanus Sol, dominus, ſigni quinti ab Ariete & ſecundi in eodem trigono. Deniq; ſitertia decuria Vaſcendat, tunc 4 dominus decanus eius conſebitur. Nam +eſt ſignum 9.ab V, & tertium in eodem trigono. Similiter facile est iudicare de ſignis reliquis. : Et ex hoc 4 Si quando quos dominatores orbis vocant, conſtituerę pla dominus ceat,tunc per ea,que fuprà capite vltimo differentię ſecunde orbis, &c. precepta sunt, diligenter inquiras horam temporalem natiuicatis, atque eiufdem horę domino attribue horoſcopum, & anaum geniturę primum:is enişi co in anno fanitatem aut egrit dinem QVARTA 447 dinem corporis nati aliaq; omnia, quæ dominus aſcendentia largiri ſolet,fignificare dicitur.Hora ſequentis dominus anno domuić; ſequenti præſidebit, atque in codem anno par impe rium cum domino domus ſecundæ ſortiri putatur. translation: The lord of the hour signifies the health or illness of the native's body, just as the lord of the ascendant of the radix (nativity) signifies it. You will assign the lord of the second hour from it to the second house from the ascendant in the second year, and through it will be signified the substance in the same year, just as it is signified by the lord of the house of substance. And you will assign the lord of the third hour from it to the third house from the ascendant in the third year, and through it will be signified in the same year the brothers, sisters, and other things, just as it is signified by the lord of the third house. You will assign the lord of the fourth hour to the fourth house from the ascendant in the fourth year, and through its position in the same year will be signified the fathers and those who are signified by the fourth house. You will do similarly with the lords of the hours in succession, that is, you will assign the lord of each hour to a house from the radical houses. And the lord of the 12th hour from the hour of nativity will be the 12th house from the radical ascendant, and the 12th year. And the lord of the 13th hour from the nativity of the radical ascendant will be the 13th year. And the lord of the 14th hour from it will be the house of substance for the 14th year. And the lord of the first hour is called the lord of the ascendant of the hour, the lord of the second hour is called the lord of the house of substance, and the lord of the third hour is called the lord of the house of brothers; and so in others similarly, the lord of each of these hours is named by the name of the house, and is also called the lord of the orb, and is signified by it in every year just as it is signified by the Alcochoden, that is, by the lord of the year. And some astrologers place the lord of the orb, namely the radical ascendant, in the first year, and in the second year the planet that succeeds it, and in the third year the planet that succeeds this one, as has been said concerning the lord of the hour. And from this comes the Alfridaria disposition, that is, when the nativity is diurnal, the Sun will preside over its disposition at the beginning of life, according to the quantity of its Alfridaria years, which are ten. After this, the planet that succeeds the Sun, which is Venus, whose Alfridaria years are eight, and after Venus, the planet that succeeds her, which is Mercury, and its years are thirteen. Then the Moon: its Alfridaria years are nine; then Saturn, and its Alfridaria years are eleven; then Jupiter, and its Alfridaria years are twelve; then Mars, and its Alfridaria years are seven; then the head of the dragon, and its Alfridaria years are three; after this the tail, and its Alfridaria years are two. The years collected together are 75 years. After this, the disposition returns to the Sun; and similarly up to the last of the planets. If, however, the nativity is nocturnal, the disposition begins with the Moon, and it will dispose its Alfridaria years, which are nine. Similarly, planet after planet, as we said before, in the Sun. And when a planet has disposed its Alfridaria years, it will specifically dispose the first seventh alone, that is, the seventh part of its Alfridaria; then the planet that succeeds it participates with it in the second seventh; after this, the third planet, which succeeds the second, participates with it in the third seventh; and so until the planet that is before it participates with it in the last seventh of its Alfridaria years; and each has judgment when it participates with another over the nativity. The astrological elements of the author are now almost all explained, as far as they pertain to obtaining the truth. The rest, however, which follows, except for the chapter on the preeminence of the planets, and the lunar horoscope or part of fortunes, seem to dissent from nature, and to be more consonant with fortune-telling and the fortuitous cases of numbers, and other such trifles, than with true and physical reasons, and not undeservedly. For Ptolemy himself touches upon these very things by name in a few places, and then immediately rejects them as idle and empty: "These things, however," he says, "are probable, but not natural, but rather based on popular opinion." And around the beginning of the third book, he gravely criticizes such sophistical and superstitious astrology with these words: "Those things which many have curiously and petulantly sought, and which bear no verisimilitude, we will relegate to those things which are not conveniently said to be of nature; but those things which can be apprehended by nature, not through lots and numbers, to which no reasons of nature are inherent, but by observing the configurations of familiar places." Therefore, since the following things are also of this nature, we will briefly touch upon them. And from this, in inquiring into the dodecatemoria of the planets, one should attend to the measured spaces of the planets in the signs in which they are found, and having noted these by degrees and minutes, multiply the completed journey by 12, and to the number generated by the multiplication, add the whole that the planet has measured in the signs. Then, from the beginning of the sign in which the planet resides, project the sum collected, giving 30 degrees to each sign, for the end of the projection or numbering will show the dodecatemorium of the planet. For example, if the dodecatemorium of the Sun is to be sought, which is in 8 degrees, 54 minutes of Leo, multiply 8 degrees, 54 minutes by 12, and 60 degrees, 48 minutes will be produced, that is, 3 signs, 16 degrees, 48 minutes, to which if you add 8 degrees, 54 minutes, it will become 3 signs, 25 degrees, 42 minutes. Now give the first sign to Gemini, the second to Cancer, the third to Leo, and finally the dodecatemorium of the Sun will reach 25 degrees, 42 minutes of Virgo. In this way, the Sun placed in 9 degrees, 53 minutes of Libra projects its dodecatemorium into 8 degrees, 26 minutes of Sagittarius. And from this follows the order or series of the nine parts. If you divide an entire sign into 9 parts, each ninth portion, as they call it, will have 3 degrees, 20 minutes. And these parts are divided among the planets in such a way that the lord of the movable sign of the same triplicity presides over the first ninth part; the lord of the sign immediately following the movable sign over the second; and so consequently according to the dominion of the planets in the signs and the natural series of those signs. For example, Mars dominates the first ninth part, for it belongs to the fiery trigon, as they call it, whose movable sign is Aries and its lord is Mars. The ruler of the second ninth part of Leo is Venus, because Taurus, whose ruler is Venus, immediately follows Aries in the order of the signs. The lord of Gemini presides over the third ninth part of Leo. The Moon is the ruler of the fourth. Jupiter is the lord of the fifth. Saturn is the lord of the sixth. Mars is the lord of the eighth. Jupiter is the lord of the ninth, and so on for the rest. Therefore, if in reading Arabic writings, sentences concerning the lord of Anaubarach, or the lord of the luminaries, occur, we are warned in this chapter that they are to be understood concerning the dominion of the ninth part of the heretical light. And from this, the following jurisdiction of the decanates, how much it differs from the decans proposed in the first difference, is quite clear from the example recited here. If the first part of Aries encircles the eastern horizon, then the lord of the ascendant sign will be its decan; and if the second decuria of the same Aries ascends, its decan will be the Sun, the lord of the fifth sign from Aries and the second in the same trigon. Finally, if the third decuria of Aries ascends, then Jupiter will be considered its decan. For Jupiter is the ninth sign from Aries, and the third in the same trigon. Similarly, it is easy to judge concerning the remaining signs. And from this, if at any time it is desired to constitute those whom they call the rulers of the orb, then through what has been taught above in the last chapter of the second difference, diligently inquire into the temporal hour of the nativity, and attribute the horoscope to the lord of that hour, and the first year of the nativity; for it is said to signify in that year the health or illness of the native's body and all other things which the lord of the ascendant is accustomed to bestow. The lord of the following hour will preside over the following year and house, and in the same year is thought to obtain equal power with the lord of the second house. In exactly the same way, you will order the lords of the hours and the succeeding celestial stations, until you reach the significator of the twelfth house. However, with the twelfth year completed, in the following 13th year of the native's life, the first house will revert to the lord of the 13th hour, from which it will proceed in the same manner up to the end of the 24th year according to the order of the houses and planets.

author: Valentin Naibod
title: Method of Horary Judgment
text: SECVNDA. 843 rei nominant. Sic ſupra quartam domum ſignificatricem patris conſtituit, id quod cum doctrina Ptolemai minimè conſentit. di - Atque hoc principium conſiderationis, eſt veluti materia ſeu ſubiectum eius quod inquirendum nobis propoſuerimus. Secundò obſeruandum eſt, quæ ſtellae habeantius exercendi dominium in hæc loca, ſecundum eos quos in prima differentia prædiximus, quinque modos. Et ſi vna ſtella vniuerſas preroga tiuas in loco ſignificatoris fortiatur, omnibusque modis prefuerit, in illam dominium omnemque eius euentus decernendi iuriſdictionem conferre debemus. Quomodo verò vna ſtella vni loco omnibus modis pręesſe queat, in prima differentia oſtenſum eſt. Sin autem duę plureſúe preſideant, in eas quæ ſuf fragiorum numero preſtant, præcipuam poteſtatem decernendi transferemus, participantibus reliquis pauciores prærogatiuas habentibus. Cum autem ex quinque modis dominiorum vnus fit aſpectus ſeu configuratio,patet, quòd nequaquam fit negligendus planeta, qui in ipſo loco quæſiti conſtituitur, vel eidem radio affulget. Hi autem locorum pręſides ſimilitudine caufæ efficientis non immeritò obtinent. Tertiò inquirenda eſt rei quæſitz qualitas, ſeu quod idem eſt,genus quæſtionis qualitatibus diſcernendum eſt. Exempli gratia, Materia ſeu genus quæſtionis eſt diuitiæ. Id qualitatibus diſtinguitur in hunc modum, quod diuitiæ futuræ ſint vel in pecunia, vel agris, vel donis, vel alijs mercibus, aut denique alia quadam induſtria ingenioque parabuntur. Cæterùm vt quale futurum fit quod decernitur coniectemus, vtrunque videre oportet,& naturas ipforum dominorum, quorum nomine, vt antè admonui, ſimul : locum intuentes & dominis radijs commiſtos intelligimus, & idiotropias ſignorum, planetas dominatores, & loca pręcipua continentium. Hic & hoc oſerua, nomine ſignorum tam afteriſmos octaui orbis, quam müdi dodecatemoria intelligi. Quar tapars methodi eſt de magnitudine effectus, cuius tres ſtatus plerunque in arte deſcribuntur, nempe ſummus, medius & in- ſumus. Media autem quæ cadunt inter illa extrema,cùm ſint quaſi infinita,&præceptis comprehendi nequeant, ad artificiofam.coniecturam & ſagacitatem eruditi mathematici pertinet. Hec autem quantitas effectorum ſeu decretorum ſumitur à 1 ; : F ; : i ! : Hh 2 ſtellaDigitized by Google DIFFERENTIA quàm ſtellarum dominantium robore, quod vt in ſequenti differen tia cap.de locis fortunatis & infortunatis Deo dante clariùs o ſtendemus, à quatuor circunſtantijs colligitur, nempe ad, ad ſuas adidas, ad ea loca cæli in quibus hærent, & denique ad ge neſin comparatione. Vltima pars methodi pertinet ad γόμκαθά. λο χρόνομ τῷ ἀποτελέσματα, ideſt, tempus decreti vniuertim cognofcendum. Hic duo in dominatoribus ſpectantur: primò vtrum ſint matutini vel veſpertini, idque tam ad horofcopum comparati. Quadrantes autem & horofcopum antecedentes ijſque oppoſitos, matutinos : Reliquos verò & ſequentes, veſpertinos à Ptolemæo nominari ſuprà eſt expoſi tum. Deinde etiam videndum eſt, vtrum ſint in centris aut in locis ſuccedentibus poſiti. Nam cùm funt matutini aut in angu lis conſtituti, vim ſuam citius exerunt:fi veſpertini aut in ſucce dentibus, tardius. Hic generalis modus coniectandi decretorú tempora ciπλάθd accipiendus eſt: nam exquifita & exacta eorum definitio ad directiones & alias particulares progreſsio nes infrà explicandas pertinet. 3 Hinc intelligi poteſt id quoq; quod ſequitur in textu, népè Et ſignifi- occidentalé ſignificare ſenectuté vltimam: orientalem verò cat ſenes ſenijinitium:ad vltimam hanc partem Methodi, id eſt,ad conie Auté &c. Auram de tempore futurorum eſſe referendum. Id enim aliquomodo excuſari poterit, ſi per orientalem & occidentalem Saturnum intelligas matutinum & veſpertinum: idque vt antè dictum eſt, tam ad Solem quam ad mundum comparatum. Sed videtur,autorem hic agere non de tempore, quod à diſpoſitione ſignificatorum ad Solem & angulos mundi ſumitur, ſed potiùs intelligere ſeptem ætates omnibus hominibus comunes, quarum ſingulæ ob certam diſpoſitionem planetis ſingulis có ſentiunt atque diſtribuutur. Quòd ſi ita eſt, dupliciter eum hic hallucinari apparet. Primò quòd aliter quam Ptolemeus atque minùs proprie, naturęq; minus conſentaned ætates ſtellis erran tibus diſtribuit, id quod ſatis facilè intelligent ij, qui autoris hanc de ætatibus opinionem conferre volent cum ſententia Ptolemei, quam mox dicemus, & autor ipſe eandem in fine huius Differentiæ deſcribet.Secundò quòd communem errorem ſequitur in hoc quòd dicit, Saturnum ſignificare ſene&utem vltimam Digitized by Google t SECVNDA. 245 vitimam ſi fuerit occidentalis, initium verd ſenectutis ſi orien. talis fuerit. Quaſi verò aliquid quodad hoc negotium attinet, interſit, planetas occidentales eſſe aut orientales, benè aut malè in genitura poſitos, atque ob id in ſingulis ætatibus expectan dos effe effectus congruentes diſpoſitioni ſtellarum, quæ ipſis ætatibus tribuuntur. Etenim errant, qui opinantur primos 4. annos (exempli gratia) Lunæ attributos, ſequentibus decem an nis, quibus Mercurius præficitur, ideo fore fœliciores, quia ab initio Lunæ melius quam & affecta fuerit. Sicut herbas, plantas, arbores, animalia, lapides, metalla, partes corporis, & ſimiles rerum formas ob temperaturam præcipuè certis ſtel. lis attribui iam ſepius annotavimus. Ita quoque ętates, quarum ſingulæ peculiari præditæ ſunt natura, maximè ob ſimilitudiné cralium certis ſtellis conſentiunt. Exempli gatia, Extrema illa & confecta ætas cùm fit frigida, arida,terreſtris,pigra, tremula, inepta, morboſa, pauida, & talibus malis quæ crafin Saturni ſequuntur,impedita, ideo quoque Saturno rectè adſcribitur. Id autem non propter Saturni in genitura diſpoſitionem, ſed pro pter huius ætatis quæ omnibus hominibus communis eſt, dyſ- crafiam. Non enim hic vel ille homo Saturnum in exortu ſuo malè habens diſpoſitum, ſed & omnesalij quibus dexter afful- ſit, in ſenili ętate humidi naturalis, το εμφύτε θόρμό defectú experiuntur.Quòd autem alij alijs ſeu mitiorem ſeu moleſtiorem ſentiunt ſenectutem, id referendum eſtadalias caufas par ticulares, nempè ad diuerfarú ſtellarum diuerfos effectus, qui in eam ætatem incidentes tales in communi ſenij natura intentiones remiſsioneſq; efficiunt. Nam cùm vniuerfaliter omnium ſenum corpora ob conſenſum diſpoſitionis incircumfu- ſo aére magis affligantur Sole eunte per æquinoctia, & vice ver ſa in maio ob humidiſsimam conſtitutionem quaſi recreentur: cui dubium iam ampliùs eſſe poterit, in particularibus quoque cóſtitutionibus, mitius ſenium experturos eſſe eos, in quorum genituris beneficæ ſtellae ſuam vim in ea etate oſtenſurę deprehenduntur, etiamſi Saturnus tempore genituræ malè affectus fuerit.Perditè verò afflictam fore ſenectutem, ſi maleficæſtella in ea ſibi imperium aſſumpture ſint, quantumuis Saturno in genitura benè poſito. Eodem modo iudicandű eſt de alijs quoHb 3 que - Digitized by Google • 246 DIFFERENTIA que ętatibus que ad numerum errantium ſiderú ſeptem à Prolemço numerantur. Sanè Ptolemeus in fine quadripartitę con- ſtructionis quatuor inſtituit vitę humane partitiones temporis.Prima & omnium generaliſsima eſt in 7. etates, que omnibus hominibus, vt dixi, communes ſunt, atque planetis ſecundum naturalem ipforum feriem diſtribuuntur. Quatuor enim primi anni cópleti, quibus humiditas nimia, & ob id incrementa ſubita, morbus comitialis & valetudinis inconſtatia maximè in corpore vigent: in mente verò omnia ſalté inchoata & inemendata cernuntur, tribuuntur Lunæ: cuius temperaturę omnia iſta reſpondent. Vocatur autem primum hoc tépus ἁλικία Βρεφώδης, id eft, etas infantię. A principio anni quinti ad finé 14. Lunæ ſuccedit Mercurij ętas, que vocatur ἁλικία παιδικά, id eſt, puerilis ętas. Hec animos ad intelligentiam componere, diſciplinarum ſemina inſerere, morum ſtudiorumque peculia res conditiones patefacere, & deniq; in primis artium rudimen tis excitare incipit. translation: The second part of the method is about the thing they name. Thus, above the fourth house, it constitutes the significator of the father, which is minimally consistent with Ptolemy's doctrine. And this principle of consideration is, as it were, the matter or subject of what we have proposed to inquire into. Secondly, it must be observed which stars have the power to exercise dominion in these places, according to those whom we mentioned in the first difference, in five ways. And if one star obtains all prerogatives in the place of the significator, and presides in all ways, we must confer upon it all dominion and jurisdiction for determining its outcome. How one star can preside over one place in all ways has been shown in the first difference. But if two or more preside, we will transfer the principal power of decision to those that excel in the number of votes, with the others having fewer prerogatives participating. Since one of the five modes of dominion is aspect or configuration, it is clear that the planet which is constituted in the very place of the question, or shines upon it with its ray, should by no means be neglected. These rulers of places, not without reason, obtain a similarity to the efficient cause. Thirdly, the quality of the thing sought must be inquired into, or, which is the same, the genus of the question must be distinguished by qualities. For example, the matter or genus of the question is riches. This is distinguished by qualities in this way: whether the riches will be in money, or lands, or gifts, or other merchandise, or finally, will be acquired by some other industry and ingenuity. But in order to conjecture what the outcome will be, it is necessary to consider both the natures of the lords themselves, by whose name, as I warned before, we understand at the same time the place and the lords mixed with their rays, and the idiosyncrasies of the signs, the ruling planets, and the principal places they contain. Here also observe that by the name of signs are understood both the asterisms of the eighth sphere and the dodecatemoria of the world. The fourth part of the method is concerning the magnitude of the effect, of which three states are usually described in the art, namely, the highest, the middle, and the lowest. But the intermediate states that fall between those extremes, since they are almost infinite and cannot be comprehended by precepts, pertain to the artful conjecture and sagacity of the learned mathematician. This quantity of effects or decrees is taken from the strength of the ruling stars, which, as we shall show more clearly, God willing, in the following difference, chapter on fortunate and unfortunate places, is gathered from four circumstances, namely, their own nature, their own rays, the places of the heavens in which they are fixed, and finally, their genesis by comparison. The last part of the method pertains to the universal knowledge of the time of the decree. Here two things are observed in the rulers: first, whether they are matutine or vespertine, and this is compared to the horoscope. Those in the quadrants and preceding the horoscope and opposite to them are called matutine; the rest and those following are called vespertine by Ptolemy, as has been explained above. Then it must also be seen whether they are placed in angles or in succedent places. For when they are matutine or constituted in angles, they exert their power more quickly; if vespertine or in succedent places, more slowly. This general method of conjecturing the times of decrees must be taken simply; for their exquisite and exact definition pertains to the directions and other particular progressions to be explained below. Hence, what follows in the text can also be understood, namely, that the occidental signifies extreme old age, but the oriental signifies the beginning of old age; this must be referred to the last part of this method, that is, to the conjecture about the time of future events. For this can be excused in some way if by oriental and occidental Saturn you understand matutine and vespertine; and this, as said before, compared both to the Sun and to the world. But it seems that the author here is not dealing with time, which is taken from the disposition of the significators to the Sun and the angles of the world, but rather understands the seven ages common to all men, each of which, due to a certain disposition, agrees with and is distributed among individual planets. If this is so, he appears to be mistaken in two ways here. First, because he distributes the ages to the wandering stars differently from Ptolemy and less properly, and less consistently with nature, which those who wish to compare this author's opinion on ages with Ptolemy's opinion, which we will soon discuss, and which the author himself will describe at the end of this Difference, will easily understand. Secondly, because he follows a common error in saying that Saturn signifies extreme old age if it is occidental, but the beginning of old age if it is oriental. As if it mattered for this matter whether planets are occidental or oriental, well or ill placed in the geniture, and therefore that congruent effects corresponding to the disposition of the stars attributed to those ages should be expected in each age. For those err who think that the first 4 years (for example) attributed to the Moon, will therefore be happier than the following ten years, to which Mercury is assigned, because the Moon was better affected from the beginning. Just as we have often noted that herbs, plants, trees, animals, stones, metals, parts of the body, and similar forms of things are attributed to certain stars mainly because of their temperament. So also ages, each of which is endowed with a peculiar nature, agree with certain stars especially because of the similarity of their temperaments. For example, that extreme and worn-out age, being cold, dry, earthy, sluggish, trembling, inept, sickly, fearful, and impeded by such evils as follow the temperament of Saturn, is therefore rightly ascribed to Saturn. But this is not because of Saturn's disposition in the geniture, but because of the dyscrasia of this age, which is common to all men. For it is not that this or that man has Saturn ill-disposed at his birth, but all others, to whom it shone favorably, experience a deficiency of natural moisture, in old age. But that some experience old age more mildly or more troublesomely than others, must be referred to other particular causes, namely, to the diverse effects of diverse stars, which, falling into that age, produce such intentions and remissions in the common nature of old age. For since universally the bodies of all old people are more afflicted by the Sun going through the equinoxes due to the consent of disposition in the surrounding air, and conversely are, as it were, recreated in May due to their very humid constitution: who can now doubt that in particular constitutions, those in whose genitures benefic stars are found to show their power in that age will experience a milder old age, even if Saturn was ill-affected at the time of birth. But old age will be utterly afflicted if malefic stars are to assume dominion in it, no matter how well Saturn is placed in the geniture. The same judgment must be made concerning the other ages, which are numbered by Ptolemy to seven, according to the number of wandering stars. Indeed, Ptolemy, at the end of the Quadripartite construction, establishes four divisions of human life in time. The first and most general is into 7 ages, which, as I said, are common to all men, and are distributed among the planets according to their natural order. For the first four complete years, in which excessive humidity, and therefore sudden growth, epilepsy, and instability of health are most prevalent in the body, while in the mind all things are at least nascent and uncorrected, are attributed to the Moon, to whose temperament all these things correspond. This first period is called the age of infancy. From the beginning of the fifth year to the end of the 14th, Mercury's age succeeds the Moon's, which is called the age of childhood. This begins to compose minds for understanding, to implant the seeds of disciplines, to reveal peculiar conditions of morals and studies, and finally to excite the first rudiments of arts. Afterwards, from 14 to the 22nd year, Venus presides, during which time the age of adolescence is comprehended. This begins to move the seminal passages, which first

translation: Lastly, because it is sometimes hindered and cannot help us, when this happens, great evils usually befall those who, turning against it, call it malefic, which is truly undeserved, as no one is better by nature. For it prolongs life, and benignly bestows a healthy body, innate strength, and a sincere and unwavering mind. It confers royal dignities and honors. It presents us with abundant wealth. It is fitting to believe that this is the soul of the entire world, or more plainly, its mind; this is the principal governance and number of nature, estimating its works. For it provides light to things and removes darkness. It conceals the other stars and tempers the most pleasing changes of seasons and the perpetually regenerating year according to nature. It dispels celestial sadness and also brightens the human mind's fog. It lends its light to other stars as well, being brilliant, exceptional, seeing all things, and even hearing all things, as Homer, the prince of letters, pleased, in the Iliad: 'the sun, he says, as they know, shines upon others.' Nazianzen compares it to the divine mind with these words: 'What the sun is to the body, God is to the soul.' There are therefore, to sum up everything, two stars, namely Saturn and Mars, which are harmful; and three, namely Jupiter, Venus, and the Moon, which are beneficial; and finally two, namely the Sun and Mercury, which are of both natures. Finally, it is useful to note that the planets which our astrologers call fortunate, unfortunate, and of both natures, Ptolemy simply names 'benefic,' 'malefic,' and 'intermediate.' His words on this are: 'Since we have distinguished two of the genera and again, two of the nature and manners of each, the unequal of the good and great, and that concerning the nature of each, and the external great ones being diminished. For the planets Jupiter, Venus, and the Moon are simply benefic, and among the intermediate are the Sun and Mercury, as in those concerning the disposition of parents and those in the soul, and those concerning outcomes and things pertaining to matters simply in the planet. For each of them differs in the quality of generation, such as the Sun, Mercury, and Moon. (And each of the others...)
